posts.summary: This guest post explores the pervasive web tracking ecosystem using Firefox add-ons Collusion and NoScript to visualize how companies collect user data. The author demonstrates how even visiting non-commercial sites like blogs results in data being shared with numerous third parties including Google, DoubleClick, and various ad networks. The post notes that blocking all cookies and scripts makes websites unusable, leaving users with no real choice about being tracked. Drawing parallels to EZ-passes and credit cards, the author argues that convenience consistently wins over privacy. Despite tools existing to limit tracking, the author concludes that maintaining privacy is too time-consuming for most people and offers no clear solution.
key_points: Web tracking is ubiquitous: even non-commercial sites share visitor data with multiple third parties including analytics, advertising, and data collection companies
Users face a false choice between functionality and privacy - blocking all cookies/scripts breaks websites, while allowing them means surrendering control over personal data
Browser add-ons like Collusion and NoScript reveal tracking but also demonstrate how difficult it is to prevent
Privacy erosion follows a pattern: technologies initially rejected (like car tracking) become normalized through convenience (EZ-pass)
Americans have virtually no legal protection against tracking compared to Europeans
money_quotes: All I know is that they know more about me than I know about them.
Block all cookies and the site won't work very well. Allow cookies and we will send your info to whomever we choose (within the law, of course).
Who would let themselves be tracked everywhere they went. Well, 40 years later, nearly everyone who has a car has an EZ-pass.
I like conveniences. Keeping your privacy is hard.
Who knew that a pixel could do so much?
The question is how to make the tradeoff. Figuring that out is time consuming. I've got better things to do with my life.
I'm going to go read a book.
posts.key_insight: The asymmetry between trackers and the tracked—combined with the friction costs of protecting privacy—means convenience will always win, making meaningful consent to data collection essentially impossible.

posts.summary: This post announces several activities of the Alternative Banking group of Occupy Wall Street. Their proposal for a panel at the Left Forum conference was accepted, where they plan to hold a 'regular meeting' on breaking up megabanks. The group recently staged a protest at Citigroup's annual shareholder meeting that garnered media coverage. They are also writing a book called 'Occupy Finance' modeled after the Debt Resistor's Operation Manual and are seeking volunteer writers and editors from the general public.
key_points: Alternative Banking group meets weekly at Columbia to discuss financial reform, with public pre-meeting talks
Their Left Forum panel proposal on breaking up megabanks was accepted for the June conference alongside speakers like Noam Chomsky and Cornel West
The group protested outside Citigroup's annual shareholder meeting, receiving coverage from Financial Times and other outlets
They are writing 'Occupy Finance' book and explicitly welcome non-specialist editors to ensure accessibility
money_quotes: The normal meeting is open to the public as well, but it can get pretty wonky.
Our probably topic will be 'Breaking up the megabanks' or something along those lines.
You don't need to be a specialist in finance to be an editor, that's the point. We're writing it for the interested public.
posts.key_insight: Financial reform activism thrives through open, accessible community organizing that welcomes non-specialists to participate in both discussions and content creation.

posts.summary: In this advice column installment, Aunt Pythia addresses questions about unwanted male attention, ethical investing, and sorority culture. She offers nuanced advice on dealing with persistent suitors, emphasizing the importance of explicit rejection while acknowledging that some men need direct statements to understand boundaries. On investing, she expresses deep skepticism of the retirement industry and 'socially responsible' investing, advocating instead for expanded Social Security. The column concludes with a sharp feminist critique of sorority culture, analyzing a viral letter that reveals how Greek life systems incentivize women to compete for male approval through flirtation and sexual attention.
key_points: Men are often trained to assume interest unless explicitly told otherwise, placing an unfair burden on women to reject unwanted advances
The 'socially responsible investing' industry is likely 99% a scam that lets people feel virtuous while still feeding money to problematic institutions
The retirement industry profits from managing money while not serving people's actual values or interests
Sorority culture creates a competitive system where women are pressured to win male approval through flirtation and sexual availability
Greek life's competitive currency system was likely designed by fraternities to benefit themselves
money_quotes: A lot of people, especially men, are trained to assume they're right unless they've been explicitly told they're wrong.

I'd love to hear from readers who have even more knowledge of 'ethical investing', specifically if it's a scam to take advantage of people who want to consider themselves environmentally conscious (probability: 99.3%).
I don't even think it should be an industry – I think we should provide for retired people directly through Social Security and stop feeding all these funds to the market.
I am grateful that I have a job that helps me pay my bills and allows me to not think about money very much.
There are a hell of a lot of people in this country way worse off than I am, and we're all going to have to figure this out somehow (expand Social Security!).
Whatever social currency these women are hoping to capture, it involves impressing men with their friendliness, flirtatiousness, and possibly their actual sexual promiscuity.
Here's a shot in the dark: this competitive currency system, whatever the hell it is, was set up by the fraternities.
posts.key_insight: Systems that appear neutral—whether dating norms, retirement investing, or Greek life—are often structured to benefit those with power while placing burdens on everyone else.

posts.summary: In this lighthearted Sunday post, Cathy O'Neil shares a collection of things making her happy, from the discovery that chocolate fondue machines cost only $9 to an upcoming Star Trek movie. She briefly touches on substantive news—a bipartisan bill to end too-big-to-fail banks—before returning to personal updates about her new knitting project with linen. The post deliberately eschews serious analysis in favor of simple pleasures and weekend optimism.
key_points: Consumer goods like chocolate fondue machines are surprisingly affordable compared to restaurant experiences
The Brown-Vitter bill to address too-big-to-fail banks represents growing momentum for financial reform
Sunday mornings after adequate sleep create space for whimsy rather than ranting
Personal hobbies like knitting provide satisfaction worth sharing
money_quotes: Sunday mornings tend to provoke me to write the most whimsical posts of the week.
Especially on a glorious sunny Spring morning like today, I can't abide any bad news or ranting.
It's like a happiness machine.
It doesn't mean it'll be passed, or that it's perfect, but the momentum is gaining, which is good.
posts.key_insight: Even writers known for sharp criticism need occasional breaks to celebrate simple pleasures and small wins.

posts.summary: This guest post by mathematician Kaisa Taipale presents visualizations of where mathematics Ph.D. graduates end up after graduation, using data from the AMS Annual Survey. She uses R and Circos to create circular graphs showing employment patterns, revealing that private R1 graduates tend to end up at private R1 institutions more often than their public university counterparts. The post explores the relationship between mathematical subfields and career outcomes, noting the cultural hierarchy between 'pure' subjects like algebra and 'applied' fields. Taipale acknowledges limitations in her analysis while inviting feedback and further questions from readers.
key_points: Ph.D. graduates from private R1 universities end up at private R1 institutions more frequently than graduates from public R1s, raising questions about self-selection versus employer preference
The visualization categorizes outcomes into academic positions, business/industry/government, non-U.S. employment, and those still seeking jobs
Different mathematical subfields lead to different career outcomes, with applied math associated with making money or 'making a difference'
The author learned R and data visualization through Coursera courses, demonstrating accessible paths into data analysis
Statistics Ph.D.s were removed from one visualization because their employment ribbons were disproportionately large
money_quotes: We all know algebra and number theory are the purest and most virtuous subjects, and applied math is for people who want to make money or want to make a difference in the world.
The other question – the question current mathematicians secretly care more about, in a gossipy and potentially catty way – is what fields lead to what fate.
Does the professoriate in the audience have any idea if this is self-selection or some sort of preference on the part of employers?
I had to start somewhere though, and as I'm a total newbie when it comes to data analysis, I decided to learn some skills while focusing on a data set that I have easy non-technical access to.
Their courses and Stack Overflow taught me almost everything I know about R.
posts.key_insight: Career outcomes for math Ph.D.s are shaped by both institutional pedigree and subfield choice, with private university graduates showing stronger pipelines back into private institutions.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that she has compiled all her mathbabe blog posts into a single downloadable book using Leanpub, totaling 1,243 pages. She charges only the platform's minimum fee of $0.99, viewing the content as already free since it's available on her blog. She expresses genuine puzzlement about why anyone besides herself would want the compilation, though notes readers had requested it. She also points out that the tool apparently doesn't require authentication to pull RSS feeds, meaning anyone could create the same book themselves.
key_points: The mathbabe book is a comprehensive archive of her blog posts, organized mostly by date, spanning 1,243 pages
She's charging only the platform minimum ($0.99), refusing to profit from content that's already freely available
The Leanpub tool works by pulling RSS feeds without requiring authentication, raising interesting questions about content accessibility
She invites readers to suggest open-source alternatives for creating books from blog content
money_quotes: I'm not charging anything over that, because the way I look at it, it's already free.

I can see why I'd want a copy of this stuff, since it's the best way I can think of to have a local version of a bunch of writing I've done over the past couple of years, but I don't actually see why anyone else would.
So please don't think I'm expecting you to go buy this book!
So if you want to avoid paying 99 cents, I'm pretty sure you can go to leanpub or one of its competitors and create another, identical book using that same feed.
posts.key_insight: A blogger's writing has value as a personal archive, but the ethical stance is to keep it accessible and not profit from content that was always meant to be free.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that megabanks must be broken up because they've become too powerful, too costly, too lawless, and too dangerous to the economy. She details how the 2008 bailout gave banks $700 billion with no accountability, how the revolving door between Wall Street and government perpetuates their influence, and how ongoing implicit guarantees cost taxpayers an estimated $83 billion annually in subsidized borrowing. She catalogs their crimes—from HSBC's money laundering to fraudulent foreclosures—and argues the protected status of megabanks creates perverse incentives that guarantee an even worse future crash.
key_points: The 2008 bailout gave banks $700 billion with no strings attached, and money meant for homeowners was diverted elsewhere
The 'revolving door' between government and banking creates an insular system of influence that citizens cannot compete with
Taxpayers implicitly subsidize megabank risk at an estimated $83 billion per year through lower borrowing costs
Banks face no meaningful consequences for crimes ranging from money laundering to market manipulation to fraudulent foreclosures
The current protected status creates worse incentives than before, making a future crash inevitable and likely more severe
money_quotes: He scared Congress and the American people to such an extent that the banks managed to receive $700 billion with no strings attached.
It is unreasonable to expect to compete with this kind of insularity and influence of the megabanks.
Make no mistake about it: risk is valuable. It may not be quantifiable at a moment of time, but over time it becomes quite valuable and quantifiable indeed.
The idea of a financial sector is to grease the wheels of commerce, to create a machine that allows the economy to work. But in our case we have a machine that's taken over the economy instead.
Now that they live in a officially protected zone, there is even less reason for them then there used to be to rein in risky practices.
The best job in finance you can have right now is inside a big bank, protected from the law, rather than working at a hedge fund where you can be nabbed for insider trading.
What we need to worry about now is how bad the next crash is going to be.
posts.key_insight: The financial sector has transformed from a machine that serves the economy into one that has captured and exploited it, with megabanks now operating in an officially protected zone where bad incentives guarantee future catastrophe.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques a Foreign Affairs article on big data that, while acknowledging some concerns, fails to seriously grapple with surveillance and privacy implications. She dissects the flawed assumption that 'N=ALL' in big data, arguing that data collection systematically excludes marginalized voices and perpetuates the myth of objective data. The piece examines how big data primarily serves corporate efficiency and profit rather than public welfare, with examples ranging from discriminatory hiring algorithms to medical data exploitation. O'Neil contrasts the article's weak treatment of 'big brother' concerns with Julie Cohen's more rigorous critique of how pervasive surveillance threatens innovation and autonomy.
key_points: Datafication exists on a spectrum from intentional participation to unintentional surveillance, but the outcomes for individuals remain the same regardless of intent
The assumption that N=ALL is dangerous because data collection systematically excludes those without time, energy, or access—making busy, marginalized people invisible
Data does not 'speak for itself'—ignoring causation in models can perpetuate historical discrimination rather than address it
Big data primarily benefits company owners through automation and efficiency gains, not the general public
Pervasive surveillance poses a greater threat to innovation than privacy protections do, contrary to industry claims
money_quotes: But here's the thing, it's never all. And we are almost always missing the very things we should care about most.
Those people, busy working two jobs and spending time waiting for buses, become invisible when we tally up the votes without them.
Data doesn't speak for itself, data is just a quantitative, pale echo of the events of our society.
Ignoring causation can be a flaw, rather than a feature. Models that ignore causation can add to historical problems instead of addressing them.
This is a bitch of a problem for people like me who work with models, know exactly how they work, and know exactly how wrong it is to believe that 'data speaks'.
It's a political decision, not a statistical one, to choose a metric of success. And it's the guy in charge who makes that decision, not some quant.
We are being datafied, or rather our actions are, and when we 'like' someone or something online, we are intending to be datafied, or at least we should expect to be.
posts.key_insight: The dangerous myth that big data captures objective truth obscures how data collection systematically excludes marginalized voices while serving corporate interests over public welfare.

posts.summary: York argues that Facebook has become the world's de facto public square, yet it operates under private corporate policies rather than legal rights. She examines how Facebook's policies on issues like anonymity and content moderation shape our privacy norms, noting that Zuckerberg's stance against anonymity serves advertising interests rather than user welfare. The piece draws a parallel to shopping malls as privatized public spaces, but notes that even malls didn't require ID verification. The core tension is that decisions affecting billions of people's privacy are made by corporate executives focused on shareholder returns.
key_points: Facebook's terms of service effectively override legal rights within the platform, creating a parallel governance system
Zuckerberg frames anonymity as a character flaw, but Facebook's real interest is accurate ad targeting
True privacy on Facebook requires anonymity through technical workarounds like separate browsers and fake accounts
Facebook has become the world's public square, making exclusion a significant social cost
Privatized public spaces aren't new (malls), but the scale and surveillance of Facebook represents a qualitative shift
money_quotes: Having two identities for yourself is an example of a lack of integrity
Anonymity is what it takes to do anything privately on Facebook
Facebook is the world's public square

It's weird that decisions like this, that affect our notions of privacy, are being decided by some dude who's probably thinking more about ad revenue than anything else, under pressure from shareholders
Even so, they didn't check ID's at the door
posts.key_insight: When private corporations control our public squares, our fundamental rights become subject to corporate policies optimized for profit rather than human welfare.

posts.summary: In this advice column, Aunt Pythia addresses four reader questions spanning personal style, relationships, hobbies, and career transitions. She reassures an academic that mathematicians are open-minded about physical appearance like nose piercings. She counsels a dumped reader that people move at different paces in relationships and honesty was the right choice. She encourages a would-be knitter to embrace imperfection and tactile pleasure over accounting precision. Finally, she advises an aspiring data scientist without a math background that self-teaching is viable but requires deep understanding of algorithms, not just surface-level tool knowledge.
key_points: Mathematicians tend to be open-minded about physical presentation and won't judge nose piercings in academia
Relationship compatibility often comes down to pace—some people need passion quickly while others prefer slow development
Knitting can be approached with an artist's tolerance for imperfection or a mathematician's clever pattern design, not just accountant-like precision
A formal math background isn't required for data science, but deep understanding of the math behind algorithms is essential
Building a public portfolio on GitHub is crucial for career changers who lack traditional credentials
money_quotes: Mathematicians may be elitist snobs about some things, but it's not about the way they're dressed.
People just move at different paces, and yours was too slow for her.
From her vantage point, she deserves an interesting and exciting love life.
You could instead have the mind of an artist, and not worry about imperfections in stitch count, since they add texture and individuality to your project.
I do think you need to understand the math behind the algorithms in order to really be a good data scientist.
There's no secret property of college learning that makes it somehow better, after all.
Since you don't have a standard background in math and stats and CS, you'll have to have evidence that you really can do this stuff.
posts.key_insight: Whether in academia, relationships, hobbies, or career changes, authenticity and deep understanding matter more than conforming to expected paths or credentials.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares a parenting strategy for getting uncooperative children to comply without resorting to drama, empty threats, or physical coercion. When a reasonable request goes ignored, she offers the child a choice between the preferred option (compliance with a reward) and an easier-for-the-parent option (compliance without the reward). The key is following through consistently so the child knows the offer is serious. She frames this as a practical hack that avoids exhausting negotiations with tired children while maintaining parental authority.
key_points: Children, especially tired ones, resist doing what parents ask, leaving parents feeling helpless
The strategy involves offering a choice between 'what I want' (good outcome for child) and 'what I really want' (easier for parent, worse for child)
Following through on consequences exactly once establishes credibility for future offers
This approach eliminates empty threats, physical coercion, and exhausting philosophical debates about rules
The method reframes compliance as the child's choice rather than a power struggle
money_quotes: Kids don't ever want to do what you want them to do, especially when they're tired, and it's awful to feel helpless to get them to something without ridiculous, possibly empty threats, or something worse.
I form a separate request which is easier for me and less good for them.
I offer him a choice between doing what I want or doing what I really want.
It's important that he knows I'm serious. I will actually not read to him if he doesn't hurry up.
I only had to follow through with this exactly once for him to understand the seriousness of this kind of offer.
What I like about this is the avoidance of drama, empty threats, and physical coercion.
posts.key_insight: Effective parenting leverage comes from offering children a genuine choice between a good option and a worse-but-enforceable option, rather than making threats you won't follow through on.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques modern overparenting culture, arguing that helicopter parenting and excessive child enrichment activities stem from parental anxiety that ultimately undermines children's development. She contends that 'spoiled' children are simply well-adapted to dysfunctional environments their parents created, and that anxious attention focused on achievement damages parent-child relationships. Drawing from her own experience as a latchkey kid with working parents, she argues that privacy and freedom from scrutiny are essential for children to develop independence and self-determination.
key_points: 'Spoiled kids' are not character-flawed but have rationally adapted to environments where whining produces results - the fault lies with parents who created those incentive structures
Anxious, achievement-focused attention (violin practice, test prep, structured activities) damages parent-child relationships the same way logistical negotiations damage marriages
Parental anxiety about children's futures projects onto kids as a lack of faith in their ability to succeed independently, undermining rather than preparing them
Privacy and freedom from scrutiny, as O'Neil experienced as a latchkey kid, allows children space to develop their own identities and make their own decisions
The goal of parenting should be producing 'productive, kind, happy individuals' who can survive on their own, not optimizing for external status markers
money_quotes: In my experience, 'spoiled kids' is just a phrase people use to describe kids that have acclimated perfectly to their imperfect environments.
Because isn't our main job as parents to make sure they can survive on their own as productive, kind, happy individuals?
The anxiety we have for our kids' futures is being projected onto them, and it often translates as a lack of faith in their ability to make it on their own.
I was never under scrutiny. What I did with my time after school was up to me.
I understood scrutiny itself to be the source of insecurity.

It makes me wonder where I'd be now if I hadn't had those afternoons to spend making unlikely friends, taking on jobs cleaning houses for cash, buying books to read, and generally speaking deciding who I was going to grow up to be.
posts.key_insight: Children need freedom from parental scrutiny and anxiety to develop genuine independence, and overparenting disguised as devotion actually undermines the very self-reliance it claims to foster.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces a new NYC Data Skeptics Meetup aimed at critically examining the hype around Big Data and Data Science. The group will explore mathematical, ethical, and business aspects of data with a focus on misuse, best practices, and unintended consequences of modeling. Unlike other data-oriented meetups, this one promises to go into technical depth while avoiding simplistic business takeaways. The post invites people to join, suggest speakers from diverse fields, and help improve the group's branding.
key_points: Big Data hype has reached 'fever pitch' with promises that need critical examination
The meetup will address feedback loops, gaming, politics, and wider consequences of mathematical modeling
The group aims to fill a gap: technical depth without simplistic business sound bites
Speakers sought from diverse fields including law, sociology, and public policy—not just data scientists
The forum is explicitly independent and open to non-technical attendees
money_quotes: The hype surrounding Big Data and Data Science is at a fever pitch with promises to solve the world's business and social problems, large and small.
How accurate or misleading is this message? How is it helping or damaging people, and which people?
Guest speakers will discuss the misuse of and best practices with data, common mistakes people make with data and ways to avoid them.
How to deal with intentional gaming and politics surrounding mathematical modeling.
Taking into account the feedback loops and wider consequences of modeling.
There are not that many currently active groups which aren't afraid to go into the technical weeds and also not obsessed with a simplistic, sound bite business take-away.
posts.key_insight: The data science community needs spaces that combine technical rigor with ethical skepticism, rather than uncritically amplifying Big Data hype.

posts.summary: The post examines e-discovery, the use of machine learning algorithms to sort through millions of documents in lawsuits, replacing expensive human review. The Justice Department has already approved its use in a major antitrust case involving beer companies. O'Neil raises concerns about the proprietary nature of these algorithms, noting they could contain subtle biases or even be trained to favor one side. She questions whether relying on black-box algorithms for legal proceedings serves the public interest or meets constitutional standards.
key_points: E-discovery uses NLP algorithms to determine document relevance in lawsuits, dramatically reducing the cost and time of human review
The Justice Department has already sanctioned e-discovery in real cases, making it an established legal practice
These algorithms are proprietary and closed-source, meaning their accuracy and potential biases cannot be independently verified
There's a risk algorithms could be trained to find documents that are both 'relevant' and favorable to a particular side
Legal outcomes increasingly depend on black-box systems inaccessible to ordinary citizens
money_quotes: With advances in Natural Language Processing (NLP), a machine algorithm can sort emails or documents by topic and can in general do a pretty good job of figuring out whether a given email is 'relevant' to the case.
These algorithms are typically not open source – companies like kCura make good money doing these jobs.
That means that they could be wrong, possibly in subtle ways.
Maybe they've been trained to find documents that are both 'relevant' and 'positive' for a given side.
The laws of this country will increasingly depend on a black box algorithm that is not accessible to the average citizen.
Is that in the public's interest?
Is that even constitutional?
posts.key_insight: When proprietary algorithms determine what evidence surfaces in legal proceedings, justice itself becomes a black box that citizens can neither examine nor challenge.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines how machine learning is being adopted in legal e-discovery (document review in litigation) and argues this is a critical moment to establish proper standards. She warns that allowing private companies to deploy black-box proprietary algorithms will create opacity and potential for manipulation. Instead, she advocates for open-source code and reproducibility as minimum standards, noting this would still support a viable industry while enabling scholarly review and continuous improvement of the tools.
key_points: Private black-box e-discovery tools create dangerous opacity - we can't verify if code was modified to favor one side
Competition for clients will create negative feedback loops where companies use the cheapest tools they can get away with
Open source code saved in repositories would enable documentation of exactly what happened in each case
Reproducibility, rather than full interpretability, is the realistic minimum standard to demand
Reproducible systems enable scholars to test how different code versions might have changed outcomes, improving practices over time
money_quotes: I see a tacit choice being made, probably by judges or court-appointed 'experts', on how machine learning is used in discovery, and I think that the field could get better or worse.

If we allow private companies to build black box machines that we can't peer into, nor keep track of as they change versions, then we'll never know why a given set of documents was deemed 'relevant' in a given case.
We can't, for example, check to see if the code was modified to be more friendly to a given side.

The resulting feedback loop will likely be a negative one, whereby private companies use the cheapest version they can get away with to achieve the best results (for their clients) that they can argue for.
Instead of asking for interpretability, which is a huge ask considering the massive scale of the work being done, we should, at the very least, be able to ask for reproducibility of the e-discovery.
This could create a feedback loop crucial to improve the code itself over time, and to improve best practices for using that code.
posts.key_insight: When machine learning enters legal proceedings, reproducibility and open-source code are the minimum standards needed to prevent algorithmic opacity from undermining justice.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares an article by journalism student Caroline Chen about the status of Mochizuki's ABC Conjecture proof, for which O'Neil was interviewed. While recommending the article, she takes issue with its title 'The Paradox of the Proof' because she dislikes the word 'paradox.' She explains her objection: calling something a paradox often means the speaker is avoiding examining their own assumptions. The post is brief, serving mainly to promote Chen's work while noting personal connections to people featured in the article.
key_points: O'Neil was interviewed about Mochizuki's ABC Conjecture proof status, following her earlier skeptical post on the subject
She recommends Chen's article despite disagreeing with its title
Her critique of the word 'paradox' reveals a methodological principle: paradoxes often indicate unexamined assumptions
The post highlights O'Neil's connections to the mathematical community through friends and her husband who appear in the article
money_quotes: When someone calls something a paradox, it means they are making an assumption that they don't want to examine.
posts.key_insight: What we call a 'paradox' is often just an unexamined assumption in disguise.

posts.summary: This satirical advice column tackles questions about online dating strategy, probabilistic programming's relevance to practical data science, whether to have children, and imposter syndrome in math teaching. Aunt Pythia dispenses irreverent wisdom that often subverts expectations, recommending dating multiple people simultaneously to avoid emotional investment, dismissing probabilistic programming as disconnected from real-world business needs, and suggesting a deliberately unpleasant 'test' for anyone considering parenthood. The column blends genuine insights about algorithms and teaching with absurdist humor.
key_points: In practical data science and business applications, interpretable algorithms that can be explained in words are far more valuable than those incorporating randomness for its own sake
The filter bubble effect in search could potentially be countered by mixing tailored results with random selections
A math teacher who loves the subject but works slowly may actually be more relatable and effective for students than one who speeds through problems with natural brilliance
Probabilistic programming's goal of simulating human intelligence through randomness doesn't align with industry needs for explainable decision-making systems
money_quotes: If anything we want interpretable algorithms, which we can explain in words.
It wouldn't make sense for us to explain what we've implemented and at some point in our explanation say, 'and then we added an element of randomness to the whole thing!'
I think there should be a test you have to take, where it's really hard but it's not graded, and also really expensive, and then the test itself shits all over your shirt.
Don't get me wrong, I freaking LOVE my kids. But I'm basically insane.
One thing that's awesome about having a teacher who both loves math and is slow is that it's incredibly relatable for the students.
Your best bet is to project your love for math to your students, by explaining your thinking at all times, and never forgetting how you thought about stuff when you were just learning it.
posts.key_insight: In real-world data science applications, the goal is interpretable algorithms that can be explained to stakeholders, not simulated creativity through randomness.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines public shaming through three analytical dimensions: usefulness, appropriateness, and problem-solving effectiveness. She argues that society misapplies shame, directing it at vulnerable groups like fat people, the poor, and children rather than at powerful wrongdoers. Using examples from anti-obesity campaigns to the Bangladesh factory collapse, she distinguishes between shame that merely assigns blame versus shame that creates accountability. Her central insight is that shame functions not to change individual behavior—since those who deserve it are often shameless—but to establish societal norms about acceptable conduct.
key_points: Shaming can be 'useful' to the shamer by assigning sole responsibility to others, even when it doesn't solve the underlying problem
The lack of empathy that characterizes shaming makes it both easy to identify and extremely harmful
Public health campaigns targeting obesity and poverty shame victims for systemic problems rather than addressing root causes
Shame is only appropriate when crimes have been committed that affect others needlessly, and when accountability might prevent future harm
Those who most deserve shame are typically the least susceptible to feeling it—they are shameless
money_quotes: As a fat person, I've dealt with a lot of public shaming in my life. I've gotten so used to it, I'm more an observer than a victim most of the time.
The lack of empathy which characterizes shaming behavior makes it very easy to spot. And extremely nasty.
These campaigns are not going to solve any problems, but they do seem politically useful – a way of doubling down on the people suffering from problems in our society.
Not only will they suffer from them, but they will also be blamed for them.
One thing about shame is that the people who actually deserve shame are not particularly susceptible to feeling it.
Shame is really more about setting the rules of society straight: notifying people in general about what's acceptable and what's not.
From my perspective, we've shown ourselves much more willing to shame poor people, fat people, and our own children than to shame the actual villains who walk among us who deserve such treatment.
Shame on us.
posts.key_insight: Society weaponizes shame against vulnerable populations while letting powerful wrongdoers escape accountability, revealing that shame serves political convenience rather than justice.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques a new Michelin workplace policy that penalizes employees financially for not meeting health metrics like waist size, blood pressure, and cholesterol levels. She argues this approach exemplifies shaming tactics that lack empathy and unfairly blame individuals for complex societal health issues. The post warns that such policies could lead to discriminatory hiring practices against overweight people. O'Neil connects these workplace wellness programs to broader concerns about unregulated health data being used by employers, drawing parallels to how credit scores already discriminate against poor job applicants.
key_points: Michelin's wellness policy requires employees to meet specific health metrics (blood pressure, glucose, cholesterol, waist size) to receive deductible credits, shifting from voluntary participation to mandatory compliance
The policy exhibits classic shaming characteristics: lack of empathy and placing blame for a complex cultural phenomenon entirely on individuals
Such policies create a slippery slope toward employment discrimination against overweight people during hiring
Unregulated health data collected by companies like Acxiom could be weaponized in hiring decisions, circumventing medical privacy laws
This practice mirrors existing discrimination where credit scores are used to screen out poor job applicants
money_quotes: This policy combines the critical characteristics of shaming, namely 1) a complete lack of empathy and 2) the shifting of blame for a problem entirely onto one segment of the population even though the 'obesity epidemic' is a poorly understood cultural phenomenon.
To the extent that there may be push-back against this or similar policies inside the workplace, there will be very little to stop employers from not hiring fat people in the first place.
What's going to stop employers from using people's full medical profiles against them in the hiring process?
We do that already to poor people by basing their acceptance on credit scores.
posts.key_insight: Workplace wellness programs that financially penalize employees for health metrics aren't about encouraging health—they're shaming tactics that blame individuals for systemic problems and open the door to discriminatory hiring based on unregulated health data.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques the broken credit rating agency system and its role in the 2008 financial crisis, advocating for open-source alternatives. She highlights Marc Joffe's open-source credit rating initiative and his upcoming participation in an SEC roundtable examining industry reforms. The post exposes how the SEC's NRSRO certification process effectively protects the 'big three' agencies' monopoly by requiring impossible-to-obtain client letters. Despite the Dodd-Frank Act mandating reform and Senator Franken's amendment requiring the SEC to address conflicts of interest, meaningful change has stalled. O'Neil expresses frustration that such an obviously broken system remains protected by the very regulators meant to oversee it.
key_points: Credit rating agencies' issuer-pay model creates inherent conflicts of interest that contributed to the 2008 financial crisis
The SEC's NRSRO certification requirements effectively protect the big three agencies' monopoly by demanding years of client history new entrants can't provide
Open-source credit rating models could provide transparency that both parties should support, as it's not a partisan issue
The Dodd-Frank Act's Section 939A requires replacing NRSRO ratings with alternative creditworthiness standards, opening a door for open-source solutions
Despite clear evidence of a broken system and legal requirements for reform, regulatory change has stalled due to lack of consensus
money_quotes: The system of credit ratings undermines the trust of even the most fervently pro-business entrepreneur out there.
The models are knowingly gamed by both sides, and it's clearly both corrupt and important.
There's no reason I can see why there shouldn't be broad support for a rule to force the ratings agencies to make their models publicly available.
This isn't a political game that would score points for one side or the other.

They don't grant 'NRSRO' status to credit rating agencies unless the given agency can produce something like 10 letters from clients who will vouch for them providing credit ratings for at least 3 years.
I'm just kind of amazed that, given how dirty and obviously broken this industry is, we can't do better than this.
SEC, please start doing your job.
How could allowing an open-source credit rating agency hurt our country? How could it make things worse?
posts.key_insight: The credit rating industry's obvious corruption and the SEC's protection of incumbent monopolies demonstrate how regulatory capture can persist even after a crisis exposes systemic failures.

posts.summary: A new meta-analysis found that limiting salt intake doesn't improve health outcomes and may actually increase mortality, contradicting decades of public health advice. The author explains this reversal happened because epidemiologists linked two separate correlations (high salt to high blood pressure, and high blood pressure to death) without proper causation studies. The actual blood pressure reduction from low-salt diets is minimal—about 1 mmHg. O'Neil expresses skepticism toward epidemiological modeling in general, notes that individual responses to salt vary widely, and argues that the real cost of inadequate studies is erosion of public trust in medical research.
key_points: Epidemiologists made flawed predictions by chaining two correlations together without establishing direct causation between salt intake and mortality
The actual blood pressure reduction from low-salt diets is trivially small (120/80 to 119/79)
Individual responses to salt intake vary—some people's blood pressure goes up, some goes down
The author sees this as part of a pattern of epidemiological overreach and expects more such reversals
The true cost of cheap, inadequate studies is the erosion of public trust in medical research
money_quotes: Epidemiologists use crazy models to make predictions on things, and in this case what happened was they saw a correlation between high blood pressure and high salt intake, and they saw a separate correlation between high blood pressure and death, and so they linked the two.
While very low salt intake might lower blood pressure a little bit, it also for what ever reason makes people die a wee bit more often.
That is like going from 120/80 to 119/79.
I'm a skeptic, especially when it comes to epidemiology.
None of this surprises me, and I don't think it's the last bombshell we'll be hearing.

They keep saying that it's too expensive to do this kind of study right, but I'm thinking that by now they might realize the real cost of not doing it right is a loss of the public's trust in medical research.
posts.key_insight: Chaining correlations together without proper causation studies leads to flawed public health advice, and the real cost of cutting corners on research is the erosion of public trust.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that Star Trek serves as a secular moral framework for her family, functioning like a religion through its ethical teachings. The show's utopian vision—a society without money or too-big-to-fail banks—matters less than its focus on collaborative ethics and moral responsibility around technology. She and her sons play a game identifying progressive messages in each episode, from gay marriage to resource sharing. While acknowledging the show offers no roadmap from our current society to utopia, she embraces it as an aspirational exercise, arguing that having unreachable goals still has value.
key_points: Star Trek treats technology as morally neutral—capable of good or evil depending on human choices, with responsibility clearly assigned rather than obfuscated
The show functions as a 'nerd bible' posing fundamental ethical questions: Can machines have souls? When should advanced civilizations share their knowledge?
O'Neil uses Star Trek as a teaching tool with her children, playing 'spot the progressive message' to identify themes like confronting sexism or overcoming hoarding instincts
The lack of a practical roadmap to utopia is acceptable because aspirational goals have value even when we can't imagine reaching them
Star Trek's ethics are collaborative and treated as more fundamental than technological power
money_quotes: It's that they have ethics, and those ethics are collaborative, and moreover are more basic and more important than the power of technology.
In Star Trek, technology is not an unalloyed good: it's morally neutral, and it could do evil or good, depending on the context.
This responsibility is not lost in some obfuscated surreality.
Each episode can be seen as a story from a nerd bible.
Some kids go to church, my kids watch Star Trek with me.
Not everything has to be pragmatic. And it probably helps to have goals that we can't quite imagine reaching.
How can we expect alien races to collaborate with us when we can't even get along with people who disagree about taxation and the purpose of government?
posts.key_insight: Star Trek's enduring value lies not in its technological imagination but in treating ethics as primary and technology as a morally neutral tool whose impact depends entirely on human choices.

posts.summary: The Dow Jones reaching an all-time high no longer signals broad economic health or prosperity for ordinary Americans. While investors and those with substantial 401Ks benefit, most people either lack retirement savings or have been forced to raid them during hard times. The market's rise is primarily driven by easy Fed money flowing to the already wealthy and foreign investors fleeing European instability, not genuine economic strength. This creates a perverse situation where good economic news could actually trigger market declines.
key_points: The 'rising tide lifts all boats' relationship between stock markets and general prosperity has broken down
401K participation and balances are low, with one in four Americans forced to dip into retirement savings despite penalty fees
The Dow's rise is fueled by Fed monetary policy benefiting the wealthy and foreign capital flight from Europe, not economic fundamentals
A healthier economy could paradoxically trigger market declines if it leads to higher interest rates or European stability
The 401K industry itself operates as a 'scammy' system extracting fees from ordinary savers
money_quotes: Once upon a time it might have meant something good, in a kind of 'rising tide lifts all boats' sort of way. Nowadays not so much.
The Dow is so high not because the economy is great, or even because it is projected to be great soon.
It's mostly inflated out of a combination of easy Fed money for banks, which translates to easy money for people who are already rich.
We're talking about a possible negative stock market response to a positive economic outlook.
The stock market has officially become decoupled from our nation's future.
posts.key_insight: The stock market has become a measure of how well the wealthy are doing, not a barometer of national economic health or shared prosperity.

posts.summary: In this advice column, Aunt Pythia addresses two questions: one from an academic struggling to complete papers they've lost interest in, and another from a data scientist frustrated by their banking employer's limited vision. For the academic, she recommends creating a spreadsheet system to prioritize projects based on weighted attributes like time to completion, career value, and personal interest. For the data scientist, she offers a cynical but pragmatic assessment that the company likely hired them for marketing purposes rather than genuine data science ambitions, and advises preparing an ultimatum document outlining needed resources.
key_points: Graduate school teaches deep focus on one project but fails to prepare academics for managing multiple competing projects in their careers
A spreadsheet-based prioritization system with weighted attributes can help triage projects into 'do now,' 'hope to get to,' and 'will never finish'
The 'how much you actually feel like working on it' metric is distinct from and often more important than abstract value calculations
Most companies hire data scientists for marketing spin (90%) rather than genuine data science capabilities (1%)
When stuck in a role without proper resources, document what you'd need and be prepared to leave if the company won't provide it
money_quotes: One thing they never teach you in grad school is how to manage projects, mostly because you only have one project in grad school, which is to learn everything the first two years then do something magical and new the second two years.
You need a process, my friend! You need a way of managing your options.
Be sure to include a column that measures how much you actually feel like working on it, which is distinct from how much you'll like it when it's done.
You might not have a perfect system, but you'll be able to triage into 'put onto my calendar now', 'hope to get to', and 'I'll never finish this, and now I know why'.

My guess is they hired you either so they could say they had a data scientist, so pure marketing spin, which is 90% likely, or because they really plan on getting a whole team to do data science right, which I put at 1%.
There's no way you can do your job right now short of becoming a data engineer yourself and starting to hit the unstructured data with mapreduce jobs.
posts.key_insight: Both in academia and industry, success requires honest self-assessment systems that acknowledge the gap between what we should want and what we actually want to work on.

posts.summary: O'Neil questions whether rising home prices in certain cities represent a true housing bubble or simply reflect income inequality and wealth concentration. She argues that the examples cited in bubble-warning articles are cherry-picked from cities where super-rich people congregate, like New York and Menlo Park. The post distinguishes between luxury markets driven by wealthy domestic and international buyers and other markets where private equity firms are buying properties for rentals. O'Neil suggests we need better metrics than average sell prices to understand a bifurcated housing market, and remains unconvinced that a broad bubble exists rather than predictable consequences of wealth concentration.
key_points: Housing price increases in elite cities may reflect wealth concentration rather than a speculative bubble
International wealth (like Russian buyers) is distorting luxury real estate markets in cities like New York
Private equity firms buying rental properties in 'unsexier' cities like Phoenix represent a different market dynamic with natural limits tied to renters' incomes
Average sell price is an inadequate metric for understanding a bifurcated housing market
The real signal in housing data may be that very rich people all want to live in the same places
money_quotes: I can't help but wonder how much of the signal they are seeing is explained by income inequality, combined with the increasing segregation of rich people in certain cities.
The fatness of this real estate tail is extraordinary, and it makes me think that when we talk about real estate recoveries we should have different metrics than simply 'average sell price'.
We need to adjust our metrics to reflect the nature of the bifurcated market.
Judging from articles like this one in the New York Times, we're now building luxury sky-scrapers just to attract rich Russians.
Their end-feeder is the renter, the average person who has a very limited income and ability to pay, unlike the Russians.
I'm just saying articles like the Bloomberg one don't convince me of anything besides the fact that very rich people all want to live in the same place.
posts.key_insight: What looks like a housing bubble may actually be the predictable geographic concentration of wealth inequality, requiring us to analyze bifurcated markets with better metrics than simple averages.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on the loss of local, community-based systems after reading about Ireland's ad hoc currency system during a financial crisis. She notes that 'living locally'—where neighbors know each other well enough to extend personal credit—is largely absent in modern urban life like New York. She connects this to broader societal trade-offs: we've gained cosmopolitan freedom but lost informal support systems for things like childcare and home cooking. She's skeptical of proposed government solutions, noting they ignore power dynamics, while acknowledging that traditional family arrangements weren't ideal either.
key_points: Ireland's 1970s banking crisis was navigated through local trust networks where neighbors could personally vouch for each other's credit
Modern urban life has eroded these local connections—even longtime residents may not know their grocery store workers
Policy proposals often ignore power dynamics and who actually benefits from the status quo
Traditional arrangements like multi-generational households solved childcare and cooking but came with their own constraints
We've traded localness for cosmopolitan freedom, gaining some benefits while losing community-based safety nets
money_quotes: It occurs to me that 'living locally' is exactly what most people, at least in New York, don't do at all.
I'm shit out of luck for food, in other words, in the case of a currency freeze.
People sometimes get confused between what could or should happen and what might actually happen, mostly because they don't think about power and who has it and what their best interests are.
In some sense we traditionally had pretty good solutions to child care and home cooking, namely we lived together with our families and not everyone had a job.
We've traded away localness for something else, which I'm personally benefitting from, but there are other direct cultural effects which aren't always so awesome.
Our dependency on international banking and credit scores and having very little time to cook for our kids are a few examples.
posts.key_insight: Modern cosmopolitan life has traded away local trust networks and informal community support systems, leaving us dependent on impersonal institutions like international banking and credit scores.

posts.summary: This guest post by Adam Obeng recounts Eben Moglen's talk about the ethical implications of data science and the networked surveillance society. Moglen argues that humanity is becoming a superorganism connected through digital networks, and that data science will transform our understanding of human behavior while simultaneously threatening privacy and freedom. He contends that privacy is not an individual concern but an ecological one, making consent-based frameworks inadequate. The post concludes with Obeng's skepticism about whether more data alone will make human behavior predictable, while acknowledging the importance of Moglen's ethical questions.
key_points: Data science represents the study of humanity's emerging 'nervous system' - a network connecting all human consciousness that will revolutionize social science prediction
Privacy must be understood ecologically rather than individually; individual consent is irrelevant when the system affects everyone, like poisonous drinking water
Surveillance technology will inevitably be used to extend political authority and regiment human behavior - if it can be engineered, it will be done somewhere
The questions data scientists pursue are determined by commercial interests (advertising), not societal benefit - we won't study whether punishment deters crime because no ad sale depends on it
Having more data doesn't guarantee predictive power - we've catalogued billions of stars yet still struggle with the three-body problem
money_quotes: What we refer to as data science is the nascent study of flows of neural data in that network.
Whatever is possible to engineer will eventually be done.
Privacy is not personal, or even bilateral, it is ecological: how others behave determine the meaning of the actions I take.

It is irrelevant whether you consent to be delivered poisonous drinking water, we don't regulate such resources by allowing individuals to make decisions about how unsafe they can afford their drinking water to be.
Whatever finished product you are used to make, you do not disappear from it.
Consider the question of whether punishment deters crime: there will be increasing amounts of data about it, but we're not even going to ask – because no advertising sale depends on it.
Evil is ethics undealt with.
We've changed everything except the way people think.
posts.key_insight: Privacy in the age of data science must be treated as an ecological concern requiring collective regulation, not an individual choice to be negotiated through consent frameworks.

posts.summary: Marc Joffe recounts his experience participating in the SEC's Credit Ratings Roundtable and explains why the Franken Amendment—which would have created a ratings agency assignment authority—is effectively dead. He attributes its failure to progressives' unwillingness to engage with the regulatory comment process, while industry insiders flooded it with status quo-preserving letters. Joffe then describes his own journey promoting open source credit rating models, tracing the obscure concept through various advocates including mathbabe. He argues that financial reformers must work within the incrementalist nature of regulatory agencies rather than expecting revolutionary change, and positions himself as a 'policy entrepreneur' who achieved modest success by persistently engaging with the system.
key_points: The Franken Amendment failed because reform proponents didn't submit thoughtful comment letters while industry insiders dominated the process
SEC policymaking follows incrementalism—marginal changes to existing concepts rather than revolutionary reforms
Open source credit rating models represent an alternative approach to credit rating reform that works within existing regulatory frameworks
Successful financial reform requires 'policy entrepreneurship'—persistently seeking opportunities to inject ideas into policy discussions
Credibility and institutional affiliation matter enormously in getting reform ideas heard, but persistence can partially overcome these barriers
money_quotes: While I can understand why Progressives think the SEC should 'get off its ass' and really fix the financial industry, their critique is not based in the real world.
When a request for comment arises they need to stuff the ballot box with varying and well informed letters supporting reform.
Regulators are also heavily influenced by the folks they regulate, since these are the people they talk to on a day-to-day basis.

I am not a proven thought leader, am not affiliated with a major research university and lack a resume that includes any position of high repute or authority. Consequently, I am only a half-step removed from the many 'crackpots' that send around their unsolicited ideas to all and sundry.
The SEC is what it is. It will remain under budget pressure for the foreseeable future because campaign donors want to restrict its activities.

If 20 Progressive academics and Occupy-leaning financial industry veterans had submitted thoughtful, reality-based letters advocating the Franken Amendment, I believe the outcome would have been very different.
posts.key_insight: Financial reform fails not because regulators are corrupt but because reformers don't engage with the incremental, comment-driven process that actually shapes policy, while industry insiders do.

posts.summary: O'Neil expresses outrage at Verizon selling customer location and browsing data despite customers already paying substantial monthly fees. She distinguishes this from free services like Google and Facebook where data collection is part of an implicit bargain. She recommends fighting back through opting out and using tools like TrackMeNot, a browser extension that confuses data gatherers by generating fake browsing activity. She calls for someone to create a mobile version of this tool.
key_points: There's a meaningful ethical distinction between free services collecting data (an implicit trade) and paid services selling customer data (double-dipping)
Marketers will use location data to price discriminate based on customer desperation
TrackMeNot fights surveillance by flooding data collectors with noise and false information
Individual action options include opting out, advocating publicly, and using obfuscation tools
money_quotes: It's one thing to make a deal with the devil when I use Facebook: you give me something free, in return I let you glean information about me.
But in terms of Verizon, I pay them like $200 per month for my family's phone usage. That's not free! Fuck you guys for turning around and selling my data!
Instead of merely offering customers a trusted conduit for communication, carriers are coming to see subscribers as sources of data that can be mined for profit.
They will know how desperate you are for their goods and charge you accordingly.
Trackmenot confuses the data gatherers by giving them an overload of bullshit information.
The data gatherers get endlessly confused about what kind of person you actually are this way, thereby fucking up the whole personal data information industry.
posts.key_insight: When you're already paying for a service, the company selling your data represents a betrayal of the customer relationship that goes beyond the implicit bargain of free services.

posts.summary: Yitang Zhang has proven that there are infinitely many pairs of prime numbers differing by at most 70 million, a major breakthrough in number theory. Unlike Mochizuki's contested ABC Conjecture proof, Zhang's work has been quickly verified by the mathematical community. The post links to Jordan Ellenberg's Slate article explaining why mathematicians expected this result based on primes behaving like randomly distributed numbers. The probabilistic heuristic suggests twin primes (differing by 2) should be infinite, and Zhang's result is a significant step toward proving that conjecture.
key_points: Zhang proved infinitely many prime pairs exist within 70 million of each other, a bounded gaps theorem
The proof has been verified by the mathematical community, unlike Mochizuki's ABC Conjecture claim
Prime numbers appear to behave as if randomly distributed, which probabilistically predicts both Zhang's result and the twin primes conjecture
The heuristic calculation shows we'd expect about N/(log N)^2 twin primes among the first N numbers
money_quotes: Unlike Mochizuki's claim of a proof of the ABC Conjecture, this has already been understood and confirmed by the mathematical community.
It's not hard to compute that, if prime numbers behaved like random numbers, you'd see precisely the behavior that Zhang demonstrated.
You'd expect to see infinitely many pairs of primes that are separated by only 2, as the twin primes conjecture claims.
Each number n would have a 1/log N chance of being prime. The chance that n and n+2 are *both* prime should thus be about (1/log N)^2.
posts.key_insight: Zhang's verified proof that infinitely many prime pairs exist within a bounded gap represents a major advance toward the twin primes conjecture, supported by probabilistic reasoning about the random-like distribution of primes.

posts.summary: This is an advice column post where Cathy O'Neil answers reader questions on eclectic topics ranging from domestic disputes about bathroom habits to the serious challenge of managing data science projects. The substantive content focuses on her response about project management for data teams, where she argues that while methodologies like Scrum don't perfectly fit data work due to its unpredictable nature, they're still worth using. She recommends setting time-bounded goals for exploratory phases, notes that managers appreciate the visibility these tools provide, and suggests using project tracking as leverage when teams are overloaded. The post showcases her characteristic blend of humor with practical professional advice.
key_points: Data science projects differ fundamentally from engineering projects because they take 'whimsical turns that can't be drawn up beforehand'
Time-boxing exploratory data analysis (e.g., 'done by end of day two') forces more thoughtful, targeted work rather than endless exploration
Project management tools provide value even when time estimates are inaccurate because 'there's more information in those estimates than in nothing'
Tracking tools can be used defensively to push back on overload by showing managers which projects won't get done if new work is added
After using Scrum for a while, you can do 'data analysis on Scrum' itself to estimate time fudge factors for unforeseen data issues
money_quotes: Data projects aren't the same kettle of fish as engineering projects. By their very nature they take whimsical turns that can't be drawn up beforehand.
Being inaccurate doesn't mean they're meaningless – there's more information in those estimates than in nothing.
If your data team is being overloaded with work, you can use the tool to push back.
You can point to all the other projects you already have and say, 'these are all the projects that won't be getting done if I take this one on.' Make the tool work for you!
After a few projects you can start doing a data analysis on Scrum, estimating how much of a time fudge factor you should add to each estimate.
We knitters do it because we love doing it.
posts.key_insight: Data science project management tools are worth using not for accurate time estimates, but for forcing disciplined exploration, satisfying stakeholders' need for visibility, and providing leverage to push back on excessive workloads.

posts.summary: This guest post by Eugene Stern warns that New Jersey is about to implement a teacher evaluation system based on Student Growth Percentiles (SGP) without any validation of its effectiveness. The model, promoted by Christie appointee Chris Cerf, simply compares students' year-to-year test scores without controlling for factors like income, tutoring, or class environment. Ironically, SGP's own creators explicitly state it shouldn't be used to make causal claims about teacher quality, yet New Jersey plans to make it 30% of teacher evaluations affecting tenure decisions. The post exposes the political shell game where SGP is marketed as 'not VAM' to avoid that methodology's tarnished reputation, then quietly deployed for the exact same purpose. Stern calls for a manifesto from the modeling community to stop this implementation before it damages teachers' careers based on essentially random data.
key_points: NYC's VAM ratings showed almost no correlation in teacher ratings from year to year, or even between grades taught in the same year, suggesting the data is essentially noise
SGP deliberately ignores all student factors except test scores, making no distinction between a wealthy suburban student and one from the projects with identical scores
The creators of SGP explicitly state their methodology is 'not sufficient to make causal claims about school or teacher quality' yet New Jersey is using it for exactly that
The political strategy rebrands VAM as SGP, claims it's just measuring student progress not evaluating teachers, then uses it to evaluate teachers anyway
This data-driven teacher evaluation framework is being implemented without any data-driven evaluation of whether it actually works
money_quotes: Wanting to make 'data-driven decisions,' many cities and states decided to start rating teachers based on 'data' before collecting any data to validate whether that 'data' was any good.

This is a bit like building a theoretical model of how cancer cells behave, synthesizing a cancer drug in the lab based on the model, distributing that drug widely without any trials, then waiting around to see how many people die from the side effects.
The people preaching data don't seem willing to actually use data to evaluate the quality of the systems they're putting in place.
You'd think that a teacher doesn't go from awful to great or vice versa in one year, but the NYC VAM ratings show next to no correlation in a teacher's rating from one year to the next.

Because these models aren't built to rate teachers, they can focus on the things that really matter (student progress), and thus end up being – wait for it – much better for rating teachers! War is peace, friends. Ignorance is strength.
This data-driven framework for teacher evaluation is being put in place without any data-driven evaluation of its effectiveness. And that this is a feature, not a bug.
Shame on us if we can't make this right.
posts.key_insight: The irony of 'data-driven' education policy is that proponents refuse to use data to validate whether their own models actually work before deploying them to make career-ending decisions about teachers.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil enthusiastically endorses New York's new Citi Bike share program despite her misgivings about its corporate sponsor Citigroup. She shares her early experiences using the bikes multiple times in a single rainy day, highlighting how the station near her workplace makes the system incredibly convenient. The post captures the excitement of an early adopter who has already integrated bike sharing into her daily routine, prioritizing efficiency over comfort even in bad weather.
key_points: The $95 annual membership offers immediate value for frequent urban travelers
Station density and proximity to work/destinations is key to the system's usefulness
Practical efficiency can outweigh discomfort (biking in rain)
Corporate sponsorship creates cognitive dissonance but doesn't override utility
money_quotes: In spite of the nasty corporate connection to megabank Citigroup, I'm a huge of the new bike share program in downtown Manhattan and Brooklyn.
I already used it three times yesterday even though it was raining the whole time.
Then later in the day I was meeting someone at Bryant Park so I biked up there, getting ridiculously wet but being *super efficient*.
Now you know where my priorities are.
Sign up today, people!
posts.key_insight: Sometimes practical urban infrastructure wins out over ideological objections to corporate sponsors.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her participation in two panels at the Left Forum conference at Pace University. The first panel is a debate with Doug Henwood about shrinking the financial sector, moderated by economist Suresh Naidu. The second panel is a public meeting of the Occupy Wall Street Alternative Banking group, focused on breaking up megabanks and demonstrating how the group operates. She encourages readers to register and attend.
key_points: O'Neil will debate strategies for shrinking the financial sector with Wall Street author Doug Henwood
The Alternative Banking group meeting will demonstrate their participatory discussion format and get audience members 'on stack'
The panels focus on breaking up megabanks as a concrete policy goal
The Left Forum brings together progressive thinkers including Noam Chomsky
money_quotes: I'll be debating how to shrink the financial sector with Doug Henwood
The idea is to explain to the panel audience how we roll in Alt Banking
We expect them to enjoy getting on stack
posts.key_insight: Progressive organizing around financial reform involves both intellectual debate about policy and participatory grassroots meetings that model democratic engagement.

posts.summary: O'Neil reports on a congressional hearing about big data that devolved into uncritical cheerleading rather than substantive policy discussion. The congresspeople, informed mainly by a McKinsey report, were awed by the panelists and focused solely on how to nurture big data rather than regulate it. The panelists—from NSF, academia, and IBM—offered self-serving testimony while dismissing concerns about skepticism and privacy. O'Neil connects this to broader concerns about technocratic power, citing a paper arguing that 'good' policies often reinforce existing power structures. She suggests big data experts may be replacing economists as the trusted technocrats whose advice goes unchallenged.
key_points: Congressional representatives approached big data with uncritical awe, asking how to nurture it rather than whether to regulate it
Panelists were self-interested: the NSF rep said nothing, the academic pushed for funding, and IBM's head offered misleading pro-data talking points
When one congressman asked about teaching students skepticism about data, the academic repeatedly dodged the question
IBM's entire position on privacy was that 'people are happy to give away their private information for the services they get in return'
A cited academic paper warns that policies strengthening already-powerful groups deserve 'double caution' because elites cannot be trusted when totally in charge of policy
money_quotes: The Congresspeople were impressed, even awed, at the intelligence and power of the panelists. They were basically asking for advice on how to let big data happen on a bigger and better scale.
Regulation never came up, it was all about, 'how do we nurture this movement that is vital to our country's health and future?'

The congressman was like, no that's not what I asked, I asked whether they can be skeptical of perceived signals versus noise, whether they can avoid making huge costly mistakes with big data. The guy was like, I teach my students to deal with big data.
He summarized his, and presumably everyone else's position on this subject, by saying 'people are happy to give away their private information for the services they get in return.' Thanks, IBM guy!
One more priceless moment was when one of the Congressmen asked the panel if industry has enough interaction with policy makers. The head of IBM Research said, 'Why yes, we do!'

You should apply double caution when it comes to policies which will strengthen already powerful groups. The central starting point is a certain suspicion of elites. You really cannot trust the elites when they are totally in charge of policy.
posts.key_insight: Congressional oversight of big data has been captured by the very technocrats it should be scrutinizing, turning what should be critical policy evaluation into a rubber-stamping exercise for industry interests.

posts.summary: In this advice column, Aunt Pythia (Cathy O'Neil's alter ego) answers reader questions ranging from porn timing statistics to kink etiquette. She critiques how mainstream porn ignores female pleasure, comments on Harvard students' sex lives based on survey data, and provides practical advice for someone navigating vanilla dating while exploring BDSM. The column blends humor with sharp observations about gender dynamics and statistics, while encouraging readers to submit more sex-related questions.
key_points: Mainstream porn systematically excludes authentic female orgasms, which Aunt Pythia calls 'very messed up'
Harvard survey data showing 72% enter as virgins and 27% graduate without sex is statistically consistent, not contradictory
When discussing personal kinks with partners, confidence in presentation shapes their reaction more than the content itself
The column playfully critiques fake questions while advocating for genuine engagement with taboo topics
Statistics about sexual behavior often contain hidden assumptions worth questioning
money_quotes: I have never once in my entire porn-watching adult life seen a real woman have a real orgasm. WTF?
Now that I know you're talking about men orgasming, I have the following response: who cares?
Those Ivy League students could really do with some more sex. It might relax them a bit – too stressed out by far.
Don't act ashamed of your kinks, because his reaction to it will be very dependent on how you present it.
Talk about it like it's a secret Star Trek series that nobody's ever heard about but which is now on Netflix.
Fake questions deserve fake answers.
posts.key_insight: Confidence and framing shape how others receive personal information, whether discussing kinks or interpreting statistics.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques the weekend hackathon culture while acknowledging some positive experiences with DataKind events. She identifies three main problems: hackathons as competitive boasting, working for free for future VCs, and solving problems only for privileged participants. She argues that meaningful hackathon results require extensive planning, real goals, and serious follow-up. The post amplifies satirical commentary about the disconnect between hackathons' self-congratulatory culture and their actual societal impact.
key_points: Hackathons often devolve into competitions about boasting rather than genuinely solving problems
Without subject matter experts and advance planning, hackathon solutions tend to address participants' privileged problems rather than broader societal needs
The tech industry applies a 'moral or altruistic gloss' to what are essentially moneymaking ventures
At best, hackathons are launching platforms for sustained work, not solutions in themselves
Government-sponsored hackathons create uncomfortable contradictions given actual policies toward hackers and information access
money_quotes: you're essentially working for free for a future VC asshole
having data and good intentions do not mean you can get serious results over a weekend
as a young affluent hacker, my problem isn't improving the city's recycling programs, it's finding kale on Saturdays
Actually a weekend hackathon is, at best, a platform from which to launch something more serious and sustained
Basically, a bunch of pallid millenials cram in a room and do computer junk. Harmless, but very exciting to the people who make money off the results.
