Pythia distinguishes between 'tough letter writers' (consistently high standards) and 'narcissistic letter writers' (inconsistently harsh based on personal whims), noting the latter do more damage because they're harder to detect. She advises the professor to counsel future students away from problematic letter writers, but acknowledges the ethical bind of having already read the letter.
key_points: The academic recommendation letter system has adapted to 'tough letter writers' who are consistently harsh, but struggles with 'narcissistic letter writers' whose praise varies based on personal favoritism
Reading a collaborator's application packet creates conflicts of interest that make ethical intervention difficult
Explicitly rating letter writers publicly could backfire by incentivizing everyone to only seek glowing letters, reducing the information value of the entire system
Young academics should explicitly ask letter writers if their letters will be 'good' and define what good means
Being singularly focused on math and academic success is fine, but one shouldn't expect everyone else to share that narrow focus
money_quotes: Don't feel bad about it! It's just how you're programmed, it's fine. You love math and not much else! Shout it loud from the rooftops and you might just find a girl nerd who's psyched with your boring self.

There are different kinds of shitty letter writers: the tough letter writer, who has consistently high standards and doesn't wax poetic about anyone ever, and the narcissistic letter writer, who is inconsistent with their praise, sometimes cold sometimes hot, depending on idiosyncratic things.
It's much much harder for people on hiring committees to spot the narcissists, and thus those narcissists probably do lots of damage.

If you had a public yet anonymous webpage which scored every letter writer on a scale of toughness, then the young people looking for jobs might feel like to compete, they'd need to only get letters from people who always write good letters, and then the entire system would fail because the letters would contain less and less information.

Choose a (possibly imaginary) friend of yours each day to write to – your audience – that is on your side but will also ask clarifying questions, and explain something to them that you find interesting. That's a blog post!
posts.key_insight: Academic systems adapt to consistent biases but struggle with narcissistic bad actors whose harm is harder to detect and correct without breaking the system itself.

posts.summary: The post examines how Detroit's bankruptcy was significantly caused by pension funds using misleading mathematical models that allowed cities to underfund their obligations. Actuaries use projection methods that assume 7-8% investment returns and 'smooth' market volatility, creating systematic bias that makes unsustainable plans appear healthy. O'Neil frames this as 'mathematical control fraud' where quantitative experts are hired to make irresponsible financial decisions seem acceptable. She notes the actuarial profession became concerned about reputational damage, but their internal investigation was oddly replaced by a panel of politicians and policy figures.
key_points: Cities use optimistic return assumptions (7-8%) for pension investments despite typically investing in lower-yield bonds, systematically underfunding obligations
Actuarial 'smoothing' methods hide market volatility but also obscure the true health of pension plans
The Society of Actuaries recognized public pensions as their largest reputational risk, suggesting the profession knows these models are problematic
An internal actuarial investigation into the problem was mysteriously replaced by a panel of politicians and policy figures
money_quotes: We don't need better models but we need to stop lying with our models
It all comes down to wanting to avoid putting required money away and hiring quants to make that seem like it's mathematically acceptable
It's a form of mathematical control fraud
Even in places where they don't end up declaring bankruptcy, something is going to shake out because of these broken models, and it isn't going to be extra money for retired civil servants
Public pension plans had come to pose the single largest reputational risk to the profession

It seems like a bunch of people in a profession, the actuaries, got worried that they were being used by politicians, and decided to investigate, but then that initiative got somehow replaced by a bunch of politicians
posts.key_insight: Mathematical models aren't neutral tools—they can be deliberately designed to justify irresponsible decisions, making 'math fraud' a vehicle for institutions to defer costs onto future generations.

posts.summary: O'Neil challenges the framing of pension critiques as right-wing attacks, arguing that progressives must engage with fiscal realities rather than cede that territory. She draws parallels between Detroit's potential bankruptcy and how airlines used bankruptcy to shed pension obligations, predicting a similar pattern for cities. The core argument is about unequal application of law: banks received bailouts despite causing their own crises, while ordinary workers and retirees face broken promises. She questions why legal contracts are sacred for some parties but disposable for others.
key_points: Refusing to discuss pension math realities cedes the conversation to those who will act on it
Detroit's bankruptcy may establish a legal template for cities to void pension obligations, similar to how airlines used bankruptcy
The law is applied unequally: banks get bailouts and bonuses while workers get broken contracts
The original bank bailouts violated the principle that failures should fail and criminals should face consequences
Contract sanctity is selectively enforced based on who holds power
money_quotes: If people on the left refuse to discuss reality, then who owns reality?
For whom do laws work? Who can trust that, when they enter a legal obligation, it will be honored?

It's all very well to rest our arguments on the sanctity of the contract, but if you look around the edges you'll see whose contracts get ripped up because of fraudulent accounting, and whose bonuses get bigger.
If we bailed out the banks, why not the people of Detroit?
I'm not a huge fan of the original bailouts, because it ignored the social and legal contracts in the opposite way, that failures should fail and people who are criminals should go to jail.
posts.key_insight: The selective enforcement of contracts and bailouts reveals that legal protections serve the powerful while ordinary workers' pension promises are treated as disposable.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that proprietary credit scoring models represent a dangerous erosion of transparency and accountability, functioning as secret laws that govern people's lives without oversight. She dismantles the 'gaming' argument for secrecy by pointing out that banks openly game regulatory models. The real value isn't the algorithm but access to vast personal data networks. Most alarmingly, she reveals that Equifax has now been embedded directly into healthcare law through a federal contract, effectively making a private company's opaque system part of official governance.
key_points: The 'anti-gaming' justification for secrecy is hypocritical since banks constantly game regulatory models without consequence
Credit scoring companies' real competitive advantage is data access, not algorithmic sophistication
Free credit reports only show input data, not how it's actually used to make decisions
Credit scores have expanded beyond finance to affect dating and now healthcare eligibility
A $329 million federal contract has made Equifax's proprietary system part of healthcare law enforcement
money_quotes: It's like we have a secondary system of secret laws where we don't actually get to see the rules, nor do we get to point out mistakes or reasonably refute them.
So either we don't mind gaming or we don't hold up our banks to the same standards as our citizens.
Let's say the models were open and people started gaming the credit score models – what would that look like? A bunch of people paying their electricity bill on time?
The real reason the models are secret is that the companies who set them up make more money that way, pretending to have some kind of secret sauce.
If I could get all that personal data on everyone, including the historical data, I could easily build a credit scoring model.
Thanks Equifax, I guess we'll just trust you on all of this.
posts.key_insight: When proprietary algorithms become embedded in law, we've created a shadow legal system where private companies write rules that govern citizens' lives without transparency or accountability.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the failure of online courses at San Jose State University, where 56-76% of students failed basic classes, and uses this as a springboard to question the true purpose of college education. Rather than seeing the high failure rates as surprising, she's impressed anyone passed given the 'light-weight connection' of online learning. Through an anecdote about a Yale graduate who skipped classes but built a valuable business network with classmates, she argues that education's primary value lies in relationship-building rather than content delivery.
key_points: San Jose State suspended Udacity online courses after failure rates of 56-76% in basic undergraduate classes
The 'light-weight connection' of online courses makes even passing them an achievement
A Yale graduate admitted he learned nothing in classes but built lasting business relationships with classmates
Education's value may be more about making connections than acquiring knowledge
Personal connections made in college can have lasting professional impacts, like introductions to mentors and advisors
money_quotes: Personally, I'm impressed that so many people passed them considering how light-weight the connection is in such course experiences.
It all depends on what you were expecting, I guess. It begs the question of what college is for anyway.
He never learned a thing in classes, that in fact he skipped most of his classes to hang out with his buddies. He somehow thought MOOCs would be a fine replacement for that experience.
When I asked him whether he still knew any of his buddies from college, he acknowledged that he does business with them all the time.
This confirms my theory that education is more about making connection than education per se.
posts.key_insight: MOOCs fail to replicate the real value of college, which lies not in content delivery but in the lasting personal and professional connections formed through shared physical presence.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates Radhika Nagpal's Scientific American post about surviving tenure-track life at Harvard by rejecting the system's oppressive expectations. Nagpal's survival tactics include treating the job as a 7-year postdoc, ignoring advice, working fixed hours, and prioritizing being a whole person. O'Neil argues that people needlessly sacrifice their quality of life in high-stakes environments like academia and finance, believing misery leads to success. She contends this is cognitive bias and that the only way to avoid oppression is to live in denial of the system while still working hard toward personal goals.
key_points: Nagpal's key survival tactic was reframing tenure-track as a 7-year postdoc with no particular goal, freeing her from constant anxiety about measuring up
People in intense environments with slow weeding processes (academia, finance) become paralyzed by wondering if superiors think they're measuring up
The belief that misery and self-sacrifice lead to faster success is a cognitive bias, not reality
Ignoring oppressive systems while still working hard is both a survival tactic and a way to undermine those systems
This problem extends beyond academia to any high-stakes environment like D.E. Shaw's path to Managing Director
money_quotes: It's just amazing how often people are willing to make themselves miserable and delay their lives when they're going for something ambitious.
Why would anyone be more efficient at learning, at producing, or at creating when they're sleep-deprived and oppressed?
Ultimately it happens to someone when they start believing in the system.
The only way to avoid that kind of oppression is to live your life in denial of the system.
People spend months or even years in near-paralysis wondering if their superiors think they're measuring up. Gross!
Let's perfect the art of ignoring the system when it's oppressive, since it's a useful survival tactic, and also intrinsically changes the system in a positive way by undermining it.
posts.key_insight: Rejecting oppressive systems by refusing to internalize their expectations is both a personal survival strategy and an act that undermines the system itself.

posts.summary: This guest post offers a psychological interpretation of why Huma Abedin continues to support Anthony Weiner's mayoral campaign despite his sexting scandals. The author argues it's entirely about protecting their young son Jordan from future stigma. By reframing Weiner as 'almost mayor' rather than 'disgraced congressman,' Huma hopes to give Jordan a less humiliating narrative about his father. The post suggests the marriage is effectively over, but Huma is trapped by the permanent reality that Weiner will always be Jordan's father. The author concludes with sympathy for both mother and child while withholding her own vote.
key_points: Huma's support for Weiner's campaign is a strategic move to protect her son's future identity, not about saving the marriage
The marriage is effectively over, but divorce doesn't solve the permanent problem of Jordan's paternity
A 'Mayor Weiner' narrative is far better for a child than 'disgraced sexting congressman Weiner'
The best Huma can hope for is that her son's father becomes known for policies rather than penis jokes
Even sympathetic observers won't reward this calculated damage control with their votes
money_quotes: She's got a child for a husband, with a weird sexual addiction that on the positive side, doesn't appear to carry the threat of STDs.
The marriage is over. What she cares about is Jordan. And this is where she's really fucked.
Whatever happens, Anthony will always be her child's father.
You've got a child you love more than anything in the world, will sacrifice anything for, and will always now be stigmatized as the son of a celebrity-sized asshole.
The best scenario for Huma is if Anthony becomes mayor. Then she can divorce his ass, get primary custody and protect her child from growing up listening to penis jokes about his loser father.
And with a whole lot of luck, they might even be about how his father's penis was a lot smaller in the mind of the public than his policies.
posts.key_insight: A political spouse's public loyalty during scandal may have nothing to do with the marriage and everything to do with damage control for the children who will inherit the narrative.

posts.summary: This advice column from Aunt Pythia addresses three readers: a new parent hoping to balance baby care with math research, a young woman tired of being mistaken for a student, and a wife frustrated with her stay-at-home husband's housekeeping. The responses are characteristically blunt and unconventional, rejecting polished encouragement in favor of honest assessments. On parenting, O'Neil warns it's brutally hard and advises investing heavily in childcare without guilt. On professional appearance, she controversially notes that being overweight has helped her be taken seriously as a woman rather than dismissed as a girl. On household chores, she counsels gratitude for a present parent and creative solutions involving children.
key_points: Parenting while maintaining a career is genuinely brutal - comparing it to writing a thesis yearly while living with someone actively sabotaging you
Investing heavily in quality childcare is worth going into debt for, specifically so you feel confident your child is better off during your work hours
Being overweight has paradoxically helped the author be taken seriously professionally, subverting the expectation that women should feel bad about their weight
Professional dress codes only matter for specific occasions like talks and first days; otherwise authenticity wins
Resentment over household chores is more damaging than the mess itself
money_quotes: it's the hardest thing you'll ever do (hopefully). You know how grad school was hard? This is like having to write a thesis once a year while living 24/7 with someone who's only goal is for you to not get that done.
be incredibly proud of yourself every day you survive this period, and don't add an ounce of guilt to yourself that you can avoid. Guilt doesn't help.
pay through the nose for good babysitting and daycare, it's worth the investment so that you don't have to worry your kid is getting love and attention. Go into debt, borrow money, or whatever
according to the social contract, I'm supposed to feel consistently bad about my weight, but here's an example where I'm like, wow I've never been underestimated as a 'girl'
People on the internet claim that I need to pluck my eyebrows to be taken seriously, but fuck that shit.
don't make the mistake I see so much of allowing resentment to build up over chores
posts.key_insight: Conventional wisdom about success and appearance often undersells how hard things actually are or prescribes conformity that doesn't serve you - brutal honesty and strategic rule-breaking are more useful than polished advice.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her upcoming keynote talk at PyData 2013, titled 'Storytelling With Data.' She notes that the conference schedule incorrectly changed her title to 'Scalable Storytelling With Data,' which she plans to address since her argument is that good tools eliminate the need to obsess over scale. She also mentions an unconventional plan to incorporate an Old Testament story into her Sunday morning presentation.
key_points: The author is delivering a keynote at PyData 2013 at Microsoft's NERD conference center
Her talk title was changed without her input to add 'Scalable' - a change she disagrees with
Her core argument: with great tools, scale becomes less of a concern
She plans to incorporate religious storytelling into a technical talk, finding it fitting for a Sunday morning
money_quotes: One of the points of the talk, in fact, is that with great tools, we don't need to worry too much about the scale.
I'm thinking of addressing this name change in my talk.
It's totally bizarre since I'm not at all religious but for some reason it feels right.
posts.key_insight: Good data tools should liberate us from obsessing over scalability, letting us focus on the storytelling itself.

posts.summary: O'Neil reports on a theory circulating among her contacts that Larry Summers is being positioned as a Fed Chair candidate alongside Janet Yellen purely as a political maneuver. The theory suggests Obama would ultimately choose Yellen, allowing him to appear progressive on gender while also signaling he's learned from past mistakes in handling the financial crisis. O'Neil finds the theory plausible depending on her mood but remains worried about the outcome.
key_points: Larry Summers may be a deliberate 'straw man' candidate for Fed Chair to make Yellen's selection look better
The political calculus would let Obama appear as a champion for women while distancing himself from Summers's crisis-era policies
O'Neil remains skeptical and worried despite the appeal of this theory
money_quotes: Larry Summers is being put in the running for the Fed Chair alongside Janet Yellen just so that, when Yellen gets the call, we can all breathe a sigh of relief it didn't go to Summers.
It's a wholly political ploy so the Obama can look like a hero for women everywhere when he chooses Yellen.
We can all conclude that at least Obama's learned this one lesson with regards to dealing with the ongoing financial crisis: Summers isn't the solution.
Depending on my mood I sometimes buy into this theory, but obviously I'm still worried.
posts.key_insight: Political appointments can involve deliberate misdirection, where an unpopular candidate is floated to make the eventual choice seem like a victory.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil interviews her husband Johan de Jong about the Stacks Project, an open-source algebraic geometry textbook that has evolved from a simple PDF download site into an interactive platform with sophisticated visualizations. The project is notable for its neurotic rigor—every result has a proof and references are strictly linear. Pieter Belmans has added features including full-text search, tag lookup, commenting, and most impressively, dynamic dependency graphs using d3.js. The new visualizations show how mathematical results depend on each other through heat maps colored by distance from root or leaf nodes, collapsible chapter-based views, and rotating graphs labeled by result type.
key_points: The Stacks Project is a self-contained, strictly linear exposition of algebraic geometry where all references point to earlier results
Every mathematical result has a permanent four-letter tag that serves as a stable reference even as content is added
New d3.js visualizations show logical dependencies between results through multiple heat map styles based on distance metrics
The graphs allow users to explore how sophisticated results depend on foundational lemmas across different chapters
The project evolved from simple file downloads to an interactive platform with search, commenting, and dynamic graph generation
money_quotes: We were quite neurotic setting it up – everything has a proof, other results are referenced explicitly, and it's strictly linear.
Of course, any mathematician thinks we can do that for all of mathematics.
I even had this deal where, if people found mathematical mistakes in the Stacks Project, they'd get a free t-shirt, and I'm happy to report that I just last week gave away my last t-shirt.
The redder results are the results that are closer to it in meaning and sophistication level.
For all of the graphs, hovering over a result will cause the statement of the result to appear, which is awesome.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical knowledge can be structured as a strictly linear, fully-proven dependency graph, and visualizing these logical relationships reveals meaningful patterns about how sophisticated results build on foundational concepts.

posts.summary: O'Neil analyzes a scatter plot of nodes vs. edges in the dependency graphs of Johan de Jong's Stacks Project, finding a remarkably linear relationship with slope 1.86. She explores why mathematical proof structures would produce such clean, consistent data, considering how overlapping subgraphs and citation patterns might explain the ratio. Her most provocative conclusion is that the consistency reflects the internal cognitive patterns of the mathematician who created it—when things feel 'complicated enough,' he wraps up a result, and that threshold is remarkably consistent within one person's brain.
key_points: The scatter plot of graph complexity (nodes vs edges) for 10,445 mathematical results is unusually linear and tightly clustered around the best-fit line
Edges grow linearly rather than quadratically because only direct proof dependencies are shown, not all logical dependencies
The 1.86 slope can be partially explained by a toy model where results reference ~2 prior results, with the gap from 2.0 representing overlap between supporting concepts
The remarkable consistency likely reflects the mathematician's internal sense of when something is 'complicated enough' to become a distinct result
Different mathematicians would likely produce different slopes, and comparing to arXiv citation networks could be illuminating
money_quotes: As a data person, let me just say that data is never this clean.
There's something going on, some internal structure to these graphs that we should try to understand.
The only real explanation of the consistency above is my husband brain.
At some point, when he thinks things have gotten complicated enough, he says to himself 'It's time to wrap this stuff up and call it a result!' and then he does so.
That moment, when he's decided things are getting complicated enough, is very consistent internally to his brain.
If someone else created the stacks project, I'd expect to see another kind of plot, possibly also very consistent, but possibly with a different slope.
posts.key_insight: The structure of mathematical knowledge graphs may reveal more about how individual mathematicians think than about the inherent structure of mathematics itself.

posts.summary: This guest post by Jordan Ellenberg explores why the Stacks Project's dependency graph shows a nearly linear relationship between vertices and edges. He hypothesizes this might be an expected property of random directed acyclic graphs rather than revealing deep mathematical structure. Using Sage, he generates random graphs with decaying connection probabilities and finds similar linear patterns. However, he notes his random models produce graphs with substantial overlap between descendant subgraphs, which may not reflect the actual Stacks Project structure.
key_points: The Stacks Project graph of 10,445 mathematical assertions shows a surprisingly linear relationship between vertex and edge counts in subgraphs
Random directed acyclic graphs generated with decaying connection probabilities also exhibit this near-linear property
The linear pattern persists whether using exponential decay or power-law decay for connection probabilities
Random models show high overlap between descendant graphs, but the Stacks Project may not share this property
A 2D lattice model with less overlap might better test whether linearity is truly generic
money_quotes: The Stacks Project autovomits out any contribution that would create a logical cycle! I wish LaTeX could do that.
Why is this so? Does it suggest some underlying structure? I tend to say no, or at least not much — my guess is that in some sense it is 'expected' for graphs like this to have this sort of property.
My guess: a random graph aficionado could prove that the plot stays very close to a line with high probability under a broad range of random graph models.
A vertex is very likely to be connected all but c of the vertices below it for a suitable constant c.
In her scatterplot we are not measuring the same graph again and again.
posts.key_insight: The linear relationship between edges and vertices in mathematical dependency graphs may be a generic property of random directed acyclic graphs rather than evidence of deep structural organization.

posts.summary: Florida Education Commissioner Tony Bennett resigned after being caught manipulating Indiana's school grading system to benefit a school operated by a major GOP donor. Jordan Ellenberg exposed the fraud as a simple lie about missing zeroes in a weighted average calculation. O'Neil uses this scandal to argue that educational accountability systems are inherently vulnerable to gaming and manipulation, especially by those with political connections. She suggests that high-stakes testing incentivizes corruption, and that more complex models like Value-Added would make such manipulation even harder to detect. The solution, she argues, is to measure school performance without punitive consequences, using data to help struggling schools rather than punish them.
key_points: The Indiana school grading scandal shows how even simple mathematical models get manipulated for political benefit by those with connections
More complex accountability models like Value-Added are even easier to game because nobody can verify the calculations
High-stakes testing creates perverse incentives that make gaming and manipulation inevitable
Selection bias undermines claims about charter school success - experiments with motivated students, parents and teachers always look good
Removing punitive consequences and using measurement to help struggling schools would produce clearer, more honest signals
money_quotes: The lesson learned for corrupt politicians in the future may well to be sure the formulae are more complicated and thus easier to game.
Look no further than the Value-Added Model for teachers and schools, which literally nobody understands or could prove is being manipulated in any given instance.
The question of who gets the best opportunity to manipulate their scores is, as shown in this example of the GOP-donor-connected school, often a question of who has the best connections.
Instead of punishing schools with bad scores, we could see how they need help.

As any statistician could tell you, any time you have an 'educational experiment' involving highly motivated students, parents, and teachers, it will seem like a success. That's called selection bias.
The proof of the pudding lies in the scaling up of the method.
We need to think longer term and consider how we're treating good teachers and school administration who have to live under arbitrary and unfair systems. They might just leave.
posts.key_insight: High-stakes educational accountability systems don't just fail to measure school quality accurately - they actively incentivize corruption and manipulation by those with political power, while the complexity of models like Value-Added makes such gaming impossible to detect.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses four reader questions spanning career transitions, social etiquette, statistical modeling of dating, and the pursuit of hedonism. Cathy O'Neil offers characteristically blunt guidance on each topic, from warning about academia's bias against career gaps to debunking a reader's belief that wealth automatically attracts romantic partners. She applies quantitative thinking even to dating advice, discussing pheromones and conditional probabilities of 'getting laid.' The column showcases her ability to combine mathematical frameworks with sharp social commentary and no-nonsense personal advice.
key_points: Academia, particularly in quantitative fields like math and economics, unfairly penalizes career gaps, disproportionately affecting women
Self-perception and confidence create feedback loops in dating - feeling attractive makes you act attractive, which increases your chances
Wealth and accomplishments don't automatically translate to meaningful relationships; treating people as commodities signals existing superficiality
Real hedonistic enjoyment requires genuine human connections, not transactional relationships with strangers
Social support networks (wingmen/wingwomen) can help break negative cycles in dating outcomes
money_quotes: If math, as a community, were serious about attracting good women, they'd change this bias. But I don't see that happening soon.
This is a great example of a question that says more about the questioner than anything about the question.
If you feel unattractive, you don't act like a sexy thang and people are less likely to approach you.
If you've gotten laid recently, you feel sexy, which makes you act like a sexy person, which is hot in itself.
Please let me be the first person to tell you that you're already quite superficial. Congratulations!
Just the way you're talking about 'girls' makes me kind of gag, as if they're lego parts that can be bought, traded, and sold.
The sneaky little thing about really enjoying a hedonistic lifestyle is, in my opinion, that you have real connections with the other people in your company.
It's not fun to do stupid sexy things with strangers whose motivations you don't know, especially if you're young and rich, because even if you don't know, I will.
posts.key_insight: Authentic human connection cannot be bought or gamed with status; the shortcuts people seek in relationships often reveal the superficiality they claim to lack.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues against embedding algorithms directly into laws, warning that this would add mathematical opacity to an already opaque legal system. She notes that lawmakers' incentives often aren't aligned with algorithms 'working better,' and that lobbyists would exploit black-box algorithms to further obfuscate regulations. While algorithms may be useful as tools for regulatory agencies like the SEC to prioritize cases, even this application risks encoding bias, as seen in Stop & Frisk policies that disproportionately target minorities. She concludes that the combination of algorithmic opacity and misaligned incentives makes direct embedding in law dangerous.
key_points: Adding algorithmic black boxes to law would compound existing opacity problems and be exploited by lobbyists who already write much financial regulation
The secrecy argument for algorithms is flawed—if people can game your model, your proxies aren't truly behavior-based
Laws must be understandable by those expected to follow them; requiring a PhD and Python knowledge to understand regulations violates this principle
Algorithms as regulatory tools (like SEC fraud detection) are reasonable, but create workflow rankings rather than law itself
Predictive policing algorithms encode and amplify existing biases, resulting in poor communities being over-policed while wealthy areas go unchecked
money_quotes: If you gave them a new tool, that of adding black box algorithms, then you could be sure they would use it to further obfuscate what is already a hopelessly complicated set of rules.
If people can game your model, that just means your model sucks, and specifically that your proxies are not truly behavior-based.
Anyone who is under the expectation of following a law should also be able to read and understand the law.
It wouldn't be enough to have the source code available, since that's only accessible to a very small subset of the population.

If you live in a nice neighborhood, the limited police force and inspection agencies never check you out since their algorithms have decided the probability of bad stuff happening is too low to bother.
Causation is for other people.
It kind of makes sense that white kids smoke pot at the same rate as black kids but are almost never busted for it.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic opacity combined with misaligned incentives makes embedding algorithms in law dangerous, as they will inevitably be exploited by powerful interests and encode existing societal biases.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil examines New York's investigation into banks using opaque databases to deny people banking services, effectively banishing over a million lower-income Americans from the financial system. She counters libertarian free-market arguments by noting banks are taxpayer-supported and should serve the public. More fundamentally, she argues we focus too much on defending against bad things happening while ignoring the denial of opportunities—good things not happening—which compounds into systematic harm for vulnerable populations.
key_points: Opaque, error-prone databases are being used to disqualify people from basic banking, with the 'underbanked' population growing 10% since 2009
Banks on taxpayer support have a reasonable obligation to provide services to most people as part of their public responsibility
Constitutional rights focus on preventing bad things, but lack protection for access to basic opportunities like banking, education, and fair mortgages
The cumulative effect of denied opportunities narrows future options just as surely as active harm would
Free market corrections for database errors don't adequately address the systematic exclusion of vulnerable populations
money_quotes: Negative reports stemming from the databases have effectively banished more than a million lower-income Americans from the financial system.
We care about and defend ourselves from our constitutional rights being taken away but we have much less energy to defend ourselves against good things not happening to us.
It's not written into the constitution that we all deserve a good checking account, nor a good college education, nor good terms on a mortgage, and so on.
Such things are basic ingredients for a comfortable existence. Yet these services are rare if not nonexistent for a huge and swelling part of our society.
Instead of only worrying about the truly bad things that might happen to our vulnerable citizens, I personally spend just as much time worrying about the good things that might not happen.
Lots of good things not happening add up to bad things happening: they all narrow future options.
posts.key_insight: The denial of basic opportunities through opaque algorithmic systems is just as harmful as active discrimination—good things not happening to vulnerable people systematically narrows their futures.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil responds to a female composer's essay about thriving as a woman in a male-dominated field, arguing that such success stories create survivorship bias. While acknowledging that being distinctive can sometimes help, she points out that we rarely hear from women who were 'squeezed out' of these fields. She reflects on her own experience of adopting gender-neutral presentation in grad school to be seen as a mathematician rather than a woman, suggesting that successful women in male fields may share coping mechanisms that allow them to minimize their perceived otherness.
key_points: Success stories from women in male-dominated fields create survivorship bias - we don't hear from those who left
Women who survive in male environments may have developed coping mechanisms to ignore their own otherness
The author wore gender-neutral clothing in grad school to be seen primarily as a mathematician, not as feminine
Celebrating being a 'woman in a man's field' may mask the contortions women make to succeed
The attention women receive in male fields isn't surprising or undue - it's something survivors train themselves to ignore
money_quotes: We hear way more from the people who stay and 'succeed', which tends to give us massive survivorship bias.
I think we'd find that the women who survive in nearly all-male environments have developed, or were born with, coping mechanisms which allow them to ignore their own otherness.
I didn't want to be seen as primarily feminine. Instead I wanted to be seen as primarily a mathematician.
We still seem to be contorting ourselves in an effort to not seem too womanly, and this makes me think it's entirely un-coincidental, and possibly a crucial part of what allows us to succeed.
I don't think it's undue attention at all – I think it's just something we train ourselves not to consider because focusing on it too much could be paralyzing.
posts.key_insight: The women who succeed in male-dominated fields may share a common trait of minimizing their own otherness, which creates survivorship bias when they later celebrate being women in those spaces.

posts.summary: A brief personal post celebrating new merchandise (cups and shirts) created for the Stacks Project, an algebraic geometry reference work. The author shares a candid family photo of herself and Johan (presumably Johan de Jong, the project's creator) wearing the new t-shirts. The post is a lighthearted celebration of the project's community and recent visualization upgrades.
key_points: New Stacks Project merchandise is available on Zazzle
The schwag celebrates recent visualization upgrades to the project
The post captures a casual family moment with both authors and their children
money_quotes: An unflattering yet adorable picture of both me and Johan in Stacks Project t-shirts, new and old.
You can see we're both instructing our 11-year-old on how to take a picture and our 4-year-old is squeezing in too. He's not shy.
Fuzzy yet awesome.
posts.key_insight: Even serious mathematical projects benefit from community-building touches like merchandise and shared celebrations.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on topics ranging from dating strategies for introverts, career paths in data science without grad school, mentoring first-generation college students, finding ethical employment after leaving the military, and the ethics of giving money to panhandlers. O'Neil provides practical, often contrarian advice grounded in her experiences as a mathematician and data scientist, while weaving in observations about how organizations and systems operate. The column showcases her characteristic blend of warmth, directness, and systemic thinking.
key_points: Most research doesn't pay for itself, so companies won't hire people to do abstract research regardless of credentials - even PhDs need to tie their work to revenue streams
Once you're sensitized to organizational ethics, you'll see problems almost everywhere that pays well, from tech giants to startups
The people who need help most aren't the ones asking for petty cash in Penn Station - poverty is a systemic issue that requires addressing root causes
First-generation and low-income students benefit from explicit offers of help and positive feedback, but sharing your own nonlinear path works better one-on-one after trust is established
Nerd communities like programming meetups can be good places to meet introverted partners, but patience is required to draw people out
money_quotes: I'ma be honest with you, most research doesn't pay for itself. Indeed it's pretty rare for research to pay off.

The truth is, once you've been politicized and sensitized to the evil that organizations do or are involved it, you start to see it everywhere. Or if not everywhere, at least most places where you get paid.
Working at Google might not be a good idea for you since we don't really know what they do.
I never give scary men money because they're threatening me, whether they're black or white, on principle.
Having worked in soup kitchens and having read enough about childhood poverty and hunger, I know that the people who need petty cash the most aren't the ones asking for it in Penn Station.
Start-ups often participate in weird shit in ways they don't want to acknowledge.
I can't take any money with me when I die, and that I expect to die with more than 12 bucks in my name, so in the end it doesn't matter.
posts.key_insight: Ethical concerns about organizations and systems are legitimate but create real tradeoffs - recognizing systemic problems means accepting that most well-paying work involves some complicity, so the question becomes how to navigate that reality rather than escape it entirely.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines two interconnected issues in finance: the costs and benefits of high-frequency trading, and the problematic relationship between finance firms and the open-source software community. She questions who truly benefits from HFT beyond the direct profiteers, and highlights the societal risks of opaque algorithmic systems. Drawing on her experience at D.E. Shaw, she describes how finance firms extensively use open-source software but resist contributing back, citing competitive concerns even for tools unrelated to trading strategies. While acknowledging that contributions eventually leak out through departing employees, she notes this process is frustratingly slow.
key_points: HFT primarily benefits those profiting directly from it, while the broader societal benefits (like lower trading fees) may be offset by predatory practices against 'dumb money' participants
The true cost of HFT includes systemic risk from poorly understood black boxes colliding with each other, plus the opportunity cost of talented people working on zero-sum games
Finance firms like Goldman Sachs and D.E. Shaw extensively use open-source software but actively resist contributing improvements back to the community
The excuse for not sharing is competitive fear, even when tools are completely unrelated to actual trading algorithms
Open-source contributions from finance eventually happen through departing employees, representing a 'lagged contribution'
money_quotes: A harder and more complete answer would involve how much risk we take on as a society when we build black boxes that we don't understand and let them collide with each other with our money.
After all, you say market making, I say arbing.
Goldman had a one-way relationship with open source. They took huge amounts of free software off the Web, but they did not return it after he had modified it.

The urge to give back to the OS community was largely thwarted, when it came up at all, because there was a fear, or at least an argument, that somehow our competition would use it against us, to eliminate our edge.
He's got a way with words and he's bloodthirsty, always an entertaining combination.
As a modeler, you have a very selfish outlook and only want to develop tools for your particular situation.
posts.key_insight: Finance firms benefit enormously from the open-source commons while contributing almost nothing back, treating shared resources as competitive advantages to be hoarded rather than community goods to be nurtured.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil examines Lending Club, a peer-to-peer lending platform where Larry Summers serves as an advisor, raising concerns about its regulatory structure and potential for predatory practices. The company circumvents banking regulations by partnering with a Utah bank that permits high interest rates, with 37% of loans carrying APRs between 19-29%. O'Neil questions how borrowers desperate enough to accept 25% APR loans could realistically repay them, comparing the practice to loan sharking. She's particularly concerned about the company's unregulated proprietary scoring model, which may discriminate against uneducated or poor borrowers rather than accurately predicting repayment ability.
key_points: Lending Club avoids bank regulation by partnering with a Utah bank that allows high interest rates, with over a third of loans at 19-29% APR
The combination of desperate borrowers and high interest rates resembles loan sharking rather than legitimate lending
Proprietary credit scoring models are unregulated and may use signals correlated with poverty or lack of education rather than actual creditworthiness
While some borrowers may benefit from access to credit they couldn't otherwise get, the concern is this becoming normalized for vulnerable populations
money_quotes: I wonder how someone is supposed to be both desperate enough to go for a 25% APR loan and also able to pay back the money. This sounds like loan sharking to me.
The company itself is not regulated as a bank. But it has teamed up with a bank in Utah, one of the states that allows banks to charge high interest rates.
I'm more worried about the idea of this becoming the new normal of how money changes hands and how that would affect people already squeezed out of the system.
Lending Club, in addition to offering credit scores and income when they have that information, also scores people asking for loans with a proprietary model which is, as you guessed it, unregulated.
The Utah connection is good for something, namely allowing high interest rates.
posts.key_insight: Peer-to-peer lending platforms that use unregulated proprietary scoring and exploit regulatory loopholes to charge high interest rates may simply be repackaging predatory lending for the digital age.

posts.summary: O'Neil skewers the Draper University of Heroes, a $9,500 eight-week Silicon Valley entrepreneurship program that she compares to pickup artist training. The curriculum includes pledging allegiance to personal brands, minimal coding instruction, pool time, go-kart racing, and graduation ceremonies involving superhero capes and trampolines. She argues the program is fundamentally a confidence-boosting ritual designed to help young men convince themselves their narcissistic worldview is honorable. The key difference from pickup artistry is that instead of tricking drunk women for hours, these would-be entrepreneurs must trick investors for much longer.
key_points: The program's curriculum prioritizes confidence-building theater over substantive skills, with only two days of coding amid weeks of networking and leisure activities
The gender imbalance is not accidental but reflects the program's design as a male confidence ritual similar to pickup artist communities
Both pickup artistry and Silicon Valley hustle culture share a core structure: convincing participants their narcissistic goals are noble and just require the right tricks
The reading list (Trump, Ayn Rand, Wall Street MBA) reveals the underlying ideology of self-serving individualism dressed up as heroism
money_quotes: Even just the name of the school – the Draper University of Heroes – feels like an Onion article
They pledge allegiance every morning to their personal brands
They submit to a full two days of coding and excel lessons
Tesla (the new conspicuous consumption choice among pseudo-progressive capitalists)
It's fundamentally a confidence-boosting ritual, where a bunch of guys convince themselves that their prospects are good, their goals are attainable, their narcissistic world view is honorable
It's just a question of acquiring the right magic tricks to entrap their prey
Whereas the pick up artists only needed to trick drunk women for a few hours in order to sleep with them, these 'Silicon Valley Heroes' have to trick way more people for way longer
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley entrepreneurship culture operates on the same psychological mechanics as pickup artistry: confidence rituals that dress up manipulation and narcissism as heroic self-actualization.

posts.summary: O'Neil questions the assumption that big businesses are inherently better, exploring whether small companies might actually be more ethical. She cites examples of large-scale corporate corruption like HSBC's money laundering and JPMorgan's London Whale scandal, contrasting these with small email providers who shut down rather than compromise customer privacy. While initially suggesting small companies have more ethical accountability due to direct customer relationships, she updates her thesis to acknowledge the complexity: small companies can also exploit their size to avoid scrutiny, and are often used as political cover for bad policies.
key_points: Large corporations benefit from 'too big to jail' protection, escaping serious consequences for major crimes like money laundering and fraud
Small companies may have stronger ethical incentives because they depend on direct customer trust and can't afford PR cleanup
The small email providers Lavabit and Silent Circle chose to shut down rather than compromise customer data—something unimaginable for Facebook or Google
Small companies can also be problematic: they may lack unionization, exploit their size to avoid accountability, and serve as political cover for bad policies
money_quotes: When asked why nobody's gotten into trouble, his answer was simple: too big to jail.
Can anyone imagine Facebook or Google doing that? The big business version of this is 'hiring really fancy lawyers' I guess, but it doesn't seem to work as well.
In general, can we claim that small companies have less to lose and therefore have more ethics?
Small companies live and die based on the relationship of trust that they have with their customers.
Larger firms, on the other hand, can hire PR firms to fix their image after the fact if things go wrong.
Small companies having less to lose can also be a super bad thing, if you want to get away with bad shit.
Small companies are often inefficient (so: no unionization) and are used as a political baby seal to justify all sorts of crappy policies.
posts.key_insight: The relationship between company size and ethics is more complex than simple 'small is virtuous' narratives suggest—scale creates different accountability structures rather than inherently more or less ethical behavior.

posts.summary: O'Neil distinguishes true 'big data' from business analytics, arguing the key difference isn't data volume but human involvement. Business analytics, no matter the scale, still requires humans interpreting charts and making decisions. True big data, as practiced by companies like Google, removes human intervention entirely—algorithms optimize and update themselves without waiting for approval. This distinction matters because it reveals that most people claiming to do 'big data' are actually doing traditional analytics at scale, and more troublingly, their jobs could eventually be automated away.
key_points: The size of your data doesn't determine whether you're doing 'big data'—the presence or absence of human intervention does
True big data systems are self-optimizing algorithms that don't wait for human decisions
Business analytics still relies on humans looking at spreadsheets and having discussions before making changes
Real big data implementations are surprisingly rare, even among companies that claim to use them
The uncomfortable truth: if you're doing business analytics, your job is potentially replaceable by an algorithm
money_quotes: I offend people daily.

No matter how big the data you use is, at the end of the day, if you're doing business analytics, you have a person looking at spreadsheets or charts or numbers, making a decision after possibly a discussion with 150 other people, and then tweaking something about the way the business is run.

If you're really doing big data, then those 150 people probably get fired laid off, or even more likely are never hired in the first place, and the computer is programmed to update itself via an optimization method.
In a true big data setup, the human has stepped outside the machine and lets the machine do its thing.
So the first thing I offend people about is that they're not really part of the 'big data revolution'. And the second thing is that, usually, their job is potentially up for grabs by an algorithm.
posts.key_insight: The defining feature of true big data isn't the volume of data but the removal of human decision-making from the loop—and that distinction should worry anyone whose job involves interpreting data for a living.

posts.summary: This advice column features Cathy O'Neil's 'Aunt Pythia' persona responding to reader questions about sex, relationships, academic philosophy's toxic culture, and maintaining idealism in a corrupt world. The most substantive response addresses the Colin McGinn sexual harassment case, using it to critique philosophy's culture of bullying and lack of external accountability compared to math and science. O'Neil argues that philosophy's reliance on rhetorical cleverness rather than evidence or logical proof enables bad actors to rationalize predatory behavior. The column balances irreverent humor with genuine ethical reflection on personal autonomy and institutional accountability.
key_points: Philosophy's lack of external verification (unlike math's proofs or science's experiments) creates a culture where rhetorical cleverness can excuse bad behavior
Women's underrepresentation in philosophy is linked to both bullying culture and sexual predation, and the field needs to actively address this
Being your own moral authority is essential - don't outsource ethical decisions to others or arbitrary rules

Maintaining idealism requires playing the long game: have clear personal standards, avoid burning out by trying to fix everything, and don't pin your ideals on individual heroes who may disappoint you
If you feel complicit in something evil, leave - even if it means quitting your job
money_quotes: I like math because it's filled with people who know how to admit they're wrong.
I don't like philosophy because arguments are slippery.
My real advice is to be your own moral authority next time. Your life, go live it.
Once you've been politicized and sensitized to the evil that organizations do or are involved in, you start to see it everywhere.
If you feel complicit in something you consider evil, then get the fuck out, even if it means quitting your job.
Don't depend on a single person or a couple of persons to embody the ideals that you care about, because they'll probably end up disappointing you.
Write up an internal list of your ideals, they'll never let you down.
I'm not saying the field can't recover, but until they work on it, I won't feel sorry for the fact that women are under-represented.
posts.key_insight: Fields without external accountability mechanisms - whether empirical evidence or mathematical proof - are more vulnerable to cultures of bullying and abuse because rhetorical cleverness can always manufacture justifications for bad behavior.

posts.summary: O'Neil presents three examples of algorithmic discrimination to counter claims that data science is objective. The cases include a black woman who received dramatically more job offers after creating a white persona, predictive crime algorithms that use physical characteristics as proxies for race, and a British university's admissions algorithm that amplified existing gender and racial biases. She frames these as predictable failures that vindicate her earlier warnings about big data's supposed neutrality. The post directly challenges Kenneth Neil Cukier's claims about data science objectivity, suggesting these problems will only multiply.
key_points: Mandatory diversity questionnaires on job sites can enable rather than prevent discrimination, as demonstrated by the dramatic difference in callbacks between black and white identities
Predictive algorithms that use physical characteristics like hair, eye, and skin color serve as proxies for race, reinforcing discriminatory practices
Algorithms don't just reflect existing biases—they can magnify them, as shown by a university admissions system that exacerbated discrimination
These failures are not surprising edge cases but predictable outcomes that critics had warned about
Claims of data science 'objectivity' collapse when confronted with real-world discriminatory outcomes
money_quotes: I'd love to hear Kenneth Neil Cukier explain how 'objective' data science is when confronted with this stuff.
So I decided to try an experiment: I created a fake job applicant and called her Bianca White.
The use of physical characteristics such as hair, eye and skin color to predict future crimes would raise 'giant red privacy flags' since they are a proxy for race.
What began as a time-saving exercise ended in disgrace when a computer model set up to streamline its admissions process exposed – and then exacerbated – gender and racial discrimination.
This is just the beginning, unfortunately.
posts.key_insight: Big data systems don't eliminate human bias—they systematize and amplify it in predictable ways that defenders of algorithmic objectivity refuse to acknowledge.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the upcoming release of 'Occupy Finance,' a book written collaboratively by the Alt Banking Occupy group, planned for distribution on the second anniversary of Occupy Wall Street. The post serves as both an announcement and a grassroots fundraising appeal to raise $350 to print 1,000 copies. The book aims to explain financial system dysfunction in plain English for the 99% who sense something is wrong but lack the vocabulary to articulate it. O'Neil humorously acknowledges Occupy's lack of financial sophistication while outlining the book's structure covering financialization's impact, how the crisis happened, and actionable solutions.
key_points: The book is modeled after Strike Debt's 'Debt Resistor's Operations Manual' and written by committee through the Alt Banking Occupy group
The mission is to translate complex financial concepts into accessible language and give ordinary people 'ammunition at the water cooler'
The book covers three main areas: real-life impacts of financialization, historical context for how we got here, and concrete actions people can take
O'Neil is personally fronting the printing costs and running what she calls a 'crappy Kickstarter' through personal checks
The target audience is 'Occupy-friendly or at least Occupy-inquisitive' members of the 99%
money_quotes: This is Occupy after all, we suck at money. Consider this a crappy version of Kickstarter.
The mission of the book, like the mission of the Alt Banking group, is to explain the financial system and its dysfunction in plain English.
We want people who know there's something wrong, but don't have the background to articulate what it is, to have a reference to help them define their issues.
We want to give them ammunition at the water cooler.
Heads They Win, Tails We Lose: Real Life Impact of Financialization on the 99%
The bailout: it didn't work, it's still going on, and it's making things worse.
posts.key_insight: Financial literacy is a form of political empowerment, and demystifying Wall Street's dysfunction for ordinary people is itself an act of resistance.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that her Occupy Finance book has been successfully funded through small donations from readers. She exceeded her initial $350 goal, collecting $596 from 15 different contributors. The grassroots fundraising effort, which she playfully calls a 'Crappy Kickstarter,' will allow for more copies to be printed and distributed. She expresses gratitude for both the financial support and the personal encouragement she received.
key_points: Crowdfunding goal of $350 was exceeded by nearly 70%, reaching $596
15 different people contributed amounts between $20 and $100, making it a genuine community effort
The funds will allow more copies of the Occupy Finance book to be printed and distributed widely
Personal messages of encouragement were as meaningful as the financial contributions
money_quotes: It means they're all part of the project.
What was particularly awesome for me about the 'Crappy Kickstarter' was the personal emails I got with words of encouragement for the blog and the book.
You guys seriously rock, and I feel very lucky to be your friend.
posts.key_insight: Small-scale grassroots fundraising can successfully support financial literacy projects when community members feel invested in the mission.

posts.summary: The Wall Street Journal revealed that websites like Staples.com, Home Depot, and Lowes charge different prices to different customers based on their location, with poor and rural customers often paying more. Staples' pricing was found to correlate strongly with distance to competitor stores, effectively penalizing those with fewer shopping options. O'Neil argues this likely has racial implications due to ZIP code segregation, and calls for hackers and entrepreneurs to create tools that let consumers manipulate their online personas to get better prices. She notes the irony that companies would have little legitimate complaint about such consumer manipulation.
key_points: Staples.com charges different prices based on customer location, with prices varying by about 8% on average for a third of products tested
The strongest pricing correlation was distance to competitor stores, meaning people with fewer shopping options pay more
ZIP code-based pricing likely has racial implications due to residential segregation, even if explicit race-based pricing is illegal
Consumers could theoretically manipulate their online personas (cookies, IP addresses) to get better prices but lack accessible tools to do so
There's a business opportunity in helping consumers game these discriminatory pricing systems
money_quotes: Specifically, poor people were more likely to pay more for, say, a stapler from Staples.com than richer people.
If anyone's ever seen a census map, race is highly segregated by ZIP code, and my guess is we'd see pretty high correlations along racial lines as well.
The messed up thing about this, or at least one of them, is that we could actually have way more control over our online personas than we think.
Hackers, get thyselves to work making this technology easily available.
You save me $1.00, I'll give you a dime.
I'd love to see them make a case for how this isn't fair to them.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic pricing that appears location-neutral actually functions as a mechanism to extract more money from poor and minority communities who have fewer retail options.

posts.summary: Aditya Mukerjee, a Hindu tech worker, was detained for nearly four hours at JFK airport after triggering a false positive on an explosives detector. Despite being cleared by TSA and NYPD, JetBlue refused to let him fly, asking discriminatory questions about his ability to follow female flight attendants' instructions and whether he needed a special place to pray. He was interrogated by multiple agencies including the FBI and Homeland Security, denied food and water, and subjected to repeated pat-downs and questioning about his religion. Upon returning home, he discovered evidence suggesting his apartment had been searched in his absence.
key_points: A Hindu man traveling during Ramadan was profiled as Muslim and subjected to extensive discriminatory screening despite having no actual connection to Islam
TSA agents refused to explain what chemical supposedly triggered the alarm, making it impossible for the detained person to help identify the cause
JetBlue corporate security asked overtly discriminatory questions about following female crew members and needing places to pray, revealing religious profiling
The subject was held for nearly four hours without food or water despite showing signs of physical distress, with officials dismissively promising release 'in a few minutes'
Multiple government agencies (TSA, NYPD, FBI, Homeland Security) were involved in questioning, yet the airline—not law enforcement—made the decision to deny boarding
money_quotes: We're not detaining you. You just can't leave.
When I asked him specifically why he couldn't do it in public, he said 'Because it would be obscene.'
I'm not going to tell you! It's right here on my sheet, but I don't have to tell you what it is!
We have some female flight attendants. Would you be able to follow their instructions?
You'll have to understand, when a person of your… background walks into here, travelling alone, and sets off our alarms, people start to get a bit nervous.
You also said a lot of things, kid.
Is this a medical emergency? Are you going to pass out? Do we need to call an ambulance?
posts.key_insight: Security theater and algorithmic false positives combine with religious profiling to create a Kafkaesque system where the detained have no recourse, no explanation, and no rights—while officials hide behind procedures that treat 'people of your background' as inherently suspect.

posts.summary: This installment of Cathy O'Neil's advice column 'Ask Aunt Pythia' covers four reader questions while also promoting a satirical column called 'Dear Miss Disruption' that skewers tech industry bootstrap mythology. The questions range from practical (pursuing a statistics degree as a career changer) to interpersonal (accepting money from a generous friend) to the bizarre (incest revelations in a relationship). O'Neil's responses blend practical wisdom with characteristic directness, and she notably highlights Miss Disruption's sharp parody of tech industry privilege, which mocks the 'learn to code' narrative by exposing the hidden advantages behind supposed self-made success stories.
key_points: The 'learn to code' bootstrap narrative often obscures the privilege (family wealth, elite education, seed capital) that actually enables tech success
When pursuing career changes into quantitative fields, formal credentials may matter more for admissions than actual learning outcomes
Money between friends can be a healthy exchange when given freely and received graciously, matching each person's natural abundance
Advice columnists must always consider whether questions are fabricated, especially those with sensational elements
money_quotes: Other than my affluent Orange County family, my Stanford bachelor's degree, and the $10 million that my uncle invested as seed capital for my innovative advice column start-up, I have nothing but my ability to code.
It might be more convincing to the admissions people to see an official set of courses in math and programming with official grades than for you to tell them you learned it on your own.
Some of them are just cash cows.
She wanted to give you something in return that was her kind of offhand bounty that she thought might help you with your life.
Go buy ingredients for some more waffles.
That's what I got for this question.
It's probably even more likely given the incest theme. But whatever, I'm almost out of questions.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's 'pull yourself up by your bootstraps through coding' mythology conveniently ignores the family wealth, elite credentials, and seed capital that actually enable most success stories.

posts.summary: Obama proposed a new college ranking system tied to federal funding, based on metrics like affordability, graduation rates, and post-graduation employment. O'Neil argues this will be heavily gamed by administrators and produce strong negative effects without solving the affordability problem. The metrics would pressure colleges to admit students likely to get high-paying jobs, lower academic standards to boost graduation rates, and effectively eliminate liberal arts education. She contends that modeling educational quality is inherently difficult, and the real solution is to invest heavily in state universities while identifying truly fraudulent institutions.
key_points: Obama's proposed ranking system will be gamed just like the US News rankings, but with more severe consequences since it's tied to federal funding
Metrics favoring quick loan repayment and workforce success will push colleges to select for finance and tech-bound students, gutting liberal arts programs
Graduation rate metrics create perverse incentives to simply lower academic standards
The fundamental problem is that educational quality is extremely difficult to model mathematically
The real solution is investing in state schools and making them nearly free, which would force private colleges to compete on cost
money_quotes: So much for liberal arts educations. So much for learning about art, philosophy, or for that matter anything that isn't an easy entrance into the tech or finance sector.
Do we really want to build the college system in this country in the image of Wall Street and Silicon Valley?
Gaming the graduation rate is easy. Just lower your standards for degrees, duh.
Ironically, much of the rise in tuition cost has gone to administrators, whose job it is to game the system for more money.

The mathematical models that already exist and are being proposed are just pathetically bad at it, partly because college, ultimately, isn't only about the facts you learn, or the job you get, or how quickly you get it.
It's actually a life experience which, in the best of cases, enlarges your world view, and gets you to strive for something you might not have known existed before going.
If you really wanted to make costs low, then fund state universities and make them really good, and make them basically free.
posts.key_insight: Any college ranking model tied to funding will be ruthlessly gamed, and metrics favoring economic outcomes will systematically devalue education that doesn't serve Wall Street or Silicon Valley.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts a conversation where a friend challenged her cliché wish for his happiness, leading her to realize that 'happiness' is often a bland substitute for meaningful engagement with life. She argues that wishing someone happiness can actually be a way of wishing them safety and disengagement from the world's problems. The post distinguishes between 'crappy suffering' (self-produced drama about time and inadequacy) and 'meaningful suffering' (striving for goals and caring about the world). She applies this insight to parenting, admitting she often prioritizes safety over adventure for her children. The conclusion is a call to embrace meaningful struggle over comfortable contentment.
key_points: Wishing someone 'happiness' is often a bland, even unkind goal that prioritizes safety and disengagement over meaningful living
There's a distinction between self-produced suffering (anxiety about time, inadequacy) and meaningful suffering (striving for goals, caring about injustice)
Parents often disguise their desire for children's safety as wanting their happiness, which can limit children's adventures
True engagement with life means being dissatisfied with how things are while staying committed to improving them
Role models who reinvent themselves late in life prove it's never 'too late' to pursue meaningful work
money_quotes: I don't want him to be happy. I just want him to have better suffering.
I want him to have meaningful suffering, not happiness.
Wishing happiness on someone is a pretty bland goal. Maybe even an unkind goal.
I'm not so sure 'happy' is all that different from 'doesn't get involved in the world too much, stays out of trouble, and is safe'.
Hate the world enough to never be satisfied with how shitty things are, love the world enough to stay engaged with it anyway.
If I'm still alive there's still time.
I want more for them, frankly, than that they're happy/safe.
posts.key_insight: Meaningful suffering—striving for goals and caring deeply about the world's problems—is more valuable than the bland contentment we typically call happiness.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces an upcoming protest against HSBC organized by the Alternative Banking group, part of the Occupy Wall Street movement. The protest will feature HSBC whistleblower Everett Stern, who exposed money laundering activities at the bank. O'Neil references Matt Taibbi's Rolling Stone article about Stern and the broader issue of banks being 'too big to jail.'
key_points: Alternative Banking group organizing direct action against HSBC
Whistleblower Everett Stern joining to lend credibility and firsthand testimony
Connected to broader 'too big to jail' critique of banking accountability
money_quotes: I'm helping organize a protest against HSBC with my Alternative Banking group.
posts.key_insight: Grassroots organizing and whistleblower testimony are tools for holding large financial institutions accountable when regulatory systems fail.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil examines how Lending Club, with Larry Summers on its board, circumvents the Equal Credit Opportunity Act by using alternative data sources like browsing history and Facebook connections to make lending decisions. Unlike traditional credit scores that must be behavior-based under FTC regulations, these algorithmic models can incorporate any data they can find online, effectively enabling discrimination that would be illegal for traditional lenders. She draws parallels to how employers use social media to perform informal background checks that bypass Fair Credit Reporting Act protections. The core problem is that people denied loans or jobs by these opaque algorithms never learn why, eliminating any possibility of recourse or appeal.
key_points: Lending Club uses browsing history, social media connections, and data from companies like Acxiom to score borrowers, bypassing Equal Credit Opportunity Act restrictions that require behavior-based credit scoring
Some lending algorithms evaluate your Facebook friends to determine if you 'run with the right crowd' before approving loans
Traditional credit and employment laws provide consent, disclosure, and recourse - but these new algorithmic systems operate invisibly with no appeals process
The same regulatory arbitrage applies to hiring: employers can glean legally protected information through casual social media searches without any of the FCRA's protections
Big investors are funding these companies precisely because bypassing credit regulation is profitable
money_quotes: Turns out that bypassing credit score regulation is great for business, so of course.
You can now blame your friends if you don't get that loan!
Maybe I should just go ahead and stop being friends with anyone who might put my electronic credit score at risk?
I think the silent failure is the scariest part for me – people who don't get jobs won't know why.
As applicants, we don't know if employers are looking, we're not privy to what they see, and we have no way to appeal.
From my perspective it's a logical continuation of his career of deregulation and/or bypassing of vital regulation to enrich himself.
Of course this rant is predicated on the assumption that we think anti-discrimination laws are a good thing. In an ideal world, of course, we wouldn't need them. But that's not where we live.
posts.key_insight: New algorithmic lending and hiring systems achieve regulatory arbitrage by using data sources that allow discrimination the law prohibits, while their opacity denies victims any knowledge of or recourse against the decisions made about them.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that young professionals' default avoidance of email for sensitive topics is often counterproductive. While people instinctively avoid documenting uncomfortable conversations, this habit frequently protects the wrong party. Strategic email documentation can protect your interests, establish credit for work, and prevent misunderstandings from escalating. The key is recognizing when documentation serves you versus when it exposes you.
key_points: Avoiding email documentation is often the wrong decision that ends up protecting the wrong person's interests
Proactively documenting your contributions via email forces clarity and makes it harder for others to claim credit for your work
When someone misrepresents an agreement in email, you must respond in writing to the whole group to correct the record
Strategic documentation prevents problems from escalating and helps set clear expectations
For serious misconduct, email yourself a timestamped account as a personal record
money_quotes: More than half the time, when I'm witness to this issue, I've noticed it's actually the wrong decision, and it ends up protecting the wrong person's interest.
People should realize that documenting sensitive issues is often just as important, and often to your advantage, as not documenting sensitive issues.
Force clarity. It's good for you, not bad, to have clarity.
If people don't want to give you credit for something, they'll have to write back and explain themselves, which is unlikely.
You need to realize that other people use email as a way of documenting stuff too.
If you ignore that email, people will assume you actually did agree to that.
By thoughtfully documenting stuff before it becomes an issue I definitely think I've consistently avoided problems becoming worse.
posts.key_insight: In workplace dynamics, the person who controls the written record often controls the narrative—so learn to use documentation strategically rather than reflexively avoiding it.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil's humorous alter ego) answers reader questions on topics ranging from dating and sexuality to career transitions. The questions include advice about masturbation and porn, suggestions for math-nerd couples looking to spice up their relationship, and career guidance for a mathematician reluctant to learn coding. O'Neil maintains a playful, irreverent tone throughout while dispensing practical advice.
