The column showcases her ability to blend humor with genuine insight, particularly in her advice to the academic mathematician that learning to code is simply 'a specific way of formally solving problems.'
key_points: Mathematicians should embrace coding rather than resist it - it's just formal problem-solving in a language computers understand
The Museum of Math in NYC is described as only good for 'school field trips and nerd dates'
Real human connection is key to avoiding emotional emptiness, regardless of other solutions
O'Neil uses humor and irreverence to make practical advice more accessible and memorable
money_quotes: It's like a tiny, possibly negative miracle.
I'd also say that if you managed to figure out how to ask Aunt Pythia a question but haven't figured out how to experiment with porn, then I can understand why you're sad.
In terms of avoiding heartless emptiness, meeting a real life person is probably key.
What is this museum good for except maybe school field trips and nerd dates?
Look, we need to give young nerds hope that someday they'll get laid, and you guys are now their role models.
Coding is just a specific way of formally solving problems in a language that computers can understand.
What doesn't work for you or your job prospects, though, is refusing to learn to code.
posts.key_insight: Beneath the irreverent humor, the column delivers practical wisdom: embrace new skills like coding rather than seeing them as beneath you, and prioritize genuine human connection over technological substitutes.

posts.summary: O'Neil draws parallels between Gary Shteyngart's dystopian novel 'Super Sad True Love Story' and emerging real-world developments in data surveillance and human commodification. She discusses Acxiom's new portal letting people view (a curated subset of) their data, noting the company's true motivation is avoiding regulation. She then connects this to Fantex, a startup enabling people to invest in celebrity athletes as stocks. O'Neil argues these developments point toward a future where everyone's credit scores become public so people can bet on each other's life outcomes—a nightmare scenario she calls 'an orgy in the exact center of tech and finance.'
key_points: Shteyngart's fictional real-time public credit scores are essentially already here, just visible to corporations rather than passersby
Acxiom's 'transparency' portal is a calculated move to avoid regulation, showing only curated data while selling complete profiles to corporations
Fantex's model of investing in celebrity athletes represents the commodification of human beings as tradeable assets
The logical endpoint of combining public credit scores with human investment markets is betting on ordinary people's misfortunes
The opt-out model exploits the fact that those most harmed by data collection are least likely to know they can opt out
money_quotes: Instead of the answers being 'anyone walking down the street' and 'when you walk by a pole' it's 'any corporation on the interweb' and 'whenever you browse'.
They're doing it because they're trying to avoid regulations and they feel like this gesture of transparency might make people less suspicious of them.
Just keep in mind that, as an individual, you won't know what they really think they know about you, but as a corporation you can buy complete information about anyone who hasn't opted out.
They're counting on people's laziness. They're allowing people to opt out, but of course the people who should opt out would likely never even know about that possibility.
I can't think of any set-up more exhilarating to the community of hedge fund assholes than a huge, new open market – containing profit potentials for every single citizen of earth.
An orgy in the exact center of tech and finance.
You get to make money when someone goes to the wrong college, or when someone enters into an unfortunate marriage and needs a divorce, or when someone gets predictably sick.
posts.key_insight: The combination of ubiquitous personal data collection and financial innovation is leading toward a dystopia where human lives become tradeable commodities, allowing investors to profit from others' misfortunes.

posts.summary: Mazzucato's Slate article argues that tech lobbyists have overstated entrepreneurial innovation while understating the critical role of state funding, using examples like the internet developed through DARPA. O'Neil supplements this with personal memories of her mother maintaining the early internet at BBN. She challenges the 'wasteful government' narrative by pointing out that most startups also fail spectacularly. While agreeing that tech companies should pay back for public investment, she's skeptical of Mazzucato's proposed solution of contingent repayment contracts, noting that corporations can simply dissolve and reform to avoid obligations. Her preferred solution is simply raising effective tax rates.
key_points: The state, not entrepreneurs, has historically provided the patient, long-term capital needed for breakthrough technologies like the internet
The 'wasteful government' criticism ignores that most startups fail entirely and produce nothing constructive
The entrepreneur myth has been weaponized by lobbyists to lower capital gains and corporate taxes
Contingent repayment schemes for government grants would fail because corporations can dissolve and reform to escape obligations
The real solution is raising effective tax rates rather than creating complex new mechanisms
money_quotes: Most new companies fail entirely and never do anything at all constructive. I personally have seen hundreds of people working on projects for years in the realm of 'entrepreneurs' that everyone knows will do nothing. Talk about waste!
It's a beautiful story aimed at confusing people about whether Apple or Google or GE should pay any taxes at all.

One critical way that corporations are not like people is in terms of long-term liability. People work at corporations, and when it's convenient for them, they leave and go somewhere else or start a new company (whereas it's harder to change bodies).
That makes for a pretty crappy Christmas morning when you're 8! My personal sacrifice for the internet.
Personally, I think the real solution is to stop listening to lobbyists and raise taxes, or at the very least make the tax rates effective rather than nominal.
posts.key_insight: The myth of entrepreneurial innovation serves to justify lower corporate taxes while obscuring that transformative technologies like the internet were built on patient public investment that private capital would never provide.

posts.summary: The post critiques the Education Department's new push for randomized trials in education, noting that while the methodology is sound, the exclusive reliance on standardized test scores as the metric of success is deeply problematic. Kolata's NYT piece celebrates these trials as pure progress, but optimizing solely for test scores will inevitably squeeze out valuable educational experiences like puzzles, deep understanding, and writing. O'Neil argues we need richer data collection including student self-assessments, attitudes toward learning, and long-term tracking. She draws a sharp contrast with medicine, noting that education's 'side effects' (like killing students' love of learning) matter enormously but go unmeasured.
key_points: Randomized trials in education are a good methodological step, but the choice of success metric matters enormously
Optimizing purely for standardized test scores will penalize anything that doesn't raise scores, including understanding why things are true or fostering love of learning
Education differs from medicine because 'side effects' like destroying curiosity are just as important as the primary outcome
Students should be given self-assessments and opportunities to evaluate their own learning experiences
Long-term tracking of attitudes and goals would reveal effects that immediate test scores miss
money_quotes: If it doesn't improve kids' test scores, it might as well not be in the book.
There will effectively be a penalty for, say, fun puzzles, or understanding *why* things are true, or learning to write.
As a data scientist who gets paid to think about the feedback loops and side effects of choices like 'metrics of success,' I have a problem with it.
Kind of like when the teacher raises his or her kids' scores but also causes them to lose interest in learning.
If you think about it, teenagers get treated better by their cell phone companies or Netflix than by their schools.
My experience is that kids actually aren't like this, know the difference between rote work and real learning, and love the learning part.
Skimping on experiments often leads to bigger and more expensive problems.
posts.key_insight: Rigorous experimental methodology in education is worthless if we optimize for the wrong metric, because what we measure determines what we value and ultimately what we destroy.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil briefly announces the creation of the Simons Center for Data Analysis and the appointment of Leslie Greengard as its director. She notes that despite Greengard's distinguished reputation in mathematics and computer science, she hadn't heard of this new center until seeing the announcement. The post is essentially a call to readers for more information, as she found only a minimal description of the center's mission to analyze large-scale data sets and develop new mathematical methods.
key_points: The Simons Foundation has established a new Center for Data Analysis
Leslie Greengard, a distinguished mathematician and computer scientist, was named its director
The center's stated mission is analyzing large-scale data and developing innovative mathematical methods
Even well-connected data scientists like O'Neil were unaware of the center's existence
money_quotes: Has anyone heard of the new Simons Center for Data Analysis?
Neither had I until just now.
Please inform me if you know more about this center. I got nothing.
posts.key_insight: Major new data science initiatives can emerge with little fanfare even in well-connected mathematical and scientific communities.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses four reader questions: career guidance for aspiring data scientists, navigating an ex's Facebook friend request, improving a married couple's sex life, and managing parental frustration with young children. Aunt Pythia delivers characteristically blunt, practical advice that cuts through reader rationalizations. On data science, she emphasizes that coding skills matter more than perfection. On relationships, she refuses to let readers hide behind excuses or blame their spouses. The parenting advice centers on establishing consistent rituals rather than attempting to reason with toddlers, while warning against emotionally abusive language.
key_points: Data scientists don't need to be great coders—they need to get data into usable format and know what questions to ask
In relationships, own your decisions rather than deflecting responsibility onto your spouse
Sexual desire mismatches in long-term relationships create dangerous feedback loops of rejection and hostility (complementary schismogenesis)
You cannot reason with children under 4; instead establish incredibly consistent rituals that provide structure and predictability
Verbal shaming and insults are as damaging to children as physical abuse—criticize actions, never define children by their mistakes
money_quotes: You just need to be able to get the data into usable format, which is tricky, and then you need to know what to do with it – it's much more about questions of what to do than it is about questions of whether your code looks great
Sounds to me like you're trying to make your wife take responsibility here for your stuff
Either man up and be her friend or leave her alone
The man or woman who wants more sex, even if it's just a little bit more, starts to feel rejected, and has moments of aggressiveness and hostility surrounding their unmet desire
There's no reasoning with kids under 4 years old
What you absolutely cannot do is get abusive when you yell. Stuff like 'you're stupid' or 'you're lazy' has been shown to be as damaging for teenagers as physical abuse
Never give in to a screaming kid, that's like asking them to scream
posts.key_insight: Good advice requires cutting through people's rationalizations and excuses to address what they're actually avoiding—whether that's learning to code, taking responsibility for relationship decisions, or accepting that young children operate on ritual rather than reason.

posts.summary: O'Neil proposes that sexism may function like an addiction, with men receiving pleasure-center stimulation when performing acts that establish dominance over women. She draws on recent NYT articles about sexism at Harvard Business School and in philosophy to illustrate how men and women experience sexist behavior completely differently—women feel the sting while men often perceive it as harmless fun. The theory suggests that sensitivity training might work by creating negative feedback to counteract the pleasurable response, similar to addiction treatment. This framework could explain why discrimination remains invisible to perpetrators while being glaringly obvious to targets.
key_points: Sexist behavior may activate the brain's pleasure center similar to drugs or sex, making it neurologically rewarding for perpetrators
Men and women experience sexist events fundamentally differently—men often remember them as positive or benign while women experience them as harmful
The pleasure comes from establishing group dominance over an 'other,' making the specific target somewhat irrelevant to the perpetrator
Sensitivity training may work by creating competing negative feedback that eventually outweighs the pleasurable response
This addiction framework could explain the 'baffling disconnect' where discrimination is obvious to targets but invisible to perpetrators
money_quotes: Women, being the targets of sexist remarks and behavior, are sensitive to its barbaric nature and status-oriented putdowns – they are aware of it because it so obviously stings.
'Why would people want me to stop having fun?' they ask.
I don't think men are lying. I think they actually experience sexist events as positive and benign.
In some sense it's kind of irrelevant who they put down in order to be rewarded, though, which is why they don't think of what they did as a bad thing, just something that they vaguely enjoyed.
How do you break someone of their addictive habits?
It would throw light on the baffling disconnect whereby such problems are glaringly obvious to some while remaining utterly invisible to others.
posts.key_insight: The neurological reward men may receive from sexist behavior could explain why they genuinely don't perceive it as harmful, suggesting discrimination persists partly because perpetrators experience it as pleasurable rather than malicious.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that mathematical definitions are an underappreciated art form that profoundly shapes the quality of research and theorems that follow. She illustrates this through her own experience with John Tate, who helped her reformulate her thesis's basic object, dramatically simplifying her proofs and improving clarity. She notes that definition-building is naturally iterative and can take years, yet this process is almost never explicitly taught in mathematics education. The post critiques how math is presented as already-known rather than discovered, hiding the creative struggle behind foundational concepts.
key_points: Good definitions lead to better theorems and cleaner proofs than bad ones - getting definitions right makes everything else work out more smoothly
Definition building is naturally iterative and can take years to get right, involving evolutionary struggles within the mathematical community
Mathematics education hides the mystery of how definitions and theorems were developed, presenting math as already-known rather than discovered
The 'importance' of theorems and why we care about them is rarely discussed openly, happening instead in quiet conversations at seminar dinners
A crystalline focus on the correct 'basic object of study' improves not just proofs but also communication and intuition about the work
money_quotes: Have you ever noticed how damn hard it is to make a good definition and yet how utterly useful a good definition can be?
The basic definitions inform the research of any field, and a good definition will lead to better theorems than a bad one.
If you get them right, if you really nail down the definition, then everything works out much more cleanly than otherwise.
I think it's strange that the building up of definitions is not explicitly taught.

I think it's a result of the way math is taught as if it's already known, so the mystery of how people came up with the theorems is almost hidden, never mind the original objects and questions about them.

For that matter, it's not often discussed why we care whether a given theorem is important, just whether it's true. Somehow the 'importance' conversations happen in quiet voices over wine at the seminar dinners.
posts.key_insight: The craft of constructing good definitions is fundamental to mathematical progress yet remains mysteriously untaught, hidden behind the facade of mathematics as pre-existing knowledge.

posts.summary: O'Neil describes her new role as an unpaid consultant in the NYC Mayor's Office, working with social service agency data to help vulnerable populations access better services. She contrasts this work with her previous experience in finance and ad tech, where data subjects were abstracted as 'the market' or treated as prey. What strikes her most is the office's genuine engagement with data privacy ethics—conversations she's never encountered in the private sector unless she initiated them herself. The piece reflects on the tension between building effective predictive models and respecting the privacy of vulnerable populations.
key_points: The goal is to use data to help people (homeless, child services clients) access better services, with efficiency as a side effect rather than the primary aim
Linking more data about people improves behavioral prediction, but creates serious privacy tensions even when intentions are helpful
In finance, data is sanitized and abstracted as 'the market'; in ad tech, users are treated as prey—neither context prompted ethical conversations about data use
Working with vulnerable populations has prompted genuine discussions about data privacy ethics that O'Neil never experienced in private sector modeling work
money_quotes: Far too many data studies only care about efficiency – how to make do with fewer police or fewer ambulances – with no thought or care about whether the people experiencing the services are being affected.
I want to start with the people, and hope for efficiency gains, which I believe will come.
There's a tension between the best model and reasonable limits of privacy and decency, even when the model is intended to be used in a primarily helpful manner.

In all my time as a predictive modeler, I've never been in that kind of conversation, about the malicious things people could do with such-and-such profile information, or with this or that model, unless I started it myself.

When you work as a quant in finance, the data you work with is utterly sanitized to the point where, although it eventually trickles down to humans, you are asked to think of it as generated by some kind of machine, which we call 'the market.'

When you work in ad tech or other internet modeling, you think of users as the targets of your predatory goals: click on this, user, or buy that, user! They are prey, and the more we know about them the better our aim will be.
posts.key_insight: The private sector treats data subjects as abstractions or targets, while public service work with vulnerable populations forces genuine reckoning with the ethics of prediction and privacy.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the publication of 'Occupy Finance,' a book created by her Occupy Wall Street working group. The book is being released on September 17th, the second anniversary of the Zuccotti Park occupation. She expresses gratitude to donors who funded the printing and invites readers to a launch event where hundreds of copies will be distributed for free.
key_points: The book 'Occupy Finance' was a collaborative effort by O'Neil's Occupy working group
The launch is strategically timed for the 2nd anniversary of Occupy Wall Street
Crowdfunding from readers made the printing possible
The distribution model is grassroots: free copies handed out in Zuccotti Park
money_quotes: Holy shit I'm just about bursting with pride to announce that my Occupy group's book, Occupy Finance, is coming out Tuesday and is at the printer right now.

Our plan is to meet in Zuccotti Park Tuesday morning, September 17th, which is the 2nd anniversary of the occupation, give a wee press conference at 10am or so, and then hand out hundreds of copies of the book to whomever shows up.
posts.key_insight: Grassroots financial education emerged directly from the Occupy movement as activists sought to demystify the systems they were protesting.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques comprehensive scoring models like college rankings for attempting to rate heterogeneous items with a single fixed score. She argues this is extraordinarily difficult because different users have different priorities—some care about alumni giving, others about location. Her proposed solution is interactive scoring systems where users assign their own weights to different attributes, creating personalized rankings rather than one-size-fits-all scores.
key_points: Scoring models that claim to be both comprehensive and heterogeneous produce absurd results, like ignoring price for sports cars
College ranking guides don't acknowledge how difficult their task actually is
The solution to heterogeneous scoring is interactivity—let users weight the factors themselves
Personalized scoring systems are technically easy to implement but haven't been widely adopted
money_quotes: At no point, however, do the college guides acknowledge the extraordinary difficulty of the task they have set themselves.
I think the issue of combining heterogeneity and comprehensiveness is addressable, but it has to be addressed interactively.
Let's let people create their own scoring system. It's technically easy.
Weird things happen when you try to do this, like not caring much about price or exterior detailing for sports cars.
posts.key_insight: Universal ranking systems fail because they impose uniform weights on attributes that matter differently to different people—the fix is letting users define their own priorities.

posts.summary: O'Neil reviews the 1977 film Network, arguing it was remarkably prescient about how television and corporate power would shape society. She notes the film's prediction of media as entertainment rather than truth-telling, and its depiction of corporate dominance over democracy, seem almost boring now because they've become our reality. The film's central tension between individual agency and corporate control resonates strongly today. O'Neil finds it both depressing and thought-provoking, praising how it balances satire with emotional authenticity across multiple subplots.
key_points: Network predicted the rise of entertainment-as-news and filter bubbles decades before Fox News and social media
The film argues television deals in illusion while viewers increasingly mistake it for reality
Corporate power has superseded national and ideological boundaries - 'the world is a business'
Despite our individual rebellions, we are all ultimately 'cowed by the power of money'
Effective communication requires balancing apocalyptic warnings with hope and calls to action
money_quotes: Anyone who wasn't alive in 1977 might not – probably cannot – appreciate how incredible it must have seemed back then. It might even seem boring to someone who is used to a world of Fox News and the internet's filter bubble.
We might have our own particular emotional and political issues and rebellions, but we are all cowed by the power of money.
Television is not the truth! Television is a goddamned amusement park.
We deal in illusion, man! None of it's true!
You're beginning to believe this illusion we're spinning here. You're beginning to think the tube is reality and your own lives are unreal.
There is no America. There is no democracy. There is only IBM and ITT and AT&T.
The world is a college of corporations, inexorably determined by the immutable by-laws of business.
Being consistently depressing and apocalyptic gets old, even if there's an element of truth to it. It's critical to balance that with hope about the power of individual action.
posts.key_insight: Network's 1977 satire about corporate media control and manufactured reality has become so accurate that younger viewers might find it unremarkable rather than prophetic.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on blogging, seeking advice, academic relationships, and career direction. She advises aspiring bloggers to diversify reading and keep posts focused on one idea. To someone who rejects advice-seeking, she defends her role by noting she often answers 'the question nobody thought to ask.' She firmly tells a professor to avoid pursuing a student romantically, citing power dynamics and professional ethics. The column blends practical wisdom with sharp observations about autonomy, power, and self-deception.
key_points: The best advice often addresses 'the question nobody thought to ask' rather than the literal question posed
When writing, stick to exactly one idea per piece—more leads to length and complication
Power differentials in professor-student relationships make apparent 'mutual attraction' unreliable and ethically fraught
Seeking advice isn't weakness; nurturing conversations can help people 'emerge courageous and determined' to make decisions they already knew were right
Reading should be diversified across sources rather than deep-diving into a few curated blogs
money_quotes: The key is to have exactly one idea and write about it. If you try to have more than one idea it will be too long, too complicated, or both.
Often, when people think they have a question about topic A, in asking it they expose that they have a much greater need to be advised on topic B.
Aunt Pythia loves her job, and wants to help people, but she often considers the best way to help is to answer the question that nobody thought to ask.
You can't derail her intellectual investment just because she happens to be attractive.

It's possible she's just psyched to get attention from a professor, and the power differential makes her willing to take that attention in any form she can. So don't get too high on it, it's called power.
People have been known to emerge courageous and determined from such conversations, and they go forth boldly and make the decisions they already should have been making.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable advice addresses the underlying question people didn't know to ask, often revealing that the real issue is about autonomy, power dynamics, or how to make decisions at all.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her decision to learn accounting through a free Coursera course, reasoning that among the subjects she knows nothing about (accounting, law, politics), accounting seems most tractable. She suspects her lack of understanding is primarily a language barrier rather than a conceptual one, and that the tricks accountants use may be similar to those she knows from quantitative finance. She also observes that Wharton offering its first-year MBA courses for free suggests the real value of business school lies in networking, not course content.
key_points: Accounting appears easier to self-teach than law or politics because the gap between theory and practice is smaller
Much of accounting knowledge is vocabulary and categorization rather than mathematical concepts
Free online courses have low completion incentives precisely because there's no financial stake
Business schools giving away course content for free reveals that the real product is networking and connections
money_quotes: There are lots of things I know nothing at all about. It annoys me not to understand a subject at all, because it often means I can't follow a conversation that I care about.
The space between what it's theoretically supposed to be and how it's practiced is smaller than with law or politics.
My impression is that my lack of understanding of accounting is mostly a language barrier, rather than a conceptual problem.
The thing about Coursera is that it's free, so there's not much incentive to keep up with the course.

The fact that all four Wharton 1st-year courses are being given away for free is proof of something – possibly that what you're really paying for in business school is the connections you make while you're there.
posts.key_insight: The willingness of elite business schools to give away course content for free reveals that the true value proposition of an MBA is the network, not the knowledge.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts the serendipitous moment when her Occupy group was drafting an op-ed against Larry Summers becoming Fed Chair, only to learn mid-meeting that he had withdrawn his name from consideration. She celebrates this as a small victory while acknowledging it's just one item on a longer list of financial reform goals. The post serves as both a celebration and a reminder that systemic issues like Too Big To Fail banks remain unresolved five years after the 2008 financial crisis.
key_points: O'Neil's Occupy group was actively organizing against Larry Summers' potential Fed Chair nomination when news broke of his withdrawal
The group was preparing for Occupy Wall Street's second anniversary, including releasing a book and holding a press conference at Zuccotti Park
Summers' withdrawal is framed as a victory but only one step on a longer path to financial reform
The post pivots to advocate for ending Too Big To Fail policies as the next priority
Five years after the 2008 crisis, O'Neil argues not enough structural change has occurred
money_quotes: That's some good fucking karma.
And yes, it's a pretty awesome moment when exactly what you're working towards comes true like that, even if it's only one thing on a very long list.
Too Big To Fail needs to end, people.
It's been 5 years since the crisis, and not enough has changed.
Another in our group had blown away my milquetoast logical argument with a couple of paragraphs of pure Occupy outrage.
posts.key_insight: Small activist victories matter and should be celebrated, but they must fuel continued organizing rather than complacency about deeper systemic problems.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the release of 'Occupy Finance,' a book written by the Alternative Banking working group of Occupy Wall Street, on the movement's second anniversary. The post includes her speech delivered at Zuccotti Park, which explains the group's mission to educate people about how the financial system exploits ordinary citizens. She defends the movement against accusations of radicalism by citing stark economic statistics showing wealth concentration and declining household income since the recession. The speech serves as both a celebration of collective action and a call to join the ongoing fight for financial reform.
key_points: The Alternative Banking working group wrote 'Occupy Finance' as an accessible guide to understanding and arguing against financial system corruption
The top 1% captured 95% of income gains since the recession ended while median household income dropped 7.3%
The group frames their demands as reasonable rather than radical: a fair system, an end to too-big-to-fail, and protection of the poor from predatory banking
Occupy's working groups continued meeting and organizing long after the initial park occupation ended
The movement emphasizes openness and collective education—anyone can join regardless of expertise
money_quotes: When the top 1% capture 95% of the income gains since the so-called end of the recession... then what we demand is not radical, it is only a system that works
We wrote it for anyone who wants to know how to argue against the side of greed, the side of corruption, the side of entitlement
We are asking lawmakers to stop banks, once and for all, from scamming people because they are poor
You don't need to be an expert—we started out as strangers who wanted to know how things work
We have made something out of our curiosity and out of our hard work
posts.key_insight: Understanding how the financial system exploits people is not radical expertise but essential knowledge that ordinary citizens can and should acquire together.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates the press coverage received for 'Occupy Finance,' a book released by the Occupy Wall Street Alternative Banking group on the movement's second anniversary. Despite discovering a missing page after publication, the book garnered attention from major outlets including NPR, Bloomberg, the New York Times, and the Financial Times. The post serves as both a celebration of the book's media reception and a practical announcement about how readers can obtain copies.
key_points: Occupy Finance received coverage from major financial and news outlets including FT Alphaville, NPR, Bloomberg, and the New York Times
The book launch coincided with the second anniversary of the Occupy movement
A production error left a page missing, which was quickly made available online as a PDF
The grassroots nature of the project is evident in the envelope-stuffing distribution method
money_quotes: Yesterday was pretty amazing, in spite of the fact that we realized a page was missing from the book.
Our group meeting this Sunday will consist of an envelope-stuffing party.
posts.key_insight: Grassroots financial education efforts like Occupy Finance can attract mainstream media attention even with DIY production mishaps.

posts.summary: This post announces a book club for 'Occupy Finance,' a collaborative book produced by the Alternative Banking working group of Occupy Wall Street. The book club will meet weekly at Columbia University, with each chapter's author leading discussions on topics ranging from financialization to banking legislation failures. The group plans to incorporate feedback from these discussions into a second edition of the book. It's essentially an organizational announcement for activist education around financial reform.
key_points: Alternative Banking group created 'Occupy Finance' as a collaborative educational resource
Book club structure pairs chapter authors with readers for deep discussion
Topics cover the full spectrum of financial crisis causes: bailouts, securitization, legislative failures
Feedback loop designed to improve the book for a second edition
Meetings held at Columbia University, suggesting academic-activist partnership
money_quotes: We in Alternative Banking are pretty proud of our book Occupy Finance
Bailout: It didn't work, it's still going on, it's making things worse
A short history of the principal legislative failures that caused the crisis and make another one likely
Old Bankers' Tales … and Why to Reject Them
posts.key_insight: Financial literacy and collective education are essential tools for grassroots movements challenging Wall Street's power.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the big data bubble is bursting as companies have invested heavily in data science without seeing meaningful returns. The hype stemmed from Google's success, leading people to believe data was magic sauce for any company. However, most companies lack the right data, ask wrong questions, and hire unqualified data scientists who can only solve pre-defined problems. The field has been commodified into a race to the bottom, with companies seeking cheap, short-term solutions. She predicts that after the hype dies, real standards for data science competency will emerge.
key_points: Companies treat data scientists as software—'data in, magic out'—rather than involving them in problem formulation
The lack of standards means anyone with one machine learning class can claim expertise, creating a market flooded with unqualified practitioners
It's nearly impossible to prove you have a bad data science team because you'd need a good one to demonstrate the counterfactual
Data science expertise has been commodified into a race to the bottom with companies seeking cheap short-term consulting
There's a meaningful difference between crude models built without understanding and thoughtful models that gain marginal edge—but most can't tell them apart
money_quotes: I've got a nose for sinking ships and sailing metaphors.

We humans are pretty good at detecting patterns, so after a few companies made it big with the secret data sauce, we inferred that, when you take a normal tech company and sprinkle on data, you get the next Google.

Since they think of their data people as little more than pieces of software – data in, magic out – they don't get their data people sufficiently involved with working on something that data can address.
It's super hard to accumulate hard evidence that you have a crappy data science team... It's a counterfactual problem.
Data science expertise has been commodified, and it's a race to the bottom.
Who will solve my business-critical data problem on a short-term consulting basis for less than $5000? Less than $4000?
Most people are unaware of even the theoretical difference between type A and type B models, nor would they recognize which type they've got once they get one.
I might have to change my job title, but I'm used to it.
posts.key_insight: The data science field's lack of standards makes it impossible for companies to distinguish between genuine expertise and credentialed incompetence, leading to a commodification death spiral.

posts.summary: This advice column installment addresses four reader questions covering math education for children, career transitions from academia to data science, dealing with 'non-selective' university students, and assumptions about gender in anonymous questions. The most substantive response tackles a professor's despair about students who 'can't code,' arguing that technical literacy isn't a universal requirement for a meaningful life. Pythia pushes back on the elitist framing, noting that technology's purpose is precisely to work invisibly for most people. She suggests the professor's real problem is their own narrow definition of human value.
key_points: The best way to nurture children's interest in math is through available conversation and puzzles, not forced instruction - 'nerd toys rock, and they don't feel pressured'
Career compromises exist everywhere - 'you never get rid of your problems, you just get a new set of problems'
Not everyone needs to understand how technology works; good technology is invisible to most users, like microwaves or government itself
Measuring human worth by technical capability is a narrow and depressing framework that says more about the measurer than the measured
The assumption that society is 'doomed' because most people can't code reflects elitist bias rather than reality
money_quotes: My motto is, 'you never get rid of your problems, you just get a new set of problems'
The hallmark of a really good technology is that most people don't have to think about it at all
Why would we even want the majority of people to know how to code? Why don't we leave that to the subpopulation of people working on stuff that needs coding?
It would be a huge waste for the entire population to learn that skill, even if they were interested and capable of doing so
People can lead super fulfilling and good lives doing nothing whatsoever technical
Give these young people some credit beyond the narrow question of whether they have this kind of talent and desire
Government itself can be seen as a technology – some big black box where money goes in and reasonable laws and order come out
posts.key_insight: Technical literacy is not a universal human requirement, and measuring people's worth by whether they can code reflects the measurer's narrow values rather than any objective truth about human potential.

posts.summary: Google's new Calico project aims to use big data and genetic research to combat aging, a move Cathy O'Neil saw coming when they hired immortality-enthusiast Ray Kurzweil. She argues that human mortality is actually necessary and beneficial - the planet can't sustain unlimited population, and frankly, intergenerational boredom is already a problem. While she supports improving quality of life, she objects to the inherent inequality of who would benefit from life extension technology: wealthy tech executives would be first in line. She remains skeptical that big data can actually achieve immortality, but notes that if it could, it could probably also convince her to accept the arrangement.
key_points: Google's Calico project follows their hiring of Ray Kurzweil, signaling serious investment in anti-aging technology
Human mortality serves necessary ecological and social functions - the planet cannot sustain infinite population growth
Life extension technology will inevitably be distributed unequally, benefiting wealthy tech executives first
The author distinguishes between improving quality of life (good) versus eliminating death for the privileged (problematic)
Big data has limits, and immortality is likely beyond its reach - but the propaganda capabilities of such technology would be equally powerful
money_quotes: We need people to die.
Our planet cannot sustain all the people currently alive as well as all the people who are going to someday be born.
Think about how boring it is already for young people to be around old people. I bore myself around my kids, and I'm only 30 years older than they are.
The idea that we're going to figure out how to keep alive a bunch of super rich advertising executives just doesn't seem right.
There will have to be a way to choose who lives and who dies, and I know who is at the top of that list.
Larry Page, Tim Cook, and Ray Kurzweil: I'd really like it if you eventually died.

If they can use big data to make people live forever, they can also use big data to convince me that super special rich white men living in Silicon Valley should take up resources and airtime for the rest of eternity.
posts.key_insight: Life extension technology isn't just a scientific challenge but a social justice issue, since access to immortality would inevitably be distributed according to existing power and wealth hierarchies.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil lists her upcoming speaking engagements in fall 2013. She will speak to Columbia's Data Science Society about data science thinking, participate in a panel on financial regulation transparency, give an MAA lecture on recommendation systems for high school math teachers, and present something 'nerdy and skeptical' at Harvard's Applied Statistics Workshop.
key_points: Speaking to engineers at Columbia about 'how to think like a data scientist'
Participating in financial reform panel discussion about what mathematical tools can and cannot do for regulation
Giving an accessible talk on recommendation systems ('Start Your Own Netflix') to high school math teachers
Her self-described approach: 'nerdy and skeptical'
money_quotes: I'll be giving my two cents on what mathematical tools can do and cannot do with respect to this stuff.
I haven't figured out exactly what I'm talking about but it will be something nerdy and skeptical.
posts.key_insight: O'Neil's speaking topics reveal her consistent focus on demystifying data science while maintaining healthy skepticism about mathematical tools' limitations.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares a Huffington Post article about 'cliteracy' - an art project and educational initiative focused on female sexual anatomy. Despite the brief post, she expresses genuine emotional connection to the piece, noting she was moved to tears by its empowering message. The post highlights her willingness to platform topics about women's bodies and education outside her typical algorithmic focus.
key_points: Recommends a HuffPost article about an art/education project called 'cliteracy'
Found the article unexpectedly inspiring and emotionally moving
Emphasizes that knowledge about female anatomy deserves celebration
money_quotes: The article is actually more inspiring than you'd think, and I found myself weeping with joy at times.
posts.key_insight: Education about women's bodies can be genuinely inspiring and worthy of celebration.

posts.summary: O'Neil highlights a set of seven ethical principles for big data projects developed by the 2013 PopTech & Rockefeller Foundation Bellagio Fellows. She endorses these principles as a broader code of conduct for data scientists, not just for 'resilience building projects.' The principles cover open source tools, transparent infrastructure, local skills development, data ownership, ethical sharing, the right not to be sensed, and learning from failures. O'Neil presents this as a framework for responsible data science practice and encourages public comment on the principles.
key_points: Data analytics tools should be open source and architecture independent to maximize accessibility
Local communities must retain ownership of their data and have the right to opt out of sensing projects
Ethical data sharing requires clear articulation of how data will be used and the ability for individuals to remove themselves from datasets
Data literacy should be developed locally rather than imposed from outside
Projects must be transparent about failures and willing to learn from mistakes
money_quotes: Local communities have a right not to be sensed.
Permission for sharing is essential.
The ability for individuals to remove themselves from a data set after it has been collected must always be an option.
Big Data and Resilience projects need to be open to face, report, and discuss failures.
Use Creative Commons and licenses that state that data is not to be used for commercial purposes.
posts.key_insight: Data scientists need ethical codes that prioritize local ownership, transparency, and the fundamental right of communities to control whether and how they are sensed and analyzed.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil endorses Dan Milstein's advice about avoiding arbitrary deadlines by understanding the true business context behind technical assignments. She applies this framework to data science, arguing that data scientists are often given filtered, distorted versions of business questions that have been translated through intermediaries who introduce proxies and artificial constraints. The solution is for data scientists to become 'part business person' and talk directly to stakeholders to understand the real problem being solved. If your manager blocks access to decision-makers, it's time to look for a new job.
key_points: Technical workers should refuse to accept specs and deadlines at face value, instead seeking to understand the underlying business problem
Data science questions are often filtered through intermediaries who introduce proxies and artificial constraints that pervert the original aims
Data scientists should talk directly to business owners to verify they're optimizing for the right thing with realistic constraints
Effective communication with stakeholders involves echoing back their concerns, framing work in terms of business risks, and presenting clear trade-offs
If management blocks direct access to decision-makers, the job is likely to produce failed projects and you should leave
money_quotes: Often that process has perverted the original aims – often because proxies have magically appeared in the place of the original objects of interest.
It behooves a data scientist who doesn't want to be working on the wrong problem to go to the original source and verify that their work is answering a vital business question.
I suggest that each data scientist 'becomes part business person,' and talks to the business owner of the given problem directly until they're sure they know what needs to get done with data.
Always think and discuss your work in terms of risks and information for the business.

If your manager doesn't let you talk directly to the Important People in the business, and you can't convince your manager to change his or her mind, then you might wanna polish up your LinkedIn profile, because otherwise you are fated to work on failed projects.
posts.key_insight: Data scientists must bypass intermediaries and understand the true business problem directly, or risk wasting their efforts optimizing for the wrong proxies under artificial constraints.

posts.summary: This advice column installment addresses four reader questions covering career reinvention, probability puzzles, academic job insecurity, and professor-student dynamics. The central throughline is Aunt Pythia's characteristically blunt advice about personal agency: she argues that people shouldn't push partners into reinvention that doesn't suit their personality, that foreign PhD students are foolish to let misplaced loyalty prevent job searching, and that professors expecting gratitude from students should adjust their expectations. The column balances math nerdery (a sock-drawer probability problem) with sharp life advice.
key_points: Reinvention isn't universal - some people are wired for it (like ENTPs) while others aren't, and pushing someone toward change they don't want creates another perceived failure
Foreign-born PhDs often show misplaced loyalty to institutions that won't reciprocate, making them passive participants in their own careers
Nobody will think strategically about your future except you - waiting passively for grants or institutional decisions is a form of learned helplessness
Expecting gratitude from students or mentees is unrealistic; being an advisor is like being a parent in a society that doesn't reward those roles with thanks
money_quotes: It just won't work and it will feel to her like another thing she's failing at.
No wonder you're feeling helpless, it's because you're acting passively and helplessly.
Nobody is going to think strategically about your future except you.
Never let this happen again, and get thee on the job market immediately.
Don't act like the stupid NSF is the voice of God.
Being an advisor is a lot like being a parent, and in this society we don't get lots of gratitude as parents.
Move to China if you want that stuff.
posts.key_insight: Feeling powerless usually stems from acting powerlessly - taking charge of your own strategic planning is the only antidote to institutional uncertainty.

posts.summary: This casual Sunday reading roundup covers several disconnected topics: a suppressed TED talk about economic myths and job creation, research on women's ability to achieve hands-free orgasms, the troubling disparity in criminal prosecution between junior employees and executives in the JP Morgan London Whale case, comedy music recommendations, and knitting. The post is characteristic of O'Neil's blog style—mixing serious critiques of economic and legal systems with irreverent personal observations. The most substantive point concerns prosecutorial discretion and corporate accountability.
key_points: Nick Hanauer's TED talk challenging the myth that businesspeople create jobs was mysteriously excluded from TED's main website
There's a troubling double standard in criminal prosecution where junior employees face charges while executives only face civil complaints and shareholders pay the fines
The lack of recourse or accountability for prosecutorial decisions leaves the public unable to challenge uneven application of justice
O'Neil mixes serious economic and legal commentary with irreverent humor about personal topics
money_quotes: So when businesspeople take credit for creating jobs, it's a little like squirrels taking credit for creating evolution. In fact, it's the other way around.
Yet it remains disquieting when the same actions result in criminal charges for some but only a civil case for others, and no individuals are held responsible for misconduct at a company.
In the end, we are left to trust that prosecutors have made good decisions.
There's no recourse for bad prosecuting either. How do we even protest bad prosecuting?
posts.key_insight: The justice system's uneven application of criminal versus civil charges based on corporate hierarchy reveals a fundamental accountability gap that the public has no mechanism to challenge.

posts.summary: The post critiques NPR's Here and Now for uncritically platforming a College Board executive to discuss declining SAT scores without acknowledging the organization's financial conflicts of interest. O'Neil argues that the College Board, despite its nonprofit status, is a big business that profits from test prep materials and has incentive to create anxiety about test scores. She explains that SAT score comparisons across years are meaningless because the tests are constantly adjusted, and that recent changes to math notation were designed to increase difficulty. The post concludes by calling out both the College Board for misleading the public and the radio program for naive journalism that amounts to free advertising.
key_points: The College Board is a profit-driven organization with executives earning over $300,000 annually, including a CEO who made $1.3 million in 2009
Year-over-year SAT score comparisons are meaningless because the test is constantly modified to maintain a bell curve distribution
Recent changes to math section notation were specifically designed to make questions harder to parse, driving demand for test prep
The SAT essay's primary grading criterion is length, not quality of writing, which actively teaches bad writing habits
Media coverage of declining SAT scores serves as free advertising for the test prep industry
money_quotes: College Board has the ability to create an 'emergency' about SAT scores, by say changing the test or making it harder, and then the only 'reasonable response' is to pay for yet more test prep.
SAT scores are fucked with ALL THE TIME.
Even if the same group of kids took those two different tests in the same year, they'd have different scores.
This has nothing to do with their knowledge, it has to do with their training for this specific test.
If the College Board is so concerned about the dumbing down of American children, they should examine their own role in lowering and distorting the standards for written expression.
Did you think public radio doesn't have advertising? Think again.
posts.key_insight: Standardized testing companies have perverse incentives to manufacture score anxieties that drive demand for their own test prep products, while media outlets uncritically amplify these narratives as news.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the publication of her new essay 'On Being a Data Skeptic' by O'Reilly, which builds on Susan Webber's work about the limits of measurement in management. The brief post serves primarily as an announcement with a link to the essay. She adds a humorous aside criticizing the stock photo choice for the cover, suggesting her 11-year-old son could have provided a better skeptical expression.
key_points: New essay 'On Being a Data Skeptic' published by O'Reilly as an e-book
Essay builds on Susan Webber's work arguing that not everything can or should be measured
O'Neil had no input on the cover image selection
money_quotes: I think my 11-year-old son would've done a better job.
posts.key_insight: This announcement post points readers to O'Neil's deeper exploration of why we should question our faith in data and metrics.

posts.summary: This post summarizes and endorses an interview with economist Chris Thornberg, who argues that financial regulation has been gutted by lobbying, that perverse incentives that caused the 2008 crisis remain intact, and that accountability for financial wrongdoing is virtually nonexistent. Thornberg connects these failures to broader systemic issues including municipal bankruptcies threatening public pensions, a captured two-party political system, and an economics profession more interested in academic publishing than practical relevance. O'Neil highlights his provocative claim that the main lesson from the S&L scandal was teaching CEOs how to avoid prosecution. The post serves as a curated guide to Thornberg's critique of post-crisis reform failures.
key_points: Dodd-Frank has been effectively neutralized through lobbying and watering-down, with negotiations over securitization 'skin in the game' signaling its demise
The perverse incentive structures that made people rich doing harmful things remain intact, guaranteeing future crises
Municipal bankruptcies in Detroit, Stockton, and San Bernardino may establish precedent for federal judges to override state pension protections
Washington D.C. avoided recession entirely due to the influx of lobbying money, illustrating regulatory capture
Economists are too focused on academic publishing to monitor the real economy, and the public should distrust economists with obvious conflicts of interest
money_quotes: The only thing we really learned from the S&L scandal was how CEO's should lawyer up
A lot of people got very rich doing very bad things, and the continued existence of those messed-up incentives will encourage a whole new generation to do the same
Dick Fuld and Angelo Mozilo as men who became filthy rich from doing bad things and are now 'untouchable'
We like to say the Chinese are 'one-party state'. But are we really two-party?
Professors don't get published writing papers about the current economy – nobody cares about that
Although economists would like to think of it as a mathematical science, they're wrong to do so
It's amazing how much stock we put into economists working for, say, the National Association of Realtors who get paid to say good things
posts.key_insight: Financial reform after 2008 failed not because we lacked understanding of what went wrong, but because the incentive structures that reward bad behavior and protect wrongdoers remain deliberately intact.

posts.summary: The post examines why the 'revolving door' between Wall Street and regulatory agencies fails to transfer genuine risk management expertise to regulators. O'Neil observes that while Goldman Sachs has superior risk management culture, the people who leave for regulatory positions never share that technical knowledge. Instead, we get figures like Jon Corzine who take reckless risks once freed from institutional constraints. The result is that regulators remain perpetually outmatched by the banks they're supposed to oversee.
key_points: Goldman Sachs has a disciplined risk culture that keeps them ahead of competitors on identifying losing trades
The revolving door produces executives who take wild risks outside Goldman rather than technical experts who understand risk modeling
People who understand risk either never leave, or once outside, have incentives aligned against helping regulators
The SEC remains 'woefully unprepared' because the revolving door never delivers the promised expertise transfer
money_quotes: Instead of getting the technical knowledge of how to think about risk, how to model a portfolio to squirrel out black holes of mystery, the revolving door instead keeps outputting crazy freaks like Jon Corzine, who blow up firms through, ironically, taking ridiculous risks at the first opportunity.
This is kind of the model of Mormon children who are finally allowed out into the world and engage in tons of sex and drugs.
They don't help the regulators understand how banks lie and cheat to regulators, because they're too busy watering down regulation so their buddies can continuously lie and cheat to regulators.
Goldman Sachs promotes crazy freaks because they make great leaders while constrained inside a disciplined culture of calculated risks, but when they get outside they go nuts.
We keep using the revolving door in hopes that someone will eventually tell us the magic that Goldman Sachs knows, but we never quite get anyone like that.
posts.key_insight: The revolving door between Wall Street and regulators systematically fails because the people with genuine technical expertise either never leave, or their incentives flip once they're outside the bank.

posts.summary: The post addresses what initially appears to be a statistical paradox: if Tea Party members are truly a small minority hijacking the Republican Party, why did a majority vote to shut down the government? Drawing on a Democracy Corps report based on focus groups, O'Neil explains that while Tea Partiers represent only 20% of Republicans, the larger Evangelical bloc (55%) supports their agenda on healthcare, creating a combined majority. The post highlights how understanding the coalition dynamics between these factions resolves the apparent contradiction.
key_points: Tea Partiers are a minority (20%) but form a majority coalition with Evangelicals (55%) on key issues like opposing the Affordable Care Act
Race plays a significant role in Evangelical opposition to Obamacare
The Tea Party-Evangelical alliance is fragile because Tea Partiers don't prioritize social issues like gay marriage that matter deeply to Evangelicals
Fox News is a central force shaping the worldview of these Republican factions
money_quotes: I was wondering what a lot of other people were wondering yesterday. Namely why, if Republicans were claiming their party was being hijacked by a small minority of Tea Party lunatics, did they actually have a majority vote for closing down the government? It's a statistical conundrum.

Tea Partiers are minority, but the largest group, namely the Evangelicals (think Fox News, anti-gay marriage) are behind the Tea Party's agenda on the Affordable Care Act as well as Obamacare, and together they represent the majority of the Republican Party.
Maybe you knew this but I didn't.
posts.key_insight: What looks like a hijacking by a radical minority is actually coalition politics—understanding faction sizes and their issue-specific alliances resolves apparent statistical contradictions in political behavior.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' responds to reader questions on diverse topics including sex work legalization, travel philosophy, European perceptions of America, and handling unwanted sexual advances. The author draws thoughtful parallels between lobbying and sex work, questioning why one is accepted while the other isn't. She shares her travel philosophy of preferring to meet ordinary people over visiting monuments. On American politics, she distinguishes between a country's policies and its people, noting that coastal cities don't reflect national stereotypes. The final letter addresses sexual harassment with practical, escalating advice.
key_points: Legalizing sex work would make it safer for workers (ability to report crimes, demand health protections) but could also spread the profession's inherent dangers more widely
The author's travel philosophy prioritizes meeting ordinary people and observing daily life over museums and monuments - 'I'd rather see their markets than their art'
There's a distinction between a country's policies and its people - Europeans shouldn't assume all Americans support controversial US policies, just as racism exists in Europe despite better policies
When rejecting unwanted advances, explicit verbal rejection ('I said no') is essential - hints and social context are insufficient for many men
The author advocates for escalating consequences when boundaries are violated, including threatening to involve the harasser's partner or seek a restraining order
money_quotes: I wasn't sure whether, after that first sentence, you wanted lobbyists banned or sex workers made legal. To tell you the truth I coulda gone either way.
Going back to the original question, how about we just outlaw lobbyists?
I don't like museums or monuments or historical sites, I never have. I like talking to the people currently living somewhere, and I like exploring how they actually live day to day.
I'd rather see their markets than their art.

While it's reasonable to avoid living in the U.S. because of its insane policies, especially as a non-citizen, it's of course not reasonable to assume that every city is filled with people who are insane.
Many many men do not hear 'no' until you actually say that word.
Make it clear to him that it's really not OK, the way he's been acting, and that you are willing to risk real discomfort in relationships (especially his) to get this resolved.
posts.key_insight: Social problems often persist because we accept comfortable hypocrisies (like tolerating lobbyists but not sex workers) and avoid the explicit confrontations necessary to change them.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil highlights Sir Andrew Wiles' criticism of mathematics being misused in the 2008 financial crisis, particularly through complex derivatives manipulation by banks. Wiles argued that math's reputation has been tarnished and that the field has drifted 'towards goals that you might not believe in.' O'Neil celebrates hearing a prominent mathematician acknowledge these ethical concerns. She notes the irony that the building named after Wiles was funded by Landon Clay, whose wealth came from investment management—precisely the industry Wiles was criticizing.
key_points: Wiles publicly stated that mathematics was abused during the financial meltdown through banks' manipulation of complex derivatives
He called for mathematicians to 'protect the good name' of their discipline from misuse
O'Neil sees this as validation for her proposed panel to investigate public math models
The Andrew Wiles Building was funded by an investment management CEO, creating an ironic tension with Wiles' critique
Wealth management tools fall squarely into what O'Neil calls 'weapons of math destruction'
money_quotes: One has to be aware now that mathematics can be misused and that we have to protect its good name.
Mathematics research, which used to be removed from day-to-day life, has diverged 'towards goals that you might not believe in'.
The abuse of mathematics during the global financial meltdown in 2009, particularly by banks' manipulation of complex derivatives, had tarnished his chosen subject's reputation.
Scientists used to worry about the ethical repercussions of their work.
In my experience, wealth management tools are definitely in the 'weapons of math destruction' toolbox.
posts.key_insight: When one of the world's most celebrated pure mathematicians acknowledges that math is being weaponized for profit, it validates growing concerns about the ethical responsibilities of the mathematical community.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her participation in an Americans for Financial Reform event focused on financial transparency. The event, 'Opening Wall Street's Black Box,' will examine progress made since the 2008 financial crisis in improving transparency in the financial system. The post highlights that despite regulatory efforts five years after the crisis, it remains unclear how much transparency has actually improved. The event features regulators, market participants, and academics discussing how better information and disclosure can make finance both fairer and safer.
key_points: The 2008 financial crisis revealed that regulators and market participants were blind to major systemic risks
Lack of financial transparency enabled large-scale fraud and contributed to market failure after Lehman Brothers collapsed
Five years post-crisis, it's unclear whether regulatory efforts have meaningfully improved transparency
The event brings together diverse voices including regulators, journalists, and academics to assess progress
money_quotes: The 2008 financial crisis revealed that regulators and many sophisticated market participants were in the dark about major risks and exposures in our financial system.

The lack of financial transparency enabled large-scale fraud and deception of investors, weakened the stability of the financial system, and contributed to the market failure after the collapse of Lehman Brothers.
Five years later, despite regulatory efforts, it's not clear how much the situation has improved.
How can better information and disclosure make the financial system both fairer and safer?
posts.key_insight: Five years after the 2008 financial crisis exposed dangerous opacity in the financial system, meaningful progress toward transparency remains uncertain despite regulatory efforts.

posts.summary: The post argues that as algorithms increasingly control important aspects of society—from Yelp reviews to teacher evaluations to political campaigns—we need new methods to understand their biases and hold them accountable. While transparency is the popular solution, it has significant limitations: legal frameworks don't compel algorithmic disclosure, corporations protect trade secrets, and algorithmic complexity creates cognitive overload. The author proposes 'algorithmic accountability reporting' as a complementary approach, using reverse engineering techniques to probe black-box systems by varying inputs and observing outputs. Examples from the Wall Street Journal, Daily Beast, and academic research demonstrate this method in practice, though the author notes that correlation doesn't equal intention—we still need traditional journalism to understand the motivations behind algorithmic behavior.
key_points: Algorithms are the new power brokers in society, affecting everything from online reviews to markets to immigration enforcement, yet they remain opaque and biased
Transparency as a solution has fundamental limits: no legal requirement for algorithmic disclosure, corporate trade secret protection, and the cognitive complexity of explaining algorithms
Algorithmic accountability reporting through reverse engineering—varying inputs and observing outputs—can reveal how black-box systems work without access to their code
Reverse engineering can diagnose problems but cannot determine intent; traditional investigative journalism is still needed to understand why algorithms behave as they do
Methods for algorithmic accountability are still nascent and messy, requiring formalization and teaching as algorithms grow more powerful
money_quotes: Algorithms, driven by vast troves of data, are the new power brokers in society, both in the corporate world as well as in government.
They have biases like the rest of us. And they make mistakes. But they're opaque, hiding their secrets behind layers of complexity.
Algorithms are essentially black boxes, exposing an input and output without betraying any of their inner organs.

You can't see what's going on inside directly, but if you vary the inputs in enough different ways and pay close attention to the outputs, you can start piecing together some likeness for how the algorithm transforms each input into an output.
The black box starts to divulge some secrets.

Correlation does not equal intention. As much as algorithmic accountability can help us diagnose the existence of a problem, we have to go deeper and do more journalistic-style reporting to understand the motivations or intentions behind an algorithm.

Despite the fact that forward thinkers like Larry Lessig have been writing for some time about how code is a lever on behavior, we're still in the early days of developing methods for holding that code and its influence accountable.
posts.key_insight: When algorithms won't reveal their secrets, reverse engineering through systematic input-output testing offers a practical method for accountability, though understanding intent still requires traditional investigative journalism.

posts.summary: The post examines two NYT articles about empathy: one showing rich people have less empathy due to paying less attention to others, and another suggesting reading literary fiction can improve 'theory of mind.' O'Neil connects these findings to her experience at D.E. Shaw, where she observed a disconnect between finance workers and regular people. She notes the studies' limitation in measuring only in-person empathy, when in reality rich people rarely share spaces with non-rich people. She concludes with a satirical proposal to make unempathetic people read Chekhov before conversations.
key_points: Rich people's lack of empathy may stem from not paying attention to others, creating a negative feedback loop with inequality
Reading literary fiction like Chekhov can temporarily improve 'theory of mind' - both cognitive and affective
Finance industry workers exhibit a 'I'm smarter so I deserve to be rich' attitude that reflects empathy deficits
The studies' focus on in-person empathy misses the bigger problem: rich people rarely share physical spaces with non-rich people
Empathy may be tweakable as a basic human attribute, offering some hope for intervention
money_quotes: For me empathy is like smell, and some people are incredibly smelly, especially on the subway.
Maybe people who are not empathetic are attracted to working at a hedge fund.

There's a tremendous disconnect between regular people and the attitude of finance people, along the lines of 'I'm smarter than those people so I deserve to be rich', and I ascribe much of this disconnect to a lack of empathy.
In the real world, even in New York, rich people don't spend lots of time in the same room with anyone except other rich people.
I like that it's being considered as a basic attribute of interest, and that it seems tweakable.
Before talking to someone I perceive as unempathetic, I will bust out a Chekhov short story and demand they read it on the spot.
posts.key_insight: The empathy gap between rich and poor is self-reinforcing: wealth insulates people from exposure to others' struggles, while lack of empathy justifies the systems that create inequality.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her transition from finance to data science, noting her surprise at the uncritical enthusiasm surrounding big data. She argues that data science is essentially applying the same techniques finance used for decades, now to manipulate people rather than markets. The post warns that data scientists need far more skepticism about their power, as the stakes have shifted from market manipulation to human manipulation, making modelers' responsibilities greater than ever.
key_points: The 'big data revolution' is largely finance techniques being applied to new domains like advertising
Data science community lacks the healthy skepticism that the field desperately needs
Manipulating people through models is scarier than manipulating markets
Data scientists have greater responsibility than ever due to their power over human behavior
money_quotes: I left finance pretty disgusted with the whole thing, and because I needed to make money and because I'm a nerd, I pretty quickly realized I could rebrand myself a 'data scientist' and get a pretty cool job
I was kind of shocked by how positive everyone was about the 'big data revolution' and the 'power of data science.'
Big data has the potential to really revolutionize the way we live our lives for the better––or sometimes not. It really depends.
From my perspective, this was, in tenor if not in the details, the same stuff we'd been doing in finance for a couple of decades
Whereas in finance we need to worry about models manipulating the market, in data science we need to worry about models manipulating people, which is in fact scarier
Modelers, if anything, have a bigger responsibility now than ever before
posts.key_insight: Data science is finance's model-driven manipulation repackaged for human targets, making skepticism and ethical responsibility more urgent than ever.

posts.summary: The post describes a data visualization technique called cumulative covariance plotting, which reveals how correlations between variables evolve over time. O'Neil explains the mathematical basis: plotting the running sum of products of demeaned variables shows upward trends for positive correlations and downward trends for negative ones. She shares a case study where a U-shaped plot revealed biased data at the beginning and end of her dataset. After removing the contaminated data, she discovered a positive correlation that the overall summary statistic had incorrectly shown as negative.
key_points: Cumulative covariance plots show the numerator of correlation computed over time, revealing how relationships evolve
A U-shaped cumulative plot can indicate data bias at the start and end of a time series
In financial modeling, this technique doubles as a cumulative PnL plot showing model performance over time
Summary statistics like correlation can be misleading when data contains temporal biases
Visual inspection of data over time often reveals problems that single numbers hide
money_quotes: Lots of things change over time – relationships between variables, for example – and it's often crucial to get deeply acquainted with how exactly that works with your in-sample data.
If you plot the cumulative sum, you get an upwards trend if things are positively correlated and downwards trend if things are negatively correlated.
It tells you how much money your model is making.

After getting rid of the biased data at the beginning and the end, I concluded that I had a positive correlation here, even though if I'd trusted the overall 'dirty' correlation I would have thought it was negative.
It's always better to visualize data over time than it is to believe one summary statistic like correlation.
posts.key_insight: Visualizing correlations as cumulative sums over time reveals temporal dynamics and data quality issues that summary statistics completely obscure.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on topics ranging from human anatomy and evolution, to career transitions into data science, youth sports parenting dilemmas, and career change advice. The most substantive advice concerns a data engineer wanting to break into data science, where she recommends building a portfolio with public data and getting hired as an engineer at a data-driven company to 'worm your way' into the data team. She also offers strongly-worded parenting advice against the competitive youth sports culture, advocating for giving children agency over their free time activities.
key_points: For career changers into data science: build projects with public data as a portfolio, then get hired as an engineer at a data company and prove your skills internally
Competitive youth sports culture is criticized as parents projecting existential anxiety onto children's activities
Children should have agency over their free time rather than having adults define their goals
Wanting to pursue a field you have no experience in may signal escapism rather than genuine desire
The advice column format allows for frank, sometimes profane commentary on social norms
