money_quotes: My impression is that no man has ever gotten that close to sex and then said, 'whoa, your vagina has a slightly peculiar shape and/or positioning relative to your clitoris. Maybe we should not procreate after all!!'
Try to get a job as an engineer in a data-driven business, and worm your way into the data group.
Literally, people seem to think that their kid will get into Harvard on the strength of their parents' SERIOUS COMMITMENT to youth sports.
It's even offensive to think that adults should define the goal for growing children in their free time.
What's wrong with you people, isn't it enough that these kids will probably have to live by other people's rules when they're working in jobs later? Why do we have to start that crap so soon?
Be consistently sane and give them absolute agency on these decisions.
How would you know you want to do math research if you don't have experience in math?
I'm sensing more escapism than desire in your words.
posts.key_insight: Whether in career transitions or parenting decisions, honest self-assessment and giving people agency over their own paths matters more than following prescribed formulas for success.

posts.summary: O'Neil works through a probability problem about inferring an unknown parameter from sample data drawn from a uniform distribution. Given n draws from a uniform distribution [0, θ], she shows how to estimate θ using only the maximum observed value. By reframing the problem as asking where the rightmost dart lands when throwing n darts at [0,1], she derives through integration that the expected maximum is n/(n+1). The solution involves recognizing symmetry, using induction from simple cases, and computing n-fold integrals to arrive at an elegant 'plumping factor' of (n+1)/n to multiply the observed maximum.
key_points: The problem reduces to finding the expected value of the maximum of n uniform random variables on [0,1]
Symmetry allows assuming one variable is the maximum, then multiplying by n to account for all orderings
The expected maximum of n uniform draws is n/(n+1), approaching 1 as n grows
To estimate the true parameter θ, multiply the observed maximum by the 'plumping factor' (n+1)/n
Building from n=1 case (answer: 1/2) helps develop intuition before tackling the general proof
money_quotes: Someone asked me a math question the other day and I had fun figuring it out.
In other words, given n what's your best guess for θ?
Because I'm a basically violent person, I like to think of as throws of a dart.
It's obvious from this phrasing that, as n goes to infinity, you can expect a dart to get closer and closer to 1.
Take the maximum value in your collection of draws and multiply it by the plumping factor (n+1)/n to get a best estimate of the parameter.
posts.key_insight: When estimating the upper bound of a uniform distribution from n samples, multiply the observed maximum by (n+1)/n to correct for the systematic underestimation inherent in using sample maxima.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces an Indiegogo crowdfunding campaign to print a second edition of 'Occupy Finance,' a book produced by her Occupy Wall Street-affiliated group. The first print run is nearly sold out and contained some typos that have been corrected. The goal is to raise $3,500 to print 5,000 copies, which they plan to distribute free to libraries, bookstores, and individuals rather than sell, ensuring access regardless of financial means. The post introduces Marni, the group's Action Committee Head and campaign spokesperson.
key_points: The first edition of Occupy Finance sold out quickly despite being rushed to meet the September 17th anniversary deadline
The group prioritizes free distribution over sales to ensure universal access to financial education
The crowdfunding goal of $3,500 would fund 5,000 copies for libraries and direct distribution
The project reflects Occupy Wall Street's grassroots, community-funded approach to financial literacy
money_quotes: We'd prefer to raise money for the printing and then give them away over selling them, since we'd like anyone who wants one to have one regardless of their financial situation.
Here's my personal evidence, for which I was nearly arrested in the post office (who knew you're not allowed to take pictures in the post office? not me.)
There was a missing page and a few other typos for which we have forgiven ourselves, since we got it out in time for September 17th.
posts.key_insight: Financial education should be freely accessible to everyone regardless of their economic circumstances, reflecting the democratizing ethos of the Occupy movement.

posts.summary: O'Neil highlights Lisa Servon's New Yorker article arguing that traditional banks may actually be worse for poor people than payday lenders. The piece challenges the conventional wisdom that payday loans are predatory by comparing them to bank overdraft fees, which can have effective APRs exceeding 5,000%. While payday loans charge 300-600% APR, banks have retreated from small-dollar lending entirely, leaving few alternatives. O'Neil suggests that a comprehensive cost analysis including time and travel might reveal payday lenders as a rational choice for low-income borrowers.
key_points: Bank overdraft fees, when calculated as short-term loans, carry effective APRs over 5,000% - far exceeding payday loan rates of 300-600%
Banks have largely abandoned small-dollar credit, leaving payday lenders as one of few options for low-income borrowers
The typical payday borrower takes out nine loans per year and remains indebted for more than half the year
Evaluating financial services requires considering all-in costs including time and travel, not just stated rates
The 'predatory lending' narrative may be incomplete without examining how traditional banking fails the poor
money_quotes: It makes me wonder whether, if someone did a careful analysis with all-in costs including time and travel, whether PayDay Lenders are not actually a totally rational choice for the poor.
An overdraft is essentially a short-term loan, and if it had a repayment period of seven days, the APR for a typical incident would be over five thousand per cent.
Banks have retreated from small-dollar credit, and many payday borrowers do not qualify anyway.
People criticize payday loans for their high annual percentage rates (APR), which range from three hundred per cent to six hundred per cent. Payday lenders argue that APR is the wrong measure.
posts.key_insight: The institutions we assume protect consumers may actually be more exploitative than the ones we condemn, and rigorous analysis of all-in costs can overturn conventional wisdom about predatory lending.

posts.summary: O'Neil announces an upcoming MAA Distinguished Lecture where she'll explain three approaches to building recommendation engines like Netflix's. She plans to cover latent factor models (matching user preferences to content attributes), co-visitation models (collaborative filtering based on viewing patterns), and latent topic models (identifying thematic preferences). The post is primarily logistical, noting the diverse audience of math enthusiasts and mentioning she'll also record a shorter TED-style version using Prezi.
key_points: Three recommendation engine approaches: latent factor models (attribute matching), co-visitation models (collaborative filtering), and latent topic models (thematic categorization)
The lecture will address challenges of massive-scale calculations required for recommendation systems
Prezi allows easy creation of shorter presentations by removing nodes from the path without deleting underlying material
References Nicholson Baker's controversial Harper's piece questioning the necessity of Algebra II
money_quotes: I'll be giving high-level descriptions of a latent factor model (this movie is violent and we've noticed you like violent movies), of the co-visitation model (lots of people who've seen stuff you've seen also saw this movie) and the latent topic model (we've noticed you like movies about the Hungarian 1956 Revolution).
I am psyched by how easy it is to prepare my slides for that short version using prezi, since I just removed a bunch of nodes on the path of the material without removing the material itself.
Apparently I need to read this and have an opinion.
posts.key_insight: Recommendation engines can be understood through three complementary mathematical approaches: matching content attributes to user preferences, leveraging collective viewing patterns, and identifying thematic interests.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the debate over requiring Algebra II for college admission, presenting both sides fairly before offering her own radical conclusion. The pro-algebra camp argues it promotes abstract thinking and maintains standards, while critics note it's often taught as rote memorization and causes suffering without real understanding. O'Neil points out that mathematicians defending the requirement are biased—they sailed through it easily and fear their field being devalued. She proposes a thought experiment: scrap all math requirements and rebuild based on actual value, arguing statistics and scientific literacy would serve students far better than trigonometry or poorly-taught algebra.
key_points: Algebra II's correlation with college success is circular logic—it's a prerequisite, so of course those who pass it go to college
Mathematicians are the wrong people to ask about math requirements because they had no trouble with these classes and have professional incentives to defend them
The way algebra is typically taught emphasizes memorization over genuine abstract thinking
Statistics and scientific literacy would be far more useful for citizens than trigonometry
Raising standards on paper means nothing if students are just learning to game standardized tests
money_quotes: We might as well have embroidery as a prerequisite and then be impressed by all the beautiful piano stool covers that result.
It's more like learning student's capacity for drudgery.
This is a group of people who pretty much across the board didn't have any problems whatsoever with the class in question and sailed through it.
It shouldn't surprised anyone that we have defensive and unsympathetic mathematicians on one side who cannot understand the arguments of the empathizers on the other hand.
I'm willing to get rid of all of it, all the math classes ever taught, at least as a thought experiment, and then put shit back that we think actually adds value.
It seems pretty clear we can chuck trigonometry out the window.
That'd be a pretty cool goal, to get people to be able to read the newspaper.
posts.key_insight: Rather than defending or attacking individual math requirements, we should rebuild math education from scratch based on what actually helps citizens navigate the modern world—starting with statistics and scientific literacy.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that her book 'Doing Data Science' co-authored with Rachel Schutt is now available on Kindle, while humorously noting the formatting problems with LaTeX and fonts in the e-reader version. She playfully critiques Amazon's category ranking system, which allows books to appear as #1 by drilling down into increasingly specific subcategories. The hard copy edition is set to release on October 22nd for those who prefer proper formatting. She also reveals her preferred subtitle was rejected for being too colorful.
key_points: The Kindle version has significant formatting issues with LaTeX formulas and special fonts, making the display 'comical'
Amazon's ranking system creates artificially impressive-looking bestseller rankings through increasingly narrow category drilling
The author's preferred subtitle 'the no bullshit approach' was rejected by the publisher
The hard copy edition offers better formatting for readers who want a cleaner reading experience
money_quotes: The kindle reader doesn't render latex well, or for that matter many of the various fonts we use for various reasons.
Amazon has this very generous way of funneling down into categories sufficiently so that the ranking of a given book looks really high.
#1 in Kindle Store > Kindle eBooks > Computers & Technology > Software > New Yorkers > Women nerd authors > one of whom is an occupier > & the other one grew up in New Jersey
But you, know, I'll take what I can get to be #1! It's all about metrics!!!
I wanted the full title to be something more like 'Doing Data Science: the no bullshit approach' but for some reason I was overruled. Whatevs.
posts.key_insight: Even in a simple book announcement, O'Neil can't resist poking fun at how metrics and ranking systems can be gamed to produce impressive-sounding but ultimately meaningless numbers.

posts.summary: This advice column tackles four reader questions with characteristic directness: validating authenticity over conformity for a Halloween costume, navigating a financially irresponsible parent's gift-giving, solving a probability puzzle about random pairings, and offering strategies for introverts to start conversations. The financial advice is particularly substantive, recommending the reader maintain legal distance from their mother's debts while preparing for an eventual bankruptcy. Throughout, Aunt Pythia balances practical wisdom with an underlying philosophy that values human connection and authenticity over social conventions.
key_points: Being authentically yourself is intimidating to people who hide behind social masks, but it's worth persevering
When dealing with a financially irresponsible family member, never co-sign anything or pay off their debts—let the credit system's consequences play out
The debt collection system has limits, and bankruptcy can be a legitimate resolution rather than something to prevent at all costs
Starting conversations requires projecting friendliness while not taking rejection personally—have a reason to talk, even a small one
Confidence in human connection creates a positive feedback loop that makes future connections easier
money_quotes: Let's face it, our society is filled with phonies getting up every morning and putting on costumes for work to hide their true inner selves.
Being an authentic human being is incredibly intimidating to such people, and they might be terrified when they see you.
Here's what I wouldn't do if I were you: pay off her debts. There would just be more where those came from.
Think of that as a separate issue from her piling-up debts, which is in the end the problem of the banks that gave her credit cards she couldn't be trusted with.
I don't feel bad for being friendly and wanting to connect with someone, because that is frankly what humans do and it's not something to be ashamed of.
The more confident you are that most people are friendly and open, the higher your chances are of making a connection.
posts.key_insight: Personal responsibility has limits—sometimes the wisest course is to let systems (whether credit markets or social conventions) bear the consequences of their own failures rather than rescuing them at your own expense.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil discusses Occupy the SEC's lawsuit against federal regulators for failing to implement the Volcker Rule on time, as required by Dodd-Frank. The court dismissed the case, ruling that citizens don't have legal standing to sue over regulatory delays—only the banks being regulated do. O'Neil argues this reveals a fundamental asymmetry in financial regulation: the people most affected by systemic risk (taxpayers who bail out banks) have no legal recourse to demand accountability, while the regulated entities control the conversation. She calls for better legal representation for average citizens in regulatory matters.
key_points: Occupy the SEC sued federal regulators for missing the deadline to implement the Volcker Rule, a key Dodd-Frank provision meant to function like a modern Glass-Steagall
The court ruled citizens don't have 'standing' to sue—only regulated banks can legally complain about regulations
This creates a structural problem: negative effects of poor regulation are so dispersed that individuals can't demonstrate sufficient harm to have legal recourse
Taxpayers who bail out banks paradoxically have no legal voice in how those banks are regulated
Class-action lawsuits are insufficient; there needs to be formal legal representation for taxpayers and systemic risk monitoring
money_quotes: They didn't have enough skin in the game, in other words. Because they're just, you know, citizens who care about having a functional regulatory environment.
That begs the question, who has skin in the regulation game? Answer: banks being regulated. So only those guys can complain to the courts about the regulation.

It seems like whenever I look around I see examples like this, where there are people getting away with crappy policies or even crappier deeds because it has a negative effect, but that negative effect is so dispersed that most people don't have enough 'standing' to sue.
Not to mention taxpayers who have bailed out the banks and want to avoid continuing doing that.
Who is looking out for the average non-insider? Who is keeping tabs on overall systemic risk?
In an ideal world that would exist inside the regulators themselves, but we all know it's not that ideal.
posts.key_insight: The legal doctrine of 'standing' creates a perverse asymmetry where only banks can challenge financial regulations, while the dispersed taxpayers who bear systemic risk have no voice in the regulatory process.

posts.summary: This post is a brief announcement of several media appearances and projects Cathy O'Neil is involved with. She mentions being a guest on the Disorderly Conduct podcast discussing how to rein in Wall Street, promotes the Occupy Finance video series featuring author interviews, and announces the release of her book 'Doing Data Science' from O'Reilly. The post serves primarily as a collection of links and updates rather than presenting substantive arguments.
key_points: O'Neil participated in a podcast segment answering 'What is the single best way to rein in Wall Street?' in three minutes
The Occupy Finance book club meetings are ongoing, with video interviews of authors being posted to YouTube
Her book 'Doing Data Science' was released by O'Reilly on the date of this post
money_quotes: What is the single best way to rein in the power of Wall Street?
And when I say 'hard copy,' keep in mind that all of O'Reilly's books are soft cover.
posts.key_insight: This is an announcement post sharing links to O'Neil's various media appearances and the release of her data science book, reflecting her involvement in both financial reform activism and technical education.

posts.summary: Chetty's NYT op-ed defending economics as a science conflates two distinct issues: whether economics can be approached scientifically, and whether economists should push their uncertain findings as definitive policy prescriptions. The author argues that true scientists acknowledge uncertainty and large error bars, but economists often present fragile results as facts to influence policy. Ironically, Chetty's comparison to epidemiology backfires since that field has been shown to produce results that flip signs based on trivial methodological changes. The post concludes that while asking hard economic questions is valuable, actual scientists remain skeptical of their own work and don't pretend their error bars are small enough for high-impact policy decisions.
key_points: Economics conflates scientific inquiry with political advocacy when economists push uncertain estimates as policy facts
The Reinhart-Rogoff debacle exemplifies how economists fail to acknowledge that different initial conditions produce wildly different results
Epidemiology is a poor defense for economics since both fields suffer from results that change sign based on trivial methodological choices
True science requires releasing data, acknowledging uncertainty, and admitting when error bars are too large to draw conclusions
Asking hard macroeconomic questions is valuable, but pretending to have definitive answers is not science
money_quotes: I'd have no problem with economists if they behaved like the people in the following completely made-up story
You see how that works? It's called science, and it's not what economists are known to do.
Instead we have economists who basically get paid to write papers pushing for certain policies.

Chetty defends economics as 'just as much science' as epidemiology, which I would claim is in the category 'not at all a science.' In the end I guess I'd have to agree with him, but not in a good way.

It's just crucial to remember that actual scientists are skeptical, even of their own work, and don't pretend to have error bars small enough to make high-impact policy decisions based on their fragile results.
posts.key_insight: The problem with economics isn't that it asks scientific questions, but that economists present highly uncertain results as definitive enough to drive policy, which is politics masquerading as science.

posts.summary: This post announces an Occupy Wall Street action to demand raising the minimum wage to $15 per hour, timed to coincide with the 75th anniversary of the federal minimum wage law. O'Neil provides context through charts showing the historical federal minimum wage and state-by-state variations. She argues that the current $7.25 federal minimum is insufficient for basic living, citing a Bloomberg Businessweek article that frames Walmart's low-wage strategy as a taxpayer subsidy. The core argument is that corporations externalize labor costs onto society while reaping profits.
key_points: The Occupy movement is demanding a $15/hour minimum wage on the 75th anniversary of the minimum wage law
State minimum wages vary but federal law sets the floor at $7.25
Low-wage employers like Walmart externalize costs to taxpayers through workers' need for public assistance
Corporate low-wage strategies harm not just workers but economic growth broadly
money_quotes: I've never done the math on how it would be even close to possible to live on an hourly wage of $7.25 but it's clearly not possible to, say, budget for emergencies even in the most frugal of approaches.
While employers like Wal-Mart seek to reap significant profits through the depression of labor costs, the social costs of this low-wage strategy are externalized.
Low wages not only harm workers and their families—they cost taxpayers.
Walmart is subsidized by taxpayers and is a drag on growth.
posts.key_insight: Low-wage corporate strategies don't just exploit workers—they shift the true cost of labor onto taxpayers, making poverty wages a public subsidy for corporate profits.

posts.summary: The post argues that the American higher education system has become a costly arms race where students continually escalate spending on degrees to improve their relative ranking, but like neighbors competing to have the nicest cars, no one actually gains lasting advantage. O'Neil draws on Tom Slee's book to explain how arms races require collective agreements to resolve, but the education system's many stakeholders—employers who benefit from over-credentialed candidates, colleges that love administrative growth, and students rationally pursuing rankings—all have incentives to continue the escalation. She criticizes a Third Way proposal to reduce tuition through administrative cuts as unrealistic while dismissing minimum wage increases, calling this prioritization backwards. Her suggested solution is widespread free or near-free basic education, though she acknowledges the political difficulty of achieving any collective agreement.
key_points: Education functions as an arms race where escalating costs don't change relative rankings, just like neighbors competing to own the nicest cars
All major stakeholders benefit from the current dysfunctional system: employers get overqualified workers, colleges expand administration, and individual students rationally pursue higher rankings
Federal loan programs combined with non-dischargeable student debt have created a feedback loop driving up tuition and student debt
Proposed 'solutions' like pegging government aid to graduation rates would create perverse incentives without addressing the underlying arms race dynamics
Raising the minimum wage would more directly address income inequality than trying to push more people through an expensive credentialing system
money_quotes: They often involve relative rankings.

From the point of view of employers, rankings are great and they can be sure to pick the highest ranked individuals from that system, even if that means – as it often does – having Ph.D. graduates working in mailrooms.
In other words, it's a shitshow, and possibly the only real disruption that could improve it would be widespread and universally respected basic and free-ish education.
From the point of view of the colleges, they're getting to hire more and more administrators, which means growth, which they love.
Am I the only one who thinks raising the minimum wage would help more to address income inequality and is easier to imagine working?
So don't expect any help from them to add sanity to this system.
posts.key_insight: Higher education has become a collective action problem where everyone rationally escalates their investment in credentials, but because it's a zero-sum ranking system, the only winners are the institutions extracting money from the arms race.

posts.summary: This extremely brief post captures a moment of political frustration where the author abandons her usual analytical approach in favor of raw emotion. She references Russell Brand's viral interview calling for revolution against economic inequality and political disengagement. The post serves as a visceral endorsement of the Occupy Wall Street movement's continued relevance. Rather than offering her typical data-driven commentary, O'Neil lets her anger speak for itself.
key_points: Sometimes anger is more appropriate than analysis
The author aligns herself with revolutionary political movements like Occupy Wall Street
Russell Brand's call for systemic change resonated with her frustration
Brief statements can be powerful political acts
money_quotes: I'm too angry today to dole out advice.
Instead I think I'll join a revolution.
posts.key_insight: There are moments when intellectual analysis must yield to righteous anger and direct action.

posts.summary: The post explains Capital Appreciation Bonds (CABs), a municipal financing instrument that allows towns to borrow money now while deferring all payments for 30-40 years, resulting in payouts 7-20 times the original loan. O'Neil highlights how CABs exploit an accounting loophole where only the original loan amount appears on balance sheets as a liability, not the eventual payout, allowing desperate or corrupt town officials to circumvent debt limits. She uses California school districts as examples, where $190 million borrowed will cost $1 billion when due. The post situates this within a broader critique of Wall Street's exploitation of unsophisticated local governments.
key_points: CABs are zero-coupon bonds with very long terms (30-40 years) where municipalities pay nothing until maturity, then owe massive sums due to compound interest
A key accounting loophole allows towns to record only the original loan amount as debt, not the eventual payout, helping them evade debt limits
The power imbalance between sophisticated Wall Street banks and 'temporary, ignorant, and desperate' local officials creates a toxic environment for municipal financing
Some states like Michigan and Ohio have banned CABs, while California is attempting restrictions, but bank lobbying creates 'serious headwinds'
The structure incentivizes short-term thinking since officials who approve these bonds won't be in office—and might be dead—when the bill comes due
money_quotes: If you add into that mix the fact that local town officials are often temporary, ignorant, and desperate, we get a toxic environment.
200 California school and community college districts issuing these bonds will end up paying 10 to 20 times more than they borrowed.
If the cash flows are the same for two different kinds of bonds, how do you get to account for them differently?

CABs are very attractive indeed, because it looks kind of innocuous and their overall debt limits don't get breached even though they've essentially sold the future of the town to a big Wall Street bank.
Plus they won't be in office when that bill comes due, and they might well be dead.
Given how much money they make for big banks, there are serious headwinds for reasonable rules.
posts.key_insight: Capital Appreciation Bonds exploit an accounting loophole that lets municipalities hide their true debt burden, effectively allowing current officials to mortgage their town's future for short-term relief while Wall Street profits enormously.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on unpublished collaborative work with Manjul Bhargava on '3x3x3 Rubik's cubes' - a mathematical construction involving 27 numbers in a 3D configuration that produces genus one curves. She explains how dividing such a cube along different axes yields three related cubic equations with interesting relationships. After leaving academia, she passed her notes to grad student Wei Ho, who collaborated with Bhargava to publish a preprint incorporating that work. The paper establishes foundations for understanding average sizes of Selmer groups of elliptic curves, a significant number theory result.
key_points: A 3x3x3 matrix of numbers can be divided along three axes to produce three different genus one curves with interesting mathematical relationships
O'Neil left academic math in 2007 with regret about not publishing this collaborative work with Manjul Bhargava
Her notes were passed to Wei Ho, who co-authored a preprint with Bhargava that incorporates sections of O'Neil's original work
The paper serves as groundwork for determining average sizes of Selmer groups for families of elliptic curves
O'Neil plans to have Wei Ho explain the new results to her as a neighbor during Ho's Columbia post-doc
money_quotes: One of my biggest regrets when I left academic math and number theory behind in 2007 was that I never finished writing up and publishing some cool results I'd been working on with Manjul Bhargava.

Just talking about that stuff gets me excited, because it's first of all a really natural construction, second of all number theoretic, and third of all it actually makes me think of solving Rubik's cubes, which I've always loved.
Turns out there are crazy interesting relationships between those curves, as well as in the space of all 3x3x3 cubes.
I gave my notes to a grad student Wei Ho when I left math.
Hopefully I will remember enough number theory to understand her!
posts.key_insight: Mathematical research lives on even when individual careers change direction - collaborative work can find completion through the next generation of scholars.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her talk at Harvard's Applied Statistics workshop, noting how her presentation evolved from focusing on data skepticism in industry to addressing broader public concerns. She describes how 'creepy models' create feedback loops that degrade culture and democracy in pursuit of short-term profits. The post connects her academic presentation to her train reading about Rupert Murdoch, suggesting a thematic link between algorithmic manipulation and media power.
key_points: Data skepticism affects both industry professionals and the general public
Algorithmic models create long-term feedback loops that harm democracy and culture
Short-term profit motives drive the deployment of problematic data systems
There's a connection between 'creepy' algorithmic practices and media manipulation
money_quotes: I talk about creepy models and how there are multiple longterm feedback loops having a degrading effect on culture and democracy in the name of short-term profits.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic systems optimized for short-term profits create insidious feedback loops that gradually erode democratic culture.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post celebrating being in Boston, presumably during the Red Sox's 2013 World Series victory when David Ortiz (Big Papi) was the MVP. The post consists of a single sentence expressing excitement about the timing of being in the city. It captures a moment of local sports enthusiasm rather than O'Neil's typical analytical content.
key_points: The post celebrates being in Boston during a significant moment (likely the 2013 World Series win)
David Ortiz ('Big Papi') is referenced affectionately in the title
This represents a rare personal/lighthearted post rather than O'Neil's usual analytical work
money_quotes: Kind of awesome to be in Boston today.
posts.key_insight: Even data scientists take breaks from algorithm critique to celebrate hometown sports victories.

posts.summary: O'Neil debunks the common argument that people willingly trade their private data for internet services, making it a fair exchange that doesn't require regulation. She argues this framing is a fallacy because users have no real understanding of what they're trading away—they don't know about data aggregation, re-identification techniques, or how their information will be used for credit and employment decisions years later. The 'trade' is actually a trick that exploits human short-sightedness and addiction to immediate gratification. Clicking 'yes' on a privacy form is not informed consent and should not be treated as a political statement against privacy regulation.
key_points: The 'informed market participant' model completely breaks down when applied to data privacy—users lack basic knowledge about data brokers, re-identification, and downstream uses
Data given away today has future consequences users aren't considering, from credit decisions to employment screening
The asymmetry of information between tech companies and users makes this exchange fundamentally unfair, not a legitimate trade
Clicking 'yes' on privacy forms represents impulse and ignorance, not meaningful consent or a political stance against regulation
money_quotes: The real problem is that people generally have no clue what it is they're trading.

This idea of a omniscient market participant with perfect information making a well-informed trade, which we've already seen is not the case in the actual market, is doubly or triply not the case when you think about young people giving away private data for the sake of a phone app.
People are ignorant of what they're giving away to play Candy Crush Saga.
It's a trick which plays on our own impulses and short-sightedness and possibly even a kind of addiction to shiny toys in the form of candy.
If you give me your future, I'll give you a shiny toy to play with right now.

People who click 'yes' are not signaling that they've thought deeply about the consequences of giving their data away, and they are certainly not making the definitive political statement that we don't need privacy regulation.
posts.key_insight: The data-for-services 'trade' is not a fair market exchange but a trick exploiting information asymmetry and human short-sightedness, and should not be used to argue against privacy regulation.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses three substantive questions: a woman dealing with a hypercritical boyfriend, someone asking about work-life balance and productivity, and a math professor complaining about socially awkward colleagues at conferences. Aunt Pythia advises the first writer to recognize her boyfriend's criticisms as projections of his own insecurities and reclaim her power in the relationship. For the productivity question, she shares her secrets: prioritizing sleep, saying no to most things, being ruthlessly efficient without perfectionism, and surrounding herself with trusted collaborators. The final response turns the professor's complaints back on him, suggesting his harsh judgment of colleagues as 'cripples' reveals his own social anxiety and insecurity.
key_points: Criticism often reveals the critic's own insecurities - 'people accuse you of the thing they are guilty of'
Prioritizing sleep and saying no to most commitments enables higher productivity and engagement with chosen activities
Ruthless efficiency means accepting imperfection: publish quickly, prepare talks in 1.5x their length, and move on from failures
Anxiety about social situations often manifests as harsh judgment of others
Building resilience and a strong support system allows you to take risks on trusting people
money_quotes: People accuse you of the thing they are guilty of.
I say 'no' almost all the time to almost everything, so I can spend more time doing stuff I love like watching Star Trek: Deep Space 9 with my kids and taking naps.
I generally think people shortchange their own sleep thinking it will make them more efficient, when in fact it does the opposite.
If things suck, I take it in stride, make a mental note to myself, and move on. In other words, I'm ruthlessly efficient and my skin is thick.
I give myself 1.5 times as long to prepare a talk as the talk will last, a trick I learned from my teaching experience.
There are assholes in math, but they are typically pretty minor league compared to, say, the finance assholes or the Silicon Valley assholes.
You are accusing these guys of all sorts of things that you yourself are guilty of.
posts.key_insight: The flaws we criticize most harshly in others often mirror our own insecurities, and recognizing this projection is the first step toward both self-improvement and healthier relationships.

posts.summary: O'Neil highlights that many problems that caused the 2008 financial crisis remain unaddressed, including Too Big To Fail banks, lack of prosecution for financial crimes, conflicted credit rating agencies, and the still-manipulable LIBOR system. She focuses on LIBOR as a potentially solvable problem, noting that hundreds of trillions of dollars in derivatives reference this rate that's still calculated by simply asking traders what they've been paying. She proposes a thought experiment: could we design a replacement benchmark that is transparent, based on public data, yet tied to a market large enough to resist manipulation? She questions whether bank-specific credit rates are even necessary, suggesting market-wide credit measures might serve most derivative holders better.
key_points: Five years after the crisis, Too Big To Fail and Too Big To Jail remain unaddressed systemic risks
LIBOR is still calculated by asking traders what they paid, with answers taken on faith despite proven fraud
Any LIBOR replacement must be public, manipulation-resistant, and based on a market too large to game profitably
Existing LIBOR contracts could be grandfathered out while new contracts use a better benchmark
The fundamental question is whether bank-specific credit rates matter, or if market-wide credit measures would suffice
money_quotes: It's kind of hard to believe, but it's true: many of the problems that led up to the financial crisis are still with us and simply haven't been addressed.
In fact they're so big they can engage in criminal activity and not fear prosecution.
They've been asked 'not be located in close proximity to traders who primarily deal in derivative products' based on Libor. That'll work, because nobody know how to text!
A huge pile – hundreds of trillions of dollars worth – of derivatives in the form of futures, swaps, and loans are tied to the LIBOR.
It should be public data, so we don't have manipulation behind the scenes.
If we base the rate on a smallish if transparent market, that would just invite market manipulation for that small market.
posts.key_insight: LIBOR reform requires finding a benchmark that balances transparency with scale—public enough to prevent hidden manipulation, yet based on a market large enough that gaming it wouldn't be profitable.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil recommends a Bill Moyers interview with Yves Smith and Dean Baker about the Trans Pacific Partnership (TPP), a trade agreement being negotiated in secret. She argues the 'free trade' label is misleading since the treaty primarily protects corporate interests and extends patents rather than reducing trade barriers. O'Neil expresses particular concern about financial regulation provisions that could create a 'race to the bottom' and pharmaceutical protectionism. She highlights a WikiLeaks-sponsored crowdfunded bounty to incentivize insiders to leak the treaty's contents.
key_points: The TPP is being negotiated secretly between the U.S. and Pacific-bordering nations
The 'free trade' framing is misleading—the treaty focuses more on corporate protections and patent extensions than trade barrier reduction
Financial regulation provisions could lead to weakened standards across participating countries
Pharmaceutical protectionism is identified as potentially the worst aspect of the treaty
WikiLeaks offered a crowdsourced bounty (over $61,000) for insiders willing to leak treaty contents
money_quotes: I recommend it for everyone interested in learning about the secret 'free trade' agreement currently under negotiation between the U.S. and a bunch of other countries which touch the Pacific Ocean.
It's really more about protecting corporate interests and extending patents than about reducing obstacles to trade.
I'm particularly outraged by the financial regulation part of the treaty, which sound like a race to the bottom in terms of common laws between the countries.
Probably the worst part of the treaty is related to pharmaceutical protectionism.
posts.key_insight: Secret 'free trade' agreements like the TPP are often vehicles for corporate protectionism and regulatory arbitrage rather than genuine efforts to reduce trade barriers.

posts.summary: O'Neil addresses the fundamental challenge of knowing when a data modeling problem has been truly 'solved.' She argues that data problems are rarely binary - while you might achieve business success (going from losing to making money), the underlying model is almost never optimal. Good modelers should set up monitors to track model quality over time and accept that models will inevitably degrade. She warns against the temptation to oversell results to ambiguity-averse CEOs, noting that the most honest data scientists often look worse because they never claim to be finished.
key_points: Data problems are rarely truly 'solved' - you can achieve an edge over random, but it's nearly impossible to know if you've achieved the best possible edge
Model quality should be measured by comparing performance against random baselines, using metrics like likelihood ratios for classification
All models degrade over time, even with regular updates, so continuous monitoring is essential
Corporate pressure from CEOs who hate ambiguity can tempt data scientists to overclaim their results
Honest data scientists may appear less competent because they refuse to declare problems 'solved'
money_quotes: It's almost never possible to tell if a data problem is truly solved.
Most of the time you're trying to achieve something better than random, some kind of edge. Often an edge is enough, but it's nearly impossible to know if you've gotten the biggest edge possible.
You can be a bad modeler or a good modeler, and either way when you try to solve a specific problem you won't really know if you did the best possible job you could have.
You should always assume that, over time, your model will degrade, even if you are updating it regularly or even automatically.
It's of course good to know how crappy things are getting so you don't have a false sense of accomplishment.
CEOs are trained to hate ambiguity, as well as trained to never hesitate.
Some of the most honest data people end up looking like crappy scientists because they never claim to be finished doing their job.
posts.key_insight: The most honest approach to data science is accepting that models are never truly 'solved' - they provide edges over random, degrade over time, and require continuous monitoring rather than declarations of victory.

posts.summary: This is a very brief post where Cathy O'Neil shares a link to a Tumblr page featuring math-themed pick-up lines, suggested to her by mathematician Wei Ho. The post is lighthearted and playful, offering nerdy humor rather than substantive analysis. It represents the casual, community-building side of math blogging.
key_points: Links to a Tumblr page collecting math pick-up lines
Credited to mathematician Wei Ho from Columbia
Positioned as potentially helpful for 'nerds' in social situations
money_quotes: I am thinking it might help out a lot of nerds.
posts.key_insight: Even serious math bloggers occasionally share lighthearted content that celebrates nerd culture and mathematical humor.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques Alan Greenspan's post-crisis mea culpa in Foreign Affairs, arguing that his proposed fix of adding behavioral economics to existing models misses the fundamental problem. She contends that Greenspan treats financial firms as unified rational actors when the real issue is the misaligned incentives of individuals within those firms. Bankers weren't irrational—they were rationally maximizing their bonuses while externalizing systemic risk. O'Neil argues that the solution isn't fancier mathematical models but simpler rules that align individual incentives with both firm and public interests, ultimately making finance 'more boring and less profitable.'
key_points: Greenspan's error is treating financial firms as single rational actors rather than collections of individuals with their own incentives
Individual bankers behaved entirely rationally given their incentive structures—maximizing bonuses while systemic risk was someone else's problem
Adding behavioral economics 'bells and whistles' to failed models is a mathematical band-aid, not a real fix
Adam Smith himself argued against large firms where individual and corporate agendas diverge
The solution is simple rule changes to align incentives, not more sophisticated models
money_quotes: He is still talking about financial firms as if they are single people.
Financial firms were not 'mistaken', because legal contracts can't think.
Everyone was furiously getting their bonuses pumped up while the getting was good.
People on the inside knew the market for exotic financial products would blow at some point, and that their personal risks were limited, so why not make systemic risk worse until then.
It bugs me to try to put a mathematical band-aid on an inherently failed model.
I'm using the word 'rules' instead of 'regulations' because people don't hate rules as much as they hate regulations.
They involve really simple rule changes and, generally speaking, making finance much more boring and much less profitable.
posts.key_insight: The financial crisis wasn't caused by irrational firms but by rational individuals whose personal incentives were perfectly aligned with creating systemic risk—a problem no amount of sophisticated modeling can fix without changing the underlying rules.

posts.summary: This advice column installment covers four reader questions spanning text editors, probability puzzles, topological data analysis, and nerd stereotypes. Aunt Pythia defends emacs while questioning the 'macho crap' around vim's keyboard-only approach. She provides a detailed visual solution to the two-daughter probability problem, arriving at 13/27. On topological data analysis, she shares her own related idea about finding holes in data manifolds. She concludes by apologizing for reinforcing stereotypes about nerds and sports while coining terms 'nerock' and 'jord' for athletic nerds.
key_points: The preference for keyboard-only text editors like vim is questioned as unnecessary machismo rather than genuine efficiency gain
The two-daughter probability problem (with Friday birth condition) has a precise answer of 13/27, solvable through careful bin-counting in a 14x14 grid
Topological data analysis could potentially identify impossible outcomes by finding 'holes' in high-dimensional data manifolds
Stereotyping nerds as necessarily bad at sports or other activities is unfair and 'fundamentally un-nerdy'
money_quotes: As an aside, I'd like to argue this point, because it's often shrouded in weird macho crap: why not use your mouse?
I honestly have never been prevented from coding efficiently because my arm is too tired from moving from the keyboard to the mouse and back.
Is the goal really to be able to stay in the exact same position for as long as possible?
Geez I don't know about you but my rear end is not topologically equivalent to my coffee cups. You either need to go to a doctor or buy some coffee cups that don't leak.
Although, statistically I believe I still have a point, there's no reason to limit people in arbitrary ways like that, and it's fundamentally un-nerdy of me to do so.
But just for the record, nerds are categorically excellent at sex. We all know that. Say yes.
posts.key_insight: Even in playful advice-column format, rigorous thinking matters—whether solving probability puzzles with careful enumeration or questioning the unstated assumptions behind cultural preferences like text editor choices.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil responds to a high school student who doubts their mathematical ability because they're slower than peers. She argues that speed is not what makes a good mathematician—it's a 'style' difference, not a shortcoming. Math competitions create a false impression that quickness equals talent, but real mathematical work rewards patience and deep thinking. She encourages the student to ignore competitive pressures, take challenging courses like Galois Theory, and recognize that math is fundamentally a collaborative, patient discipline that welcomes those who love it.
key_points: Speed in mathematics is a style difference, not a measure of ability or potential
Math competitions create harmful false impressions about what it means to be 'good at math'
Being good at math is mostly about how much you want to spend your time doing it
As math gets more advanced and 'awesomer,' the pace naturally slows down
Mathematics is a collaborative, crowd-sourced endeavor despite media narratives about competitive geniuses
money_quotes: There's always someone faster than you.
But it's not a race. Mathematics is patient and doesn't mind.
Think of it, your slowness, or lack of quickness, as a style thing but not as a shortcoming.
In reality, mostly of being good at math is really about how much you want to spend your time doing math.
As the math gets awesomer, the speed gets slower.
Believe me, as you know more you will realize more and more how little you know.
Math is not a competitive sport. It's one of the only existing truly crowd-sourced projects of society.
It's been around a long time and is patient to be added to by you when you have the love and time and will to do so.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical ability is not about speed but about sustained curiosity and willingness to spend time thinking deeply—math is patient and collaborative, not a competitive race.

posts.summary: O'Neil draws a parallel between the financial industry's failed self-regulation that led to the 2008 crisis and the current state of big data, arguing that tech companies are following the same playbook. She critiques the notion that 'transparency' in data policies constitutes meaningful ethical behavior, pointing out that companies use complex privacy policies to shift responsibility onto consumers who lack the expertise to evaluate them. The post examines how tech companies, like banks before them, lobby against regulation while claiming the market will self-correct. O'Neil concludes that market solutions to ethics are impossible when industry incentives fundamentally conflict with public interests.
key_points: Financial deregulation failed because banks' self-interest didn't align with public good, and big data is repeating this pattern
'Transparency' in data policies is a facade that shifts burden to consumers while letting companies claim good faith
Tech companies actively lobby against regulation while claiming market forces will naturally produce ethical behavior
The complexity of data mining and algorithms provides perfect cover for obfuscation, just as complex financial instruments did
Without rules, regulations, and public leverage, industry self-regulation will never protect consumer interests
money_quotes: There never has been, nor there ever will be, a market solution for ethics so long as the basic incentives between the public and an industry are so misaligned.
What's great about letting customers know exactly what you're doing with their data is that you can then turn around and complain that customers don't understand or care about privacy policies.
It's really well set up for that since there are so many technical terms and fancy math models. Perfect for obfuscation.

If tech companies really did care about the consumer, they'd help set up reasonable guidelines and rules on these issues, which could easily be turned into law. Instead they send lobbyists to water down any and all regulation.
The result in any case is a meaningless, one-sided conversation where the tech company only hears good news.
The public needs to be represented somehow, and without rules and regulations, and without leverage of any kind, that will not happen.
posts.key_insight: Industries with fundamental incentive misalignment to the public interest will never self-regulate ethically—they will instead use complexity and 'transparency' theater to maintain the appearance of accountability while lobbying against real oversight.

posts.summary: Occupy Wall Street's Strike Debt group has purchased and forgiven nearly $15 million in medical debt affecting 2,700 people across 45 states. O'Neil celebrates this action not just for its direct financial impact, but for challenging the moral stigma attached to being in debt. She argues that medical debt—the leading cause of bankruptcy—reveals how debt often results from systemic dysfunction rather than personal failing. While acknowledging the relatively small financial dent, she emphasizes the campaign's larger cultural achievement in reshaping how Americans think about debt.
key_points: Strike Debt purchased $15 million in medical debt for pennies on the dollar and forgave it entirely
Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy in America, undermining narratives of debt as moral failure
The action reframes debt-ridden people as victims of a dysfunctional system rather than lazy promise-breakers
The campaign's greatest impact is cultural—changing how people conceptualize and moralize debt
money_quotes: By freeing people of medical debt in particular, which is the biggest cause of bankruptcy filings, it emphasizes the lie of the 'moral sin' often associated with crushing debt.

Instead of imagining poor and debt-ridden people as lazy and glibly unable to keep their promises, the Rolling Jubilee action bestows a much-needed act of compassion for some of the millions of the unlucky people in this country caught in a dysfunctional health and credit system.
Strike Debt's debt action has made huge strides in how people think about debt in this country, which is tremendously important.
posts.key_insight: The true value of debt forgiveness activism lies not in the dollars erased but in dismantling the false moral framework that blames individuals for systemic failures in healthcare and credit.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates JP Morgan Chase's disastrous Twitter Q&A session, where the bank's PR team opened themselves up to public questions using the #AskJPM hashtag. The event quickly became a viral public shaming, with critics flooding the hashtag with pointed questions about the bank's ethical failures. Fellow Occupy Wall Street activist Alexis Goldstein was highlighted as particularly effective in the takedown. The Q&A was ultimately canceled, but not before the hashtag went viral as a spectacular example of corporate PR backfiring.
key_points: JP Morgan Chase's PR team badly misjudged public sentiment by opening a Twitter Q&A
The #AskJPM hashtag was hijacked by critics and became a viral public shaming
Occupy Wall Street activists like Alexis Goldstein effectively mobilized on the platform
The event demonstrates how social media can be turned against corporate image management
money_quotes: I don't usually shill for companies but this morning I'm completely into how much of a circus my Twitter feed became
The questions asked which were tagged with #AskJPM are stunning and constitute a well-deserved public shaming of JP Morgan
Needless to say the Q&A was canceled, but not until the #AskJPM hashtag went viral in an amazing way
posts.key_insight: Social media can transform corporate PR attempts into powerful platforms for public accountability when institutions have unaddressed ethical failures.

posts.summary: In this interview, Cathy O'Neil discusses the challenges of balancing motherhood with a mathematics career, arguing that academia's rigid structure and front-loaded pressure during the postdoc years systematically disadvantages women with children. She contrasts this with industry, where people can reinvent themselves and recover from gaps without permanent career damage. O'Neil reflects on how her own mother's gift of 'privacy'—not scrutinizing her or making her self-conscious about her abilities—gave her the space to self-define. She concludes with advice for women in math to give themselves a break and remember that math is beautiful.
key_points: Academia front-loads all career stress into the 3-4 years after PhD and the tenure track period, which systematically punishes women who have children during these years
Industry allows people to reinvent themselves after setbacks or gaps in ways that academia's granular publication-tracking does not
The academic system's rigidity doesn't just hurt women—it hurts mathematics itself by preventing reinvention and losing diverse talent
Growing up without parental scrutiny gave O'Neil 'elbow space to self-define' rather than performing for others' expectations
Women face more scrutiny than men, and parents find it harder to give daughters the same unexamined freedom they give sons
money_quotes: I did not want to wait until I was tenured because I didn't want to risk being infertile, which is a real risk.
It simply wasn't enough to feel like I was actually competing with my contemporaries who didn't have children.
Ultimately it's not my fault, it's not women's fault, it's the fault of the academic system.
People can reinvent themselves in a way that simply does not happen in mathematics.

If you're up for tenure and there's a 3 year gap where you didn't publish, even if in the other years you published a lot, you still have to explain that gap. It's like a moral responsibility to keep publishing all the time.
Why don't women stay in math? I can answer that very easily: because it's not a very good place for women, at least if they want kids.
Privacy for me is having elbow space to self-define.
Cardinal Rule Number 1: Before all else, don't become bitter. Cardinal Rule Number 2: Remember that math is beautiful.
posts.key_insight: Academia's front-loaded pressure system during the postdoc and tenure-track years is structurally incompatible with having children, driving women out of mathematics not through individual failure but through systemic design.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reports on her participation in a DataKind datadive event focused on Crisis Text Line, a texting hotline for teens in crisis. The volunteers are analyzing metadata from crisis texts—including timestamps, message length, topics (often suicide), and outcomes—to help counselors improve their responses. Key questions include whether counselors handling up to 5 simultaneous conversations is optimal and whether wait times between texts affect outcomes. O'Neil expresses optimism about the group of data scientists working on this socially beneficial project.
key_points: DataKind organized a datadive to analyze Crisis Text Line metadata for improving counselor effectiveness
The data includes timestamps, character length, conversation topics, and outcomes reported by both texters and counselors
Practical questions being investigated include optimal counselor workload and appropriate response timing
The project represents data science being applied to mental health crisis intervention
money_quotes: The idea is that we're analyzing metadata around a texting hotline for teens in crisis.
We're trying to see if we can use the information we have on these texts to help the counselors improve their responses.
For example, right now counselors can be in up to 5 conversations at a time – is that too many? Can we figure that out from the data?
It looks like we have a pretty amazing group of nerds over here looking into this.
posts.key_insight: Data analysis of crisis hotline metadata could help optimize counselor practices and improve outcomes for teens in mental health crises.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil recounts a discussion with economist Suresh Naidu that challenges the myth that people deserve their earnings because they reflect 'added value' to society. She argues that measuring any individual's marginal value is essentially impossible because success depends entirely on cultural context and collaboration with others. Furthermore, many wealthy people accumulated riches not through genuine value creation but through avoiding costs like environmental damage, controlling legal mechanisms, or profiting from financial opacity. The post concludes that belief in market-determined worth is largely circular reasoning embraced by those who benefit from it, while inequality of opportunity makes such myths increasingly untenable.
key_points: Individual 'marginal value' is unmeasurable because every person's contributions exist within a cultural context and depend on countless others
Many fortunes come not from value creation but from unpaid externalities, legal control over others' labor, or financial complexity rather than genuine productivity
Belief in market-determined worth is circular: the market values you because you're paid well, and you're paid well because the market values you
Supply and demand explanations fail at the high end—childcare workers add obvious value but are poorly paid, while executives claim unique irreplaceability
Unequal access to opportunity makes the 'deserved earnings' myth increasingly difficult to sustain
money_quotes: Even Mozart wrote his symphonies within a cultural context. In another context he would have been a kid who hums to himself a lot.
Anyone who doesn't have a job doesn't deserve a job since the market isn't offering them a job, which must mean they are adding no value.
It's mostly the people that benefit from the market system that believe in it.
Most rich people are generically good at their job and not all that unique.

In many examples of super rich people, they got that way through not paying for negative externalities like polluting the environment, or because they had control of the legal mechanisms to reap profits off of other peoples' work.

The unequal access of opportunities in this country is becoming increasingly entrenched, and as it does so the myth of the market giving us what we deserve is becoming increasingly difficult to swallow.
posts.key_insight: The idea that earnings reflect individual value is a self-serving myth that ignores how success depends on cultural context, collaboration, and unequal access to opportunity.

posts.summary: O'Neil criticizes the trend of senior women in corporate settings policing younger women's dress codes, arguing it represents yet another layer of scrutiny women face that men don't. She questions why self-expression through clothing needs to be monitored at all, noting the double standard where male employees wearing casual attire face no similar intervention. The post challenges the framing that such advice is helpful, suggesting it may be a form of hazing or internalized sexism rather than genuine mentorship.
key_points: Senior women policing junior women's dress creates an additional burden of scrutiny that men don't face
The justification that 'women have more clothing choices' masks a double standard where casual male attire goes unchallenged
Corporate dress code enforcement is framed as protecting women from being judged on appearance, but actually reinforces that judgment
Women are already scrutinized on motherhood and femininity before their actual job performance is even considered
money_quotes: I want women to be paid attention to for what they say–and not how they look.
Why can't people just leave each other alone to express themselves?
It's just another in a long list of things you are scrutinized on if you're a woman.
In addition to whether you are a good mother, a feminine-enough-without-being-too-feminine employee, and, as a tertiary issue, if that, whether you actually do your job well.
Fuck this.
What does it really mean that these 'senior women' are taking it upon themselves to scrutinize and criticize young women?
posts.key_insight: Corporate dress code policing of women by women isn't mentorship—it's another mechanism that prioritizes appearance over competence and perpetuates gendered double standards.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reflects on how being fat since childhood unexpectedly freed her to pursue intellectual interests without social pressure. She argues that being already excluded from conventional social hierarchies—like dating and popularity—meant she had nothing to lose by also being a 'nerd' and joining the math team. She extends this to professional life, noting that plus-sized women are often taken more seriously because they're perceived as outside the 'sex game.' The post suggests that social exclusion, while painful, can paradoxically open doors by removing the pressure to conform.
key_points: Being socially excluded as a fat kid removed the pressure to also conform intellectually, freeing her to be openly smart
The dual pressures on young girls—to be thin AND to not be nerdy—are both intense, but one form of nonconformity can provide cover for the other
In professional settings, being outside conventional attractiveness norms can lead to being taken more seriously
She distinguishes between observing fat hatred externally versus internalizing it, which gives her time otherwise spent fretting about her body
Girls who aren't already socially excluded may avoid the math team to prevent voluntary exclusion
money_quotes: You're already fat, so why not be smart too? You're not doing anything else, nobody's paying attention to you, and there's nothing to gossip about, so might as well join the math team.
The happy truth is, one can be cover for the other.

I am an observer of fat hatred, in other words. That doesn't make me entirely insulated but it does give me one critical advantage: I have a lot of time on my hands to do stuff that I might otherwise spend fretting about my body.
People take me more seriously because I have taken myself out of the sex game altogether.
Being fat is something you don't have control over but joining the math team is something you do have control over.
While people claim that corporate dress codes are meant to keep our minds off of sex, that is clearly a huge lie when it comes to many categories of women's work clothes.
posts.key_insight: Social exclusion can paradoxically liberate you from conformity pressures, freeing up mental energy and social permission to pursue intellectual interests.

posts.summary: O'Neil proposes 'Cathy's Wager,' a philosophy for handling interpersonal friction: always assume others' negative behavior stems from their own struggles rather than anything you did. Inspired by Pascal's Wager, this approach suggests you have nothing to lose by not taking things personally. She illustrates with examples ranging from friends who stop responding to professional no-shows to people who suddenly seem to hate you. The strategy saves time and emotional energy by eliminating futile self-doubt about unchangeable situations. She acknowledges the caveat that this assumes you're not actually an awful person, but notes that awful people already use this logic anyway.
key_points: When someone treats you poorly, default to assuming they're dealing with their own issues rather than responding to something you did
This approach is 'selfish' in a productive way—it preserves your mental energy and prevents endless second-guessing
Not taking things personally actually enables more outreach and relationship repair, not less
The wager has a logical structure: if it's not about you, you've saved yourself grief; if it is about you, you probably can't change it anyway
The only caveat is if you're genuinely awful—but awful people already use this reasoning, so you might as well too
money_quotes: My theory is that you always assume it's something they're going through, and you try to never take it personally.
Err on the side of compassion, not blame.
The assumption that 'it's not about me' is super useful and time-saving.
I don't spend a lot of time second guessing myself or trying to change things that I can't change about other people's feelings.
It's kind of a selfish version of the Serenity Prayer, if you will, without all the religious stuff.
I just don't feel personally affected if it doesn't work. And I think that actually helps me do it more often in the end.
Consider the idea that awful people are already using Cathy's Wager, so you may as well too.
posts.key_insight: Assuming others' negative behavior isn't about you is both emotionally protective and practically effective—a rational bet that costs nothing and preserves your capacity for genuine connection.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that insufficient firing in both academia and industry creates serious organizational problems. She left academia partly because tenure protected colleagues who failed students, and observes that industry often has the same problem despite lacking tenure protections. She contends that keeping underperformers demoralizes good employees and undermines organizational culture. Controversially, she suggests firing can actually benefit the fired person by pushing them toward better-fitting work, and ends by unexpectedly agreeing with Jack Welch's management philosophy on this issue.
key_points: Tenure's lack of accountability was a major factor in her decision to leave academia
Industry often protects underperformers too, especially 'rainmakers' who violate social contracts but deliver results
Free-riders demoralize colleagues and make everyone question why they should care about their work
Many underperformers are skilled people in the wrong job who would benefit from being pushed to find better fits
Clear feedback loops are essential - people shouldn't be fired out of the blue
money_quotes: One of the most satisfying things about leaving academia and the tenure system and going into industry is how, at least in the ideal situation, you can get fired for not doing your job.
Tenure has great aspects and not-so-great aspects, and a total lack of leverage is not a great one. I feel for deans sometimes.
Social contracts are hard to quantify and somewhat squishy. You see people getting away with stuff because they're rainmakers or higher ups.
Retaining people who should by all accounts get fired makes the veneer of the kool-aid drinking camaraderie even more flimsy and scrutinizable.
What's so great about working here if people can just slack off and not care? Why do I give two shits about this project anyway?
These people I'm talking about are generally high skilled and temporarily depressed. They're in the wrong job.
In my experience people who deserve to get fired totally know it and can't believe their luck that they've not been fired yet.
posts.key_insight: The reluctance to fire underperformers harms organizations by demoralizing good employees and often harms the underperformers themselves by trapping them in ill-fitting roles.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses various reader questions ranging from family dynamics to academic career decisions. The centerpiece is advice about dealing with an assertive older sister, where Aunt Pythia draws on self-reflection to suggest the sister likely wants engagement rather than submission. Other topics include the unverified ABC conjecture proof, Obamacare enrollment data as an engineering-becomes-political issue, and advice for someone considering leaving industry for a PhD. The column maintains a conversational, self-deprecating tone throughout.
key_points: Assertive people often want to be challenged rather than deferred to - their strong opinions are invitations for debate, not final pronouncements
Engineering failures are increasingly becoming political issues (Knight Capital, Obamacare) as technology systems become more central to society
You never get rid of problems, you just get a new set of problems - career decisions are about which problems suit your personality
Politics exists everywhere including academia and startups; the key is finding work absorbing enough that you can tune it out
Family obligations aren't absolute - it's acceptable to distance yourself from family members who make you feel suffocated
money_quotes: The funny thing about family is, we are our most vulnerable with our family, and yet we are also very comfortable with them, because we know them so well.
She's a know-it-all loudmouth, true, but the sooner you can either get on her wavelength or roll your eyes and love her in spite of her pushy know-it-all ways the better for you and for her.
I don't agree that you have to hang out with family, unless possibly if they're dying or in need.
We are increasingly seeing pure engineering issues become political issues.
The nerds are taking over, in other words, or at least their mistakes are.
My motto is 'You never get rid of your problems, you just get a new set of problems.'
If you're really allergic to politics, I suggest you find a place where you can safely ignore that stuff, like maybe in a cave in the woods.
posts.key_insight: Strong personalities often want engagement and challenge rather than deference, and what feels like dominance may actually be an invitation to participate.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the arms race between Twitter and spam bots that inflate follower counts, framing it as a 'modeling war' between sophisticated parties. She notes that follower count has become a flawed proxy for influence, creating a market for fake accounts. Despite having massive resources post-IPO, Twitter appears to have reduced efforts to combat spam. O'Neil argues this is shortsighted: while Twitter may feel protected by lack of competition, the degraded user experience from spam could ultimately drive users away entirely.
key_points: Follower count as an influence metric creates perverse incentives to game the system through fake accounts
Twitter's anti-spam efforts have reportedly declined despite having substantial resources to combat the problem
The lack of competing platforms may be making Twitter complacent about maintaining quality
User experience degradation from spam is a more fundamental threat than reputational damage from a gamed system
Modeling wars exist not just between corporations and an unwitting public, but between sophisticated adversaries
money_quotes: The modelers are working hard trying to convince or trick the public into clicking or buying or consuming or taking out loans or buying insurance, and the public is on the other, barely aware that they're engaging in anything at all resembling a war.
It's a measure of a person's influence, albeit a crappy one for various reasons (and not just because it's being gamed).
The high impact of the follower count means it's in a wannabe celebrity's best interest to juice their follower numbers, which introduces the idea of fake twitter accounts to game the model.
If everyone knows how gamed the system is, they'll stop trusting it.
That argument only really holds if people have something else to use instead as a better proxy of influence.
At some point, if Twitter doesn't maintain standards, people will just not spend time on Twitter, and its proxy of influence will fall out of favor for that more fundamental reason.
posts.key_insight: When a metric becomes a target, it ceases to be a good metric—and platforms that rely on gameable proxies for influence risk losing users not because competitors emerge, but because the degraded experience makes engagement pointless.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces a project to develop a platform for open-source models, seeking examples of algorithmic models that can be made transparent and interactive. She's collaborating with a Python developer and has already filed a FOIA request for NYC's teacher value-added model. The post solicits suggestions for open-source models in areas like credit scoring, municipal ratings, and weather prediction. The core motivation is that models can only be truly understood, validated, and improved when people can interact with them directly.
key_points: O'Neil is building a platform to showcase and interact with open-source algorithmic models
She's actively seeking models through FOIA requests (NYC teacher evaluations) and existing open-source projects (municipal credit ratings)
The platform aims to build interactive tools that let people explore how models work
Models don't need to be perfect - seeing flaws and enabling improvement is part of the point
True understanding of a model requires the ability to fiddle with parameters and check robustness
money_quotes: You don't really know what a model does until you can interact with it.
You don't know if a model is robust unless you can fiddle with its parameters and check.
You don't know if a model is best possible unless you've let people try to improve it.
They don't have to be great, because the point is to see their flaws and possible to improve them.
They don't have to be creepy!
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic transparency requires more than publishing code - it requires interactive tools that let people probe, test, and challenge how models actually behave.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her admiration for Pope Francis's Apostolic Exhortation, which delivers a scathing critique of modern capitalism and trickle-down economics. The Pope argues that our economy has moved beyond exploitation to outright exclusion, creating 'disposable' humans who are no longer even part of society. He condemns the 'idolatry of money' and the rejection of ethics in favor of market absolutism. The exhortation connects economic inequality directly to violence, arguing that no amount of security spending can address systemic injustice. O'Neil endorses this vision and notes she has invited the Pope to join Occupy.
key_points: The modern economy doesn't just exploit people—it excludes them entirely, creating 'leftovers' who aren't even part of society
Trickle-down economics represents 'crude and naïve trust' in the goodness of the powerful and has never been confirmed by facts
The financial crisis originated in a 'profound human crisis' where humans are reduced to consumers
