Ethics is now viewed with 'scornful derision' because it threatens the absolute autonomy of markets
Inequality and exclusion are root causes of violence that cannot be solved through security measures or arms
money_quotes: How can it be that it is not a news item when an elderly homeless person dies of exposure, but it is news when the stock market loses two points?
Human beings are themselves considered consumer goods to be used and then discarded.
The excluded are not the 'exploited' but the outcast, the 'leftovers'.
This opinion, which has never been confirmed by the facts, expresses a crude and naïve trust in the goodness of those wielding economic power.
The culture of prosperity deadens us; we are thrilled if the market offers us something new to purchase.
The worldwide crisis affecting finance and the economy lays bare their imbalances and, above all, their lack of real concern for human beings.
Money must serve, not rule!
It is evil crystallized in unjust social structures, which cannot be the basis of hope for a better future.
posts.key_insight: Modern capitalism has evolved from exploiting people to excluding them entirely—creating disposable humans whose suffering doesn't even register as news.

posts.summary: The post critiques 'people analytics' tools that claim to objectively evaluate employees and ideas using algorithms. O'Neil argues that these systems don't achieve objectivity but instead encode existing biases from historical data into mathematical models. Using Royal Dutch Shell's idea-vetting algorithm as an example, she shows how training on 'previous winners' embeds whatever preferences and biases existed in the original human process. She contrasts this with truly blind orchestra auditions, where evaluation is based on a single transparent criterion. The result is companies that unknowingly perpetuate homogeneity while believing they've achieved objectivity.
key_points: Algorithms trained on 'previous winners' encode whatever biases existed in the original selection process
True blind evaluation (like orchestra auditions) requires commitment to a single, transparent criterion—not comparison to past successes
These hiring algorithms are unlikely to obviously fail because they efficiently find people similar to existing successful employees
The fundamental problem is transparency: black-box algorithms obscure what criteria are actually being used
Companies using these tools will increasingly hire people just like those they already have, reinforcing homogeneity
money_quotes: Whatever their process used to be, it's now embedded in an algorithm.
They might very well pass on really excellent ideas that happened to have come from a modest person – no discussion necessary on what kind of people are being invisible ignored in such a set-up.
They will believe their process is now objective because it's living inside a mathematical model.
Their success relied, in other words, on a community's support of their idea and their ability to command that support.
These algorithms don't make things objective, they make things opaque.
They embed our old cultural problems in new mathematical models, giving us a false badge of objectivity.
The most obvious consequence of using this model, for the companies that use it, is that they'll get more and more people just like the people they already have.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic hiring tools don't create objectivity—they launder existing biases through mathematical models, making discrimination harder to see while giving it a veneer of scientific legitimacy.

posts.summary: This advice column covers four reader questions spanning philosophy of mathematics, work-life balance, academic economics research, and psychology of criticism. Cathy O'Neil argues that mathematics is a tool of approximation rather than a literal description of reality, advises a bored working mother to demand more from both her career and her husband, critiques a paper on Fields Medalists' productivity for questionable statistical methodology and cherry-picked comparison groups, and explains how psychological sensitivities create selection biases in how we perceive others' flaws.
key_points: Mathematics doesn't describe the universe literally - it's an approximation tool, and idealized concepts like 'circles' don't exist in physical reality
Being bored at work is unacceptable self-neglect; mothers should model self-advocacy and career fulfillment for their children
Economists shouldn't be trusted to run the economy given their vested interests, and their statistical methodology is often questionable
The Fields Medal productivity study appears to cherry-pick comparison groups by requiring 'above-median citations' in ways that muddy the analysis
Psychological sensitivities create selection bias - we notice in others what we're insecure about in ourselves
money_quotes: As a mathematician I'm here to tell you that mathematics doesn't describe the universe. It's at most used as a tool to understand certain parts of the universe, but only at the level of an approximation.

There's no such thing as a circle in reality. It's an idealized shape we use in mathematics that comes in super handy for various reasons, but because actual matter is made up of stuff, there's never going to be a true circle, except in our brains.
You need to be interested in your own life, and being bored off your butt is seriously not cutting it. Be more selfish than that, and do it for your kids.
He's not helpful because he's gotten away with not being helpful. It's a deal you made with him, possibly (probably) without thinking enough about it. Time to renegotiate.
Without even reading the paper I'd say we shouldn't trust economists to run our economy. They have already proved their vested interests are too distracting for such a responsibility.
If you were doing stuff in order to win a Fields Medal, then after getting it, you'd stop, right, and do something else that's interesting?
When someone is sensitized to an issue, even if it's not one of insecurity, then they see it everywhere, all the time, as if for the first time.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical models are approximations rather than reality, and similarly, the frameworks we use to understand careers, relationships, and research all deserve skeptical examination of their underlying assumptions.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces an upcoming protest at JP Morgan headquarters organized by her OWS Alt Banking group as part of a broader 'Week of Action' during New York's mayoral transition. She reflects on how Occupy's role has evolved from provoking outrage to inspiring hope for positive change. The post describes the coalition effort and previews protest signs, including one referencing a recent viral Twitter confrontation with JPMorgan. She notes the challenge of communicating complex financial critique (like the London Whale scandal) to a general audience.
key_points: The protest is part of a coalition effort timed to NYC's Bloomberg-to-de Blasio transition, framing it as a moment for civic change
Occupy's messaging has shifted from provoking outrage to inspiring hope that 'things could be a whole lot better'
The 'be in the know' theme emphasizes financial education over pure confrontation
Complex financial scandals like the London Whale require too much context to communicate effectively on protest signs
money_quotes: There's so much widespread distrust and hatred of the big banks at this point that I feel like Occupy's role has gone from provoking people to be outraged to provoking people to be hopeful.
Hopeful about the fact that things could be a whole lot better than this, if we work together.
I also made a sign that referenced the London Whale and the risk model, but someone said we might need to give people a copy of our recent book, Occupy Finance, just to understand that sign.
posts.key_insight: As public anger at big banks became mainstream, Occupy's role evolved from sparking outrage to channeling that energy into hope for collective change.

posts.summary: O'Neil questions whether large employers like Walmart have incentives to raise wages as they grow, challenging the progressive assumption that paying workers more would benefit these companies through increased consumer spending. She argues that the 'welfare queen' status of Walmart—where taxpayers subsidize poverty-level wages through food stamps and Medicaid—means the company's interests are fundamentally misaligned with national prosperity. Rather than appealing to corporate self-interest, she concludes that political solutions like raising the minimum wage or establishing a basic income guarantee are necessary.
key_points: The assumption that massive employers' interests eventually align with national prosperity is flawed when government subsidies fill the gap between poverty wages and survival
Walmart employees are the largest group of Medicaid and food stamp recipients in many states, receiving about $1,000 per person in public assistance
The argument that raising wages would increase sales at low-wage retailers may not hold when companies can externalize labor costs to taxpayers
Political solutions (minimum wage increases tied to inflation, basic income) are more realistic than appeals to corporate self-interest
money_quotes: Is there ever a point, as that employer grows in size and employs more and more of the nation, that its agenda becomes aligned with the national agenda of prosperity and well-being?
Given their welfare queen status, the agenda of Walmart and other huge low-wage employers is not actually aligned with the nation's.

As long as it can lower wages below poverty level, leaving Uncle Sam to make up some of the difference, and either pocket that difference or distribute them to their shareholders, they'll continue to do so.
The incentives are wrong.

Instead of appealing to their greed and telling them it's in their best interest to raise wages, which I don't believe is true, we need to raise political awareness about how the system actually works.
posts.key_insight: Corporate welfare through taxpayer-subsidized poverty wages breaks the feedback loop that might otherwise align massive employers' interests with workers' prosperity, making political intervention necessary.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the recent food stamp cuts in America and questions whether the savings actually make economic sense when accounting for downstream healthcare costs. She draws on personal experience managing blood sugar to illustrate how expensive healthy eating is, noting that cheap food is inherently bad for metabolic health. The post argues that forcing poor people onto cheaper, unhealthier diets will likely increase diabetes diagnoses and worsen existing cases. With diabetes care costs already rising 40% over five years to $245 billion, she suggests the billions saved on food stamps may be dwarfed by increased medical costs. She frames this as both a moral failing and potentially a fiscal miscalculation.
key_points: Food stamp cuts have overwhelmed food pantries and forced diabetic recipients to choose between appropriate food and cheaper unhealthy alternatives
Healthy eating requires real money - cheap calories come from foods that destroy blood sugar control
The scale of potential costs is comparable: billions in food stamp cuts versus a diabetes care industry already at $245 billion and growing 40% every five years
The analysis should consider not just immediate savings but the long-term cost of creating new diabetics and worsening existing cases
Policy decisions that seem fiscally conservative may actually be penny-wise and pound-foolish when externalities are included
money_quotes: Eating well in this country is expensive, and I've had to spend real money on food here in New York City to avoid sugary and fast carb-laden food.
Because here's the thing, and it's not a secret: shitty food is cheap.
If I need to buy lots of food (read: calories) for a small amount of money, I can do it easily, but it will be hell for my blood sugar control.
I'm guessing I'd be a full-blown diabetic by now if I were poor and on food stamps.

How much money are we really saving by decreasing the food stamp allowance in this country, if we consider how many more people will be diagnosed diabetic as a result of the decreased quality of their diet?
Never mind in a moral context – I'm definitely of the mind that people should be able to eat – I'm wondering if the food stamp cuts make sense in a dollars and cents context.
posts.key_insight: Food stamp cuts may cost more in downstream diabetes care than they save, making them not just morally questionable but potentially fiscally irrational.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her participation in a panel at Columbia's Journalism School focused on algorithmic accountability in reporting. The panel, organized by Nick Diakopoulos, will respond to a draft paper from the Tow Center examining how investigative journalism should handle black box algorithms. Other panelists include Scott Klein from ProPublica and Clifford Stein from Columbia. The discussion centers on understanding and investigating opaque algorithmic systems that increasingly shape society.
key_points: Algorithmic accountability requires new frameworks for investigative journalism
Black box algorithms present unique challenges for transparency and public understanding
Academic institutions like Columbia's Tow Center are developing formal approaches to algorithm investigation
The intersection of journalism and algorithm auditing is an emerging field
money_quotes: Its overall goal is to understand how investigative journalism should handle a world filled with black box algorithms.
posts.key_insight: As algorithms increasingly govern society, journalism must develop new investigative methods to hold these black box systems accountable.

posts.summary: This guest post by Marc Joffe announces a PDF Liberation Hackathon aimed at improving tools for extracting data from PDFs. The post argues that open source modeling efforts are hindered by the high cost of cleaning data trapped in unstructured PDF formats. While tools like Tabula and DocHive exist, they have limitations for large-scale extraction tasks. The hackathon, sponsored by the Sunlight Foundation, will bring together developers to work on PDF extraction challenges and improve open source tools for data journalists and transparency advocates.
key_points: Data cleaning costs can be prohibitive for open source modeling efforts, allowing biased analytics to persist
PDFs are a major obstacle to data accessibility, trapping information that modelers and journalists need
Existing open source tools like Tabula and DocHive help but cannot handle large-scale batch processing
The hackathon will offer prizes for solutions that advance PDF data extraction capabilities
All winning entries must publish source code publicly on GitHub
money_quotes: Since open source modeling efforts are usually low dollar operations, the costs of data cleaning may prove to be prohibitive. Hence no open model – distortion and bias continue their reign.

A corporation or well funded research institution can purchase an expensive, enterprise-level ETL tool to migrate data from the PDFs into a database. But this is not much help to insurgent modelers who want to produce open source work.
If, like me, you want to submit hundreds of PDFs to a software tool, press 'Go' and see large volumes of cleanly formatted data, you are out of luck.
Open source models can bring much needed transparency to scientific research, finance, education and other fields plagued by biased, self-serving analytics.
The PDF Liberation Hackathon is going to be a great opportunity to advance the state of the art when it comes to harvesting data from public documents.
posts.key_insight: The inaccessibility of data locked in PDFs creates a barrier that favors well-funded institutions over transparency advocates, perpetuating biased analytics in finance and public policy.

posts.summary: This advice column by 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) addresses reader questions on topics ranging from deceptive job descriptions in finance, data science methodology for mathematicians, gender panic in academia, phishing emails, and birth control effectiveness. O'Neil playfully chides her readers for being nerds who ask too many math questions and not enough 'spicy' ones. Her advice tends toward practical directness: leave bad jobs rather than hoping employers will change, embrace experimentation in data science, focus on individual rather than group identity when facing gender anxiety, and trust that rhythm/withdrawal birth control can work with discipline and regular cycles.
key_points: Finance employers routinely lie about job descriptions, and employees shouldn't expect them to change - just find another job, preferably outside finance
Data science is a craft learned through experimentation: start with exploratory data analysis, try multiple algorithms, test hypotheses about why certain approaches work
Women in male-dominated fields should focus on their individual experience rather than identifying too strongly with statistics about women leaving the field
The 'broken grammar' phishing email about an 18-year-old 'ready to learn' is obvious spam that should be marked as such
Birth control methods like rhythm and withdrawal can be effective with discipline, regular cycles, and good memory
money_quotes: You guys are nerds. I have like a 5-to-1 ratio of math-related versus sex-related questions.
For whatever reason, which I certainly don't relate to, there are some people that still desperately want to work in finance as front-office quants.
One last thing: look outside finance! There are plenty of other ways to be a nerd.
Put the science in data science. Try a bunch of different ones and see which works better, and hypothesize on why, and try to test that hypothesis.
You are not a statistic, you are an individual person.
Nobody is asking you to explain why other people do the things they do. We can barely explain why we do the things we do.
In other words, you are a special, special man with a very excellent memory.
posts.key_insight: Whether facing deceptive employers, methodological uncertainty in data science, or identity-based anxiety, the solution is to focus on your individual situation and take practical action rather than dwelling on systemic problems you cannot change.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts a talk by Merlyna Lim about social media's role in grassroots organizing and revolution. She explores how the Arab Spring's Mohamed Bouazizi became a revolutionary symbol partly through embellished details that created a more compelling narrative. The post examines why certain stories mobilize people while others don't, connecting this to Peter Turchin's theory about 'elite overproduction' and unemployed educated classes driving social unrest. O'Neil questions whether society sympathizes more with hungry poor people or frustrated educated elites, concluding she personally worries more about the former.
key_points: Social media is effective at mobilizing people against a common enemy but poor at facilitating nuanced discussions for building lasting change
The Bouazizi narrative that sparked revolution included 'white lies' (college-educated, slapped by female officer) that made it more compelling than similar true stories
Previous self-immolations in Tunisia didn't spark revolution because 'killing yourself because you are frustrated at not having enough to eat just isn't as compelling'
Peter Turchin's theory suggests unemployed but highly educated populations ('elite overproduction') create conditions for revolution through disgruntled elite-wannabes
O'Neil personally sympathizes more with those whose food stamps were cut than with unemployed lawyers
money_quotes: Whereas social media is pretty good at energizing people to 'get rid of their enemy' at a given critical moment, and mobilize on the street, it's not that great at nuanced discussions for how to build something permanent and lasting afterwards.
Killing yourself because you are frustrated at not having enough to eat just isn't as compelling.
A large class of disgruntled elite-wannabes, often well-educated and highly capable, has been denied access to elite positions.
Does one have more sympathy for people whose foodstamps have been recently cut or for someone who got a law degree and can't find a job?
Personally, and this is maybe because I've been reading Jonathan Kozol's Savage Inequalities, I'm not as worried about the lawyer.
posts.key_insight: Revolutionary movements require compelling narratives that resonate emotionally, and society often finds educated people's frustrated ambitions more mobilizing than pure poverty and hunger.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil examines research by Seena Fazel revealing that predictive risk models used to assess prisoners with mental disorders are deeply flawed, performing barely better than a coin flip for the most violent populations. These models are widely used across the US, UK, and other countries to make consequential decisions about indefinite detention and treatment. The high false-positive rates mean some offenders spend longer incarcerated than their actual risk warrants. O'Neil argues these tools provide a false veneer of scientific credibility that leads to over-reliance by courts and clinicians. She calls for transparency, open models, and a return to behavior-based methodology rather than trusting flawed algorithmic predictions.
key_points: Predictive risk models for violent and sexual offenders are already in widespread use across multiple countries, influencing decisions about indefinite detention
Meta-analysis shows these models perform poorly, especially for the highest-risk populations they're supposedly designed to assess
High false-positive rates result in people being incarcerated longer than their true risk level justifies
The 'veneer of scientific respectability' leads courts and clinicians to over-rely on flawed tools
O'Neil advocates for open models with protected data sets to enable proper scrutiny
money_quotes: In practice, the high false-positive rate probably means that some offenders spend longer in prison and secure hospital than their true risk would suggest.
This seems to be yet another example of a mathematical obfuscation and intimidation that gives people a false sense of having a good tool at hand.
It doesn't make sense to put people in jail an extra 10 years because a crappy model said so.
The veneer of scientific respectability surrounding such methods may lead to over-reliance on their findings.
The data itself is extremely sensitive and protected, but the model itself should be scrutinized.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical models used in criminal justice create a dangerous illusion of scientific precision that masks their fundamental unreliability, leading to unjust extended incarceration.

posts.summary: O'Neil explores the implications of facial recognition algorithms that could predict marital success by reading subtle emotional expressions at the altar. She draws a parallel to Huntington's disease genetic testing, where only 5% of at-risk people choose to learn their status. The post questions whether some information is better left unknown, particularly when there's no actionable cure or intervention. O'Neil concludes that for deeply personal matters like love, she'd rather not have algorithmic optimization, even if it were technically possible.
key_points: Facial emotion-reading algorithms combined with research on 'gut feelings' about marriage could theoretically predict relationship outcomes
The value of information depends heavily on whether it's actionable—people avoid knowing bad news when nothing can be done about it
Only 5% of people at risk for Huntington's disease take the genetic test, preferring ambiguity over certainty of bad outcomes
Faith and ambiguity may be essential ingredients in intimate human relationships that optimization would undermine
There's a meaningful distinction between information that helps us and information that merely predicts our fate
money_quotes: Is there some information we are better off not knowing?
I don't think knowing my marriage is likely to be in trouble would help me keep the faith. And every marriage needs a good dose of faith.
For them the value of not knowing that information is larger than knowing.
Until that happens the ambiguity is preferable.
When things get that personal and intimate, I'd rather be left alone, even if an algorithm could help me 'optimize my love life'.
Maybe in 100 years people will treat their computers like love oracles.
posts.key_insight: The power to predict doesn't always translate into the power to help, and some of our most intimate human experiences may require the protection of uncertainty rather than the clarity of algorithmic prediction.

posts.summary: Occupy the SEC, a working group from the Occupy movement, successfully influenced the final Volcker Rule by submitting a detailed public comment letter that was cited 284 times in the rule's preamble. Despite giving the final rule only a C- grade and facing obstacles like being denied standing when they sued regulators for delays, the group demonstrated that citizen engagement in regulatory processes can work. The author witnessed their methodical, workmanlike approach—meeting after hours to review the proposed rule line by line—and contrasts their grassroots effort with the extensive lobbyist access to rule-writers. The post celebrates public commenting as a critical democratic tool while acknowledging it requires both committed citizens and receptive regulators.
key_points: Occupy the SEC's public comment letter was referenced 284 times in the Volcker Rule's final preamble, demonstrating measurable impact
The group worked methodically through the proposed rule line by line in after-hours meetings, prioritizing substance over rhetoric
Public commenting is a powerful but underutilized tool for citizen participation in regulatory processes
Success required both dedicated citizen effort and regulators willing to listen—the combination is rare but possible
The group graded the final rule a C-, showing they maintained critical standards even while celebrating their influence
money_quotes: It was a great example of an Occupy group that was all work and no talk.
I even remember talking to them about the chances anybody would even read their letter, never mind take it seriously.
They knew the rules, though, and the rules were that the regulators would have to read all the letters, although how seriously they took them was a different matter.
They were doing it because they knew it was one of the best shots at having their voices heard. It was an incredible commitment to that ideal.
Nothing could have been more workmanlike or hopeful.
Public commenting is a critical tool.
posts.key_insight: Effective activism sometimes means mastering bureaucratic processes rather than just protesting—Occupy the SEC showed that citizens who do the unglamorous work of reading regulations line by line can actually influence policy.

posts.summary: The post highlights a New York Times investigation revealing how tobacco companies use international trade agreements to bully small nations into weakening their public health laws. O'Neil explains that trade treaties now allow corporations to sue countries directly, which she argues represents a fundamental threat to national sovereignty. She connects this to broader concerns about the Trans-Pacific Partnership (TPP) potentially enabling similar corporate bullying on financial regulation. The piece emphasizes the power imbalance between wealthy corporations with vast legal resources and smaller nations unable to defend themselves, noting that Uruguay only survived Philip Morris's legal challenge because Bloomberg's foundation funded their defense.
key_points: International trade agreements now allow corporations to sue countries directly over regulations they claim harm trade or violate intellectual property rights
Tobacco companies are using these mechanisms to intimidate small African nations into weakening public health laws
The TPP threatens to expand this corporate power to challenge financial regulations and other domestic policies
The resource imbalance means only nations with outside help (like Bloomberg's foundation aiding Uruguay) can effectively fight back
These secretive trade negotiations undermine democratic sovereignty by prioritizing corporate interests over public welfare
money_quotes: In order to protect companies from being taken over by foreign nations, and in the name of free trade, it's now possible for companies to sue countries directly.
It's kind of a supernational Citizen's United, in that only companies and industries that have enough lawyer power can get their way.
Many companies easily have more resources than many countries.
If you count up all the money that will go to bullying and compare that to all the money available to help out the bullied, you quickly come to a sad conclusion.
The TPP is being negotiated in secret, among a bunch of nations many of whom claim to be democratic.
posts.key_insight: International trade agreements have created a legal framework that allows wealthy corporations to override democratic governance by suing nations that attempt to regulate them, representing a fundamental transfer of power from citizens to capital.

posts.summary: This is an advice column post where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) responds to reader questions on topics ranging from teaching math to young children, relationship communication, career reinvention at 50, political discourse between libertarians and liberals, and holiday stress management. The post opens with a playful tangent about flannel clothing before diving into substantive advice. O'Neil's responses blend practical wisdom with sharp observations about human nature and social dynamics. She advocates for direct communication in relationships, embraces career reinvention at any age, and suggests finding common ground as the key to cross-political dialogue.
key_points: Young children (ages 4-5) learn math best through Socratic dialogue about concrete phenomena like the solar system and seasons, not formal instruction from books
In relationships where one partner avoids difficult conversations, directly requesting a scheduled discussion is essential—and if they refuse to engage, that itself is meaningful information
Career reinvention is possible at any age, though experienced workers often face pressure toward management because their salary demands more responsibility
Cross-political dialogue works best by starting with shared concerns (like surveillance and too-big-to-fail banks) before addressing solutions
For high-stress family gatherings, keeping expectations low leads to more pleasant surprises than agonizing over perfection
money_quotes: It's incredibly important to make this an interactive Socratic dialog, not a monologue from an adult!
Super concrete things that require mind-expanding 3-dimensional visualizations and/or questioning of basic assumptions and definitions.

Whatever it is that's going on, she's not spending much time with you and is not even really willing to explain why. Those two things in combination make me want to advise you to just break up with her now.
In breaking up with her, you might get a discussion for free, if she really wants to keep you.
It's never too late to reinvent yourself. Some of my favorite people did so when they were 75.
Discuss what your commonalities are first, and move cautiously and carefully outwards from there.
These big meals are just so so difficult, I honestly think you should keep standards as low as possible and be pleasantly surprised at every moment of happiness.
Stop agonizing in the kitchen, nobody will appreciate your effort anyway!!
posts.key_insight: Whether in relationships, careers, or political debates, progress comes from direct engagement and finding common ground rather than avoiding difficult conversations or expecting others to change first.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques a New York Times editorial arguing that America needs to do more for 'gifted' students (defined as high test-scorers). She challenges both the premise that this is a crisis and the proposed solutions, arguing that bright kids who disengage from school can find valuable learning through work experiences, self-directed reading, and real-world adventures. She points out that test-taking ability correlates with wealth and practice rather than innate talent, meaning any program focused on high scorers will primarily benefit already-privileged kids. The piece concludes that doubling down on elite academic tracking would make elite youth more elite without benefiting the country.
key_points: America's supposed crisis of underperforming gifted students is based on standardized test comparisons that may not reflect actual technological or scientific competitiveness
When bright kids 'check out' of school, the solution isn't necessarily more AP classes—it could be giving them meaningful real-world experiences like jobs and internships
Separating, isolating, and venerating great test-takers is the opposite of how to develop future successful people
Test-taking ability is not a pure measure of talent; it's heavily influenced by practice and wealth, so programs targeting high scorers will be dominated by 'the usual suspects'
Modern resources like online communities give nerdy kids more options for self-directed learning than ever before
money_quotes: If that's true, if bright kids who aren't academically challenged at school start checking out more and more, then it makes just as much sense to me to see if there's something they can check out towards.
I learned way more about how the world worked than I would have if I'd followed the advice of this editorial, which was to take lots more AP classes and then enter college when I was 14.
Enrichment happens when kids learn about stuff that's outside their usual rhythm and when there are no adults scripting their activities and telling them what to do or how many laps to swim.

What drives me nuts just as much as the idea of further separating and isolating and venerating great test-takers, which as far as I'm concerned is the opposite way you should treat future successful people, is the idea that there should be such a well-defined funnel for children at all.
The idea that, just because someone's good at tests they should be treated as if they're already running the Fed only increases the weird worshippy aspect of how our culture treats math nerds.
It's just plain untrue that test-taking actually exposes talent. It's well established that you can get better at these tests through practice, and that richer kids practice more.
This is a plan to make elite youth even more elite. And I don't know about you, but my feeling is that's not going to help our country overall.
posts.key_insight: Programs designed to help 'gifted' students based on test scores don't identify talent—they amplify existing privilege while ignoring that real enrichment comes from self-directed experiences outside scripted academic funnels.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her identification with what Conan O'Brien calls 'the Irish mind' - a personality type characterized by instinctive rule-breaking and compulsive storytelling. She describes how she inherited these traits from her half-Irish father, including the need to tell and embellish stories before experiences feel complete. The post humorously explores how this storytelling compulsion has shaped her life and occasionally frustrated her more literal-minded Dutch husband. She concludes with dreams of visiting Ireland to fully embrace this heritage.
key_points: The 'Irish mind' instinctively rebels against rules - not from bravery but from being 'not wired' to follow them
Experiences don't feel complete until they've been told as stories and properly embellished
The anticipation of future storytelling actually motivates present behavior and risk-taking
This personality trait can clash with more literal-minded people who want factual accuracy over narrative impact
money_quotes: Irish minds cause people never follow rules, and not because they're brave but because they are just not wired to do so.
If you've had an experience, it doesn't really count until you've told the story to a friend, or maybe 6 close friends.
Many of my actual experiences have been enhanced and even gone from pleasant to unpleasant (or vice versa) through this process.
I've also done lots of crazy things just in anticipation of telling the story later.
It's a constant little Irish voice in my head urging me on with things like, 'think of how awesome this story is gonna be!'
It took me about 15 years of our marriage to train him out of correcting me.
People, does that help the story? I think not. Therefore we do not do that.
posts.key_insight: The compulsion to tell good stories doesn't just shape how we remember experiences - it actively drives what experiences we seek out in the first place.

posts.summary: O'Neil questions why insulin tracking isn't more accessible given its potential to explain metabolic differences between individuals. She suspects her own insulin levels spike dramatically in response to sugar and carbohydrates, which could be a precursor to diabetes. She wants a continuous insulin monitor similar to those available for blood sugar tracking. Her core argument is that tracking insulin levels, combined with insulin resistance data, could shift conversations about weight from willpower to science.
key_points: Insulin regulates how quickly sugar becomes fat and how quickly fat is released into the bloodstream
Elevated insulin that spikes dramatically after eating may be a precursor to diabetes, where the body overproduces insulin until organs become resistant
Continuous blood sugar monitors exist but continuous insulin monitors appear unavailable
Understanding individual insulin responses could explain why people with identical diets retain fat differently
The focus on willpower in weight discussions could be replaced with scientific understanding of insulin mechanisms
money_quotes: I am fairly convinced that the insulin levels – combined with a measure of their insulin resistance – would explain a lot about why certain people retain fat where others don't with the same diet.
And it would be such a relief to stop arguing about willpower and start understanding this stuff scientifically.
Although that might sound like the opposite of diabetes, it's actually the precursor to diabetes, where my body goes nuts making insulin and then my organs become resistant to it.
Please do not comment below telling me how to lose weight or talking about how to have more willpower: I will delete such comments!
posts.key_insight: Individual insulin tracking could transform weight discussions from moral judgments about willpower into scientific understanding of metabolic mechanisms.

posts.summary: This guest post by Tom Adams argues that the Volcker Rule's primary purpose and measure of effectiveness should be preventing another Citigroup-style bailout. Citigroup required the largest government bailout in history in 2008, primarily due to its massive holdings of ABS CDOs that it accumulated to keep its lucrative securitization business running. When external buyers for super-senior CDO tranches dried up, Citi simply held them as 'investments' requiring minimal capital due to AAA ratings. The post contends that had Volcker Rule-style fair market accounting been required earlier, Citi would have hit capacity problems sooner and potentially cooled the mortgage market before the crisis.
key_points: Citigroup's bailout was the largest in government history, primarily caused by $150 billion in super-senior CDO holdings that became nearly worthless
Citi kept its lucrative CDO business running by buying its own super-senior tranches when external buyers dried up, exploiting AAA ratings to minimize capital requirements
The Volcker Rule's requirement to move covered assets to fair market valuation would have forced earlier recognition of CDO problems
Zions Bank's immediate impairment charge under the new rule demonstrates its potential effectiveness
Citi's CDO portfolio had massive leverage - $150 billion in CDOs was theoretically backed by $1.5 trillion in mortgage loans
money_quotes: The real purpose of the Volcker Rule is to prevent another Citigroup bailout and therefore the measure of its effectiveness is whether the rule would accomplish this.
Undeterred, Citi began to take down the super-senior bonds from the deals they were selling and holding them as 'investments' which required very little capital because they were AAA.
This approach enabled Citi to continue the very lucrative business of selling CDOs (to themselves!).
By any decent measure, Citi should have been broken up after this fiasco.
Had Citi been forced to do this as they acquired their CDOs, there is a decent chance they would have run into CDO capacity problems much sooner.
They might have had to justify their valuations with actual data rather than self-serving models.
posts.key_insight: The Volcker Rule's true test is whether it prevents banks from accumulating toxic assets under favorable accounting treatment while the music plays, forcing them to face market reality before bailouts become necessary.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses three questions: how professional women should handle inappropriate personal questions from journalists about marriage and motherhood, how to navigate sexual compatibility with intellectually compatible but inexperienced partners, and how to explain abstract algebra to non-mathematicians. The gender dynamics question draws on examples of Swedish CEO Annika Falkengren and German minister Ursula von der Leyen to illustrate double standards in how media treats successful women. O'Neil argues that men often try to force women into boxes of 'sex object' or 'mother' and that deviation from these norms shouldn't require explanation or apology. The column blends serious feminist critique with practical advice delivered in a characteristically direct style.
key_points: Journalists probe women's personal lives about marriage and children in ways they never would with men in equivalent positions
Men who feel threatened by successful women try to categorize them as either sex objects or mothers, and confusion when women don't fit becomes 'your problem to explain'
Women should turn inappropriate questions back on interviewers and don't need to be polite with journalists who have 'super thick skins'
People who deviate from 'normal' should be celebrated rather than required to justify their choices
When communicating complex technical work to laypeople, connect abstract concepts to familiar structures they already understand
money_quotes: Some men are deeply threatened by women and want to put them into one of two boxes: sex object or mother.
If you are neither it makes them very confused. They want that confusion to be your problem, not theirs.
In general it's the people who deviate from normal that make life worth living. Abnormal people should be celebrated, not reprimanded.
You don't have to be polite with journalists. They have super thick skins.
It's totally fine to respond with, 'this is off the record, but your question is totally inappropriate and I won't agree to finish this interview until you agree to remove it from this interview.'
Make them aware of how inappropriate it is.
posts.key_insight: Women in professional settings shouldn't feel obligated to justify or apologize for deviating from traditional expectations around marriage and motherhood—the burden of that discomfort belongs to those asking inappropriate questions, not those being asked.

posts.summary: The post questions whether 'efficient markets' actually benefit ordinary people, noting that hedge funds use expensive surveillance technology and data gathering to gain trading advantages unavailable to the public. While this theoretically makes markets more efficient by incorporating information faster into prices, O'Neil argues this is merely an expensive arms race that makes markets more opaque rather than better for non-insiders. She dismisses the notion that this leads to 'true prices' and suggests most data gathering may ultimately serve trading rather than public benefit.
key_points: Hedge funds claim to make markets efficient but their primary motivations are profit and lifestyle perks
Companies like Genscape sell expensive ($90,000/year) real-time data from surveillance and sensors to traders, information the public never sees
From the public's perspective, there's no clear benefit to prices adjusting faster to events we don't know about versus adjusting after events become public
The resources poured into gaining trading edges are vast - from drilling through mountains for nanosecond advantages to deploying hidden sensors
Market prices reflect not just real-world events but a chaotic mix of hedging, liquidations, Fed policy, and traders funding their Hamptons houses
money_quotes: The raison d'être of hedge funds is to make the markets efficient. Or at least that's one of the raisons d'être, the others being 1) to get rich and 2) to leave early on Fridays in the summer.
The high cost of much of the new information means that some forms of trading are becoming even more the province of firms with substantial resources.

Is this actually an improvement over the alternative version where something happens, and then the price adjusts? It's an expensive arms race, taking up vast resources, where things have only become more opaque.
Having worked in finance, I know the answer is a shit-ton, if it is profitable in a short-term edgy kind of way.
I'd like to perpetuate a new somewhat false myth that most data gathering and mining happens for the benefit of trading.
posts.key_insight: Market efficiency achieved through expensive proprietary surveillance benefits traders, not the public, creating an opaque arms race rather than a fairer system.

posts.summary: O'Neil explores the connection between empathy and gratitude, arguing they are fundamentally linked rather than separate virtues. She critiques common approaches to teaching gratitude, like having children list things they're fortunate to have, as hollow exercises that miss the deeper emotional work required. Drawing from her own experience of recognizing her father's flaws within herself and choosing forgiveness over self-hatred, she argues that true empathy comes from acknowledging our shared capacity for weakness and failure. This recognition of our own vulnerability—that we are 'one mistake away from fucking up'—creates genuine empathy, from which gratitude naturally flows. Unable to offer a simple formula, she concludes that the best approach is to model these qualities rather than demand them.
key_points: Gratitude and empathy are deeply connected—gratitude flows naturally from genuine empathy rather than from listing fortunate circumstances
Asking children to enumerate things they're grateful for is hollow because being fortunate is not the same as feeling grateful
True empathy emerges from recognizing our own capacity for the same weaknesses and mistakes we see in others
Privilege (education, race, social connections) insulates some people from the consequences of their mistakes while others suffer for similar errors
The best way to encourage empathy in children is through modeling it rather than demanding it
money_quotes: I don't think people can just conjure up gratitude.
Being fortunate is not the same as feeling grateful.
I could either hate myself as much as I hated him, or I could forgive both of us.
I am one mistake away from fucking up, or having too much debt, or being homeless or jobless.

I've been bailed out because of friends, or because I'm educated, or because I'm white. I am largely insulated from my bigger mistakes, but when I see someone in pain, it's my pain, because it could be me.
We also have empathy in recognition of making super shitty decisions and even of being cruel. Because cruelty is a weakness of ours too.
Instead of asking my kids to be grateful, I just try to do my best to role model empathy and gratitude myself, in my words and actions and especially in my inactions.
posts.key_insight: Genuine empathy—and the gratitude that flows from it—requires confronting and accepting our own capacity for the same failures and cruelties we observe in others.

posts.summary: O'Neil reviews a research paper examining how opinions and conviction levels propagate through social interactions. The study found that while people update their opinions when hearing similar views from others with strong convictions, they tend to ignore very different opinions. Crucially, conviction levels always rise through interaction but never fall, meaning that after many exchanges, high conviction correlates with consensus rather than correctness. The simulation revealed two attractor states: small groups of confident 'experts' (who may be wrong) and large majorities with weak but shared opinions.
key_points: People update opinions when hearing similar views with stronger conviction, but largely ignore very different opinions
Conviction rises when people hear agreement but doesn't fall when they hear disagreement, leading to high confidence regardless of accuracy
After many interactions, conviction correlates with consensus, not correctness
Two stable states emerge: influential 'experts' with strong convictions (regardless of accuracy) and large agreeing majorities
Random noise in the population weakens both the expert effect and majority effect
money_quotes: At the end of the day, after many interactions, the convictions of the group always ended up high even if the answer was wrong.
Conviction correlated to consensus rather than correctness after a few interactions.
In the presence of not much information, strong convictions might just imply lots of local agreement.
Small groups of 'experts,' defined as people with very strong convictions but who were not necessarily correct (think Larry Summers).
The 'I heard this so many times it must be true' effect seems to be directly in line with the agenda of Fox News.
If you're trying to effect opinions, you might either go with 'informing and educating the general public' or 'building up a sufficient squad of experts.'
posts.key_insight: Social dynamics cause conviction to correlate with consensus rather than truth, since hearing agreement strengthens beliefs while disagreement is simply ignored.

posts.summary: This advice column tackles three substantive questions and one playful one. A math tutor struggles with administrators who prioritize attendance numbers over effective teaching methods that require student participation. A woman asks how to handle sexist attacks in online forums, receiving advice to avoid toxic spaces entirely and never engage with identified assholes. The column also humorously addresses questions about sexy knitwear and women-friendly erotica, showcasing the author's irreverent range from serious gender issues to lighthearted topics.
key_points: Effective teaching that requires active student participation may conflict with institutional metrics that reward passive attendance numbers
Online anonymity enables hateful behavior, and the best response to sexist attacks is complete disengagement rather than continued interaction
Comment moderation is a necessary but burdensome investment to maintain quality discourse online
Women face a double standard in online spaces where they're targeted with gendered slurs for simply making intellectual points
The author advocates for finding curated spaces (online or offline) rather than tolerating toxic environments
money_quotes: If someone has identified themselves as an asshole, by saying something hateful and sexist to you after you try to make an intellectual point, please ignore them forever after.
What are you doing on discussion forums that allow such comments? Surely there are better things for you to do with your time. Like even just taking a nap would be a better use of your time.
It's a sad fact about anonymity of the web that there are weird, sad people who spend their time being hateful, as hateful as they can be, to as many people as they can be, via comments.
And if you tell me I'm being sexist because men still have the option to be on such forums without being called a cunt, I'd argue that anyone there is wasting their time.
Am I too low on the food-chain to be this principled about how to run a supplementary problem solving session for a single lower division math class?
I've had to go from allowing comments to moderating all mathbabe comments because of this problem, and it's a total pain in the ass, but the only other option is frankly to forbid all comments.
posts.key_insight: The best response to online harassment is not engagement or even dignified silence within the space, but complete withdrawal from toxic environments combined with permanent dismissal of anyone who reveals themselves to be arguing in bad faith.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines why women are underrepresented in finance and business leadership, rejecting the 'lean in' narrative in favor of a more provocative explanation: women may be less willing to compromise their ethics for money and status. She cites a research paper showing women are significantly less interested in jobs requiring ethical compromises, but equally interested when good ethics are present. The post argues that corporate diversity efforts often fail because companies want diverse workforces without changing their underlying culture.
key_points: Research shows women are less interested in jobs requiring ethical compromises, but equally interested in jobs with ethical cultures
The gender gap in business may partly reflect women's unwillingness to sacrifice ethics for success rather than lack of ambition
Companies that are diverse may be successful because they're 'nice places to be,' not the reverse
Diversity initiatives fail when companies try to achieve diversity without changing their underlying culture
Men face enormous cultural pressure to succeed at all costs, which may explain their greater willingness to accept ethical compromises
money_quotes: Here's another possibility, which I dig. They're less willing to sacrifice their ethics than their male colleagues for the sake of money and business success.
Is it because they don't like success? Or they don't like money? Are they forgetting to lean in sufficiently?
It's probably because they are nice places to be that women and others are there, which in turn makes them more successful.
They want to achieve diversity without changing their underlying culture.
I'm going to have to admit that men are under enormous pressure to succeed at all costs, which could explain why they're more willing to become ethically compromised to be successful.
Whenever I hear someone talking about 'leaning in' from now on, I will ask them, 'lean in to what?'
posts.key_insight: The underrepresentation of women in finance may reflect not a failure to 'lean in' but a rational refusal to lean into ethically compromised corporate cultures.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews a podcast discussion between Russ Roberts and Dallas Fed President Richard Fisher about Too-Big-To-Fail banks and Federal Reserve monetary policy. Fisher proposes a two-part solution: explicitly limiting federal protection to only commercial deposit-taking activities, and requiring all non-depositors to sign covenants acknowledging their investments aren't government-protected. The discussion also covers quantitative easing, which O'Neil argues primarily benefits wealthy people and corporations while doing little for ordinary Americans. She agrees with Fisher's TBTF proposals but expresses skepticism about free market solutions and impatience for policy changes that would direct federal spending toward ordinary people rather than financial institutions.
key_points: Fisher's TBTF solution involves limiting federal protection to deposits only and requiring signed covenants from all other bank stakeholders acknowledging no government guarantee
The current regulatory system is ineffective - it would take 24 million man hours just to interpret existing law to break down a single bank
Quantitative easing benefits rich people and corporations while GDP has decoupled from the fate of working Americans
Both Fisher and Roberts acknowledge TBTF will only be solved when a president wants it solved, but politicians don't want to lose Wall Street lobbying money
Large companies aren't hiring despite improved balance sheets because they've realized they don't need workers - they've harnessed IT
money_quotes: Given that he's an active central banker, he's refreshingly open and honest about stuff, even if every now and then he deliberately makes it seem like everything that happened was a mistake rather than a criminal act.
Roberts makes the point that he doesn't care so much that the system isn't fair, he cares only that this banking system isn't effective.
If the threat is that France might overtake us in an ineffective field such as finance, then so be it.
They both mention that TBTF will only be solved when a president wants it to be solved. But they also both agree that no politician wants to lose the money they get from Wall Street lobbyists.
Right now we see the GDP decoupled from the fate of the working man, and QE is a primary cause of that decoupling.
We still use GDP as proxy for growth and success for our economy, even though the benefits are almost exclusively going to rich people.
If we're going to spend $85 billion a month to save our economy, how about making state college tuition go down with that money?
posts.key_insight: Federal Reserve policies like quantitative easing and implicit TBTF guarantees have decoupled economic metrics from ordinary Americans' wellbeing, directing trillions in government support to wealthy investors while politicians refuse to act because they depend on Wall Street money.

posts.summary: This post, written by the Alternative Banking group of Occupy Wall Street, catalogues the worst Wall Street abuses of 2013 and highlights grassroots victories against financial industry power. The authors document how banks manipulated commodities markets, evaded prosecution, exploited homeowners through 'zombie foreclosures,' influenced legislation, and pushed for trade deals that would constrain regulation. They also note silver linings including Occupy the SEC's influence on the Volcker Rule, Richmond's creative use of eminent domain to help underwater homeowners, and Larry Summers not becoming Fed Chair. The piece argues that Wall Street's grip on Washington remains strong, but citizen activism and investigative journalism are fighting back.
key_points: Citigroup literally wrote legislation (HR 992) to roll back derivatives restrictions, with language adopted nearly word-for-word by Congress
Despite JP Morgan setting aside $28 billion for legal fees, no criminal prosecutions occurred—Eric Holder admitted megabanks were 'too big to prosecute'
Banks exploited homeowners through 'zombie foreclosures' where they evicted owners but didn't file papers, continuing to rack up fees on people who thought they'd lost their homes
Occupy the SEC's 325-page comment letter was cited 284 times in the final Volcker Rule, demonstrating grassroots impact on financial regulation
AIG's CEO compared criticism of executive bonuses to Jim Crow lynchings, revealing Wall Street's cultural disconnect from accountability
money_quotes: Mistaking a papercut for a crucifixion is the inevitable delusion of a pampered and under-prosecuted criminal upper class.
If only more cases had been brought to trial: juries are chomping at the bit to find banks guilty. Which is probably why more weren't.

Holder also admitted to Congress in March that the megabanks were too big to prosecute. While he later retracted the statement, his actions — never bringing criminal charges despite admitted criminal activities — speak louder than his words.
Minimal fines the banks have paid are simply the cost of doing business and are far outweighed by the money they've made.
When JPMorgan Chase wanted to settle their many legal difficulties, CEO Jamie Dimon had a private meeting with Holder to negotiate.

Larry Summers may finally have made too many obvious and ridiculous mistakes — deregulating financial derivatives to pave the way for the financial crisis, for one, and aiding criminal fraud in Russia for another — to be made, once again, one of the most powerful people in the world.
posts.key_insight: Wall Street's worst abuses persist not despite government oversight but because of regulatory capture, yet grassroots movements like Occupy the SEC prove that organized citizen engagement can still influence the rules.

posts.summary: This advice column by 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) addresses questions about dating politics, career gaps for caregiving, body acceptance, and genetic privacy concerns. The most substantive response tackles body image, where O'Neil offers practical advice while critiquing the weight loss industry and medical profession's unhelpful approach to weight. She advocates for 'body eumorphia' - a deliberate belief in one's own attractiveness. The column also touches on 23andMe's data collection practices and their connection to Google through Sergey Brin, expressing concern about genetic privacy.
key_points: Doctors telling patients to lose weight is 'kind of useless and promotes guilt' without solving the problem, since nobody really knows how to lose weight
The weight loss advertising industry deliberately makes people feel bad to sell products - recognizing this manipulation is key to body acceptance
O'Neil coins 'body eumorphia' as the opposite of body dysmorphia: deliberately believing you're attractive and willing it to be true
23andMe's genetic testing has 'incredible potential for creepiness' especially combined with Google's data collection
Career gaps for caregiving deserve honest explanation - mathematicians have sympathy for caring for ill parents
money_quotes: Keep in mind that nobody really knows how to lose weight, including doctors, so a doctor telling you to deal with it is kind of useless and promotes guilt in you without solving the problem. Fuck that.
There's an industry out there trying to make you feel bad so you will pay for their products. Fuck that.
I've invented another condition which I've termed 'body eumorphia' and which consists of believing I'm pretty much irresistibly attractive, at least to people who are wise enough to acknowledge it.
Other people are at risk for diseases too, and that doesn't make them question their attractiveness. Try to separate the two issues.
The whole industry has an incredible potential for creepiness, and I'm glad you're thinking about ways to push back.
Always talk politics afterwards. If then.
posts.key_insight: Body acceptance requires recognizing that both the medical establishment and weight loss industry profit from making people feel inadequate, and the antidote is deliberate self-belief combined with rejecting manipulative media.

posts.summary: Parents across New York and other states are fighting back against InBloom, a data platform that collects extensive information about children's learning without parental consent or opt-out options. The platform, funded by the Gates Foundation and developed by Rupert Murdoch's News Corp, allows third-party companies to plug into student data for various purposes. Despite claims that data won't be sold, InBloom itself has admitted it cannot guarantee data security. The backlash has been significant, with six of nine original partner states withdrawing from the program. O'Neil supports using technology in education but argues that meaningful public discussion about data sharing, control, and protection must happen first.
key_points: InBloom collects student data (names, addresses, test scores, health records) without parental consent or opt-out options, all done through school systems
The company has explicitly stated it cannot guarantee data security or prevent interception during transmission
The platform is designed to let third parties plug into student data, opening doors for mining and marketing purposes
Six of nine original partner states have already backed out due to parent outcry, and NYC's Bill de Blasio has sided with privacy advocates
New York spent $50 million in federal grants on this partnership despite the lack of public input
money_quotes: The company 'cannot guarantee the security of the information stored … or that the information will not be intercepted when it is being transmitted.'
Although the InBloomers claim that no data about their kids will ever be sold, that doesn't mean it won't be used by third parties for various mining purposes and possibly marketing.
Not cool that this is being done on the downlow.
Critics said that school districts, through inBloom, were giving their children's confidential data away to companies who sought to profit by proposing a solution to a problem that does not exist.
First we need to have a really good, public discussion about how this data is being shared, controlled, and protected, and that simply hasn't happened.
posts.key_insight: Educational technology initiatives that collect children's data without parental consent or public debate, while admitting they cannot guarantee security, represent a dangerous precedent regardless of their stated good intentions.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil summarizes and comments on Judge Jed Rakoff's essay explaining why no Wall Street executives went to jail after the 2008 financial crisis. Rakoff systematically dismantles the Department of Justice's excuses—difficulty proving intent, sophisticated counterparties, and 'too big to jail'—showing each to be legally flimsy. He argues the real reasons include prosecutorial distraction, government complicity in deregulation, and the misguided trend of prosecuting corporations instead of individuals. O'Neil endorses most of Rakoff's analysis but disagrees about the revolving door's impact, believing captured regulators lack incentive to pursue difficult cases against potential future employers.
key_points: The DOJ's three official excuses for not prosecuting (proving intent, sophisticated counterparties, too big to jail) don't hold up under legal scrutiny
'Willful blindness' is an established legal doctrine for proving intent, making the 'we couldn't prove they knew' excuse hollow
Prosecuting corporations instead of individuals is both legally suspect and morally wrong—it punishes innocent employees while letting guilty executives walk
The government's own complicity in deregulation and crisis management created conflicts of interest that discouraged prosecution
The lack of prosecution exposes a two-tiered justice system where wealth and crime complexity determine accountability
money_quotes: If there was intentional fraud, then it's a reflection on a dysfunctional justice system that nobody has gone to jail
That poor people can be prosecuted but the rich are protected

Under the law, you should not indict or threaten to indict a company unless you can prove beyond a reasonable doubt that some managerial agent of the company committed the alleged crime; and if you can prove that, why not indict the manager?
Punishing a company and its many innocent employees and shareholders for the crimes committed by some unprosecuted individuals seems contrary to elementary notions of moral responsibility
Depending on how rich you are and how complicated your crime is, you can avoid going to jail
In the past few months I've gone from being angry at the bankers to being angry at the prosecutors
posts.key_insight: The failure to prosecute Wall Street executives isn't about legal impossibility but about institutional capture, misaligned incentives, and a two-tiered justice system that protects the wealthy and powerful.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post promoting an upcoming Alt Banking meeting where members will watch and discuss 'Four Horsemen,' a documentary by Renegade Economists. The post provides logistical details including time, location at Columbia University, and how to join the email list. Rather than offering analysis or argument, it serves as a community organizing notice for the Occupy Wall Street-affiliated alternative banking group.
key_points: Alt Banking OWS group is hosting a documentary screening instead of their usual meeting format
The film 'Four Horsemen' is available both on YouTube for free and for purchase ($5 download)
The meeting is open to newcomers who can join via email list or the Alt Banking webpage
money_quotes: We're having a special Alt Banking meeting where, instead of having our usual format, we're all watching Four Horsemen
posts.key_insight: This post functions as community organizing outreach rather than substantive analysis, reflecting the author's involvement in grassroots economic reform movements.

posts.summary: O'Neil predicts Facebook's decline, arguing that the platform has become too bland and commercialized to attract younger users who want to escape parental oversight. She contends that Facebook's success in welcoming parents and making the platform inoffensive has paradoxically sealed its fate with the next generation. The post extends this analysis to email, suggesting it's being replaced by texting as the privileged form of communication, with even newer, more exclusive communication methods likely to emerge.
key_points: Facebook's parent-friendly design has made it unappealing to younger users who see it as another space for authority figures to monitor them
A new social platform will eventually capture the younger generation who want distance from family and teachers
Email is losing its status as a privileged communication channel because too many people have access to each other's addresses
Future communication may require additional authentication beyond contact information to signal message importance
money_quotes: Facebook is boring. Super boring, and super commercial and getting worse.
It's used anyway, by almost everyone, because everyone else they care about is already on it.
Inoffensive to the point of being utterly bland.

The kids today are just not going to be interested in adopting Facebook. Why should they, when their parents and teachers and coaches are on there already, and it's just another place where they can get into trouble with authority?

My theory is that some other sexier and edgier social media platform is going to come along and sweep up the next generation of users who want to get the hell away from their parents and grandparents.
Too many people know each others' email addresses, so knowing someone's phone number is really the only sign of authentic connection.
posts.key_insight: Platforms that optimize for mass adoption and safety inevitably become too bland for the next generation, who will flee to wherever their parents aren't.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil previews her participation in a Joint Math Meetings panel about the public face of mathematics. She distinguishes her work from other panelists who explain math to the public, noting that she instead focuses on critiquing mathematical culture and calling out the misuse of math's authority. She specifically targets how public trust in mathematics gets exploited to lend false objectivity to political agendas, citing Bill Gates as an example. The post solicits reader questions for the panel moderator.
key_points: O'Neil sees her role as critiquing math culture rather than explaining math concepts to the public
Public trust in mathematics is being exploited to give political campaigns a veneer of objectivity
Mathematical modeling is often presented as objective when it is not
She focuses on issues like women in math and the misapplication of mathematical authority
There's a distinction between teaching math and calling out its misuse
money_quotes: I specialize in calling out the misapplication of mathematical imprimatur.
The public's trust in mathematics and mathematician is being perverted into a political campaign about the (supposed) objectivity of mathematical modeling.
I don't spend too much time explaining math to the general public.
posts.key_insight: The public's trust in mathematics is being weaponized to make political arguments appear objective, and someone needs to call this out.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses three reader questions: a woman scientist struggling with her husband's defensiveness about gender bias in academia, a couple wondering about transactional sex for household chores, and a woman tired of pressure about having children. Aunt Pythia draws on her own experience as an academic married to another academic to explain how cognitive dissonance prevents her husband from acknowledging systemic sexism that benefits him. She advises understanding that his ego is invested in his success being purely earned, while channeling his feminism toward helping others. The other questions receive characteristically frank, pragmatic advice about sexual negotiations and deflecting social pressure.
key_points: Cognitive dissonance explains why successful academics struggle to acknowledge that systemic bias helped them - their identity is wrapped up in their achievements being earned
The letter writer's marriage improved dramatically when she took a job at a different institution, removing her husband's conflicting loyalties
Ambivalence about personal choices invites unwanted advice - stating preferences definitively shuts down pushy conversations
Partners may have very different perceptions of what is 'demeaning' - the men polled found transactional arrangements perfectly acceptable while women worried about demeaning them
Systemic bias is frustrating precisely because individuals cannot directly control it, even when they recognize it exists
money_quotes: It's not an exaggeration to say that my marriage improved 100% on the 'supportiveness' front, when I took a job in a place my husband didn't work.
He had no stake in those assholes liking him, so he could hate them with me and for me.

When you challenge the fact that he's being offered good jobs and you're not, he can't actually think clearly about it, because the key fact for him is that he's being offered good jobs and that's something he's been working towards for half his life or more.
I don't avoid fights at home. I mean, I don't go looking for them, but I certainly don't avoid them.
The problem here is that it's a systemic bias thing, and your husband is not directly in control of it, and neither are you. It's really frustrating but that's one reason it continues to exist.
People interpret ambivalence as an opportunity for advice, and people want kids.
posts.key_insight: Even well-meaning people experience cognitive dissonance that prevents them from acknowledging systemic biases that benefit them, because doing so would undermine their sense of having earned their success.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces she is writing a book called 'Weapons of Math Destruction' for Random House, working with editor Amanda Cook. She describes the book proposal process with her agent Jay Mandel, noting that Crown Publishing made a pre-emptive offer before a bidding war could develop. The book will argue that mathematical modeling has become a destructive force across finance, education, medicine, politics, and the workplace, exacerbating inequality and endangering democracy. She solicits reader input on examples of problematic models and feedback loops to include in the book.
key_points: The book will examine how mathematical models have become pervasive and destructive across multiple sectors of society
Key themes include feedback loops, perverted incentives, and 'creepy modeling' that exacerbate inequality
Value Added Models for teachers and Value-at-Risk in finance are already confirmed as examples
O'Neil is actively crowdsourcing examples from readers to ensure comprehensive coverage
The book builds on her unique background as a Harvard math PhD, former Wall Street quant, and Occupy movement advisor
money_quotes: Mathematical modeling has become a pervasive and destructive force in society—in finance, education, medicine, politics, and the workplace
Current models exacerbate inequality and endanger democracy
I'd love my readers to weigh in on crucial examples of models and industries that you think might get overlooked
Your favorite example of a family of models... that is illustrative of feedback loops, or perverted incentives, or creepy modeling
posts.key_insight: Mathematical models, despite their veneer of objectivity, can become weapons that entrench inequality and threaten democratic institutions when deployed without accountability.

posts.summary: O'Neil poses a thought experiment about a hypothetical pill that makes you appear perfectly average for five years. She initially considers taking it, but realizes she'd prefer to spend time with people who refused the pill because they'd be more interesting and less insecure. This leads her to conclude she wouldn't take the pill because signaling interestingness matters more to her than easily-obtained attractiveness. The experiment reveals how social dynamics and signaling shape our choices about conformity and appearance.
key_points: A pill offering cheap, perfect averageness seems appealing until you consider the social implications
People who refuse easy conformity signal something valuable: confidence, interestingness, authenticity
Our choices about appearance are less about how we look and more about what those choices signal to others
The thought experiment separates appearance from health to isolate purely social dynamics
The duration of the effect (5 years vs 12 hours) fundamentally changes the calculation
money_quotes: I'd rather hang out with people who refused the pill.
They'd be strangely not insecure. They might even be proud of pimples and asymmetries, in a world of everyone looking super perfect.
I wouldn't take the pill because it would matter more to me to signal interestingness than to be considered attractive, especially if attractiveness was cheaply gotten.
Average is in a certain sense quite beautiful... But of course, it's also not very interesting, being totally average.
The entire game changes if the pill only lasted for 12 hours.
posts.key_insight: When conformity becomes cheap and accessible, nonconformity becomes the more valuable social signal—we judge people not just by how they look but by the choices they made to look that way.

posts.summary: O'Neil proposes two ideas to make math research more engaging and collaborative. First, she suggests mathematicians adopt the Royal Statistical Society's tradition of holding public discussions about papers, where experts debate a paper and the author responds, with the entire conversation published. Second, she proposes using NLP techniques to cluster math papers by topic, going beyond simple citation graphs to find related work through textual analysis. She acknowledges the organizing challenges but argues both ideas would reduce the isolation mathematicians often feel and help researchers find relevant work more easily.
