key_points: The Royal Statistical Society has a long tradition of formal public paper discussions that mathematics could adopt
Finding people reading the same math papers is rare and requires significant effort to organize
Citation graphs miss papers on similar topics; NLP-based clustering could find related work through titles, abstracts, and introductions
Mathematicians' love of distinctive terminology (like 'Flabby Sheaves') makes NLP techniques particularly well-suited to the field
Cross-field discovery remains challenging because different disciplines often name the same concepts differently
money_quotes: One thing that kind of depressed me about math is how rarely you'd find people reading the same papers unless you specifically got a group of people together to do so, which was a lot of work.
Right now the most obvious way to find a 'nearby' paper is to look at the graph of papers by direct reference, but you're probably missing out on lots of stuff that way.
The good news here is that mathematicians are in love with terminology, and give weird names to things that make NLP techniques very happy.
My favorite recent example which I hear Johan muttering under his breath from time to time is Flabby Sheaves. There's no way that's not a distinctive phrase.

Such techniques won't help at all in finding different fields who have come across the same idea but have different names for the relevant objects. But that's OK, because it means there's still lots of work for mathematicians.
posts.key_insight: Mathematics research would benefit from borrowing collaborative practices from other fields—both social (public paper discussions) and technological (NLP-based paper discovery)—to combat the isolation inherent in specialized research.

posts.summary: O'Neil explores the fundamental purpose of regulation after attending a webinar on K-12 student data privacy. She argues that regulation exists so ordinary people don't have to become experts to protect themselves—just as HIPAA protects medical data without requiring patients to understand healthcare IT. She applies this framework to financial regulation, noting that the industry has made things deliberately complex to evade oversight. While education data privacy lags behind technology, she's optimistic it's solvable and will gain bipartisan support since children are involved.
key_points: Regulation exists to protect people who cannot reasonably become experts in every domain that affects their lives
The financial industry has weaponized complexity to make effective regulation nearly impossible
Anti-regulation arguments assume everyone is an omniscient free agent with unlimited time to become expert on everything
Education data privacy is solvable because the issues aren't inherently complex—regulators just haven't caught up with technology
The educational benefits of mass data collection remain unproven and don't justify poor privacy standards
money_quotes: Does every parent have to become a data expert in order to protect their children's data?
The financial industry has figured out how to make things so goddamn complicated that nobody can figure out how to regulate anything well.
They've somehow, at least so far, also been able to insist things need to be this complicated.
Most of the time they act like everyone is a omniscient free agent who spends all their time becoming expert on everything.

We live in a world where most people have no clue about the issues of data privacy, never mind when it's being shielded by ridiculous and possibly illegal contracts behind their kids' public school system.
The education benefits of collecting all this data have not been proven at all, nor do they really require such shitty privacy standards even if they do work.
posts.key_insight: Regulation's core purpose is to protect ordinary people from having to become experts in every complex system that affects their lives—and industries that weaponize complexity are essentially evading this social contract.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her attendance at the Joint Mathematics Meeting, noting the irony that she's attended more JMM conferences since leaving academia than during her 17 years in math. She mentions her upcoming panel on Public Facing Math and fondly recalls meeting Cora Sadosky at a past conference. The post highlights her plans to attend the HCSSiM breakfast to lobby for a summer teaching position and to engage in hallway conversations and gossip, which she suggests might be the conference's true purpose.
key_points: O'Neil has attended more math conferences (3) in 7 years outside academia than in 17 years within it (2)
Meeting mathematician Cora Sadosky at a JMM remains her favorite conference memory
She uses the HCSSiM breakfast as an annual opportunity to pitch herself for summer teaching
She views hallway gossip and networking as the real accomplishment of academic conferences
money_quotes: I've been to more Joint Math Meetings in the 7 years since I left academic math (3) than I did in the 17 years I was actually in math (2).
Besides the above plan, I plan to meet people in the hallways and gossip. That's all I have ever accomplished here.
I hope it is the official mission of the conference, but I'm not sure.
posts.key_insight: The informal networking and gossip at academic conferences may be more valuable than the official programming.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on topics ranging from academic job searches to gender identity to relationship dynamics. The questions span professional etiquette in academia, whether personality traits should be categorized by gender, cultural adjustment challenges, and navigating intimacy during a partner's mental health crisis. O'Neil's responses are characteristically direct and often challenge the premises embedded in readers' questions, particularly around gender stereotypes. The column showcases her pragmatic, no-nonsense approach while occasionally deflecting questions she finds unanswerable.
key_points: In academic job searches, direct personal contact from applicants is welcomed and important - but spouses should not contact departments on behalf of applicants
Categorizing personality traits as 'male' or 'female' is unnecessarily limiting; people are considered creative when they successfully ignore stereotypes
Most people feel like 'freaks on the inside' - the key is to own your inner freak rather than conforming to categories including gender
Consensual kink with proper communication and safe words can be a healthy release during difficult times, not necessarily harmful to someone experiencing anxiety or depression
money_quotes: If people interpret her power as male then they are just being narrow.
I don't categorize my many traits as male or female. Why should I? They're all mine.
Don't most people feel like freaks on the inside? I think so. The trick is to own the inner freak, and freaks transcend categories, including gender categories.
People get considered 'creative' if they successfully ignore stereotypes.
He's gotta take one for the team here!
posts.key_insight: Authentic self-expression means refusing to categorize your traits and behaviors into socially-prescribed boxes - creativity and freedom come from transcending stereotypes rather than conforming to them.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues against the mathematics community's growing reliance on billionaire philanthropy to solve its public relations and funding problems. While acknowledging that funders like Jim Simons have done good work, she warns that private funding creates dependency that erodes public funding, concentrates power over the field's direction in unaccountable hands, and removes democratic control over how mathematics presents itself to the world. She advocates instead for mathematicians to engage politically, represent themselves, and fight to restore NSF funding rather than accept private money that comes with hidden costs.
key_points: Mathematicians' apolitical nature makes them eager to outsource PR to wealthy donors rather than engage themselves
Private funding creates a feedback loop: public funding never returns once philanthropy fills the gap, because the need appears solved
Billionaire funding shifts control over the field's image and priorities to unaccountable individuals who could change their minds at any moment
Distribution of private funds may reflect donors' preferences rather than good governance or community needs
The mathematics community should represent itself politically and focus on restoring public funding rather than becoming addicted to private money
money_quotes: We lose something when we consistently take money from rich people, which has nothing to do with any specific rich person who might have great ideas and great intentions.
The first thing we lose is power, and specifically control over our own image.
It might be weird to all of a sudden need to know what some rich guy thinks of a specific issue.
As public funding dries up for something like public parks (or from the NSF) and as a community gets desperate for basic operating funds, money from rich individuals seems like a godsend.
The public funding never ever comes back. Because, after all, why should it? It looks like everything is well-funded.
The entire system depends on the generosity of someone who could change his mind at any moment.
My suggestion is that we should think about representing ourselves in this PR campaign, if we have one to wage.
posts.key_insight: Accepting billionaire philanthropy as a solution to public funding shortfalls creates a dependency trap that permanently displaces democratic accountability with private control.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that math research in academia is fundamentally subsidized by calculus teaching, which serves as the 'cash cow' that funds faculty positions. With MOOCs and online tools poised to replace traditional calculus instruction at scale, she predicts a dramatic reduction in math faculty positions—potentially over 50%—within a decade. She criticizes mathematicians for failing to recognize this threat or promote the value of their research, having never needed to justify their existence before. The post warns that without organized advocacy, math research could regress to 1850s levels, concentrated only at elite institutions.
key_points: Math research is essentially a byproduct of calculus teaching, with 85% of college math students taking only calculus
MOOCs and online tools will soon replace much of traditional calculus instruction, potentially outsourcing human support to overseas call centers
Mathematicians have never had to promote their work because calculus teaching subsidized it automatically
The disruption timeline suggests math departments will shrink rather than expand within 10 years
Effective advocacy for math research requires community organizing rather than dependence on billionaire donors
money_quotes: Math research is essentially funded through these teaching jobs.
It's called progress, and machines have been doing it for more than a hundred years, replacing skilled craftspeople.
Math research, which we've basically been getting for free all this time, as a byproduct of calculus, will be severely curtailed.

Mathematicians haven't been promoting their work for the simple reason that they haven't had to, because they had this cash cow called calculus which many of them aren't even aware of as a great thing.

We're going to think about it pretty hard and learn how to promote math research very soon, or else we're going back to 1850 levels of math research, where everyone knew each other and stuff was done by letter.
For my younger friends who are interested in going to grad school now, I'm not writing them letters of recommendation before having this talk.
posts.key_insight: The entire edifice of academic math research rests on the fragile foundation of calculus instruction revenue, and technologists are about to kick that foundation out from under it.

posts.summary: O'Neil criticizes a viral Numberphile video that claims the sum of all positive integers equals -1/12, arguing that presenting this result without proper context is destructive to public mathematical understanding. She explains the crucial distinction between convergent series (like geometric series that have intuitive values) and divergent series (like 1+2+3+...) that don't converge in any traditional sense. While mathematicians have assigned values to divergent series through analytic continuation, these assignments exist in a specialized mathematical framework that requires careful explanation. The video's failure to explain this context, instead calling it 'mathematical hocus pocus,' represents a fundamental betrayal of what math actually is.
key_points: The sum 1+2+3+... doesn't have a value until mathematicians assign it one through specific, rigorous methods that must be explained
Convergent series like 1/2+1/4+1/8+... have intuitive values because their partial sums approach a limit, unlike divergent series
Mathematicians have constructed internally consistent frameworks where divergent series can be assigned values, but these are qualitatively different from intuitive convergence
Public math communication must respect and build on intuition rather than undermine it with unexplained 'hocus pocus'
The video's MSRI endorsement and Slate coverage made its pedagogical failures particularly damaging
money_quotes: Anything that is hocus pocus isn't actually math.
People who don't understand that shouldn't be making math videos for public consumption.

But here's the thing, that doesn't actually have a value. That is, it doesn't have a value until you assign it a value, which you can do but then you might want to absolutely positively must explain how you've done so.
That doesn't mean the identity is 'true' in any intuitively plausible sense of the word. It means that mathematicians are scrappy.
If you can't make any sense of it, intuitive or otherwise, and if you have to ascribe it to 'mathematical hocus pocus,' then you're definitely doing something wrong. Please stop.
We use it as a demonstration of flawed mathematical thinking. Then we take note and make sure we're more careful in the future.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical results that seem like 'hocus pocus' aren't magic—they're either poorly explained rigor or actual errors, and conflating the two destroys public trust in mathematical reasoning.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post about Cathy O'Neil's upcoming speaking engagements. She mentions modifying her 'Start Your Own Netflix' talk for Worcester Polytechnic Institute, planning an MSRI-Evans talk at Berkeley where she's deciding between her Netflix talk or a 'World Is Going To Hell' talk about algorithmic dangers, and hoping to visit the Noetherian Ring women in math group she participated in as an undergrad in the early 1990s.
key_points: She has multiple versions of talks: a technical Netflix recommendation system talk and a 'doomsday' talk about algorithmic harms
She's actively soliciting audience input on which talk to give at Berkeley
She fondly recalls the Noetherian Ring as an early supportive environment for women in math that helped her give her first talks
money_quotes: I still haven't decided whether to give a 'The World Is Going To Hell' talk, which would be kind of the technical version of my book
It really was a great example of how to create a supportive environment for collaborative math
posts.key_insight: This administrative post reveals O'Neil's dual identity as both a technical mathematician interested in recommendation systems and a critic warning about algorithmic dangers, while showing her commitment to supporting women in math.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that mathematicians must proactively define success metrics for calculus MOOCs before administrators impose purely financial measures. She draws a parallel to the failures of Value-Added Models in K-12 education, where poorly defined metrics have caused widespread harm. The post proposes randomized experiments comparing different teaching methods with pre-defined success criteria that go beyond test scores to include student experience and conceptual understanding. She acknowledges the irony that mathematicians studying MOOCs might accelerate their adoption, but argues it's better to shape the outcome than let others control it.
key_points: Large-scale educational models require fixed definitions of success, and if mathematicians don't define them, administrators will default to financial metrics
Value-Added Models in K-12 teaching serve as a cautionary tale of what happens when success metrics are poorly defined and opaque
Success metrics should be flexible enough to acknowledge different contexts need different outcomes
Experiments should be designed with pre-registered metrics, including oral exams and student attitudes, not just final exam scores
Taking control of the research now is preferable to having solutions imposed later
money_quotes: The problem with large-scale models is that they are easier to build if you have some fixed definition of success against which to optimize.
If we mathematicians don't get busy thinking this through, my fear is that administrations will do it for us, and will come up with things based strictly on money and not so much on pedagogy.
Let's keep things as complicated as they need to be to get things right!
One might complain that, if we do this, we're actually speeding on our own deaths by accelerating the MOOCs in the classroom. But I think it's important we take control before someone else does.
Design the questions you will ask your students and yourself before the semester begins so as to practice good model design – we don't want to decide on our metric after the fact.
Value-Added Models have no defined metric of success and are tearing up a generation of students and teachers.
posts.key_insight: When large-scale educational systems require optimization metrics, the people who understand pedagogy must define success before administrators reduce it to cost savings.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her 25+ years of volunteer work with Fair Foods, a food rescue program in Dorchester, Massachusetts. She contrasts the crack epidemic era of the late 1980s with today's quieter but equally devastating poverty crisis. The lines for salvaged food have grown longer, neighborhoods show signs of economic decay, and many children she once knew are now incarcerated. She argues that despite lower crime rates, we've created a different kind of hell—one of quiet desperation and growing hunger, made more unjust by simultaneous food stamp cuts and Wall Street bonuses.
key_points: Fair Foods rescues produce too old for supermarkets but still edible, distributing it at 'dollar bag' sites to feed low-income families
Demand for salvaged food has increased dramatically over 25 years, despite the end of the crack epidemic and lower crime rates
The visible markers of neighborhood decline—abandoned Victorians, fewer cars, incarcerated youth—tell a story of quiet economic devastation
The juxtaposition of food stamp cuts with banker bonuses reveals a profound moral failure in American priorities
money_quotes: But nowadays, when the crime rate is so much lower, we're seeing another kind of hell. It's a lot quieter.
It's incredibly sad to see how much more demand there is for salvaged food now than there was 25 years ago.
Business is booming this winter.
Many of the customers of Fair Foods are old friends by now, they've been coming weekly to various sites for many years to feed their children and their grandchildren.
It makes me wonder what kind of country we've created where people are so hungry, we're reducing food stamps, and Jamie Dimon is getting an extra big bonus.
Where is the justice in that?
posts.key_insight: The decline in visible crime has masked a quieter, deeper crisis of poverty and hunger that reveals the moral bankruptcy of a system that rewards bankers while cutting food assistance.

posts.summary: O'Neil uses biting satire to argue that Jamie Dimon's $20 million raise actually undervalues his contribution to the banking industry—not for legitimate business reasons, but for establishing the precedent that massive financial fraud carries minimal consequences. She argues that by personally negotiating settlements with the Attorney General and avoiding criminal prosecution despite documented fraudulent behavior, Dimon effectively set the 'price of fraud' low enough that all Too-Big-To-Fail banks benefit. The post suggests competing banks should collectively reward Dimon for this regulatory arbitrage that will enable future profitable misconduct across the entire industry.
key_points: JP Morgan's settlements established a precedent that large-scale bank fraud results in negotiated fines rather than criminal prosecution
The beneficiaries of Dimon's precedent-setting extend beyond JP Morgan to all Too-Big-To-Fail banks
Dimon personally calling the Attorney General to negotiate settlements represents an extraordinary level of access and impunity
The regulatory capture is so complete that the author sarcastically suggests Eric Holder himself deserves a bonus from the banks
money_quotes: the guy should have gotten way more, considering he set the price of fraud by big banks so low and in so many different ways

the regulatory arbitrage which he displayed, and which now exists as a precedent for all bankers for the rest of eternity, benefitted not just him, not just JP Morgan Chase, but all the Too-Big-To-Fail banks
this is the guy who called Attorney General Eric Holder on the phone and negotiated a settlement, for christ's sake! Who DOES that?
That's really above and beyond the chutzpah of even the most criminal of masterminds
Only the creamiest of the crop, only the most devoted of banker psychopaths can get away with that shit
I say you guys perform the equivalent of a kowtow in Wall Street terms, which is of course monetized, in the form of a check
posts.key_insight: The true scandal isn't Dimon's raise—it's that he established the precedent that Too-Big-To-Fail banks can negotiate their way out of criminal liability for fraud, making that raise a bargain for the entire industry.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil summarizes Diane Ravitch's lecture criticizing the Common Core initiative, which Ravitch argues was developed by Gates Foundation people with ties to the testing industry rather than experienced educators. Ravitch contends the standards are rigid, untested, and based on the flawed premise that harder tests make students learn more. The post highlights how standardized testing is used for punishment rather than diagnosis, and how blaming teachers distracts from the real cause of educational underperformance: poverty. O'Neil emphasizes Ravitch's point that the manufactured 'education emergency' primarily benefits private testing and charter school companies.
key_points: Common Core was developed by Gates Foundation affiliates connected to the testing industry, not experienced educators, raising concerns about vested interests
The underlying assumption that raising standards and increasing testing makes students learn faster is fundamentally flawed
Standardized testing functions as punishment rather than diagnostic tool - if we wanted to help struggling students, we would help them rather than make tests harder
Poverty is the actual cause of educational underperformance, and blaming teachers is a distraction from addressing the real problem
The narrative of an 'education emergency' is manufactured to benefit private testing companies and charter schools through privatization
money_quotes: Testing makes kids learn, and more testing makes kids learn faster. That setting a high bar makes kids suddenly be able to jump higher.
Just because you raise standards doesn't mean people suddenly know more.
Standardized testing is used as punishment rather than diagnostic.
If we were interested in finding out who needs help and giving them help, we wouldn't need harder and harder tests, we'd just look at who is struggling with the current tests and go help them.
Poverty causes kids to underperform in schools, and blaming the teachers on poverty is a huge distraction and meaningless for those kids.
There is no actual educational emergency in this country. In fact, test scores and graduation rates have never been higher for each racial group.

If people are convinced there is an emergency in education, then the private companies – test prep and testing companies as well as companies that run charter school – stand to make big money from our response.
posts.key_insight: The education reform movement's focus on standardized testing and teacher accountability serves corporate interests while ignoring poverty as the root cause of educational inequality.

posts.summary: This post announces a Friday protest in Queens against the Trans-Pacific Partnership Agreement (TPP) as part of a coordinated global day of action. O'Neil challenges the 'free trade' framing of TPP, arguing the agreement actually increases corporate legal power against governments. She cites the example of tobacco companies gaining leverage over countries like Namibia. The post provides detailed logistics for the Queens rally, which features street theater mocking Representative Crowley and performances by activist groups.
key_points: The TPP is framed as 'free trade' but actually expands corporate power over sovereign governments
Language in political initiatives is deliberately chosen to sound appealing regardless of actual content
The 'Fast Track' process for TPP limits democratic oversight of the agreement
The protest uses creative tactics like street theater and satirical performances to draw attention
money_quotes: Language is a tricky thing, and people choose the names of their initiatives to make them sound good.
Turns out that, in this case, 'free trade' is a weird phrase to describe a campaign that increases the legal power of corporations (ex: tobacco companies) against governments (ex: Namibia).
We know that from the 'pro life' and 'pro choice' debate.
posts.key_insight: The label 'free trade' obscures how agreements like TPP actually function to expand corporate power at the expense of democratic governance.

posts.summary: The post recaps a Columbia Journalism School panel on journalism in the post-Snowden era, featuring editors from the NYT and Guardian alongside legal and government perspectives. The discussion centered on how journalists verified and published the Snowden documents, the legal protections historically afforded to press, and the chilling effect of recent government actions against whistleblowers. Panelists debated whether secure communication between journalists and sources is even possible anymore given mass surveillance. O'Neil concludes by reflecting on how journalism's explicit consideration of public good contrasts sharply with the profit-first mentality she observed in tech and finance.
key_points: The Guardian applied two key tests before publishing: Is the story true? Is it in the public interest?
Recent government actions, including labeling journalists as 'accomplices,' have created a chilling effect on national security reporting
The lack of secure messaging systems affects what ordinary citizens can learn about the world, not just whistleblowers
Obama's aggressive prosecution of whistleblowers directly contradicts his campaign promises on transparency
Journalism's explicit focus on public good is notably absent from tech and finance industries
money_quotes: We normal citizens might not think we care about having secure communications, since we don't intend to do top secret messaging, but even so the lack of secure messaging systems for other people effects what we learn about the world.
I find it absolutely amazing that an entire profession like journalism would actually consider the public good as a major question they put before them before they choose what to work on.
I'm coming from inside the tech industry and finance, where the only question that is ever asked is whether an idea is profitable and, secondarily, legal.
Is this story true? Is this story in the public's interest?
The question came up of whether it even makes sense for a journalist to suggest to a source that they'd be protected, given how much surveillance now exists.
I do think we need to have people like Snowden, and whistleblowers more generally, and that in any case journalists absolutely need legal protection to do their jobs.
posts.key_insight: Mass surveillance doesn't just threaten whistleblowers and journalists—it ultimately determines what information ordinary citizens can access about their own world.

posts.summary: This advice column tackles questions about dating, relationships, and ethics with characteristic bluntness. Aunt Pythia advises a self-proclaimed expert lover to openly brag about his skills to interested women, counsels an academic partner to consider all family options including leaving academia, delivers a harsh reality check to a man who lost his fortune to a swindler and still maintains wealthy airs, critiques the gendered 'bro formula' for age-appropriate dating, and greenlights using OCR technology even when hired as a typist.
key_points: The academic two-body problem persists because the tenure system was designed for an era when wives followed husbands' careers
The dating age formula reflects the difficulty of maintaining equitable relationships across vastly different life experiences
People who lost money to bad investments often maintain false facades rather than confronting their true financial situation
Hypersexualized pop music like Girls Generation is designed specifically for leering, making viewers complicit in its purpose
If someone hires you without specifying methods, using efficient technology instead of manual labor isn't unethical
money_quotes: What you've explained is that you exceed expectations, but then again that means you set them low to begin with.
The truth is that this system was set up in a different age, where women and families were expected to follow husbands around. So sometimes it just sucks.
You made a bunch of money for the sole reason that you sold your soul at the right time. Then you got out and gave all your money to a swindler.
Get over yourself, and especially the idea that you need to be rich. Just get a job like everyone else and make sure you live within your means.
Why is that the 'bro' formula? Why does it have to always be about men and younger women?
When you check out the hip maneuvering on a music video, you are probably not expecting a long-term relationship with the doll-like characters.
If I had a young daughter I'd probably want to keep that stuff away from her, it looks like a breeding ground for eating disorders.
If someone is dumb enough to not use technology, no reason you shouldn't.
posts.key_insight: Advice-giving works best when it punctures pretension and forces people to confront uncomfortable truths about their self-image and motivations.

posts.summary: O'Neil reviews the 1954 classic 'How to Lie with Statistics' and evaluates whether her forthcoming book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' serves as its modern equivalent. She finds that while some statistical deception techniques remain relevant (selection bias, survivorship bias, misdirection), the big data era has introduced new challenges: data is now too large to examine directly, and complex models obscure the gap between what we think is being measured and what actually is. The core problem has shifted from misleading charts to opaque algorithms that claim to measure things like teacher quality while actually measuring something quite different.
key_points: Classic statistical deception techniques like selection bias and survivorship bias remain relevant in the big data age
The 'semi-attached figure' - confusing people about topic A by discussing related topic B - is the most relevant historical technique to modern algorithmic problems
Big data's size and complexity intimidate people into accepting models without scrutiny
We no longer even see the data or charts that might reveal manipulation - the data is presumed too big to access
Cultural assumptions have shifted dramatically: in 1954, companies tried to downplay profits to avoid seeming excessive
money_quotes: In the age of big data, we often don't even see charts of data so how can we be misled by them? In other words, the presumption is that the data is so big as to be inaccessible.
Google doesn't bother showing us the numbers. Plus they don't have to since we use their services anyway.

In the age of big data, and partly because the data is 'too big' to take a real look at, we spend an amazing amount of time talking about how a model is measuring something we care about when in fact the model is doing something quite different.
If we were aware of those discrepancies we'd have way more skepticism, but we're intimidated by the size of the data and the complexity of the models.
It kind of makes me pine for the olden days, except not really if I'm black, a woman, or at risk of being exposed to polio.
It seems that many of the lies that people are propagating with statistics are to downplay their profits so as to not seem excessive. Can you imagine?!
posts.key_insight: The danger of big data isn't misleading charts we can scrutinize, but opaque models so complex we've stopped questioning whether they measure what they claim to measure.

posts.summary: This guest post by Manya Raman-Sundström argues that mathematics education systematically neglects the aesthetic dimension that motivates professional mathematicians. While mathematicians cite beauty as a primary reward for their work, curricula focus exclusively on achievement and testing rather than aesthetic appreciation. The author contends this oversight may prevent students from fully developing their intellectual capacities and from making informed decisions about pursuing mathematics. Using the sum of first n integers as an example, she demonstrates how different proofs can vary dramatically in their aesthetic and explanatory appeal. The post announces a workshop in Sweden exploring the relationship between mathematical beauty, explanation, and visualization.
key_points: Mathematics curricula worldwide (with rare exceptions like Korea) ignore beauty as a goal, focusing instead on measurable achievement
Seymour Papert identified three components of scientific thought—cognitive, affective, and aesthetic—but education research has largely neglected the aesthetic dimension
The way mathematics is taught differs fundamentally from how it is practiced, potentially misleading students about the nature of mathematical work
Beauty and explanation may be related—what makes a proof aesthetically pleasing might also be what makes it understandable
Visual and elegant proofs often convey understanding more effectively than technically correct but opaque ones like proof by induction
money_quotes: We would consider it odd to teach music via scales and theory without ever giving children a chance to listen to a symphony. So why do we teach mathematics in bits and pieces without exposing students to the real thing, the full aesthetic experience?

Mathematics teaching is about achievement, not about aesthetic appreciation, a fact that test-makers are probably grateful for – can you imagine the makeover needed for the SAT if we started to try to measure aesthetic appreciation?
How can young people make wise decisions about whether they should continue to study mathematics if they have never really seen mathematics?
To overlook the aesthetic components of mathematical thought might be to preventing our children from developing their intellectual capacities.
Indeed, the fact that a proof of known theorem can be published just because it is more elegant is one evidence of this fact.

Perhaps our minds are drawn to what is easiest to grasp, which brings us back to central questions of teaching and learning: how do we best present mathematics in a way that makes it understandable, clear, and perhaps even beautiful?
posts.key_insight: By stripping beauty from mathematics education, we may be hiding the very essence of mathematical thinking from students and stunting their intellectual development.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil enthusiastically recommends Matt Stoller's new Tumblr blog, 'Observations on Credit and Surveillance.' She highlights his diverse range of topics, from historical connections between communism and U.S. slavery to Gerald Ford's Watergate involvement. O'Neil is particularly interested in his research on data provisions in the TPP trade agreement and the history of credit cards and debt collection. The post serves as a brief endorsement encouraging readers to follow Stoller's work.
key_points: Matt Stoller has started a Tumblr called 'Observations on Credit and Surveillance'
His content spans diverse historical topics including slavery, Watergate, and banking history
O'Neil finds his research on TPP data provisions and credit/debt collection history most compelling
The post functions as a recommendation and signal boost for a fellow researcher
money_quotes: I've been super impressed by Matt Stoller's recent foray into 'tumbling,' which is kind of like blogging except it's called tumbling.
Even the spam emails from tumblr are worth following him, because sometimes they contain his newest posts.
Most interesting to me is the information he's uncovered about data provisions in the TPP.
Go take a look, he's been on fire.
posts.key_insight: Original source document research can uncover important but overlooked connections between credit, surveillance, and political history.

posts.summary: O'Neil documents her ongoing research into the Common Core math standards, having interviewed key figures including Diane Ravitch and Bill McCallum (a lead writer of the standards). She's seeking multiple perspectives by also planning to interview a Brooklyn school principal. Her preliminary finding is that outsiders to the education debate tend to conflate distinct issues, and she aims to disentangle these to identify precise points of agreement and disagreement.
key_points: People outside the education system conflate multiple distinct issues when discussing Common Core
O'Neil is interviewing both architects of the standards and practitioners implementing them
She acknowledges she hasn't yet examined the actual content of the standards themselves
Her goal is to reach 'well-defined points' of agreement or disagreement rather than muddled debate
money_quotes: One thing I can say already for sure: people who are not insiders here conflate a bunch of different issues.

I'm hoping to at least separate them and understand where people stand on each issue, and if I at the very least get to the point of agreeing to disagree on well-defined points then I will have done my job.
Of course one thing I'm not doing is delving directly into the content of the standards, and that may very well be essential to understanding them.
posts.key_insight: Productive debate requires first disentangling conflated issues so disagreements can be precisely identified rather than talked past.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil invites readers to join Alt Banking, her Occupy Wall Street working group that meets weekly at Columbia University. The group has built an informative website, hosts notable speakers including Neil Barofsky and Sheila Bair, and published a book called 'Occupy Finance.' They're launching a new multimedia project exploring the intersection of three topics (visualized as a Venn diagram), potentially culminating in a conference. The post emphasizes both the substantive work and the community aspect of the group.
key_points: Alt Banking meets every Sunday at Columbia and welcomes new members, including visitors passing through New York
The group has hosted impressive speakers from finance and activism, with more events scheduled
Their book 'Occupy Finance' sold out its first printing and is available as a free PDF
A new multimedia project is in development, potentially combining book, video, and animated content for a September rollout
The group combines serious financial activism with genuine community and social connection
money_quotes: I'm really proud of my Occupy group, Alt Banking.
We continue to meet every Sunday at Columbia and we welcome new members.
I'd say it's a great group to know about for the speaker list alone.
We wrote a pretty cool book called Occupy Finance, and we recently got a second printing made since the first printing went so quickly.
I also wanted to mention that we have a blast every week.
posts.key_insight: Sustained financial reform activism requires combining serious intellectual work with welcoming community to keep people engaged over the long term.

posts.summary: This advice column features Aunt Pythia and guest Tante Nina answering reader questions on diverse topics: deflecting nosy questions with humor, dealing with workplace sexism, knowing when to end a lukewarm relationship, and whether to list 'ABD' on a resume. The longest and most substantive response addresses professional sexism, offering practical advice about documentation while acknowledging the unfair reality women face. The columnists advocate for resilience, humor, and not letting aggressors undermine confidence. Throughout, the tone balances warmth with directness, encouraging readers to maintain their standards while navigating difficult situations.
key_points: Workplace sexism should be documented thoroughly via personal email records to build a potential legal case if needed
The strategy for dealing with workplace bullies and sexists is to deny them the satisfaction of a reaction - humor and brush-offs work better than showing upset
Historical women like Ruth Bader Ginsburg succeeded through 'grit, excellent results, hard work, and playing dumb, dumb like a fox'
In relationships, being bored after four months is not a good enough standard - better to walk away than waste time
Listing 'ABD' on resumes implies active dissertation work; using it when not actively pursuing the degree looks 'weirdly defensive'
money_quotes: Sexism, like racism, is alive and well.
Don't let these guys win by undermining your confidence, because that is what they are trying to do.
With the little micro-aggressions they are trying to put you in your place.
Jerks and bullies generally give up and go away when they can't get a rise from you.
Laugh, brush them off with humor whenever possible, and remember that success is the best revenge.
How did women of that generation get by? On grit, excellent results, hard work, and playing dumb, dumb like a fox.
You gotta have standards, and being bored is not a good enough one.
posts.key_insight: When facing workplace sexism or other forms of bullying, document everything for protection but don't give aggressors the emotional reaction they seek - success and resilience are the best responses.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques Suze Orman's petition to have credit bureaus use debit card data to help people build credit scores. She argues that debit cards are fundamentally irrelevant to credit since they can't go negative, and that giving credit bureaus more data is the wrong direction. Instead, she advocates for a Post Office bank with free mobile money services like those in Kenya and Ghana, which would free poor people from predatory fees. She concludes that bad FICO scores are primarily a sign of being broke, not a moral failing, and the real solution is addressing poverty through policies like a Basic Guaranteed Income.
key_points: Debit cards are conceptually irrelevant to credit since they can't carry a balance, making Orman's proposal logically flawed
Credit bureaus already have too much data about us and their expansion into social media data is concerning
A Post Office bank with free mobile money (like M-Pesa in Kenya) would help the poor without predatory fees
Poor FICO scores are primarily a symptom of poverty, not a moral failing that needs to be fixed with better credit-building tools
If we want to help people escape debt spirals, we should address root causes through policies like Basic Guaranteed Income
money_quotes: My first, second, and third intuitive response to credit bureaus is to give them less information, not more.
Instead of shitty and expensive debit cards, let's have the Post Office open a national bank and let people put money for free on their phones.
In the short term, such a system will free poor people from getting ever increasingly ripped off by banks and companies with their crappy pre-paid debit cards.
It would be great if we stopped pretending (I'm looking at you Suze Orman) that having a bad FICO score is a moral failing: it's really mostly a sign of being broke.
If we want to help people get out of debt spirals, then let's talk about a Basic Guaranteed Income.
posts.key_insight: Trying to help poor people build credit scores through fee-laden financial products misdiagnoses the problem—bad FICO scores are a symptom of poverty, not a character flaw, and the solution lies in accessible public banking and addressing root economic inequality.

posts.summary: This interview with Bill McCallum, lead writer of the Common Core Math Standards, clarifies misconceptions about how the standards were developed and their relationship to testing. McCallum explains that the standards were written by educators and mathematicians, not testing companies, and emphasizes that standards are not curriculum. He argues that common standards actually empower consumers and small publishers while providing clearer guidance for struggling students and poor schools. McCallum pushes back against critics who conflate standards with testing or curriculum, noting that excessive testing is a legacy of No Child Left Behind, not Common Core. He advocates for focused, coherent standards that tell a mathematical story across grade levels.
key_points: The Common Core Math Standards were written by educators and mathematicians, not testing companies - the Work Team had no testing or textbook company representatives
Standards are not curriculum: standards describe what students should learn, while curriculum is what teachers actually follow in classrooms

Common standards can actually reduce testing costs and give more power to consumers and small curriculum publishers who previously couldn't compete with large publishers across 50 different state standards
The goal was to replace 'mile wide inch deep' state standards with focused, coherent progressions that help struggling students and under-resourced schools
Americans don't understand the advantages of common standards - they enable national communities of teachers to collaborate across state lines
money_quotes: Standards are descriptions of what you want students to learn. What you do with them is up to you.
It doesn't make sense to spend the money we spend on education ($12000 per student each year) and then not bother to see if it has an effect.
Education in this country goes between extremes, and right now we are undeniably on the extreme with respect to testing.
Standards don't cause testing.
Having common standards gives us power if we use it.
Good standards, as I believe the CCSS represent, help everybody by providing clear guidance, which particularly helps struggling students and poor schools with less than ideal conditions.
We as a country don't understand common anything.
posts.key_insight: Common educational standards are distinct from both testing and curriculum, and their 'commonness' actually empowers teachers and small publishers while providing clearer guidance for disadvantaged students.

posts.summary: In this interview, Cathy O'Neil discusses her journey from childhood math enthusiast to Harvard PhD to Wall Street quant to activist blogger. She explains how the 2008 financial crisis opened her eyes to problems she'd been ignoring, leading her to leave finance and start blogging to expose what she'd witnessed. She announces her upcoming book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' and articulates her mission to promote 'open models' - demanding public transparency for high-stakes algorithms like credit scores and teacher evaluations that function like 'secret laws.' Throughout, she emphasizes that mathematical models are not inherently objective or benign, and society needs more skepticism toward algorithmic decision-making.
key_points: O'Neil left academia after achieving her goal of becoming a math professor, finding it 'wasn't the right speed,' then left finance disillusioned by what she witnessed during the 2008 crisis
Her central mission is promoting 'open models' - public transparency for high-impact algorithms like credit scoring and teacher evaluation systems
She argues that mathematical modeling has a 'dark side' and people wrongly assume math-based systems are objective or benign
She compares opaque algorithmic systems that affect people's lives to 'secret laws'
Blogging serves as both intellectual processing and activism - she describes it as essential to organizing her thoughts and moving past issues that 'get stuck in her system'
money_quotes: We need to stop blindly assuming that because it has a mathematical aspect to it that it should be considered objective or benign.
I think it's a crime that these models are opaque and yet have so much power over people's lives. It's like having secret laws.
The idea is that the modeling we're seeing proliferate in all kinds of industries has a dark side and could be quite destructive.
I've been really happy with a decision to never let mathbabe be anything except fun for me. There's no money involved at all, ever, and there never will be.
It's more of a central activity in my life that has no other purpose than being itself.
Most of the time I kind of hate books written about modeling, to be honest, because usually they are written by people who are big data cheerleaders.
posts.key_insight: High-stakes algorithmic models that affect people's lives should be treated like laws - transparent and open to public scrutiny rather than hidden behind claims of mathematical objectivity.

posts.summary: O'Neil grapples with how to justify continued public funding for mathematics research in the face of potential MOOC-driven cuts to academic math positions. She presents three arguments—math as practical tool, math as beautiful art form, and math as training for rigorous thinking—but finds each incomplete. The 'usefulness' argument feels hollow because no mathematician actually pursues research for practical applications. The 'beauty' argument seems elitist since most research is inaccessible to the public being asked to fund it. She invites readers to help construct a more compelling case for why society should invest in pure mathematics.
key_points: The MOOC revolution threatens to dramatically reduce professional math jobs, making the case for research funding urgent
The 'math as useful tool' argument is true but rings hollow because mathematicians don't actually pursue research for practical reasons
Framing math as art feels elitist when the 'musical piece' can only be heard by mathematicians themselves
Mathematicians are uniquely trained to admit they're wrong and feel psyched when errors are pointed out—a rare quality in the real world
The challenge is justifying public funding for something the public cannot directly appreciate or access
money_quotes: I've even started to discourage young people from their plans to become math professors.
It's an answer that, although utterly true, and one we should definitely use to make our case, will actually leave the math research community itself cold.
If we mathematicians are performing a kind of art, like an enormous musical piece, then arguably it's a musical piece that only we can hear.

One of the most amazing things about mathematicians versus anyone else is that mathematicians – and other kinds of scientists – are trained to admit they're wrong. This is just so freaking rare in the real world.
There's no shame in being wrong – it's an inevitable part of the process of learning.
It's kind of a stretch to be asking the public for support on something that they can't appreciate directly.
posts.key_insight: The strongest arguments for funding math research may not resonate with the researchers themselves, while the arguments mathematicians find most compelling may be too insular to persuade the public.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil dissects a Wall Street Journal column by Scott Hodge that argues income equality is impossible to achieve. She demonstrates how Hodge deliberately conflates 'transfers' (like food stamps) with total 'federal spending' (including wars and infrastructure) to make the middle class appear to be net beneficiaries of government largesse. O'Neil shows that when properly understood, the middle quintile actually pays more in taxes than they receive in transfers. She argues Hodge's framing is mathematically inevitable given income distribution, and reveals his organization receives Koch brothers funding.
key_points: Hodge conflates 'transfers' (direct benefits like food stamps) with total 'federal spending' (including military, infrastructure) to mislead readers about what the middle class actually receives
The middle quintile pays more in taxes than they receive in direct transfers - they only appear to be net beneficiaries when general government spending is included
Given skewed income distribution, it's mathematically inevitable that the middle quintile would receive slightly more in total spending than they pay - this is just how averages work
Historical top tax rates have been much higher than 74% (including as recently as 1969) without catastrophic consequences
The Tax Foundation, which Hodge leads and calls 'nonpartisan,' has received funding from Koch brothers and Big Oil
money_quotes: In the first paragraph Hodge intentionally conflates the concept of 'transfers' and 'spending'.
It is easy to imagine a middle-quintile family paying $100 in taxes and receiving $119 in food stamps. This is of course not true at all.
What's nuts about this is that it's mathematically equivalent to complaining that half the population is below median intelligence.

Since we have a skewed distribution of incomes, and therefore a skewed distribution of tax receipts as well as transfers, then in the context of a completely balanced budget, we would expect the middle quintile – which has a below-mean average income – to pay slightly less than the government spends on them.
It's a mathematical fact as long as our federal tax system isn't regressive, which it's not.
This guy is just framing stuff in a 'middle class is lazy and selfish, what could rich people possibly be expected do about that?' kind of way.
Turns out that Hodge is the President of the Tax Foundation, which touts itself as 'nonpartisan' but which has gotten funding from Big Oil and the Koch brothers.
posts.key_insight: When someone uses statistics to argue that the middle class takes more than they give, check whether they're conflating direct transfers with general government spending - and who's funding their think tank.

posts.summary: This advice column covers a range of reader questions, from credit card security and Bitcoin's potential to disrupt banking, to career changes, tipping customs for foreign visitors, and managing sexual desire in long-distance relationships. Aunt Pythia dismisses fears about credit card theft as overblown while highlighting tracking as the real concern, debunks the idea that Bitcoin will fix Wall Street's problems, and offers pragmatic advice on navigating American tipping culture. The column concludes with frank advice about the unrealistic expectations of six-year long-distance relationships.
key_points: Credit card fraud protection is actually quite good ($50 liability cap), but pervasive tracking through phones and cameras is the real privacy threat that cash alone won't solve

Bitcoin and alternative currencies won't fix the fundamental problems of Wall Street power and bailouts - people's retirement savings are too entangled with banks for any currency change to be a panacea
American tipping culture isn't bribery - it's a broken system where restaurant workers earn $2.13 minimum wage and depend on tips to survive
When you quit academia or change life paths, you don't eliminate problems - you exchange them for new problems, so plan to optimize toward your 'ideal set of problems'
Six years of long-distance relationship while expecting complete fidelity is unrealistic - either move in together, negotiate an arrangement, or end it
money_quotes: To get off the grid you'd need to use cash, leave your cell phone at home, and avoid the various cameras and sensors being placed everywhere. Good luck!
The reasons that regular folks are on the hook for crazy bets on things like mortgage backed securities is complicated, deep, and will not go away because of bitcoin.
Plus it would be great if we could find an alternative currency where you get more money for saving energy, not for wasting it. Too much to ask?
You never get rid of problems, you just exchange them for new problems.
First of all, it's not bribery. These people depend on good tips for their salary. Waiting staff in restaurants have a minimum wage of $2.13, which is outrageous.
Nobody – and I mean nobody – can be expected to live apart from their lover for that long. It's just nuts.
posts.key_insight: The problems we worry about most (credit card fraud, Bitcoin revolution) are often distractions from the deeper structural issues (pervasive surveillance, entrenched financial power) that resist simple technological fixes.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines a study by Cheng and Hoekstra showing that 'Stand Your Ground' laws led to increased homicide rates in states that adopted them. Florida's homicide rates rose 8% after passing the law while national rates fell 6%. The researchers controlled for demographics, policing, and economic factors, and used falsification tests on unrelated crimes to strengthen their causal claims. O'Neil acknowledges some limitations in the data but notes the ethical tension between wanting more data as a researcher and wanting to act on concerning findings as a citizen.
key_points: States with Stand Your Ground laws saw relative increases in homicide rates only after passing the laws, not before
The study controlled for demographics, policing, economic conditions, and welfare spending with results unchanged
Falsification tests showed unrelated crimes like larceny and motor vehicle theft did not increase, supporting a causal link
The research reveals a tension between scientific rigor (wanting more data) and moral urgency (preventing deaths)
Higher baseline homicide rates in adopting states could indicate confounding, though researchers attempted to address this
money_quotes: Making it easier to kill people does result in more people getting killed.
The problem is that, as soon as you suspect there's a real effect, you'd want to act on it, since it directly translates into human deaths.
Your natural reaction as a researcher is to 'collect more data' but your natural reaction as a citizen is to abandon these laws as ineffective and harmful.
There is no evidence that states like Florida just generally experience increases in homicide rates relative to other states, even when they don't pass these laws.
We find that the magnitude of the increase in homicide rates is sufficiently large that it is unlikely to be explained by chance.
posts.key_insight: When statistical evidence suggests a policy is causing deaths, the ethical imperative to act conflicts with the scientific desire for more conclusive data.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares a brief personal update about recovering from illness and directs readers to a New York Magazine article about a reporter who infiltrated Kappa Beta Phi, a secretive Wall Street fraternity. The post serves primarily as a link recommendation rather than original analysis, though her framing ('Pretty amazing, if true') suggests both intrigue and skepticism about elite financial culture.
key_points: Wall Street has secretive social institutions like Kappa Beta Phi that operate outside public scrutiny
Investigative journalism can expose the hidden culture of financial elites
O'Neil positions herself as a curator of content critical of Wall Street excess
money_quotes: I have a whole backlog of rants and vents but no time this morning to even start
Pretty amazing, if true
posts.key_insight: Wall Street's secretive social institutions reveal a culture of elite insularity that deserves public scrutiny.

posts.summary: O'Neil debunks the media frenzy around three JP Morgan employee suicides in three weeks, arguing it's a case of the clustering illusion rather than evidence of a systemic problem. Using back-of-the-envelope calculations based on JP Morgan's 255,000 employees and standard suicide rates, she shows we'd expect about 2.85 suicides in that timeframe—making three completely unremarkable. She compares this to the Foxconn suicide coverage, where rates were actually lower than the general Chinese population. The post demonstrates how media attention can transform ordinary statistics into conspiracy theories.
key_points: With 255,000 employees and average suicide rates, JP Morgan would expect roughly one suicide per week—three in three weeks is statistically normal
The clustering illusion causes humans to perceive patterns in random events that don't actually represent meaningful clusters
Media coverage of the Foxconn suicides similarly created false impressions—their suicide rate was actually lower than China's general population
The observed 3 suicides versus the expected 2.85 isn't even a statistical anomaly, let alone evidence of a conspiracy
money_quotes: A little back-of-the-envelope calculation tells me it's not at all impressive, and this is nothing but media attention turned into conspiracy theory with the usual statistics errors.
We expect to see about one suicide a week considering how huge JP Morgan is. So let's get over this, it's normal.
People commit suicide pretty regularly.
We heard all about suicides at Foxconn, but then heard that the suicide rate at Foxconn is lower than the general Chinese population.
My calculation above points to something even dumber, which is that we expected 2.85 suicides and we saw 3, so it's not even a proven cluster.
I feel suicidal just thinking about working there myself. But I don't want us to jump to any statistically unsupported conclusions.
posts.key_insight: Before crying conspiracy about clusters of tragic events, do the basic math—large populations produce regular tragedies that only seem suspicious when media attention makes them visible.

posts.summary: O'Neil highlights a rigorous study showing the 2009 CARD Act successfully reduced credit card fees, saving U.S. consumers $12.6 billion annually. The regulation disproportionately helped consumers with the lowest FICO scores, who saw borrowing costs drop by over 5.5%. Contrary to industry predictions, there was no offsetting increase in interest charges or reduction in credit availability. The study used data from over 150 million credit card accounts to make these claims. O'Neil argues this disproves the myth that regulating predatory fees simply causes them to resurface elsewhere, noting that myth assumes competitive markets with informed participants—which doesn't describe the credit card industry.
key_points: The CARD Act reduced overall borrowing costs by 1.7% of average daily balances, with the poorest consumers (lowest FICO scores) seeing reductions of over 5.5%
No evidence emerged of offsetting increases in interest charges or reduced credit availability after the regulation
The data shows 'rich people buy a lot, poor people pay lots of fees'—making low-income consumers profitable targets for card companies
The study debunks the 'whack-a-mole' theory that regulating one predatory practice simply causes another to emerge
The credit card market's asymmetric information creates predatory opportunities that regulation can effectively address
money_quotes: Every now and then you see a published result that has exactly the right kind of data, in sufficient amounts, to make the required claim.
Rich people buy a lot, poor people pay lots of fees.
Poor people are great customers but there aren't so many of them.
This data, and the results in this paper, fly directly in the face of the myth that if you regulate away predatory fees in one way, they will pop up in another way.
That myth is based on the assumption of a competitive market with informed participants.
This is a great example of how asymmetric information causes predatory opportunities.
posts.key_insight: When markets lack informed participants, regulation can successfully eliminate predatory practices without triggering the offsetting effects that free-market theory predicts.

posts.summary: This advice column covers four reader questions: a female mathematician's anger at receiving a romantic solicitation at an academic conference, ethical investing dilemmas, whether to review NSA grant proposals given surveillance concerns, and Facebook's relationship data studies. Aunt Pythia advises the mathematician that while the email was 'obtuse to the point of idiocy,' she overreacted and should develop thicker skin as a 'warrior' woman in math. On investing, she suggests dividing money by liquidity, risk, and ethics concerns. She counsels reviewing NSA grants while working on the larger math funding problem, and praises Facebook's aggregated data studies while noting selection bias caveats.
key_points: Women in mathematics should develop thick skin and 'warrior' mentality to navigate sexism without exhausting themselves on every minor offense
Ethical investing can be approached by dividing funds into liquidity, risk, and ethics buckets rather than seeking one perfect solution
The decline of math research funding may push academics toward ethically compromised funding sources like NSA grants
Aggregated data studies are ethically acceptable; the scary stuff is non-aggregated, hidden personal data use
Conference sexual solicitations, while inappropriate, exist on a spectrum—explicit awkward advances may be preferable to manipulative pretexts
money_quotes: You are a woman in math, which means you are a warrior, even if you didn't want to sign up for that.
The best and easiest way to be a warrior is to have a thick skin, to remember the victories, and to ignore the defeats.
At least he made his explicit desires known. It would have been worse, in my opinion, if he'd come up with some math pretext for meeting and then put his hand on your knee at lunch.

I'd like to take this opportunity to defend sex at math conferences in general. I mean, it's one of the classic ways of blowing off some steam after a long day of whirlwind 10-minute talks, married or unmarried.
Who is going to fund math research when calculus is gone? The obvious answer is private companies, private individuals, and places like the NSA. Not a pretty picture.
The stuff I think is scary is the stuff that isn't aggregated and is hidden from us.
posts.key_insight: Navigating systemic problems like sexism in academia or ethical compromises in funding requires strategic thinking about where to spend your energy rather than fighting every battle.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines a 1989 Federal Reserve paper that outlines three regulatory approaches to anti-discrimination in credit decisions. The Equal Credit Opportunity Act left implementation details to regulators, who had to operationalize what 'discrimination' actually means. The paper identifies effects-based (outcome-focused), intent-based (proving deliberate discrimination), and practices-based (lists of prohibited factors) approaches. O'Neil notes that effects-based regulation is considered too extreme while intent-based is nearly impossible to enforce. The practices-based approach currently in use is easy to implement but requires constant updating. She argues this framework is crucial for understanding how to regulate discrimination in the age of big data.
key_points: Anti-discrimination law often delegates the hard definitional work to regulators rather than specifying it in legislation
Effects-based regulation looks at outcomes regardless of process, but is considered too extreme and rarely used
Intent-based regulation requires proving deliberate discrimination, which is nearly impossible since discriminators can embed bias in models with plausible deniability
Practices-based regulation creates lists of prohibited factors but must be continuously updated as data practices evolve
The big data era demands we revisit and update these regulatory frameworks
money_quotes: It's super rare that you can do that, because it's super rare that people aiming to discriminate are dumb enough to make it obvious.
Far easier to embed discrimination in a model where you can maintain plausible deniability.

This is what the Fed finally decided to use, and it makes sense in that it's easy to implement, but of course the lists change over time, and that's the key issue (for me anyway): we need to update those lists in the age of big data.

By itself, this rule does not offer an unquestionably unambiguous operational definition of socially unacceptable discrimination in a screening context where limited selections are constantly being made from a longer list of applicants.
These regulatory approaches correspond to different ways of thinking about or even defining discrimination, which is itself a great reason to list them comprehensively.
posts.key_insight: The three regulatory approaches to discrimination—effects-based, intent-based, and practices-based—each reflect fundamentally different definitions of discrimination itself, and our current practices-based system requires constant updating to remain relevant in the big data era.

posts.summary: This satirical takedown dissects a Cadillac commercial that aired during the Olympics, exposing its celebration of American workaholism and wealth as toxic ideology. The author, writing as Leopold Dilg, follows the ad's 'Master of the Universe' protagonist as he sneers at European work-life balance while strutting through his mansion. Through biting commentary, the piece reveals how the commercial's 'you make your own luck' message ignores the millions of unemployed, foreclosed, and struggling Americans. The essay exposes the cruel irony of selling bootstrap mythology to a nation gutted by the very Wall Street class the ad glorifies.
key_points: The Cadillac ad uses nationalist pride and anti-European sentiment to glorify overwork as an American virtue
The 'you make your own luck' ideology conveniently ignores structural inequality and the millions harmed by economic policies favoring the wealthy
The commercial co-opts genuinely admirable figures like Muhammad Ali to legitimize plutocratic values they would likely reject
The ad's protagonist represents the Wall Street class whose excesses caused the very economic devastation that makes 'making your own luck' impossible for most
Using French phrases while mocking the French reveals the ad's incoherent cultural posturing
money_quotes: There's little chance we can underestimate our American virtues, since our overlords so seldom miss an opportunity to point them out.
Those French will stop at nothing.
Never in a million years would the hip jazz guitarist insist on taking an August holiday. And the imprisoned-for-draft-dodging boxer couldn't possibly side with the café-loafers on the WMD thing.
Damn, if only the NASA budget wasn't cut after all that looting by the Wall Street boys to pay for their $10 million vacation homes.
The 17 million unemployed and underemployed, the 47 million who need food stamps to keep from starving, the 8 million families thrown out of their homes – WE ALL BELIEVE.
You work hard, you create your own luck, and you gotta believe anything is possible.

From the 70-year olds doing Wal-mart inventory because their retirement went bust, from all the kitchens of all the families carrying $1 trillion in college debt, I hear the national chant, 'YOU MAKE YOUR OWN LUCK!'
posts.key_insight: The 'you make your own luck' mythology serves to justify plutocratic excess while blaming victims of systemic economic injustice for their own suffering.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that privacy advocates are often the wrong people worrying about privacy—they're typically well-educated, affluent individuals who face little actual risk from data collection. She points out that privacy-conscious behavior itself signals wealth and sophistication to data miners, who then leave those users alone. The real victims of predatory data practices are vulnerable populations who lack the knowledge or resources to protect themselves. She criticizes a Slate writer who abandoned privacy concerns after realizing he personally wasn't being harmed, calling this perspective dangerously myopic. O'Neil concludes that society needs to focus on protecting those who are actually targeted by manipulative data practices rather than privileged individuals protecting themselves.
key_points: Privacy advocates are typically privileged individuals who face minimal actual risk from data collection
Using ad blockers and privacy tools actually signals wealth to predatory companies, causing them to leave you alone
The most vulnerable populations—those most harmed by data mining—are least likely to be privacy-conscious
Individual privacy protection is ineffective as a societal solution; we need systemic protections
Privileged people concluding 'privacy doesn't matter' based on their own experience miss the larger picture
money_quotes: The very people obsessing over their privacy are people who are not particularly vulnerable to the predatory attacks of either the NSA or the private companies that make use of private data.
The mere presence of privacy concerns signals that this person isn't worth pursuing with my manipulative scheme.
That article should have been called something like, 'Well-educated white guy was a privacy freak until he realized he didn't have to be because he's a well-educated white guy.'
The problem is that the most vulnerable people – the very people that should be concerned with privacy but aren't – are the ones getting tracked, mined, and screwed.

It's silly for certain people to be scrupulously careful about their private data if they are the types of people who get great credit card offers and have a stable well-paid job and are generally healthy.
posts.key_insight: Privacy is a class issue: the people most obsessed with protecting their data are the least vulnerable to its exploitation, while predatory data practices systematically target those without the resources or knowledge to defend themselves.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that data journalism is becoming essential as data proliferates into all corners of culture, making statistical literacy a requirement for journalists. She emphasizes that coding skills alone aren't sufficient—journalists must also be skeptical of models and data. The post highlights how opaque, proprietary algorithms provide perfect cover for discriminatory intentions that would be obvious if done outright. O'Neil proposes that newsrooms form teams with combined computing and investigative expertise, pointing to ProPublica's reverse-engineering of Obama's messaging model as an exemplar of what good data journalism looks like.
key_points: Basic data and computing literacy is becoming vital for journalism as data proliferates into all aspects of culture
Learning to code is necessary but not sufficient—skepticism of models and data is equally important
Opaque, proprietary models provide 'a perfect hiding place for bad intentions' where discrimination can be embedded and never discovered
Newsrooms need teams that collectively have computing and investigative expertise, not impossible do-everything individuals
Without data-literate journalism, problematic models will go unchallenged
money_quotes: The proliferation of data is leaking into all corners of our culture, and basic data and computing literacy is becoming increasingly vital to the job of journalism.
If you don't learn to code, and even more importantly if you don't learn to be skeptical of the models and the data, then you will have yet another obstacle between you and the truth.
In this day and age, we have complicated, opaque, and proprietary models: in other words, a perfect hiding place for bad intentions.

It would be idiotic for someone with the intention of being discriminatory to do so outright. It's much easier to embed such a thing in an opaque model where it will seem unintentional and will probably never be discovered at all.
The alternative is that the models go unchallenged, which is a really bad idea.
posts.key_insight: Opaque algorithms have become the perfect hiding place for discriminatory intent, and only data-literate investigative journalism can challenge them.

posts.summary: O'Neil reports from a panel discussion at MSRI about MOOCs in mathematics education, finding her fears about their long-term effects confirmed rather than allayed. The critical moment came when a respected statistics professor explained she needed an 'air-tight legal contract' to prevent EdX from offering her course without her participation. O'Neil argues this contractual protection is fragile and questions whether a professor's presence truly matters when 28,000 students receive at most an hour of her daily attention. She concludes that MOOC evangelists love MOOCs because they personally benefit, without examining broader consequences for math research funding and PhD employment.
key_points: EdX wanted to offer a professor's MOOC three times a year without her involvement, revealing platforms' ultimate intentions
Legal contracts are the only thing preventing MOOCs from being 'replayed forever' without instructor participation
The value proposition for average MOOC students is essentially the same whether the professor is present or not
MOOC evangelists are blind to side effects because the current system benefits them personally
Decreased need for math PhDs is an acknowledged but under-discussed consequence of MOOC proliferation
money_quotes: We don't hand out textbooks and claim they are courses, so we by the same token cannot hand out MOOC videos and claim they are courses.
How long until someone in math or stats makes a kick-ass MOOC and doesn't remember to have that air-tight legal contract?
How much sense does it make to claim that you and your presence are super critical to the success of a MOOC if 28,000 people took this class and you interacted at most one hour a day?
The value proposition for the average MOOC student is very similar whether you are there or not.
The overall impression I got from the speakers was that they loved MOOCs because MOOCs were working for them. They weren't looking much beyond that point to side effects.

I'm claiming that the MOOC Revolution will lead to the end of math research, and I wanted to be proven wrong. Unfortunately, I left feeling like I have even more evidence that my fears will be realized.
