posts.key_insight: The people building and promoting MOOCs are personally benefiting from them and therefore failing to examine how these platforms will ultimately commoditize education and eliminate the need for research mathematicians.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines a Financial Times calculator that reveals how much advertisers will pay for your personal data based on various attributes. She highlights how certain conditions like obesity or being a new parent dramatically increase your data's value, from fractions of a cent to nearly 40 cents. The post exposes how data warehouses profit from knowing intimate details about people's lives, particularly their vulnerabilities. She connects this data marketplace to broader concerns about unregulated uses of personal information, including bypassing credit protection laws.
key_points: Personal data has wildly varying market value depending on exploitable attributes like health conditions or life transitions
Being obese increases your data value nearly 400-fold because of the predatory weight-loss marketing industry
Data brokers like Acxiom assume baseline knowledge of age, gender, education, and ethnicity by default
This personal data marketplace enables companies to build unregulated scoring systems that bypass consumer protection laws
money_quotes: You can bump up your worth to $0.27 from the standard $0.0007 if you're obese, and another $0.10 if you admit to being the type to buy weight-loss products.
Data warehouses can only get that much money for your data if they know about your weight, which they may or may not since if you don't buy weight-loss products.
Some of the default assumptions are that it knows my age, gender, education level, and ethnicity.
Plenty of assumed information to, say, build an unregulated version of a credit score to bypass the Equal Credit Opportunities Act.
posts.key_insight: Your personal data is worth more to advertisers when you're vulnerable, turning your struggles into profit opportunities for data brokers and predatory marketers.

posts.summary: O'Neil draws a provocative parallel between how credit default swaps were designed to evade risk detection models and how corporate structures like franchises and supply chains are designed to evade legal responsibility. She suggests these structures may have evolved deliberately or through a kind of natural selection where arrangements that best hid risk or liability thrived. Using McDonald's and Walmart as examples, she shows how workers striking against franchisees or suppliers have no leverage against the actual corporate power. Her pessimistic conclusion is that making risk or legal systems more complex only creates more opportunities for gaming.
key_points: Credit default swaps may have been deliberately designed by people who understood VaR models to hide risk from standard detection methods
Corporate franchise and supply chain structures similarly isolate parent companies from legal responsibility for worker conditions
Even if not deliberately designed, these structures may have evolved through selection pressure favoring those that best hide risk or liability
Only long-term, powerful enterprises have the resources to game large systems in this way
Making regulatory systems more complex may paradoxically give corporations more ways to exploit them
money_quotes: Perhaps credit default swaps might have been deliberately created by someone who knew all about the standard risk models to game the system.
A bunch of weird instruments were created and those which hid risk the most thrived, kind of an evolutionary approach to the same theory.
The legality of strikes has historically been uncorrelated to the existence of strikes – strikers cannot necessarily wait for the legal system to catch up with the needs of the worker.
The real money lives in the mother company but is legally isolated from the franchises.
The legal structure of McDonalds and Walmart almost seems deliberately set up to avoid legal responsibility from disgruntled workers.
Only long-term, powerful enterprises can go to the trouble of gaming such large systems. It's an artifact of the age of the corporation.
We could try to improve our risk or legal system but that makes them – probably – even more complicated, which in turn gives massive corporations more ways to game them.
posts.key_insight: Complex regulatory and legal systems designed to protect against corporate malfeasance may paradoxically create more opportunities for powerful corporations to game them.

posts.summary: This is a brief event announcement for Cathy O'Neil's speaking engagement at the NYC Open Data Meetup. She will discuss her essay 'On Being a Data Skeptic' and her book 'Doing Data Science.' The post provides logistical details including time, location at Enigma HQ, and the evening's schedule.
key_points: O'Neil is presenting at NYC Open Data Meetup organized by Vivian Zhang
The talk focuses on her 'On Being a Data Skeptic' essay and 'Doing Data Science' book
The event includes networking, a workshop, and audience Q&A
money_quotes: I'll be discussing my essay from last year entitled On Being a Data Skeptic
posts.key_insight: This event announcement promotes O'Neil's data skepticism work to the NYC open data community.

posts.summary: O'Neil documents her attempt to obtain the source code for New York's teacher Value-Added Model through a FOIL request, only to be stonewalled with bureaucratic delays and ultimately sent a white paper instead of the actual code she requested. After months of waiting, the Department of Education responded with exactly what she had explicitly said she didn't want. She argues that without access to the underlying code, teachers are being judged by a secret system with enormous power over their careers, while their individual scores have already been publicly released and used to shame them.
key_points: FOIL requests for algorithmic source code are met with bureaucratic obstruction and deliberate misunderstanding
Teachers' VAM scores were publicly released via FOIA, but the algorithm that produces those scores remains hidden
White papers describing models are insufficient for understanding actual model behavior and sensitivity to inputs
Algorithms with significant power over people's lives operate as 'secret sets of rules' without accountability
Three months of delays resulted in receiving exactly what was explicitly not requested
money_quotes: Without knowledge of what the model consists of, the VAM scoring regime is little more than a secret set of rules, with tremendous power over teachers and the teacher union.
I think teachers deserve better, and I want to illustrate the weaknesses of the model directly.
It strikes me as strange that it took them more than 3 months to send me a link to a white paper instead of the source code as I requested.
The best way to get at that is the source code.
Am I getting the runaround?
posts.key_insight: When institutions publicly release algorithmic scores that damage individuals' reputations while refusing to release the code that generates those scores, they create an accountability asymmetry that protects the powerful and exposes the vulnerable.

posts.summary: This advice column parodies and critiques Susan Patton's controversial Wall Street Journal piece urging college women to find husbands before graduating. Aunt Pythia systematically dismantles Patton's arguments about educated men wanting 'less challenging' women and the urgency of finding a mate in college. The column also addresses questions about alpha female dynamics in relationships and income disparities between partners, arguing that real-life connections transcend the artificial filters people create. A final question about measuring national fairness leads to a discussion of the Gini coefficient and economic mobility data.
key_points: Susan Patton's advice reduces people to external attributes like age and education level, which is equally insulting to all genders
Pressuring young people to marry early is a form of social control that leads to desperate, bad choices made from insecurity
The 'men won't buy the cow if the milk is free' mentality is sexual objectification disguised as advice
In real relationships, people fall in love based on genuine connection rather than abstract criteria like relative income or education
Fairness can be quantified through measures like the Gini coefficient, and the US scores poorly on income inequality
money_quotes: Some of the stupidest people I've ever met were at Harvard and MIT, and some of the wisest – and in some sense, most threatening – people I've met are young children, who can really say it like it is.
People make desperate and bad choices when they are insecure, boxed in, and panicking for time.
The way I see it, getting people to marry young is a kind of social control that old people exert on the young, before they really know how to say 'fuck this particular model of conformity'.
That's sexual objectification, pure and simple, and it's not empowering.

Just as you think you want a tall guy when you fill out a form, if you meet someone in real life who is two inches shorter than you but makes you laugh yourself silly, you will not even notice his height.
I don't think anyone ever thinks they're 'not as smart' as their partner.
posts.key_insight: Advice urging women to prioritize finding a husband in college reduces human relationships to transactional exchanges of measurable attributes, which is both insulting and a form of social control over young people.

posts.summary: The post examines the ongoing mortgage crisis, arguing that despite media narratives of housing recovery, millions of homeowners remain on the brink of foreclosure while banks lack clear legal ownership of many mortgages. O'Neil criticizes Obama's failure to use TARP for mortgage modification and the ineffective HAMP program. She explores Strike Debt's proposal to use eminent domain to force resolution of both underwater mortgages and broken chains of title, acknowledging the tool's problematic history while suggesting desperate times may call for desperate measures.
key_points: Obama promised mortgage modification through TARP but instead delivered the ineffective and possibly harmful HAMP program
Banks can renegotiate business mortgages and credit card debt, but refuse to do so for homeowners because it serves their interests
Many foreclosing banks lack clear legal ownership of mortgages due to botched paperwork, yet regulators won't act to avoid 'blowing up the system'
Strike Debt proposes using eminent domain to force courts to address both underwater mortgages and broken chains of title
Using eminent domain on current (non-delinquent) mortgages could set dangerous precedents for property condemnation based on statistical likelihood
money_quotes: Whatever the banks want, the banks seem to get.
Nobody wants to 'blow up the system' so nobody is dealing with it.
This is especially ironic considering how often we hear about the so-called sanctity of the contract.
Banks got bailed out and the system never got cleared of its actual debt and paperwork problems.
Maybe it's time a crude tool is used against a cruel enemy.

We're in a situation where all these homeowners are being crushed with unreasonable monthly payments, and hugely inflated principals, where the legal ownership of the mortgage itself is under question, and nobody seems to want to do squat about it.
posts.key_insight: When powerful institutions refuse to fix systemic problems they created, unconventional legal tools like eminent domain may be justified to force accountability, despite their own problematic history.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques the SAT overhaul announced by College Board President David Coleman, arguing that while the changes acknowledge the test's problems, they don't solve the fundamental issue. The SAT has become a sorting mechanism by income rather than a measure of aptitude, as wealthy families can afford more test prep. The new content-focused SAT with 'free' online prep still requires time and resources that correlate with wealth. O'Neil predicts the income gap in scores will persist and advocates for colleges to abandon standardized testing entirely.
key_points: The SAT now primarily sorts students by family income rather than measuring innate aptitude or college readiness
Colleges gaming US News rankings through SAT scores creates a harmful feedback loop with test prep culture
Some colleges have made SAT scores optional and seen more diversity without suffering consequences
The College Board's 'free' online prep still requires time and attention that remain unequally distributed by income
The overhaul changes what students are tested on but doesn't address the structural advantage wealthy families have
money_quotes: At this point the SAT serves mainly to sort people by income.
It's no longer an appropriate way to gauge 'IQ' as it was supposed to be when it was invented.
So it's one feedback loop feeding into another.
But since the College Board makes their livelihood by testing people, they were never going to just shut down.
But here's the thing, it's still online, and it still involves lots of time and attention, which still saps resources.
I would love to see more colleges step outside the standardized testing field altogether.
posts.key_insight: Reforming a standardized test cannot fix the fundamental problem that test preparation itself is a resource that wealthy families can purchase, making any testable metric inevitably correlate with income.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques 'gotcha' surveys designed to prove public ignorance, using the classic 'does the sun go around the earth' question as her example. She demonstrates that the question is actually frame-of-reference dependent and technically answerable as 'yes' from an Earth-based observational standpoint. The post exposes how these surveys privilege one framing as the only correct answer while ignoring legitimate alternative interpretations. Her imaginary dialogue with a pollster illustrates how forced yes/no responses can misrepresent actual understanding.
key_points: Surveys designed to prove ignorance are often unscientific and obnoxious
The sun-earth question is frame-of-reference dependent, making 'yes' technically defensible
Forced binary answers strip away nuance and can misrepresent genuine understanding
These surveys conflate simplified communication with actual ignorance
money_quotes: Periodically you have people conducting surveys to prove how dumb people are.
I hate these surveys, and I'm usually able to ignore these obnoxious and unscientific nature of them.
It depends on your frame of reference.
If I'm standing on the earth, and I look up in the sky, I will observe the sun going around the earth in a wobbly path.
It would be quite a bit simpler to understand the model of the solar system whereby the earth and other planets revolve around the sun.
posts.key_insight: Questions designed to expose ignorance often reveal more about the survey's oversimplified framing than about respondents' actual understanding.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews Julia Angwin's book 'Dragnet Nation,' which chronicles Angwin's attempts to understand and escape the pervasive surveillance infrastructure tracking everyday citizens. O'Neil praises the book's detailed 'data audit' and Angwin's pragmatic approach to privacy—not seeking perfection but making trackers work harder. The review highlights how opting out of surveillance is nearly impossible even for a tech-savvy journalist willing to use burner phones and esoteric privacy services. O'Neil critiques the book for not fully exploring the aggregate societal implications of surveillance, though she endorses its educational value. She concludes that privacy is becoming commoditized—soon only the wealthy will be able to afford it.
key_points: Angwin's 'data audit' reveals how much personal data exists and how futile attempts to delete or opt out truly are
The surveillance industry operates without regard for public interest, as exemplified by the 'Wiretapper's Ball' selling tracking tech to governments
Staying off the grid requires either extreme sophistication or extreme wealth, making privacy increasingly a luxury good
The psychological toll of caring about surveillance in a world of 'blithe acceptance' pushes people toward paranoid 'data survivalism'
The critical unanswered question is whether any data trackers pursue the public interest at all, and what the long-term consequences are if none do
money_quotes: She doesn't want to hand herself over to them on a silver platter. That is already very very hard.
The key question is, who has access? and what do they do with it?
We don't really get into asking, 'Is in the public's interest?'
To what extent is the public's interest being pursued, if at all, by all of these data trackers and data miners?
You have to either be incredibly sophisticated or incredibly rich to get this done, at least right now.
My guess is that, in the future, it will be more about the latter category: privacy will be enjoyed only by those people who can afford it.
It is not a lot of fun to be the only person caring about the tracking in a world of blithe acceptance.
posts.key_insight: Privacy is rapidly becoming a luxury that only the wealthy or technically sophisticated can afford, while the rest of society is left with no choice but blithe acceptance of pervasive surveillance.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces she's helping launch a new data journalism program at Columbia's Journalism School. The program is a one or two semester post-baccalaureate course designed to teach data skills, coding, and visualizations to people from non-technical backgrounds. While housed in the J-School, the program is open to people from various humanities fields who want to become data-competent. The program launches in May 2014 and the team is actively recruiting students and teachers.
key_points: The program targets people from non-technical and humanities backgrounds who want to work with data
It's a practical post-bacc program covering data, coding, and visualizations
Despite being at the J-School, enrollment is open beyond journalists to other humanities fields
The program was launching quickly with institutional support and scholarship funding available
money_quotes: The idea is that people from other humanities who see value in working with data can enroll in the program and emerge competent with data.
There's no time to waste, as the program starts soon and we don't even quite have a name for it.
posts.key_insight: Data literacy should be accessible to humanities professionals beyond just journalists, bridging the gap between technical and non-technical fields.

posts.summary: The post examines how Google's PageRank algorithm has been systematically gamed by SEO professionals, forcing Google into an escalating arms race. O'Neil argues that using 'links' as a proxy for 'quality' inevitably leads to the proxy being decoupled from its original meaning when stakes are high. She predicts Google will shift to using comprehensive user behavior data—email, browsing, GPS—to better approximate quality. The post draws a provocative parallel to education, suggesting that if we want to evaluate teachers properly, we should similarly move beyond gameable standardized tests to direct observation of classroom engagement.
key_points: When you use a proxy to measure something complex like 'quality,' high-stakes gaming will decouple the proxy entirely from its original meaning
SEO experts predict Google will shift from link-based ranking to comprehensive user behavior analysis including email, browsing, and GPS data
Google's motivation for good organic search isn't altruistic—it's to keep users on the platform so it can sell ads
The same logic that's pushing Google toward deeper measurement should apply to teacher evaluation: move from gameable standardized tests to direct classroom observation
The proxy problem creates outcomes that are 'the complete opposite of what you'd intended'
money_quotes: Since it's so high stakes, the gaming acts to decouple the proxy entirely from its original meaning.
You end up with something that is in fact the complete opposite of what you'd intended.
It's hard to address except by giving up the proxy altogether and going for something much closer to what you care about.
Because, let's face it, it's all about money.
Google wants good organic searches so that people won't abandon its search engine altogether so it can sell ads.
We should go deeper and try to define what we actually think makes a good teacher, which will require sensors in the classroom to see if kids are paying attention and are participating and such.
posts.key_insight: High-stakes proxies inevitably get gamed until they measure the opposite of what was intended, forcing systems toward ever-more-invasive direct measurement of the thing they actually care about.

posts.summary: O'Neil responds to Rick Bookstaber's proposal to replace the SAT with big data analytics that would mine students' high school performance, socioeconomic data, and behavioral information to predict college success. She argues this approach is naive because colleges already have such data and use it for self-interested purposes like admissions yield and alumni giving, not student welfare. More fundamentally, she contends that subjecting children to comprehensive data mining constitutes 'pre-consensual surveillance' that's far more invasive than a single standardized test. The supposed anonymization doesn't protect students since they'll still be judged by how 'others like them' performed, perpetuating systemic inequalities while shifting accountability to individuals.
key_points: The SAT has become a weak proxy that mainly sorts students by income, and like Google's PageRank, it's being gamed
Colleges already possess extensive student analytics but use them for institutional self-interest (admissions, aid optimization, alumni giving) rather than student success
Replacing one flawed metric with comprehensive data mining would constitute 'pre-consensual surveillance' of children
Anonymization doesn't prevent discrimination since students are still judged by how demographically similar people performed
The big data approach inverts the American dream: the system escapes accountability while individuals are held responsible for group statistics
money_quotes: It shouldn't be a surprise to anyone when a weak proxy gets gamed.
I think Bookstaber is being a bit naive and idealistic if he thinks colleges will use this information for good. They already have it and they're not.
We're basically talking about pre-consensual surveillance, not to mention the collection and mining of information far beyond the control of the individual child.
Childhood is a time which we should try to do our best to protect, not quantify.
Stripping out names in historical data doesn't change or obscure the difference between coming from a rich high school or a poor one.
In the end you will be judged by how 'others like you' performed, and in this regime the system gets off the hook but individuals are held accountable.
If you think about it, it's exactly the opposite of the American dream.
posts.key_insight: Replacing a flawed standardized test with comprehensive big data surveillance of children doesn't solve the fairness problem—it amplifies it by judging individuals based on group demographics while letting institutions escape accountability.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts her traumatic year as a graduate student at Princeton's math department, describing an intensely male-dominated, antisocial environment hostile to women. She develops a theory that Princeton has optimized itself to produce 'geniuses' modeled on Einstein, which by definition excludes women and minorities. She then critiques Susan Patton's advice for women to get plastic surgery and husband-hunt in college, arguing that while Patton correctly identifies the shift to a 'free market' mating system, her strategies are both degrading and doomed to fail. The essay connects Princeton's worship of male success with Patton's call for women to objectify themselves.
key_points: Princeton's math department culture is described as extremely male-dominated, antisocial, and frozen in time around Einstein-era ideals of genius worship
The town's advice to women seeking connection was to join a church or have children as 'something to do' - revealing women's roles as supporting characters to potential male geniuses
Susan Patton's advice to get plastic surgery and husband-hunt represents the logical extreme of a culture that values women only for their ability to serve successful men
Modern dating has shifted from social contract to 'free market' dynamics where men assert masculinity through the sexual attractiveness of their partners to other men
Patton's strategies are self-defeating because withholding sex and pushing for early marriage signals desperation in the current system
money_quotes: There are two kinds of people in the world: people who hate Princeton, New Jersey, and people who are über successful white men.
I did suspiciously have my boobs with me. I must be kitchen staff.
If it helps, I think I eventually see every female graduate student at Princeton.
Would-be geniuses are worshipped in weird ways, and anyone who is not themselves a genius candidate has to tailor themselves to those who are.
And since by definition geniuses are not women – and nor are minority men – we know what their roles turn out to be.
Women, at least white women, are seen as useful in as much as they can have man-children who may grow up to be geniuses.
mooooooo, motherfucker, moooooooooooo!!
It's really a homo-erotic, all-male issue: be something that other men will be jealous of.
posts.key_insight: Elite academic institutions like Princeton optimize for a narrow definition of genius that systematically excludes women, creating a culture where women's only valued role is to support or attract successful men.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that the shift from government to billionaire funding of academic research poses a serious threat to academic freedom. As tenure-track positions decline and researchers become increasingly dependent on private funding, they must market themselves to wealthy donors who prioritize flashy, personally interesting projects over basic research. This creates a system where scientists lose the independence that tenure once protected, becoming beholden to the tastes and priorities of individual billionaires rather than pursuing research for the public good.
key_points: Billionaire funders prioritize flashy research aligned with their personal interests over basic science research that benefits the public
Academics must now learn to market themselves to wealthy donors, creating a 'dating game' with workshops, coaching, and video appeals
Tenure-track positions are declining while teaching positions grow, mirroring the broader trend of job growth in 'shitty jobs'
Without tenure's safety net, researchers dependent on outside grants can lose their jobs when funding disappears
The combination of fewer tenure jobs and billionaire-driven funding priorities effectively eliminates academic freedom
money_quotes: As government financing of basic science research has plunged, private donors have filled the void, raising questions about the future of research for the public good.
Flashy academic content goes hand in hand with flashy marketing. Let's say goodbye to the true nerd who doesn't button up their cardigan correctly.
I believe that academic freedom itself is at stake.
Although theoretically you can have academic freedom without tenure, it is certainly easier with it.
The dwindling tenured positions means there are increasing number of people trying to do research dependent upon outside grants and funding, and without the safety net of tenure.
We've got fewer and fewer tenure jobs, which are precariously dependent on outside funding, and we've got rich people funding their own tastes and proclivities.
Where does academic freedom shake out in that picture? I'm going to say nowhere.
posts.key_insight: The convergence of declining tenure and rising billionaire influence over research funding is quietly dismantling academic freedom by making scientists dependent on the personal whims of wealthy donors.

posts.summary: This advice column installment covers questions about graduate school prestige versus happiness, dating struggles for physics students, navigating boring workplace conversations, dealing with anti-immigration email spam from acquaintances, and a brief exchange about conference culture. The advice consistently emphasizes practical action over overthinking, the importance of being genuinely interesting rather than self-focused, and knowing when engagement with difficult people is futile. O'Neil advocates for being a 'loudmouth' who changes boring conversations rather than suffering through them, and suggests that deeply personal grievances rarely respond to factual rebuttals.
key_points: Prestige matters more for women in academia who don't get the benefit of the doubt that men receive, but matters less outside academic circles
Self-involvement is unattractive; having opinions about the world and ideals to fix problems is what makes someone interesting and even sexy
Boring conversations persist out of habit, and people often welcome someone willing to redirect them to more interesting topics
When someone's political position stems from personal grievance rather than facts, no amount of evidence will change their mind
Sticking with an exciting advisor you trust is more important than comfort in choosing graduate programs
money_quotes: It definitely still seems true that you have to be top-notch to impress people if you're female whereas men often get the benefit of the doubt.
Unless someone is already your friend, they probably don't care about that stuff. At all.
Have ideals. Have plans to fix problems. That's interesting! That's possibly even sexy!
Joy is extremely catchy. Worst case you make some new friends.
I pretty much just barge into conversations and change the topic if I'm bored.
He's gotten it into his head that he lost his job for an abstract political reason. In doing so, he's made it incredibly personal, and no amount of factual evidence is going to change his stance.
posts.key_insight: The best way to connect with others and influence conversations is to be genuinely engaged with the world rather than focused on yourself, and to know when changing someone's mind is impossible.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the launch of the Lede Program at Columbia Journalism School, a data journalism certificate program she's helping to lead. The post shares practical details about upcoming information sessions and the four summer courses being offered. She expresses particular enthusiasm for the final course on platforms, which will examine how biases in data systems affect our understanding of truth. The post also serves as a call for instructors to teach the intensive seven-week summer courses.
key_points: The Lede Program is a new data journalism initiative at Columbia designed to teach journalists data practices
The curriculum covers four areas: basic computing, data and databases, algorithms, and platforms
The platform course will specifically investigate biases inherent in data systems and how they shape perceptions of objective truth
The program is actively recruiting instructors for intensive seven-week summer sessions
money_quotes: I really cherish that addiction like a child. Don't tell my other kids.
We will investigate biases inherent in data, systems, and platforms and how they affect our understanding of objective truth.
posts.key_insight: Training journalists to understand data systems must include examining the biases inherent in those systems and how they shape what we consider objective truth.

posts.summary: This interview with a New York City high school principal explores the implementation challenges of Common Core math standards, focusing on how teacher evaluations, curriculum gaps, and excessive testing create a 'terrorizing regime' for educators. The principal explains that while CC standards exist, there is no CC-aligned curriculum in NY high schools, yet teachers are being evaluated on CC-aligned tests their students were never prepared for. The rollout created massive anxiety because teacher evaluations became tied to test scores before curriculum materials were available. Despite these problems, the principal argues testing shouldn't disappear entirely but needs to be 'dialed down' and made reasonable.
key_points: Teachers spend one-eighth of instructional time administering tests, plus additional time grading them, representing a massive loss of teaching time
The CC rollout created a fundamental sequencing problem: tests were deployed before curriculum existed, and raised expectations without addressing where students actually were
Standards, curriculum, and testing form an interconnected triangle - you cannot evaluate one without the others, yet they were rolled out in isolation
When 75% of students score 1s and 2s on a 4-point scale, mass failure becomes meaningless and tests simply get curved, undermining the entire accountability framework
Teacher evaluation tied to test scores before curriculum alignment gave principals data that could be weaponized against teachers they wanted to remove
money_quotes: The real problem is this: we've created these new standards to make things more difficult and more challenging without recognizing where kids are in the present moment.
What does failure mean when everyone fails? It just means the test was too hard, or that what the kids were being taught was not relevant to the test.
They rolled out the test before any CC curriculum.
Education is a bi-polar pendulum and it never stops in the middle.
There's no test that can't be gamed that's also easy to grade.
You could give everyone a bad number and then fire the people you want, right? Correct. Is that the goal? Under Bloomberg it was.
If teacher evaluations were taken out of the mix, we'd have a very different roll-out environment.
posts.key_insight: The Common Core rollout became a 'terrorizing regime' for teachers not because of the standards themselves, but because high-stakes evaluations were tied to tests before any aligned curriculum existed, creating an impossible accountability trap.

posts.summary: This advice column tackles questions about Bitcoin's viability, emotional sensitivity across cultures, New York City travel recommendations, and whether to date investment bankers. On Bitcoin, Aunt Pythia suggests viewing current troubles as growing pains while noting Google's gmail-based payment system as a serious competitor. The investment banking question prompts a nuanced discussion about balancing individual judgment against statistical filtering in online dating. Throughout, she maintains her characteristic blend of practical advice and social commentary.
key_points: Bitcoin's troubles (Mt. Gox bankruptcy, regulatory confusion) are growing pains, but Google's gmail payment plans pose real competition
Being openly emotional is manageable by preemptively warning others rather than explaining yourself mid-tears
Authentic New York sightseeing means walking neighborhoods and finding local spots, not tourist attractions
Investment bankers aren't automatically morally bankrupt, but using job as a filtering criterion in online dating is rational given limited time
Online dating has profoundly changed cultural mating practices in ways deserving serious study
money_quotes: I would go to the bathroom and trade bitcoin on my smartphone and then return to my real desk to do my real job trading real currency.
Instead of paying heavy fees, you – guess what – tell Google about your checking accounts and other financial information.
Official tourist destinations are incredibly boring and expensive for what they offer (and what they offer is bum-bag bearing tourists, which you can already see in NZ anyway).
Never disqualify someone on one attribute, especially if they otherwise seem great and you find yourself liking them at a basic human level.
If you are playing the numbers – which is all you can do on these websites – then you might well decide to avoid investment bankers.

There are plenty of people in finance and even in investment banking that are perfectly nice and even sensitive and empathetic. On the other hand, there is some story explaining why they're there, and it often exposes a weird side to them.
I would love someone to do a quantitative and qualitative investigation to see how people have changed their dating and mating habits through online dating.
posts.key_insight: Individual exceptions don't invalidate statistical reasoning—whether filtering potential dates by profession or evaluating new technologies, playing the numbers while remaining open to surprises is the pragmatic approach.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques a new algorithm designed to measure whether regulatory agencies take public comments into account when finalizing rules. The model, developed in part by a former CFTC economist, uses NLP to score 'regulatory sentiment' from public comments. O'Neil argues the fundamental flaw is that industry lobbyists dominate public commenting systems both in volume and length of submissions, meaning the algorithm would primarily capture industry preferences rather than genuine public sentiment. She concludes this represents a mathematical model that could legitimize regulatory capture while appearing objective.
key_points: The algorithm measures regulatory sentiment on a -1 to +1 scale by analyzing word clusters in public comments
Industry insiders and lobbyists systematically overwhelm public commenting systems in both number and length of submissions
Aggregating all public comments into one score will inherently be dominated by well-resourced corporate interests
If such a model were adopted, it would create even stronger incentives for lobbyists to flood comment systems
Mathematical objectivity can mask deeply biased inputs and assumptions
money_quotes: What I do have a problem with is the apparent assumption that the 'the number assigned to all the public comments' makes any sense, and in particular whether it takes into account 'the public's view'.
The problem with this is that the industry insiders and their lobbyists overwhelm public commenting systems.
Now think about how the NLP algorithm will deal with this in a big pot: it will be dominated by the language of the pro-industry insiders.
If such a model were to be directly used, say to check that public commenting letters were written in a given case, lobbyists would have even more reason to overwhelm public commenting systems.
The take-away is that this is an amazing example of a so-called objective mathematical model set up to legitimize the watering down of financial regulation by lobbyists.
posts.key_insight: Algorithms that treat all public comments equally will systematically amplify the voices of well-resourced lobbyists while creating a veneer of democratic legitimacy.

posts.summary: O'Neil sharply critiques a New York Times article that purports to help women negotiate raises but actually advises them to conform to sexist stereotypes. She highlights a particularly damning quote where experts acknowledge the unfairness of requiring different behavior from women while still recommending compliance. O'Neil rejects the premise that navigating systemic sexism requires accepting its terms, comparing the advice to Susan Patton's regressive counsel for young women seeking husbands.
key_points: Advice telling women to 'pay attention to' gender barriers actually means conforming to sexist stereotypes
There's a troubling double standard where women must modify their behavior in ways not required of men
Mainstream media packages regressive gender advice as empowerment
Navigating an unfair system should not require accepting its unfairness as legitimate
money_quotes: If you read on you realize that what they mean by 'pay attention to' is 'roll over and conform to stereotypes'. Super gross, and fuck that.
It's totally unfair because we don't require the same thing of men. But if women want to be successful in this domain, they need to pay attention to this.
What happened to the feminists?!!
posts.key_insight: Framing conformity to sexist expectations as practical advice for women perpetuates the very barriers it claims to help them navigate.

posts.summary: The post draws a parallel between two seemingly unrelated phenomena: rising college costs and Monsanto's big data strategy for agriculture. Both are characterized as arms races that benefit the powerful institutions while harming individuals (families and farmers respectively). O'Neil argues that the only solution to an arms race is an armistice, but such agreements won't happen when one party profits from the escalation. Colleges benefit from tuition competition while parents suffer, and Monsanto deliberately creates competitive pressure to force independent farmers into their system.
key_points: College costs are rising due to a perverse combination of US News rankings gaming, federal loan incentives, and reduced state funding
Arms races can only be solved through armistice, but armistices won't happen when powerful players benefit from the race
Monsanto already has farmers' data through surveillance regardless of whether farmers opt into their analytics services
Monsanto is deliberately creating an arms race to force non-Monsanto farmers into economic dependence on their system
The asymmetry of power means individual actors (families, farmers) cannot escape these systems without collective action
money_quotes: When you have an arms race, the only solution is an armistice.
An armistice won't happen if the players in question are benefitting from the arms race.
Monsanto already has all your data, and will have it forever, due to their surveillance.
They are explicitly creating an arms race between Monsanto farmers and non-Monsanto farmers.

If they give Monsanto farmers an extra boost, it will lead other farmers into the conclusion that, without such a boost, they won't be able to keep up, and they will be forced into the Monsanto system by economic necessity.
posts.key_insight: Powerful institutions deliberately engineer arms races that force individuals into dependency, and these races persist because the institutions profit from the escalation while individuals bear the costs.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions about academic research opportunities, breaking into data science, measuring societal fairness, and an off-color question about anatomy. The most substantive discussion centers on criminal justice inequality, where she argues that the real measure of unfairness should be the probability that making a mistake lands someone in prison—a probability that varies dramatically by race and class. She also provides practical advice for aspiring data scientists and reassures an undergrad that REU experience, while increasingly common, isn't strictly necessary for graduate school admission.
key_points: REU programs have proliferated since the 1970s and have become a common signaling device for grad school, but aren't strictly necessary—alternative research experiences like reading courses can substitute
For breaking into data science, start with Python, probability/statistics, then progress to machine learning, Bayesian statistics, and ethics
The prison system is a 'truly disgusting and predatory big business' that profits from incarcerating those too poor to be profitable otherwise
A better measure of societal fairness would be the probability that a mistake lands someone in jail—this varies dramatically by race due to discriminatory enforcement like Stop & Frisk
Everyone makes mistakes, especially young people, but the consequences of those mistakes are distributed profoundly unequally
money_quotes: If you're too poor for us to make money off of you directly, we will throw you in prison and make money off of your incarceration.
What is the relative chance that someone can get away with a mistake?

We all make mistakes, and young men (and women) are especially impulsive and judgment-lacking. So instead of asking whether they make mistakes, ask instead what the chances are that such mistakes will land them in jail or prison.

The rate of marijuana use is similar for whites and blacks but blacks are going to jail way more for possession, essentially because of Stop & Frisk. In other words, blacks are more likely to get caught.
I feel like those probabilities might be a good start at what you're getting at.
posts.key_insight: True societal fairness should be measured not by whether people make mistakes, but by the wildly unequal probability that their mistakes result in incarceration.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines how the U.S. poverty threshold, unchanged since 1964 except for inflation adjustments, has become fundamentally broken. The original formula multiplied a family's food budget by 3, based on the observation that poor families spent a third of income on food. Since food now represents only one-sixth of poor families' spending, the multiplier should be 6, effectively doubling poverty thresholds. This outdated measure affects eligibility for Medicare, Medicaid, and census poverty statistics, systematically undercounting who is actually poor.
key_points: The poverty threshold formula from 1964 (food budget × 3) assumed families spent one-third of income on food
Poor families now spend only one-sixth of income on food, suggesting the multiplier should be 6 instead of 3
Updating the formula would double official poverty numbers and expand program eligibility
Geographic cost variations like NYC rent mean even an updated formula might still undercount poverty
The fundamental questions are what the formula is meant to accomplish and who benefits from keeping it unchanged
money_quotes: Food has gotten a lot cheaper since 1964, and other stuff hasn't.
Now if you think about it, the formula should be more like 'economic food budget' * 6, which would effectively double all the numbers.
If we double those numbers we will be seeing a huge surge in the official numbers.
The truth is, in some locations, like New York, rent is so high that the formula would likely be needing even more adjustment.
As usual the key questions are, what are we accomplishing with such a formula, and who is 'we'?
posts.key_insight: Outdated poverty formulas aren't just technical oversights—they're political choices that determine who counts as poor and who receives help.

posts.summary: This brief post shares two items: an interview with Kaiser Fung on Junk Charts where O'Neil discusses trust in data analysis, and a recommendation of Brian Keegan's iPython notebook that revisits FiveThirtyEight's analysis of women in film. O'Neil highlights the notebook as a model of open data journalism, connecting it to broader discussions about transparency and reproducibility in quantitative analysis. The post serves as a link roundup connecting various figures in the data journalism community.
key_points: O'Neil was interviewed by Kaiser Fung about trust in data analysis
Brian Keegan created an iPython notebook revisiting FiveThirtyEight's Bechdel test analysis
The notebook demonstrates how open, reproducible analysis should work in data journalism
O'Neil endorses transparency and openness as essential values in quantitative journalism
money_quotes: Keegan's notebook is truly a model of open data journalism, and the underlying analysis is also interesting.
posts.key_insight: Open, reproducible analysis through tools like iPython notebooks represents the gold standard for transparent data journalism.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the intense focus on high-frequency trading is disproportionate to its actual harm compared to other Wall Street abuses. While she acknowledges HFT is a net negative that skims money from large fund investors and represents wasteful human resources, she contends it's essentially 'business as usual at a microscopic scale.' She urges readers to redirect their outrage toward more consequential issues: money laundering at major banks, ongoing mortgage fraud, Too-Big-To-Fail getting worse, the two-tiered justice system, and regulatory capture that lets financial masterminds escape accountability.
key_points: HFT acts as a small negative tax on average people whose money is in regularly-traded funds, but its harm is overstated
The real scandal of HFT is the wasted human talent and resources spent on marginal advantages like laying cables through mountains
Far worse problems deserve attention: money laundering at HSBC and Citi, mortgage fraud, predatory lending targeting the poor, and Too-Big-To-Fail growing worse
The justice system has become two-tiered, with financial fraud masterminds facing no jail time while the SEC and Obama administration enable this through inaction
Media attention on HFT, boosted by Michael Lewis's engaging writing, serves as a distraction from systemic ongoing fraud
money_quotes: It's unusual that I find myself in the position of defending Wall Street activities, but here goes.

Nobody should really be surprised to learn that people will do anything to make money that's technically possible and technically legal, and that they will brag about how they're making the world a better place while they do it.
It's really business as usual at a microscopic scale.
Let's talk about the increasing distance between the justice system for the poor and the justice system for the rich in this country.
The HFT noise is misplaced and a distraction from the ongoing real story.
Let's talk about the people who created the underlying fraud still at work in places like Bank of America, and how few masterminds have gone to jail.
posts.key_insight: Outrage is a finite resource, and the media frenzy over high-frequency trading diverts attention from far more damaging financial crimes that continue with impunity.

posts.summary: The post examines whether the Heartbleed bug in OpenSSL represents a failure of the open source model. O'Neil argues that while the two-year delay in catching the bug is concerning, open source ultimately worked as intended—the bug was found and disclosed. She points to proprietary software disasters like Knight Capital and the ObamaCare website to show that bugs are universal, not unique to open source. The real issue is who knows about bugs and can exploit them before they're publicly exposed.
key_points: Open source worked as intended with Heartbleed—it just took two years instead of two days to catch the bug
Proprietary software has equally serious bugs (Knight Capital, ObamaCare website), proving this isn't an open source problem
Security bugs that don't cause visible failures are harder to catch than bugs that make software crash
The key question isn't whether bugs exist, but who knows about them and can exploit them before disclosure
Success requires both more vigilant code review from developers and public acknowledgment of programming's difficulty
money_quotes: First of all, it did work as intended, even though it took two years instead of two days like people might have wanted.

Even when people are paid to fix bugs, they fix the kind of bugs that cause the software to stop a lot sooner than the kind of bug that doesn't make anything explode, lets people see information they shouldn't see, and leaves no trace.
In other words it's more a question of who knows about the bugs and who can exploit them.

This would mean that, on the one hand the nerds becoming more vigilant in checking vitally important code, and learning to think like assholes, but also the public would need to acknowledge how freaking hard it is to program.
If I'm the guy who put in the bug, I'm changing my name and going undercover just in case.
posts.key_insight: The Heartbleed bug isn't evidence that open source failed—it's evidence that finding subtle security flaws is extraordinarily difficult regardless of development model, and open source at least eventually exposes them.

posts.summary: The post critiques the economic assumption that envy (relative status-seeking) drives human behavior, arguing this framework only works locally and misses the deeper feelings of dispossession affecting those in poverty. Falkenstein's theory that people optimize for relative ranking rather than absolute wealth may explain office politics but fails to capture the despair of severe inequality. O'Neil distinguishes between local envy (coveting a colleague's bigger office) and the more dangerous sense of humiliation and dispossession that comes from extreme proximity to wealth while destitute. She argues that projecting envy onto poor people leads to misguided policies like pushing stock market investment for retirement when people actually want security, not risk. The piece concludes that elites' myopic envy-based worldview prevents them from understanding what struggling people actually need.
key_points: Envy operates locally and myopically - people compare themselves to neighbors and colleagues, not to distant billionaires, which is why they consistently underestimate actual wealth inequality
The feeling poor people experience isn't envy but something deeper and more anarchic: dispossession, humiliation, and despair
Envy actually functions as a distraction that keeps people 'acting safely inside the system' rather than confronting structural inequality
Policy elites project their own envy-based worldview onto poor people, leading to tone-deaf proposals like encouraging stock market investment when people want security, not risk
The distance between policymakers and the poor is too large for their system of envy to translate meaningfully into good policy
money_quotes: When members of my Occupy group talk about living on $2 a day while sleeping at homeless shelters in New York City, surrounded by men in suits with chauffeurs, it is very relevant that the privations described are combined with a deep sense of humiliation of their understanding of their relative position.
I'm starting to think envy is something to distract us from something more dangerous, which is that sense of privation and dispossession, which runs deeper and is more anarchic.
Envy seems like a myopic feeling that keeps us acting safely inside the system, where if we follow the rules but we're a little bit better at them, we will get that bigger office or bigger car.
For most people I meet and talk to, at this point retirement is not at all about the thrill of risk-taking, but rather the avoidance of risk altogether.
The distance between the authors and the people they claim to be trying to help is too large for their system of envy to translate meaningfully.
It's a safe kind of assumption that doesn't boil the blood.
posts.key_insight: Elites misread economic despair as mere envy because they can only see the world through their own local, myopic status competitions - a projection that produces policies utterly disconnected from what struggling people actually need.

posts.summary: This advice column installment opens with Aunt Pythia's unsolicited discussion of female orgasm physiology and male objectification before addressing three reader questions: one about accessing student test data to fight VAM teacher evaluations, one about a lonely man over-relying on a female friend for emotional support, and one from a college student doing sex work to pay tuition. The column blends frank sexual discussion with policy critique and practical life advice, maintaining a characteristic irreverence throughout.
key_points: Student test score data used in VAM teacher evaluations is deliberately opaque and legally protected, making data-driven opposition nearly impossible - the fight must be political
The column argues that male genitals are not 'ripe for objectification' the way women's bodies are, reflecting broader gender asymmetries
Lonely people seeking excessive emotional support from one friend need to diversify their social connections before that friendship can be healthy
Sex work's impact on future opportunities depends heavily on identifiability and career goals, but society's prudishness and hostility toward women is the real problem
Educational policy battles around teacher evaluation models are fundamentally about who controls data and uses it as 'an opaque and threatening weapon'
money_quotes: The whole point of it is that they control the data and are using the model as an opaque and threatening weapon.
Once you have a real live woman to talk to in New York I bet she'll be psyched for you and more willing to return your (less frequent and less pathetic) calls.
We are super prudish in the best of times and at less good times we are freaky and hostile towards women. That's not your fault.
Whether it's demeaning is probably something you will not be able to fully answer until you live with the ramifications of having been in it.
I'm hoping you gird yourself for assholes and assumptions, and I hope you can push back.
Making friends is easier than finding a girlfriend and often helps.
posts.key_insight: Whether discussing opaque teacher evaluation algorithms or personal life choices, power asymmetries and societal double standards shape outcomes more than individual merit or data.

posts.summary: O'Neil declares that people who obsessively exercise are boring because they constantly talk about their times, progress, and equipment. Drawing from her own experience training for a triathlon, she describes becoming disgusted by her own self-obsession during the race. She connects this to her hostility toward the Quantified Self movement, arguing that fitness trackers are just props for having the same tedious conversations. While acknowledging exercise is good and some people genuinely need tracking for health reasons, she contends most quantified-self enthusiasts are driven by narcissism and anxiety rather than health.
key_points: Obsessive exercisers are boring because they turn every conversation into a monologue about their stats and progress
The author trained for a triathlon and became disgusted by her own self-obsession during the race itself
Quantified Self gadgets are essentially props for having boring, self-centered conversations about fitness
Most people tracking their exercise aren't doing it for health but for narcissistic, anxiety-shielding, or competitive reasons
There's a difference between enjoying exercise and making it your entire conversational identity
money_quotes: You could just replace yourself with a gadget that listens, nods, and then says encouraging things like, 'Way to go!' at the very end.
I wondered how I'd become so incredibly boring and self-obsessed that I had paid good money and driven miles and miles just to obsessively exercise in front of other people.
I love doing it, or at least I love having done it because it makes you feel good.
Sorry but can we talk about something not incredibly boring now?
All those gadgets and doodads are essentially props to pull out and use to have that same boring conversation that I've already refused to give into.
Most people are not doing this for health. They are doing it for some other weird, narcissistic and anxiety-shielding coping-mechanistic self-competitive reason.
I'm not hating on them exactly, because I get it, and I've been there. But I don't want to talk about them with them.
posts.key_insight: The Quantified Self movement often serves narcissism and social performance rather than genuine health improvement, turning exercise into an excuse for tedious self-centered conversation.

posts.summary: The post argues that randomized experiments, the gold standard in science, should be applied more broadly beyond medicine to test policies and business practices. O'Neil explains the difference between randomized experiments (which establish causation) and observational studies (which only show correlation), using examples from Swedish work-hour experiments, HR department decisions, and money in politics research. She highlights how non-randomized comparisons, like companies with versus without HR departments, conflate treatment effects with cultural differences. The post concludes by advocating for more experimentation using accessible tools like Mechanical Turk to settle policy debates with evidence rather than arguments.
key_points: Randomized experiments establish causal relationships, while observational comparisons only reveal correlations that may reflect cultural or selection biases
Medical experiments face ethical constraints that don't apply to workplace or policy experiments, yet we underutilize experimentation in these domains
The Swedish 6-hour workday experiment demonstrates how governments can test policy changes systematically before widespread implementation
Companies eliminating HR departments can't be compared to those keeping HR because the decision itself reflects underlying cultural values about employee protection
Tools like Mechanical Turk make conducting randomized experiments accessible and affordable for testing hypotheses about human behavior
money_quotes: Experiments are hard in medicine. But they don't have to be hard outside of medicine! Why aren't we doing more of them when we can?

In a randomized experiment, you are setting up and testing a causal relationship, but in a non-randomized experiment like the HR companies versus the no-HR companies, you are simply observing cultural differences without getting at root causes.

I would never work in a company that doesn't value HR, because, as a woman, I am very aware of the fact that women get sexually harassed by their bosses and have essentially nobody to complain to except HR.

If you read the article, it becomes clear that the companies that get rid of HR don't think from the perspective of the harassed underling but instead from the perspective of the boss who needs help firing people.

If we are arguing about whether a certain method or policy works versus another method or policy, can we set up a transparent and reproducible experiment to test it? Let's start making science apply to our lives.
posts.key_insight: We should extend the rigorous experimental methods used in medicine to policy and business decisions, using modern tools to replace endless debates with reproducible evidence.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reflects on a question posed by organizer Omar Freilla about how recently one has 'experienced democracy.' Initially skeptical, she comes to see its value when reframed as asking about meaningful participation in local decision-making rather than formal voting. She examines democracy in various contexts—workplace hierarchies, family dynamics, and Occupy's direct democracy model—concluding that many people are denied democratic participation in their daily lives, which she describes as 'soul-crushing.'
key_points: Democracy should be understood as meaningful participation in local decisions that affect groups, not just formal elections
Workplace democracy varies dramatically—some workers make decisions while others have every choice dictated to them
The absence of democratic participation in daily life is dehumanizing and worth examining
Even small democratic experiences (like children choosing dinner) create genuine joy and engagement
Direct democracy, as practiced in Occupy, provides a tangible emotional experience that sustains participation
money_quotes: How recently has your vote mattered in a local decision that affects a group?
It's about looking around and deciding who around me gets to participate in democratic decisions and who doesn't.
And that latter workplace anti-democratic situation is, I imagine, maximally soul-crushing.
Turns out my kids love making decisions, it makes them gleeful in fact, even if it's just what to eat for dinner.
I know how good participating and experiencing democracy actually feels there, and it's a big part of why it works.
posts.key_insight: Democracy isn't just about voting in elections—it's about having meaningful voice in the daily decisions that shape our lives, and most people are denied this experience in their workplaces and communities.

posts.summary: O'Neil reviews a Princeton study by Gilens and Page that empirically tests who actually influences U.S. policy outcomes. Using polling data correlated with policy changes over four years, the researchers found that economic elites and business interest groups have substantial independent impact on government policy, while average citizens have near-zero independent influence. The study shows that ordinary people only 'win' when their preferences happen to align with elite interests. O'Neil finds the methodology impressive despite acknowledging caveats about modeling choices and proxies.
key_points: Multivariate analysis shows average citizens have near-zero independent influence on policy outcomes
When average citizens' preferences conflict with economic elites, citizens generally lose
Business interest groups have far more policy influence than mass-based groups like unions
The study tracked polling data against actual policy changes over 4-year periods
Using 90th percentile wealth as the cutoff actually underestimates elite influence compared to 98th percentile
money_quotes: Economic elites and organized groups representing business interests have substantial independent impacts on U.S. government policy, while average citizens and mass-based interest groups have little or no independent influence.
Average citizens and mass-based groups only win when their interests align with economic elites, which happens sometimes, or business interests, which rarely happens.
In the United States, our findings indicate, the majority does not rule — at least not in the causal sense of actually determining policy outcomes.
When a majority of citizens disagrees with economic elites and/or with organized interests, they generally lose.
Because of the strong status quo bias built into the U.S. political system, even when fairly large majorities of Americans favor policy change, they generally do not get it.
posts.key_insight: Empirical analysis of U.S. policy outcomes reveals that democracy functions as an oligarchy where economic elites and business interests determine policy while average citizens have no independent influence.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the faculty lineup for the Lede Program, a new data journalism program she's directing at Columbia's Graduate School of Journalism. She highlights the instructors for the summer courses including Jonathan Soma for Basic Computing and Algorithms, along with several collaborators from Columbia. The program is actively accepting applications and has already made some admission offers. O'Neil also mentions recent media coverage of the program in PBS MediaShift.
key_points: The Lede Program summer faculty includes Jonathan Soma as primary instructor, with Dennis Tenen and Chris Wiggins supporting
The program covers Basic Computing, Algorithms, and Databases as core summer courses
Applications are open with some strong candidates already receiving admission offers
O'Neil wrote about the program's genesis and goals for PBS MediaShift
money_quotes: I don't use the word 'innovative' lightly!
We've already gotten some super strong applications and made a couple offers of admission.
posts.key_insight: The Lede Program at Columbia represents a serious effort to train journalists in data and algorithmic literacy with strong interdisciplinary faculty.

posts.summary: This advice column installment covers four questions: why Olympic athletes have more casual sex than math conference attendees (freedom assertion after controlled childhoods vs. already having agency), a follow-up from someone who escaped a misrepresented job, ideas for a better dating site that focuses on compatibility questions rather than photos and demographics, and advice for a gay STEM graduate student worried about career impacts of coming out. Aunt Pythia argues that Olympic village hookup culture stems from athletes finally asserting control after highly regimented childhoods, while mathematicians already have autonomy in their daily lives. She critiques existing dating sites for making '99% of all people unfairly unattractive' by leading with the wrong information.
key_points: Olympic athletes' hookup culture is really about asserting freedom and control after childhoods of intense parental and coach supervision, not just about sex
Kids with controlling parents go wild in college for the same psychological reasons - it's about reclaiming agency over their destiny
Current dating sites fail because knowing age, race, height, weight, and seeing photos 'makes 99% of all people unfairly unattractive' - it's the wrong information to lead with
In organic meetings, people connect first and notice physical details later without caring as much
STEM fields remain unfriendly to gay people, and the academic job market's expectation of geographic mobility compounds this by potentially forcing moves to backward places
money_quotes: I don't think what's going on there in the Olympic Village is really about sex. I mean, that's super dumb for me to say, because obviously tons of sex is happening, but it's really about freedom and control and conquests.

The kids who had controlling parents and no freedom in high school are particularly prone to spending too much money, drinking too hard, and fucking anything that moves, even relative to the kids whose upbringing was more relaxed and free.
If you account for the selection bias for a minute and just think about the childhoods these kids have, your heart breaks for them.
Generally speaking math people have agency over their own lives, time to have sex when they want and so on, so when they get to a math conference, they just have more of the same.
Knowing someone's age, race, height, and weight and seeing a picture of them makes 99% of all people unfairly unattractive.

Some woman going online for a date might think she needs the man to be taller than she is, and filter stuff out that way, but in real life she'll meet someone at a party and be really into him and later realize he's probably a couple inches shorter than she is and she won't care at all.
My short-term advice to you is to do what you need to do to keep your options wide, and if that includes coming out because you're fed up with this bullshit, then definitely keep that in mind.
posts.key_insight: Behavior that looks like hedonism is often really about asserting agency and freedom after periods of intense external control, whether for Olympic athletes escaping regimented childhoods or college students breaking free from controlling parents.

posts.summary: A mathematician and mother vents about the epidemic of rude children and their parents' failure to teach basic manners. Drawing from a frustrating vacation at a Mexican resort, she observes wealthy tourists—especially Europeans—treating service workers as 'nonhumans' while their children lack basic courtesy. She argues that while children's creativity and spirit are innate, good manners require deliberate parental effort. The rant extends to academia, citing grad school applicants who ghost programs without declining offers, ultimately placing blame squarely on parents who neglect this fundamental aspect of raising children.
key_points: Good manners aren't innate—they require deliberate parental effort to develop
Wealthy tourists often treat service workers in developing countries with shocking disrespect
Teaching 'please' and 'thank you' is a non-negotiable parental duty, including in foreign languages when traveling
The rudeness epidemic extends to educated adults, like PhD applicants who ghost admissions committees
Children's natural exuberance should be preserved, but basic courtesy toward others must be taught
money_quotes: While my kids are creative, beautiful souls, so are many (perhaps all) children I've met, and it would be the height of arrogance to take credit for that as a parent.
Teach your fucking kids to say 'please' and 'thank you'.
What is it that when you're in France everyone's all 'thank you and have a nice day' but when these petit bourgeois assholes come to Cancun they treat Mexicans like nonhumans?
My son held the door for a face-lifted Russian lady today who didn't even say thank you.
Just teach them to treat other people with basic respect and courtesy.
Whose fault is this? THE PARENTS' FAULT. Damn parents. Come on!!
posts.key_insight: Parents can't take credit for their children's innate qualities, but they are entirely responsible for teaching—or failing to teach—basic human courtesy.

posts.summary: This satirical post applies rigorous statistical analysis to a Daily Show segment about Medicare-funded penis pumps. Guest author Stephanie Yang calculates the probability that at least one male member of Congress has received a federally funded penis pump, arriving at 93.8% - just shy of statistical significance. The analysis assumes uniform distribution among male Medicare recipients and uses actual spending figures ($172 million for 478,000 devices). The post demonstrates how statistical reasoning can be applied to absurd questions while poking fun at both government spending and the language of probability.
key_points: Medicare spent $172 million on penis pumps from 2006-2011, purchasing 478,000 devices at $360 each
2.15% of male Medicare recipients received a federally funded penis pump based on these figures
With 128 male Congress members over 65, there's a 93.8% probability at least one received a device - just under the 95% confidence threshold
The probability of two or more congressional recipients is lower but still 'probably' true
Would need 138 Medicare-eligible male Congress members to reach 95% confidence for at least one recipient
money_quotes: Statistically, a lot of things are 'probably' true, and many details are contained in the word 'probably'.
Medicare bought one penis pump for every 46.5 eligible men.
The chances of at least one member of congress having said penis pumps is 93.8%, which is just shy of the 95% confidence that most statisticians agree on as significant.
The estimate is close enough for us to agree with Ilya that there is probably someone member of congress who has one.
As a corollary to these calculations, I became the first developer in the history of mankind to type the following command: git merge --squash penispump.
posts.key_insight: Rigorous statistical methods can illuminate even the most absurd questions, revealing how much information is hidden in probabilistic language like 'probably.'

posts.summary: Fawn Nguyen, a middle school math teacher, offers a practitioner's perspective on the Common Core State Standards, distinguishing between standards (ingredients), curriculum (the dish), and testing while defending the CCSS against critics who conflate these elements. She argues the standards are superior to previous state standards, bringing consistency, equity, and coherence to math education nationwide. Her main concern isn't the standards themselves but improper implementation and insufficient teacher training in mathematical content knowledge. She emphasizes that teaching math requires deep understanding of math, not just a college degree, and highlights the 8 Mathematical Practices as the most valuable aspect of CCSS for developing lifelong learning habits.
key_points: Standards, curriculum, and testing are related but distinct - blaming standards for bad curriculum or tests is like blaming ingredients for a poorly prepared dish
The biggest risk to CCSS success is improper implementation and lack of sustained professional development for teachers in content knowledge
Teaching elementary mathematics requires deep mathematical understanding, not just a college education - a perverse notion that anyone can teach basic math
Testing should help students learn and inform teaching, but over-testing takes time away from actual learning
The 8 Mathematical Practices are the most valuable part of CCSS, teaching lifelong habits applicable across all disciplines
money_quotes: We can't blame the ingredients when the dish is blandly prepared any more than we can blame the standards when the curriculum is poorly written.
It is a perverse notion to think that anyone with a college education can teach elementary mathematics. Teaching mathematics requires knowing mathematics.
To know a concept is to understand it backward and forward, inside and outside, to recognize it in different forms and structures.
My elementary teacher lied to me! She said we always get a smaller number when we divide two numbers.
Just because one can make tuna casserole does not make one a chef.
Testing is only good for kids when it helps them learn and become more successful.
I struggle with telling my kids that I value them and their learning, yet at the end of each quarter, the narrative sum of their learning is a letter grade.
We teachers do this very hard work – day in and day out, late into the nights and into the weekends – because we love our kids and we love teaching.
posts.key_insight: The success or failure of Common Core math standards depends not on the standards themselves but on whether teachers receive adequate professional development in deep mathematical content knowledge to implement them properly.

posts.summary: O'Neil eviscerates a Wall Street Journal editorial attacking Obama's Pay As You Earn student loan program, calling it vitriolic and illogical. She argues the piece fails to advance debate and misrepresents who benefits from loan forgiveness (including police, healthcare workers, and military, not just government employees). O'Neil proposes free state schools as a real solution to break the feedback loop of rising tuition and student debt. She concludes by pointing out the editorial's logical incoherence: claiming that pushing people toward high-paying private jobs will reduce income inequality contradicts basic economics.
key_points: The WSJ editorial mischaracterizes loan forgiveness beneficiaries, omitting that police, healthcare workers, and military personnel also qualify
Rising college tuition is a long-standing problem driven by a feedback loop between available loans and schools raising prices to match students' ability to pay
Free state schools would be a real solution, representing an investment in the future and addressing mobility problems
The editorial's claim that encouraging high-paying private sector jobs reduces inequality is logically incoherent
Declining state funding for education, not federal loan programs, is a core driver of the tuition crisis
money_quotes: I just don't understand how an article like this can be published in a respectable newspaper. It does not advance the debate.
It's not just that tuition has been going up, at least at state school. It's that state funding has been going down.
What we have here is a feedback loop, and it's hard to break.
My personal approach would be to make state schools free to make the overall field competitive for college costs.
It's called an investment in our future, and it also would help with the mobility problems we have.
