At some point I'm sure it seemed like a terrible idea to form a public elementary school system for free, but we don't think so now.
The claim that by encouraging a bunch of people to go into super high paying private jobs we will reduce income inequality is just weird.
I say if you are so gung-ho about private high-paying jobs, then you also need to embrace rising inequality. Do it in the name of free-market capitalism or something, but please stay logical.
posts.key_insight: Critiquing student loan policy while ignoring declining state education funding and the feedback loop between loans and tuition is intellectually dishonest—and claiming high-paying private jobs reduce inequality is logically absurd.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil explores what distinguishes mathematics from other scientific fields by examining how different disciplines conduct their internal debates. She observes that while fields like computer science, statistics, economics, and physics have political turf wars over fundamental assumptions and validity, mathematics uniquely fights over aesthetics rather than validity. Since mathematical constructs are valid as long as they're internally logical, mathematicians argue about whether work is beautiful or important, not whether it's legitimate. This leads her to conclude that mathematics is more of an art than a science.
key_points: Academic fields reveal their underlying assumptions through their internal turf wars and debates
Sciences like statistics, economics, and physics fight over the validity of foundational assumptions
Mathematics has no validity debates because any internally consistent logical system is acceptable
Mathematical disputes center on aesthetics—whether something is beautiful or important
This aesthetic rather than validity-based conflict structure makes math more art than science
money_quotes: I left academic math in 2007, but I still identify as a mathematician. That's just how I think about the world, through a mathematician's mindset, whatever that means.
The fights themselves, which are often emotional and brutal, expose the underlying assumptions in certain ways.

In the context of mathematics, where nothing can be considered truly inappropriate as long as the assumptions are clear, it's all about whether something is beautiful or important, not whether it is valid.
Validity has no place in mathematics per se, which plays games with logical rules and constructs.

I could go off and build a weird but internally logical universe on my own, and no mathematician would complain it's invalid, they'd only complain it's unimportant if it doesn't tie back to their field and help them prove a theorem.
I claim that this turf war issue is a characterizing issue of the field of mathematics versus the other sciences, and makes it more of an art than a science.
posts.key_insight: Mathematics is distinguished from sciences by the nature of its internal debates: where sciences fight over validity of assumptions, mathematicians fight over aesthetics, making math fundamentally more art than science.

posts.summary: This advice column installment tackles questions about career anxiety in science, racial fairness in the justice system, pursuing pure math as a career, and society's obsession with body-related pseudoscience. Aunt Pythia counsels a new PhD to stay connected and generous rather than stewing in jealousy. She strongly recommends 'The New Jim Crow' and argues that measuring outcomes matters more than examining colorblind rules. On pure math careers, she's bluntly realistic: very few people get paid to just think about math. The column closes with a meditation on how insecurity, not gullibility, drives our obsession with trivial ideas.
key_points: Career longevity depends on staying connected, asking good questions, and being generous rather than jealous
Laws may be colorblind on paper but discriminatory in deployment—effective racism matters more than deliberate racism
Very few people get paid to simply think about pure math, even professors must teach
People are notoriously bad at assessing their own mathematical talent due to social expectations and flawed testing
Insecurity is what makes us gullible and obsessed with inconsequential ideas—the beauty industry exploits this pattern
money_quotes: Stay connected, go to interesting talks, don't be an asshole, ask good questions, be generous to others, prevent yourself from stewing in jealousy, and you'll be fine.
The official rulebooks are rarely on their face unfair. The system has grown smarter than that.
It's an argument for understanding effective racism rather than deliberate racism, for example, by measuring outcomes.
Very few people get paid to simply think about pure math. It's a fact.
People are notoriously bad at knowing that kind of thing because they get distracted with things like social expectations and shitty timed math tests.
First they make us insecure, then we obsess over trivial and inconsequential things. But it's insecurity that makes us gullible.
posts.key_insight: Systems and industries exploit insecurity—whether through discriminatory enforcement of colorblind laws or beauty standards that manufacture anxiety—making outcome measurement and self-awareness essential defenses.

posts.summary: O'Neil playfully explores the Fermi Paradox and Drake Equation, examining why we haven't been contacted by alien civilizations despite the apparent abundance of Earth-like planets. She notes that recent discoveries of habitable exoplanets actually imply bad news: if more planets can support life, then something else in the equation must be keeping alien civilizations from reaching us—most likely that technological civilizations don't last very long. The post treats this existential implication with characteristic humor, suggesting alternative explanations like aliens being distracted by their equivalent of Candy Crush or put off by our reality TV.
key_points: The Fermi Paradox asks why, given the vastness of the universe, we haven't encountered alien life
The Drake Equation attempts to estimate the number of detectable civilizations by multiplying probability factors
Finding more Earth-like planets means other terms in the equation must decrease to explain the silence—most likely L, how long civilizations survive
This reasoning suggests advanced civilizations may regularly destroy themselves shortly after developing technology
O'Neil offers humorous alternative explanations: aliens too busy with their own distractions, or repelled by humanity's cultural output
money_quotes: So the bad news is that, due to scientists discovering more earth-like planets recently, we're probably all going to die soon.
We exist as proof that some planets do develop intelligent life with the technology to send out signals into space but we have no idea how long we'll last.
We need another term which represents the probability of the invention of Candy Crush Saga!!!
Maybe they took a look from afar and saw reality TV and decided we weren't ready, a kind of updated Star Trek first contact kind of theory.
Does life happen on them but once they get intelligent they immediately kill each other with atomic weapons?
posts.key_insight: The discovery of more habitable planets paradoxically suggests that technological civilizations tend to destroy themselves before making contact—a sobering mathematical implication delivered with gallows humor.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates the shutdown of InBloom, a Gates Foundation and Rupert Murdoch-backed company that collected student data and shared it with third parties. She frames this victory within a broader critique of commodification, citing economist Suresh Naidu's argument that wealth inequality is more dangerous today because money can buy so much more, including education. InBloom had been bypassing FERPA child data protection laws, with school officials unknowingly signing away student privacy. Parent activism and a Fordham Law study exposing these practices led New York State to ban such data sharing, ultimately killing the company.
key_points: InBloom planned to collect vast student data and share it with third-party apps under the guise of 'personalized learning'
The company operated like platform companies (Uber, AirBNB), using its matchmaker status to duck regulations
A Fordham Law study found InBloom was offering 'free' services in exchange for children's data, often violating FERPA
School officials frequently had no idea what they were signing away and hadn't even heard of FERPA
Parent activism and legislative action in New York State ultimately forced InBloom to shut down
money_quotes: From health care to schooling to philanthropy to politicians, we have put up everything for sale.
Inequality in this world is potentially much more menacing than inequality in a less commodified world, simply because money buys so much more.
They are platforms, not cab companies or hoteliers, and they can use that matchmaking status as a way to duck regulations.
InBloom and other companies were offering what seemed like 'free' educational services, but of course the deal really was in exchange for the children's data.
The school officials who were agreeing to the deals had no clue as to what they were signing. The parents were bypassed completely.
I'm not saying that the urge to privatize education – and profit off of it – isn't going to continue after a short pause.
Let's take a moment to appreciate the death of one of the more egregious ideas out there.
posts.key_insight: The InBloom shutdown shows that organized parent resistance can defeat data-harvesting schemes in education, but the broader threat of commodifying children's data for profit remains.

posts.summary: This post appears to be a brief May Day acknowledgment related to the Occupy movement. Given the title 'Occupy May Day' and the date (May 1, 2014), it likely references International Workers' Day and the Occupy movement's participation in labor solidarity events. The post appears to contain minimal or no text content beyond the title, suggesting it may have been a placeholder, a photo post, or contained embedded media that wasn't captured in the scrape.
key_points: References May Day (International Workers' Day) in connection with Occupy movement
Posted during the height of post-Occupy activism period
May indicate O'Neil's solidarity with labor and economic justice movements
posts.key_insight: The post signals O'Neil's alignment with labor movements and economic justice causes through her acknowledgment of Occupy May Day.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques Sandy Pentland's book 'Social Physics,' which argues that comprehensive behavioral data collection will allow scientists to develop mathematical explanations for social behavior and use 'micro-nudges' to influence people's actions. She finds his vision invasive and his premise insulting, but reserves her harshest criticism for his claim that big data analysis will make traditional class categories obsolete. O'Neil argues, citing Nicholas Carr's review, that such approaches ignore how social norms are shaped by history, politics, economics, power, and prejudice. She illustrates her point by contrasting blind orchestra auditions (which deliberately exclude discriminatory information) with typical big data models that codify historical discrimination into automated systems.
key_points: Pentland's vision of comprehensive behavioral surveillance to predict and nudge human behavior is invasive and dehumanizing
Big data models that ignore class, history, and structural inequality will perpetuate and optimize existing discrimination rather than challenge it
The orchestra blind audition example shows how models can be designed to be explicitly non-discriminatory by carefully defining what information matters
Most big data models automate historical patterns of success and failure without examining whether those patterns were fair or just
Pentland's privileged position as a 'successful white dude' may explain his comfort with optimizing a status quo that benefits people like him
money_quotes: It will encourage us to optimize the status quo rather than challenge it.

A statistical model of society that ignores issues of class, that takes patterns of influence as givens rather than as historical contingencies, will tend to perpetuate existing social structures and dynamics.

Using historical training data, this model notices that women don't fare so well historically at a the made-up company as computer programmers – they often leave after only 6 months and they never get promoted. A model will interpret that to mean they are bad employees and never look into structural causes.

They decided to define the requirements for the job to be 'makes good sounding music' and they decided that other information, like how they look, would be by definition not used. It is explicitly non-discriminatory.
Rather than challenging their past definition of success, and making it deliberately fair, they are if anything codifying their discriminatory practices in code.
It honestly cannot surprise anyone that he is a successful white dude as well when he talks about how big data is going to optimize the status quo.
posts.key_insight: Big data systems that treat historical patterns as neutral inputs rather than products of discrimination will automate and entrench inequality rather than solve it.

posts.summary: This advice column installment finds Aunt Pythia in a generous mood, answering questions about productivity, cultural etiquette, academic politics, and internet authenticity. She reveals her time management secrets involve saying no to almost everything, skipping school events, and being 'a neglectful parent.' She addresses the complex and fraught relationship between Barnard and Columbia, tracing institutional inequities to historical sexism in alumni giving. She also acknowledges being fooled by a fake letter mimicking the Belle Knox story but stands by her original advice.
key_points: Productivity comes from ruthless prioritization: using a calendar to block time for yourself rather than fill it with obligations, and saying no to nearly everything optional
The Barnard-Columbia inequity stems from historical sexism where wealthy couples gave to the husband's alma mater, leaving Barnard chronically underfunded
When institutions or people treat you badly, the healthy response is to blame them rather than internalize it as self-loathing
Being fooled by a fake letter doesn't invalidate the advice given - the underlying questions remain worth addressing
Appearing diverse and accomplished is partly illusory - switching jobs frequently creates the impression of doing more than you are
money_quotes: Actually putting stuff on my calendar is a regrettable and final option.
I have not once gone to an event at my kids' school that I didn't have to, and I've also skipped the majority of required events, which makes me a huge asshole but also saves an asston of time.
I also rarely exercise or shower. My biggest luxury is sleep, which I do whenever I can. I am, in short, a disgusting human being.
If someone treats you bad, do you blame them or do you blame yourself? I blame them.
A Columbia man would marry a Barnard girl, they'd get rich, and then they'd give money to Columbia. The end result is that Barnard is relatively poor.
The first trick is to switch jobs a lot. That makes you seem really diverse.
posts.key_insight: True productivity requires the willingness to be seen as an asshole by saying no to social obligations, while institutional inequities often trace back to compounding historical injustices rather than present-day merit.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews the Obama administration's Big Data report by John Podesta, praising its acknowledgment of algorithmic discrimination as a step forward while arguing the recommendations fall short. She notes that while expanding technical expertise at civil rights agencies is positive, it won't be sufficient to actually detect or combat discrimination. The core problem is that discriminatory models are proprietary and often invisible to those being modeled. She argues that meaningful progress requires laws forcing companies to provide deep access to their secret algorithms, not just polite requests from regulators.
key_points: The Podesta report represents progress by acknowledging 'digital redlining' as a real concern, unlike previous congressional discussions
Expanding technical expertise at agencies is necessary but insufficient without legal authority to investigate proprietary models
Discrimination is easy to hide because models are secret and people often don't know they're being modeled
The report addresses discriminatory pricing but ignores harder-to-track redlining issues where you can't know about offers you never received
Effective enforcement requires laws compelling access to secret algorithms, which companies won't provide voluntarily
money_quotes: It's one thing to expand one's expertise – and I'd be more than happy to be a consultant for any of the above civil rights and consumer protection agencies, by the way – but it's quite another to expect those groups to be able to effectively measure discrimination, never mind combat it.
It's just too easy to hide discrimination: the models are proprietary, and some of them are not even apparent; we often don't even know we're being modeled.
How do you know if you haven't been made an offer?
That's not going to happen just because the CFPB asks nicely.
posts.key_insight: Acknowledging algorithmic discrimination is meaningless without laws that compel companies to open their proprietary models to regulatory scrutiny.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews the Podesta Big Data Report to Obama, praising its incorporation of civil rights perspectives from researchers like Danah Boyd and Kate Crawford, and highlighting the Civil Rights Principles for the Era of Big Data signed by multiple civil rights groups. She presents all five principles, which address profiling, automated decision fairness, constitutional protections, individual data control, and data accuracy. However, she criticizes the report for treating finance as separate from big data problems, when in fact finance gave birth to big data and its worst modeling abuses. She notes that the report's recommendations are disappointingly lukewarm and contradicted by the report's own findings, suggesting political constraints limited what could be recommended.
key_points: The Podesta Report benefited significantly from input by researchers like Danah Boyd, Kate Crawford, and Frank Pasquale, as well as civil rights groups
Five key civil rights principles for big data address profiling, automated decision fairness, constitutional protections, individual control, and data accuracy
Finance was misleadingly portrayed as using big data only to combat fraud, when it actually pioneered many of big data's worst modeling errors
The financial crisis resulted from modeling errors and 'politically intentional odious models' that prefigured current big data problems
The report's recommendations are lukewarm and contradicted by its own findings, revealing political constraints on what could be recommended to the Obama administration
money_quotes: Computerized decisionmaking in areas such as employment, health, education, and lending must be judged by its impact on real people, must operate fairly for all communities, and in particular must protect the interests of those that are disadvantaged or that have historically been the subject of discrimination.
Systems that are blind to the preexisting disparities faced by such communities can easily reach decisions that reinforce existing inequities.
I see finance as having given birth to big data.
Many of the mistakes we are making as modelers in the big data era, which require the Civil Rights Principles as above, were made first in finance.
Those modeling errors – and when not errors, politically intentional odious models – were created first in finance.
In fact finance should have been in the report standing as a worst case scenario.
That's interesting, and it shows that politics played a large part of what the authors could include as acceptable recommendations to the Obama administration.
posts.key_insight: The financial industry's modeling failures that led to the 2008 crisis should serve as a warning for big data's civil rights implications, not be excluded from the conversation.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on topics ranging from tax policy and workplace dynamics to mental illness in academia and unusual biological discoveries. The column blends practical advice with sharp social commentary, covering de Blasio's tax returns, whether women showing cleavage affects professional respect, alternative banking systems, managing bipolar disorder in an academic math career, and a scientific article about insects with female penises. O'Neil's responses are characteristically direct, often challenging conventional wisdom while offering pragmatic suggestions.
key_points: Mortgage tax deductions are regressive policies that should be phased out, and getting upset about de Blasio's taxes while ignoring Bloomberg's is selective outrage
Cleavage and physical attributes are forms of power that some find threatening; the double standard between how we treat women's bodies versus men's height or muscles reveals underlying biases
Banking requires trust infrastructure and legal recourse that communities alone cannot provide, though public banks and credit unions offer simpler alternatives
Math communities are not necessarily more compassionate about mental illness despite stereotypes; they may actually be less empathetic due to being more on the Asperger's spectrum
Children are resilient and benefit more from seeing conflict resolved with love than from having a perpetually perfect environment
money_quotes: If men had boobs, they'd be showing them off all the time.
Cleavage is a kind of power, and some men and women find that threatening and/or distracting, and they don't like feeling threatened or distracted so they make up nonsense about respectability.
You never hear a woman tell a man not to show off their height if they want to be respected. In fact, that sounds ridiculous.
Banks really do something, at least in conjunction with the threat of jailtime, and you might not want to depend on your neighbor for that function.
Kids don't need their families to be happy and perfect all the time. In fact some strife and tension is good for kids, especially when that strife is resolved and the love is there.
Math people have their quirks, and it's possible that they are somewhat more likely to have mental health problems, but when it comes to compassion and empathy I don't think we're there.
Sometimes I worry that I am unqualified to give advice in certain situations. In this case I'm not worried because I am absolutely sure that I'm unqualified to give you advice.
posts.key_insight: Social conventions around respectability, whether in dress codes, tax policy, or mental health stigma, often serve to protect those in power from feeling threatened rather than reflecting any genuine moral principle.

posts.summary: The post examines how plagiarism detection software can be easily circumvented through minor text modifications, using a real example from a British research ethics organization. Neuroskeptic demonstrated that simple changes like reordering words and phrases allowed plagiarized text to pass through Grammarly's detection. O'Neil argues this reveals a fundamental flaw in standardized detection systems: when everyone uses the same tools, gaming them becomes trivial. She extends this critique to essay grading software, noting that automated assessment creates opportunities for automated cheating. The post concludes with a darkly humorous prediction that face-to-face testing may become necessary to prevent algorithmic cheating.
key_points: Standardized plagiarism detection software can be defeated through simple text 'laundering' - minor word reordering and additions
When detection tools become universal standards, they create a roadmap for circumvention rather than prevention
The same vulnerability applies to automated essay grading - algorithms can be gamed by iteratively running text through them
Access to cheating software becomes a matter of money, not intelligence, creating inequality
The arms race between detection and evasion may force a return to in-person, face-to-face assessment
money_quotes: The rules aren't that you can't plagiarize, but instead that you can't plagiarize without adequate laundering.
This is the problem with having standard software for things like plagiarism. If everyone uses the same tools, then anyone can launder their text sufficiently to jump through all the standard hoops.
And if you think that someone smart enough to code this up deserves an A just for the effort, keep in mind that you can buy such software as well. So really it's about who has money.
In my cynical moments I'm starting to believe we're going to have to talk to and test people face to face to be sure they aren't using algorithms to cheat on tests.
Of course once our brains are directly wired into the web it won't make a difference.
posts.key_insight: Standardized algorithmic detection systems paradoxically enable the very cheating they're meant to prevent by providing a clear, gameable target.

posts.summary: O'Neil highlights a satirical project by German activist group Peng Collective that parodied Google's acquisition of Nest by creating fake product announcements. The spoof products—including data insurance, emotion-tracking hug apps, personal surveillance drones, and posthumous digital life management—extrapolate current tech trends to their absurd but logical conclusions. The parody was convincing enough to fool attendees at the re:publica conference in Germany. O'Neil presents this without extensive commentary, letting the satire speak for itself about the trajectory of data-driven tech companies.
key_points: German activists created a convincing parody of Google-Nest products at a major tech conference
The fake products satirize real trends: data breaches, emotion tracking, drone surveillance, and digital afterlife services
The spoof included a fake 'Google data security evangelist' presenter
The satire works by taking existing Google/Nest capabilities and extending them to uncomfortable logical endpoints
money_quotes: Data insurance, because accidents will always happen, and we at Google won't protect your data but we will do our best to protect you after the fact.
Opt in for total protection
An app about connections. It always knows where you are and how you feel at any given moment, and it crowdsources hug matches nearby.
A personal drone equipped with livestreaming video capacity, to watch over your home and family. Also takes out the garbage.
Your little friend in the sky
Sustaining your digital life after you die. Plus informing your friends of your death by text.
posts.key_insight: The most effective tech criticism sometimes comes through satire that simply extends current trends to their logical, uncomfortable conclusions.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her initial impressions while reading Tim Geithner's memoir 'Stress Test' in preparation for a podcast discussion. She notes the book contains a mix of surprising honesty and predictable dishonesty. Despite her lack of respect for Geithner, she finds value in reading it because it reveals how elite power networks function. She plans to write a full review after finishing.
key_points: Geithner's book displays a contradictory mix of unexpected candor and predictable misdirection
Reading perspectives from those you disagree with can yield valuable insights about power structures
The book serves as a window into how the 'old boys network' operates in finance and government
money_quotes: Geithner is surprisingly honest about certain things and predictably dishonest, or at least misleading, about other things.

Although at first I thought it would be purely painful to read this book, since I don't have any respect for the guy, now I'm glad I'm doing it, because it exposes so much about how the old boys network operates.
It's material.
posts.key_insight: Even books by people you distrust can be valuable for what they inadvertently reveal about how elite power structures operate.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines why reactions to Jill Abramson's firing from the New York Times split along gender lines. She argues that men and women have fundamentally different 'priors' about sexism based on their lived experiences. Women, who regularly experience sexism, naturally consider it as an explanation when bad things happen to powerful women. Men, who don't experience sexism firsthand, default to assuming events aren't sexist unless proven otherwise. This creates a self-reinforcing cycle where men's priors about sexism rarely get updated.
key_points: When women face setbacks, other women immediately recognize potential sexism while men tend to dismiss that interpretation
Bayesian priors about sexism differ dramatically between genders because men don't personally experience it
Occam's razor actually supports considering sexism as an explanation given how frequently it occurs
The lack of official 'sexism' verdicts means men rarely update their priors even when sexism is present
Powerful women face a double bind: behavior that would be expected from male leaders gets them punished
money_quotes: My prior is that there is sexism in the world, and it happens all the fucking time, especially to women with perceived power (or to women with no power whatsoever).
Men are men, so they don't experience sexism. So they don't update their priors like women do.
If she'd been a man she would have been expected to act like a dick, but because she's a woman they couldn't handle it.
Their prior is that nothing is sexist unless proven to be, since it's so freaking unlikely, according to their prior.
Because there is rarely a moment when an event or reaction is officially deemed 'sexist,' men even categorize events differently than women.
posts.key_insight: Men and women have systematically different Bayesian priors about sexism because only women accumulate the direct evidence needed to update those priors.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil describes the Lede Program at Columbia's Journalism School, designed to train journalists in data skills while maintaining critical skepticism about algorithms and models. The first half teaches technical skills like Python, APIs, and web scraping with emphasis on reproducibility. The second half focuses on understanding algorithmic bias and the politics behind modeling. O'Neil argues that data journalism should demand transparency and scrutiny of calculations, not just accept results at face value. She hopes this approach will lead to evidence-based conversations where assumptions are explicit and code is shared.
key_points: Technical data skills must be paired with deep skepticism about algorithms and their inherent biases
Computing tools are never neutral - they carry the biases of their designers and design processes
Reproducibility and transparency should be the highest standards for data journalism
Even compelling data visualizations are incomplete without access to underlying calculations, assumptions, and data sources
Shared code and explicit assumptions could help establish common ground truths while clarifying genuine disagreements
money_quotes: In the second half of the summer, they will learn more about algorithms, on the one hand, and how deeply to distrust algorithms, on the other.
Computing tools are the products of human ingenuity and effort. They are never neutral and they carry with them the biases of their designers and their design process.
It's easier to criticize than it is to create, and I don't want to train a whole generation of journalists that they should just swear off mathematical modeling altogether.
I'm hoping that we stop hiding misleading arguments behind opaque calculations and start talking about which assumptions are valid.
To make this a truly great video, we would need to be able to scrutinize those calculations and for that matter the data sources and the data.
I hope that, as we share our code and try out different approaches, we find ourselves acknowledging certain ground-level truths that we can agree on.
posts.key_insight: Training data journalists requires teaching them both how to build models and how deeply to distrust them, demanding transparency and reproducibility as the foundation for evidence-based public discourse.

posts.summary: This advice column tackles questions about body acceptance and nudity, media coverage of female politicians, dating compatibility, and casual sex. Aunt Pythia argues that body acceptance is about mindset rather than clothing, defends a Mother Jones headline comparing Gov. Martinez to Sarah Palin as fair political analysis, and observes that casual sex is common but socially acceptable to discuss only in abstract terms. The column maintains a playful, irreverent tone while sneaking in observations about gender double standards in both politics and sexual behavior.
key_points: Body acceptance is a mindset challenge, not a clothing problem - finding beauty in all bodies doesn't require nudity
Politicians have profiles that are fair game for comparison, including gender, especially when parties cynically exploit identity
There's a gendered double standard around discussing casual sex - acceptable for men to admit personally, not for women
The statistical impossibility of mismatched partner counts between men and women reveals widespread lying about sexual behavior
Media titles are often chosen for clicks without author input, so criticism should target publications not writers
money_quotes: It's not the clothes that are the barrier, it's our mindset.
Finding beauty in everyone's body, including our own, is a kind of mindset challenge that is both entertaining and uplifting.
They are both vindictive, narcissistic, and uninformed. They are also both women and being used in a cynical way by the Republican Party for their gender.
You can be offended, but I advise to instead be a bit more realistic about how politics actually works.
For women, casual sex is in this category, and for men it isn't.
The averages do not match, a mathematical impossibility unless a bunch of men had sex with a bunch of old ladies that just died.
I don't think anything holds people back as a general rule. There's a whole bunch of casual sex happening all the freaking time, all around us.
posts.key_insight: Social taboos around bodies and sex create mismatches between what people actually do and what they're willing to admit, and the constraints are often gendered rather than universal.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that Tim Geithner was fundamentally unqualified for his roles at the NY Fed and Treasury, a fact he essentially admits in his memoir 'Stress Test.' Rather than focusing on Geithner's individual failures, she identifies him as a symptom of a broken appointment system that prioritizes connections to figures like Larry Summers and Robert Rubin over actual expertise. She warns this system remains unchanged and will produce more unqualified regulators. The post calls for basic competency testing for financial regulatory positions and expresses concern that a Hillary Clinton presidency would perpetuate the same crony appointment patterns.
key_points: Geithner admits in his book he didn't understand what banks actually did, yet still ran the NY Fed during the crisis
He deflects blame onto Brooksley Born, arguing that because her warnings were dismissed, he couldn't be expected to understand shadow banking
Geithner was 'an effect, not a cause' - the product of an appointment system based on connections rather than competence
The current qualification for these jobs is essentially 'ability to get along with Larry Summers and Robert Rubin and to look like a banker'
Understanding the financial system is achievable for curious, skeptical people with access - it doesn't require being a Goldman Sachs insider or becoming captured by the industry
money_quotes: Tim was unqualified for his jobs and head of the NY Fed, during the crisis, and then as Obama's Treasury Secretary. He says so a bunch of times and I believe him. You should too.
So it was kind of her fault that he wasn't forced to understand stuff, even though she warned people.
The truth is, Tim Geithner was a perfect product of the system. He was an effect, not a cause.

When I dwell on the fact that he got the NY Fed job with no in-the-weeds knowledge or experience on how banks operate, there's no reason, not one single reason, to think it's not going to happen again.
The process currently is their ability to get along with Larry Summers and Robert Rubin and to look like a banker.
It's actually not impossible to understand this system for a curious, smart, skeptical, and patient person who asks good questions and has the power to make meetings with heads of trading floors.
You can remember that it's your job to understand and regulate the system, that it's actually a perfectly reasonable way to protect the country. From bankers.
posts.key_insight: The financial regulatory system isn't broken because of individual bad actors like Geithner, but because appointments are based on political connections rather than actual expertise in how banks operate.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that debates about open data miss the point by ignoring power dynamics. She supports data transparency when it scrutinizes the powerful (like tracking politicians' money) but opposes surveillance of powerless people (like tracking subway riders). She observes that data collection systematically targets those without power while shielding those who have it—workers get scored but managers don't, stopped citizens are identified but police badge numbers aren't recorded. Her core message is that we should stop fetishizing data and instead ask who wields the models and who gets to decide.
key_points: Open data is good when it exposes the powerful, problematic when it surveils the powerless
There's a systematic asymmetry in data collection: the less power you have, the more you're tracked
Crime data reveals this asymmetry—stopped citizens are identified in detail while officers get only precinct-level anonymity
Data and modeling are 'crude tools' that distract from the real questions of power and control
The urgency is in passing protective laws now, before models for all this collected data are even invented
money_quotes: I like data that shines light on powerful people.
I tend to want to protect powerless people, like people who are being surveilled with sensors and their phones.
The power is where the data isn't.
Bloomberg never scored his lieutenants or himself even when he insisted that teachers should be scored.
Data and data modeling are not magical tools. They are in fact crude tools.
It's a boondoggle to think about data when we should be thinking about when and how a model is being wielded and who gets to decide.
One of the biggest problems we face is that all this data is being collected and saved now and the models haven't even been invented yet.
posts.key_insight: The question isn't whether you believe in open data—it's whether the data shines light upward toward power or downward onto the powerless.

posts.summary: O'Neil uses biting satire to expose the stark double standard in how the American legal system treats corporate criminals versus poor individuals. She contrasts big banks paying negligible fines for felonies while maintaining business relationships, against poor defendants being charged for public defenders, jail time, and jury trials—debts that can extend their incarceration. The post highlights how 'equality before the law' is a fiction when corporations receive waivers while indigent people face punitive fees they cannot pay. Her conclusion—choosing to 'be a big bank' next time she's a criminal—underscores the absurdity of a system where legal personhood benefits corporations while harming actual people.
key_points: Big banks pay fines that amount to 'pocket change' and continue business as usual after criminal convictions
Poor defendants are charged fees for public defenders, jail time, and even jury trials—services supposedly guaranteed as rights
Inability to pay court fees can result in extended jail time, creating a debtors' prison dynamic
The legal fiction of corporate personhood grants banks protections while the principle of 'equality before the law' fails poor individuals
Regulatory and legal waivers ensure corporate felons face no meaningful consequences
money_quotes: Next time I'm tried for a criminal act, I think I'd like to be tried as a big bank.
Then I can pay a smallish fine for my misdeeds – pocket change for me, a cost of doing business really.
I'd prefer not to be tried as a poor person, where I'd likely be charged money I don't have for my free lawyer, for any time I spend in jail, and possibly extra for a full jury.
And if I didn't have the money I'd have to spend extra time in jail, until I came up with the money I don't have.
And since all people are equal under the law, even corporations, it shouldn't matter who I choose to be, so I choose to be a big bank.
posts.key_insight: The American legal system's treatment of corporate versus individual crime reveals that 'equality before the law' protects the powerful while punishing poverty.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts presenting her concerns about discriminatory, inequality-increasing, and democracy-threatening big data models to NYC tech leaders. She proposed testing proprietary models for discrimination, helping regulators develop audits, and making certain models transparent. The positive reception led to discussions about commercializing 'fairness audits' as a business opportunity. While she sees potential in effects-based testing that treats models as black boxes, she warns against proprietary fairness audits that could be untrustworthy and ultimately counterproductive.
key_points: Big data modeling poses three major threats: discrimination, increased inequality, and undermining democracy
Effects-based testing can evaluate algorithmic fairness without requiring full model transparency, making it palatable to businesses protecting trade secrets
There's emerging business incentive for companies to demonstrate their models are fair following bad press about discrimination
Fairness audits should be open-source and usable by regulators like CFPB, not proprietary tools from private companies
A proprietary 'fairness audit' model would be worse than nothing because it cannot be trusted to solve the real problems
money_quotes: Big data modeling is discriminatory, increases inequality, and threatens democracy.
Quick, someone, monetize fairness!
I want to give a resume-sorting algorithm different resumes with similar qualifications but different races. An algorithmically induced randomized experiment, if you will.
A test that allows a model to remain a black box feels safe, because it does not require true transparency, and allows the 'secret sauce' to remain secret.
What I don't want, and which would be worse than nothing, would be if some private company developed a proprietary 'fairness audit' model that we cannot trust.
posts.key_insight: While there's growing business incentive to audit algorithms for fairness, proprietary fairness audits would be counterproductive—only open-source, publicly accessible audit methods can be trusted to address discrimination in big data.

posts.summary: Obama's plan to rank colleges for federal loan eligibility addresses the wrong problem in the student debt crisis. While for-profit schools like University of Phoenix do exploit the loan system, the real issue is the explosion of federal loans combined with declining state funding for public education. The post argues that creating a ranking algorithm won't fix the fundamental problem of college affordability. Instead, increased state school funding would create competition that naturally drives down private school tuition.
key_points: Obama's college ranking system is a mathematical model solving the wrong problem
For-profit colleges like University of Phoenix are gaming the federal loan system to extract money from desperate students
Federal loan growth has exploded while state funding for public schools has decreased, especially since the financial crisis
The gap between college costs and available grants continues to widen
Competition from well-funded state schools is the real solution to bringing down private tuition rates
money_quotes: It's a great example of a mathematical model solving the wrong problem.
It's not a college system, it's a business which extracts federal and private loan money from unsuspecting people who want desperately to get a good job some day.
The bottomline is that we – and especially our children – need more state school funding much more than we need a ranking algorithm.
The best way to bring down tuition rates at private schools is to give them competition at good state schools.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic ranking systems can't solve problems rooted in structural funding failures—the student debt crisis needs more public investment, not better sorting mechanisms.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her excitement about the first day of the Lede Program at Columbia Journalism School, a data journalism training initiative. The program kicked off with 17 students learning cloud computing basics by setting up Amazon EC2 servers. The day included a talk on technological determinism and the ethics of reproducibility, followed by hands-on Python exercises. The post conveys pure enthusiasm for teaching data skills to journalists.
key_points: The Lede Program launched with 17 students at Columbia Journalism School
Students immediately got hands-on experience with cloud computing (Amazon EC2)
The curriculum emphasizes ethical considerations like reproducibility alongside technical skills
Course materials are open and available on GitHub
money_quotes: After introducing ourselves – and the 17 students are each amazing – we each fired up an EC2 server on the Amazon cloud
In other words, we rocked out.
posts.key_insight: Data journalism education should combine hands-on technical training with ethical frameworks from day one.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews Jordan Ellenberg's book 'How Not To Be Wrong,' framing it through her personal friendship with him from graduate school. She praises the book as both mathematically rich and genuinely funny, noting it captures Ellenberg's infectious positivity and comedic timing. While celebrating how the book makes mathematical thinking accessible and argues that logical scrutiny is a courageous act available to everyone, she pushes back on some claims about municipal bonds and public policy. The review concludes with a charming anecdote about teaching her son multiplication through chocolate drops, demonstrating the book's core message about mathematical reasoning.
key_points: The book succeeds because it reads exactly like Ellenberg talks—funny, positive, and deeply mathematical
Ellenberg's core argument is that mathematical scrutiny is both courageous and democratically accessible, not limited to elite geniuses
O'Neil disputes Ellenberg's claims about municipal bonds always being repaid and stocks always going up in expectation, calling these beliefs the result of 'cynical political acts'
She disagrees with his sympathy for policymakers making proclamations under uncertainty, seeing flip-flopping dietary advice as harmful and often industry-driven
The book's real value is making readers want to engage and argue with mathematical ideas, even when disagreeing
money_quotes: Jordan has an infectious positivity about him, which balances my rather intense suspicions, and moreover he's hilariously funny.
He's really somewhere between a mathematician and a stand-up comedian, and to be honest I don't know which one he's better at.
Every page is a standalone positive and ontological examination of the beauty and surprise of mathematical discovery.
The act itself is courageous and should be appreciated, and he explains why, and he tells us how much we've already benefited from people in the past who have had the bravery to do so.
It's not an elitist crew of the usual genius suspects, that in fact we can all do this in our own capacity.
No, Jordan, municipal bonds do not always get paid back, and no, stocks do not always go up, not even in expectation.
Even when I strongly disagree with Jordan, I always want to have the conversation. He forces that on the reader because he's so darn positive and open-minded.
And that, my friends, is how not to be wrong.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical thinking is a democratically accessible form of courage that helps us navigate uncertainty, though we should remain skeptical of confident proclamations in genuinely uncertain domains.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her decision to boycott Amazon after learning about the company's dispute with book publisher Hachette and its treatment of warehouse workers. She cites Amazon's bullying tactics in business negotiations, employee surveillance, and harsh working conditions as her primary reasons. The boycott extends to both shopping and blogging habits, meaning she will no longer include direct Amazon links on her blog. However, she acknowledges a practical limitation: she still needs to use Amazon's EC2 cloud services for the Lede Program and is open to suggestions for alternatives.
key_points: The Amazon-Hachette publishing dispute revealed Amazon's willingness to use its market power as leverage against partners
Amazon's treatment of warehouse workers, including monitoring and surveillance, raises serious ethical concerns
Consumer boycotts can extend beyond purchasing to include linking behavior that drives traffic and sales
Even principled boycotts face practical limitations when companies have near-monopoly positions in certain services like cloud computing
money_quotes: I have come to the conclusion that Amazon has become a huge bully.
I'm boycotting Amazon for my shopping as well as my blogging habits, so no more direct links.
Not sure how to avoid that actually, and I'd welcome suggestions.
posts.key_insight: When a company's business practices conflict with your values, a meaningful boycott requires examining all the ways you support them—including the indirect ones like linking—though complete disentanglement from tech giants may be practically impossible.

posts.summary: This advice column installment features Aunt Pythia (Cathy O'Neil) answering reader questions while admitting to being hungover. She addresses topics including academic guilt, multilingual communication struggles, and trans inclusivity. The most substantive advice concerns a grad student's constant guilt about not working enough, where O'Neil reflects on her own academic experience and recommends dedicating time to joy rather than guilt-driven productivity. She also graciously accepts correction about excluding trans people from a previous discussion about sex and gender.
key_points: Academic guilt is often self-imposed and driven by competitive environments, but working from excitement rather than guilt is both healthier and more efficient
Giving yourself permission to do what you love may actually increase productivity rather than decrease it
Language mastery requires patience and focused immersion rather than constant movement between countries
Being empathetic requires actively including marginalized groups like trans people rather than inadvertently erasing their experiences
Sleep should never be sacrificed for productivity
money_quotes: One of the things I don't miss about being an academic is the constant guilt I imposed upon myself.
Instead I do things out of sheer excitement about the ideas.
My advice to you is to give yourself one day a week to do stuff that you just totally love, and banish guilt from your life.
Never skimp on sleep. Skimp on reading Aunt Pythia, but never skimp on sleep.
I'm of the opinion that people get good at what they focus on and what they are patient for.
Although I strive to be empathetic, ignoring someone is a common way to be the opposite.
posts.key_insight: Working from genuine excitement rather than guilt-driven obligation is both more sustainable and more productive than the relentless self-criticism common in academic culture.

posts.summary: O'Neil discusses research showing wealthy people exhibit more unethical behavior, from cutting off pedestrians to stealing candy from children. She argues this stems from rich people viewing greed as virtuous, which itself results from rarely facing consequences, benefiting from systemic advantages, and being insulated from needing others. She connects this to Libertarianism and notes with amusement that some wealthy Libertarians, including Peter Thiel, plan to create floating nations—though their extreme individualism makes agreeing on governance difficult.
key_points: Research demonstrates seven different ways that higher social class correlates with unethical behavior
Once you account for 'greed feelings,' wealth alone doesn't predict cheating—the key factor is believing greed is good
Rich people rarely face meaningful consequences: fees aren't onerous, better lawyers get better outcomes
Wealth insulates people from needing others, enabling entitled behavior without social repercussions
Libertarian seasteading projects face an ironic problem: extreme individualists can't agree on collective governance
money_quotes: The authors figure out seven ways to measure the extent to which rich people are bigger assholes than poor people, a plan that works brilliantly every time.
It's easy to think greed is good if you never get punished for cheating.
That feeling that they have won leaks onto other feelings of entitlement.
Who needs friends when you have resources?
Not all rich people are greed-is-good obsessed assholes. But there are some that specialize in it. They call themselves Libertarians.
The only problem is, they all are so individualistic it's hard for them to agree on ground rules and, you know, a process by which to decide things (don't say government!).
Wouldn't you love it if a good fraction of the people who cut you off in traffic got together and decided to leave town?
posts.key_insight: Wealth breeds unethical behavior not directly, but by insulating people from consequences and fostering a belief that greed is virtuous.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her new role as a non-faculty administrator at Columbia's Journalism School, using it as a lens to examine the broader problem of administrative bloat in American institutions. She draws parallels between rising administrative costs in universities and hospitals, noting that both have grown into expensive behemoths that seem necessary at each stage but become unsustainably complex. She distinguishes between healthcare, where external complexity (insurance, regulations) drives administrative growth, and universities, which created their own complexity largely through internal forces like rankings gaming. The post is self-aware about her own complicity in the system she critiques.
key_points: Administrative costs consume 20-30% of US healthcare spending, far exceeding other developed countries
Private colleges increased administration spending 36% versus only 22% for instruction between 1998-2008
Each administrative addition seems productive or necessary in the moment, but accumulates into unwieldy, expensive organizations
Healthcare complexity is largely externally imposed (insurance, regulations), while universities created their own complexity
The US News & World Report rankings game has become an inescapable driver of university administrative growth
money_quotes: I am now part of the administrative bloat over at Columbia.
I now have a view into how things got so bloated. And how they might stay that way.

At each stage they seem to be adding something either seemingly productive or vitally needed to contain the complexity of the existing machine, but in the end you have enormous behemoths of organizations that are much too complex and much too expensive.
The university system made itself complicated, it wasn't externally forced into complexity, except if you count the US News & World Reports gaming that seems inescapable.

Parents who wonder why college tuition is so high and why it increases so much each year may be less than pleased to learn that their sons and daughters will have an opportunity to interact with more administrators and staffers—but not more professors.
posts.key_insight: Institutional bloat grows through a series of individually reasonable decisions that collectively create unsustainable complexity, and universities—unlike healthcare—have largely imposed this complexity on themselves.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces an upcoming Alternative Banking meeting featuring Adam Reich, who will discuss a collaborative summer project between Columbia's INCITE and the OUR Walmart campaign. The project sends twenty students across the country to organize and conduct oral history interviews with Walmart workers in five regions. O'Neil draws a parallel to the 50th anniversary of Freedom Summer 1964, when volunteers helped register black Mississippians to vote. The post serves as both an event announcement and a framing of contemporary labor organizing within the tradition of civil rights activism.
key_points: Columbia students will conduct oral history interviews with Walmart workers across five regions as part of an organizing effort
The project is a collaboration between Columbia's INCITE research center and OUR Walmart, a worker advocacy campaign
The timing deliberately echoes Freedom Summer 1964, connecting labor organizing to civil rights history
Adam Reich brings both academic credentials as a sociologist and activist experience to the project
money_quotes: It is also, not coincidentally, the 50th anniversary of the Freedom Summer of 1964, when a bunch of volunteers including students helped register black Mississippians to vote.
The plan involves twenty students to scatter across the country, organizing and conducting oral history interviews alongside Walmart workers in five regions.
posts.key_insight: Contemporary labor organizing efforts at corporations like Walmart can be understood as a continuation of the civil rights movement's tradition of student-led grassroots activism.

posts.summary: This is a brief link roundup post where O'Neil shares four articles she's reading. The pieces cover Tim Geithner and Democratic Party financial politics, a profile of hedge fund manager Steve Cohen, Google's algorithmic power over smaller websites like Metafilter, and a piece challenging the concept of 'sluthood.' The post offers minimal commentary but reveals O'Neil's interests in finance, algorithms, and social critique.
key_points: Curates reading on Democratic Party financial establishment and the 2008 crisis response
Highlights coverage of Steve Cohen and Wall Street insider trading culture
Flags concern about Google's algorithmic dominance affecting independent websites
Shares social commentary challenging gendered language and double standards
money_quotes: The power of Google's algorithms can make or break smaller websites.
posts.key_insight: Even in a quick link roundup, O'Neil's interests consistently circle back to power structures—whether in finance, tech platforms, or social norms.

posts.summary: In this advice column, Cathy O'Neil (as Aunt Pythia) responds to questions about data-driven dating, career paths for math PhDs, the data science hype cycle, and statistical validation. Her central critique targets people who 'hack' dating sites by treating potential partners as data points rather than humans, arguing this dehumanizing approach reflects a broader societal problem. She also warns aspiring data scientists that the field is full of hype, with many jobs being glorified Excel work or worse, fronts for predatory models. Throughout, she advocates for maintaining humanity and moral grounding in technical work.
key_points: Using algorithms to 'optimize' dating treats people as bundles of attributes rather than humans, reflecting pickup artist-adjacent thinking regardless of the practitioner's gender
The data science field is experiencing a hype cycle where many jobs don't match expectations - ranging from SQL monkey work to unwitting complicity in predatory modeling
Math PhDs seeking non-academic careers should learn to code, experience company work, and maintain their moral compass
Statistical validation requires more than just testing on a small sample - 8 out of 12 successes proves very little without understanding the problem's structure
New fields like data science inevitably attract both brilliant amateurs and frauds riding the hype wave
money_quotes: He was making himself less receptive, not more receptive, by going on all those dates.
Women who are ABSOLUTELY OBSESSED WITH FINDING MR. RIGHT also do this. They stop thinking about men as people and start thinking of them as bundles of attributes.
I just think that the key thing is to stay human.
Worst case they're asked to audit and approve models they don't understand which are being used in a predatory manner so they're on the hook when shit gets real.

This is what new fields look like, a bunch of amateurs coming together trying to figure out what they're doing. Sometimes it works brilliantly and sometimes it produces frauds who ride the hype wave because they're good at that.
They might find out their job is really nothing like the job they thought they had. They might end up being excel or SQL database monkeys.
8 out of 12 doesn't sound very convincing, and 12 doesn't sound big enough for much of anything.
posts.key_insight: Whether in dating or data science careers, the danger lies in letting algorithmic thinking override our humanity and moral judgment.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts finally building a string telephone between her apartment and her friend's apartment after living in New York for nine years. The project required lowering strings from a 9th floor and 5th floor window to the street, then tying them together with help from people below. The unusual sight of someone holding a very long string while wearing a cowboy hat prompted numerous New Yorkers to stop and ask what was happening. The experiment succeeded, creating a working string telephone across the street that the whole family enjoyed testing.
key_points: The string telephone project was conceived nine years earlier but only just completed
The logistics required street-level coordination to lower and connect strings from different floors
The spectacle broke the typical New York norm of ignoring strangers on the street
A cop car observed the experiment but left once the string was connected
The telephone actually worked, allowing communication between the two apartments
money_quotes: There aren't many things that can make New Yorkers talk to each other on the street, but I've found that holding on to very very long strings whilst wearing a ridiculous hat does the trick.
You're pulling someone's tooth!!
It was one of the most characteristic New York moments of my existence, and it made me feel like a real New Yorker.
For a suburban girl like me this was a hoot.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most delightful urban experiences come from embracing absurd projects that force strangers to connect with each other.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil provides a progress update on the Lede Program, a data journalism course at Columbia where she teaches mornings. The students have rapidly progressed from Python basics through data structures, regular expressions, git, and pandas dataframes. They've worked with real datasets like Enron emails and discussed the relationship between unsupervised learning and surveillance. O'Neil reflects on the challenges of teaching technical tools to groups and notes the need for better visualization libraries than matplotlib.
key_points: Students progressed rapidly from Python fundamentals to pandas dataframes and git in just a few weeks
Real-world datasets like Enron emails make learning programming concepts more engaging
Teaching technical tools to groups surfaces every possible edge case and failure mode
The course connects technical skills to broader societal issues like surveillance
money_quotes: The students are awesome and are learning a huge amount in a super short time.
Turns out that always means you're really happy as in 'woooooohooooooo!!!' or really sad as in 'aaaaaaarghghgh'.
We had a nice discussion about unsupervised learning and how such techniques relate to surveillance.
When you have a class of 20 people installing git, everything that can go wrong does (versus if you do it yourself, then just anything that could go wrong might).
There really should be a better viz tool than matplotlib.
posts.key_insight: Teaching technical skills at scale reveals both the fragility of developer tools and the importance of connecting data techniques to their societal implications.

posts.summary: O'Neil reviews 'House of Debt' by Mian and Sufi, which argues that the 2008 financial crisis was caused by massive household debt rather than banking failures, and that government responses protected wealthy lenders while devastating poor borrowers. The authors use distributional analysis rather than averages to show how the crisis fell disproportionately on poor homeowners. They argue monetary policy is insufficient because it only helps the wealthy, and propose 'shared-responsibility mortgages' where payments automatically adjust with housing prices. O'Neil endorses the core analysis while expressing skepticism that such reforms would ever be implemented given political realities.
key_points: The banking crisis was an effect, not a cause, of household debt - consumers pulled back spending first, then banks collapsed
Government bailouts protected creditors and shareholders while leaving underwater homeowners to suffer, exacerbating inequality
Monetary policy fails because cheap money flows through banks who won't lend to poor people already underwater
The authors propose automatic debt restructuring contracts that would share losses with lenders during downturns
Financial innovation should be interpreted as 'an instrument whose risk hides somewhere in the creases that investors are not yet aware of'
money_quotes: The financial system works against poor people ('borrowers') much more than rich people ('lenders') in times of crisis, and the response to the financial crisis exacerbated this discrepancy.
Whenever you hear the phrase 'financial innovation,' please interpret it to mean 'an instrument whose risk hides somewhere in the creases that investors are not yet aware of.'
The banking crisis was an effect, rather than a cause, of enormous household debt and consumer pull-back.

The financial system did a very poor job of figuring out how to allocate capital and the people who made those loans were not adequately punished, whereas the people who got those loans were more than reasonably punished.

Monetary policy is insufficient, since it works through the banks, who are unwilling to lend to the poor who are already underwater, and only rich people benefit from cheap money and inflated markets.

If we saw debt contracts being written like this, where borrowers really did have down-side protection, then the people of our country might start actually feeling like the financial system was working for them rather than against them. I'm not holding my breath for this to actually happen.
posts.key_insight: The financial system systematically shifts risk onto poor borrowers while protecting wealthy lenders, and policy responses to crises reinforce rather than correct this fundamental inequity.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that eliminating teacher tenure won't solve the achievement gap between poor and wealthy students because there's little evidence teachers or tenure cause this gap. She explains that removing tenure would drive skilled teachers to other careers due to job insecurity and unreliable evaluation methods, leaving schools with either worse teachers or dramatically higher costs to attract talent. The real culprit, she contends, is poverty and inequality, not bad teachers. She predicts this Silicon Valley-backed reform will fail but warns we may have to sacrifice a generation of students to prove that point.
key_points: Removing tenure would cause the best teachers to leave for careers with better job security, since teaching's appeal partly depends on pension benefits requiring long-term employment
Teacher evaluation systems like value-added models are unreliable and can randomly flag good teachers as bad, making the profession even less attractive without tenure protections
The achievement gap correlates strongly with poverty, not teacher quality, so firing teachers won't address the root cause
Schools would face a choice between accepting worse teachers or paying dramatically higher salaries to compensate for lost job security
Silicon Valley's business-minded approach to education reform ignores that incentive structures work both ways—removing benefits drives away talent
money_quotes: The problem is, there's little evidence for this very real achievement gap problem as being caused by tenure, or even by teachers.
People with options and skills will seek other opportunities. After all, we wanted to make it more like a business, and that's what happens when you remove incentives in business!
The only reason teacher salaries have been low historically is that uber competent women like Laura Ingalls Wilder had no other options than being a teacher.
If we want to be data-driven, let's start to maybe blame poverty for bad scores instead.
The actual root cause, which is very likely to be poverty and inequality, hopelessness and despair.
Not sure we want to sacrifice a generation of students just to prove a point about causation.
So we either have worse teachers or education doubles in price, both bad options.
posts.key_insight: Treating schools like businesses and eliminating teacher tenure ignores that the achievement gap is caused by poverty, not teachers, and will only drive talented educators out of the profession.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that Raj Chetty's influential Value-Added Model studies fail to account for a crucial feedback loop: the data comes from the pre-VAM era (1989-1990s) when teachers weren't teaching to the test, making it irrelevant to today's high-stakes testing climate. She points out that no studies compare current VAM scores with other measures of teacher success, suggesting such comparisons likely yield unfavorable results. The author contends that VAM scores are too noisy and inconsistent to be used for tenure decisions, a position she notes is shared by professional statisticians and mathematicians. She calls for qualitative measures of teaching that agree with each other and remain consistent over time before any mathematical model should be trusted for high-stakes decisions.
key_points: Chetty's studies use pre-VAM data from 1989 when teachers weren't teaching to the test, making conclusions inapplicable to current high-stakes testing environments
The absence of studies comparing current VAM scores with other teacher success measures suggests such comparisons produce unfavorable results
VAM scores show ridiculous inconsistencies for the same teachers across years and different VAM implementations
Professional statisticians and mathematicians have formally stated that VAM scores are too unreliable for tenure decisions
We shouldn't trust 'objective mathematical models' when we can't even agree on a definition of teaching success
money_quotes: Saying that the current VAM works because a retrograded VAM worked in 1989 and the 1990's is like saying I must like blueberry pie now because I used to like pumpkin pie.
There's nothing like telling teachers they might lose their job to create a mighty strong feedback loop.
I've never said the VAM scores are totally meaningless, but just that they are not precise enough to hold individual teachers accountable.
The lack of such studies reminds me of how we never hear about scientific studies on the results of Weight Watchers. There's a reason such studies never see the light of day.
We should never trust a so-called 'objective mathematical model' when we can't even decide on a definition of success.
I just don't think we should punish teachers arbitrarily to have that conversation.
It's an all-out war of supposed mathematical objectivity against the civil servant.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical models used for high-stakes decisions like teacher tenure are fundamentally untrustworthy when they rely on outdated data from a different era and cannot even be validated against current qualitative measures of success.

posts.summary: The post argues that positive correlation between Value-Added Model (VAM) teacher scores and other measures is insufficient justification for using VAM in high-stakes decisions like firing teachers. O'Neil distinguishes between contexts where correlation is acceptable (high-frequency trading) versus where near-equality is required (safety-critical decisions, career-ending assessments). She reveals that VAM scores for the same teacher teaching the same subject in the same year are only 24% correlated with each other, fundamentally undermining the model's reliability.
key_points: Positive correlation does not imply equality, and the distinction matters enormously depending on stakes
Mathematical models meant to replace expensive human judgment must be validated against that judgment, not just shown to be correlated with it
The standard for teacher evaluation should be high because jobs are on the line—the 'carnival ride' standard, not the 'trader making thousands of bets' standard
VAM scores are only 24% correlated with themselves when comparing the same teacher's scores across two classes in the same subject and year
Teachers and unions should demand scoring systems that are consistently equivalent to understood systems, not merely positively correlated
money_quotes: Positive correlation does not imply equality.
If I told you that there's a ride at a carnival that has a positive correlation with not killing children, that wouldn't be good enough. You'd want the ride to be safe. It's a higher standard.
Instead we have a machine that nobody understands that is positively correlated with things we do understand. I claim that's not sufficient.

Say your 'true value' as a teacher is 58 but the VAM gave you a zero. That would not just be frustrating to you, since it's taken as an important part of your assessment. You might even lose your job.
If you're a teacher, you should demand a scoring system that is consistently the same as a system you understand rather than positively correlated with one.
That makes it even more clear that the VAM is an insufficient tool, because it is only 24% correlated with itself.
posts.key_insight: When mathematical models are used for high-stakes individual decisions like firing teachers, positive correlation with ground truth is not good enough—the model must reliably reproduce the judgments it claims to replace.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines a debate between Marc Andreessen and Alex Payne about whether technology democratizes wealth or concentrates it. Andreessen argues smartphones give everyone access to the means of production, while Payne counters that owning a device isn't the same as owning the platforms and patents that generate wealth. O'Neil frames this as the central unresolved question of technological utopianism: who owns the robots, and how will wealth be distributed? She criticizes Andreessen for failing to acknowledge this logical gap, attributing his blind spot to his position as a beneficiary of the current system.
key_points: Owning a smartphone is fundamentally different from owning the factories, patents, and platforms that create wealth
The essential question Andreessen avoids is 'who owns the robots?' - the distribution problem of automated wealth
O'Neil identifies a 'singularity of moral enlightenment' as the unexplained mechanism by which technological abundance becomes shared prosperity
Andreessen's failure to see his argument's flaws stems from his position as someone who benefits from the current tech power structure
money_quotes: Owning a smartphone is not the equivalent of owning a factory.
What you left out was the essential question: who owns the robots?
Who owns the robots and when are they going to learn to share?
It's one thing to lack an explanation for the SME, and to consider it an aspirational quasi-religious utopian goal, but it's another thing entirely to fail to acknowledge it.
It's yet another case of someone thinking 'this system works for me therefore it is super awesome for everyone and everything, amen.'
posts.key_insight: Technological optimism about democratized access conveniently ignores the question of ownership—those who control the platforms assume universal benefit because they personally reap the rewards.

posts.summary: This is a brief link post directing readers to Scott Aaronson's essay about using PageRank-like algorithms to understand human morality and decision making. O'Neil endorses the linked post as funny, clever, thoughtful, and substantial. The post itself contains no original analysis, serving purely as a recommendation to her audience.
key_points: Recommends Scott Aaronson's post on algorithmic approaches to understanding morality
Suggests the intersection of network algorithms (like PageRank) and human ethics is worth exploring
Demonstrates O'Neil's practice of curating and sharing relevant technical-philosophical content
money_quotes: The post is funny, clever, very thoughtful, and pretty long.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most valuable contribution is pointing readers toward someone else's deep thinking on algorithms and morality.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her plans to attend and volunteer at the Clearwater Festival, a folk music gathering in Croton-on-Hudson. The post is a personal, lighthearted account of her preparations for camping and kitchen volunteer duties. She notes that this year's festival will be a tribute to Pete Seeger, who founded it and passed away earlier that year. She mentions musicians she's excited to see and lists her eclectic packing items including instruments and 'bluegrass juice.'
key_points: The Clearwater Festival was Pete Seeger's creation and this year serves as a tribute following his death
O'Neil is volunteering in the kitchen alongside 1,000 other volunteers
She's bringing her fiddle and banjo, suggesting active participation in the folk music tradition
The post reveals her love of bluegrass and folk music culture outside her data science work
money_quotes: If you've never done that, let me tell you that there's something gross and creepy about wet pillows.
I love doing dishes for some reason.

Stuff I've packed for the trip: tent, sleeping bag, pillow (dry so far), bluegrass juice (of the Jack Daniels variety), my fiddle, my banjo, a wooden bowl and utensils, and some metal coffee cups and shot glasses.
