You should totally come by for either day or for the whole weekend if you're nearby and in the mood for some really old hippy reminiscences! And really, who isn't.
posts.key_insight: This is a personal lifestyle post about attending a folk music festival, revealing O'Neil's life outside data science but containing no analytical or thematic content related to her usual topics.

posts.summary: A guest post by Nathan, a mathematician preparing to teach college calculus, grapples with fundamental questions about the purpose of the course. He identifies three possible goals: teaching technical skills for other subjects, building intuitive understanding of mathematical concepts, and practicing precise communication. However, he finds each goal problematic: technical skills are rarely used again by most students, true intuitive understanding is achieved by fewer than 10% of students, and current assignments aren't designed to teach communication. The post invites readers to help articulate a clear, actionable purpose for calculus education.
key_points: Concrete teaching decisions (calculator policies, required skills, proof-writing) depend on having a clear course goal, but most educators give muddled answers when asked
The 'technical skills for other subjects' justification fails because most students never compute integrals again after the course
Intuitive understanding (like recognizing concavity in real-world applications) is valuable but achieved by fewer than 10% of students under current methods
Teaching precise communication is relevant but would require fundamentally different assignments than currently used
The gap between stated educational goals and realistic outcomes creates genuine angst for conscientious instructors
money_quotes: My question is: what is the goal of a college calculus course?
If I'm listing the pros and cons of permitting calculators, I need some yardstick to measure these pros and cons.
I've asked many people this question, including people I respect enormously for their teaching, but often end up with a muddled answer.
I don't buy number 1, because the bread and butter of calculus class, like computing integrals, isn't something most doctors or scientists will ever do again.
Number 2 is a noble goal, but it's overly idealistic; if this is the goal, then our success rate is less than 10%.
I would love to have a clear and realistic answer to this question.
posts.key_insight: Without a clear, realistic answer to what calculus courses are actually trying to achieve, instructors cannot make coherent decisions about how to teach them.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that users of free services like Google and credit agencies like Experian fundamentally misunderstand their role: they are the product being sold, not the customer. This framing explains why Google finds Europe's 'right to be forgotten' laws unreasonable and why credit agencies can ignore consumer complaints with impunity. She uses the metaphor of cows at a meat farm to illustrate how absurd it seems to companies when their 'products' demand better treatment. The post connects this business model to real harm, showing how Experian's $4.8 billion revenue makes FTC fines of $25.7 million over 14 years essentially meaningless. The power imbalance between billion-dollar companies and individual consumers renders existing consumer protection laws toothless.
key_points: Google's actual customers are advertisers, not users—users provide data that makes advertising more valuable
Anti-trust analysis of Google should focus on advertiser choices, not whether users have alternatives to Gmail or Search
Credit agencies like Experian treat consumers as products, which explains why they ignore the Fair Credit Reporting Act
Regulatory fines are too small relative to company revenues to change behavior—millions in penalties versus billions in revenue
Consumer protection laws lack sufficient enforcement power when the scale mismatch between fines and profits is this extreme
money_quotes: As a gmail and Google Search user, you are not a customer of Google. You are the product.
It'd be like a meat farm where the cows go on strike and demand better food. Cows are the product, and they aren't supposed to complain.
Mere people, whose mortgage application is being denied because of mistakes, are the product, not the customer, and they are almost by definition unimportant.
It's all about the scale of the fines compare to the scale of the business.
Million versus billion. It seems like the cows don't have much leverage.
At worst we treat them better when our customers demand it, not when the cows do.
posts.key_insight: When you are the product rather than the customer, consumer protection laws fail because companies have no economic incentive to treat you well—only paying customers matter.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil introduces the concept of 'big data dark matter' - the vast, hidden ecosystem of algorithmic models that secretly filter and score people without their knowledge. She illustrates this with a teacher screening algorithm being considered by Madison schools and connects it to broader concerns about health data from fitness trackers being exploited in regulatory gray areas. The post argues that these secret models are proliferating rapidly due to the explosion of personal data, and that most people have no idea they're being evaluated by black-box systems. O'Neil calls for crowdsourced documentation of these hidden algorithms.
key_points: A company sells pre-VAM teacher screening algorithms that filter job applicants before humans ever see their applications
Fitness tracker data exists in a regulatory gray area where health privacy laws don't apply, making it ripe for exploitation by employers and insurers
The secrecy of proprietary models combined with their secret deployment means the public has no way to know they're being scored
This dark matter of big data is only occasionally visible through local journalism or insider leaks
The problem isn't new (targeting subprime customers for fees existed decades ago) but has exploded with social media and personal data proliferation
money_quotes: It's just one example of the dark matter of big data.
The vast majority of big data dark matter is still there in the shadows. You can only guess at its existence and its usage.
Since the models themselves are proprietary, and are generally deployed secretly, there's no reason for the public to be informed.
The key is that phrase 'regulatory gray area'; it should make you think 'big data dark matter lives here'.
When you have unprotected data that can be used as a proxy of HIPAA-protected medical data, there's no reason it won't be.
Cold comfort for people who got removed by some random black box algorithm.
Her job was to model which customers to offer subprime credit cards to, and she was specifically told to target those customers who would end up paying the most in fees.
posts.key_insight: There exists a vast shadow ecosystem of secret algorithmic scoring systems that evaluate people without their knowledge, and we can only detect them through accidental disclosures like local budget meetings or insider tips.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil offers irreverent, practical parenting advice that emphasizes long-term thinking over short-term anxiety. She argues that parents should first seriously consider not having kids given the costs, but if hormones win out, they should plan accordingly. Her core philosophy centers on not doing things for children that they need to learn themselves, letting them get bored, and modeling good behavior rather than lecturing. She advocates for low-intervention parenting that prioritizes enjoying your children over anxious micromanagement.
key_points: Consider all reasons not to have kids before having them - they're loud, expensive, and limit your freedom
Long-term thinking should guide all parenting decisions, from sleep training to homework help
Let children get bored and figure things out themselves rather than structuring all their time
Model good behavior instead of lecturing about it, and reward kids for calling out your hypocrisies
Parental guilt is counterproductive - enjoy your children instead of anxiously optimizing
money_quotes: Never take parenting advice from anyone, because the people who are actually eager to give it are usually super weird.
Even if they cry for an hour, it's an investment for a lifetime, namely yours.
If you do their homework for them, they will never do it themselves.
If you structure all their time, they will never figure out what they love to do, so make sure they get bored sometimes.
Don't make them take music lessons. Instead, wait for them to beg for music lessons, and then say no for a while until you're really sure they want them.
Reward them for pointing out your hypocrisies.
Don't feel guilty, you're doing your best. Guilt is a waste of time and gets in the way of enjoying the popcorn.
posts.key_insight: Good parenting is about long-term thinking and benign neglect - let kids struggle, get bored, and figure things out, rather than anxiously optimizing every moment of their lives.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil responds to calls from Google's Larry Page and healthcare systems to open up health data for mining, acknowledging the potential life-saving benefits while warning of serious risks. She notes that while the ACA prevents insurers from discriminating based on pre-existing conditions, no such protections exist for employers. Companies like Walmart could use health predictions and behavioral data to deny jobs to people at risk of future illness. The data broker industry (like Acxiom) operates entirely unregulated, and much of the health-relevant data reflects poverty more than personal choice. She concludes that we should wait for laws to catch up before opening data floodgates, though she admits it's unclear how such laws could even be written.
key_points: Health data mining could save lives, but existing privacy laws exist for important reasons
ACA protections against insurance discrimination don't extend to employment decisions
Data brokers like Acxiom are entirely unregulated and can provide health-relevant information to employers
Health behaviors often reflect poverty and circumstances rather than conscious choices, making data-based discrimination another form of birth lottery stratification
The legal framework hasn't caught up, and it's not even clear what counts as 'medical' data anymore
money_quotes: I think Larry Page is right – we might be able to save lots of lives if we could mine this data which is currently siloed through various privacy laws. On the other hand, there are reasons those privacy laws exist.

A large employer like Walmart might take into account your current health and your current behaviors and possibly even your DNA to predict future behaviors, and they might decide not to give jobs to anyone at risk of diabetes, say.
There are laws that prevent employers from looking into HIPAA-protected health data, but not Acxiom data, which is entirely unregulated.
If we 'opened up all the data' then the laws would be entirely moot. It would be a world where, to get a job, the employer got to see everything about you, including your future health profile.
Lots of the information gleaned from this data – even behaviors – have much more to do with poverty levels than circumstance than with conscious choice.
It's another stratification of society along the lucky/unlucky birth lottery spectrum.
I'm all for saving lives but let's wait for the laws to catch up with the good intentions.
posts.key_insight: Opening up health data for mining would benefit some but devastate others, as unregulated data brokers and employers could use health predictions to further stratify society along existing lines of privilege and poverty.

posts.summary: This is an advice column post where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions about dating, relationships, career transitions, and exam anxiety. The most substantive exchange involves a young man seeking relationship advice, where O'Neil gently but firmly challenges his self-centered framing of his new relationship. She emphasizes that successful relationships require gratitude for one's partner and genuine pleasure in making them happy. Other questions cover compatibility assessments, leaving a PhD program for data science, and managing exam stress.
key_points: Successful relationships require two key elements: enjoying making the other person happy, and feeling grateful that they put up with you
Being too self-absorbed prevents you from developing the emotional foundation needed for lasting relationships
When evaluating career transitions (like PhD to data science), timing matters - acquiring skills before job hunting is strategic
Dating questionnaires reveal interest through participation itself, not just answers - someone filling out a 'ridiculous questionnaire' signals they're interested
Study groups are effective for exam preparation because explaining concepts to others solidifies learning and reveals how much you already know
money_quotes: I spend about half of my time being grateful that my partner puts up with me, which is probably not enough, and that gratitude makes my marriage work better.

If you are too self-absorbed, you will miss the two most crucial elements of successful relationships: first, enjoying making the other person happy and, what is the flip side of the same coin, feeling grateful that the other person will put up with you.

Falling in love is a rush, with dopamine and all that good stuff, so I'm going to guess you aren't actually falling in love. Maybe your positive feelings are just relief that you're no longer alone? That's not the same thing.
It seems like you're prematurely categorizing her as someone you won't take seriously, which I think is strange and self-defeating.
She agreed to fill out this ridiculous questionnaire, which she'd only do if she was interested, and which you'd only ask her to do if you were interested, the vital ingredient.
posts.key_insight: The foundation of successful relationships is not passion or compatibility metrics, but cultivating genuine gratitude for your partner and finding joy in their happiness.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil welcomes the controversial Facebook mood manipulation study as a valuable teaching opportunity, noting it exemplifies the ethical and methodological problems inherent in big data experimentation. She points readers to critical analyses of the study while acknowledging the irony that such experiments happen constantly at tech companies—we just rarely get to see the evidence. Her core observation is that the scandal isn't that Facebook ran this experiment, but that it became public. She worries the backlash will simply drive such research further underground rather than stopping it.
key_points: The Facebook mood study serves as a perfect case study combining ethical dilemmas, flawed methodology, and sociological implications
Experiments like this happen daily at Facebook and Google—the unusual part is public visibility into the data
The likely corporate response to criticism will be to hide future experiments, not to stop conducting them
The study has generated valuable critical analysis worth studying, including pieces on research methods and engineering of public emotion
money_quotes: What's special about this experiment isn't that it happened, but that we get to see the data.
The response to the critiques might be, sadly, that we never get another chance like this, so we have to grab the opportunity while we can.
I know this kind of experiment happens on a daily basis at a place like Facebook or Google.
It's got everything a case study should have: ethical dilemmas, questionable methodology, sociological implications, and questionable claims.
posts.key_insight: The real scandal of the Facebook mood study isn't that tech companies manipulate users experimentally—that's routine—it's that this time we caught a glimpse of it.

posts.summary: O'Neil introduces a course she's teaching called 'The Platform' and recommends danah boyd and Kate Crawford's paper 'Critical Questions for Big Data' as essential reading. She praises the paper's clear articulation of provocations about big data's non-neutrality. The post highlights how platform limitations shape research (Twitter's poor archiving biases researchers toward present events) and how computational scientists often mistake interpretation for neutral fact-finding. She connects the paper to the recent Facebook emotional manipulation experiment as a case study in data ethics.
key_points: Computing tools are never neutral - they carry the biases of their designers and design processes
Platform limitations (like Twitter's poor archiving) shape what research questions get asked, biasing work toward the immediate present
Computational scientists engaging in social science often falsely claim their work deals in facts rather than interpretation
The Facebook emotion experiment exemplifies these concerns about treating algorithmic interventions as neutral scientific inquiry
Journalists covering big data need to understand these critiques even if they don't directly analyze large datasets
money_quotes: Computing tools are the products of human ingenuity and effort. They are never neutral and carry with them the biases of their designers and their design process.

Twitter and Facebook are examples of Big Data sources that offer very poor archiving and search functions. Consequently, researchers are much more likely to focus on something in the present or immediate past.
As computational scientists have started engaging in acts of social science, there is a tendency to claim their work as the business of facts and not interpretation.
A model may be mathematically sound, an experiment may seem valid, but as soon as a researcher seeks to understand what it means, the process of interpretation has begun.
Not all numbers are neutral.
posts.key_insight: Big data tools and platforms are never neutral - they embed their creators' biases and their technical limitations fundamentally shape what questions researchers can even ask.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on a heated podcast debate about whether concerns over health data profiling require hard evidence to be taken seriously. She argues that health scores derived from non-HIPAA-protected data like web searches could soon be used to filter job applicants, but the proprietary nature of this data makes documentation nearly impossible. She draws a parallel to money laundering in real estate, where disclosure requirements are deliberately weak. The post questions whether demanding evidence for systemic harms is reasonable when the systems are designed to avoid creating that evidence.
key_points: The ACA's push to keep populations healthy is creating dangerous side-effects like health profiling scores similar to credit scores
Data used to create health risk scores isn't covered by HIPAA, making its use in employment decisions technically legal
The proprietary nature of data collection makes it nearly impossible to document discriminatory practices
Systems can be designed to avoid collecting evidence of their own harms, as seen in real estate money laundering
Journalism and public discourse must grapple with when anecdotal evidence suffices when data is intentionally obscured
money_quotes: Am I a conspiracy theorist for worrying about this? Or is Felix lacking imagination if he requires evidence to believe it?
It's proprietary all the way down.
They are avoiding collecting evidence of money laundering, so it's hard to complain there's a lack of data.
When do we require data – versus anecdotal evidence – to believe in something? And especially when the data is being intentionally obscured?
Web searches and the like are being legally collected and legally sold and then it's legal to use risk scores or categories to filter job applications.
posts.key_insight: Demanding empirical evidence of systemic harm becomes unreasonable when the systems causing harm are deliberately designed to avoid generating such evidence.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post promoting Zephyr Teachout's upcoming visit to the Alternative Banking group meeting. Teachout, a Fordham Law professor, is running against Andrew Cuomo in the Democratic primary for New York Governor. The post highlights her anti-corruption and trust-busting campaign platform, as well as her background in antitrust law and progressive organizing. O'Neil encourages readers to attend and spread the word about the event.
key_points: Zephyr Teachout is challenging incumbent Andrew Cuomo in the NY Democratic gubernatorial primary
Teachout's campaign focuses on anti-corruption and breaking up big bank power
Her background includes directing internet organizing for Howard Dean's 2004 campaign and co-founding A New Way Forward
The Alternative Banking group meeting will also discuss a Stiglitz article on economic mythology if time permits
money_quotes: Come hear candidate Teachout talk about her anti-corruption trust-busting campaign against Governor Cuomo.
She co-founded A New Way Forward, an organization built to break up the power of big banks.
posts.key_insight: This announcement post signals O'Neil's support for anti-corruption political challengers who take on entrenched financial power, connecting her Alternative Banking activism to electoral politics.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates the closing of Corinthian Colleges, Inc., a for-profit college that gamed the federal student loan system while providing worthless credentials. The company was caught lying about job placement rates (claiming 100% when reality was closer to 0%) and advertising nonexistent programs. Most disturbing was their deliberate targeting of vulnerable populations—low-income, isolated individuals with low self-esteem—using predatory marketing tactics. O'Neil expresses interest in understanding the data-driven methods behind this targeting and suspects data warehousing services played a role.
key_points: For-profit colleges like Corinthian survive almost entirely on federal student loans while delivering terrible services
CCI was caught fabricating job placement statistics and advertising programs that didn't exist
Internal company documents revealed deliberate targeting of vulnerable demographics described as 'isolated,' 'impatient,' with 'low self-esteem'
The predatory advertising included fake program listings designed to harvest potential students' contact information
O'Neil suspects data warehousing services enabled the sophisticated targeting of vulnerable populations
money_quotes: They survive almost entirely on federal student loans of their students, while delivering terrible services and worthless credentials.
In some cases, they said 100% when the truth was closer to 0%.

CCI describes its target demographic as 'isolated,' 'impatient,' individuals with 'low self-esteem,' who have 'few people in their lives who care about them' and who are 'stuck' and 'unable to see and plan well for future.'
I'm guessing it was substantially online, and I'm guessing they got help from data warehousing services.
Talk about predatory advertising!
posts.key_insight: For-profit colleges weaponize data-driven marketing to systematically target society's most vulnerable people, extracting federal loan money while delivering nothing of value.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reports on the successful completion of the first half of the Lede Program, a data journalism training initiative. The students presented impressive projects and learned valuable lessons about data work, particularly around data cleaning and drilling down to find stories. The second half will involve more advanced topics including geocoding, algorithms, and prerequisite math concepts. O'Neil is enthusiastic about the students' progress and their readiness for more complex data journalism projects.
key_points: Students completed their first data projects and presented them successfully
Key learning: data cleaning takes most of the time, and finding the story requires drilling down into data
Journalists are motivated by deadlines and portfolio-building opportunities
The program will cover algorithms, geocoding, and math prerequisites like linear algebra and statistics
O'Neil is using accessible statistics books including 'How to Lie with Statistics' and 'Statistics Done Wrong'
money_quotes: I spent most of my time cleaning my data
Next time I will spend more time thinking about how to drill down in my data to find an interesting story
These are key phrases for people learning lessons with data
Since they are journalists they love projects because they love deadlines and they want something they can add to their portfolio
They'll be well equipped to do some seriously cool shit for their final project
posts.key_insight: Learning to work with data means discovering that most of your time goes to cleaning it and that finding a compelling story requires thoughtful exploration rather than surface-level analysis.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses four reader questions spanning health disclosure in dating, navigating a friend's accidental outing, parameter optimization in computing, and whether women are obligated to fight sexism in male-dominated fields. The author balances pragmatic advice with personal candor, often distinguishing between 'good advice' and what she would actually do. The most substantial response tackles workplace sexism, arguing there is no obligation to be a role model for a miserable position. She reframes competitive behavior as systemic rather than personal, advising women to stay internally focused on their own goals rather than getting distracted by external slights.
key_points: Private health information has no disclosure obligation on early dates, but serious relationships require transparency
Competitive and sexist behavior in academia and business is a product of systems built around status and money
There is no obligation for women to 'stand and fight' in hostile fields just to serve as role models
Taking workplace sexism personally is a distraction from pursuing your actual goals
The author distinguishes between 'good advice' (stay out of it) and what she'd actually do (tell her friend)
money_quotes: There is absolutely no obligation at all to stand and fight, by any woman or man, whatsoever.
It's a silly argument that one should role model for a position that's miserable. It's almost ludicrously bait and switch, in fact.
I exist as such a threat against other people (both men and women), that I recognize sexist pushback almost as a sport.
Don't be surprised that, as you get closer to the goal, people will be more threatened, not less, and they will embarrass themselves with bad behavior.
It's not about some vague obligation to society. It's about a very real obligation towards yourself, which you set.
Staying external is always a mistake, just look at the dieting industry.
As for brute force optimization, yes go ahead but remember to have a clean data set to test your parameters on, because you're surely overfitting.
posts.key_insight: Women in hostile professional environments owe nothing to abstract societal obligations and should focus on their own goals rather than martyring themselves as reluctant role models.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines a partnership between NYC's Mayor's Office of Data Analytics and private startup Placemeter, which uses cameras (including those installed by private citizens) to count and categorize pedestrians. She highlights a troubling contradiction: Placemeter claims to delete footage after counting, yet also collects detailed categorical data like gender, age, and shopping behavior. The post raises concerns about mission creep, potential expansion to race and facial recognition, and the lack of transparency when surveillance is conducted by private companies outside Freedom of Information laws.
key_points: NYC government is partnering with a private startup to conduct surveillance using cameras installed in citizens' windows
There's a contradiction between claiming to delete footage and simultaneously collecting detailed categorical data about individuals
Private company status may shield surveillance methodologies from public transparency via FOIL requests
The scope of categorization could expand beyond stated uses to include race, weight, and facial recognition
money_quotes: Personally, it disturbs me, but perhaps because of my priors on how much we can trust other people with our data, especially when it's in private hands.

On the one hand they try to make us feel safe by saying that, after gleaning a body count with their video tapes, they dump the data. But then they turn around and say that, in addition to counting people, they will also categorize people: gender, age, whether they are carrying a shopping bag or pushing strollers.
That's what they are talking about anyway, but who knows what else? Race? Weight? Will they use face recognition software?
At some point, after mining videos enough, it might not matter if they delete the footage afterwards.
Since they are a private company I don't think such information on their data methodologies will be accessible to us via Freedom of Information Laws either.
posts.key_insight: When government partners with private companies for surveillance, the combination of categorical data collection and exemption from transparency laws creates accountability gaps that should concern citizens regardless of promises to delete raw footage.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the launch of her data platform class, sharing educational resources she's prepared. She provides links to the course syllabus on GitHub and an iPython notebook demonstrating basic statistics in Python. The notebook uses the controversial Facebook Emotion study as a case study for understanding statistical significance. She encourages readers to follow along by installing Anaconda and promises future blog posts about guest lecturers.
key_points: New data platform class launching with open syllabus available on GitHub
Teaching materials include iPython notebook for hands-on learning of basic statistics
Uses Facebook Emotion study as real-world example of statistically significant but tiny differences
Course will feature guest lecturers whose talks may be blogged about
money_quotes: I prepared an iPython notebook showing how to do basic statistics in python, and culminating in an attempt to understand what a statistically significant but tiny difference means, in the context of the Facebook Emotion study.
posts.key_insight: Understanding the difference between statistical significance and practical significance is crucial, especially when analyzing controversial studies like Facebook's emotion manipulation experiment.

posts.summary: O'Neil highlights two articles exposing deep flaws in standardized testing: one showing how poor schools can't compete because tests are tied to expensive textbooks (with Pearson caught using verbatim textbook passages on tests), and another examining how Atlanta's high-stakes testing culture predictably led to widespread cheating. She argues that the system creates two tiers of 'cheating'—wealthy schools buying their way to success through test-prep materials, and desperate teachers changing answers—while the testing regime functions more as punishment than education. She notes Christie's decision to reduce test score weight in teacher evaluations as a small positive development.
key_points: Standardized tests are designed to favor schools that can afford expensive textbooks from the same companies that create the tests
Pearson was caught using passages from their own textbooks verbatim on standardized tests, creating a pay-to-win system
Atlanta's cheating scandal was a predictable consequence of high-stakes 'data-driven' education policy
Data was weaponized to 'embarrass and punish' rather than to help students and teachers
There's a class-based double standard: buying expensive test prep is acceptable 'cheating' while changing answers is criminal
money_quotes: Pearson came under fire last year for using a passage on a standardized test that was taken verbatim from a Pearson textbook.
A 'culture of fear, intimidation and retaliation has infested the district, allowing cheating—at all levels—to go unchecked for years.'
Data had been 'used as an abusive and cruel weapon to embarrass and punish.'

There's an acceptable way to cheat, which is by stocking up on expensive test prep materials in the form of testing company-sponsored textbooks, and then there's the unacceptable way to cheat, which is where teachers change the answers.
Either way the standardized test scoring regime comes out looking like a penal system rather than a helpful teaching aid.
posts.key_insight: Standardized testing creates a rigged system where wealth buys 'legitimate' advantages while punishing those who can't afford to play by the rules designed to benefit test-makers' profits.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the transformation of work from agricultural to manufacturing to what she calls 'the age of service,' questioning how this economic transition will function. She criticizes the celebration of improving jobs reports while ignoring that new jobs are often temporary, part-time, and lower-paying than the full-time jobs being lost. She proposes that the service economy operates like a pyramid scheme where the wealthy employ servants who then employ slightly less wealthy servants, requiring either extreme wealth concentration or alternative wealth transfers. The post questions whether economists have adequately explained this transition and wonders how care workers at the bottom of the pyramid will themselves be cared for in old age.
key_points: Monthly jobs reports mask the reality that new jobs are often temporary/part-time with lower wages than the full-time jobs being lost
The economy is transitioning from 'the age of stuff' (manufacturing) to 'the age of service' following the earlier shift from agriculture
The service economy functions as a pyramid scheme requiring either extreme wealth at the top or systematic wealth redistribution
Examples like concierge medicine and personal assistants for social calendars show this service economy already emerging
Most modern work including programming, writing, and teaching already qualifies as service industry work
money_quotes: The age of service is a kind of pyramid scheme where rich people employ individuals to service them in various ways, and then those people are paid well so they can hire slightly less rich people to service them, and so on.
I kind of want to complain at this point that economists are kind of useless when it comes to questions like this.
I feel like, when it comes to social meetings, if you have time to be social, you have time to arrange your social calendar.
The mathematician in me can't help but wonder, who will take care of the old people who used to be taking care of the old people?
Hell, the future could be pretty awesome if we arrange things well.
For this particular pyramid to work out, the rich have to be SUPER rich and they have to pay their servants very well indeed for the trickle down to work out.
posts.key_insight: The transition to a service economy creates a pyramid structure that only functions with extreme wealth concentration at the top or deliberate wealth redistribution—neither of which current economic systems are designed to handle.

posts.summary: This is a brief personal update about the author's visit to HCSSiM (Hampshire College Summer Studies in Mathematics) for Yellow Pig Day celebrations. She describes helping teach a workshop on Euler characteristics of planar graphs and counting Platonic solids using stereographic projection. The post captures a moment of mathematical community and education, mentioning plans to play Nim with students later.
key_points: Author visited HCSSiM for Yellow Pig Day, a quirky math camp tradition
Helped teach workshop on Euler characteristic and Platonic solids using stereographic projection
Successfully covered duals in addition to planned material
Plans to play mathematical games (Nim) with students at evening activity
money_quotes: I can never remember which one is the icosahedron.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical community and playful learning thrive at summer programs where even experts admit to forgetting which solid is which.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil defends her habit of assuming everyone has a crush on her, explaining it as a reciprocal and non-sexual appreciation for others. She argues this mindset creates positive feedback loops in social interactions, making people more likely to actually develop fondness for you. The post playfully reframes what others see as preposterous self-confidence as a practical social strategy. Her core argument is that assuming positive regard from others costs nothing and creates a more enjoyable world to inhabit.
key_points: Having a 'crush' in this context means finding someone super awesome, not romantic or sexual attraction
Acting like someone has a crush on you creates a positive feedback loop that makes it more likely to become true
The worst-case scenario is just that someone thinks you were weirdly happy to see them
The assumption is reciprocal - she also has crushes on everyone else
This mindset transforms everyday social interactions into more pleasant experiences
money_quotes: First of all, I also have a crush on everyone else.
It's more fun that way!
No categories left uncrushed except meanies.
When you act like someone has a crush on you, they are more likely to develop a crush on you.
It's what I call a positive feedback loop.
It's a nice world to live in where a majority of the people you run into have a crush on you.
Try it and see for yourself!
posts.key_insight: Assuming positive regard from others is a self-fulfilling prophecy that costs nothing and makes social life more enjoyable.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines how the $7 billion Citigroup settlement was determined, contrasting the DOJ's 'lawyerly' approach (based on incriminating emails) with Matt Levine's proposed 'quantitative' approach (based on actual damages caused). She argues both methods have flaws: the lawyerly approach just teaches people to avoid paper trails, while a predictable quantitative formula could be gamed by incorporating expected fines into trading models. Surprisingly, she concludes that arbitrary-seeming punishments may actually be better deterrents because their unpredictability prevents optimization around them. The post reflects her broader concern that financial crimes result in fines rather than jail time.
key_points: The DOJ's approach punishes 'culpable-looking' behavior (bad emails) rather than actual damage caused
A purely quantitative punishment formula could be incorporated into trading models as just another cost of doing business
Randomness in punishment amounts may be a feature, not a bug, because it prevents gaming the system
The lawyerly approach primarily teaches people to never put incriminating things in writing
Since these settlements result in fines rather than jail time, the deterrent effect is questionable regardless of methodology
money_quotes: With the lawyerly approach, you are basically just sending the message that you should never ever write some things on email, and most or at least many people know that by now.
In other words, you are training people to game the system, and if they game it well enough, they won't get in trouble.

If I'm a quant at a nasty place that wants to trade in toxic securities, and I know that there's a chance I'd be caught but I know the formula for how much I'd have to pay if I got caught, then I could include this cost in an optimization engine to determine exactly how many toxic securities I should sell.
To avoid this scenario, it makes sense to have an element of randomness in the punishments for getting caught.
Maybe what I'm saying is that arriving at a random number, like the DOJ did, is probably better even though it is less satisfying.
Given that this was yet another fine and nobody went to jail, you could make the argument that nobody got in trouble anyway.
posts.key_insight: Predictable punishment formulas for financial crimes can be gamed by incorporating expected fines into profit optimization models, making seemingly arbitrary penalties potentially more effective deterrents.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts a guest lecture by economist Suresh Naidu explaining the core principles of applied microeconomics: people respond to incentives, supply and demand explain data, and causality matters more than prediction. She notes that economists have been largely unmoved by the big data revolution because big data focuses on description and prediction rather than causality. The post then applies this economic lens to a debate about overseas child labor, showing how economists approach problems by seeking tweakable variables to improve systems. O'Neil concludes with a nuanced acknowledgment that while belief in economic data varies, randomized experiments remain the best way to learn about the world.
key_points: Applied microeconomics rests on three simple metaphors: incentives, supply/demand, and prioritizing causality over prediction
Big data has left microeconomists unmoved because it focuses on description and prediction rather than the causal relationships economists care about
Economists approach problems by trying to identify tweakable variables that can improve systems, as illustrated by differing solutions to child labor
There's a spectrum of how much people 'believe' in economics and supporting data, even among economists themselves
Despite uncertainty, randomized experiments remain the best available method for learning about the world
money_quotes: Causality is more important than prediction.

The real reason they're unmoved, as I infer from his talk yesterday, is that big data almost always focuses on descriptions of human behavior, and sometimes predictions, and almost never causality, which is what economists care about.
A side question: why is it that economists only care about causality? Well they do, and let's take that as a given.
So the economists don't agree, but they all share one goal: to figure out how to tweak a tweakable variable to improve a system.
There's a spectrum of how much people 'believe' in economics, and for that matter believe in data that seems to support a theory or experiment.
It's not clear there's a better way to learn things about the world than doing your best to run randomized experiments.
posts.key_insight: Big data's focus on prediction misses what economists actually care about—understanding causality to identify interventions that can improve systems.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts a day of teaching about secrets and cryptography, featuring a guest lecture on declassified documents and her own lesson on RSA encryption. Historian Matthew Connelly discussed how digitization has made it easier both to delete archival records and to trace leaks, potentially explaining the Obama administration's harsh treatment of whistleblowers. O'Neil then taught her students about prime numbers and RSA cryptography, creating an iPython Notebook that demonstrates encoding and decoding a simple message. The post reflects on how technology shapes both information preservation and surveillance.
key_points: Digitization makes it easy to permanently delete large quantities of archival records
Digital records also make it easy to trace access and identify leakers
Obama's harsh approach to whistleblowers may be explained as 'letting the system work' - technology enabling surveillance
O'Neil created an educational iPython Notebook demonstrating RSA encryption with a complete encoding/decoding example
money_quotes: As records have become digitized, it has gotten easy for people to get rid of archival records in large quantities. Just press delete.
As records have become digitized, it has become easy to trace the access of records, and in particular the leaks.
Connelly explained that, to some extent, Obama's harsh approach to leakers and whistleblowers might be explained as simply 'letting the system work.'
Yet another way that technology informs the way we approach human interactions.
posts.key_insight: Digitization is a double-edged sword for transparency: it makes records easier to destroy while simultaneously making those who access them easier to surveil.

posts.summary: In this advice column, Aunt Pythia addresses four reader questions covering sexism at academic conferences, dating app culture, career anxiety in data science, and workplace dynamics for female faculty. She provides practical, direct advice on being an active bystander against harassment, defends her critique of treating dating like an optimization problem, reassures a statistician that Kaggle competitions miss the real skills employers need, and coaches a female professor on navigating subtle sexism from support staff. The column blends humor with substantive guidance on navigating gendered professional challenges.
key_points: Active bystanders should escalate intervention progressively: talk to the harasser, check in with the target, and involve organizers if needed
Kaggle competitions are poor proxies for real data science because they pre-define problems and success metrics—the actual job is figuring out what questions to ask
Workplace sexism often manifests in small, deniable interactions that accumulate over time
Loving one or two people per day through speed-dating is emotionally impossible, distinct from casual sex which she supports
Female academics should create 'small but useful confrontation' rather than passively accepting gendered treatment
money_quotes: As soon as someone responds with a 'not interested' signal, it is of course the responsibility of the interested party to tone it down.
Make him try to think about her perspective rather than his own dick's perspective.
Hiring people who are good at Kaggle competitions is like hiring the chef who has been supplied with a menu, a bunch of recipes, and all the ingredients to run your new restaurant.
It's not actually all that impressive to be able to follow directions, you need to be creative and thoughtful.
I don't think loving one or two people per day is possible.
I don't know why you are 'rigorously avoiding confrontation' here.
Do not let this stuff build up inside you! Make it an amusing part of your day to see how people wiggle out of their responsibilities.
posts.key_insight: Being a good ally against workplace sexism requires escalating action rather than passive sympathy, while real professional competence lies in asking the right questions rather than optimizing pre-defined metrics.

posts.summary: O'Neil proposes that smartphone apps and video games have fundamentally altered social behavior by keeping people, especially young men, indoors and disengaged from both street life and civic participation. She suggests this 'app effect' may partially explain declining crime rates alongside other factors like abortion access and reduced lead exposure. The post argues that digital entertainment functions as a 'pacifier for grown-ups' that keeps populations docile and distracted. While acknowledging some benefits, O'Neil worries that we've trained people to retreat into pointless activities that serve mainly to distract and collect marketing data.
key_points: Crime rates may have fallen partly because potential troublemakers are now gaming indoors rather than congregating on hot summer streets
Political engagement has declined as people choose Candy Crush over newspapers and civic participation
Digital devices pacify people in situations that would previously spark social interaction or collective action
The distraction comes with a hidden cost: data collection for marketing purposes
The thought experiment of apps disappearing reveals our dependency on digital pacification
money_quotes: I have a theory which I'm slightly embarrassed about but whatever, that's what blogs are for

When I was growing up in Boston in the 1980's, you'd just see way more people out on the streets on summer evenings because it was too freaking hot to do anything inside and people were damn bored. That's when the trouble would start.
People are less politically engaged. Partly it's because Congress sucks, but partly – yes – it's because people are playing Candy Crush!
It is a pacifier for grown-ups, a nationwide babysitting service that keeps people in line.

We have trained an entire population to take refuge in a pointless activity that doesn't serve any real purpose except to distract us and to mollify us, not to mention collect our data for later marketing purposes.
If all the apps and all the video games stopped working for a few weeks, what would happen? What would people do with themselves?
posts.key_insight: Smartphones and gaming have become a mass pacification tool that reduces both crime and civic engagement by giving people an endless escape from boredom and each other.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares an article by former Teach for America teacher Allie Gross that critiques the charter school movement. The piece argues that charter schools lack democratic accountability since their boards are appointed rather than elected, opening doors for corruption and opportunism. The article highlights how for-profit management companies exploit public education dollars while hiding behind rhetoric about helping poor students. O'Neil endorses Gross's evidence-based critique and recommends the broader Jacobin series on education reform.
key_points: Charter school boards are appointed, not elected, creating a fundamental democratic accountability gap
For-profit management companies are profiting from public education dollars, particularly in states like Michigan with lax oversight
The 'education marketplace' approach doesn't weed out corruption—it can actually make socioeconomic problems worse
Failed tech entrepreneurs face few consequences, but failed education ventures harm children and communities
Reform rhetoric about helping poor students provides cover for opportunism and mismanagement
money_quotes: You haven't heard about the failed education ventures of the person now running your district? Probably a bigger deal.

When we welcome schools that lack democratic accountability (charter school boards are appointed, not elected), when we allow public dollars to be used by those with a bottom line, we open doors for opportunism and corruption.
Even worse, it's all justified under a banner of concern for poor public school students' well-being.
Letting parents pick from a variety of schools does not weed out corruption.
The lax laws and lack of accountability can actually exacerbate the socioeconomic ills we're trying to root out.
posts.key_insight: Market-based education reform removes democratic accountability while allowing profit motives to corrupt a system ostensibly designed to help disadvantaged students.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil enthusiastically reviews the Head First Java book, praising its unconventional teaching approach with quirky cartoons and engaging style. She notes she's reading it to understand object-oriented programming and bridge to courses her students will take. While impressed by the book's ability to make technical content a 'thrilling read,' she criticizes its sexist references to women in tight sweaters as programmer motivation. She also mentions Head First Design Patterns and declares she'll never write technical books in the 'old-fashioned dry way' again.
key_points: Head First books use quirky cartoons and humor to make technical content engaging and memorable
The book explicitly uses emotional attachment to help readers remember technical details
The series contains sexist elements, using 'women in tight sweaters' as assumed programmer motivation
Programming is just a toolset - learning to think about constructing with those tools (design patterns) is the real goal
Traditional dry technical writing may be inferior to more engaging approaches
money_quotes: I'm on page 213 after less than a week, and yes that's out of more than 600 pages but what I'm saying is that it's a thrilling read.
My one complaint is how often the book talks about motivating programmers with women in tight sweaters.

At the beginning they made the point that people remember stuff better when there is emotional attachment to things, so I'm guessing they're getting me annoyed to help me remember details on reference types.
Ultimately, programming is just a tool set and you need to learn how to think about constructing stuff with those tools.
Honestly I will never write a technical book in the old-fashioned dry way again.
posts.key_insight: Technical education can be dramatically more effective when it's engaging and emotionally resonant, though the tech industry's assumptions about who programmers are often leak into even progressive educational materials.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her personal experience with anxiety-induced insomnia, noting that while she used to suffer from depression, having children shifted her struggles toward anxiety. She describes her inability to fantasize or let go of concrete problems, leading to sleepless nights once or twice a week. Rather than offering a cure, she provides practical coping mechanisms including accepting worry without meta-worry, listening to boring radio or baseball games, using a pillow speaker to avoid disturbing her husband, and watching intentionally dull Netflix content. She enthusiastically recommends a PBS documentary about deer as the ultimate sleep aid.
key_points: The transition from depression to anxiety after having children changed the nature of her mental health struggles
Her analytical mind that focuses on concrete problems makes traditional relaxation techniques like fantasy visualization ineffective
Accepting sleeplessness without added worry about being worried is a key coping strategy
Boring content (sports radio, baseball games, nature documentaries) serves as an effective distraction from anxiety
Practical accommodations like pillow speakers can help manage anxiety without disrupting partners
money_quotes: But ever since I had kids, I suffer much more from anxiety.
I basically have no imagination and spend my brain cycles trying to solve really concrete problems, and if there's something out of my control then it bugs me and I have trouble letting go.
First of all, don't worry about being worried.
Chances are the next night you will be super exhausted and catch up on sleep, no harm done.
Sports radio is almost always soothingly boring.
Of course an actual baseball game is perfect, because nothing ever happens.
posts.key_insight: For analytical minds that can't simply 'let go,' the best sleep strategy isn't fighting anxiety but redirecting attention toward sufficiently boring content.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses four reader questions: how to handle discovering academic plagiarism, dealing with feelings of inadequacy compared to successful peers, navigating a relationship with an unkind partner, and coping with romantic rejection. Aunt Pythia advises reporting plagiarism to journal editors rather than confronting the plagiarizer directly. For the struggling student, she offers 'cold comfort' that prestigious internships are overrated and math skills outlast popularity. On relationships, she distinguishes between partners who are merely grumpy versus those who are genuinely unkind, advising to leave the latter. For dating woes, she recommends the 'wing woman' strategy and maintaining multiple crushes to protect one's ego.
key_points: Institutional channels (journal editors) are the appropriate route for addressing academic misconduct, not personal confrontation or public shaming
Prestigious internships often involve subservient worship of powerful people and aren't as valuable as finding meaningful independent work
Being an outsider teaches empathy and understanding of social stratification - wisdom to carry forward when you eventually succeed
In relationships, you don't need a partner to be 'nice' - you need them to be trustworthy and on your team; genuine unkindness is a dealbreaker
Dating success requires community support (wing women) and emotional diversification (multiple crushes) to protect self-worth from individual rejections
money_quotes: Fuck that, never be worshipful of anyone, especially just because they're powerful or successful. Gross.
Math skillz are sexier and more valuable than you now know. Plus they last longer than prettiness and popularity.

Being on the outside helps you understand the construct of social stratification and the pain of being excluded. Remember this for later when you are one of the cool kids, so you will always have empathy for outsiders.
You don't need your boyfriend to be 'nice'. You just need to trust him.
If he is truly unkind to you, then leave him. He's not on your team. No kidding.
At all times maintain a rotation of crushes on a bunch of men, or else the ego crashing at low moments will overwhelm.
Go find something meaningful to do with yourself, don't depend on other people's organizational skills, because they will only send you to artificially constructed or corporate environments.
posts.key_insight: Success and self-worth shouldn't be measured by conventional status markers; authentic engagement with meaningful work and honest relationships matters more than prestigious positions or being 'nice.'

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that smart, feminist women often abandon their critical thinking skills when they become pregnant, deferring excessively to doctors on matters they're perfectly capable of deciding themselves. She attributes this to pregnancy hormones, high stakes, and fear-inducing pregnancy literature. The post pushes back against medical paternalism in pregnancy care, noting that evidence for many prescriptive guidelines is weak. She concludes that women should apply the same skepticism to pregnancy advice that they would to any other paternalistic nonsense, remembering that their own wellbeing matters alongside their baby's.
key_points: Pregnancy shouldn't mean surrendering decades of competent self-care to doctors who dictate eating and pain management
Much pregnancy advice lacks solid evidence but gets treated as medical gospel
Childbirth is safer than ever, making fear-based compliance less justified
Medical paternalism doesn't stop being paternalism when the doctor is a woman
Maternal wellbeing matters too—happy mothers make for fine babies
money_quotes: And then they get pregnant and the lose most if not all of those properties.
They get doctors who tell them what to eat, and how much, even though they've been doing quite well feeding themselves for 30 odd years without help.
Plus there's all those ridiculous and scary pregnancy books out there which just serve to make women neurotic and should be burned.
But here's the thing, there's not much evidence about exactly how you should eat when you're pregnant, unless you are doing something absolutely weird.
So if you have one of those pushy-ass doctors, fuck 'em. This is your body, you get to decide that stuff.
For goodness sakes don't forget your feminist training.
It's not just your baby here, it's also you, and yes you deserve to eat food you want to eat and to moderate pain if it gets overwhelming.
posts.key_insight: Pregnancy doesn't revoke your feminist credentials or your right to question medical authority about your own body.

posts.summary: The post explains how McDonald's franchise model is fundamentally a legal risk management strategy that shifts liability for worker treatment onto individual franchise owners while the corporation maintains control over virtually every aspect of operations. McDonald's uses surveillance systems to monitor revenue-to-labor ratios and 'encourages' franchise owners to cut worker hours, while contractual arrangements ensure worker complaints can't reach the parent company. The franchise owners are squeezed between corporate demands and worker needs, with labor costs being the only variable they can control. A recent NLRB ruling could allow workers to name McDonald's as a joint employer, potentially enabling unionization or forcing better franchise contracts.
key_points: 90% of McDonald's locations are franchises, designed to insulate the corporation from legal liability while maintaining operational control
McDonald's installs real-time surveillance tracking revenue-to-pay ratios and 'encourages' owners to cut hours when profitability dips
Franchise owners control only one variable—worker pay—but reducing it is the only way to stay profitable under McDonald's contractual demands
The franchise model transformed McDonald's from a burger company into a brand management and risk containment operation
The NLRB ruling could allow workers to hold McDonald's directly accountable as a joint employer
money_quotes: In order to contain your risks, you franchise.
Restaurants aren't the only business to learn that it's easier to sell and manage a brand than it is to sell and manage an actual product.

McDonalds essentially controls everything except one variable, but since it's a closed system of equations, that means the franchise owners have to decide between paying their workers reasonably and going in the red.
When did McDonalds stop being a burger joint?
It's a way to box in the legal risk of the parent company.
Their pesky legal issues are kept away from you.
Encourage, mind you, not require. They are not the employers or anything remotely like that, clearly.
posts.key_insight: The franchise model is a mathematical sleight of hand that lets corporations control everything that matters while legally disclaiming responsibility for the workers who generate their profits.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her experience launching a data journalism program at Columbia, identifying fundamental cultural clashes between computer science and journalism that make the field difficult to teach. Computer science emphasizes efficiency, narrow problem-solving, and getting code to work fast, while journalism prioritizes broad context-gathering, understanding ambiguity, and synthesizing information into compelling narratives. She argues that data journalism requires a middle ground that combines programming power with deep awareness of human bias in data collection. The piece concludes that success depends not just on academic programs but on media organizations actually valuing and hiring thoughtfully trained data journalists.
key_points: Academia's departmental silos create unintentional hostility toward interdisciplinary fields like data journalism
Computer science training rewards narrow, efficient problem-solving without questioning context or design choices
Journalism training emphasizes breadth, context, and awareness of knowledge gaps before drawing conclusions
Computer scientists can be 'blithe with respect to messy human details' and miss selection bias because their programs technically work
The ideal data journalism training would combine programming power with deep investigation of human bias in data
money_quotes: In cartoonish form, introductory computer science classes are competitive weeder classes that promote a certain kind of narrow, clever, problem-solving approach.
It's kind of the least context-laden and most content-heavy way of learning that you can imagine.
The context is what makes a topic interesting and important and newsworthy.
Journalists are ever aware of the things they know they don't know, and desperately want to fill in their knowledge gaps because otherwise they feel fraudulent.
Computer scientists don't care about not knowing things as long as their programs work.
They can be blithe with respect to messy human details, which of course means they sometimes don't notice or figure out their data has selection bias because they got an answer.
Both sides emphasize a kind of learning and a definition of success that the other side is blind to.
The outside world has to actually want to hire the emerging thoughtful fruit of that labor.
posts.key_insight: Data journalism struggles because computer science rewards efficiency without context while journalism rewards context without efficiency, and bridging this gap requires both academic investment and industry demand for thoughtfully trained practitioners.

posts.summary: This is an advice column post where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on topics ranging from why she loves living in New York City despite its crowds and expense, to handling unresponsive academic collaborators. The post opens with playful bragging about her crepe-making skills before diving into reader mail. The meatiest content involves her passionate defense of urban life—embracing crowds, diverse subway encounters, and even appreciating rudeness as a form of honest human interaction. She also provides pragmatic advice to an academic dealing with collaborators who've gone completely silent, suggesting direct ultimatums while maintaining compassion for unknown personal circumstances.
key_points: O'Neil articulates a counter-cultural appreciation for urban density, dirt, and even rudeness as authentic human experiences compared to suburban passive-aggression
She frames subway delays and crowded conditions as opportunities for genuine human connection rather than inconveniences
On academic collaboration, she advises pragmatic directness: give clear deadlines and consequences while withholding resentment since you don't know others' circumstances
The column reveals her personality through lifestyle details—no car ownership, weather machismo, sending kids out independently in NYC
She expresses frustration at running low on reader questions, insisting all submissions must be actual questions
money_quotes: I like things crowded and dirty. I like people of all ages and races and ethnicities sweating on each other in the subway.
That's why I like it when the subway stops for an hour in the tunnel and we all end up missing whatever appointments we had and we talk to each and behave like human beings. That's New York!
I don't know how people in suburbia deal with hostility! Maybe through those commuter traffic jams? Too passive aggressive to me, I want it to be face-to-face.
Just seeing other people be pushy and pointy helps me find my zen.
Chances are, though, they just get overwhelmed with other projects and don't quite want to shit and don't quite want to get off the pot either. It's your job to make them decide which one to do.
No reason to hold back your publications either.
posts.key_insight: Authentic human friction—whether in crowded subways or difficult collaborations—is preferable to the passive-aggressive avoidance of suburban or academic politeness.

posts.summary: The post examines why white liberals avoid discussing racism explicitly, even when addressing issues with clear racial dimensions like criminal justice, education, and debt. O'Neil draws on her Occupy group's discussions about systemic racism and cites a study showing that white people become more accepting of punitive policies when told prisoners are disproportionately Black. She observes that politicians like Elizabeth Warren and Obama strategically avoid mentioning race, instead framing issues around class, veterans, or cost savings because focus groups show this is more effective with white audiences. The post argues this political cunning is insufficient and calls for finding ways to make people directly confront racism.
key_points: A psychological study found that white people are more accepting of mass incarceration when reminded that prisoners are disproportionately Black, contradicting the assumption that highlighting racial disparities builds support for reform

Liberal politicians deliberately avoid mentioning race when discussing racially disparate issues like student loans or for-profit colleges because focus groups show white audiences respond better to class-based or cost-based framing
The euphemistic treatment of racism mirrors historical whitewashing, like calling the genocide of Native Americans 'Manifest Destiny'
Ta-Nehisi Coates' 'Case for Reparations' provided a framework for understanding affirmative action and the legacy of practices like reverse redlining
money_quotes: I'm left feeling a lot like I felt in 7th grade social studies when we studied the period of mass genocide of American Indians and called it 'Manifest Destiny.'
The question seems to be which instinct wins out: the belief that our prison system isn't fair, or the assumption that a prisoner must be a criminal.
If you tell them most of those prisoners are black, then they're less likely to think it's terrible.

Politicians and activists have figured out that, if they want to agitate for criminal justice reform, they can't mention systemically racist unfairness, because that just doesn't upset powerful people enough.
Instead, they need to focus on important stuff like saving money, which is how you get white people up in arms.
The problem with understanding stuff like this is that it's primarily used to be politically cunning, which is not enough.
posts.key_insight: Highlighting racial disparities in the justice system actually decreases white support for reform, forcing advocates to hide the very racism they're trying to address.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques the growing use of predictive risk models in criminal sentencing across 20+ US states, arguing they constitute a 'weapon of math destruction.' These models use factors like unemployment, education, neighborhood, and family criminal history to predict recidivism, effectively penalizing people for being poor rather than for their actual crimes. She argues this creates discriminatory feedback loops that compound existing racial and economic biases in the justice system, while modelers defend the practice as 'scientific.'
key_points: Risk-based sentencing models use socioeconomic factors unrelated to the actual crime committed, effectively jailing poor people longer
The models create negative feedback loops: longer sentences for 'high risk' populations make them riskier in future models
Confused causality is baked in—if police arrest certain groups more often (e.g., Stop & Frisk), the model treats them as higher risk regardless of actual behavior
Claiming to only use models for sentence mitigation still discriminates, since letting low-risk wealthy people off easier is equivalent to harsher sentences for poor minorities
Defenders calling these models 'scientific' represents a severe abuse of science and mathematics
money_quotes: The modelers defend this crap as 'scientific,' which is the worst abuse of science and mathematics imaginable.
Letting a bunch of rich white people off easier because they are not considered 'high risk' is tantamount to sentencing poor minorities more.
It is also presumably unconstitutional to jail someone longer for being poor, which is what this effectively does.
The model will impose longer sentencing on the population it considers most risky, which will in turn make them even riskier in the future.
How great would it be if we used data and models to make sure rich people went to jail just as often and for just as long as poor people for the same crime, instead of the other way around?
We could easily imagine a certain group that gets arrested more often for a given crime just because the police have that practice for whatever reason.
posts.key_insight: Predictive sentencing models launder existing social inequalities through mathematical legitimacy, punishing poverty and race while claiming scientific objectivity.

posts.summary: A payday loan syndicate run by Carey Vaughn Brown was busted in New York for charging interest rates of 350-650% annually, far exceeding the state's 25% usury cap. The operation deliberately used a complex web of twelve companies across different states and overseas to obscure the predatory lending chain. O'Neil highlights a crucial question: how did these predatory lenders target vulnerable New Yorkers through online advertising, and how does that targeting simultaneously hide such operations from affluent people and authorities who might stop them?
key_points: The syndicate used deliberate complexity with twelve companies across jurisdictions to obscure predatory lending operations
New York usury laws cap interest at 25% annually, but these lenders charged 350-650%
Usury laws apply based on borrower location, not lender location, making overseas shell companies irrelevant
Targeted advertising to vulnerable populations creates a two-sided problem: exploitation of the desperate while hiding the crime from those who could stop it
One company alone had $50 million in proceeds from New York in 2012
money_quotes: The complexity of the operation was deliberate and intended to obscure the chain of events that would start with a New Yorker online looking for quick cash online and end with a predatory loan.

How did MyCashNow.com advertise? Did it use Google ads, or Facebook ads, or something else, and if so, what were the attributes of the desperate New Yorkers that it looked for to do its predatory work?

One side of this is that vulnerable people were somehow targeted. The other side is that well-off people were not, which meant they didn't see ads like this, which makes it harder for people like the Manhattan District Attorney to even know about shady operations like this.
posts.key_insight: Targeted advertising creates a dangerous invisibility cloak for predatory operations—by only reaching vulnerable populations, these schemes hide from the very authorities and advocates who might expose them.

posts.summary: O'Neil enthusiastically announces StemForums, a new Q&A platform modeled after StackOverflow and MathOverflow but covering STEM subjects beyond coding and pure math. She explains how StackOverflow's crowdsourced, vote-ranked answers have become indispensable for programmers, and predicts similar potential for other scientific fields. The post lists the twelve subject areas StemForums will cover, from statistics to physics. O'Neil expresses mild disappointment that data science and data journalism aren't separate categories but remains optimistic about the platform's potential.
key_points: StackOverflow has become essential for coders because it provides searchable, copy-paste-ready solutions that are often more reliable than asking knowledgeable friends
MathOverflow serves a similar function for mathematicians, hosting both factual Q&A and substantive discussions
StemForums aims to extend this model to twelve STEM fields including statistics, biology, chemistry, physics, and economics
The platform is still nascent with few questions and answers, but early adopters can win Springer books as incentives
money_quotes: It is exactly what you need to get stuff working, and it's better than having a friend to ask, even a highly knowledgable friend, because your friend might be busy or might not know the answer, or even if your friend knew the answer her answer isn't cut-and-paste-able.
Just yesterday I met a cool coding chick who was learning python and pandas (of course!) with the assistance of stackoverflow.
I'm super excited for this site, it has serious potential to make peoples' lives better.
posts.key_insight: Crowdsourced Q&A platforms like StackOverflow have revolutionized how people learn and solve problems, and extending this model to broader STEM fields could democratize scientific knowledge in the same way.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reflects on her deep love of mathematics and the welcoming community that nurtured her career, contrasting this with her criticism of the Fields Medal. She argues that the medal perpetuates a false narrative of individual genius while obscuring the collaborative nature of mathematical research. The 40-year age limit particularly disadvantages women and involved parents. She calls for science journalism that better represents mathematics as a community effort rather than focusing on singular heroes.
key_points: Mathematics is fundamentally a collaborative community effort, not the work of isolated geniuses
Journalism's need to 'put a face on the story' distorts public understanding of how math actually works
The Fields Medal creates a false hierarchy between 'superstars who have it' and everyone else
The 40-year age limit discriminates against women and parents who may hit their stride later
Being part of a mathematical community where progress is celebrated is its own reward
money_quotes: Math is, after all, not an individual act, it is a community effort, and progress is to be celebrated and adored.
When journalists are told to 'put a face on the story,' they end up with all face and no story.
The Fields Medal is easy to understand ('it's the Nobel Prize for math!') but it's incredibly and dangerously misleading.
It gives the impression that we have these superstars who 'have it' and then we have a bunch of wandering nerds who 'don't really have it.'
That stereotype is a bad advertisement for mathematics and for mathematicians, who are actually much more generous and community-spirited than that.
As a 42-year-old woman myself, may I say I'm just getting started.
There are many great mathematicians who never get awards, and doing great math and making progress is its own reward.
posts.key_insight: The Fields Medal undermines mathematics by replacing its collaborative reality with a damaging myth of individual genius that discourages participation and obscures how mathematical progress actually happens.

posts.summary: This guest post by Sophie Chou critiques the buzzword status of 'Data Science' while introducing her vision paper on democratizing the field. She argues that despite the marketing hype, data science talent typically ends up working on advertising problems rather than societal issues. The paper, co-authored with MIT researchers, explores how to redirect data science resources toward social good. Chou acknowledges that while the field has potential for great social impact, resources are rarely distributed to optimize that potential.
key_points: 'Data Science' and 'Big Data' are largely marketing buzzwords used to attract talented scientists who end up working on advertising problems
The real question is how to direct data science talent toward problems focused on societal issues
Current applications of data science have potential for great social impact, but resources aren't distributed to optimize social good
Democratizing data science means making the field more accessible and oriented toward public benefit
money_quotes: "Data Science" is one of my least favorite tech buzzwords, second to probably "Big Data", which in my opinion should be always printed followed by a winky face (after all, my data is bigger than yours).

It's mostly a marketing ploy used by companies to attract talented scientists, statisticians, and mathematicians, who, at the end of the day, will probably be working on some sort of advertising problem or the other.
How can we direct these data scientists towards problems focused on societal issues?
The current applications of Data Science has the potential for great social impact, but in reality, resources are rarely distributed in a way to optimize the social good.
