posts.key_insight: Data science talent is being funneled into advertising rather than social good, and democratizing the field means redirecting these resources toward societal problems.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil responds to Ethan Zuckerman's Atlantic piece about advertising as the internet's original sin. While she agrees with his core argument about the inherent conflict between privacy and ad-based business models, she pushes back on his claim that users have simply 'accepted' manipulation because they haven't left Facebook. She argues that addiction and habit explain continued use better than acceptance, drawing an analogy to smoking. The post concludes by asking what social networking alternatives might emerge and whether privacy will become a luxury only premium users can afford.
key_points: The ad-based internet economy creates an inherent conflict with privacy, requiring ever-deeper surveillance to improve targeting
Continued use of manipulative platforms like Facebook reflects addiction and habit, not acceptance of manipulation
User sentiment toward Facebook has shifted even if behavior hasn't yet caught up
The real question is what alternatives people will eventually leave Facebook for
Privacy may become commoditized, with protection available only to those who can pay
money_quotes: It is a mistake to assume that 'users accept this sort of manipulation' because not everyone has stopped using Facebook.
People don't give up addictive habits overnight. But it doesn't mean they are feeling the same way about Facebook that they did 4 years ago.
People are adjusting their opinion of the user experience as that user experience is increasingly manipulated and creepy.
An analogy should be drawn to something like smoking, where the rates have gone way down since we all found out it is bad for you.
What is the social networking model of the future?
Will privacy be entirely commoditized, whereby only premium pay members will be protected, while the rest of us will be thrown to the dogs?
posts.key_insight: Confusing users' continued engagement with manipulative platforms for acceptance ignores the stickiness of addiction—the real question is what they'll eventually leave for.

posts.summary: This is an event announcement for the Alt Banking group, promoting a talk by British author and journalist Nafeez Ahmed on mass surveillance and civilizational crisis. Ahmed, an international security scholar who writes for The Guardian, will discuss the collapse of old paradigms and potential for new ones. The post provides background on Ahmed's work covering interconnected environmental, energy, and economic crises, and links to his investigative pieces on Pentagon surveillance programs and data-mining tools targeting activists.
key_points: Nafeez Ahmed will speak at Alt Banking about mass surveillance and civilizational crisis
Ahmed is an international security scholar who writes for The Guardian on interconnected global crises
Recommended reading includes his Pentagon pieces on civil breakdown preparation and activist tracking
The talk frames current surveillance as part of a broader systemic paradigm shift
money_quotes: Mass Surveillance and the Crisis of Civilization: The inevitable collapse of the old paradigm and the potential for the rise of the new
Pentagon preparing for mass civil breakdown
Exposed: Pentagon Funds New Data-Mining Tools To Track and Kill Activists
posts.key_insight: This event announcement highlights growing concern about the intersection of mass surveillance, data-mining tools, and their potential use against civilian activists.

posts.summary: The post contrasts old-school computer-assisted reporting with new interactive data journalism approaches. Traditional data journalism treats data as evidence supporting a narrative with clear protagonists and conclusions. New-school data journalism creates interactives that tell personalized stories to each reader, making the interaction itself the story. O'Neil suggests these approaches serve different purposes: one speaks truth to power through investigation, while the other educates the public by letting them explore how data affects them personally.
key_points: Traditional data journalism uses data as evidence for claims, requiring secondary source verification, and focuses on investigations that speak truth to power
New interactive data journalism tells multiple personalized stories simultaneously, with the reader interaction becoming the story itself
Interactive data tools educate the public in 'untamed' ways by revealing variations across time, space, and demographics
Old-school editors may not recognize interactives as 'real' journalism because they lack traditional narrative elements like villains and plot twists
Data interactives can reveal how seemingly innocuous information accumulates into profiles, teaching people about surveillance and profiling
money_quotes: You are investigating and interrogating the data as you would a witness, but the data isn't itself a story, but rather a way of gathering evidence for the claims posed in the story.
The new interactive stories do not tell one story. Instead, they tell a bunch of stories to a bunch of people, and that interaction itself becomes the story.
They also educate the public in a somewhat untamed way: by interacting with a database a reader can see variations in time, or in space, or in demographic.

By seeing how each question on a jury duty quiz nudges you towards the plaintiff or the defendant, you can begin to see how seemingly innocuous information collected about you accumulates, which is how profiles are formed, on and offline.
I'm not sure many of them would even get past the pitch stage if proffered to a curmudgeonly editor (and all editors are curmudgeonly, that's just a fact).
posts.key_insight: Interactive data journalism transforms readers from passive consumers into active participants, making the personalized exploration of data itself a form of storytelling and public education.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil recounts Jeff Larson's visit to the Lede Program at Columbia, where he demonstrated impressive data journalism projects from ProPublica. Larson showed how they reverse-engineered Obama's political targeting algorithm using decision trees, applied clustering algorithms to sort foreign language emails, and bypassed captcha systems using neural networks he built over a lunch break. The students then toured ProPublica's offices and met other data journalists working on secret projects. O'Neil emphasizes how inspiring it was that most of these skilled data journalists, including Larson himself, lacked formal computer science training.
key_points: ProPublica crowdsourced and reverse-engineered Obama's political targeting algorithm, discovering it used decision trees
Clustering algorithms like cosine similarity and k-means can be used to find interesting content in foreign language email datasets
Neural networks can bypass captcha systems about 90% of the time
Most data journalists at ProPublica lack formal CS training - Larson was a literature major
Hands-on demonstrations helped students realize they already had applicable skills
money_quotes: They first crowdsourced, then reverse engineered Obama's political targeting algorithm.
Since it was an awesome project important to democracy, we also got to talk about democracy.
For a moment the class was like, 'hey we can do this!' and it was true.
He seemed to somehow remain humble whilst explaining that he did this over a lunch break.
Only one of them had formal CS training (Jeff was a lit major!).
posts.key_insight: Data journalism that holds power accountable doesn't require formal computer science credentials—curiosity, persistence, and willingness to learn technical skills can take you remarkably far.

posts.summary: This is an advice column focused on sex and relationships, where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil's alter ego) answers reader questions about dating frustrations, condom negotiations, age-related sexual dysfunction, and what constitutes great sex. The column blends humor with practical advice, encouraging readers to communicate directly, initiate intimacy rather than wait passively, and maintain firm boundaries around safe sex. O'Neil emphasizes that sex should be characterized by generosity, empathy, and fun, while challenging traditional definitions of what counts as 'sex.' The tone throughout is playful and sex-positive while addressing real relationship concerns.
key_points: If a partner consistently avoids intimacy, take initiative yourself rather than waiting passively—then you'll know where you stand
Safe sex boundaries are non-negotiable, and anyone who tries to bully you out of using condoms deserves a firm rejection
Sex isn't narrowly defined as vaginal intercourse; expanding that definition can improve both safety and pleasure
Sexual aids like Viagra should be viewed as just another tool in the toolbox, alongside lubricant and communication
Great sex is characterized by generosity, empathy, and fun—not so different from a great dinner or bike ride with someone
money_quotes: Why are you waiting for him to initiate? That's old fashioned and silly.
Sex is not defined as vaginal intercourse.
That's how grownups who do not want to be monogamous or have children have vaginal intercourse.
It's another tool in the sex toolbox, sitting there right next to KY Jelly and the feather duster.
Make it about her pleasure. Who can resist that? Answer: nobody.
Great sex is when both people feel great about it. It is characterized by generosity, empathy, and fun.
I like books that make me think differently and challenge my assumptions.
posts.key_insight: Healthy sexual relationships require direct communication, firm personal boundaries, and a generous focus on mutual pleasure rather than narrow definitions or passive expectations.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques Gillian Tett's Financial Times article that defends Chicago's predictive policing model as a 'neutral tool.' O'Neil dismantles the weak statistical evidence Tett uses to claim the program reduced murder rates, noting that correlation doesn't prove causation—as demonstrated by NYC's murder rates not rising when Stop & Frisk declined. More fundamentally, O'Neil argues that no algorithm trained on human behavior can be neutral because it inherently codifies historical biases, such as the documented racial disparity in marijuana arrests despite equal usage rates across races. She concludes that if even sophisticated commentators like Tett can be fooled by claims of algorithmic neutrality, there's much work to be done in public understanding.
key_points: Tett's evidence for predictive policing success is merely correlative—the small uptick in murders after the program ended could be statistical noise
NYC's Stop & Frisk experience disproves simple cause-effect claims: murder rates didn't spike when the practice was reduced
Algorithms trained on historical human behavior necessarily encode and perpetuate the biases embedded in that history
Racial disparities in arrest rates (like 4x higher marijuana arrests for Black Americans despite equal usage) get treated as 'neutral facts' by such models
If predictive models had been used for college admissions in 1870, they would have perpetuated the 0.7% female enrollment rate
money_quotes: No, Gillian Tett, there is no such thing as a neutral tool.
No algorithm focused on human behavior is neutral.
Anything which is trained on historical human behavior embeds and codifies historical and cultural practices.
Put it another way: if we allowed a model to be used for college admissions in 1870, we'd still have 0.7% of women going to college. Thank goodness we didn't have big data back then!
This is very scary to me, when even Gillian Tett, who famously predicted the financial crisis in 2006, can be fooled.
Here's the thing, it's really hard to actually know why murder rates go up and down.
posts.key_insight: Algorithms trained on historical data don't neutrally predict the future—they perpetuate and legitimize the biases of the past.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews Carole Marshall's memoir about teaching in urban Providence schools, using it to critique how standardized testing policies have driven talented teachers out of education. Marshall developed effective, relevant curriculum that engaged poverty-stricken students, but quit after her school system was taken over by rigid testing mandates that forced canned curriculum and eliminated creative teaching approaches. O'Neil argues that while reform rhetoric focuses on removing 'bad teachers,' these policies simultaneously push out dedicated, inspirational educators. The post connects individual teacher experience to the broader national disaster of testing-obsessed education reform.
key_points: Standardized testing policies force teachers to abandon effective, student-relevant curriculum for expensive canned materials from publishers who 'knew nothing about urban teaching'
Administrative surveillance (with 'shiny new iPads') enforces rigid conformity, requiring all teachers to be on the same page every day regardless of student needs
The focus on removing 'bad teachers' ignores the collateral damage of losing talented, dedicated educators who refuse to participate in what they see as non-teaching
Education reform has created perverse incentives including consultant scams and cherry-picking students to game arbitrary metrics
The correlation between poverty and low test scores becomes obvious when you understand students' actual struggles, not just their numbers
money_quotes: After spending years refining strategies for getting my students to become enthusiastic readers and writers on thoughtful, relevant curriculum, I was being forced to teach canned curriculum purchased for millions of dollars from textbook publishers who knew nothing about urban teaching.
Every path to good teaching was effectively blocked off.
What was going on in the classrooms could no longer be called teaching.
She makes this shit very very real.
We are so used to pointing at examples of bad and defeated teachers and saying that they are the problem.
It is painful to imagine how many great teachers have left the educational system because of No Child Left Behind and Race To The Top.
posts.key_insight: Education reform policies designed to weed out bad teachers are simultaneously driving out the most dedicated and effective ones, and we're not even measuring that loss.

posts.summary: This is a brief personal post where Cathy O'Neil shares a video of her 14-year-old son's stand-up comedy performance at the Gotham Comedy Club. She notes that she doesn't typically write about her children but has received explicit permission to share this. The post highlights a proud parent moment, with O'Neil calling out her favorite part: the look her son gives the audience at the end of his set.
key_points: O'Neil breaks from her usual content to share something personal about her family
Her son performed stand-up comedy at a professional venue at age 14
She obtained explicit consent before posting about her child online
money_quotes: I don't usually blog about my kids, but my 14-year-old son has explicitly given me his blessing to post his recent stand-up performance at the Gotham Comedy Club.
posts.key_insight: A rare personal glimpse into O'Neil's family life, showcasing her son's comedy debut with parental pride.

posts.summary: O'Neil announces her project to build a decision tree that helps data practitioners choose the right algorithm based on the question they're trying to answer, not just technical factors like data size. She's using an online flowchart tool and has just started. She notes that existing algorithm cheat sheets focus too much on data characteristics rather than the underlying question being answered. The challenge is that mapping questions to algorithms is 'orthogonal' to how algorithms are typically taught, making the framework harder to construct.
key_points: Existing algorithm selection guides focus too heavily on data size and technical constraints rather than the conceptual question being answered
The mapping from 'what question am I asking?' to 'which algorithm answers it?' is underexplored and non-obvious
Many projects end up using clustering or classification even when the original question wasn't framed that way
Real student questions from her Lede Program would have been valuable examples of 'data questions in search of an algorithm'
money_quotes: And yes, I think it's kind of cool that 'decision tree' would be an outcome on my decision tree. Kind of like a nerd pun.

It was one of those moments when I said to myself, this morning at 6am, 'there should be a start-up that allows me to build a flowchart online!' and it totally worked. I almost feel like I willed gliffy.com into existence.
What kind of question are you answering with which algorithm?
Plenty of projects have ended up being clustering or classifying algorithms whose motivating questions did not originally take on the form 'how would we group these things together?'
The process of getting at algorithms from questions is somewhat orthogonal to the normal way algorithms are introduced.
posts.key_insight: Choosing the right algorithm should start from the question you're trying to answer, not from the technical characteristics of your data—but this question-first approach is rarely how algorithms are taught.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques a New York Times article that she argues misleadingly blames teachers for declining student test scores. She points out that the score drops resulted from testing students on Common Core material they hadn't been taught, not teacher incompetence. The post dismantles the logical fallacy of comparing high teacher effectiveness ratings with low student proficiency rates, arguing this ignores the overwhelming correlation between poverty and test scores. O'Neil warns that the 'blame the teacher' narrative serves anti-tenure interests while distracting from the real issue of childhood poverty.
key_points: The Common Core test score decline reflects a curriculum-test mismatch, not teacher failure
The VAM (Value-Added Model) for teacher evaluation is random and punitive, not insufficiently strict
Standardized test scores correlate strongly with student poverty levels across countries
The 'bad teacher' narrative is a logical fallacy that ignores systemic factors
Focusing on rooting out bad teachers distracts from addressing childhood poverty
money_quotes: The truth is closer to 'students were tested on stuff that wasn't in their curriculum.'
How can we have 26% English proficiency among students and 94% effectiveness among teachers?! Let's blame the teachers and question the legitimacy of tenure.

Unless you think bad teachers have somehow taken over poor schools everywhere and booted out the good teachers, and good teachers have taken over rich schools everywhere and booted out the bad teachers, poverty has much more of an effect than teachers.

We could blame bad journalists for lower rates of newspaper readership at a given paper, but since newspaper readership is going down everywhere we'd be blaming journalists for what is a cultural issue.
I'm not saying there aren't bad teachers, because I'm sure there are. But by only focusing on rooting out bad teachers, we are ignoring an even bigger and harder problem.
Considering how many poor kids we have in the U.S., we are actually doing pretty well.
posts.key_insight: Blaming teachers for poor student performance is a convenient distraction from the much harder problem of childhood poverty, which data shows is the dominant factor in educational outcomes.

posts.summary: In this advice column, Cathy O'Neil (as Aunt Pythia) addresses questions from readers about career choices, mathematical thinking, social etiquette, and data journalism. She strongly advises an MIT math student against pursuing finance for money while ignoring the real-world impacts of their work. She discusses how mathematicians visualize problems, offers perspective on asking about ethnic backgrounds with good intentions, and reflects on bias in data journalism. The column emphasizes that transparency about bias is more achievable than objectivity, and that the ethics of applied mathematics deserve serious consideration.
key_points: Pursuing pure math you love is more valuable than taking courses for 'cred' with financial companies - you can learn practical skills later
Once you leave pure math, your work has real-world consequences that affect real people, so consider carefully who you work for
Most mathematicians think visually, though not all, and even geometers often lack good 3D spatial sense or sense of direction
True objectivity in data journalism is impossible because bias exists at minimum in how data is collected - transparency about bias is the realistic goal
Asking about someone's background is fine if your intention is friendly connection rather than hostility
money_quotes: Don't take the math to get 'cred' with financial companies. Do what is sexy and beautiful to you.
Once you leave pure math there are real applications of your work, and they affect real people. Shit gets real real quick and stuff matters.
We will never have unbiased accounts. Never. At the very least we will have bias in the way that data is collected.
Transparency is the new objectivity!
If your intention is to be mean, then don't ask it. If your intention is to be friendly and to make a connection, then go ahead and ask it!
posts.key_insight: Mathematical work has real-world consequences once it leaves the realm of pure theory, so practitioners must think critically about who benefits and who is harmed by their contributions.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that single aggregate statistics like GDP are inadequate for measuring economic health because they obscure vastly different realities across economic classes. While the wealthy enjoy record stock markets and favorable conditions, the middle class faces stagnant wages and job losses to automation, and the poor are trapped in cycles of poverty exacerbated by predatory legal and probation systems. She advocates for 'distributional thinking'—examining economic conditions from multiple class perspectives rather than collapsing everything into one number. The post uses Ferguson as an example of how court fines and fees create a self-reinforcing poverty trap that strips the poor of political voice.
key_points: GDP growth does not reflect the lived economic reality of most Americans, making it a poor proxy for national economic health
The wealthy, middle class, and poor are experiencing completely divergent economic realities despite aggregate statistics suggesting recovery
Private probation companies and court fee systems create a predatory 'poverty capitalism' that traps poor people in debt
Felony disenfranchisement silences the poor politically, removing their ability to advocate for systemic change
No single statistic can capture economic progress when different classes are moving in opposite directions
money_quotes: I am pushing an unusual way of considering economic health. I call it 'distributional thinking.'
It's a good time to be rich right now. Really good.
It's a really bad time to be poor in this country.

We let this happen in the name of saving money: private for-profit companies like this guarantee that they won't cost anything to the local government because they make the people on probation pay for services.
Once they are officially branded as criminals, the poor often lose their voting rights, which means they have little political recourse to protect themselves.
Once you think about our economic health this way, you realize how comparatively meaningless the GDP is.
It is a mathematical fact that no one statistic could possibly capture the progress we are or are not making.
posts.key_insight: Aggregate economic statistics like GDP are mathematically incapable of representing national wellbeing when different economic classes are experiencing divergent realities.

posts.summary: Gary Cornell argues that the ubiquity of calculators in math education has eliminated students' need to develop order-of-magnitude estimation skills, which were naturally required when using slide rules. He and Cathy O'Neil agree that math curricula should be restructured around probability, statistics, and simplified calculus to be more practical and help students identify statistical misinformation. Cornell proposes either bringing back slide rules or implementing a 'significant digits only' calculator mode to force students to think about scale. The underlying concern is that calculator precision creates false confidence in exact answers when real-world problems rarely have or need 14 digits of accuracy.
key_points: Slide rules forced students to continuously practice order-of-magnitude calculations because they only display significant digits, not trailing zeros
Modern calculators have made Fermi estimation an 'add-on rather than a baseline skill' which is harmful to scientific thinking
A unified probability-statistics-calculus curriculum would be more practical and help students identify statistical misinformation
High-precision calculator displays create a false belief that real-world problems have or need 14 digits of accuracy
A simple 'significant digits only' calculator mode could restore the educational benefits of estimation without eliminating calculators entirely
money_quotes: Calculators have made skill at doing order of magnitude calculations an add-on rather than a base line skill and that is a really bad thing.
Students would hopefully learn a skill that is essential nowadays: how to separate out statistically good information from the large amount of statistical crap that is out there.
Using a slide rule requires the user to keep track of the 'order of magnitude' of the answers—because slide rules only give you four or so significant digits.
When you see a high precision result in the real world, it is likely to be totally bogus when examined under the hood.
Nothing they are likely to encounter in their work (and certainly not in their lives) will ever need (or even have) 14 digits of accuracy.
posts.key_insight: The shift from slide rules to calculators inadvertently eliminated the constant practice of order-of-magnitude thinking that is essential for detecting bogus precision in real-world claims.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques the use of student evaluations as a metric for teacher performance, drawing on her experience teaching calculus at Barnard. She found that identical teaching produced vastly different evaluations depending on student population—eager freshmen loved her while reluctant upperclassmen taking a requirement did not. She argues that tying teacher evaluation to student feedback creates perverse incentives to make classes easier rather than more rigorous. The piece is a warning about a Silicon Valley startup called Panorama that aims to systematize this flawed data collection.
key_points: Student evaluations reflect student attitudes and motivations more than teaching quality—the same teacher with the same methods received dramatically different scores from different student populations
Incentivizing good student evaluations encourages teachers to make classes easier rather than more challenging
Most students don't like homework or being pushed outside their comfort zones, so optimizing for their approval conflicts with educational goals
Student evaluation data is useful for teacher self-reflection but becomes harmful when used punitively
The move toward data-driven teacher evaluation may simply shift from one flawed metric (standardized tests) to another (student surveys)
money_quotes: The students in the Spring were older, less eager, probably just as smart, but less hard-working. They didn't like me or the class.
Was there some way I could have gotten better evaluations from that second group? Absolutely. I could have made the class easier.
Once we add 'get good student evaluations' to the mix of requirements for our country's teachers, we are asking for them to conform to their students' wishes, which aren't always good.
Many of the students in this country don't like doing homework (in fact most!). Only some of them like to be challenged to think outside their comfort zone.
We think teachers should do those things, but by asking them to get good student evaluations we might be preventing them from doing those things.
I'd love to see teachers be allowed to see these evaluations without there being punitive consequences.
posts.key_insight: Using student evaluations as a high-stakes metric for teacher performance creates a perverse incentive to make classes easier, undermining the educational rigor we claim to want.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil wraps up her Lede Program for data journalism at Columbia, celebrating with students and her bluegrass band. Rather than writing a full post, she shares a curated reading list covering data visualization failures, private equity in distressed communities, payday loan targeting, income inequality, and gender bias in negotiations. The links reflect her ongoing interests in algorithmic targeting, economic justice, and data literacy.
key_points: The Lede Program taught data journalism students to recognize bad data visualization practices
Private equity firms buying distressed homes in places like Ferguson raises complex questions about investment and community impact
Payday loan companies use sophisticated online ad targeting that remains opaque
CEO-to-worker pay ratios in fast food (1252:1) illustrate extreme income inequality
Research shows people are more likely to lie to women in negotiations
money_quotes: My students all know better than this.
I'm actually not sure this is a bad thing, though, if nobody else is willing to do it. Please discuss.
I am still on the search for someone who knows exactly how those guys target their ads online.
The ratio of average CEO pay to average full-time worker pay is around 1252.
People lie to women in negotiations. I need to remember this.
posts.key_insight: Even in a casual reading roundup, O'Neil's selections reveal her persistent focus on how money, power, and information asymmetries shape everyday life.

posts.summary: Aunt Pythia, Cathy O'Neil's advice column persona, defers her usual column to highlight an exceptional Ask Polly advice piece about self-worth and relationships. The column so impressed her that she considers it the best advice she's ever seen, applicable to both men and women. Rather than answering her own questions, she urges readers to read Polly's response about stopping the search for external validation.
key_points: The post is a rare instance of Aunt Pythia ceding her column to recommend another advice writer
The Ask Polly piece addresses why someone feels unloved by romantic partners
The advice centers on rejecting external validation and embracing self-worth
O'Neil finds the advice universally applicable regardless of gender
money_quotes: Is it time to stop being so good and start discovering what's going to transform your life into something big and vibrant and shocking?
Block the 'other' from this picture. No more audience. You are the cherished and the cherisher.
Fuck wondering if you're lovable. Fuck asking someone else, 'Am I there yet?' Fuck listening for the answer.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best advice is to stop seeking external validation and become your own source of worth and love.

posts.summary: The post examines Steven Ma, an ex-hedge fund manager who claims to have reverse-engineered Ivy League admissions algorithms and charges wealthy families up to $1.1 million for guaranteed college placement. Ma's company ThinkTank uses data-driven optimization to game both college admissions and the US News rankings system, treating education access as an arbitrage opportunity. O'Neil draws a parallel to financial regulation, arguing that complex systems inevitably advantage wealthy insiders who can afford to decode and exploit the rules. The piece questions whether the predictability of elite college admissions undermines meritocracy and suggests disclosure laws might help, while acknowledging they too could be gamed.
key_points: A former hedge fund manager has turned college admissions into a quantitative modeling problem, complete with probability-weighted contracts and money-back guarantees
The commodification of college coaching creates a two-tier system where wealthy families can essentially purchase algorithmic advantages
Complex admissions systems, like financial regulations, tend to benefit insiders who can afford to reverse-engineer them
Ma plans to scale his operation by purchasing completed applications and building an 'optimizer' for choosing activities and classes
The predictability of elite admissions suggests the meritocratic ideal may be illusory
money_quotes: It's a huge modeling war that you can pay to engage in.
This whole college coaching industry reminds me a lot of financial regulation.
We complicate the rules to the point where only very well-off insiders know exactly how to bypass the rules.
To the extent that getting into one of these 'top schools' actually does give young people access to power, influence, and success, it's alarming how predictable the whole process has become.
Here's a thought: maybe we should have disclosure laws about college coaching and prep? Or would those laws be gamed too?
posts.key_insight: When systems designed to measure merit become complex enough to be reverse-engineered, they inevitably become tools for wealthy insiders to game, transforming meritocracy into plutocracy.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that sports talk radio has unexpectedly become the most honest forum for discussing domestic violence, racism, and homophobia in American culture. Writing in response to the Ray Rice domestic violence video, she praises ESPN's coverage for asking uncomfortable questions that politicians and academics avoid. She highlights how the NFL initially gave Rice only a 2-game suspension and made his wife apologize, revealing institutional complicity in domestic violence. The piece challenges readers to confront why society needed video evidence to take the assault seriously.
key_points: Sports talk radio has become a surprisingly honest venue for discussing violence against women, race, and homophobia
The NFL's initial 2-game suspension and forcing Janay Rice to apologize reveals institutional tolerance of domestic violence
Law enforcement and the NFL failed to act appropriately until video evidence forced their hand
Society's shock at the video exposes the uncomfortable question of whether people believed the victim deserved it
money_quotes: In the past decade, sports talk radio has become the best, rawest, and most honest source of information about how our culture condones and ignores violence against women, not to mention issues of race and homophobia.
You are not going to hear this stuff from politicians or academics.
What does it say about the NFL that they had the wife, Janay Rice, apologize for her role in the incident?
What did people think it would look like when a professional football player knocks out a woman?
Did people really think she did something to deserve it, and now they are shocked to see that she didn't?
posts.key_insight: The public's need for video evidence to take domestic violence seriously reveals a cultural assumption that victims somehow deserve abuse until proven otherwise.

posts.summary: This guest post by Marc Joffe argues that new federal banking regulations requiring large banks to hold high-quality liquid assets (HQLAs) illogically exclude municipal bonds while allowing riskier assets like foreign government securities, corporate bonds, and stocks. The author contends this will force banks to divest from municipal bonds, reducing demand and raising interest rates for infrastructure financing. This directly contradicts the Obama administration's stated commitment to infrastructure improvement. Joffe presents data showing municipal bonds have lower default rates than foreign government debt and comparable liquidity to corporate bonds, making the exclusion arbitrary and counterproductive.
key_points: New HQLA rules allow banks to hold foreign government bonds, corporate bonds, and stocks, but exclude US municipal bonds despite their lower default rates
No state has defaulted on a general obligation bond since 1933, while foreign governments like Greece have imposed significant losses on bondholders
Corporate bonds trade even less frequently than municipal bonds, undermining regulators' liquidity justification for exclusion
The regulation creates the absurd situation where banks can hold securities from municipal bond insurers but not the safer insured municipal bonds themselves
Municipal bond issuance is already depressed, and this rule will further increase borrowing costs for infrastructure projects
money_quotes: Obama administration officials frequently talk about the need to improve the nation's infrastructure. Yet new regulations published by the Federal Reserve, FDIC and OCC run counter to this policy by limiting the market for municipal bonds.
No state has defaulted on a general obligation bond since 1933.
Bonds issued by foreign governments can and do default. For example, private investors took a 70% haircut when Greek debt was restructured in 2012.
The average corporate bond trades less than once per day. Not very liquid.

Under the new rules, a bank can purchase bonds or stock issued by Assured Guaranty or MBIA – two major municipal bond insurers – but they can't buy state and local government bonds insured by those companies.
Mandating that banks hold safe securities is a good thing; now we need a more data-driven definition of just what safe means.
posts.key_insight: Banking regulations meant to ensure stability are undermining infrastructure investment by arbitrarily excluding low-risk municipal bonds while permitting demonstrably riskier assets, revealing a disconnect between stated policy goals and regulatory implementation.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the end of her role directing Columbia's Lede Program in Data Journalism and her transition to writing her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' full-time. She expresses urgency about finishing the book while its topics remain timely. She outlines upcoming speaking engagements, including a keynote at Yale where she'll advocate for creating a data science institute with broad, interdisciplinary scope. She also mentions continuing her Slate Money podcast.
key_points: The Lede Program in Data Journalism at Columbia has concluded, with O'Neil leaving her director role
She feels urgent pressure to complete 'Weapons of Math Destruction' before the cultural moment passes
Her Yale keynote will argue for data science institutes that include law and journalism scholars, not just traditional data science fields
She emphasizes the need for urgent cross-disciplinary conversations about the big data revolution
The Information Society Project at Yale Law School represents a promising model for this broader approach
money_quotes: The internal pressure I have to finish this book is reminiscent of how I felt when I needed to write my thesis: enormous, but maybe even worse than then since the timeliness of the book could not be overstated.
I want to get this book out before the moment passes.
My Yale talk is basically a meditation on what can be achieved by academic data science institutions, what presents cultural and technical obstacles to collaboration, and why we need to do it anyway.

It's no less than a plea for Yale to create a data science institute with a broad definition of data science – so including scholars from law and from journalism as well as the fields you think of already when you think of data science.
A broad mandate to have urgent conversations across disciplines about the 'big data revolution.'
posts.key_insight: Data science institutions must include voices from law, journalism, and other disciplines to have the urgent interdisciplinary conversations that the big data revolution demands.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) responds to reader questions about sexism in mathematics (regarding Fields Medal coverage and comments about Maryam Mirzakhani's appearance), career mentorship in data science, dealing with mean people in academia, and grammar debates about 'data.' The column blends personal anecdotes with sharp social commentary, particularly on how women in STEM navigate clothing choices and professional respect. O'Neil draws from her own experience leaving academia for finance to offer perspective on workplace dynamics. The overall message encourages readers to trust themselves, ignore petty meanness, and focus on doing meaningful work.
key_points: Women in STEM often dress in 'men's clothes' for practical reasons—to be taken seriously, avoid harassment, and not invite catcalls—not because of sexuality or 'having a man's brain'
Good data scientists are rare, but young analysts should trust their own integrity and learning drive rather than depending on finding a perfect mentor
Mean people exist everywhere—academia, finance, any field with scarce resources—so the goal is learning to ignore the bickering and focus on the work you love
The singular/plural 'data' debate is settled: always use singular, and anyone insisting on 'data are' sounds like a grammar nazi
money_quotes: Men's clothes are, generally speaking, great for looking totally unobjectionable, not getting harassed or hit on, and not evoking catcalls compared to short skirts and high heels, and if men could wear them they totally would. Oh wait, they already do.
Being taken seriously as a scholar comes to mind. I defend everyone's rights to trousers and a boring button-down shirt.

You have the obvious urge to do something cool and fun with your life, and because you have integrity, and because you are too smart to trick yourself into thinking what you're doing is great when it isn't.
I left academic math in part because people were so mean. They were really mean to me, and especially because I was a woman.
If you want to see hugely assholic behavior, work in finance for a few years.

As long as there are resources to be fought over, there will be fights, and the question is how to ignore all the stupid bickering and get some math done, because after all math is beautiful and awesome.
Every time I hear someone incredibly awkward like, 'Are your organization's data as clear as they can be?' I just wanna retch.
posts.key_insight: Whether facing sexist commentary, workplace meanness, or career uncertainty, the solution is the same: trust your own integrity, ignore the noise, and focus on doing work you find meaningful.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques Christian Rudder's book 'Dataclysm,' which uses OKCupid data to make claims about human behavior and attraction. She argues that Rudder commits fundamental statistical errors: failing to compare his user base to the general population, not explaining how data was collected or questions were worded, ignoring confounding variables in experiments, and making careless accusations of racism without rigorous analysis. Despite having potentially valuable data, Rudder's provocative approach sacrifices thoughtfulness for shock value, rendering his conclusions unreliable and his accusations about user behavior unfair.
key_points: Rudder never compares OKCupid users to the general population census, only to 'wider internet users' - an inappropriate and meaningless baseline for drawing cultural conclusions
The wording of survey questions and how profiles are presented matters enormously but is never disclosed, making it impossible to interpret results about male vs. female attraction patterns
The 'blind date' experiment that supposedly proved looks don't matter actually just selected for people who aren't superficial, then found they weren't superficial - a classic confounding error
Rudder buries the crucial finding that racial preference patterns don't exist outside the U.S., while leading with accusations that 84% of users are hypocritical racists

The book represents a missed opportunity: the data could support nuanced cultural discussions about attraction, fantasy vs. realistic expectations, and social conditioning, but Rudder chooses provocation over thoughtfulness
money_quotes: It's the online equivalent of a video camera in a changing booth at a department store.
A standalone number wouldn't be interesting, that you have to compare that number to some baseline that people can understand.
Words matter, and they matter differently for men and women.
This is more likely the difference between how we live our fantasies and how we actually realistically see our future.
What he's done is select for people who are not superficial about looks, and then collect data that suggests they are not superficial about looks.
It is a step too far for the detective on the other side of the camera to turn around and point fingers at us for doing what we're told.
Rudder seems to confuse provocation for thoughtfulness.
posts.key_insight: Big data analyses that ignore basic statistical principles - proper baselines, question wording effects, confounding variables, and population representation - can produce provocative but ultimately meaningless conclusions about human behavior.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil presents the classic blue-eyed/green-eyed island puzzle, where islanders must leave if they discover their own eye color. A visitor's seemingly redundant statement that 'some people have green eyes' triggers a logical chain reaction that eventually forces all green-eyed people to leave after N days, where N is their number. The puzzle's conundrum is that the visitor added no new factual information—everyone already knew green-eyed people existed—yet his statement transforms the situation entirely. O'Neil explores whether this represents an unstable equilibrium or demonstrates how 'common knowledge' differs from individual knowledge.
key_points: The puzzle demonstrates inductive reasoning: if you see N green-eyed people and they don't leave after N-1 days, you must also have green eyes
The visitor's statement seems information-free when many green-eyed people exist, since everyone already knows some people have green eyes
The key insight is that the statement creates 'common knowledge'—not just that green eyes exist, but that everyone knows everyone knows this
The puzzle raises questions about equilibrium: was the original social silence a stable or unstable state?
Aaron and Jamie coined the phrase 'a green-eyed/blue-eyed thing' for apparently information-free facts that change everything
money_quotes: By social convention they never discuss eye color, because there's a tragic rule that states that, if you ever figure out your eye color, you have to leave the island within 24 hours.
The guy who started this whole mess really didn't do much. He just stated what was obvious to everyone already on the island.
So really the guy just started a count-down more than anything.
Is it not an equilibrium at all? Or is it an unstable equilibrium?
It's a green-eyed/ blue-eyed thing, which means it's an apparently information-free fact which changes everything.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes stating what everyone already knows individually creates common knowledge that transforms a stable situation into an inevitable cascade—information can be about shared awareness, not just facts.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares her personal experience growing up with an abusive father and a mother who stayed with him, using the Ray Rice NFL scandal as a catalyst to process her own trauma. She describes witnessing violence from age 4, being asked at 13 whether her mother should leave, and enduring years of her father's revisionist attempts to gain forgiveness. The public condemnation of domestic violence by NFL commentators gave her permission to accept that some acts are unforgivable. She publishes this to show that domestic violence happens everywhere and to advocate for stronger laws that don't rely on victims pressing charges.
key_points: The economics of leaving an abuser are rarely discussed—managing two households on one salary while already struggling makes leaving seem impossible
Being asked as a child to make adult decisions about family safety destroys childhood and forces premature responsibility
Abusers often engage in revisionist history and guilt-transfer, making their victims' refusal to forgive into the victim's moral failing
Public discourse labeling abuse as 'unforgivable' can liberate survivors from internalized pressure to forgive
Breaking the cycle of violence is a conscious choice and a legitimate point of pride for survivors
money_quotes: When you are a 13-year-old girl who has decided to stand up for your mother and brother against a large and very strong man, who often becomes an enraged and unreasonable bully, you forget about fear and intimidation, because it's just something you cannot think about.

It was my last day of childhood, the day I realized there were no responsible people in my family, and that I would have to step up and be the person who negotiated reasonable boundaries or, failing that, call the cops.
It is unforgivable, but until now I hadn't realized that I was allowed to think so.
He made it my problem for being so unkind as to not forgive him.
Human beings are really good at conflicting emotions.
I've been feeling so guilty for so long at not being able to forgive my father, I never realized that I could just be okay with it.
My proudest accomplishment is that I have not perpetuated the cycle of violence on my own family.
posts.key_insight: Survivors of domestic violence need not carry guilt for refusing to forgive their abusers—some acts are genuinely unforgivable, and accepting that is part of healing.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post inviting readers to join the Climate Convergence march in New York City. O'Neil promotes the event as a way to raise awareness about climate change and mentions that her Alternative Banking group will be participating. The post provides logistical details for meeting up at the Natural History Museum.
key_points: Climate Convergence march scheduled for Sunday to raise climate change awareness
Alternative Banking group participating as an organization
Meeting point at 79th and Central Park West at 11am
money_quotes: This Sunday there'll be a huge march to raise awareness about climate change.
posts.key_insight: This is a logistical announcement rather than an analytical post, showing O'Neil's activism connecting financial reform advocacy with climate action.

posts.summary: This is an advice column by 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) responding to reader questions about graduate school applications, transitioning from academia to industry while maintaining teaching opportunities, and statistical analysis of gender bias in hiring. The column mixes playful humor with substantive advice on navigating academic careers and understanding research methodology. The most substantive discussion concerns a study claiming male PIs hire fewer female postdocs, where O'Neil critiques the statistical methodology and explains why observational studies cannot establish causation without randomized experiments. She notes that while she's skeptical of this particular study's proof, randomized experiments have consistently shown systemic sexism exists in hiring.
key_points: When applying to PhD programs, reaching out to professors is normal and flattering - they like taking students and appreciate genuine interest in their work
Leaving academia doesn't mean abandoning teaching - options include tutoring, summer programs, adjunct positions, and data science training programs
Observational studies about hiring bias suffer from confounding factors that make causal claims difficult to support
Small sample sizes in social science research cannot produce the high-sigma significance levels that physicists expect from their experiments
Despite methodological critiques of individual studies, randomized experiments have consistently demonstrated systemic sexism in hiring
money_quotes: Physicists are kind of spoiled for data. They often just collect way more data than other people can, and their experiments don't typically affect the results nearly as much, nor are they as messy, as you see in human experiments.
This paper seems to think there's a natural experiment going on, but there almost never is. There are almost always confounding factors.

If we really wanted to say men are less willing to hire women, we'd need to set up a randomized experiment and send a bunch of resumes that differ only in the gender of the applicant, and see what happens next.
Every time a randomized experiment has been performed, to my knowledge, there has been systemic sexism in place.
You can never get a 5-sigma results when your n is small.
At the end of the day math is a community of people, and the sooner you start getting to know the people the better.
posts.key_insight: Observational studies with small samples and confounding factors cannot prove causation, but the consistent results from properly randomized experiments suggest systemic bias is real even when individual studies are methodologically flawed.

posts.summary: The post examines research suggesting brain biomarkers could predict financial risk-taking behavior, with researchers explicitly suggesting policymakers could use existing medical brain scans to characterize populations. O'Neil warns this could lead to discriminatory practices where people are judged by biological markers rather than actual behavior, such as employers screening job applicants based on biomarker-associated health risks. She notes that even inaccurate predictions could be commercially profitable, making the accuracy question somewhat beside the point. She concludes by calling for regulation to ensure such knowledge is used only to help people stay healthy, not to discriminate against them.
key_points: Researchers found a potential brain biomarker for risk attitude and explicitly suggested policymakers could use existing brain scans to categorize populations
Biomarker research is expanding into areas like psychosis and PTSD risk prediction, and will likely become more common
Such data could be used discriminatorily in hiring decisions, judging people by statistical associations rather than individual behavior
Accuracy of predictions is largely irrelevant since even inaccurate biomarker assessments could be profitable for commercial interests
Regulation is needed to ensure biomarker data is used to help people stay healthy rather than to discriminate against them
money_quotes: Not getting hired doesn't say anything about your behaviors, just what happens with 'people like you'.

I'm largely sidestepping the issue of accuracy. It's quite likely that, at an individual level, many such predictions will be inaccurate but could still be used by commercial interests – and even be profitable – even so.
In the best case scenario, we would use such knowledge strictly to help people stay healthy. In the worst case, we have a system whereby people are judged by their biomarkers and not their behavior.
If there were ever a case for regulation, I think this is it.
Studies like this are common and I don't see a reason they won't become even more common. The question is how we're going to use them.
posts.key_insight: Biomarker-based predictions don't need to be accurate to be profitable or harmful—they just need to sort people into categories that benefit commercial interests while penalizing individuals for biological traits rather than behavior.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the polarized landscape of data-driven educational reform, finding herself struggling to write about it without sounding like a conspiracy theorist because the situation is genuinely extreme. She identifies two camps: those who see educational data as a business opportunity and push for charter schools, testing, and anti-union policies while bypassing privacy laws, and those who defend public education and see no proven benefits from the testing regime. While she aligns with the skeptics due to the money, secrecy, and ugly politics surrounding reform, she acknowledges that thoughtful, non-commercial educational experiments with strict privacy protections could theoretically be valuable. The current acrimonious environment makes reasonable progress impossible.
key_points: The data-driven education reform movement has become more about money and politics than actual education since No Child Left Behind
Pro-reform advocates often personally benefit from data collection on children while dismissing privacy concerns
There are no proven benefits of standardized testing, yet it continues to expand
A middle path of careful, non-commercial educational experiments with privacy protections is theoretically possible but practically unimaginable
The polarization between reformers and public education defenders has made reasonable discourse nearly impossible
money_quotes: Things have really changed in the last 13 years, it's really incredible how much money and politics – and not education – are involved.
In fact I'm finding it difficult to write the chapter without sounding like a wingnut conspiracy theorist. Because that's how freaking nuts things are right now.
Because, you know, it's big data, so it has to be awesome.
They see No Child Left Behind and Race To The Top as business opportunities.
There just aren't enough data skeptics in the first group to warrant my respect, and there's way too much money and secrecy around testing and 'value-added models.'
There is a place for testing, but not as a punitive measure but rather as a way of finding where there are problems and devoting resources to it.
The current landscape, however, is so split and so acrimonious, it's kind of impossible to imagine something reasonable happening.
posts.key_insight: The data-driven education reform movement has been captured by commercial interests and political agendas, making it nearly impossible to have a reasonable conversation about how data could actually help students.

posts.summary: The post critiques the NIH's historical exclusion of women from clinical trials, highlighting a recent $10.1 million initiative to address this gender bias. O'Neil deconstructs the reasoning behind male-only studies—that female hormonal cycles add 'variability'—exposing it as treating male bodies as the default while female bodies are treated as noise. She points out the absurdity of optimizing treatments for men while women, who make up over half the population, suffer unknown side effects. The post notes this bias persists even for conditions like migraines where women are the majority of sufferers.
key_points: Medical researchers historically excluded women from clinical trials because hormonal cycles would 'skew' results, implicitly treating male physiology as the baseline
The statistical preference for 'clean data' has real-world consequences: treatments optimized for men may harm or fail women
Even for conditions disproportionately affecting women (like migraines), clinical trials have focused on male subjects
The justification of 'protecting' potentially pregnant women masks a systemic bias in how medical research is designed
The NIH is only now providing extra funding to study women as if they were a 'special group' rather than half the population
money_quotes: If you are afraid that women's data would 'skew study results,' that means you think the 'true result' is the result that works for men.
Adding women's data would add noise to the true signal, that of the men's data. What?!
The downside is that if there is a difference between male and female, they're not going to know about it.
Instead of testing the effect on women, we just go ahead and optimize stuff for men and let women just go ahead and suffer the side effects of the treatment we didn't bother to study.
After all, women only comprise 50.8% of the population, they won't mind.
They've decided to give 'extra money' to study this special group, namely females.
posts.key_insight: The statistical pursuit of 'clean data' by excluding women from clinical trials reveals how methodological choices embed and perpetuate systemic bias, treating half the population as noise rather than signal.

posts.summary: This is an advice column installment where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on topics ranging from racial profiling and marijuana arrests to teaching calculus with limited resources to academic job negotiations. The column opens with a plea for more reader questions. The most substantive exchange addresses a reader who questioned O'Neil's claim about racial bias in marijuana arrests, to which she responds by pointing out the systemic inequalities in policing and privacy that lead to disparate arrest rates. She also discusses teaching strategies for underprepared students and advises an academic on when to mention competing job offers.
key_points: Racial disparities in marijuana arrests reflect systemic racism and unequal policing, not differences in drug use or 'education about hiding drugs'
White suburban kids have privacy to smoke pot at home while Black kids often must use public spaces, leading to higher arrest exposure
The anecdote about Stuyvesant parents negotiating with cops to avoid arrests for their kids illustrates how privilege operates in the justice system
For teaching underprepared students, interactive problem-solving during class time is more valuable than pure lecturing
In academic job negotiations, don't mention competing offers until they're firm
money_quotes: Clearly black kids are much more educated about cops than the average white kids. Are you kidding?
White kids smoke pot in their own rooms in suburbia, and it's harder for them to get caught.
Imagine that deal being made in Harlem.
I wish more things were that informal combined with great.
Keep in mind that the quiet ones will probably be the ones that feel most behind, so make sure you don't just listen to the loud people!
posts.key_insight: Racial disparities in arrest rates are not neutral facts but consequences of systemic inequalities in privacy, policing, and privilege that algorithms would incorrectly treat as natural patterns.

posts.summary: O'Neil highlights Paul Pfleiderer's concept of 'chameleon models' - theoretical models in finance and economics that shift between being presented as abstract theory and being used to make real-world policy claims. She notes that models aren't inherently harmful but become dangerous when weaponized politically. She partially pushes back on Milton Friedman's view that models should be judged by predictions rather than assumptions, arguing that stakes matter. She applies this framework to education policy, suggesting even flawed models like value-added teacher assessments could theoretically be used constructively.
key_points: Chameleon models are theoretical constructs that get misused by shifting between abstract theory and concrete policy justification
Whether to judge models by assumptions versus predictions depends on the stakes involved
Most models are harmless until deployed as political weapons
Even problematic models like value-added teacher assessments could theoretically serve constructive purposes like identifying schools needing support
Current education data is distorted by teaching to the test and cheating, making signals unreliable
money_quotes: Most models are harmless until they are used as political weapons.
The larger the stakes, the more the assumptions matter.

Even the value-added teacher model could be used to identify school systems that need support, although in the current climate of distorted data due to teaching to the test and cheating, I think the signal is probably very slight.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical models become dangerous not through their construction but through their political deployment, and the higher the stakes, the more we must scrutinize their underlying assumptions.

posts.summary: O'Neil responds to personal attacks she's received as a podcast host by contextualizing them within broader patterns of gendered criticism. She cites research showing 76% of negative feedback to women includes personality criticism versus 2% for men. Rather than being deterred by anonymous attacks calling her 'annoying' or 'dumb,' she interprets vague character attacks as sexism and reframes hostility as evidence she's reaching beyond her echo chamber. She concludes by acknowledging the privilege of being able to withstand public hatred without professional or personal risk.
key_points: Women receive personality-based criticism at vastly disproportionate rates compared to men (76% vs 2%)
Vague personal attacks without constructive specifics are best dismissed as sexism rather than legitimate feedback
Provoking negative reactions indicates ideas are reaching audiences beyond those who already agree
Being able to withstand public hatred without fear is a privilege not all women share
Thick skin develops through experience in male-dominated fields and public-facing work
money_quotes: I know that it's not really about me when people anonymously complain that I'm annoying or dumb.
When I see something like that, which isn't a specific criticism that might help me get better but is rather a vague attack on my character, I immediately discount it as sexism if not misogyny.
When people hate me, I feel like I must be doing something right.
If that guy hates me then maybe 100 other people are listening and not quite hating me. They might even be agreeing with me.
It is a luxury indeed to be a woman who can afford to be hated.
I don't need to bother myself about getting them to like me.
Let's have ideas and argue about them and disagree! It's what freedom is all about.
posts.key_insight: Vague personal attacks on outspoken women are usually sexism in disguise, and provoking hostility often signals that challenging ideas are reaching beyond a sympathetic audience.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil expresses surprise and delight at the quality of the latest Notices of the AMS, which she typically views as narrowly focused. She highlights two articles: one demonstrating that the h-index is essentially just the square root of total citations, and another exposing mathematical flaws in a widely-cited social science paper about diversity. She praises the publication for expanding to address broader public issues relevant to mathematicians.
key_points: The h-index, a popular academic metric, is mathematically just a simple transformation (square root) of total citations
A widely-cited social science paper on diversity was critiqued for mathematical vapidness by mathematician Abby Thompson
The Notices of the AMS appears to be broadening its scope to address public issues that intersect with mathematics
People often embrace new metrics that are merely repackaged versions of old, discarded ones
money_quotes: People often fall in love with new fancy metrics that turn out to be simple transformations of old discarded metrics.
There's an article that explains the mathematical vapidness of a widely cited social science paper.
Generally speaking I think of it as rather narrowly focused but things seem to be expanding and picking up.
posts.key_insight: Academic metrics and social science claims deserve mathematical scrutiny, and it's encouraging when the mathematics community takes on this critical role.

posts.summary: O'Neil recommends the website StemFeminist.com, which collects anonymous stories about everyday sexism in STEM fields. She notes that the stories range from seemingly minor incidents to outrageous, actionable ones, and many resonate with her own experiences or those of her friends. She argues the site serves as valuable evidence when men question the level of sexism in STEM, expressing disbelief that such issues persist in 2014.
key_points: StemFeminist.com aggregates anonymous accounts of everyday sexism in STEM fields
The experiences range widely in severity, from subtle to clearly actionable
The site provides concrete evidence to counter skepticism about sexism in STEM
Many stories resonate with O'Neil's personal and secondhand experiences
money_quotes: It consists of a bunch of anonymously contributed stories, most but not all by women, about everyday sexism that happens in the STEM fields.
It's a great idea to have this, if just for women to be able to point to when men question the level of sexism in STEM fields.
Sometimes it's hard to believe it's 2014.
posts.key_insight: Documenting everyday sexism in STEM provides necessary evidence against those who downplay or deny its prevalence.

posts.summary: This brief post warns readers to be skeptical of Ello, a social media platform marketed as a privacy-friendly alternative. O'Neil directs readers to journalist Meredith Broussard's analysis of Ello's security policy, which reveals concerning fine print. The post serves as a quick cautionary note about trusting new platforms' privacy claims at face value.
key_points: Ello's reputation as a 'safe' social media platform may be undeserved
The fine print in Ello's security policy contains troubling provisions
Users should read and understand platform security policies before trusting them with their data
money_quotes: If you think Ello is the newest safest social media platform, you might want to think again.
posts.key_insight: Privacy-focused marketing claims from new social media platforms deserve skeptical scrutiny of their actual policies.

posts.summary: This is an advice column covering four reader questions: navigating monogamy conflicts in relationships, an app helping low-income Americans access food stamps, combating sexism in academic STEM environments, and the limitations of dating site algorithms. The author provides characteristically blunt advice while weaving in observations about Silicon Valley privilege, systemic sexism in academia, and how dating algorithms ask the wrong questions. The column showcases the author's signature style of mixing personal warmth with sharp social critique.
key_points: Sexual compatibility issues in relationships tend to resolve themselves one way or another - suppressing fundamental desires is an unstable equilibrium
Silicon Valley's focus on trivial convenience apps for the wealthy contrasts sharply with apps that could genuinely help low-income people navigate bureaucratic systems
Women in male-dominated academic fields routinely have their ideas credited to male peers, requiring explicit correction and coalition-building to combat
Dating algorithms focus on superficial categorical matches rather than meaningful compatibility, asking the wrong questions entirely
Cultural practices around crediting ideas are deeply sexist and require active intervention to change
money_quotes: I think I heard him describe it as 'solving the most important problem of my life.' Which says a lot about these guys' problems.
It's one reason I like to focus on ideas rather than the so-called 'owners' of these ideas, because I know that, behind the curtain there could very well be an uncredited woman.
When we were 18 we wouldn't let 'she is allergic to dogs' be the reason we were separated from our true love. We wouldn't give two shits about dog allergies, in fact.
The shitty irrelevant data which comes out of those wrong questions get us all confused about what's important to us.
After all, what was all that progress we made in the last 50 years for if not the rights of the slutty women to go be sluts?

This is exactly what I am always explaining to people when they argue against my 'Great Men With Big Ideas' rant, whereby I complain that people who explain the history of ideas in terms of Great Men With Big Ideas are using a narrative crutch which is both sexist and inaccurate.
posts.key_insight: Whether in dating apps, academic credit, or tech innovation, the systems we build tend to encode existing biases and ask the wrong questions, obscuring what actually matters.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques the Koch brothers' sophisticated information control strategy, where they've shifted from openly libertarian rhetoric to philanthropic 'window dressing' while continuing harmful practices. She connects this to Detroit's water crisis, where 15,000 residents had water shut off to facilitate privatization. The post warns that private 'solutions' to public crises allow wealthy individuals to accumulate control over essential infrastructure while being celebrated as heroes. O'Neil argues this represents a dangerous asymptotic limit where intermittent crises become opportunities for the rich to consolidate power over public resources.
key_points: The Koch brothers changed their public rhetoric to pro-environment messaging while continuing to face pollution violations, demonstrating how wealth enables information control
Out-of-court settlements for corporate misdeeds prevent investigative journalism and public accountability—we only learn details when cases become public record
Detroit's water shutoffs affect 15,000 residents owing an average of $540, done to privatize the water system with favorable Wall Street terms
Private philanthropy solving public crises creates a dangerous dynamic where the wealthy accumulate infrastructure ownership while being celebrated as saviors
The UN declared Detroit's water shutoffs a human rights violation, yet no federal emergency was declared—suggesting poor victims are treated differently
money_quotes: It's not that they stopped polluting, it's that their rhetoric changed.
There's no way we can keep a score sheet with 'good deeds' on one side and 'shitty deeds' on the other. We don't have enough information for the second side.
The United Nations has called this water shutoff a violation of the human rights of the people of Detroit.
Wouldn't we be doing that in another city that had 15,000 residents without water? Why is this an exception to that rule? Because the victims are poor?

You can almost imagine David Koch coming to the rescue here, as part of his 'positive optics' campaign, and bailing out the Detroit citizens and then, for good measure, buying up the water system altogether. A hero!

A few very rich people gradually take control of resources, and then there are intermittent famines of various types in different cities, and the rich people swoop in and heroically save the day whilst scooping up even more ownership of what used to be public infrastructure.
It's frustrating to live in a country that has so many resources but which can't seem to get it together to meet the basic human needs of its citizens.
posts.key_insight: When private philanthropy replaces public responsibility for basic needs like water, it creates a cycle where crises become opportunities for the wealthy to accumulate power while being celebrated as saviors.

posts.summary: This brief post announces O'Neil's intention to combine two topics she's been meaning to write about: the Carmen Segarra whistleblower recordings from the NY Fed and the fairness of workplace personality tests. She creates a satirical 'mash-up' imagining what personality test Segarra might have faced when applying for her job at the Federal Reserve. The post is essentially a teaser linking to a satirical Google Forms personality test O'Neil created.
key_points: The post references Carmen Segarra's leaked recordings exposing regulatory capture at the NY Fed
O'Neil connects concerns about workplace personality tests to the broader culture of regulatory agencies
The satirical test format suggests personality screening may filter out exactly the kind of independent thinkers regulators need
money_quotes: I've decided to do a mash-up just for fun, imagining what Carmen had to go through to get her job.
posts.key_insight: The culture and hiring practices of financial regulators may systematically select against the independent-minded people needed to actually hold banks accountable.

posts.summary: The post highlights a Harvard research paper that experimentally studied Chinese social media censorship by creating accounts, submitting test posts, and observing which were removed. The researchers even contracted with Chinese firms to install censoring technology on their own site, allowing them to reverse-engineer how it works. Their key finding was that criticism of government leaders is allowed, but posts about events that could spark collective action are censored. O'Neil notes the irony of getting help from Chinese firms to study censorship while acknowledging a sociologist's caveat that local officials' personal interests in maintaining stability may matter more than coherent central policy.
key_points: Researchers conducted the first large-scale experimental study of Chinese censorship by creating social media accounts and randomly submitting different texts
They reverse-engineered censorship technology by contracting with Chinese firms to install the same systems on their own site
The study found that criticism of the state is permitted, but posts with 'collective action potential' are censored
A caveat notes that local officials' personal interests in suppressing social movements may be more important than central government policy
money_quotes: Criticisms of the state, its leaders, and their policies are published, whereas posts about real-world events with collective action potential are censored
Interesting that they got so much help from the Chinese to censor their posts
I'm a sucker for reverse-engineering powerful algorithms, even when there are major caveats
Local officials have a personal interest in suppressing content that could lead to social movements
posts.key_insight: Chinese censorship prioritizes preventing collective action over suppressing criticism of the government itself.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques a panel of male tech executives at the Grace Hopper Celebration whose advice for women was predictably unhelpful ('lean in,' 'speak up'). She argues that being a true male ally requires two fundamental steps: first, actually believing women when they report experiencing sexism, and second, actively intervening when witnessing sexist behavior rather than leaving women to handle it alone. She shares her frustration that even men close to her have dismissed her own experiences of sexism, illustrating how difficult the first step proves to be.
key_points: Corporate male allies at tech conferences offer hollow, predictable advice that places the burden on women to change their behavior
The most basic requirement for male allyship is simply believing women when they describe experiencing sexism
Even well-meaning men often fail this basic test, dismissing women's reported experiences
True allyship requires men to actively intervene when they witness sexism, not leave women to confront it alone
Acknowledging sexism requires empathy and will lead to disappointment in the world, but that's the reality
money_quotes: The advice was stale and trite and included things like 'speak up,' 'lean in,' and 'get excited about your ideas like men do.'
Trust women who say they experience sexism.
When I repeated this to a couple of them, they actually said that I didn't experience the stuff that I had.
