key_points: Scientific progress can be tracked by who gets to name discoveries, as naming follows discovery
Islamic civilization led mathematical and scientific advancement from 800-1100 AD before religious opposition halted progress
Half of Americans don't acknowledge evolution, which Tyson sees as a warning sign for the country's future
Unlike Europe and Japan, America's scientific power is shrinking rather than expanding
Space flight should be reinvested in as an inspiration for scientific curiosity
money_quotes: You can follow scientific progress by who gets to name things, because naming follows discovery.
Then some imam decided it was anti-religious to do anything like that, and progress – scientific and otherwise – stopped.
Cultures that embrace science have more growth.
In the U.S., about half of the people don't acknowledge evolution, and that's a bad sign for our future.
We are a hugely prolific scientific force, like Europe and Japan, but unlike them, our power is shrinking rather than expanding.
Childlike and insatiable curiosity and wonderment is the goal.
posts.key_insight: A society's embrace or rejection of science—visible through who names discoveries and whether evolution is accepted—directly predicts its cultural and economic trajectory.

posts.summary: This is a brief, emotionally charged post responding to the Eric Garner grand jury decision in Staten Island. O'Neil expresses her reaction by sharing Nina Simone's protest song 'Mississippi Goddam,' drawing a parallel between civil rights struggles of the 1960s and the 2014 police brutality case. The post's brevity itself is a statement—words feel inadequate in the face of injustice. By letting the song speak for her, she invokes a long history of Black American protest and grief.
key_points: The post responds to the Staten Island grand jury's decision not to indict in Eric Garner's death
O'Neil uses Nina Simone's 'Mississippi Goddam' as her sole commentary, connecting historical and contemporary racial injustice
The deliberate brevity suggests that some injustices leave one speechless
The title adaptation ('Staten Island Goddam') updates Simone's protest for 2014
money_quotes: This is all I got this morning
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most powerful response to injustice is to step aside and let decades of protest music speak the unspeakable.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on the Eric Garner case and finds hope in the widespread, multiracial outrage it has sparked among white Americans. She argues this represents 'white progress' - white people increasingly recognizing and speaking out against racial injustice. The post also grapples with the terminology of 'white privilege,' suggesting that many so-called privileges are actually basic rights that everyone deserves but not everyone receives. She proposes 'white entitlement' as an alternative term, while acknowledging the political baggage of that word. The #crimingwhilewhite hashtag is cited as evidence of white people confronting their unearned advantages.
key_points: The multiracial protests and white outrage over Eric Garner represent meaningful 'white progress' in racial awareness
'White privilege' is often a misnomer - what white people have are rights that everyone should have but doesn't
The security of not fearing police violence for one's children should be a universal right, not a privilege
The #crimingwhilewhite hashtag demonstrates genuine white privilege - getting away with crimes that would harm Black people
Finding hope requires reframing: racial injustice is no longer seen as 'a black person's problem'
money_quotes: It's exposing white progress, and it's been a long time coming.
It is not a black person's problem anymore.
For the most part, 'white privilege' really refers to the rights white people have, which everyone should have, but which not everyone has.
It is my white privilege not to worry about my three sons getting shot by the police. But that's not a privilege, it's a right.
I'm entitled to that security. Everyone is, but not everyone gets to have it.
I'm looking for a way to be proud to live in this country, and white progress might be the way I can do it.
posts.key_insight: What we call 'white privilege' is often just basic rights that everyone deserves but only white people reliably receive - reframing the conversation around universal rights rather than special privileges.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses four reader questions covering math education policy, relationship negotiations around fidelity, supporting women in STEM fields, and dealing with difficult PhD advisors. Cathy O'Neil (as 'Aunt Pythia') brings her characteristic directness to each problem, emphasizing practical solutions over abstract principles. On algebra requirements, she questions rigid definitions and prioritizes statistical literacy for real-world health decisions. On relationships, she advocates for equity in negotiations rather than absolute rules. She encourages a mother to help her daughter find supportive math environments and urges a frustrated PhD student to actively seek a new advisor.
key_points: The definition of 'algebra' is contested, and what matters more than solving quadratic equations is the ability to critically evaluate statistical claims about health and research
Successful marriages don't follow universal rules but negotiate arrangements that work for both partners, with equity being essential to avoid resentment
Women entering math and CS often lack the proof-writing background their male peers gained from math camps, requiring supportive introductory courses
PhD students must advocate for themselves and shouldn't hesitate to change advisors when the relationship isn't working
money_quotes: I'm probably someone who would rather see people be able to read, understand, and dissect statistical statements about medical research than solve the quadratic equation from scratch
There is no absolute answer, there is only the negotiation you come up with with your partner. And the definition of 'it's working' is 'it's working for us.'
Resentment kills relationships

Most male math majors came in with lots of experience from high school math camps and math competitions in how to write proofs, but most women interested in math came in just interested and excited about math, but very little background in writing proofs
This is your life and your career, you have to advocate for yourself. Don't give up before you've tried everything.
posts.key_insight: Whether in education, relationships, or career decisions, rigid rules matter less than pragmatic negotiations that account for real-world circumstances and power dynamics.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her participation in the FATML (Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency in Machine Learning) workshop at NIPS in Montreal. She will be on a closing panel discussing real-world examples where fairness and accountability in data-driven decisions were relevant. Upon returning, she plans to join the Eric Garner protest at Washington Square Park, connecting her academic work on algorithmic fairness to street-level activism against police violence.
key_points: FATML workshop at NIPS conference addresses fairness, accountability, and transparency in machine learning
Panel discussion will focus on real-world examples where data-driven decisions raised fairness concerns
O'Neil draws an implicit connection between algorithmic accountability and civil rights activism
The timing juxtaposes academic discourse on fairness with grassroots protests against systemic injustice
money_quotes: On the panel, we will be discussing examples of data driven projects and decisions where fairness, accountability, and transparency came into play, or should have.
I've got lots!
posts.key_insight: Academic work on algorithmic fairness and street activism against police violence are connected struggles for accountability and justice.

posts.summary: The post argues that while the Eric Garner case demonstrates body cameras aren't a magic solution to police brutality, they remain valuable tools for building evidence and shifting public opinion. O'Neil contends that video footage should be viewed as systematic data collection that enables comparison to baselines of 'normal police behavior.' She draws an analogy to marketing data collection, noting that understanding patterns across many incidents is more valuable than any single case. The post also highlights a fundamental power imbalance: data is typically collected on the powerless while the powerful remain opaque. O'Neil advocates for more surveillance of those in power—police, corporations, politicians—while acknowledging the challenges of ensuring public access to such footage.
key_points: A single number or video is meaningless without a baseline for comparison—we need aggregate data on police behavior across precincts, situations, and time periods
The Eric Garner video already achieved progress by galvanizing nationwide protests and shifting public opinion, even without an indictment
Data collection typically targets the powerless; we should flip this dynamic and collect more data on police, corporations, and politicians
Video evidence is vulnerable to manipulation through selective recording—officers turning cameras off at critical moments undermines the system
Body cameras should be viewed as evidence-collecting devices for public discussion, not as a panacea that will solve problems overnight
money_quotes: I am no data evangelist, but neither am I someone who dismisses data. It can be powerful and we should use its power when we can.
A number by itself is usually meaningless. What we need is to compare that number to a baseline.

In general data is being collected on the powerless, on the consumers, on the citizens, or the job applicants, and we should be pushing for more and better data to be collected instead on the powerful, on the police, on the corporations, and on the politicians.
There's a reason there is a burgeoning privacy industry for rich and powerful people.
We should think it as a mere evidence collecting device, and use it in the public discussion of how the most powerful among us treat the least powerful.
We want to see the many examples of good and great policing as well as the few examples of terrible, escalating policing.
posts.key_insight: Body cameras should be understood not as accountability silver bullets but as systematic data collection tools that derive their power from enabling comparisons across many incidents, shifting the surveillance paradigm to focus on the powerful rather than the powerless.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil alerts readers to last-minute political maneuvering to roll back Dodd-Frank financial regulations as part of budget negotiations. She highlights that politicians are exploiting end-of-year distractions to sneak through provisions benefiting Wall Street banks. The post promotes an Occupy the SEC petition opposing these changes, referencing the group's previous effective work on Dodd-Frank public comments. It's a brief call to action urging readers to sign the petition and resist deregulation.
key_points: Politicians are using end-of-year budget negotiations to quietly roll back Dodd-Frank protections
The proposed changes would give banks government backing for risky trades
Occupy the SEC, known for effective regulatory advocacy, is organizing opposition through a petition
The timing is deliberately chosen to exploit public distraction during the holiday season
money_quotes: There's some tricky business going on right now in politics, with a bunch of ridiculous last-minute negotiations to roll back elements of Dodd-Frank and aid Wall Street banks in the current budget deal.
Hell, it's the end of the year, and people are distracted, so the public won't mind if the banks get formal government backing for their risky trades, right?
posts.key_insight: Politicians exploit public distraction during holiday budget negotiations to slip through Wall Street-friendly deregulation.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses three main topics: critiquing a New York Times op-ed claiming academic science isn't sexist, responding to frustration about gendered language in academic titles, and giving tough love to a student seeking grad school recommendations. Cathy O'Neil dismantles the sexism denial article by pointing out that unreported sexism doesn't mean nonexistent sexism, comparing the data collection problem to police shootings. She validates a reader's frustration about headlines like 'Women Are Different' that assume male as default baseline. She also calls out a struggling student for self-delusion about their mathematical abilities and social difficulties.
key_points: Data about sexism is incomplete because most sexist experiences are never recorded in any database - absence of data is not evidence of absence
The word 'different' forces comparison to an assumed baseline, and when that baseline is unstated, it defaults to white male as cultural norm
Women who survive academia's selection process should theoretically outperform male peers, so equal promotion rates actually indicate bias
Math is a social endeavor requiring communication - you can't be a mathematician in isolation without others understanding your ideas
Sexual preferences in private contexts have no bearing on professional identity - many powerful people enjoy submission in intimate settings
money_quotes: It's famously hard to prove something doesn't exist, especially when you don't have a search algorithm.
A common mistake researchers make. They take the data as 'objective truth' and forget that it's a human process to collect it (or not collect it!). Think police shootings.

The women have already gone through a mighty selection process, so in general you'd expect them to be smarter than their colleagues, so in general their promotion rates should be higher, but they aren't.
The word 'different' forces us to compare something to a baseline, and given that there is no baseline even mentioned, we are forced to guess at it, and that imposes the 'man as default' mindset.
We don't entitle a blog post 'Whites are different' and leave it at that, because we'd be like, different from whom?
Math is a social endeavor, where you have to communicate your ideas well enough for others to understand them, or else you aren't doing math.
It is absolutely not in conflict to want something in a sexual context that is abhorrent to you in normal life. It is in fact a well-known pattern!
posts.key_insight: The absence of data about sexism reflects failures in data collection, not the absence of sexism itself - a principle applicable to any domain where lived experiences go unrecorded.

posts.summary: O'Neil briefly highlights Elizabeth Warren's speech criticizing Citigroup, praising the senator for presenting complex financial facts in a clear, accessible manner. She finds Warren's straightforward approach refreshing and surprising for a politician. The post serves primarily as a recommendation to watch the speech rather than providing detailed analysis.
key_points: Elizabeth Warren delivered a notable speech about Citigroup that deserves attention
Warren presented financial facts in an uncomplicated, accessible way
Such clarity from politicians is rare enough to be noteworthy
money_quotes: She lays out the facts about Citigroup in an uncomplicated way.
Surprising and refreshing coming from a politician.
posts.key_insight: Politicians who communicate complex financial issues clearly and directly are rare enough to warrant special attention.

posts.summary: O'Neil reports on the FATML workshop at NIPS 2014, one of the first gatherings of computer scientists, lawyers, and policy experts addressing fairness in machine learning. The workshop covered topics including Google's ad targeting discrimination, methods for making algorithms accountable through encrypted audit trails, and techniques for achieving fairness through category-aware ranking. Researchers found that Google showed different job ads to men versus women and that advertiser targeting profiles include variables users cannot see or modify. The day concluded with debates about whether credit scoring models should be open source and how to communicate algorithmic harms to policymakers through compelling stories.
key_points: Research showed Google displayed drastically different job ads to fake 'male' versus 'female' profiles, with men seeing far more ads for $200K+ positions
Google's ad profiles contain targeting variables like marital status that users cannot see or modify, representing a transparency failure
One proposed fairness method involves ranking people within demographic categories by percentile, then using only that percentile—essentially a mathematical formalization of affirmative action
Encrypted audit trail systems could verify that algorithms are applied consistently to everyone, addressing 'procedural fairness'
Algorithmic experiments face a fundamental challenge: since platforms constantly update their systems, results may be evanescent and hard to replicate
money_quotes: This is what progress feels like!
We certainly can't count on social scientists to be on hand when important mathematical models are getting ready for production.
In your advertising profile, for example, you cannot see (and therefore cannot change) your marriage status, but advertisers can target you based on that variable.
If we know women are systemically undervalued, we have to fight against it somehow, and this seems like the best and simplest approach.
To combat cherry-picked examples we see in Congressional Subcommittee meetings, we need to have cherry-picked examples of our own to illustrate what can go wrong.
People respond to stories, especially to stories with innocent victims that have been wronged.
posts.key_insight: The emerging field of algorithmic fairness requires collaboration across disciplines—computer science, law, and policy—because technical solutions alone cannot address the social and political dimensions of how algorithms affect people's lives.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines two contrasting visions for data-driven governance prompted by the Cromnibus spending bill controversy. She dismisses the Bloomberg proposal for using data to evaluate policy effectiveness, citing concerns about how data production and success metrics are politically manipulated. Instead, she champions a reader's suggestion for transparent, detailed breakdowns of spending bills that would reveal allocations, sponsors, voting histories, and lobbyist connections. She sees this transparency-focused approach as a worthy goal for emerging data journalism.
key_points: Using data to evaluate policy effectiveness is problematic because data production and success definitions are politically manipulated
True data-driven governance should focus on transparency: showing who put what in bills, how much money goes where, and which lobbyists pushed for it
An ideal system would provide interactive tools showing spending allocations, legislative history, and the money trail behind each provision
Data journalism is an emerging field that could eventually deliver this kind of democratic transparency
money_quotes: Given what I know about how data is produced, and how definitions of success are politically manipulated, I don't have much hope for this idea.

What if, when Congress gave us a shitty bill, we could see stuff like: how much money is allocated to each thing, both raw dollars and as a percentage of the whole bill, who put it in the omnibus bill, the history of that proposed spending, and the history of voting, which lobbyists were pushing it, and who gets paid by them
That's the kind of data that I'd love to see.
Data journalism is an emerging field, and we might not be there yet, but it's something to strive for.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable application of data in politics isn't evaluating whether policies 'work' but exposing who benefits from them and how the sausage gets made.

posts.summary: This brief post shares Cleveland Browns player Andrew Hawkins' powerful statement about justice following protests over Tamir Rice's death, using it as inspiration for direct action. O'Neil endorses Hawkins' call that seeking justice shouldn't require apology or be seen as offensive. She announces her own planned protest at Citigroup headquarters, connecting the broader justice movement to Elizabeth Warren's critique of Wall Street from the previous week. The post links racial justice concerns with financial accountability activism.
key_points: Andrew Hawkins articulated that justice means fair treatment and the innocent being found innocent, and calling for it shouldn't offend anyone
O'Neil connects racial justice protests (Tamir Rice) with financial accountability activism (Occupy Citibank)
The post serves as a call to action, encouraging readers to stand up for causes they believe in
Warren's speech criticizing Wall Street provides additional inspiration for the Citigroup protest
money_quotes: I was taught that justice is a right that every American should have.
A call for justice shouldn't offend or disrespect anybody. A call for justice shouldn't warrant an apology.
Stand up for them. Speak up for them. No matter what it is because that's what America's about and that's what this country was founded on.
posts.key_insight: Demanding justice—whether for police violence victims or financial system accountability—is a fundamental American right that requires no apology.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques a Wired article that promised big data solutions for the wage gap but delivered only vague platitudes. The piece, written by a Raytheon executive, suggests using analytics to understand why women leave companies and tracking their social media posts, but offers no concrete plan. O'Neil argues that what actually combats sexism is transparent, measurable job requirements—as demonstrated by blind orchestra auditions—rather than fuzzy appeals to data. She points out that corporations hide bias behind unmeasurable criteria like 'cultural fit' and 'leadership qualities' that mysteriously favor men.
key_points: The Wired article promises a big data solution to the wage gap but delivers only vague suggestions about collecting data without explaining what to do with it
Simply having more data doesn't eliminate bias—the assumption that 'data is objective' ignores how bias gets baked into models and measurements
Real progress requires transparent, measurable job requirements, as proven by blind orchestra auditions which increased gender diversity
Corporate hiring often relies on unmeasurable criteria like 'good cultural fit' and 'leadership qualities' that systematically disadvantage women
O'Neil proposes a specific alternative: audit whether stated promotion criteria match actual promotion decisions and examine whether those criteria are inherently biased
money_quotes: Wage Gaps Don't Magically Get Smaller Because Big Data
That plan doesn't seem worthy of the title of the piece. It's super vague and fluffy and meaningless.

Ask people in your corporate structure what the actual characteristics are of people they promote, and how they are measured, or if they are measured, and look at the data to see if what they say is consistent with what they do, and whether those characteristics are inherently sexist.
What combats sexism is a clarification and transparent description of job requirements and a willingness to follow through.

My experience with the corporate world is that, when hiring or promoting, they often list a long series of unmeasurable but critical properties like 'good cultural fit' and 'leadership qualities' that, for whatever reason, more men are rated high on than women.
It's a very specific plan and no fancy mathematical techniques are necessary, but we don't have to tell anyone that.
posts.key_insight: Big data doesn't solve discrimination—transparent, measurable criteria and accountability do.

posts.summary: This advice column features Aunt Pythia (Cathy O'Neil) responding to four reader questions covering career transitions into math, managing workplace crushes while married, digital decluttering, and a math major's crisis of confidence. The column opens with a humorous plea for more sex-related questions. O'Neil encourages a 29-year-old considering applied math to 'take the fuck out of available math classes' while noting it's never been a better time to learn math with online resources. She reframes adult crushes as 'the super glue of post-college friendship' - a quick-bonding mechanism for busy adults. For the anxious math junior, she advises shedding the weight of representing an entire demographic and seeking counseling to explore options.
key_points: Career changers into math should consume available courses voraciously, as resources have never been better, though entering math late is unusual
Adult crushes serve a functional purpose as quick-bonding mechanisms that help busy adults form meaningful friendships they otherwise wouldn't have time to develop
Being a 'math nerd in an MBA program' is valuable as long as you're not an 'MBA asshole' - math is the heavy lifting tool for all fields
Students struggling with motivation shouldn't carry the extra weight of representing their demographic - live your own unrepeatable life
Digital clutter is a non-problem since data storage keeps getting cheaper - just buy modern hard disks and stop overthinking organization
money_quotes: Basically, take the fuck out of the available math classes and learn some good shit.
Math is a field in its own right, of course, which I hope you find your way into if that's your thing, but it's also the major heavy lifting tool for all other fields.
Being a math nerd in an MBA program is still a good and useful thing, especially if you're not an MBA asshole.
Think of adult crushes as a way for your body to force you to make friends with people when you're busy.
Enter the adult crush. It's a quick-bonding mechanism. Think of it as the super glue of post-college friendship.
You are not living The Life Of The Upper Middle Class Latino. You are living your own, personal, never-to-be-repeated life, and you gotta figure out how you want to live it.
It's a great sign that you once were passionate about calculus. You have the talent and ability to master a difficult subject when the moment is right.
posts.key_insight: Whether navigating career changes, relationships, or identity pressures, the key is to shed external expectations and pursue what genuinely engages you with voracious curiosity.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the mortgage tax deduction—America's largest individual tax break—and argues it accelerates gentrification rather than promoting homeownership. She models how the deduction inflates housing prices differently based on income brackets, creating a dynamic where wealthier buyers can more easily afford borderline properties. Since the tax benefit scales with income, the same house effectively costs more for poorer buyers, giving richer neighborhoods a systematic advantage in expansion. She concludes the deduction should be slowly phased out, starting with freezing the cap to let inflation erode it over time.
key_points: International comparisons show the mortgage tax deduction doesn't actually increase homeownership rates
Housing prices inflate to absorb tax deductions, but the inflation varies by neighborhood income level
Border properties between income zones become systematically underpriced for wealthier buyers, mechanically driving gentrification
Home builders are incentivized to construct for the wealthy to maximize the markup from tax deduction inflation
The same house at the same price costs more for poorer buyers because the deduction doesn't affect everyone equally
money_quotes: It's the single biggest tax deduction in America for individuals.
Namely, the prices go up to compensate. It's kind of like a rebate: this house is $100K with no deduction, but with a $20K deduction I can charge $120K for it.
As a group neighborhoods, and their associated classes, represent a dynamical system, where certain kinds of neighborhoods expand or contract.
In other words, it's under-priced from the perspective of the richer neighborhood. They will have an easier time affording it.
The same house at the same price is more expensive for a poorer person because the mortgage tax deduction doesn't affect everyone equally.
If I'm a home builder, I will want to build homes with a maximal mark-up, a maximal inflation level.
posts.key_insight: The mortgage tax deduction creates a hidden mechanism that systematically advantages wealthier buyers in border neighborhoods, making gentrification not just a social phenomenon but a mathematically predictable outcome of tax policy.

posts.summary: O'Neil humorously recounts the experience of family vacations causing a complete loss of personal identity and physical desire. Using the metaphor of 'losing her genitals,' she describes how the chaos of traveling with children—screaming kids at waterparks, terrible food choices dictated by picky eaters, and shared hotel rooms—eliminates any sense of adult pleasure or sexuality. The post satirically suggests family vacations should be mandatory for anyone considering pregnancy as the ultimate deterrent. She concludes with a self-deprecating plea for compassion from readers while maintaining her comedic tone throughout.
key_points: Family vacations with children completely eliminate adult sexuality and personal identity
The chaos of child-focused environments (waterparks, hotels) makes it impossible to feel like anything other than a parent
Food choices on family trips are dictated by the pickiest eater, leading to unhealthy and depressing meals
The cost-benefit analysis of parenting always favors giving in to children's demands to avoid meltdowns
New Year's diet resolutions stem from holiday food disgust rather than weight concerns
money_quotes: Conclusion: family vacations are the ultimate birth control.

Honestly, there should be a law that anyone who is thinking of getting pregnant should spend a couple of days at Great Wolf Lodge. If they are still horny after that experience then they deserve whatever they get.
Why, oh why, did we decide to have so many kids? And how can it be so incredibly expensive to pay for them to complain about every moment of the day that doesn't contain wifi?
The disgusting food you end up eating when with your entire family prevents you from feeling sexy. Never mind sexy, it makes you borderline suicidal.
It's not that you want to lose weight, it's that you never want to eat again.
It's a cost benefit analysis, and the costs always outweigh the benefits.
posts.key_insight: The relentless demands of family vacations—catering to children's needs, eating terrible food, and losing all personal space—temporarily erase adult identity and desire entirely.

posts.summary: O'Neil responds to a debate about male nerd privilege sparked by Scott Aaronson's comments, agreeing with Laurie Penny that nerd suffering doesn't cancel out male privilege, but criticizing the essay's approach. She argues that competitive suffering comparisons are unproductive and that Penny fails to acknowledge her own privileges as a white nerd with good job prospects. O'Neil advocates for reframing privilege discussions in aspirational terms focused on basic human rights and fairness, rather than shaming. She emphasizes that addressing sexism in STEM requires team effort since both men and women exhibit bias, making it a cultural problem rather than a blame game.
key_points: Competitive suffering comparisons are counterproductive because most people have experienced misery in some form
Acknowledging privilege should include all dimensions—Penny doesn't mention that nerds get good jobs or that white people receive systemic advantages
Naming privilege can feel like shaming; an aspirational framing focused on basic human rights might be more effective
Sexism in STEM is a cultural problem where both men and women exhibit bias, requiring collaborative solutions rather than blame
Being able to discuss privilege at all is itself a privilege when others worry about basic survival
money_quotes: We can't actually win when we count up all the ways we were miserable.
At the end of the day, we are privileged to be sitting around talking about privilege. We are not worried about dying of hunger or exposure.
When Aaronson complained that naming male privilege is shaming, I'm prone to agree, at least if it's done like this.
What I'd propose is to figure out a way to talk about these structural problems in an aspirational way.
We've already studied this and we all know that both men and women are sexist, so it's obviously not a blame game here.
Physics is a social community activity, after all, just like mathematics, and it is important to feel safe doing physics in that community.
We need to sympathize, not only with our former selves, but with other people.
posts.key_insight: Privilege discussions become more productive when framed aspirationally around shared human rights rather than as competitive suffering or shame-based callouts.

posts.summary: This advice column covers several topics: a reader grappling with their implicit bias after being surprised that a math blogger was an African-American woman, another seeking advice on managing frequent crushes while in a committed relationship, a brief exchange on sex tourism and legalized prostitution, and thoughts on gender segregation in sports. Aunt Pythia advises the first reader to openly admit their prejudice as a first step toward addressing it, and encourages the second to embrace their crushes rather than suppress them. The column also critiques American football as a modern gladiator contest where poor minority men sacrifice their bodies for entertainment.
key_points: Everyone carries implicit biases, and acknowledging prejudice is the essential first step toward addressing it
Suppressing emotions like crushes does 'weird things' - it's healthier to experience them fully while trusting yourself not to act destructively
Legalized prostitution in impoverished countries isn't truly a 'chosen profession' when driven by dire economic need
American football has become a sport where poor minority men sacrifice their bodies while wealthier families opt out
Getting to know someone better usually diminishes rather than intensifies attraction - people are 'sexiest' when you know very little about them
money_quotes: Yes, you are prejudiced! We all are! I am too! It's an important part of growing up, admitting such things. We are flawed, and we are contributing to the problems of our culture. Fact.
You've got to live your life fully, and engage in the things that attract you, and trust yourself not to lose it entirely. You've really got no other options.
Almost nobody gets sexier when you get to know them better. People are at their very sexiest when you know about 10 minutes about them, scattered over a few weeks or months.
When you're an emo, it's all about enjoying the pain.
It's not really a 'chosen profession' if you are forced by dire need to do it.
American football is quickly becoming a sport to which poor minority men sacrifice their bodies.
We might as well just admit it's a modern day Gladiator Contest, used to maintain a culture of violence for a people convinced they must be warriors.
posts.key_insight: Acknowledging our flaws and biases honestly - whether about prejudice or emotional patterns - is more productive than suppression or denial.

posts.summary: The post examines how 'big data' surveillance in education reinforces class divides, using a New York Times article about student tracking at Ball State as a springboard. O'Neil highlights how elite institutions don't track their students the same way, with advocates justifying this disparity as a 'moral obligation' to help less privileged students succeed. She argues this reveals a deeper pattern: the elite are exempt from monitoring while everyone else is surveilled 'for their own good.' The core insight is that classism determines who gets subjected to big data surveillance in the first place, not just who is harmed by it.
key_points: Elite institutions don't track students the way non-elite schools do, yet big data advocates see no problem with this disparity
The justification for surveillance of non-elite students is framed as a 'moral obligation' to help them succeed
Privacy exists on a spectrum, and relative privacy is a privilege of the rich and powerful
Big data doesn't just create classism—classism determines who enters the realm of big data surveillance to begin with
money_quotes: The elite don't need to be monitored, but the rabble does.
The rich and powerful get to be quirky philosophers but the rest of the population need to be ranked and filed.
We are spying on them for their own good.
Never mind how big data creates and expands classism; classism already helps decide who is put into the realm of big data in the first place.
Privacy, or at least relative privacy, is for the rich and powerful.
And it starts early.
posts.key_insight: Classism doesn't just result from big data—it determines who gets surveilled in the first place, making privacy itself a class privilege.

posts.summary: The post examines disturbing big data practices in healthcare, where private companies partner with hospitals and insurers to build predictive models about patients' mental health. A key example is Aetna's use of voice analysis software to detect depression in disability benefit recipients without their knowledge. The author highlights that this data often falls outside HIPAA protections and could be sold to third parties like employers. She questions whether the real purpose is to find reasons to disqualify people from benefits, and argues that the 'fear of gaming' justification for non-disclosure is itself a red flag.
key_points: Third-party apps collecting mental health data from postpartum women don't fully disclose their practices and can sell data to employers
Aetna has secretly analyzed voices of disability recipients since 2012 to detect depression, telling patients calls are only 'recorded for quality'
Much of this health data falls outside HIPAA protections, leaving patients vulnerable
Companies justify non-disclosure by claiming patients might 'game the model' if informed
The true purpose of these models may be to find reasons to deny benefits rather than help patients
money_quotes: They own the data they've collected at the end of the day and, presumably, can sell it to anyone interested in whether a woman is depressed. For example, future employers.
In other words, in the name of 'fear of gaming the model,' we are not disclosing the creepy methods we are using.
Since they don't know they are being modeled, they will never know.
I'm wondering if the real goal is to catch someone off their meds and disqualify them for further benefits.
To be clear, this data is generally not covered by HIPAA.
posts.key_insight: Healthcare companies are secretly using big data to model patients' mental health without disclosure, creating a surveillance system that may be designed to deny benefits rather than provide care.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post about two upcoming events. O'Neil shares that the Alternative Banking group has published a second Huffington Post essay critiquing the bank bailout's effectiveness and warning about future bailouts. She also announces she'll be giving an invited MAA talk at the Joint Math Meetings in San Antonio titled 'Making the Case for Data Journalism.'
key_points: The Alternative Banking group published an essay arguing the bailout didn't work and another financial crisis is being prepared
O'Neil is giving an invited MAA talk on data journalism at the Joint Math Meetings
She encourages math conference attendees to stay through Monday and offers to meet up with readers
money_quotes: The bailout isn't over, how it didn't work, and how we're already preparing for the next one.
I honestly feel like I don't know too many mathematicians anymore, so it would be nice to see or meet a friendly face.
posts.key_insight: A short logistical post announcing a critique of ongoing bailout policies and an upcoming academic talk on data journalism.

posts.summary: Tom Adams critiques the Obama Administration's decision to cut FHA mortgage insurance premiums by 0.5%, arguing it will do little to address housing affordability. He points out that mortgage rates are already at historic lows and that the real obstacle to homeownership is stagnant wages relative to home prices, not borrowing costs. The policy primarily benefits new purchasers while existing borrowers face refinancing costs that negate savings. Adams concludes this is less about helping homeowners and more about FHA competing for market share with Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac after their recent policy changes.
key_points: The 0.5% FHA premium reduction saves borrowers roughly $75/month on an average home—unlikely to meaningfully expand homeownership
The real barrier to homeownership is that wages haven't kept pace with home prices, not that credit is too expensive
Existing homeowners wanting prices to rise have strong lobbying power; potential buyers wanting affordable homes have none
The policy is primarily a competitive response to Fannie/Freddie's new 97% loan-to-value offerings, not genuine affordability reform
The Administration's solution to consumer problems continues to be encouraging more debt rather than addressing structural issues
money_quotes: Are they going to increase wages? Or reduce the price of homes? No, they're going to attack mortgage rates for Federal Housing Administration (FHA) borrowers.
If it's already really cheap to borrow money, is another 0.5% reduction going to make that big a difference? Probably not.

Would-be homeowners don't have the income to support buying the houses listed for sale in their markets – which is another way of saying that, for average Americans homes are too expensive for them to afford.

Existing homeowners and home sellers want home prices to go up... On the other hand, potential home buyers would rather not have home prices going up – because that makes buying much harder. For whatever reason, this group has about zero lobbying juice.
It is somewhat disheartening that the Administration still seems to believe that the solution to consumer issues is to have the consumers take on more debt.

The various government housing agencies are competing for market share among the same limited universe of qualifying borrowers by trying to get them to take on bigger mortgages than they would qualify for previously.
It's hard to believe that anyone in the Administration believes that this will do much for making homes more affordable for Americans.
posts.key_insight: Housing affordability policies that focus on cheaper credit rather than addressing the wage-to-home-price gap are ineffective theater that primarily serves government agencies competing for market share.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that citing or using educational content from problematic figures like sexual harasser Walter Lewin or anti-semite George Birkhoff involves more than just accessing knowledge—it's an act of honoring them and including them in a community. She uses the example of MIT removing Lewin's physics lectures after harassment revelations to explore whether the quality of someone's work justifies continuing to platform them. Her conclusion is that the 'delta' between slightly better explanations and the cost of honoring someone without integrity doesn't add up to a net positive, especially when institutions are trying to build welcoming communities for women in fields like physics.
key_points: Citation is not merely a technical shortcut but an act of honoring someone and including them in an intellectual community
The value of superior educational content must be weighed against the cost of platforming problematic individuals
Institutions like MIT must consider community-building goals when deciding whether to host content from harassers
Alternative explanations of the same material exist, making no single lecturer indispensable
Women's sense of welcome in STEM communities is a legitimate factor in these decisions
money_quotes: By citing him, I am doing more than giving him credit for proving something, I'm including him in the community of mathematics, which is actually an honor.
And honestly I'd rather not honor the wisdom of someone I detest.
If written citation is received wisdom, then actually sitting and listening to a person is even more intimate.
On the one hand, it's a better explanation, which adds, but on the other hand, it's the knowledge that we are honoring a man with no integrity, which subtracts.
For me, personally, these two opposite considerations don't add up to a net positive. I'd rather watch someone else explain the physics.
They want women in particular to feel welcomed to that community, and they decided that the videos' presence made that more difficult (and it's already difficult enough in physics).
posts.key_insight: Citation and educational content consumption are acts of community inclusion and honor, not just knowledge transfer, which means ethical considerations about creators legitimately factor into whether to use their work.

posts.summary: Leaked documents reveal Palantir's extensive client list including the CIA, NSA, FBI, LAPD, and military contractors. A quoted military contractor's claim that Palantir lets you 'know every single bad guy in your area' demonstrates a dangerous misunderstanding of data mining's limitations. O'Neil argues that no algorithm can be both comprehensive and accurate—there will always be false positives or false negatives. The lack of transparency around these powerful tools used by law enforcement and intelligence agencies makes this particularly concerning.
key_points: Palantir's clients as of 2013 included major government agencies (CIA, NSA, FBI, DHS, CDC) and the LAPD
Military contractors wrongly believe data mining tools can identify 'every single bad guy' with perfect accuracy
No data mining algorithm can simultaneously achieve comprehensiveness and accuracy—false positives and negatives are inevitable
Companies have financial incentives to oversell their tools as prophetic even when they regularly fail
The combination of powerful surveillance tools and zero public oversight is deeply troubling
money_quotes: No data mining tool can be both comprehensive and accurate – find all the bad guys with no accidental good guys getting caught in the net.

It behooves an expensive and fancy consulting company to act like their tools are prophetic, however, even if that means false positives or false negatives happen all the time, which of course they do, with any algorithm.
It's bad enough when stupid start-up companies claim big data solves everything, when what they're doing is trying to solve a problem nobody cares about.
It's another thing altogether when it's our military and military contractors and police and secret services, and when we don't have any view into what it actually does.
posts.key_insight: The dangerous combination of oversold algorithmic capabilities and zero transparency becomes especially alarming when the clients are military, police, and intelligence agencies with life-and-death power.

posts.summary: This is a brief administrative post sharing a link to a Prezi presentation given at the Joint Mathematics Meetings (JMM). The author notes she found free wifi at the San Antonio airport and uses the opportunity to share her talk. She acknowledges an embarrassing oversight in her presentation: failing to mention math podcasts as a form of public-facing mathematics communication, despite participating in a weekly podcast herself. An audience member who pointed out this gap has agreed to write a guest post surveying math podcasts.
key_points: The author presented at the Joint Mathematics Meetings and is sharing her Prezi slides
She admits to overlooking math podcasts in her discussion of public-facing mathematics
The omission is particularly ironic given her own involvement in a weekly podcast
A follow-up guest post surveying math podcasts is promised
money_quotes: Embarrassing not because I knew about them – I didn't – but because I should have, since after all I participate in a weekly podcast myself, so of course I know it's a new and exciting medium.
posts.key_insight: Even experts in public communication can have blind spots about the very mediums they participate in.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews Frank Pasquale's 'The Black Box Society,' initially worried it would overlap too much with her own forthcoming book on algorithms. She finds the books complement rather than compete: Pasquale writes for a more academic and legal audience, focusing on secrecy as power, while O'Neil focuses on models themselves. She praises Pasquale's metaphor of the internet as a 'one-way mirror' favoring corporations over individuals, and highlights his insight that government agencies can circumvent privacy laws by purchasing data from commercial warehouses like Acxiom.
key_points: Pasquale's book targets an academic/legal audience with policy recommendations for lawyers, unlike O'Neil's more general approach
The internet has become a 'one-way mirror' where corporations see into individuals' lives but not vice versa
Data warehousing companies like Acxiom serve as loopholes allowing government agencies to bypass civil liberties protections
The focus differs: Pasquale examines secrecy as power, while O'Neil examines models as objects of concern
Algorithms may not be intentionally evil, but engineers can potentially fix the problems they create
money_quotes: We've ended up with an internet that acts as a 'one way mirror,' whereby powerful corporations can see into individual's lives but those individuals can't look back.
The data warehousing industry acts as a giant loophole in the set of rules protecting our civil liberties.
His focus is on secrecy itself as a means of power, whereas I focus on models as the object of interest.
While they might be legally prevented from gathering certain kinds of data about people, nothing prevents them from buying information and profiles from data warehouses like Acxiom.
posts.key_insight: The commercial data industry has created a surveillance loophole that allows powerful institutions to circumvent privacy protections while maintaining information asymmetry over individuals.

posts.summary: O'Neil discusses a Science study showing that fields perceived as requiring innate 'brilliance' or 'genius' have fewer women, while fields emphasizing teachable skills have better gender representation. Mathematics exemplifies this problem, with its genius mythology discouraging women, while biology and statistics show better representation. She argues that educators should actively dispel the genius myth by emphasizing practice over innate talent, noting this approach works in her own teaching. The cultural shift becomes harder at higher research levels where established academics are reluctant to abandon the genius label they've earned.
key_points: Fields that emphasize 'genius' as a requirement for success systematically discourage women, while fields emphasizing teachable skills have better gender diversity
The genius effect explains gender disparities outside STEM too—philosophy, a 'macho field,' has few women despite not being technical
The study found a negative correlation between a field's emphasis on innate brilliance and its representation of women
Teaching approaches that emphasize practice over innate gifts work better and are more enjoyable for everyone
Cultural change is harder at higher levels because established researchers are reluctant to abandon the genius label they worked hard to earn
money_quotes: Mathematics is definitely one of those fields; if you are exceptionally successful in mathematics, people call you a genius, and it's pretty hard to be successful if people don't think you're a genius.
Philosophy is the obvious example here, a very macho field.
I am particularly struck by the difference between statistics and math.
I deliberately teach kids an introduction to proof that emphasizes practice over the silly and distracting concept of having an innate gift.
You just need to create an environment of learning that emphasizes practice over genius. Actively dispel the genius myth.
Achieving that cultural shift gets harder the higher up the research ladder you go, though, partly because it's hard for older people to give up the 'genius' label they worked so hard for.
posts.key_insight: The mythology of genius in certain fields acts as a gatekeeping mechanism that systematically excludes women, and dismantling it through practice-focused teaching is both effective and achievable.

posts.summary: This advice column covers several topics: the continued work of the Occupy Wall Street's Alt Banking group despite media silence, a lengthy response about dealing with defensive Google employees who dismiss criticism of their company's shuttle buses, the absurdity of UK porn bans, and a Bayesian defense of FiveThirtyEight's research showing economist partisanship. The central theme is how people defend their own interests and employers even when the evidence contradicts them. O'Neil argues that defensiveness leads to poor reasoning and bullying tactics in arguments. She suggests that when someone makes you feel dumb in a conversation, it's almost always about their insecurities, not your intelligence.
key_points: The Obama administration is 'owned by Wall Street' and Republicans plan to further weaken Dodd-Frank financial regulations
When people make you feel dumb in arguments, 99% of the time it reflects their defensiveness, not your intelligence
Google operates like a cult where employees feel compelled to defend company practices even when indefensible
Data analysis is evidence-gathering, not proof—results become believable when they remain robust across different methodologies
The UK porn ban will simply push market share to shadowy corners where content becomes less consensual
money_quotes: In terms of the Obama administration, yes, it's owned by Wall Street, and to be honest I didn't think it could get worse but we'll see if I'm wrong starting now.
Defensiveness leads to terrible reasoning abilities, so the only way for defensive people to win arguments, since they can't do it with their logic, is to do it with a bullying attitude.
99 times out of 100, if you are in a conversation where the person talking to you is making you feel dumb, then it's about them, not you.

Google employees work for Google, and it's kind of a cult, like many companies are, and they feel lucky to be there and want other people to think they're lucky too, so they defend things even when those things don't make sense.
People are pretty good at defending their own interests.
Data analysis is, at best, an evidence-gathering activity, not a proof.
When market share gets moved to places further in the shadow, things get less consensual and more coerced, and that's never good.
posts.key_insight: People instinctively defend their employers and interests with aggressive rhetoric precisely because they lack logical ground to stand on, making defensiveness a reliable indicator of weak arguments.

posts.summary: Peter Woit critiques two AMS Notices articles by NSA officials that appear to mislead the mathematics community about the NSA's role in backdooring the DUAL_EC_DRBG cryptography standard. The first article by Richard George claimed no proven NSA-linked cryptographic weaknesses exist, while a follow-up by NSA Director of Research Michael Wertheimer expressed 'regret' without actually denying the backdoor's existence. Woit notes that the core mathematical question—how were the elliptic curve points P and Q chosen—remains deliberately unanswered, with the NSA refusing to cooperate with investigations. He argues the AMS leadership must demand explicit assurances from the NSA or sever cooperative programs, as the organization is being used to spread disinformation to mathematicians.
key_points: The NSA has published two articles in AMS Notices that appear to deny backdooring crypto standards while carefully avoiding explicit statements about the specific DUAL_EC_DRBG case
The mathematical question is simple and verifiable: how were points P and Q chosen? The NSA refuses to answer and told NIST staff they couldn't discuss it publicly
Wertheimer's 'regrettable' statement expresses hindsight about not dropping support after the backdoor potential was discovered, but never denies the backdoor exists
Security expert Matthew Green confirms the articles are misleading and believes NSA policy of tampering with cryptographic products continues
Woit presents two stark possibilities: either there's no backdoor and NSA inexplicably won't say so, or the AMS is being used to mislead mathematicians
money_quotes: I have never heard of any proven weakness in a cryptographic algorithm that's linked to NSA; just innuendo.
With hindsight, NSA should have ceased supporting the dual EC_DRBG algorithm immediately after security researchers discovered the potential for a trapdoor.
In truth, I can think of no better way to describe our failure to drop support for the Dual_EC_DRBG algorithm as anything other than regrettable.

The only record of what happened when the NIST asked about how P and Q were chosen early on in the development of the standard indicates that people were told by the NSA that they were not allowed to publicly discuss the question.
I think that the NSA has really adopted a policy of tampering with cryptographic products and they're not going to give that up.
I don't think there can be any argument about the significance of the AMS being used by the NSA to mislead the mathematics community.
posts.key_insight: When officials express 'regret' about a controversy without explicitly denying the underlying accusation, their careful word choices often reveal more than their statements conceal.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares two articles examining feminism from different angles: one by a dominatrix arguing for 'intersectional feminism' that addresses male sexual repression to reduce misogyny, and another about how the 'innate genius' stereotype blocks women from academic fields like philosophy and STEM. She endorses the idea that men's constrained sexuality contributes to extremism and misogyny, and highlights how stories of women being told they 'don't have what it takes' contradict claims that academic sexism is merely anecdotal. She concludes optimistically that widespread awareness of these issues represents progress.
key_points: Men are sexually 'boxed in' just as women are, and this frustration fuels misogyny and extremist movements like pickup artists and 'men's rights' groups
The 'innate genius' myth serves as a gatekeeping mechanism that disproportionately excludes women from fields like philosophy and STEM
Anecdotal dismissals of academic sexism ignore the countless unreported stories of qualified women being told they lack what it takes
Progress can be measured simply by the increased awareness and willingness to discuss these issues openly
money_quotes: It is clearly true that men are boxed in in terms of their sexuality just as women are, and for men that don't fit the standard mold it amounts to a kind of torture.
It's not just about competing for jobs with women, it's also about not getting laid.
I cannot tell you how many people I know who have gone through something similar.
Such stories, which are generally completely unreported, flies in the face of ridiculous claims... that sexist mistreatment in science is minor and anecdotal.
I see it as progress just to be able to assume that other people I run into are sufficiently aware of the issues to talk about them, including my teenage sons.
posts.key_insight: Both male sexual repression and the 'innate genius' myth are systemic forces that perpetuate gender inequality, and acknowledging these dynamics openly is itself a form of progress.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines a pending Supreme Court case on the Fair Housing Act that will determine whether discrimination law covers only intentional discrimination or also 'disparate impact' on groups. She notes that Chief Justice Roberts seems skeptical that racial discrimination persists. The piece highlights how the Justice Department used disparate impact analysis to secure a $335 million settlement against Countrywide Financial for charging minorities higher rates. O'Neil warns that limiting discrimination law to intentional acts would enable algorithmic systems to produce discriminatory outcomes without accountability.
key_points: The Supreme Court case will decide if Fair Housing Act violations require proof of intentional discrimination or can include statistical disparate impact
Disparate impact analysis has been upheld by 11 courts of appeals over 40 years and enabled major discrimination settlements
Intentional discrimination has become harder to prove even as discriminatory outcomes persist
Algorithmic decision-making systems can easily produce discriminatory results without explicit intent
The ruling's implications extend beyond housing to job applications, credit, and other algorithmic systems
money_quotes: The ability to show discriminatory effect has only become more important as intentional discrimination has become harder to prove.
If we stick to the 'intentional discrimination' only, we are opening up a door to (even more) widespread use of algorithmic decision-making that produces unfair and discriminatory results.
And as it turns out, it's easy to produce a model that effectively discriminates.
And if you are not in charge of your own system, then who is?
Chief Justice Roberts seems to think we've moved past racial discrimination in this country.
posts.key_insight: Requiring proof of intentional discrimination rather than disparate impact creates legal cover for algorithmic systems that produce discriminatory outcomes, since it's easy to build models that discriminate without explicit intent.

posts.summary: This advice column covers several topics including whether to reform or replace the financial system, getting started with Doctor Who, parental concerns about college safety, and most substantially, a math PhD student's anxiety about transitioning from coursework to research. Aunt Pythia argues that the academic selection process optimizes for people good at structured learning with continuous feedback, which differs from the skills needed for research: loving math, tolerating uncertainty, and working without external validation. She advises the anxious PhD student to develop the 'muscle' of losing oneself in mathematical exploration without milestone pressure, starting with short practice sessions.
key_points: The financial system shouldn't be burned down due to short-term chaos, but parallel alternatives like public banks are worth pursuing
Academic selection attracts people skilled at mastering well-defined tasks with continuous feedback, not necessarily those suited for open-ended research
Research requires loving math, tolerating uncertainty about progress, and working without external validation—different skills than excelling at coursework
The transition from structured academic success to independent research is a learnable skill that requires developing new 'muscles' for unstructured exploration
External measures of progress and addiction to encouragement can actually hinder research ability
money_quotes: If you are feeling desperate, may I suggest ignoring it and starting a new one.

Instead, we attract to math research, via our post-college applications selection method, people who know how to master a well-defined skill where they get continuous feedback from tests and other people that they are making progress.
You need to stop caring about outside measures of progress so you can lose yourself in your work and make progress.
You don't owe your advisor anything except your gratitude. You are doing math for yourself from here on in.
Find yourself a few hours of time and lose yourself in thought around some mathematical object, with no specific need of a milestone.
Play with the math, see what you find, and don't feel like you've wasted your time at the end, even if you feel like you have. It was your time to waste, after all.
Once you get good at it, you can lose yourself for days or weeks at a time and then every now and then stumble on actual progress.
posts.key_insight: Success in structured academic environments selects for skills opposite to those needed for research—the ability to work without external validation and tolerate prolonged uncertainty.

posts.summary: The post discusses Greece's 2015 election where the anti-austerity party Syriza won, promising to refuse further debt payments. O'Neil frames this as a democratic pushback against technocratic negotiations that occurred behind closed doors since 2008. She speculates about potential consequences including a 'Grexit' from the Eurozone, noting Greece's developed black market economy and Argentina's recent default as relevant precedents.
key_points: Syriza's victory represents democracy fighting back against undemocratic technocratic debt negotiations
Greece's debt negotiations since 2008 were conducted behind closed doors without meaningful public input
A Greek default and potential Eurozone exit could serve as a template for other small countries dealing with debt
Argentina's recent default went better than predicted, potentially emboldening Greece
Greece's robust black market economy may buffer ordinary citizens from banking disruptions
money_quotes: From the perspective of the Greek people, the negotiations around their economic problems have been taken on by their last two governments since 2008 with a bunch of European technocrats behind closed doors and in an intensely undemocratic process.
Well, this is when democracy fought back.
The world will be watching Greece to see what happens and to see how smaller countries can and will deal with stifling debt in the future.
posts.key_insight: Greece's election of an anti-austerity government represents a democratic rejection of technocratic debt management, with potential implications for how smaller nations handle crushing sovereign debt.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil promotes two upcoming speaking engagements. The first is a math colloquium talk on data science at Dartmouth's Math department. The second is a panel she will moderate at All Souls Unitarian Church in Manhattan. The post is purely promotional with no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: O'Neil will give a data science colloquium talk at Dartmouth Math department
She will moderate a panel on 'Mega-Foundations' at All Souls Unitarian Church in Manhattan
The post includes promotional posters for both events
posts.key_insight: This is a simple event announcement with no analytical content to extract insights from.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines two recent news events—Deflategate and an overhyped blizzard forecast—to illustrate the public's poor understanding of scientific uncertainty. She argues that both cases involve error bars and measurement variability that the media and public struggle to accept. The NFL hired physicists unnecessarily when simple experiments could demonstrate expected measurement ranges. Weather models correctly predicted storm intensity but erred on location, yet the public blamed the science rather than understanding inherent uncertainty. O'Neil warns this inability to accept imperfect information bodes poorly as society becomes increasingly datafied.
key_points: Both Deflategate and the blizzard forecast controversy stem from misunderstanding error bars and measurement uncertainty
Weather models have improved significantly at predicting storm power, but location and timing remain harder to forecast accurately
The public resists scientific explanations of uncertainty because it challenges our sense of control and omnipotence
Society's assumption that more data equals perfect information is fundamentally flawed
money_quotes: A few good experiments with temperature and friction and lots of measurements by lots of different pressure gauges will empirically demonstrate how much of a range we might expect from such things.
Again, it's a case of errorbars, and the public seems not to understand it. Or maybe they just don't want to.
The truth is, it's not as interesting a story to think about it just happening outside our control.
It messes with our sense of omnipotence and control.
This is bad news for society, as more and more things become 'datafied' and as we assume that will translate into perfect information.
posts.key_insight: Society's growing reliance on data is undermined by a fundamental unwillingness to accept that more data doesn't eliminate uncertainty—it just quantifies it.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional post where Cathy O'Neil shares her recent appearance on the Slate Money podcast. Due to travel and lack of sleep, she couldn't post her regular Aunt Pythia column. The podcast episode focused on sports economics, featuring ESPN's Mina Kimes as a guest. Topics discussed included paying college athletes, stadium economics, and NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell.
key_points: O'Neil appeared on the Slate Money podcast discussing sports economics
The episode covered three main topics: college athlete compensation, stadium economics in cities, and Roger Goodell
Guest Mina Kimes from ESPN the Magazine joined the discussion
money_quotes: We talked about money issues around sports, namely whether college athletes should be paid, the economics of stadiums in cities, and the NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell.
posts.key_insight: Sports economics involves complex questions about fair compensation for college athletes and the public costs of stadium financing.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that Obama's retraction of the 529 college savings tax benefit, while politically unpopular, points to a deeper problem: making college more 'affordable' through loans and tax shelters actually drives tuition higher. She presents data showing federal aid growth correlating with tuition increases, arguing that easy money enables colleges to raise prices. The result is that college remains out of reach for many, but now students emerge burdened with debt. Her solution is counterintuitive: make paying harder for everyone while directly funding state schools to be cheap or free.
key_points: The 529 plan is a regressive tax benefit since most Americans can't afford to save for college anyway
Federal aid and loan programs have enabled tuition increases rather than improving access - college is still unaffordable, but now students graduate with debt
College has become a mandatory 'hazing ritual' for employment despite not being more accessible
Reduced state school funding is partly justified by the existence of federal loans, creating a vicious cycle
If everyone has trouble paying for something, the price goes down - universal difficulty creates market pressure
money_quotes: Whenever we make college more affordable by helping people pay for college, it just makes college more expensive.

The result of our federal loan programs, which were started with good intentions, is that whereas before college was out of reach for lots of people, now it's still out of reach, they go anyway, and then emerge loaded with debt.

It's not actually a huge improvement for the vast majority of the middle class, but it's become a requirement to get a reasonable job so people are forced to go through it, kind of like a hazing ritual.
If you're the only person having trouble paying for something, that's bad. But if everyone has trouble paying for something, the price goes down.
Tuition rises to meet our new-found ability to pay.
posts.key_insight: Subsidizing demand for college through loans and tax benefits doesn't improve access—it just inflates tuition; the real solution is to directly fund supply by making state schools free.

posts.summary: S&P settled a lawsuit for $1.5 billion over inflated ratings for mortgage-backed securities that contributed to the financial crisis, with no jail time and no admission of wrongdoing. Their defense hinged on claiming that their use of words like 'objective' and 'independent' was mere 'marketing puffery' that no reasonable investor should rely on. O'Neil connects this to a broader pattern where mathematical models across industries use claims of objectivity as marketing to convince people to trust black boxes over their own judgment. She questions whether this puffery defense will become widespread, effectively rendering the word 'objective' legally meaningless.
key_points: S&P used the 'puffery defense' to argue that their claims of objectivity were marketing language, not factual statements
Claims of objectivity are essential marketing for complex mathematical models, convincing people to trust algorithms over their own judgment and sense of fairness
Value-added models for evaluating teachers make similar questionable claims of objectivity
The legal acceptance of 'puffery' around objectivity claims could undermine accountability across industries
money_quotes: Their use of the word 'objective' when describing their models was mere 'marketing puffery,' not to be taken seriously.

It's part of the marketing for those models, a requirement to lure people into using complex, mathematical black boxes instead of their own brains, and crucially, in place of their own sense of fairness and accountability.

S&P said in its request to dismiss the case that the government can't base its fraud claims on S&P's assertions that its ratings were independent, objective and free of conflicts of interest because U.S. courts have found that such vague and generalized statements are the kind of 'puffery' that a reasonable investor wouldn't rely on.
Are we going to have a redefinition of that word so that companies are officially allowed to claim objectivity while actually meaning nothing like objectivity?
posts.key_insight: When companies can legally dismiss their claims of 'objectivity' as marketing puffery not meant to be believed, the word becomes a tool for avoiding accountability rather than ensuring it.

posts.summary: A guest post by FogOfWar pushes back on Cathy O'Neil's critique of 529 college savings plans. Using detailed numerical analysis across four income brackets (poor, middle class, mass affluent, and rich), the author argues that 529 plans provide substantial benefits specifically for middle-class families while offering little to no advantage for the very poor or very rich. The post demonstrates that for a NYC middle-class couple, 529 plans yield 30% higher returns than paying off student debt due to state tax benefits and tax-free compounding. The author concludes by speculating about the political motivations behind Obama's failed proposal to cut 529 benefits, suggesting it may have been a deliberate strategy to marginalize progressive voices.
key_points: 529 plans provide negligible benefits to poor families (who lose liquidity and may hurt financial aid eligibility) and are suboptimal for the truly wealthy (who have better tax strategies)
For middle-class families, 529 plans yield approximately 30% higher returns than paying off student debt, with nearly half the benefit coming from state-level tax advantages
The total budgetary impact of 529 plans is only $1 billion/year—described as 'a rounding error' in federal budget terms
State tax treatment varies dramatically: states like Massachusetts and California offer no state deduction, while high-tax states like New York provide significant benefits
The author speculates Obama's failed 529 proposal may have been strategic political theater to marginalize the progressive wing of the Democratic Party
money_quotes: Beating up on 529 plans is like stealing oatmeal from orphans—they're too small to defend themselves, you don't really get much if you win and everyone loves the soot-smudged little munchkins.
The 529 plan is an extremely powerful tool for the true working class.
There's been a lot of progressive spite thrown at this couple. Cries of 'they really don't need any help—we should take this away' are there either explicitly or just below the surface.

529 plans aren't relevant to the rich at all. They're glad you're spending your time thinking about 529 taxation rather than something that matters, like carried interest or the step up in basis at death.
There's a special phrase for a number like $1bn/year in the beltway: it's called 'a rounding error'.
These plans are one of the only reeds the government gives to parents grasping at straws to find the money to cover college.
Cathy's short response: nobody except the affluent middle class (and the rich) has $100 extra to begin with.
posts.key_insight: 529 plans are a powerful middle-class savings tool whose benefits are overstated for both the poor and rich, making progressive opposition to them misguided class warfare against the wrong target.

posts.summary: This is an advice column ('Aunt Pythia') where Cathy O'Neil responds to reader questions on relationships, gender dynamics in academia, parenting concerns about advertising, medical decisions, and intimacy issues. The most substantive exchange involves a male grad student accused of sexism after discussing affirmative action with a female colleague. O'Neil delivers a pointed lesson about perspective-taking, explaining that even factual statements about systemic advantages can land poorly when framed as 'people like you have it easier.' She advises him to demonstrate understanding of documented sexism in academia before expecting absolution, noting that being gossiped about often means you hurt someone first.
key_points: When discussing sensitive topics like gender and affirmative action, how your words land matters more than your intent - you must understand your audience's perspective
The impulse to defend yourself against accusations of bias often backfires; acknowledgment of systemic problems should come before self-vindication
Being gossiped about is often a consequence of having hurt someone else first - focusing only on your own wounds prevents progress
Parents can use provocative advertising as opportunities to teach children critical thinking about media manipulation
Quality of life often matters more than length of life when making difficult medical decisions
money_quotes: Life lesson learned! Or, otherwise put, you play with fire you gonna get burned.
You talked to a person you didn't really know, when you didn't have enough time to have a proper conversation, about how 'people like them' have it less tough than 'people like you.'

It sounds like you decided to spend your time trying to prevent people from thinking you're an asshole, but that will only make matters worse, because you haven't acknowledged any mistake, real or perceived.
She wasn't telling lies. She was telling people how she felt after your conversation.
If we spend all our time licking our own wounds rather than understanding what went wrong, then no progress will be made.
Hopefully you can understand that you were likely gossiped about because you hurt someone else humongously, and that this is just as much about them than about you.
