People overemphasize length of life over quality of life.
posts.key_insight: When accused of bias after discussing sensitive topics, the instinct to defend yourself misses the point - understanding how your words landed and acknowledging systemic realities must come before self-vindication.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the S&P $1.5 billion settlement with the Justice Department, questioning where settlement money actually goes and whether the current system of large fines without jail time has created perverse incentives. She argues that settlements are designed to be manageable for financial institutions and don't threaten business as usual. More troublingly, she suggests that states like New York may become 'addicted' to settlement cash flows, creating incentives to allow wrongdoing in order to collect fines later. The post calls for transparency in tracking where settlement funds actually end up.
key_points: Massive settlements are deliberately sized to be manageable for financial institutions - the S&P fine equals about two years of profit
Settlements literally 'settle' things back to normal rather than creating systemic change or accountability
States receiving settlement money may have perverse incentives to maintain the current system rather than pursue jail time or institutional dissolution
The 'big fines but no jail time' practice effectively legalizes and taxes white-collar crime
There's a lack of transparency about where settlement money actually goes and whether victims receive their designated funds
money_quotes: In the end, the settlements don't substantially threaten business as usual.
The settlements do nothing but 'settle' things back to the way they were.
If a given state gets addicted to this cash flow, there will be no reason to change tactics and, instead of massive fines, simply jail wrong doers and break up or dissolve corrupt institutions.
There will even be reasons for places like New York State to allow wrongdoing in order to cash in for the settlement a few years later.
The more you think about it, the more this 'big fines but no jail time' settlement practice we now have in place seems like a way to legalize and tax crime.
posts.key_insight: The current system of massive financial settlements without criminal prosecution may have created a perverse incentive structure where states benefit from ongoing corporate wrongdoing rather than preventing it.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on NPR's 'Bored and Brilliant' challenge, which encouraged adults to reduce phone usage to foster creativity through boredom. While the challenge showed minimal impact on actual phone habits, she argues its value lies in shifting attitudes about boredom. She then pivots to question why parents who recognize boredom's creative benefits still overschedule their children, noting that she deliberately keeps her older kids unscheduled to allow for the kind of unstructured time that fueled her own childhood creativity.
key_points: The 'Bored and Brilliant' challenge had minimal measurable impact on participants' actual phone usage
Even without behavioral change, shifting cultural attitudes about boredom has value
There's a contradiction between adults seeking boredom for creativity while overscheduling their children
Children are better positioned than adults to benefit from boredom—fewer responsibilities, more creative potential
O'Neil's best childhood experiences emerged from unstructured, adult-plan-free time
money_quotes: your most creative moments happen when you're bored
I always cherish the couple of days when I've lost my phone but before I get my new one. Sweet freedom!
why don't we let our kids get bored if we think it's so great?
I deliberately keep my kids virtually unscheduled outside of their school and homework
kids aren't addicted to phones (yet), and they don't have as many family responsibilities, and they do have plenty of reasons and avenues to be incredibly creative
My best moments of childhood – playing music, forming lifelong friendships, reading Dostoyevsky, and yes, experimenting with things – only happened because I was utterly without other grown-up plans
posts.key_insight: Adults who recognize boredom's creative value for themselves often fail to extend the same gift to their children, whose unstructured time may matter even more.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines Sandberg and Grant's column about women performing unrewarded 'housekeeping' tasks at work, such as training colleagues and maintaining wikis. While acknowledging sexism as a factor, she argues this is fundamentally a management problem—organizations fail to track and reward genuinely helpful behaviors. She pushes back on advice that tells women to simply adapt, instead calling for systemic changes to incentive structures that would benefit both genders and the company.
key_points: Studies show women face a double standard: they're rated lower than men for the same helpful behavior, and penalized more for declining to help
Community-building work like documentation and training is genuinely valuable but systematically unrewarded, so men rationally opt out
The psychological toll of helping comes partly from having efforts ignored, not just from the work itself
Simply tracking helpful acts isn't enough—organizations need incentive systems that actually reward this behavior
Advice telling women to conform to broken reward systems misses the point; the systems themselves need fixing
money_quotes: For staying late and helping, a man was rated 14 percent more favorably than a woman. When both declined, a woman was rated 12 percent lower than a man.
Because of that weirdness, men (in general) don't try and don't care as much about it, so given a men-only group, it simply wouldn't get done.
Should women stop doing those extra things, or should men start? Should we continue to ignore how much stuff like that actually helps, or should we begin to keep track?
I'm already really efficient, though, so I'd like more advice on that first part, keeping track of acts of helping.
Obviously just keeping track of stuff won't help if you don't actually care about the numbers. Or, even worse, if you just pity the poor fool who helped the most.
Getting rid of assholes is already hard, and this is more nuanced than that.
posts.key_insight: The gender gap in 'office housework' isn't just about sexism—it's a management failure to build incentive systems that reward behaviors genuinely valuable to organizations.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her upcoming participation in Yale's Rebellious Lawyering Conference, an annual student-run public interest gathering focused on progressive legal approaches to social change. She will be on a panel called 'Using Law To Occupy Wall Street' alongside Akshat Tewary from Occupy the SEC and Rebecca Wilkins. The conference is grounded in Gerald Lopez's vision of challenging hierarchies of race, wealth, gender, and expertise within legal practice. O'Neil expresses enthusiasm about the event and her fellow panelists.
key_points: RebLaw is the nation's largest student-run public interest legal conference
The conference focuses on progressive approaches to law and social change, challenging traditional hierarchies
O'Neil's panel connects legal activism to the Occupy Wall Street movement
The event brings together practitioners, students, and community activists
money_quotes: RebLaw is the nation's largest student-run public interest conference.

The conference seeks to build a community of law students, practitioners, and activists seeking to work in the service of social change movements and to challenge hierarchies of race, wealth, gender, and expertise within legal practice and education.
posts.key_insight: Legal activism and social movements like Occupy Wall Street can intersect to challenge systemic inequalities in finance and governance.

posts.summary: This Valentine's Day-themed advice column tackles questions about career transitions for math PhDs and various relationship/sex questions. O'Neil advises a math PhD student to consider data science (and plugs her book), while offering a tongue-in-cheek quiz suggesting actuarial math for 'boring' people and finance for 'evil' ones. The sex advice portions emphasize open communication with partners, noting that many couples assume their partner doesn't want something without actually asking. She argues that insisting on any specific sexual act every time 'borders on abusive' and advocates for balance and mutual satisfaction.
key_points: Math PhDs seeking industry careers should consider data science, learn Python, and build a public portfolio of projects
Her satirical career quiz implies actuarial work is boring and finance attracts morally questionable people
Many relationship communication problems stem from assuming what partners want without actually asking them
Sexual relationships require negotiation and balance—no one should insist on the same thing every time
When sharing sexual fantasies, the rule is: share if it increases arousal, keep quiet if it causes jealousy, or adapt it to move from the second category to the first
money_quotes: If she said 'yes' to #1, she might consider actuarial math. If she said 'yes' to #2, she should think finance.

A lot of people complain about their sex life and when I ask them whether they've tried various things, they tell me their partner 'doesn't want that.' But when I ask whether they've asked their partner, explicitly, about that, they admit they haven't.
It's not ok for someone to insist on something, anything, to happen every time, unless both people want it. It borders on abusive, in fact.
Everyone thinks about sex all the time, so by the pigeon hole principle most people think for at least a few minutes a day about any one question.

Share when it will make your partner hornier. Keep it to yourself if it will make your partner angry or jealous or less horny. And third, change it ever so slightly to bring it from the second category to the first.
posts.key_insight: Whether in careers or relationships, people often make assumptions about what's possible or acceptable without actually testing those assumptions through direct communication.

posts.summary: This brief post shares Cathy O'Neil's current reading list, touching on cultural criticism, financial lobbying, algorithmic bias in neighborhood rating apps, the predatory for-profit college industry, and the historical connections between slavery and American capitalism. She expresses ambivalence about 50 Shades of Grey's cultural impact, highlights concerns about Washington Post coverage that appears to favor for-profit colleges, and draws attention to the continuity between historical 'financial innovations' involving slaves and modern finance.
key_points: Neighborhood rating apps like SketchFactor and PlaceToLive raise concerns about encoded bias and discrimination
The for-profit college industry is described as 'toxic' while mainstream media may be providing favorable coverage
Financial lobbying uses community banks as pawns in larger political games
Historical connections exist between slavery and the development of American capitalism's financial innovations
money_quotes: I still can't decide whether the net effect of the film is bad, because the characters are so terribly stereotypical and stalkerish, or good, because it at least forces people to ask the question, what do I desire and how is that different from other people's desires.
I'm researching the toxic industry that is for-profit colleges.
On the other hand, the Washington Post seems to be shilling for that same industry.
posts.key_insight: A simple reading list reveals the through-lines connecting algorithmic bias, predatory education, financial lobbying, and the historical roots of exploitative capitalism.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques a USA Today ranking of 'top colleges for math majors' that prioritizes early-career salaries and undergraduate focus over research output. She notes that mathematicians value doctoral degree production and exposure to research mathematics, metrics that this ranking weights minimally. The methodology reveals a fundamental disconnect between what outsiders think makes a good math program and what mathematicians themselves care about. She finds the ranking interesting as a thought experiment about what would happen if math departments actually optimized for these metrics.
key_points: The USA Today ranking weights early-career salary highly but doctoral degree production lowly, inverting mathematician priorities
Mathematicians care about undergraduate exposure to research mathematics, not just job outcomes
The ranking methodology reveals non-mathematicians' assumptions about what makes math education valuable
If departments gamed this ranking like they do US News, they would eliminate pure math and compete aggressively for students
The disconnect between insider and outsider values in evaluating academic programs goes largely unexamined
money_quotes: These are some fine schools, but not at all the typical list one would consider.
We mathematicians think that money doesn't buy happiness, and we don't care so much about early career salaries.
We do care about the extent to which the average undergraduate math major is exposed to research mathematics.

I guess to the non-expert, it makes sense to care way more about the undergraduate focus of a college for judging the quality of an undergrad math major, but it all depends on what you want to have happen next.
I'm enjoying how different this list is from the typical inside baseball list.
We'd throw out pure math majors altogether in order to focus our attention on applied math majors that will make loads of money out of college.
We'd also compete for students against other majors, something very few math departments actually do.
posts.key_insight: Rankings that claim to measure educational quality often reveal more about the rankers' values than about actual program quality, and optimizing for outsider metrics would fundamentally distort academic disciplines.

posts.summary: O'Neil describes her involvement in an AAPOR task force examining big data's role in public opinion research. The report addresses the growing temptation to use Twitter and other digital sources instead of traditional surveys because they're cheaper, despite serious quality control issues. The task force produced six recommendations emphasizing that big data and surveys should be complementary, not competing, and that standards for transparency and ethics need to be developed. O'Neil recommends the report to anyone entering the field who hasn't thought through the intrinsic difficulties of using big data for research.
key_points: The biggest threat to public opinion research isn't flawed online polls but the temptation to mine Twitter for sentiment because it's cheaper
Big data volume doesn't compensate for other data deficiencies - a common misconception that needs addressing
Surveys and big data should be viewed as complementary tools, not competitors, with each suited to different research questions
Most users of digital services don't realize their behavioral data may be repurposed for research
Effective big data research requires interdisciplinary teams, but different fields use terminology inconsistently, creating barriers
money_quotes: The biggest problem facing the world of public opinion research isn't that online opt-in polls, but rather the temptation to troll twitter to 'see what people are thinking.'
And that's exactly what's happening, in large part because it's cheaper.
One common misconception is the belief that volume of data can compensate for any other deficiency in the data.
Most users of digital services are unaware of the fact that data formed out of their digital behavior may be reused for other purposes, for both public and private good.
Research is about answering questions, and one way to answer questions is to start utilizing all information available.
Data ownership is not well defined and there is no clear legal framework for the collection and subsequent use of Big Data.
posts.key_insight: The appeal of cheap, abundant social media data is undermining rigorous public opinion research, and the field needs standards to ensure big data complements rather than replaces sound methodology.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines a New York Times article about bipartisan justice reform efforts, connecting it to evidence-based sentencing practices she's researched extensively. She notes that data-driven approaches to reducing recidivism can either perpetuate inequality by penalizing poverty or genuinely improve resource allocation. The unusual coalition of Koch Industries and the ACLU working together on this issue gives her rare optimism, though she cautions that reformers must understand that models aren't inherently objective. She argues data systems could actively fight unfairness rather than codify existing biases.
key_points: Evidence-based sentencing approaches range from harmful (punishing poverty) to helpful (focusing resources effectively)
The Koch-ACLU coalition on justice reform represents a rare moment of bipartisan potential
Data-driven models in justice are not inherently objective and require people who understand modeling to implement them properly
Data systems can be designed to actively combat unfairness rather than simply automate existing discriminatory practices
money_quotes: This stuff runs the gamut between putting poor people away for longer because they're poor and actually focusing resources where they're needed.
I'm desperately hoping they have involved people who understand enough about modeling not to assume that the results of models are 'objective'.

There are, in fact, lots of ways to set up data-gathering and usage in the justice system to actively fight against unfairness and unreasonably long incarcerations, rather than to simply codify such practices.
posts.key_insight: Data-driven justice reform can either entrench inequality or fight it, depending entirely on whether the people implementing it understand that models are not inherently objective.

posts.summary: The post critiques the media's sensationalized interpretation of a Rate My Professor data visualization tool. While acknowledging real gender bias exists in teaching evaluations, the author demonstrates that journalists and academics are drawing unsupported conclusions from the data. Through her own analysis, she shows that 'genius' appears mostly in negative reviews (meaning difficult courses, not praise), 'bossy' has negligible usage rates, and 'terrible teacher' shows smaller gender gaps than implied. She calls for more rigorous data interpretation even when findings might support causes we believe in.
key_points: The word 'genius' in reviews predominantly appears in negative contexts like 'you have to be a genius to pass' - not as praise for professors
'Bossy' occurs fewer than 5 times per million words - statistically insignificant despite dramatic-looking visualizations
Gender bias in teaching evaluations is real and documented by peer-reviewed research, but this particular tool doesn't support the conclusions being drawn from it
Math professors receive the highest 'terrible teacher' ratings across disciplines, with minimal gender difference (187 vs 192 per million words)
Both journalists and academics are failing to critically evaluate data that confirms their existing beliefs
money_quotes: Bad interpretations of data don't advance the cause.
There's beautiful link-bait out there that justifies its conclusions on the flimsy 'hey, look at this chart' understanding of big data.
As a young woman in academia, I want deans and chairs and presidents to take these observed phenomena seriously when evaluating their professors.
Journalists, get your shit together and report data responsibly.
Academics, be a little more skeptical of stories that simply post screenshots of a chart coupled with inciting prose from conclusions drawn, badly, from hastily scanned data.
We can geek out on experimental design, the fact that the research is peer-reviewed and therefore passes some basic legitimacy tests.
Is this tool useless? No. Is it fun to futz around with? Yes. Is it being reported and understood well? Resounding no!
posts.key_insight: Even when data appears to support a legitimate cause like addressing gender bias, sloppy analysis and sensationalized reporting undermine the credibility of that cause and set back the conversation.

posts.summary: The post tackles the ethical dilemma of porn consumption: viewers can never be certain the performers fully consented, meaning they may unknowingly derive pleasure from recorded assault. The author proposes a deliberately provocative solution modeled on healthcare reform—a regulated exchange with government subsidies, union protections, and mandatory inspections to ensure ethical production. However, the author acknowledges this 'Obamneycare for Sex' faces serious obstacles: registration requirements conflict with privacy concerns around stigmatized behavior, and stigmatized workers may still face abuse from the officials meant to protect them. The piece argues that sex-positive advocates must grapple with these production ethics rather than ceding the conversation to anti-porn absolutists who refuse to discuss harm reduction.
key_points: The core problem is 'Schrödinger's Victim'—consumers cannot verify consent in porn because appearing enthusiastic is part of the performance
Ethical porn exists but costs more money and effort than free alternatives, creating a market failure when consumers are 'horny and impatient'
The proposed regulatory scheme mirrors ACA structure: mandatory standards, worker unions, producer exchanges, and subsidies to make ethical content price-competitive
Two fatal flaws: real-world ID requirements conflict with shame-driven privacy concerns, and stigmatized workers may be abused by the officials meant to protect them
The piece challenges sex-positive feminists—especially men who consume porn—to engage seriously with production ethics rather than avoiding the conversation
money_quotes: Some folks really can't handle the possibility that some women in porn might not be fully consenting. Once you allow that possibility, then you have to allow the possibility that you have watched and gained pleasure from an act of rape.
The only thing you can confidently take away from an interview is that the participant wants to earn another paycheck. It is part of the performance.
If we're demanding that consumers think rationally about these choices, right at the moment when they're feeling horny and impatient, we're not making any progress.
We need to make ethical porn cheaper and more convenient than its alternatives.
As long as we treat porn use as shameful, this raises privacy concerns. If people are hesitant to claim the subsidies, the system fails—a regulated market is a three-legged stool.
Perhaps we should consider at least a modicum of sexual gratification to be a basic need, like shelter, food, and clean water.
Those of us who think that performative sex can be joyous, beautiful, and valuable, face a real moral burden in formulating a response to this problem that rejects sex-negativity and shame.
posts.key_insight: The ethical consumption of porn is structurally impossible under current market conditions because shame prevents the transparency and regulation that would guarantee consent.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil promotes an upcoming panel discussion on how mega-foundations and big philanthropy undermine democratic processes. The panel, which she will moderate, features experts who have written critically about the outsized influence of wealthy donors on public policy. She also mentions an upcoming talk on data journalism at Cornell Tech and reflects on her participation at the Rebellious Lawyer's Conference at Yale, where she discussed Occupy and the legal profession.
key_points: Mega-foundations wield undemocratic influence over public policy through their concentrated wealth
The panel brings together critics of big philanthropy including authors who have written 'Plutocrats at Work' and 'Beware of Big Donors'
O'Neil connects her work on data journalism to broader concerns about power, accountability, and activism
The post highlights a network of activist lawyers and scholars working on tax justice and financial reform
money_quotes: How Big Philanthropy Undermines Democracy
Plutocrats at Work: How Big Philanthropy Undermines Democracy
posts.key_insight: The concentrated power of mega-foundations and big donors poses a threat to democratic decision-making that deserves serious public scrutiny.

posts.summary: Fifteen students from Corinthian College's Everest program have launched a debt strike, refusing to repay loans for an education they claim was fraudulent. O'Neil supports the strikers, arguing that Corinthian was a predatory company that gamed the federal loan system while making false promises to students. She frames the students as fraud victims who shouldn't bear the financial burden. The post also highlights the Rolling Jubilee initiative, which freed $13 million in student debt.
key_points: The 'Corinthian 15' are refusing to repay student loans as an act of protest against predatory for-profit education
Corinthian College exploited the federal loan system while defrauding students with false promises
The debt strike challenges the Department of Education to protect students rather than creditors
For-profit schools represent an extreme version of the broader problem of debt-financed higher education in America
money_quotes: Corinthian College is a predatory and fraudulent company which was in the business of gaming the federal loan system while making false promises to its students.
Those students are victims of fraud and should not be the ones paying back the government money for an education they never got.
Instead, Corinthian should pay back the money.
For-profits schools are just an extreme version of our increasingly untenable system of debt-financed higher education.
By declaring a strike, they are taking a stand for all student debtors.
posts.key_insight: When for-profit colleges commit fraud, the victims—students—should not be forced to repay loans for an education they never received; the predatory institutions should bear that burden.

posts.summary: O'Neil draws a direct parallel between 19th-century phrenology and modern big data hiring tools, arguing both use pseudo-scientific methods to validate existing biases. She uses her 1911 Encyclopedia Britannica's racist 'Negro' entry as evidence of how scientific-sounding data once legitimized discrimination. The post critiques an NPR story about video game-based personality assessments used in hiring, noting these tools have no scientific validation yet are trusted simply because they involve data. O'Neil argues that big data hiring tools may simply replicate existing biases by selecting for people who remind us of ourselves, just dressed up in modern technological language.
key_points: Phrenology was once considered legitimate science and used to justify racist beliefs with data and measurements
The 1911 Encyclopedia Britannica demonstrates how elite scholarly institutions embraced racist pseudo-science
Modern big data hiring tools like video game personality assessments have no scientific validation behind them
People become 'awed and confused' around scientific facts and figures, making pseudo-science effective as validation
Big data hiring may replicate discrimination not through deliberate racism but through algorithmic selection for similarity
money_quotes: This was produced by a culture that was using pseudo-scientific nonsense to validate an underlying toxic and racist mindset.
Because people become awed and confused around scientific facts and figures, it seemed to work as a validating argument in 1911.
What we've got is a new kind of awful pseudo-science, which replaces measurements of skulls with big data.

There's no reason to think this stuff is any less biased or discriminatory either: given that there's no actual science behind it, we might simply be replicating a selection method to get people who we like and who remind us of ourselves.
We're supposed to just believe in it because, you know, data is objective and everything.
This is, unfortunately, not innovative. This is just the big data version of phrenology.
posts.key_insight: Big data tools used without scientific validation are simply modern phrenology—pseudo-science that lends a veneer of objectivity to existing biases and discrimination.

posts.summary: Scott Walker's attacks on the University of Wisconsin misidentify the real crisis in higher education: the predatory for-profit college industry. These schools have exploded in the past decade, targeting vulnerable populations with fraudulent marketing while extracting billions in federal aid. Students leave with crushing debt, poor education, and low graduation rates. Rather than Obama's proposed ranking system, O'Neil argues for aggressive fraud detection and redirecting public funds from for-profits to community colleges and state universities that actually serve the public good.
key_points: For-profit colleges game federal student aid programs, with Corinthian alone obtaining $1.4 billion in federal dollars in 2010—more than all 10 UC campuses combined
The industry deliberately targets vulnerable populations (poor, minority, single mothers) with misleading ads and aggressive 'poke the pain' recruitment tactics
Community colleges can't meet demand partly because public funds are being diverted to for-profits, forcing students into more expensive predatory alternatives
Even when fraud is discovered, neither students nor taxpayers get their money back
Education as a profit center transforms admissions counselors into salespeople and students into consumers to be exploited
money_quotes: When education becomes a profit center, things go awry: admissions counselors become salespeople, students become consumers to be wrung for every last dime, and administrators become executives who cash out while students and taxpayers are left with the tab.
They represent a revenue-maximizing industry which game the federal student aid programs for the poor and for veterans.
The industry specifically targets vulnerable, poor, minority single mothers online with misleading ads promising an easy degree and a new life.
Once they have a phone number, they have trained recruiters repeatedly call and 'poke the pain' of their targets.
People who have been told to get a degree to pull themselves out of poverty are often faced with two options: enrolling at a nearby community college, or at a for-profit.
Instead he should create a flat-out fraud detection system, built explicitly to be harder to game than the current watered-down regulatory framework.
posts.key_insight: The real higher education crisis isn't lazy professors at state schools but a predatory for-profit industry that extracts billions in public funds while targeting vulnerable populations with fraudulent promises.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where Cathy O'Neil (as 'Aunt Pythia') answers reader questions on relationships, ethics, and life decisions. She critiques a TV show called Sex Box, recommends books about human sexuality, and gives frank advice on topics ranging from unhappy marriages to awkward romantic advances. The column showcases her characteristic bluntness, particularly when she tells someone in an unhappy marriage to stop being cowardly and actually communicate. She also delivers a cutting one-liner about marketing being brain manipulation when asked about Google's algorithmic power.
key_points: Waiting for your spouse to end a marriage is cowardly - if you're unhappy, say so directly rather than hoping they'll make the 'easier' choice for you
Marketing IS brain manipulation - that's literally what it is, 'turning brains into things that pay you for stuff'
When someone crosses boundaries (like hitting on you inappropriately), respond with empathy rather than judgment, but also don't encourage it - go boring and distant
Your professional reputation matters more than convenience - maintain your work's integrity even after publication
Life decisions aren't game theory - 'This is not a game, it's your life'
money_quotes: Marketing *is* manipulating brains. That's exactly what marketing is, turning brains into things that pay you for stuff.
I think that people who wait for their spouse to end a marriage are cowardly.
Fuck that. Get more selfish (if you call it that) and focus on your own unhappiness, and screw the guilt.
This is not a game, it's your life.
Tell your spouse you're unhappy, because it's more courageous, because it might be fixable, and because it will be much more likely to get fixed if you talk about it.
Some of this stuff might be pseudo-science, but I don't care, because it's about sex.
posts.key_insight: Stop treating your life like a strategic game where you wait for optimal outcomes - be direct about what you want and need, whether in relationships, work, or understanding how corporations manipulate you.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines two developments in statistical methodology: a psychology journal's decision to ban p-values, and concerns about evidence-based sentencing models. She notes that while p-values are easily gamed through repetitive testing, any replacement metric faces similar vulnerability. The sentencing models suffer from both gaming (respondents lying on questionnaires) and fundamental unfairness in punishing people for circumstances beyond their control like poverty or family history.
key_points: P-values are 'thoroughly gamed' through repetitive testing, skewing the scientific corpus toward certain testing situations
Any replacement for p-values risks becoming similarly gameable
Evidence-based sentencing models can be manipulated by inmates lying on questionnaires
These models punish people for factors like poverty, associations, and parental incarceration—circumstances beyond individual control
money_quotes: P-values are thoroughly gamed and too easy to achieve with repetitive testing, resulting in a corpus that is skewed towards such testing situations.
If you get rid of p-values you have to replace them with something to give you an idea of whether a statistical result is interesting.
They too may quickly become gamed.
The people filling out the questionnaires can and do lie in order to game their scores and leave jail earlier.
They punish people for being poor or for 'hanging out with the wrong crowd' or even for having parents that went to jail.
posts.key_insight: Statistical measures and algorithmic systems share a fundamental vulnerability: any metric that becomes a target for optimization will inevitably be gamed, and the resulting systems often punish people for circumstances beyond their control.

posts.summary: This post highlights a Huffington Post piece by Alt Banking critiquing Mayor de Blasio's affordable housing plan. The piece argues that the plan's definition of 'affordable' is disconnected from reality, with apartments deemed affordable requiring incomes more than twice the NYC median. The critique exposes how the city gives massive tax breaks to private developers while producing minimal truly affordable units. The authors suggest that direct public investment of those forgone tax revenues would be far more effective than incentivizing private development.
key_points: The mayor's plan defines 'affordable' as $41,500/year rent for families earning $138,435, more than double the actual NYC median income of $63,000
NYC gave developers $1.2 billion in tax breaks in 2013 to build 153,000 units, of which only 12,000 met even the inflated affordability definition
The plan will 'build or preserve' 200,000 units but only actually build 8,000 units per year
The current approach enriches developers while failing people in shelters or at risk of homelessness
money_quotes: Most New Yorkers cannot afford an 'affordable apartment' by the mayor's standards.
The mayor's plan will squander public assets, enrich real estate developers, but do very little for the record number of people living in the shelter system.

In 2013, New York City gave private developers a pass on $1.2 billion in taxes in order to stimulate the building of 153,000 units of housing ― just 12,000 of which met the messed-up definition of affordability.
Hard to believe we couldn't have done a lot better by simply collecting those taxes.
The mayor's plan tracks the pattern New York City has religiously followed for quite some time of trying to 'incentivize' private development.
posts.key_insight: New York's affordable housing policy uses definitions of affordability that exclude most residents while funneling billions in tax breaks to developers who deliver minimal public benefit.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares her preparation for a Slate Money podcast episode, admitting uncertainty about why patent trolls deserve their negative reputation. She tentatively defends the patent system as working well when time limitations aren't extended and patents are valid, while inviting readers to challenge her view. The post also teases other podcast topics and a mysterious 'number of the week.'
key_points: Patent trolls may not be as problematic as their reputation suggests
The patent system generally works well when time limits are respected and patents are legitimate
Open invitation for readers to provide counterarguments
money_quotes: To tell you the truth, so far I don't know why patent trolls are all that bad, besides the fact that they obviously have a terrible sounding name.
It seems like the patent system works in many ways for good, at least when there's no weird extensions of the time limitations and the original patent was valid.
posts.key_insight: The negative framing of 'patent trolls' may obscure a more nuanced reality about how the patent system functions.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) responds to reader questions on personal and social dilemmas. She advises a gay math student on how to address a friend's inappropriate sexual advances toward straight colleagues, suggesting direct but compassionate confrontation. She responds to a website navigation complaint with self-deprecating honesty about WordPress limitations. She counsels someone struggling with infidelity to have an honest conversation with their partner about their nature rather than making empty promises. Finally, she humorously deflects a question about math study recommendations by admitting she learns from Wikipedia and people, not textbooks.
key_points: Direct, honest confrontation is the best approach when a friend's behavior is harmful - even if it causes temporary shame and anger
When dealing with recurring infidelity, honesty about one's nature is preferable to empty promises of change
The 'six-month pass' concept suggests newly-out individuals deserve grace while adjusting, but not indefinitely
Self-deprecating humor can be an effective way to acknowledge limitations while still engaging with readers
Unwanted sexual advances are inappropriate regardless of the gender or orientation of those involved
money_quotes: Think of it as powerful self-love which has been donated to you by a good friend, along with a strong cup of tea and a delicious piece of chocolate babka from Breads Bakery.
It's that time again when I take your perfectly reasonable questions and utterly fuck them up with terrible suggestions.
My opinion (barring exceptional cases) is that people who come out before the age of 25 should get a 6-month pass to clean up whatever shit they brought with them from their straight days.
For the cheaters I know, at least the successful ones, they don't lie to their partners and pretend they'll never again stray.
For fuck's sake, don't wait until you are dying to fuck some cutie at work to bring up this topic, because that will come out and then jealousy will ensue.
I learned everything I know from wikipedia and other people. I basically never read technical books.
Congratulations, you've wasted yet another Saturday morning with Aunt Pythia!
posts.key_insight: Honest, direct communication about difficult truths - whether confronting a friend's harmful behavior or acknowledging one's own nature to a partner - is more valuable than comfortable evasions or empty promises.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the concept of illegitimate international debt, questioning when citizens should be obligated to repay loans taken on by corrupt previous regimes. She cites Argentina, Greece, and Spain as examples where citizens feel no personal responsibility for debt that enriched elites and banks. The post highlights a new proposal called Preemptive Contract Sanctions, which would allow the international community to declare certain debts odious from the outset, making it harder for corrupt regimes to borrow. O'Neil endorses the broader idea that all debt should be viewed on a spectrum of morality rather than automatically legitimized once it enters the financial system.
key_points: Debt taken on by corrupt regimes and amplified by predatory banks creates a moral question about citizen obligation to repay
The Preemptive Contract Sanctions proposal would allow international agreement to declare certain debt illegitimate before it's even issued
Bank bailouts funded by taxpayers, as in Spain, raise questions about whether that debt is legitimate
Current debt crises in Argentina and Greece are more complex because they built up over many years
Financial contracts become 'sanitized and legitimized' once they enter the system, obscuring their moral origins
money_quotes: When is debt so odious that the taxpayers of a government have no obligation to pay it back?
The average individual citizen feels very little personal responsibility to pay that debt back, consisting as it does of interest payments to the banking system.

If the international community can get together and agree that such debt is odious, and that they will not lift a finger in the future to help the borrower get their money back, then it would be harder to borrow the money.
I like the idea of all debt living on a spectrum of morality.
Too often when contracts enter the financial system, they are utterly sanitized and legitimized in the eyes of the international community.
posts.key_insight: Financial debt should exist on a moral spectrum rather than being automatically legitimized once it enters the system, and the international community should have mechanisms to declare predatory or corrupt debt illegitimate.

posts.summary: This is a brief personal post about the author's bluegrass band, the Tomtown Ramblers, having an energetic band practice. The post shares photos of the band learning songs and singing three-part harmony. It includes a self-deprecating joke about compensating for lack of talent with numbers, and invites musicians to jam with them at the Clearwater Festival.
key_points: The Tomtown Ramblers had an enthusiastic band practice session
The band performs three-part harmony, requiring close physical proximity
Self-aware humor about amateur musicianship
Open invitation to join them at Clearwater Festival
money_quotes: What we lacked in talent we made up for in numbers.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted personal update about the author's musical hobby, showing a playful side outside her usual data science commentary.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares a reflective moment about finding perspective through contemplating astronomical phenomena. She describes how thinking about long-term cosmic events helps her escape from the cynical topics she usually dwells on. The post centers on a video about Earth's orbital mechanics and the realization that nothing in the solar system is truly permanent unless physical laws force it to be. She explores questions about Earth's aphelion and perihelion, noting that the timing of perihelion near winter is coincidental rather than predetermined.
key_points: Contemplating cosmic time scales provides mental relief from day-to-day cynicism
Nothing in the solar system is permanent unless a physical law forces it to be
The timing of Earth's perihelion near the beginning of winter is coincidental, not inevitable
The different periods of Earth's orbital plane and axial tilt demonstrate cosmic impermanence
money_quotes: Sometimes the stuff I think about gets me down. I mean, jeez, I think about cynical stuff all the time, and I need to rest my brain sometimes.
The take-away: nothing is permanent unless there is actually a physical law forcing it to be.
There's really nothing that we can rely on, is there?
posts.key_insight: Contemplating the impermanence of even seemingly fixed cosmic phenomena can provide perspective and relief from everyday concerns.

posts.summary: This is an advice column post where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil's humorous persona) responds to reader questions on Pi Day. The column covers an elaborate (likely fabricated) romantic scenario involving a boyfriend's mother, questions about online gambling legality, office cat management, finding university data for op-eds, and a sincere question about navigating dating with vaginismus. O'Neil's responses range from playful disbelief to genuinely supportive advice, showcasing her characteristic blend of irreverence and empathy.
key_points: O'Neil recognizes and calls out an obviously fabricated erotic letter while appreciating its creative writing quality and math-themed innuendos
She provides practical perspective on online gambling's complicated legal status in the US
When asked about finding public university data, she admits uncertainty and defers to readers, acknowledging gaps in her knowledge
Her response to the vaginismus question is genuinely supportive, emphasizing that women owe no explanations or apologies about their bodies to casual partners
The column format parodies public radio while being 'more titillating' - self-aware about its entertainment value
money_quotes: It's just like public radio except more titillating.
Holy. I can't, even. I mean, for fuck's sake.
You should totally start writing singles for Amazon Kindle. You are really miles ahead of your competition.
Personally I have been very very consistently unlucky with poker, so I'd say luck.
It is very very clear in my head that you have not made any vaginal promises to a man just by picking him up in a bar.
You have no apologies to make, and neither do you have explanations.
Don't stand for anything except gratitude.
posts.key_insight: Even in a playful advice column format, O'Neil demonstrates her core values: skepticism toward suspicious claims, honesty about the limits of her knowledge, and fierce advocacy for women's bodily autonomy.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the launch of Data Justice, a new collaborative project with lawyer Nathan Newman and law professor Frank Pasquale. The initiative aims to challenge the exploitation and economic inequality arising from big data practices. The project brings together expertise from law, computer science, and technology to build a movement for data justice. O'Neil positions this as a continuation of the critical work her collaborators have already done on algorithmic discrimination and corporate opacity.
key_points: Data Justice is a cross-disciplinary collaboration between a data scientist, lawyer, and law professor
The project specifically targets economic inequality and exploitation enabled by big data
The initiative aims to build a broader movement, not just conduct academic research
The team brings together complementary expertise: O'Neil's data science background, Newman's policy advocacy on Google ad discrimination, and Pasquale's legal scholarship on algorithmic opacity
money_quotes: Challenging Rising Exploitation and Economic Inequality from Big Data
We hope to build a movement on the data justice front by working across various disciplines like law, computer science, and technology
posts.key_insight: Addressing the harms of big data requires cross-disciplinary collaboration between technologists, lawyers, and policy advocates to build a movement for accountability.

posts.summary: O'Neil dismantles Jonathan Knee's New York Times review of Bruce Schneier's 'Data and Goliath,' arguing that Knee's critique fundamentally misunderstands both surveillance and data privacy issues. She contends that Knee dismisses legitimate concerns about mass surveillance by appealing to authority and ignores the real costs of data collection including false positives and potential for misuse. O'Neil argues that Knee cannot comprehend any argument that threatens existing business models, even when those models may themselves be anti-democratic. She defends Schneier's work as a patriotic act of alerting citizens to threats they don't fully understand, while Knee represents the establishment interests invested in maintaining the status quo.
key_points: Mass surveillance creates real costs (false positives, misuse potential, resources) that surveillance defenders conveniently ignore
Business professors and investment bankers have an inherent blind spot for arguments that threaten existing business models
Citizens cannot meaningfully consent to trading their privacy when they don't understand what they're trading
Alerting people to threats they don't yet recognize is not anti-democratic—it's patriotic
Appeals to 'we normal citizens can't judge' on surveillance issues favor opaque authority over democratic accountability
money_quotes: Any time someone uses the phrase 'bad guys,' it makes me wince.
Wait, before we move on, who is more pro-democracy, the guy who wants to stop totalitarian social control methods, or the guy who wants to leave it to the opaque authorities?
I can't help but think that Knee cannot understand any argument that would threaten the business world as he knows it.
By Knee's logic, even if the current business model is subverting democracy – which I also argue in my book – we shouldn't tamper with it because it's a business model.
Americans as a group do not feel this way because they do not understand what they are trading when they trade their privacy.
There are very few people devoting themselves to alerting people to the dark side of big data, and Schneier is one of them. It is a patriotic act.
It's just like waterboarding – Knee says – we can't be sure it isn't a great fucking idea.
posts.key_insight: Defenders of mass surveillance and corporate data collection often cannot recognize threats to democracy because they are embedded in the very systems that benefit from the status quo.

posts.summary: This guest post presents a nuanced dialogue between two parents (one a former English teacher, one a math professional) about PARCC standardized testing and Common Core education reforms. While both support having common educational standards and see value in consistent assessment, they strongly oppose using test scores for teacher evaluations, citing statistical unreliability of value-added models. They discuss concerns about computer-based testing widening achievement gaps and reducing computers to 'paperweights' instead of learning tools. The authors conclude by letting their children take PARCC while advocating for systemic change from within, noting that New Jersey has stepped back from high-stakes implementation compared to New York.
key_points: Common standards for curriculum can be valuable, but standardized testing should be separated from teacher evaluation—the models simply aren't accurate enough at the individual level
Computer-based testing represents a massive missed opportunity: technology should be a teaching tool for experiments and visualization, not primarily a testing medium
The achievement gap widens when computer-based tests essentially measure keyboard proficiency and technology access rather than actual knowledge
Schools treating PARCC as high-stakes end up doing harmful test prep at the expense of real learning—ironically losing the most
Whether to opt out depends on local implementation: low-stakes environments in NJ versus Cuomo's push for 50% weight on test scores in NY create very different calculations for parents
money_quotes: Having student test results impact teacher evaluations is my biggest objection to PARCC, by far.
Here's the most amazing tool human beings have ever invented, and we're going to use it primarily as a paperweight.
Students cannot be taught to think critically in six months.
I also found NCLB naïve in its 'every child can learn it all' attitude. I mean, yes, sure, any child can learn. But kids aren't starting out at the same place or with the same support.
Ironically, schools that treat the PARCC like a high-stakes test are the ones losing the most.
Shame on us, as grown ups, for polluting our kids' education with our anxieties!
The models we have today, even the most state-of-the-art ones, can give you useful aggregate information, but they just don't work at that level of detail.
posts.key_insight: Standardized testing and common educational standards are fundamentally different issues that get unhelpfully lumped together, and while consistent standards can benefit students, the statistical models used for teacher evaluation are too unreliable to be fair or effective.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil examines the Obama campaign's celebrated use of A/B testing and questions whether optimizing for donations and sign-ups actually serves democracy. She explains that while the campaign ran 500 A/B tests to increase donation conversions by 49%, the definition of 'success' was purely financial. She argues this creates an arms race where both parties become increasingly manipulative in their fundraising tactics. The post challenges readers to consider whether data science in politics should optimize for something beyond money.
key_points: A/B testing in the Obama campaign measured success solely by donations and email sign-ups, not democratic engagement
Optimizing political campaigns for money creates an arms race that makes both parties more manipulative over time
The easiest thing to measure in politics is money, so everything gets optimized to that metric while other considerations are ignored
Email sign-ups are essentially just a pipeline to future donation requests, not meaningful civic engagement
The structural question of whether A/B testing finds global vs local maxima is less important than what we're optimizing for
money_quotes: Nowhere is there even a slight nod to the question of whether this stuff will improve or even maintain democracy.
In the end what they did was optimize a bunch of people's donation money. Is that something we should cheer?
It seems more like an arms race with the Republican party, in which the Democrats pulled ahead temporarily.
All it means is that the fight for donations will be even more manipulative, by both sides, by the next presidential election cycle.

The problem with big data in politics is that the easiest thing you can measure in politics is money, which means everything is optimized to that metric of success, leaving all other considerations ignored and probably stifled.
Please tell me I'm wrong. Or suggest a way we can measure something and optimize to something that is less cynical than the size of a war chest.
posts.key_insight: When political campaigns use data science to optimize for easily measurable metrics like donations, they create an escalating arms race of manipulation while ignoring harder-to-measure democratic values.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on why science fiction never appealed to her growing up as the daughter of sci-fi-loving atheist nerds. She identifies the genre's adolescent male fantasy tropes—brilliant misunderstood men, stupid but gorgeous women who exist to be conquered—as fundamentally alienating to female readers. Using a memorably absurd Heinlein quote as evidence, she explains how boys could identify with these narratives while girls faced impossible choices: deny their gender or prove themselves exceptions to the 'bimbo' rule. The post concludes warmly, noting that she and her husband now mock these tropes together.
key_points: Classic sci-fi was dominated by adolescent male power fantasies with minimal actual science
Female readers faced a lose-lose proposition: either deny their gender or constantly prove they weren't like 'those women'
Male readers could genuinely identify with protagonists in ways that were impossible for women
The genre's treatment of women—as stupid, vile obstacles to be sexually conquered—made sustained interest impossible for her
Progress exists: her husband can now laugh at the ridiculous posturing he once identified with
money_quotes: I felt warm and relaxed, as if I had just killed a man or had a woman.
Too much getting laid and proving yourself to other men, too little science. Waaay too little science.
Most of the time, the set-up seemed along these lines: some extremely macho guy, misunderstood and brilliant, gets into some kind of jam and uses his brilliant mind to find his way out of it.
When I read it, I naturally concluded that it wasn't about me at all, that it was in fact alien to me.
Either of these attitudes, both of which I tried on at different times, were and are fucked up.
Unlike those bimbos who couldn't even fix a broken warp drive, I would be different. I'd have deep thoughts too, just like men.
posts.key_insight: Genre fiction that centers male power fantasies forces female readers into psychologically damaging choices—erasure or exceptionalism—neither of which allows genuine engagement with the work.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post about an upcoming talk at American University. Cathy O'Neil mentions she will be speaking to math and statistics faculty and the general public about her then-in-progress book 'Weapons of Math Destruction.' The post serves primarily as a promotional notice with a link to the event poster.
key_points: O'Neil is giving a public talk at American University's math and statistics department
The talk covers the themes of her upcoming book 'Weapons of Math Destruction'
She describes the content as 'the nerdy low-down' on her book's subject matter
money_quotes: I'll be giving an evening talk to the math and statistics folks (and the general public) at American University on Weapons of Math Destruction.
So basically the nerdy low-down on what I'm writing about in my book.
posts.key_insight: This announcement post offers a glimpse into O'Neil's early efforts to bring academic and public attention to algorithmic accountability before her book's publication.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares a personal playlist of songs by male singer-songwriters about their relationships with their mothers. As a mother of three sons, she finds comfort in these songs that explore complex mother-child bonds, noting she'd 'rather have hate than nothing' when it comes to being remembered. The post moves through Sufjan Stevens, Iron and Wine, John Lennon, and Conor Oberst, creating an emotional arc from melancholy to hope.
key_points: Music about mother-child relationships resonates deeply with parents facing eventual separation from their children
Complex or 'fraught' relationships are still preferable to being forgotten entirely
The curated playlist moves deliberately from grief through pain to hope
Art helps process the inevitable letting go that parenting requires
money_quotes: I have a soft spot for sensitive singer songwriting men talking about their mothers, even when it's a fraught relationship.
I've got three sons, and it's nice to imagine they won't forget about me once they leave.
And hey, I'd rather have hate than nothing!
a song I credit with the internal efforts I've made (so far) to let my sons someday leave me
posts.key_insight: The fear of being forgotten by one's children is so primal that even songs about painful mother-child relationships offer comfort to parents.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses several reader questions, with the central piece tackling a 30-something virgin's anxieties about dating and sex. Aunt Pythia challenges his fear-based approach to relationships, arguing that worrying about partners' sexual history and STDs is counterproductive and likely stems from his mother's religious sex-negativity. She advocates for treating sex as normal human activity rather than something mythical or dangerous, and criticizes the assumption that online dating profiles provide sufficient information about compatibility. Other questions cover career pivots to data science, appropriate conference behavior for meeting romantic partners, and the validity of Google search data about sex.
key_points: Sexual compatibility can only be determined by actually having sex with someone, and multiple partners may be necessary before finding the right match
Asking about someone's number of sexual partners is never appropriate or relevant - it only matters to 'freaked out virgins' convinced to worry about STDs
Online dating data is mostly misleading and insufficient for understanding whether you could love someone
Conference behavior crosses into creepy territory when professional interest in someone's work is used as a pretext for romantic pursuit
Google search data about sex reveals people's insecurities, but suffers from selection bias - not everyone asks Google about their anxieties
money_quotes: Sex is just like prolonged hugging, except stickier.
Cultivate a reason for a woman to fall in love with you that is better and sexier than fear.
We are in a free market in terms of sex, for better or for worse.
If you run across someone who has had sex a bunch of times with a bunch of other people, then you should assume that they are doing it right.
Once you've gone this long without getting laid, it takes on mythical proportions. It doesn't need to.
Instead of saying people are insecure about their smelly particulars, I think we might be tempted to conclude that insecure people ask google about their insecurities.
I kind of love it when statistics point out how much people lie about sex.
posts.key_insight: Fear and shame around sex, often inherited from religious upbringing, create self-defeating dating behaviors that can only be overcome by treating intimacy as normal human connection rather than a dangerous mythical act.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares a mathematical wallet she received as a 'bribe' from mathematician Jeffrey Hakim, which features a visual proof of Nicomachus's Theorem. The theorem states that the sum of cubes from 1 to n equals the square of the sum from 1 to n. Rather than using the traditional induction proof, the wallet displays an elegant geometric proof using nested frames of squares. O'Neil walks readers through understanding the proof by computing the area of the figure two different ways—one yielding the sum of cubes, the other yielding the squared sum—demonstrating they must be equal.
key_points: Nicomachus's Theorem (sum of cubes equals squared sum) can be proven visually without induction
The geometric proof works by building frames of increasingly larger squares around a center
Computing the area two ways—by quarter-square dimensions versus by counting colored squares—yields both sides of the identity
The pattern generalizes because the dimensions align: (n+1) boxes of length n equals n boxes of length (n+1)
Visual proofs can be more illuminating than algebraic ones for certain identities
money_quotes: I accept mathematical bribes.
It's all in the name of education and fun with mathematics.
This kind of identity is kind of made for induction, no? Don't you think? Well Jeffrey Hakim had an even better idea.
It took me a couple of minutes to see why this is a proof.
If you can, just go ahead and pat yourself on your back.
That's why I got the wallet!
posts.key_insight: A beautiful visual proof can replace algebraic induction by showing that computing the same area two different ways yields both sides of a mathematical identity.

posts.summary: O'Neil reviews Nicole Aschoff's 'The New Prophets of Capital,' which examines how figures like Sheryl Sandberg, Oprah, John Mackey, and Bill Gates function as storytellers who simultaneously critique and protect capitalism. The book argues these 'prophets' encourage individual solutions while ignoring structural inequality. O'Neil finds the analysis of Sandberg's Lean In movement particularly valuable, though she considers the concluding chapter underdeveloped. She notes a potential inconsistency in how Gates is portrayed as both a free-market believer and an undemocratic influencer of education policy. Overall, she recommends the accessible, non-academic critique and plans to follow Aschoff's future work.
key_points: The four 'prophets' (Sandberg, Oprah, Mackey, Gates) serve as both public critics and private protectors of capitalism
Lean In ideology tells women to conform to existing systems rather than challenge them
Oprah's self-help philosophy assigns personal responsibility for happiness while ignoring structural inequality
Free market solutions to poverty are inherently flawed because poor people lack money to participate in markets
Mega-philanthropy like Gates' education reform efforts is fundamentally undemocratic despite good intentions
money_quotes: The unifying theme throughout the four chapters devoted to the prophets is how these four people manage to be both public critics and private protectors of the current economic system, with an emphasis on protection.
When Sandberg tells us to lean in, she's telling us to conform to the way things are, not to threaten it in any way.

When Oprah tells us that we have it in ourselves to live fantabulous lives, she's giving us personal responsibility to be happy and fulfilled, and structural inequality is not acknowledged or recognized.
When John Mackey or Bill Gates sees a problem, they set up a 'free market solution' to that problem, even though, by definition, poor people don't have money to pay for what they need.
If I really believed the Sandberg line, I'd still be working at a hedge fund, doing my best to please my colleagues and ignore my kids.
If I had bought into Oprah's context-free attitude, I'd blame people for their poverty and think it amounts to bad decision making.
posts.key_insight: Popular business leaders and philanthropists who appear to challenge the system actually reinforce it by promoting individual solutions that ignore structural inequality.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that New York's Value-Added Model (VAM) for teacher evaluations functions essentially as a random number generator that arbitrarily determines which teachers receive tenure. Under Cuomo's new rules, teachers cannot receive 'effective' ratings if their VAM score is 'ineffective,' regardless of principal evaluations or classroom observations. Using probability calculations, she demonstrates that if 30% of teachers are deemed 'ineffective' by VAM in any given year, then 35% of teachers would be denied tenure over four years purely by chance. She contends this arbitrary punishment is the point—reformers prefer random evaluation to systems where most teachers pass.
key_points: VAM scores override all other evaluation methods: a teacher cannot receive an 'effective' rating regardless of principal reviews or observations if their VAM score is 'ineffective'
Using binomial probability, if 30% are deemed ineffective yearly, 35% of teachers would fail to achieve tenure over four years through pure randomness
The political utility of VAM lies precisely in its ability to arbitrarily punish teachers, wielding power over unions
Reformers explicitly prefer arbitrary evaluation systems to ones where most teachers are rated effective
The true 'ineffective' cutoff percentage is unknown but deliberately set high enough to fail a significant number of teachers
money_quotes: It is approximately a random number generator.
Even so, it's still in use, mostly because it wields power over the teacher unions.
This is the political power of a terrible scoring system.
More than a third of teachers are being arbitrarily chosen to be punished by this opaque and unaccountable test.
Think of it as a biased coin flip, and 30% of the time – for any teacher and for any year – it lands on 'ineffective'.
In fact, they'd rather see arbitrary and random evaluation than see most people get through unscathed.
posts.key_insight: The VAM teacher evaluation system's value to reformers lies not in its accuracy but in its capacity to arbitrarily deny tenure to a substantial percentage of teachers, functioning as a tool of political power rather than genuine assessment.

posts.summary: O'Neil calculates that approximately 578 New York City teachers per year will be arbitrarily denied tenure due to the statistical noise inherent in Value-Added Model (VAM) scores. Building on a previous thought experiment, she uses data showing 16% of teachers are rated 'ineffective' and estimates that 12.7% of tenure decisions will be wrongly negative due to VAM's unreliability. Starting with roughly 7,000 new teachers hired annually and accounting for 35% attrition before tenure review, she arrives at 4,550 candidates—of whom 578 will be unfairly punished by what she characterizes as a 'random number generator.'
key_points: About 16% of NYC teachers are rated 'ineffective' under the VAM system across multiple years
After four years, an estimated 12.7% of teachers will be arbitrarily denied tenure based solely on VAM score variability
With approximately 4,550 teachers going up for tenure annually, this translates to 578 people wrongly denied
The calculation doesn't account for broader damage to teacher morale and instruction quality from standardized testing pressures
money_quotes: About 578 people will be denied tenure simply based on this random number generator we call VAM.
This says nothing about the hard-to-measure damage done to all the good teachers trying to teach their kids but having to deal with this standardized testing nonsense.
It's a wonder anyone is willing to work here.
posts.key_insight: Behind the seeming objectivity of teacher evaluation algorithms lies statistical noise that will arbitrarily derail hundreds of careers each year in New York City alone.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' responds to reader questions on topics ranging from career decisions in mathematics to dealing with sexism in academia to relationship issues. The most substantive response addresses a female math undergraduate facing pervasive sexism from peers who dismiss her accomplishments and assume she benefits from affirmative action. Aunt Pythia offers practical advice including never baking for the math department, ignoring bullying students, and challenging herself to speak up in class. The column also tackles whether to pursue a math PhD versus journalism, with advice to get the education you want while keeping options open.
key_points: Sexism in academic mathematics manifests as peers attributing women's success to affirmative action rather than merit, and treating female TAs with less respect than male ones
A math degree is valuable and flexible even if you end up doing something else - it teaches humility about your abilities and how to admit when you're wrong
The arrogant young men who make academic environments hostile often become less insufferable with age and humility, or self-select out entirely
To break into data journalism, start doing it - post pieces on blogs or Huffington Post to build a portfolio
Being quietly successful while others are loudly mediocre is still success - the people admitting you to programs see your merit even if insecure peers don't
money_quotes: Fuck those assholes. Seriously. Fuck them.
The worst kind of person is the arrogant young man, they are unbelievably insufferable.
Never bake anything for math people. Seriously, there's something about the act of baking in a department that brings out sexism.
Their egos are crying out in pain because they are threatened, and sometimes the noise is deafening, but learn to put on a set of ego headphones.
It's not something math professors tell you, mostly because they don't know this, but math Ph.D.'s or masters degrees impress people in the outside world.
Don't worry too much that the nerds around you are laser-focused, they might have changed completely in a few years, and it's really not a competition.
The truly insecure people often self-select out of the math scene altogether because they're afraid they can't cut it.
posts.key_insight: Women in mathematics should refuse to change their personalities to fit hostile environments, instead recognizing that peer hostility stems from insecurity and that their success is validated by the institutions that admit and recruit them.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques a New York Times opinion piece that blamed rising college costs solely on administrators, arguing the analysis was poorly sourced and misleadingly narrow. She appreciates a Slate takedown of the piece while noting she doesn't fully agree with it either. O'Neil argues that while administrator bloat is a real issue, it's a mistake to ignore other factors like reduced public funding and the diversion of federal aid to corrupt for-profit colleges. She calls for thinking beyond simple scapegoating toward systemic solutions like affordable no-frills state college options.
key_points: The NYTimes piece by Paul Campos blamed college costs entirely on administrators while playing 'fast and loose' with data and lacking proper citations
Hating college administrators is universal and valid, but it's intellectually lazy to pretend it's the only factor in rising costs
Reduced public funding for colleges combined with massive federal aid flowing to corrupt for-profit schools is a major overlooked problem
Corinthian Colleges received $1.4 billion in federal aid in 2010 alone—more than all 10 UC campuses combined
The conversation should shift toward creating truly affordable no-frills state college alternatives rather than just assigning blame
money_quotes: I really think the Times needs to either have footnotes or hyperlinks in their opinion pieces, because this guy was playing so fast and loose with his numbers that I had really no idea what he was talking about most of the time.
I mean really, who doesn't hate college administrators? As a former college administrator myself, I know it's universal; I certainly hated myself the entire time.
But that doesn't mean there's no other factors at all.
Corinthian obtained $1.4 billion in federal grant and loan dollars in 2010 alone, more than the 10 University of California campuses combined for that same year.
This system is in terrible need of repair.
Instead of simply hating on college admin, or rather, in addition to hating on admin, can we start thinking about an alternative no-frills state college system that is truly affordable?
posts.key_insight: Scapegoating college administrators for rising tuition costs is satisfying but intellectually incomplete—the real crisis involves defunded public education and federal dollars flowing to predatory for-profit schools.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques the Value-Added Model (VAM) for teacher assessment after attending a lecture by Boston College professor Henry Braun. She draws a parallel between Wall Street's 'culling' mentality and the education reform movement's obsession with individual teacher accountability. The post argues that focusing on assessing and blaming individual teachers ignores systemic issues like unequal funding, curriculum problems, and school culture. O'Neil questions whether individual responsibility can solve what is fundamentally a collective, societal problem, connecting this to broader critiques of how American culture places systemic burdens on individuals.
key_points: The VAM approach to teacher assessment is an inconsistent 'black box' that may not actually improve education
There's a fundamental split between reformers who want to improve educational systems versus those who want to assess individual teachers
The 'assess the teachers' crowd ignores systemic factors: school culture, curriculum quality, unequal funding
Wall Street's 'cull the bottom 10%' mentality is being inappropriately applied to education
Placing systemic problems on individuals (like Oprah's self-help advice) ignores context and structural barriers
money_quotes: The same mindset which served the country so poorly is now somehow being held up as a success story, and applied to other fields like public education.
Why do we think individual responsibility – i.e. finger pointing at individual teachers – is going to improve the overall system?
Fuck the school culture, fuck communicating with other teachers in the school. Fuck differences in curriculum or having old books or not having enough books due to unequal funding.
The problem with Oprah's reasoning is that it ignores real systemic problems and issues that radically affect certain parts of the population and make it much harder to take her advice.
It's context free in a world where context is more and more meaningful.
Whose problem is the achievement gap? Is it owned in tiny pieces by every teacher who dares to enter the profession?
posts.key_insight: Reducing systemic educational failures to individual teacher accountability is the same flawed thinking that let Wall Street escape responsibility for the financial crisis.

posts.summary: This is an advice column installment where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil's alter ego) answers reader questions on topics ranging from relationship communication and sex to career decisions and media messaging about fitness. The column opens with irreverent commentary on news about orthodox Jewish men refusing to sit next to women on planes. The centerpiece is practical life advice: she counsels a computer science graduate torn between academia and industry to pursue the masters degree while promising themselves to quit at the first sign of boredom, not difficulty. Throughout, she maintains a playful, frank tone about sex and relationships while touching on serious issues like fat-shaming in children's fitness programs.
key_points: When it comes to sexual communication in relationships, doing is sometimes better than saying, but pillow talk should be timed after both parties are satisfied to avoid defensiveness
There's an important distinction between fat-shaming children (harmful) and encouraging fitness (beneficial) - pro-fitness messaging is fine without the shaming component
For career decisions, instead of trying to plan your whole life, commit to something you'll likely enjoy for a while with a strict promise to change direction once you stop liking it
Challenges are reasons to stick with something; boredom is the signal to start looking around for new opportunities
Leo Buscaglia was 'an inspirational speaker before they became full of shit'
money_quotes: I am simply of the opinion that doing is even better than saying in some cases, especially cases where feelings can get easily bruised.

Instead of thinking about what you want to do for the rest of your life, do something that you are likely to enjoy for at least a while, with a strict promise to yourself to quit and change directions once you stop liking it.
A challenge is a reason to stick with it. At the first sign of boredom, however, you should start looking around.
This guy was an inspirational speaker before they became full of shit.
