There's a difference between fat-shaming kids (bad) and convincing kids to exercise (good).
Yes, I do love talking about sex as much as sex. I mean, maybe not as much, but it lasts much longer, so yes, as much.
posts.key_insight: Career and life decisions shouldn't be about finding your forever path, but about choosing something engaging now with permission to pivot when boredom—not difficulty—sets in.

posts.summary: Insurance companies use credit scores to set rates for homeowners and auto insurance, often charging people with poor credit double or more than those with good credit. O'Neil questions whether this practice reflects genuine risk assessment or exploitation of people with fewer options. She notes that homeowners insurance covers property contents, not mortgage payments, making the credit score connection dubious. The post also highlights that insurers target customers who don't shop around, undermining free market arguments. O'Neil frames this as predatory pricing that takes advantage of information asymmetry and limited choices for lower-income consumers.
key_points: Homeowners insurance rates can more than double based on credit score, despite covering property contents rather than mortgage payments
Auto insurance in Maryland charges people with bad credit 40% more, even though credit score doesn't indicate driving ability
Insurance companies deliberately target customers who don't comparison shop and charge them higher rates
The practice raises questions about whether pricing reflects actual risk or exploitation of people with fewer options
Free market competition fails when consumers lack good information
money_quotes: How much of this price discrimination comes from the insurance companies trying to understand and account for actual risk, and how much comes from their understanding that poorer people have fewer options and will simply pay predatory rates?
It's not a free market if not everyone actually has good information.
Someone with a poor credit score isn't obviously a bad driver.
Insurance companies actually target people who don't shop around and charge them more.
posts.key_insight: Credit score-based insurance pricing may be less about measuring risk and more about exploiting the limited options of lower-income consumers.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her participation in a UC Berkeley conference on Open Data, focusing on privacy, security, and civil rights challenges. She will give a tutorial explaining data science and modeling to lawyers, highlighting potential ethical concerns. She will also respond to a panel discussion on security and privacy law, bridging the gap between technical modeling and legal frameworks. The post reflects her role as a translator between the data science and legal communities.
key_points: Open Data initiatives release government datasets for public analysis, enabling useful applications but raising privacy concerns
There's a fundamental tension between promoting government transparency and protecting citizen privacy
Lawyers and data scientists need better communication to address the ethical implications of modeling
Legal frameworks around data need input from people who understand what modelers actually do with data
money_quotes: How can open data promote trust in government without creating a transparent citizenry?
I'm doing my best to explain to a room full of lawyers, in 40 minutes, what it is that modelers actually do with data, and how there might be ethical concerns.
This will be my chance to talk about whether the laws they are talking about, or the proposed changes in the laws, make sense to the world of modeling.
posts.key_insight: Effective data privacy law requires genuine dialogue between legal experts and data scientists who understand how models actually use and potentially misuse data.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction: How Big Data Increases Inequality and Threatens Democracy' will be published in May 2016. She notes the lengthy process of writing and publishing a book, expressing excitement about the timing before the election. The brief post also includes a nod to the Fight for 15 minimum wage movement happening at the time.
key_points: Book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' scheduled for May 2016 publication
Strategic timing to appear on summer reading lists and before the 2016 election
Writing and publishing a book takes longer than expected
Connects her work to broader social justice movements like Fight for 15
money_quotes: Weapons of Math Destruction: How Big Data Increases Inequality and Threatens Democracy
it takes a while to write a book, and then another few months to publish it
Fuck yeah! I'm so excited
posts.key_insight: The title alone—'Weapons of Math Destruction'—signals O'Neil's thesis that algorithms and big data are not neutral tools but can actively harm democracy and increase inequality.

posts.summary: The post critiques the growing use of workplace personality tests, which are now deployed by most major employers to screen out job applicants before interviews. Weber argues that while companies claim these tests reduce turnover by matching personality to 'company fit,' the real explanation is simpler: economic precarity and the tests themselves have stripped workers of bargaining power. She dismisses these assessments as pseudoscientific tools that primarily serve to cut HR costs while giving employers more control over desperate job seekers.
key_points: 8 out of 10 top private employers and 57% of employers overall now use personality tests to screen applicants
The tests use deliberately ambiguous questions and automatically disqualify over half of applicants without human review
Companies claim 25% lower turnover, attributing it to better 'company fit' screening
The author argues reduced turnover actually stems from economic precarity and reduced worker mobility caused by the tests themselves
These tests function primarily as cost-cutting measures that allow companies to shrink HR departments
money_quotes: Red lighted people never get an interview, and yellow lighted may or may not.
I have another theory about why people no longer leave shitty jobs, though.
The recession has made people's economic lives extremely precarious. Nobody wants to lose a job.
Now that everyone is using arbitrary personality tests, the power of the worker to walk off the job and get another job the next week has gone down.
Workplace personality tests are nothing more than voodoo management tools that empower employers.
I've compared them in the past to modern day phrenology, and I haven't seen any reason to change my mind since then.
The real 'metric of success' for these models is the fact that employers who use them can fire a good portion of their HR teams.
posts.key_insight: Workplace personality tests are pseudoscientific tools that don't actually predict job fit—they simply shift power to employers by creating barriers that trap workers in precarious positions while cutting HR costs.

posts.summary: The post compares three approaches to explaining NSA surveillance: the documentary CitizenFour, Bruce Schneier's technical book Data and Goliath, and John Oliver's comedic interview with Snowden. O'Neil argues that Oliver's 'dick pic' framing—showing how nude photos get captured in NSA dragnets—is the most effective at communicating what surveillance means to ordinary people. She agrees with Snowden that changing behavior due to surveillance represents a loss of liberty, and notes that private companies already have access to the same personal data we worry about the government collecting.
key_points: John Oliver's approach of explaining surveillance through the lens of nude photos is more effective for public understanding than technical explanations
Changing normal behavior because of surveillance is itself a loss of liberty, even if the behavior seems trivial
Most data collected by the government is also available to private companies like Facebook and Snapchat
Technical privacy protections as 'protest' require too much effort for most people to adopt
The focus on government surveillance distracts from the broader reality that our data is already widely accessible to corporations
money_quotes: He cleverly puts the dick pic on the story.
Changing what we normally do because of surveillance is a loss of liberty, even if it's dumb.
Almost every piece of data collected about us by our government is more or less for sale anyway. Just think about that.
When we are up in arms about the government having our dick pics, we forget that so do our phones, and so does Facebook, or Snapchat, not to mention all the backups on the cloud somewhere.
It is more meaningful for people worried about large scale discrimination, like me, than it is for people worried about case-by-case pinpointed governmental acts of power and suppression.
He frames it as a form of protest, but it seems like a LOT of work to me.
posts.key_insight: The most effective way to communicate complex surveillance issues is through relatable personal stakes, and our outrage at government data collection ignores that corporations already have the same access.

posts.summary: This is an advice column featuring 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) and guest philosopher 'Uncle Aristippus' answering reader questions on topics ranging from sexual intimacy to mathematics self-study to pickup artist culture. The column blends humor with genuine advice, treating questions about relationship dynamics, Amazon sales rankings, and abstract algebra with equal irreverence. The most substantive discussion critiques pickup artist communities as misogynistic and counterproductive for building real relationships, while acknowledging why socially anxious men find them appealing. The advice style is direct, witty, and occasionally dismissive of questions deemed insufficiently interesting.
key_points: Pickup artist culture is criticized as adversarial and counterproductive for forming genuine relationships, despite its appeal to socially anxious men
Mathematical self-study requires community engagement and active learning rather than just reading texts - 'It's a language! You can't just read it, you need to learn how to speak it'
Authors obsessing over Amazon sales rankings is a common but ultimately unproductive mania that distracts from actual writing
Sexual openness in long-term relationships is normal and healthy, with shame being something to either unlearn or consciously enjoy as part of the experience
Social anxiety underlying dating difficulties should be addressed at the root through therapy rather than band-aided with pickup techniques
money_quotes: It's a language! You can't just read it, you need to learn how to speak it.
Your kink is not my kink, but your kink is OK too!
I'd suggest laying off hanging out with them until they become humans.
Straight women's sexual liberation is greatly to your benefit.
It's worth addressing this problem at the root, rather than trying to band-aid it with fancy hats and rote conversational routines.
They are just so excited to finally feel in control and to get laid that nothing will penetrate their consciousness except if it stops working.
posts.key_insight: Whether discussing mathematics, relationships, or self-improvement schemes, the best approach is honest engagement with fundamentals rather than shortcuts or obsessive metric-tracking.

posts.summary: O'Neil voices support for the growing standardized testing opt-out movement, arguing that schools spend too much time on testing. She notes that the movement is gaining traction not just in New York but also in Florida, where limits on testing hours were recently enacted. While acknowledging that Common Core and over-testing are separate issues, she understands why parents conflate them. She encourages participation in a Brooklyn rally for the opt-out movement.
key_points: The standardized testing opt-out movement is growing nationally, with both New York and Florida taking action
Too much classroom time is devoted to testing, making opt-out a reasonable response
Common Core concerns and over-testing are distinct issues but understandably conflated by frustrated parents
Even conservative states like Florida are pushing back against excessive standardized testing
money_quotes: There's just too much time spent in our schools on testing, so pulling our kids out of that complete waste is an obvious step in the right direction.
And although I see lots of reasons to discuss Common Core issues separately from the over-reliance on testing, I can understand why parents are wrapping all of that stuff up together.
In Florida, of all places, they recently limited the number of hours kids can be made to take standardized tests.
posts.key_insight: The widespread parental revolt against standardized testing reflects legitimate concerns about how much instructional time is being sacrificed to assessment.

posts.summary: O'Neil identifies four distinct political camps in the big data world: corporate data collectors, security advocates, open data proponents, and civil rights advocates concerned with how data affects marginalized populations. She attended a UC Berkeley conference that unusually brought these camps together for cross-boundary dialogue. Each camp has different definitions of risk and different priorities, leading to divergent regulatory proposals. O'Neil positions herself in the civil rights camp, focusing on how big data disproportionately harms poor people through targeted scams and predatory practices. While the conference reached no firm conclusions, she values the conversation and calls for continued public debate between these groups.
key_points: The big data world contains four camps with distinct interests: corporate profit-maximizers, security/surveillance critics, open data advocates, and civil rights defenders
Each camp defines risks differently, leading to fundamentally different policy prescriptions and priorities
The civil rights camp focuses on how data systems harm vulnerable populations in daily life, not just dramatic surveillance scenarios
Open data advocates are split between activists seeking transparency and government officials promoting win-win narratives
Corporate interests were notably absent from the Berkeley conference, busy lobbying against regulation in Washington
money_quotes: I care about hackers and people unreasonably tracked and targeted by the NSA, but I don't think about that nearly as much as I think about how easy it is for poor people to be targeted by scam operations when they google for 'how do I get food stamps'.
The corporate big data camp was not represented in that room as far as I know, but that's because they're too busy lobbying for a continuation of little to no regulation in Washington.
There is no one ideal regulation that will fix everything, and for that matter some people involved don't believe that government regulations can ever work.

All of these camps have their various interests, and can make good arguments for them. Even more importantly, they each have their own definition of the risks, as well as the probability of those risks.

The people who worry about how this avalanche of big data is affecting the daily lives of citizens, not only when we are targeted by the NSA or by someone stealing our credit cards, but when we are born poor versus rich.
posts.key_insight: Different stakeholders in the big data debate aren't just disagreeing about solutions—they have fundamentally different definitions of what the risks are and who is most harmed.

posts.summary: This post documents Q's experience being stopped by police in the Bronx while driving a new car—the fourth time that week. After refusing to show his license due to the officer's aggressive tone and being subjected to racial slurs, Q was arrested on shifting charges (tinted windows, then a warrant for littering, then disorderly conduct). Police deleted his recordings of the incident, lost his possessions until a white friend arrived, and disabled his car. The piece illustrates how routine harassment and systemic abuse target Black men, with Q noting this happens 'every single day.'
key_points: Q was stopped four times in one week for 'driving while Black,' with police using pretextual excuses like tinted windows
The charges against Q shifted three times (tinted windows, littering warrant, disorderly conduct), revealing the arbitrary nature of the arrest
Police deleted Q's recordings of the incident, destroying evidence of their misconduct
Q's possessions were 'lost' until a white, well-dressed friend arrived—whom police assumed was a lawyer
The experience reflects a daily reality for Black men, where clothing and appearance determine whether you're treated as a citizen or criminal
money_quotes: The officers told Q to 'give me your fucking license'
He recorded them saying, 'We should just lock this black guy up.'
Q later found out his recordings had been deleted.
She said there was no warrant on his record and that he'd been locked up for disorderly conduct. This was the third charge he'd heard about.
Q thinks they assumed his friend was a lawyer because he was white and well dressed.
If you're wearing a suit and tie it's different, but when you're wearing something fitted and some jeans, you're treated as a criminal.
It's sad that people have to go through this on a daily basis, for what?
posts.key_insight: The criminal justice system operates with such arbitrary power over Black men that charges can shift three times, evidence can be deleted, and property can vanish—until a white witness appears.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil's humorous alter ego) dispenses relationship and life advice to readers. The column covers topics including open marriages and whether they'll become more common, the value of social justice activism and protests like Occupy, advice for a romantically inexperienced computer science student, and dating preferences. The tone is playful and irreverent while touching on substantive issues about social change, human connection, and the power of activist networks.
key_points: Social and cultural norms around relationships change rapidly - what seems fixed is often ephemeral, as evidenced by the swift progress in LGBTQ+ acceptance
Activism and protest movements create networks that connect issues in our minds that previously seemed disconnected, revealing patterns of systemic inequity
Attraction is something we generate ourselves rather than passively receive from others - we project attractiveness onto people and allow them to see our best selves
Being overly picky in romantic pursuits is like clenching too hard - it prevents satisfaction with reality
The Ferguson DOJ report represents progress made possible through sustained activist pressure and networked organizing
money_quotes: The longer I'm married the more I realize how much of a miracle it is that anyone can stand being in a long-term relationship with anyone else, including themselves.

People have always found ways to fuck each other, married or not, and this new-fangled way of talking and thinking about it is just that: a new-fangled way of talking and thinking about what's already happening.

We live in a network of people and in a system of power, and the way we learn about how that system functions or doesn't function is by questioning and critiquing the corners of the system we understand.
Those corners give way to corridors and rooms that we thought were disconnected but aren't, and we start to see patterns of inequity and structural failure.
People think that attraction comes from other people, but it's a damn lie. Attraction stems from ourselves.
When I have a crush on someone, I swoon half at their captivating mojo and the other half at my own ability to detect it and to amplify it.
Once upon a time we women got married because of stuff like economic need. Now that is thankfully more or less off the table.
posts.key_insight: Understanding systemic problems requires following the connections between seemingly separate issues until patterns of inequity emerge, while meaningful relationships require us to actively generate attraction rather than passively wait for it.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the feared surveillance state isn't a future possibility—it's already here, targeting black communities rather than the general population. She highlights a Guardian report on NYPD surveillance of Harlem residents, including children as young as 10, through social media monitoring and physical cameras. The program tracks 28,000 residents permanently, with police posing as young women to access private accounts. She contrasts this with the absence of similar surveillance of white Columbia students despite documented drug crimes, calling for limits on police data collection and its discriminatory application.
key_points: Mass surveillance concerns have materialized, but through local police targeting black communities rather than NSA monitoring everyone
NYPD surveils 28,000 Harlem residents including children as young as 10, using social media infiltration and permanent tracking lists
Police use deceptive tactics like posing as young women to access private social media accounts without parental notification
The surveillance is racially discriminatory: white Columbia fraternity members involved in drug crimes face no equivalent monitoring
Half of court cases in the area use social media data for convictions, making this surveillance consequential not theoretical
money_quotes: The thing that people like Snowden are worried about with respect to mass surveillance has already happened.
It's being carried out by police departments, though, not the NSA, and its targets are black men, not the general population.
People never get off these surveillance lists.
They pose as young women to gain access to 'private' accounts.
White fraternity brothers two blocks away at Columbia University are not on the lists, even though there was a big drug bust in 2010.
For anyone who wonders what a truly scary police surveillance state looks like, they need look no further than what's already happening for certain Harlem residents.
posts.key_insight: The surveillance state isn't a hypothetical future threat—it's a present reality selectively deployed against black communities while white populations committing similar offenses remain unwatched.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques the 'sharing economy' for exploiting workers under the guise of innovation. Companies like Uber avoid legal responsibilities by classifying workers as independent contractors on a 'platform' rather than employees. She highlights how these companies profit by skirting regulations while their workers lack basic protections. The post draws parallels between gig work and undervalued women's jobs from decades ago, both characterized as providing 'extra money' rather than real employment. O'Neil endorses legal efforts to hold these companies accountable for worker compensation.
key_points: Sharing economy companies evade employer responsibilities by calling themselves 'platforms' rather than employers
The term 'sharing' is a misleading euphemism for precarious piecework without benefits or protections
Framing gig work as 'extra money' echoes historical dismissal of women's labor
Companies valued at billions claim they cannot afford basic worker protections
Class-action lawsuits are beginning to challenge the sharing economy's labor practices
money_quotes: It seems like they are making money by skirting regulation and pretending not to have employees.
Plus it is offensive to think of the word 'share' in this context.
TaskRabbit and others call their workers 'micro-entrepreneurs,' but that is a poor description of precarious piecework.
The preferred phrasing of 'extra money' harks back to women's jobs of forty years ago.
And like those jobs, they don't come with things like insurance protection, job security, benefits — none of that old economy stuff.
Uber is what, the most highly valued startup in the world right now? Valued at over $40 billion? I think they can afford to pay for workers' comp and unemployment.
posts.key_insight: The 'sharing economy' is a rebranding of precarious labor that allows wealthy companies to externalize the costs of employment onto vulnerable workers.

posts.summary: O'Neil discusses her editor's request to survey existing big data books to ensure her forthcoming 'Weapons of Math Destruction' offers a distinct perspective. She notes that current books by Schneier, Pasquale, and Angwin focus primarily on privacy and corporate surveillance concerns. Her book differs by centering on social justice—examining how big data harms the poor, minorities, the mentally ill, and public school teachers. She situates her work within a framework of four political camps in big data discourse, positioning herself in the inequality-focused camp rather than the privacy or corporate accountability camps.
key_points: Existing big data books primarily address privacy and corporate surveillance, not social justice
O'Neil's book focuses on how big data increases inequality and harms vulnerable populations
She identifies four distinct political camps in the big data discourse
Each chapter of her book examines a specific population harmed by algorithmic systems
The open data angle represents yet another camp likely distant from her social justice focus
money_quotes: My book is primarily concerned with social justice issues, and each chapter gives an example of how big data is being used a tool against the poor, against minorities, against the mentally ill, or against public school teachers.
My book focuses on the ways big data increase inequality and further alienate already alienated populations.
posts.key_insight: The big data critique landscape has focused on privacy and corporate power, leaving a gap for analysis of how these systems specifically harm marginalized and vulnerable populations.

posts.summary: O'Neil proposes a definition of civilization based on freedom from harassment, arguing that truly civilized places allow people—especially women—to exist in public spaces without being bothered. She critiques the pervasive pressure on women to conform to beauty standards, pointing to media that celebrates effortless sexiness as part of the problem. The post calls for celebrating women's right to be 'dowdy' and indifferent to appearance without consequence.
key_points: Civilization should be measured by whether people can exist in public without harassment
Women face constant bombardment of messages about conforming to male-defined beauty standards
Media articles celebrating 'effortless' beauty still reinforce that women must be attractive
True freedom means being able to be utterly indifferent to one's appearance in public
money_quotes: Let's defend our right to be dowdy as shit.
A place where young girls and grown women aren't constantly bombarded by messages that they are expected to conform to a male fantasy definition of beauty.

I'd like instead an article specifically written for the woman who wants to know where they can walk around minding their own business and being utterly indifferent to their appearance, and they won't be bothered.
Special kudos to places where young women can walk around dressed in anything they want and still be left alone.
What makes a society civilized?
posts.key_insight: A truly civilized society would grant women the freedom to be completely indifferent to their appearance without facing harassment or social penalty.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' answers reader questions on topics ranging from Pi Day date formats to grading anxiety to Seattle's minimum wage debate. The column opens with a meta-plea for readers to submit more questions (especially provocative ones) to keep the column alive. The advice given is characteristically irreverent: suggesting arbitrary mathematical bases could make every day 'Super Pi Day,' recommending teachers lower expectations and grade on a curve to preserve sanity, and noting that restaurant closures blamed on minimum wage hikes are likely statistical noise. The column closes with brief political commentary on Bernie Sanders' candidacy and how Obama's micro-targeting machine may have closed the door on future insurgent candidates.
key_points: Pi Day's 'once in a century' framing is arbitrary - using different date formats or number bases, almost every day could qualify as special
Teachers should cope with disappointing grades by lowering expectations beforehand and grading on a curve, treating any comprehension as a victory
Claims that Seattle restaurant closures are caused by minimum wage hikes should be viewed skeptically given the industry's inherent volatility
Obama built the modern political micro-targeting machine but 'closed the door behind him,' potentially blocking future insurgent candidates
The column maintains a deliberately absurdist, self-deprecating tone while sneaking in substantive points about statistics and political economy
money_quotes: Instead of being disappointed when not everyone understands everything, you have to be overjoyed when someone understands something.
I lower my expectations to the point where I'm like, man I hope someone passed this test.
That way my fantasies always come true, even if the scores are crowded down around 17.
It doesn't mean those days becomes less super, just that almost every day is super.
Anybody looking for evidence that they are going out of business for a given reason would have plenty of statistical noise ready and willing to distract them.
Obama was an insurgent and won but also built the modern micro-targeting machine. He closed the door behind him.
posts.key_insight: Behind the column's irreverent humor lies a consistent skepticism toward arbitrary framing and cherry-picked evidence, whether applied to mathematical holidays, student assessment, or economic policy claims.

posts.summary: China has announced plans to implement a comprehensive citizen scoring system using big data, developed in partnership with tech giants Alibaba, Baidu, and Tencent. The system expands beyond traditional credit scores to include purchasing behavior, social connections, and political alignment. While Western observers frame this as uniquely Chinese authoritarianism, O'Neil argues that similar 'social credit scoring' mechanisms are already being implemented in the United States through alternative lending and reputation systems. The post challenges the assumption that such surveillance-based scoring is unimaginable in Western democracies.
key_points: China's citizen scoring system incorporates online behavior, purchases, social networks, and political loyalty into a single reputation score
Major Chinese tech companies (Alibaba, Baidu, Tencent) are partnering with the government to build this surveillance infrastructure
A Chinese official justified the system by arguing that when morality doesn't constrain behavior, systems must restrict actions instead
Western observers claim such government-corporate data exploitation is 'unimaginable' in the West
O'Neil contends that similar social credit scoring is already being implemented in the US through alternative lending practices
money_quotes: When people's behavior isn't bound by their morality, a system must be used to restrict their actions
Government and big internet companies in China can exploit 'Big Data' together in a way that is unimaginable in the West
I guess I'm wondering whether that's really true
I see this kind of 'social credit scoring' being widely implemented here in the United States
posts.key_insight: The dystopian citizen scoring system China is building openly may already be emerging in subtler forms in the United States through private-sector data exploitation.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the launch of Occupy Summer School, a three-week program organized by the Alt Banking group for high school students in downtown Brooklyn. The program is structured progressively: the first week brings in organizers and activists to inspire students, the second week lets students explore issues and choose what to protest, and the third week involves planning and executing an actual protest with media outreach. The approach emphasizes student agency, letting them lead the way on topic selection based on their interests.
key_points: The program runs three weeks at a downtown Brooklyn high school, structured around inspiration, exploration, and action
Students will receive training on safe protesting techniques before staging their demonstration
The curriculum is being shaped by student focus groups rather than imposed top-down
First week will include discussions about events in Baltimore and creative protest techniques
The Alt Banking group is organizing this as an extension of their activist work
money_quotes: The second week we will delve into topics and the kids will decide what they care about and, by the end of the week, what they will protest and how
The third week the students will plan the protest, including training on safe protesting techniques, they will stage it and write it up, and hopefully help the issue get media attention
We are waiting on a focus group to come back to us from the students to see what topics they get really excited about. We want them to more or less lead the way
posts.key_insight: Teaching activism effectively means giving students real agency to choose their causes and execute actual protests rather than just learning about social issues abstractly.

posts.summary: O'Neil exposes what she calls the 'conspiracy theory' of New York real estate corruption that turned out to be true. She connects luxury housing overbuilding, money laundering through real estate, lax disclosure laws, and corrupt tax abatement schemes into a coherent picture of systemic dysfunction. The post highlights how the 421a tax abatement law benefits developers while costing $1.2 billion in uncollected taxes, with politicians like Dean Skelos allegedly demanding payments for favorable treatment. She notes the irony that political payoffs are relatively small compared to the massive public costs.
key_points: Luxury housing is being built beyond actual demand because apartments function as financial instruments for wealthy investors hiding or laundering money
The 421a tax abatement law has cost $1.2 billion in uncollected taxes while supposedly promoting affordable housing
Political corruption enables the system, with payoffs remarkably small ($200K) relative to the public costs
Disclosure laws for foreign real estate buyers are 'suspiciously lax,' enabling money laundering
Construction unions are being decimated even during a building boom, victims of the same corrupt system
money_quotes: Sometimes your conspiracy theory turns out to be absolutely true.
Except perhaps the way to look at such apartments is that they are not apartments at all but financial instruments for rich people.
Specifically, rich people who want to hide or launder their money. The disclosure laws are suspiciously lax.
The ironic thing is how small the pay-offs are: on the level of $200K. Compare that to the $1.2 billion of taxes that have gone uncollected by the 421a law.
Housing is a big deal.
posts.key_insight: New York's luxury real estate boom is less about housing and more about creating unregulated financial instruments for the wealthy, enabled by a corrupt political system where small bribes unlock billions in public losses.

posts.summary: Eugene Stern (guest posting) dismantles Nick Kristof's New York Times op-ed claiming American students are failing at math compared to other countries. By examining the actual 2011 TIMSS data, Stern shows that US eighth graders placed 9th out of 42 countries—solidly in the top third. Kristof's alarming examples were cherry-picked: he selected the two questions out of 88 on which the US performed worst to support his predetermined narrative. The post demonstrates how punditry can manipulate statistics to manufacture a crisis that doesn't exist, while ironically ignoring the same standards of numeracy Kristof preaches.
key_points: US eighth graders actually ranked 9th out of 42 countries on the 2011 TIMSS, placing solidly in the top third
Kristof cherry-picked the 2 worst-performing questions out of 88 to manufacture a false narrative of American failure
American students excelled at statistics, data interpretation, and estimation—outperforming Russia, Finland, Japan, and Israel on some questions
The column offers no constructive suggestions while lecturing about the importance of numeracy
Pundits who moralize about statistical literacy often abuse statistics themselves to support predetermined narratives
money_quotes: The trouble with this narrative is that it's utterly, demonstrably false.
Kristof literally went and picked the two questions out of 88 on which the US did the worst, and highlighted those in the column.
Instead of a story about kids learning stuff and doing decently on a test, we have yet another hysterical screed about Americans 'struggling to compete with citizens of other countries.'
Kristof gives no suggestions for what we can actually do better, by the way.
So do op-ed columnists, apparently.
He has a narrative of American underperformance in mind, and if the overall test results don't fit his story, he'll just go and find some results that do!
posts.key_insight: Pundits who lecture about statistical literacy often cherry-pick data to support predetermined narratives, committing the very sins they condemn.

posts.summary: This is an advice column ('Aunt Pythia') where Cathy O'Neil answers reader questions on academic self-doubt, dating etiquette for mathematicians, graduate school funding negotiations, and dating challenges for black gay men. The column blends humor with substantive guidance, particularly emphasizing that intelligence is cultivated through habits rather than innate gifts, and that standardized tests don't determine one's potential. O'Neil advocates for doing what you love regardless of perceived limitations, while also acknowledging systemic challenges like racial bias in dating.
key_points: Intelligence is something you do, not something you are - good habits of mind (skepticism, curiosity, humility) matter more than test scores
Students in accelerated programs often have an advantage in 'acting as if they know stuff' rather than actually knowing more
No test has the ability to determine whether someone is well-suited to a given topic - that's a personal decision
When approaching potential romantic interests in academic settings, avoid bait-and-switch tactics where math discussions become pretexts for dating
Systemic racial bias in dating is real and documented, and the practical advice is to 'play the numbers' by accessing larger, more diverse dating pools
money_quotes: You don't have to be a certified genius to be a scientist.
They are my favorite people, because they're doing what they love in spite of feeling out of place.
A couple of semesters of college is worth an entire high school career.
Nobody – and especially no test – has the ability to determine whether you are well-suited to a given topic. That's up to you to decide.
My husband thinks that intelligence is something you do, not something you are.

If you focus on good habits of mind, including being skeptical, disciplined, curious, and earnest (with a good dose of humility), then you will be far more prepared for a lifetime of science than by being anxious, competitive, or even cocky.
Being on a date is just like hanging out and talking with someone. The only real difference is, if it goes well, you can get all crushed out on them and not feel weird about it.
posts.key_insight: Intelligence and academic success are built through deliberate habits and persistence, not predetermined by aptitude tests or natural gifts.

posts.summary: The sharing economy's hidden cost is being shifted onto the general public through insurance. Companies like Uber and AirBnB tell workers to rely on their personal insurance, but insurers didn't price policies for commercial use. O'Neil predicts insurers will quietly absorb these costs rather than fight each claim, then spread the expense across all customers. The result: everyone's premiums rise to subsidize gig economy businesses, whether they use these services or not.
key_points: Uber and AirBnB externalize risk by telling workers their personal insurance should cover commercial activities
Insurance companies face a choice: investigate each claim aggressively or quietly absorb the costs
Insurers will likely choose to spread costs across all customers rather than create adversarial relationships
Car insurance premiums will rise proportionally to how many people become full-time Uber drivers
The 'sharing economy' ends up sharing its insurance costs with everyone, including non-participants
money_quotes: One consequence of the 'sharing economy' that hasn't been widely discussed, at least as far as I've seen, is how the externalities are being absorbed.
The insurance companies obviously don't want to cover a huge number of people for circumstances they didn't expect when they offered the coverage.
The insurance companies will quietly pay for the damages accrued by Uber and AirBnB usage.
They also won't actually refuse to pay the costs, which they will simply transfer to the larger pool of customers.
Our insurance costs will go up in proportion to how many people become Uber drivers.
My overall conclusion is that the new 'sharing economy' businesses really will end up sharing something with the rest of us soon, namely the cost of insurance.
Thanks, guys.
posts.key_insight: The sharing economy's business model works by quietly socializing its insurance costs across all policyholders, making everyone subsidize companies like Uber and AirBnB whether they use them or not.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares her excitement about mathematician Jordan Ellenberg visiting New York to accept a Guggenheim Fellowship. She notes that while Guggenheims typically go to artists, a few mathematicians receive them each year. The post centers on a video of Ellenberg and his son promoting the National Math Festival, where Ellenberg spoke about his book 'How Not To Be Wrong.' O'Neil closes with a humorous aside about her own children's distaste for math, which she reframes as independent thinking.
key_points: Guggenheim Fellowships occasionally go to mathematicians despite being primarily associated with artists
The National Math Festival in D.C. featured Ellenberg discussing his popular math book
O'Neil's own children openly dislike math and school, which she humorously spins as raising 'independent thinkers'
money_quotes: You might have thought that Guggenheims were awarded to starving artists, and you would be mostly right, but they also give them out to a couple of math people each year as well.
My favorite part (and Jordan's) is where he puts his head inside a Fibonacci sequence, even though that makes no sense.
My sons would never be this math positive. They enjoy talking about how much they hate school in general and math in particular.
I'm kind of proud of how I'm raising them to be 'independent thinkers,' though, which is what I call that.
posts.key_insight: Even a prominent math advocate can have children who reject math, and there's humor in reframing that resistance as independent thinking.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces she has completed the first draft of her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' with a May 2016 publication date. She also shares that she'll be starting part-time work at the GovLab on data questions for the Department of Justice. The post reflects on the different mindsets required for book writing versus blogging, expressing enthusiasm about returning to short-form writing. She notes that being annoyed helps fuel good blog posts, and expresses gratitude for the experts who generously shared their time during her book research.
key_points: First draft of 'Weapons of Math Destruction' is complete, with May 2016 publication planned
New part-time position at GovLab working on Department of Justice data questions
Book writing requires carrying enormous context; blogging requires one idea expressed well
Being annoyed is good fuel for blog posts
People are extremely generous with their time and expertise when you're researching a book
money_quotes: When you write a book you have to carry around in your head an enormous amount of context, but when you write a blogpost you just need to have one idea and to say it well.
It also helps if you're annoyed.
I'm pretty good at being annoyed, and I love and miss being mathbabe.
Turns out people are extremely generous with their time and expertise, and I am grateful for that!
posts.key_insight: Book writing and blogging require fundamentally different mental approaches—books demand sustained context while blogs thrive on singular, well-expressed ideas fueled by productive annoyance.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) responds to reader questions on trigger warnings in education, ethical porn consumption, career advice for data jobs, and sexism in academia. The column opens with a heartfelt letter from a female computer science student thanking O'Neil for inspiring her return to math. On trigger warnings, O'Neil argues against removing challenging material but emphasizes that warnings shouldn't replace substantive discussions about difficult content. The responses blend practical advice with feminist analysis and sharp wit, particularly in addressing a reader's encounter with academic sexism.
key_points: Trigger warnings are acceptable but should never replace substantive classroom discussions about difficult content - articulated rage and intellectual inquiry are essential to learning
Ethical porn consumption requires actually paying for content from reputable sources that treat performers well, contrary to the reader's assumption that not paying was virtuous
Data science jobs prioritize problem-solving ability and experience over specific programming languages
Sexism in academia is best confronted with direct humor that makes the offender reevaluate their approach rather than with anger or violence
Personal encouragement and visible role models matter enormously for women entering math and computer science
money_quotes: It's critical we don't replace that with a tepid catch-all phrase that renders our rage unarticulated.
What started out as shocking became a learning experience.
As learners, we each must consume and metabolize the things we read, and a well-led discussion is when that all happens.
Very very very little of it concerns the woman's pleasure.
It's almost like the viewer is being trained to ignore what women actually want in bed.
Languages are the medium through which you formalize your solutions to problems, but they are only that; the main obstacle to most data questions is thoughtful approaches.
Tell the guy – through humor, if it helps – that this attitude is a direct obstacle for him getting what he wants.
posts.key_insight: Difficult content in education should provoke discussion and intellectual engagement, not be sanitized away or hidden behind warnings that allow people to opt out of wrestling with uncomfortable truths.

posts.summary: O'Neil reports on her work with a data task force for the Camden County Police Department, coinciding with President Obama's visit praising Camden as a policing model. She notes the tension between Obama's endorsement and the department's history of excessive force accusations. The post signals her involvement in rethinking how the department collects records and responds to complaints. While impressed by Police Chief Scott Thomson's emphasis on morality and community trust over 'occupying force' mentality, she remains realistic that police reform alone cannot solve a troubled city's deeper problems.
key_points: O'Neil is directly involved in advising Camden police on data systems and complaint response processes
There's a notable gap between Obama's praise of Camden as a national model and the department's actual record of excessive force accusations
Police Chief Thomson's rhetoric emphasizes community trust over militarized policing culture
Police reform, even when successful, only exposes the underlying social problems it was masking
money_quotes: I was struck yesterday by his emphasis on morality and community trust over the culture of an occupying force.
Camden is a tough place, and not everything suddenly gets better even with a police force doing their best.
If we take the problems with the police away from a troubled city, you expose a whole pile of other problems.
posts.key_insight: Fixing policing in a troubled city doesn't solve the city's problems—it reveals them.

posts.summary: Meena Boppana reflects on her journey from passionate math enthusiast to questioning why she and nearly all her female friends with strong math backgrounds didn't major in math at Harvard. She co-directed a survey revealing that women aren't just entering Harvard with less math preparation—they're actively dropping out during their college years, with female math majors writing theses and pursuing graduate school at far lower rates than men. The survey found cultural issues at the core: women feel uncomfortable in math department spaces and unwelcome despite wanting to participate. In response, she founded Gender Inclusivity in Math (GIIM) to build community among women in math while engaging male allies in addressing systemic barriers.
key_points: The gender gap in Harvard math is not primarily a 'pipeline issue' from high school—women are leaving math during their college years at disproportionate rates
Zero tenured women faculty exist in Harvard's math department, and only two women graduated with a primary math concentration
Survey data shows women feel uncomfortable in math department spaces and want to be involved but aren't
Breaking the silence about the gender gap revealed widespread concern among students of all genders who felt disempowered rather than apathetic
Male allies at student and faculty levels have shown strong support for change, suggesting institutional will exists
money_quotes: I think about how it is that virtually all my female friends with insanely strong math backgrounds (e.g. math competition stars) decided not to major in math
There are also a total of zero tenured women faculty in Harvard math
The main finding of our survey analysis is that the dearth of women in Harvard math is far more than a 'pipeline issue' stemming from high school
Women are dropping out of math during their years at Harvard
I had always assumed that because no one was talking about the gender gap, no one cared
I realized that my classmates of all genders feel more disempowered than apathetic
Mathematics is too beautiful and important to lose 50 percent (or much more when considering racial and class-based inequities) of the potential population of math lovers
posts.key_insight: The absence of women in elite math programs stems not from inadequate preparation but from a hostile culture that pushes qualified women out during their studies.

posts.summary: O'Neil reacts to news that five major banks pleaded guilty to manipulating foreign exchange markets, expressing frustration that she can't even keep track of all the financial fraud cases anymore. She points out that despite criminal guilty pleas, no one goes to jail and banks receive immediate waivers to continue business as usual. The Justice Department even delayed announcing charges so waivers could be granted without disrupting business. She argues that fines are meaningless when quantitative easing essentially gifts cash back to these same banks, calling the entire enforcement regime 'theater' designed to create the appearance of accountability.
key_points: Five major banks pleaded guilty to forex manipulation via a price-fixing cartel coordinated through chat rooms
Despite criminal guilty pleas, no individuals went to jail and banks received immediate SEC waivers to continue normal operations
The Justice Department delayed announcing charges specifically to ensure business wouldn't be disrupted
Fines are essentially meaningless when quantitative easing transfers cash back to the same banks
The repetitive nature of these scandals has created public fatigue, making it impossible to track all the fraud cases
money_quotes: If you aint cheating, you aint trying
Here I am, someone who is actively taking an interest in financial reform, and I actually can't remember all the fines, settlements, and fake guilty pleas to criminal charges.
I say 'fake' because – yet again – nobody has gone to jail, and the banks found guilty have immediately been given waivers by the SEC to continue business as usual.
The Justice Department even delayed announcing the charges by a week so those waivers could be granted in time so that business wouldn't even be disrupted. For fuck's sake.
The continued quantitative easing has essentially been a gift of cash to those same banks, so I wouldn't even count the fines as meaningful.
In fact I'd call this whole thing theater. And really repetitive, boring theater at that, where we all nod off because every scene is the same and they've turned up the heat too high.
I'd argue that we've actually gone backwards on incentives not to commit fraud, since now everything has been formalized as pathetic.
We will all be so bored we won't even keep track, because nobody can.
posts.key_insight: The sheer volume and repetitiveness of big bank fraud cases, combined with the theater of meaningless fines and immediate waivers, has been designed to exhaust public attention rather than actually punish or deter criminal behavior.

posts.summary: Kansas has passed legislation limiting welfare recipients to withdrawing only $25 per day from ATMs using their state-issued debit cards. Due to ATM mechanics requiring $20 increments and fees totaling $3.50 per transaction, this effectively creates a 17.5% tax on welfare benefits. O'Neil argues this policy is a punitive transfer of money from the poor to banks, making it even harder for welfare recipients to access their own benefits and pay their bills.
key_points: The $25 daily limit effectively becomes $20 due to ATM withdrawal increments
ATM fees ($1 state fee + $2.50 bank fee) create a 17.5% tax on each $20 withdrawal
Many welfare recipients lack bank accounts, forcing them to use fee-charging ATMs
The policy punishes poor people while enriching banks
money_quotes: So altogether, they're giving $3.50 for every $20 of their welfare benefits, which I'd characterize as a bank tax of 17.5%.
Because poor people don't need that money, never mind the convenience of paying their actual bills.
For fuck's sake, Kansas.
posts.key_insight: Poorly designed welfare policies can function as regressive taxes that transfer money from the poorest citizens directly to financial institutions.

posts.summary: O'Neil responds to Adam Elkus's argument that critics of algorithmic accountability are misplacing their blame on algorithms rather than bureaucracy. She argues that algorithmic unaccountability is often a deliberate feature, not a bug, designed to allow people to punish or reward others without taking personal responsibility. While she agrees that algorithms themselves aren't at fault—humans who deploy them are—she rejects the notion that algorithms represent nothing new. Instead, she contends that algorithms give bureaucrats powerful new tools that are intimidating, opaque, and carry a false sense of objectivity.
key_points: Algorithmic unaccountability is often deliberately designed to shield decision-makers from responsibility
Value-Added Models for teachers and evidence-based sentencing are examples of algorithms used to avoid personal accountability
The fault lies not with algorithms but with humans who choose to deploy them and act automatically on their outputs
Algorithms represent something genuinely new: tools that are naturally intimidating, opaque, and carry false objectivity
Organizations deploying algorithms at scale have a responsibility to monitor them for flaws
money_quotes: The unaccountability of algorithms is moreover a feature, not a bug, for such people, and is often entirely deliberate – the algos represent new ways of punishing and rewarding people without having to do it in person and without taking responsibility.
We can't pretend that it's the 'algorithm's fault.' It is most definitely the fault of the people who decide to trust the algorithm and act automatically on the basis of the algorithm's output.
Algorithms have given bureaucrats a new set of tools for their arsenals, ones that are naturally intimidating, opaque, and which carry a false sense of objectivity.
In the case of evidence-based sentencing, the judges can use and then point to the models without fear of being held personally responsible for decisions.
I've never met anyone in this field who imagines that algorithms sprung up out of the computers themselves, ready to act in an unaccountable way.
posts.key_insight: Algorithms don't absolve humans of responsibility—they're deliberately deployed as accountability shields, and those who use them to make consequential decisions must be held responsible for the outcomes.

posts.summary: This post announces Cathy O'Neil's participation in two panels at the 2015 Left Forum conference, formerly the Socialist Scholars Conference. The conference theme focuses on capitalism, democracy, and the #BlackLivesMatter movement. She will speak on a panel about post-Occupy Wall Street activism and host an Alternative Banking working group meeting. The post provides logistical details and descriptions of both sessions, highlighting the ongoing work of groups that emerged from the Occupy movement.
key_points: The Left Forum 2015 theme is 'No Justice, No Peace: Confronting the Crises of Capitalism and Democracy' with emphasis on #BlackLivesMatter and Ferguson
Multiple Occupy-affiliated groups remain active years after Zuccotti Park, including Occupy the SEC, OWS Alternative Banking, and Strike Debt
The Alternative Banking group uses participatory assembly-style discussions to address financial system dysfunction
The group has successfully used Left Forum appearances to recruit new consistent members to their weekly meetings
money_quotes: The occupation is over but groups with roots in Zuccotti Park are working actively in many ways.
We grew out of Open University sessions at the Occupy protests, and have been meeting ever since.

We are open to all comers, and meet every Sunday afternoon at Columbia University to discuss current events and theory related to the dysfunction of the financial system, develop strategies, and endeavor to implement them.
posts.key_insight: The Occupy Wall Street movement's legacy lives on through sustained organizing efforts like Alternative Banking that continue to analyze and challenge financial system dysfunction.

posts.summary: O'Neil proposes creating a Ravelry-like social platform specifically for fitness enthusiasts. She draws parallels between knitters and people who work out, noting both groups obsess over projects, track progress meticulously, and enjoy community engagement. The post describes Ravelry's successful model—user profiles, project showcasing, group activities, and targeted advertising—and suggests the same framework could work for fitness tracking and discussion. She envisions features like workout profiles with Fitbit integration, training graphs, equipment discussions, and group workout challenges analogous to knit-alongs.
key_points: Ravelry succeeds because it facilitates exactly the kind of sharing its users actually want, making it 'much less creepy' than Facebook
Exercise enthusiasts share behavioral traits with knitters: obsessive project planning, progress tracking, community discussion, and group activities
The business model would mirror Ravelry's: free for users, funded by highly targeted ads for products obsessive hobbyists genuinely want
Existing fitness forums lack the thoughtful, comprehensive community design that makes Ravelry work
Social platforms work best when they align with users' natural sharing instincts rather than extracting data they'd prefer to keep private
money_quotes: It's kind of like a Facebook for knitters, but much less creepy, because it's the exact kind of information you want to be sharing, and the exact kind of showcasing of others that you want to be peering at.

I'm convinced that people who work out are almost like knitters. They have little projects that they like to obsess over, they plan them extensively, they like to keep track of progress, they love talking to other worker-outers about their plans.
It's fair to say that the ads I see for silk/cashmere blend yarns that appear on ravelry are kind of predatory. But they definitely work.
Instead of pictures of half-done works in progress, you'd have graphs and pictures of sweaty t-shirts (that sounds kind of gross, but I still think people would dig it).
Free business idea for y'all, I hope you like it.
posts.key_insight: The best social platforms aren't the ones that extract the most data, but the ones where sharing feels natural and users actually want to showcase what they're doing.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her upcoming speaking engagement at the Personal Democracy Forum, where she will present a 12-minute talk titled 'Weapons of Math Destruction.' The conference theme is 'civic tech,' which she interprets as examining how technology and data usage affects the public. She notes her perspective on this topic is 'mostly skeptical' and mentions she'll be sharing the stage with notable speakers like Cory Doctorow.
key_points: O'Neil will speak at the Personal Democracy Forum at NYU on the topic of 'Weapons of Math Destruction'
She interprets 'civic tech' to mean how technology and data usage affects the public
Her stance on technology's impact on the public is characterized as 'mostly skeptical'
money_quotes: I'm interpreting it to mean 'how technology and data usage affects the public.' I have a lot to say about that subject, and it's mostly skeptical.
posts.key_insight: O'Neil signals her critical stance toward civic technology, previewing the skeptical perspective she would develop in her influential book on algorithmic harms.

posts.summary: This advice column features Aunt Pythia responding to reader questions on topics ranging from cereal storage optimization to female grooming standards, birth control choices, relationship trust issues, and hacking ethics. The most substantial response addresses a physics PhD student's insecurity about not meeting her friends' beauty standards, where Aunt Pythia systematically deconstructs grooming 'rules' by examining their purposes and whether they're worth following. She argues that many beauty standards are commercially driven or based on outdated signals, and that women criticizing each other's appearance serves mainly to validate their own time investments in grooming rituals.
key_points: Beauty and grooming rules should be evaluated by examining their original purpose, whether that purpose matters to you, and whether following the rule is worth the effort
Female hairlessness standards are partly commercial constructs designed to make women appear younger, despite men being attracted to women rather than girls
Women criticizing each other's appearance functions as mutual reinforcement that their grooming efforts aren't wasted time
Hormonal birth control affected the author's mood, ambition, and energy levels negatively, though effects vary by individual
The business school applicants who viewed their admissions decisions early weren't really 'hacking' - they were cheating by following instructions, which doesn't deserve celebration
money_quotes: I strive to be that old lady with a beard, wearing purple hats and poking young people on the subway with my umbrella when they misbehave.
What's the purpose of a bunch of women getting together and criticizing each other?
The truth is, though, that it is kind of a waste of time, often, but it's a difficult subject to breach in certain company.
You're enjoying your girliness in your own way and at your own level, but not at the level that your friends expect. In my book, that means you're enjoying it but not wasting time on it.
Men are actually attracted to women, not girls, so they should be comfortable with at least a certain amount of hair, unless they've been talked out of it somehow.
I am OK with them getting kicked out of B-School because this wasn't really hacking, this was cheating. They didn't even figure it out, for god's sake, they just followed instructions!
posts.key_insight: Social rules and beauty standards should be interrogated for their actual purpose before deciding whether they're worth the effort of following.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques a business school paper claiming that performance follows a power law distribution rather than a bell curve, arguing this is used to justify extreme pay inequality. She demonstrates through thought experiments that the 'performance' differences measured are largely artifacts of feedback loops, luck, and accumulated advantage rather than inherent talent differences. The paper conflates output metrics (songs produced, papers published) with actual performance ability, then applies findings from elite fields to justify CEO compensation. O'Neil exposes how the Matthew Effect—where success breeds success—creates skewed distributions that are then used to retroactively justify the inequality they created.
key_points: Initial differences between competitors are small; massive output disparities emerge from feedback loops where success breeds more resources, opportunities, and support
The paper redefines 'performance' by measuring output (which compounds over time) rather than ability, then applies elite field findings to justify general workplace inequality
The 'superstar' argument that top performers deserve exponentially more pay is the same logic used to justify CEO compensation packages
Even in academia where output follows power laws, top performers don't earn 100x more than others—only in executive compensation does this logic get applied so extremely
The study excludes regular workers (secretaries, programmers, cashiers) while drawing conclusions meant to apply to all workplaces
money_quotes: Luck, resources, and self-confidence were all important indicators of success in that first round, some just as important as native skill.
It's all about the feedback loop of success.
So it's really more accurate to say that the primary goal of the paper is to redefine the word 'performance'.
If you think back to our original thought experiment, there is actually very little difference between good candidates at the beginning.
This 'we absolutely need to keep our talent' mentality is exactly the argument we see time and time again excusing pay raises for CEO's. And now there's a 'mathematical' reason for it.
In pop music and pro sports, there is a crazy pay differential, but that's not something to be proud of or something we want to replicate.
posts.key_insight: Power law distributions in output metrics reflect accumulated advantage and feedback loops, not innate talent differences—and using them to justify extreme pay inequality is circular reasoning dressed up as mathematics.

posts.summary: The post examines two recent challenges to Uber's dominance in NYC: Mayor de Blasio's proposed regulation requiring city approval and $1000 fees for app interface changes, and a lawsuit from credit unions arguing Uber violates taxi medallion owners' exclusive hailing rights. O'Neil notes the tech industry's collective pushback against the regulatory proposal while acknowledging the legal ambiguity around what constitutes 'hailing' in the digital age. She remains genuinely conflicted about the situation, refusing to fully side with either tech companies or regulators.
key_points: De Blasio proposed requiring Uber to pay $1000 and get city approval for every app interface change, prompting a coalition letter from major tech companies
Credit unions that finance taxi medallions are suing, arguing Uber's app-based hailing violates medallion owners' contractual exclusive rights
The legal definition of 'hail' is becoming contested territory as technology blurs traditional distinctions
O'Neil suggests yellow taxis should develop their own hailing apps rather than fight the technology
The author remains conflicted, particularly about unresolved insurance liability questions in the sharing economy
money_quotes: If you know anything at all about how tech companies work, you know this would be a serious friction if it goes through; user interfaces are changed on a weekly basis, to add features or even just test them.
Kind of a tech posse wielding its power.
On the one hand, it does seem to be a different act to raise your hands on Broadway versus using an app on your phone.
But by the time we have chips implanted into our heads, just *thinking the words* 'hail a taxi' might do the trick, and that's where the grey area lives.
Is de Blasio simply a pawn of the taxi commission? Should we feel sorry for tech companies?
posts.key_insight: The fight over Uber in NYC reveals how technological change creates genuine legal and ethical grey areas that resist easy ideological sorting.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil follows up on her analysis of insurance gaps in the sharing economy, specifically the dangerous period when Uber drivers have their app on but no passengers. She notes that Allstate has created a new policy to fill this gap, but questions whether the stated $15-20 annual cost is realistic. She suspects the true cost is much higher, Uber doesn't want to pay it, and if drivers must cover it themselves, their already low wages will drop further. The post illustrates how businesses built on regulatory arbitrage eventually face margin compression when rules catch up.
key_points: There's a dangerous insurance gap when Uber drivers have their app on but no passenger - a gap that has already resulted in children's deaths
Uber drivers on chat boards discuss hiding that their app was on during accidents, suggesting this gap affects everyone's insurance rates
Allstate's new $15-20/year policy to fill this gap seems suspiciously cheap, especially given Uber's fierce opposition to regulations requiring such coverage
When businesses profit by bypassing regulations, their margins fall once those regulations catch up
money_quotes: A child got killed by an Uber driver in exactly this situation. Actually more than one child.
There are chat boards of Uber drivers suggesting how to hide the fact that their app was on in case of an accident.
This supports my original theory that all of our car insurance policies will be going up because of Uber drivers.
That's kind of what happens when you create a business models that make money in part by bypassing regulations, and then the regulations catch up with you: your profit margins fall.
Uber was working very very hard to avoid this insurance from existing.
posts.key_insight: When tech companies build profitable business models by exploiting regulatory gaps, the eventual cost of compliance reveals just how much of their margin depended on shifting risk onto workers and the public.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil's humorous alter ego) answers reader questions on romance, education, language, and relationships. Topics range from how to ask out an online crush, to staying present while studying math, to mishearing song lyrics, to navigating a new relationship when you have a history of sleeping with friends. The tone is playful and frank, with O'Neil dispensing practical wisdom while maintaining her characteristic directness about sex, relationships, and academic life.
key_points: When asking someone out, combine sincere flattery with an unimposing invitation that gives them an easy out - 'it doesn't have to be a date if she doesn't wish it to be'

A chemistry professor's advice to minorities to avoid science careers because they're 'lonely and costly' is dismissed as 'crazy racist' - O'Neil encourages embracing challenges and keeping options open
On studying math: read problem sets immediately, think about why the professor assigned them, and 'stay a few steps away from panic'
In relationships, it's not acceptable for a partner to separate you from your community based on jealousy principles, but couples don't need to socialize in the same circles to be happy
money_quotes: There's really nothing objectionable about flattery combined with an unimposing dinner invitation. You've got nothing to lose. Even if she says no she will be charmed.
Wow, what terrible advice. Did he really say that was his advice 'for minorities'? Crazy racist.
Start working on them right away. In a word, stay a few steps away from panic in the local sense.
Be flexible if things don't work out longer term, but take on challenges and be a bulldog in the short term. Keep your options open and grow them at every turn.
I don't think it's OK for Tim to separate you from your community because of a bizarre principle of 'no seeing any exes.' That is too bullying.
After all, nobody can be everything to someone. Plenty of happy couples I know don't socialize in the same circles.
posts.key_insight: Good advice combines directness with generosity - whether asking someone out, tackling math problems, or navigating relationship boundaries, the key is being honest about what you want while giving others room to respond on their own terms.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil examines the 2008 D.C. cheating scandal under Michelle Rhee's tenure as chancellor, where high-stakes testing with cash bonuses led to widespread cheating evidenced by suspiciously high erasure rates on standardized tests. While Atlanta teachers were jailed for similar cheating under comparable incentive structures, the D.C. investigation was effectively buried. O'Neil argues the fundamental flaw wasn't the teachers who cheated but the high-stakes regime itself that predictably created perverse incentives. She criticizes Rhee for readily firing teachers while refusing to acknowledge that her celebrated reform successes were built on fraudulent test results.
key_points: Cash bonuses of $8,000-$10,000 for teachers and principals were tied to student test score improvements in high-poverty D.C. schools
Erasure rates on tests averaged 12.7 per test versus the normal rate of less than 1, indicating systematic answer changes
The investigation into cheating was 'smothered' unlike Atlanta where teachers were imprisoned for similar offenses
The high-stakes testing regime itself created the conditions that made cheating predictable and rational
Rhee fired hundreds of teachers, sometimes without explanation, while her own 'successes' were based on fraudulent data
money_quotes: Certain high poverty schools weren't doing so well. Michelle Rhee came in as chancellor and suggested that the teachers and principals simply needed some more incentives to achieve better student learning.
An investigation followed but came up pretty empty.
I'm not suggesting we want more jailings, by the way. I'm suggesting that the original high-stakes regime was fundamentally flawed and naturally gave rise to the cheating in the first place.
Michelle Rhee's legacy was one were she was very happy to fire people but very reluctant to admit that her educational reform successes were based on lies.
posts.key_insight: High-stakes testing regimes that attach large financial rewards and punishments to metrics predictably corrupt those metrics, and the architects of such systems rarely face accountability for the fraud they incentivize.

posts.summary: O'Neil explores the moral spectrum surrounding student debt default, from sympathetic Corinthian College fraud victims to Lee Siegel's controversial choice to default on Columbia loans. She introduces a third framework borrowed from finance: treating debt as amoral, purely contractual obligations that can be strategically renegotiated. Using the example of a failed condo developer who escaped his mortgage while sailing the Caribbean, she acknowledges consumers likely can't fully adopt this mindset. Nevertheless, she argues for removing moral weight from student debt discussions to enable productive debate about college affordability and the broader harms of burdening young people with extreme debt.
key_points: Student debt exists on a moral spectrum from sympathetic (fraud victims) to condemned (privileged defaulters)
In high finance, debt contracts have been entirely stripped of moral meaning - defaults and renegotiations are purely strategic
The moral weight individuals attach to small consumer debts seems 'random and quaint' compared to how corporations treat massive obligations
A widespread student debt strike could force reform regardless of whether all participants are 'perfectly sympathetic'
Extreme student debt harms young people's ability to make good life decisions and is bad for the country overall
money_quotes: In other words, debt contracts, in the context of high finance, have been entirely removed from their moral roots.
By contrast, the moral weight that individual consumers attach to what are tiny little contracts in comparison seem kind of random and quaint.
I picked up from finance (where, I like to say, you 'learn to think like an asshole'). Namely, that there's no morality attached to debt at all.
Some of these schools have brought the ethics of payday lending into higher education.
I think burdening young people with extreme debt is bad for the country, and especially bad for their ability to make good decisions about what to do with their lives.
I'm all for a national discussion on this with the debt morality taken out.
posts.key_insight: The finance industry treats debt as purely strategic and amoral, yet society burdens individual students with intense moral obligation over comparatively tiny sums—a double standard worth questioning.

posts.summary: This anonymous guest post explains the controversy surrounding tenure changes at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. The state is moving tenure authority from state law to the politically-appointed Board of Regents, while adding vague language about 'program redirection' that could allow termination of tenured faculty. Combined with weakened shared governance that makes faculty subordinate to chancellors on program decisions, the worst-case scenario would give politically-appointed administrators unilateral power to eliminate tenure protections. The author, writing anonymously to demonstrate why tenure matters, warns that this could happen at any university.
key_points: Tenure authority is moving from state law to the Board of Regents, who are effectively political appointees of the Governor
New language about 'program redirection' expands grounds for terminating tenured faculty beyond the traditional standard of financial exigency
Shared governance is being weakened, making faculty subordinate to chancellors on program and curriculum decisions
The combination of these changes could allow politically-motivated termination of faculty, which is precisely what tenure is designed to prevent
The author's anonymity itself demonstrates the chilling effect that weakened tenure protections create
