money_quotes: I choose to do so anonymously, in part to make a point about the value of tenure and the protection it affords faculty from becoming political targets.
Other than dismissal for cause, most schools only allow for termination of tenured faculty for reasons of financial exigency.
The Regents are appointed by the Governor, with the most recent appointee being the son of a Bradley Foundation member.
Add these things up, and the Regents could appoint a Chancellor with the authority to unilaterally make program changes and terminate tenured faculty.
If someone's house was on fire, would you grab a bucket of water, or would you think about stealing their TV?
Make no mistake that your university could be next, so do onto others as you would have them do onto you.
posts.key_insight: The real threat to academic freedom isn't the formal elimination of tenure but the transfer of its definition to political appointees who can rewrite its protections at will.

posts.summary: The post argues that the market for personal data is maturing as venture capitalists grow impatient for returns on user acquisition investments. While consumers have freely given up their data in exchange for services, they haven't yet experienced the consequences because of a lag between data collection and monetization. Recent changes to EBay and PayPal's user agreements—allowing them to make marketing calls with no opt-out—signal that this lag is ending. O'Neil connects this to other developments like the Verizon/AOL merger and RadioShack's attempted data auction, warning that personal datasets are becoming corporate assets as valuable as physical property.
key_points: User agreements are meaningless because nobody reads them and they can change at any time
There's a built-in lag between giving up data and experiencing the consequences of that loss of privacy
VC impatience is accelerating the timeline for exploiting user data
Personal data is becoming a corporate asset as important as land or warehouses
Telemarketing calls are just the beginning of how our data will be monetized
money_quotes: You might not be concerned, but I'd like to point out that there's a reason you're not. Namely, you haven't actually seen what this enormous loss of privacy translates into yet.

The lag represents the time it takes for the market in personal data to mature. It also represents the patience that Silicon Valley venture capitalists have or do not have between the time of user acquisition and profit.
The less patience they have, the sooner they want to exploit the user data.
There is no possibility to opt out, except perhaps to abandon the services.
They claim their intention is to 'benefit our relationship.'
It's still a sign that the personal data market is ripening, and in particular that such datasets are becoming assets as important as land or warehouses.
Given how much venture capitalists like to brag about their return, I think we have reason to worry about the coming wave of 'innovative' uses of our personal data.
Telemarketing is the tip of the iceberg.
posts.key_insight: The reason consumers aren't alarmed about data privacy is simply that the market for exploiting their data hasn't matured yet—but VC impatience is about to change that.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts discovering growler shops during a visit to Atlanta, describing them as take-away bars with vast beer selections at good prices. She explains the concept for uninitiated readers and shares her specific purchases. The post takes an unexpected turn into regulatory analysis, explaining how varying state interpretations of federal law about beer containers prevent growler shops from existing in New York. It's a light personal travelogue that touches on the quirks of alcohol regulation.
key_points: Growler shops are take-away bars with large beer selections available for sampling and purchase at good prices
State-by-state interpretation of federal container laws creates different business possibilities across states
New York's interpretation only allows shops to sell beer from a single brewery, making the growler shop model unviable
The concept extends beyond beer to include growler wine shops in states with permissive regulations
money_quotes: It's basically a take-away bar, with an enormous number of beers that you can sample and of course purchase, at great prices.
Left Hand Milk Stout, which is as close to a meal in a drink as you can get if you've been weaned.
Law is weird, but what it means is that New York State laws would only allow a shop to sell beer from a single brewery, which is super disappointing.
posts.key_insight: Differing state interpretations of the same federal law create vastly different consumer experiences across state lines, even for something as simple as buying beer to take home.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses questions about data science master's programs, sexual thoughts in professional settings, and workplace dynamics between engineers and data analysts. O'Neil suggests that university data science programs are both educational and cash cows, noting that few hire actual industry practitioners. On the question of sexual thoughts during math talks, she normalizes such human experiences while emphasizing the importance of returning focus to the mathematics. She advises a job-seeking data scientist that it's normal to change jobs when learning plateaus or office politics become toxic.
key_points: University data science master's programs are likely valuable for learning but also function as cash cows capitalizing on Big Data hype
Few data science programs hire faculty with actual industry experience as data scientists, limiting practical career guidance
Having distracting human thoughts in professional settings is normal; what matters is how quickly one refocuses on the work
Data science jobs aren't meant to last forever - people should move on when learning slows or office politics deteriorate
Tension between engineering and analytics teams exists at some companies but isn't universal
money_quotes: I think most of them would teach you something, but I'm pretty sure these programs are also cash cows.
One thing I've noticed is how few of the programs want to hire someone who has actually worked as a data scientist in a company.
Obviously it's better for both audience members and the speaker at a math talk if the math is the center of attention, but there's no way to remove our humanness entirely.
I remember concocting a crush on the speaker, male or female, of most talks I went to in order to enjoy their talk more. It worked!
The thing about data science jobs is that they don't last forever. People are expected to jump ship once they get the basic idea of stuff and the learning curve decelerates.
posts.key_insight: Academic data science programs and professional settings both involve navigating the gap between institutional incentives and practical human realities.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares her excitement about volunteering at the Clearwater Festival, an environmental music festival founded by Pete Seeger in 1969 to support cleaning up the Hudson River. She describes the logistics of arriving early to set up camp before her volunteer shift in the kitchen. The post focuses on the personal pleasures of the festival: camping, jamming with bandmates, and discovering new music acts.
key_points: The Clearwater Festival was founded by Pete Seeger in 1969 as an organizing effort to clean up the Hudson River
O'Neil volunteers at the festival and plays fiddle with her band
The festival takes place at Croton Point Park and features camping for volunteers
She highlights performers she's excited to see including The Lone Bellow and The Felice Brothers
money_quotes: I know it's almost summer, not because of the sticky heat but because it's almost time to go to Clearwater
learning about new talent is what summer music festivals is all about
posts.key_insight: A personal post about the author's participation in the historic Clearwater Festival, showing her life outside of data science criticism.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the concept of 'opting in' has been stretched beyond recognition, from passive acceptance of unread terms to mere inaction being treated as consent. She presents two examples: Belgium's pushback against Facebook treating link clicks as consent to tracking, and a political operative claiming voter registration constitutes opting in to microtargeting. The post highlights how companies and political campaigns exploit ambiguity around consent to justify surveillance. O'Neil concludes with concern that even automatic voter registration could be weaponized as implied consent for tracking.
key_points: Traditional 'opt-in' already required only passive action—checking boxes without reading terms—but new interpretations have lowered the bar to mere inaction
Belgium correctly recognized that clicking a link to Facebook is not the same as consenting to tracking and surveillance
Political campaigns justify microtargeting by claiming voter registration constitutes 'opting in' to receive targeted messages
Registering to vote is a civic duty, not consent to be tracked or manipulated
The erosion of meaningful consent threatens even beneficial reforms like automatic voter registration
money_quotes: It's gone beyond passive action to what you could only describe as inaction.
We have become more or less used to the idea that we'll check on agreement boxes, written in inscrutable legalese, in order to get free stuff.
Opting in requires doing something, and it doesn't count if the 'doing something' has nothing to do with terms of service.
They opted in by registering to vote.
Registering to vote is simply a part of being a citizen, and does not even indirectly imply a willingness to be tracked.
We should all be automatically registered anyway, although now I'm worried about what that might mean we're signing up for.
posts.key_insight: When merely existing as a citizen counts as 'opting in' to surveillance, the concept of consent has lost all meaning.

posts.summary: O'Neil uses the metaphor of a dysfunctional family to analyze the Greek debt crisis, arguing that Greece should default on its loans while remaining in the Eurozone. She frames the standoff between Greece and its creditors (ECB, IMF, European Commission) as a game of chicken, noting that the people making debt decisions aren't the ones who suffer from austerity. Drawing from an Occupy meeting discussion, she suggests that just as families don't evict sick members who can't repay debts, Europe shouldn't push Greece out, even if everyone remains annoyed about the unpaid money.
key_points: The Greek debt crisis is fundamentally a game of chicken between creditors and a country that simply cannot pay
There's a disconnect between decision-makers negotiating debt terms and ordinary Greeks who suffer the consequences of austerity
The European project's founding vision of unity and family makes expelling Greece over unpayable debt morally untenable
Real families don't evict sick members who can't repay debts—they absorb the loss and move on
Greece's democratic election of Syriza made the country's position more legitimate than when technocrats ran the show
money_quotes: There's a game of chicken going on in Europe, whereby the moneylenders are trying to get Greece to pay back money they previously borrowed, but Greece doesn't have any extra cash to do it.

Greece is actually a country, and the people making big decisions about how debt payments will work in Greece are not the same people that suffer when they run out of jobs, medicine, and pension payments.
Pushing Greece out because of failure to pay money it doesn't really have would be shameful to many who still believe in that vision.
The sick person would be allowed to stay, even though they didn't pay back the money. And everyone would be annoyed, but family is family.
posts.key_insight: When a debtor genuinely cannot pay, creditors face a choice between moral principle and financial recovery—and sometimes family bonds should trump balance sheets.

posts.summary: The post critiques a Wall Street Journal article about college accreditation failures, which used graduation rates and loan default rates to identify underperforming schools. While O'Neil agrees these metrics reveal problematic institutions, she argues they're deeply flawed as standalone measures. A low graduation rate might indicate a school taking chances on disadvantaged students or serving as a transfer institution, while high default rates could reflect economic conditions or non-engineering disciplines. She warns that using simplistic metric thresholds could push out non-traditional students, turn schools into corporate training centers, or incentivize fraud.
key_points: Current college accreditation systems appear dysfunctional, possibly due to misaligned incentives or insufficient pressure on underperforming schools
Graduation rates can be misleading: schools may have low rates because they take chances on at-risk students or serve as transfer institutions
Loan default rates reflect factors beyond school quality, including the economy and whether graduates enter high-paying fields like engineering
Simple metric thresholds for accreditation could create perverse incentives: pushing out non-traditional students, corporate capture of education, or widespread fraud
Identifying failing colleges is genuinely difficult and resists easy data-driven solutions
money_quotes: A graduation rate can be lower or higher for good or bad reasons, and taken alone is not a great indicator.
Engineering schools tend to graduate students who find jobs quickly and easily, but that doesn't mean every school should become an engineering school.
Deciding whether a given college has become a failure is actually pretty tricky.
We can't claim that it's as simple as looking at two metrics and deciding what the cut-off is.
If we closed all the schools that don't keep graduation rates high and default rates low, we might see non-engineering students pushed out of the system.
We might see schools create partnerships with corporations and become federal aid-funded corporate training centers.
We might just see (even more) widespread fraud in terms of reporting such things.
posts.key_insight: Metrics that seem straightforward for evaluating institutions often obscure the complex tradeoffs involved, and naive enforcement of thresholds creates perverse incentives that harm the most vulnerable.

posts.summary: This guest post by Todd Schneider explores the newly available loan-level data from Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, which was released in 2013 following their $200 billion government bailout. Schneider demonstrates how personal computers and open-source software now enable individuals to analyze massive financial datasets that once required million-dollar budgets. The analysis reveals that loans originated from 2005-2008 performed dramatically worse than other vintages, with default rates exceeding 15% compared to 4% for earlier periods. Post-2009 lending shows much better performance due to significantly tighter credit standards, with average FICO scores jumping from 720 to 765. Geographic analysis identifies California, Florida, Arizona, and Nevada as the worst-performing 'sand states' during the crisis.
key_points: The 'medium data' revolution allows individuals with laptops to analyze 215 GB of mortgage data (38 million loans, $7.1 trillion in originations) using free, open-source tools—analysis that would have cost over $1 million annually in the past
Loans from 2005-2008 had default rates exceeding 15%, compared to just 4% for 1999-2003 vintages and under 2% for post-2009 loans
Credit standards tightened dramatically after the crisis: average FICO scores rose from 720 to 765, and loans below 680 FICO nearly disappeared
The 'sand states' (California, Florida, Arizona, Nevada) experienced the worst default rates during the mortgage crisis
Home price adjusted loan-to-value ratio is the strongest predictor of default rates
money_quotes: [M]ortgages were acknowledged to be the most mathematically complex securities in the marketplace. The complexity arose entirely out of the option the homeowner has to prepay his loan; it was poetic that the single financial complexity contributed to the marketplace by the common man was the Gordian knot giving the best brains on Wall Street a run for their money.

The GSEs went through a nearly $200 billion government bailout during the financial crisis, motivated in large part by losses on loans that they guaranteed, so I figured there must be something interesting in the loan-level data.

In the not-so-distant past, an analysis of loan-level mortgage data would have cost a lot of money. Between licensing data and paying for expensive computers to analyze it, you could have easily incurred costs north of a million dollars per year.

Personal computers are so powerful that my MacBook Air is capable of analyzing the entire 215 GB of data, representing some 38 million loans, 1.6 billion observations, and over $7.1 trillion of origination volume.
More than 15% of all loans originated in those years became distressed.

Tighter agency standards, coupled with a complete shutdown in the non-agency mortgage market, including both subprime and Alt-A lending, mean that there is very little credit available to borrowers with low credit scores (a far more difficult question is whether this is a good or bad thing!).
posts.key_insight: The democratization of data and computing power now allows individuals to conduct sophisticated financial analyses that were once the exclusive domain of well-funded institutions, revealing clear patterns in the mortgage crisis that confirm what many suspected about reckless lending practices.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that trigonometry should be removed from high school curricula because it's taught as rote memorization without motivation or understanding, turning students off from math entirely. She acknowledges trig's importance in advanced applications like Fourier Analysis, but notes that by that level, students have the mathematical tools (particularly Euler's Formula) to work with trig functions elegantly. She systematically dismantles common defenses of trig education—shop class applications, periodic function examples, physical sciences use—noting that all either no longer apply or could be taught more simply. Her core argument is that trig exemplifies how math education makes the subject feel deliberately mysterious and inaccessible, when alternatives like algebra are at least derivable and explainable.
key_points: Trigonometry is taught as pure memorization without proofs or motivation, making math feel intentionally mysterious to students
Euler's Formula makes all trig identities trivially derivable, but this elegant tool isn't introduced until college
Common defenses of teaching trig (shop class, periodic functions, physical sciences) either no longer apply or don't require deep trig knowledge
Even if some curriculum content is arbitrary, trig is worse than alternatives like algebra because algebra is at least derivable and explainable
The way trig is taught may be responsible for many people's hatred of mathematics
money_quotes: Fuck trigonometry. It's a terribly unmotivated subject, and as a student you are expected to memorize double angle formulas with no proofs.
It's almost a case study in how to make someone feel like math is meant to be mysterious.
When they ask me what I do for a living, I often say I'm a mathematician. Then they pretty much always tell me how much they hate math.
Someone mentioned it's needed in 'shop class.' But then they went on to explain the shop classes no longer exist.
My mom did me the favor of explaining the above formula to me when I was in high school so I could avoid all the memorization.
It's still more defensible to teach them to factor quadratic polynomials than it is to introduce arctan.
posts.key_insight: The way trigonometry is taught—as mysterious formulas to memorize rather than derivable concepts to understand—exemplifies everything wrong with math education and drives students away from the subject.

posts.summary: This is a brief link roundup post sharing three items the author found interesting: a book about the Vatican bank's financial corruption and Nazi ties, a blog post about flood insurance pricing that touches on the fundamental tension in insurance design, and coverage of a new political social network called Brigade that collects user beliefs data. The post is light on original analysis but reveals the author's interests in financial corruption, insurance mathematics, and the potential risks of political data collection.
key_points: The Vatican bank has a dark history of dealings with Nazis, as documented in Gerald Posner's 'God's Bankers'
Insurance faces a fundamental paradox: pricing it too accurately makes it unaffordable for those who need it most
Brigade, a new political social network, collects detailed belief data from users with an undisclosed business model, raising concerns about how campaigns might use this information
money_quotes: One of my favorite point about insurance comes up in this piece, namely if you price insurance too accurately, it fails in its most basic function, and gets too expensive for those at highest risk.
Could be scary, could be useful.
posts.key_insight: When insurance is priced with perfect actuarial accuracy, it paradoxically defeats its core purpose by becoming unaffordable for those most in need of protection.

posts.summary: O'Neil describes her years-long experiment of playing 'chicken' with pedestrians on New York City streets to test whether men and women receive equal spatial courtesy. Her methodology involves mimicking the other person's movements—if they move, she moves; if they don't acknowledge her presence, she doesn't yield, resulting in a bump. Her findings show that women of all races and most men practice mutual accommodation, but a subset of white men consistently fail to yield, expecting her to move for them. She frames this as a question of who gets the privilege to be 'lost in their thoughts' in public space—a form of carefree obliviousness that's not equally distributed.
key_points: Women are socialized to apologize and yield, but the issue extends beyond verbal politeness to physical space and movement
O'Neil's experiment involves mimicking pedestrians' movements to test who practices mutual accommodation versus who expects others to yield
The overwhelming majority of failed 'chicken games' resulting in bumps involve white men who don't acknowledge her presence
Being able to walk obliviously through crowded spaces is itself a privilege—certain people get to be 'lost in their thoughts' while others must remain vigilant
O'Neil acknowledges her own position as a large white woman may affect her results and invites others to share their experiences
money_quotes: I've collected a LOT of data.
There is a certain amount of ballet-level choreography that one learns when one lives in a busy place, and it mostly happens at an unconscious level.
It's only when that unspoken agreement doesn't happen that we notice.
The point is to see who is ignoring common courtesy.
Some of them even turn around and say things like, why didn't you get out of my way?
Who gets to get lost in their thoughts on a busy street?
Getting lost in one's thoughts is a form of carefree behavior that only certain people have regular access to.
posts.key_insight: The privilege to move through public space obliviously—expecting others to accommodate you—is unequally distributed along lines of race and gender.

posts.summary: This post shares Portia Nelson's famous poem 'Autobiography in Five Short Chapters,' which uses the metaphor of repeatedly falling into a hole in the sidewalk to illustrate the process of personal growth and breaking destructive patterns. The five chapters trace a journey from helpless victimhood through gradual self-awareness to finally choosing a different path entirely. O'Neil presents the poem without commentary, letting it speak for itself as a meditation on how we learn from our mistakes.
key_points: Personal growth follows a predictable pattern: denial, recognition, acceptance of responsibility, avoidance, and finally choosing a new path
Taking responsibility ('It is my fault') is the turning point that enables faster recovery
True change isn't just avoiding the problem but choosing an entirely different route
Habits and patterns persist even when we intellectually recognize them
money_quotes: I fall in. I am lost… I am hopeless. It isn't my fault.
I pretend I don't see it. I fall in again.
I can't believe I am in this same place. But it isn't my fault.
I still fall in… it's a habit… but, my eyes are open.
I know where I am. It is my fault. I get out immediately.
I walk around it.
I walk down another street.
posts.key_insight: Breaking harmful patterns requires not just awareness or avoidance, but the wisdom to choose an entirely different path.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses four reader questions covering college choices for math students, the ethics and psychology of camgirl interactions, campus sexual assault policies, and gender identity at academic conferences. Aunt Pythia recommends Worcester Polytechnic Institute for applied math education, critiques camgirl interactions as inherently performative rather than authentic representations of female sexuality, argues that campus sexual assault problems stem from broader societal failures in how we handle rape cases, and encourages a reader questioning gender norms to challenge the math community's binary assumptions.
key_points: Camgirl interactions, while potentially less exploitative than traditional porn, still present a biased view of female sexuality because performers are incentivized to always appear aroused regardless of actual feelings
The intimacy in paid camgirl relationships may actually be more threatening to marriages than physical affairs, despite seeming safer due to the transactional nature
Campus sexual assault problems persist because colleges have conflicts of interest in handling cases, and the real solution requires improving how society and police handle rape broadly
Gender categories used for administrative purposes like conference housing are outdated social constructs that don't reflect the actual continuum of gender identity
Parents should ensure college-bound children have agency in choosing their own educational paths rather than imposing parental preferences
money_quotes: From my experience, actual physical sex is not the threat to marriage, intimacy and feelings are.
That's not going to happen, because what you're seeing isn't reality, it's a performance.
These women don't want to ruin their brands by admitting they aren't horny, and why would they.
There's an obvious conflict of interest for campuses. They want to preserve their image, and seen that way it seems dumb that they are in control of such issues at all.
When the police are good at this outside of college campuses, then we won't need to rely on college administrators to do this.
For administrative purposes people introduce categories like race and gender, as if these are well-defined categories, but they are not.
The math community should be shaken up a bit, it's old-fashioned for sure.
posts.key_insight: Whether discussing camgirl performances, campus assault policies, or gender categories, institutions and individuals consistently mistake constructed performances and administrative conveniences for authentic reality.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her contribution to an Intercept article investigating student privacy risks in the age of big data. The piece, written by journalist Farai Chideya, examines how children's data is being mined and the implications for student privacy. O'Neil was interviewed as an expert source for the investigation. She invites readers to engage with the article and share their thoughts on this important topic.
key_points: Student privacy is increasingly at risk as big data practices extend to children
O'Neil contributed expert perspective to investigative journalism on data mining in education
The article was published by The Intercept, a publication known for serious investigative reporting
money_quotes: No Child Left Un-Mined?
posts.key_insight: The data mining practices that affect adults are now being applied to children in educational settings, raising serious privacy concerns.

posts.summary: O'Neil describes a job interview experience where, after a full day of interviews, she was unexpectedly asked to complete open-ended 'homework' that appeared to be an NP-complete problem directly relevant to the company's business. When she declined due to time constraints working on her book, she was told it wasn't a dealbreaker—but then heard nothing for weeks. She later discovered through a contact that the hiring manager had actually been waiting for her to complete it, making the requirement a dealbreaker after all despite what she'd been told.
key_points: Companies sometimes use interview 'homework' assignments that cross the line into unpaid consulting work
The assignment was both extremely difficult (possibly NP-complete) and directly applicable to the company's actual business problems
There was a clear communication failure: she was told the homework wasn't required, but the decision-maker was actually waiting for it
The power imbalance in hiring allows companies to extract significant free labor from candidates under the guise of evaluation
O'Neil raises the legitimate question of what standards should exist around interview assignments
money_quotes: When I eventually got it, the homework was very open-ended and very very hard.
It seemed like they wanted me to give them free consulting in addition to applying for a job.
I felt like it was a fair question for them to have me work on if I had been hired, but it seemed too much to ask for me to work on it before then.
So it had been a dealbreaker after all.
When does an interview, and homework afterwards, become free consulting?
posts.key_insight: Interview homework crosses into exploitation when it solves real business problems and extracts significant unpaid labor from candidates who have already invested substantial time.

posts.summary: Anna Bernasek and Dan Mongan argue that online advertising should be called 'negotiation' because companies use big data to determine each consumer's maximum willingness to pay and make personalized offers accordingly. The concept of 'consumer surplus'—the difference between what you'd pay and actual price—is being systematically extracted by algorithms that infer your spending power. This creates one-sided negotiations with massive information asymmetries, where you never see what others are offered. The author notes this subverts free market ideals where all offers are visible, enabled by monopolistic platforms and auction-based ad systems that favor higher-margin products within your price range.
key_points: Online 'ads' are actually personalized price negotiations where algorithms determine your maximum willingness to pay
Consumer surplus—the money you save when prices are below your maximum—is being systematically captured by data-driven pricing
The concept of consumer surplus breaks down for desperate purchases like cars needed for work, where getting any loan matters more than the terms
Information asymmetry is extreme: you see only your personalized offer, never what others are shown
Monopolistic platforms and auction-based ad systems that reward higher margins make this extraction possible
money_quotes: Instead of an advertisement, which sounds like something we imagine many many people see, this is a personalized negotiation, just to you personally, and crucially, you don't see what other people are being offered.
So it's a one-sided negotiation with enormous information asymmetries.
The 'consumer surplus' is less obvious when it's a matter of debt rather than cash, and when it's a desperately needed purchase rather than discretionary.
Yes, there might be cheaper versions of these things that you'd be happy with, and that would save you money, but the algorithm has determined your spending power, which is all they care about.
This is the opposite of what we'd expect in the 'free market,' where all the offers are on the table and you get to choose the one with the lowest price.
posts.key_insight: What we call 'online advertising' is actually algorithmic price negotiation designed to extract the maximum amount each individual consumer is willing to pay, conducted with radical information asymmetry.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil questions the business model behind Sidewalk Labs' plan to convert New York City phone booths into wifi hubs backed by Google. She argues that the 'free wifi' offering is actually a surveillance exchange, where users trade their location data and browsing habits for connectivity. Even non-users with wifi-enabled devices will be tracked as they walk by. The real business is tailored advertising based on real-time pedestrian surveillance, fundamentally changing the nature of public space.
key_points: Sidewalk Labs will track all wifi usage and can identify devices even from non-users walking by
The business model likely involves tailoring advertisements based on who is nearby and what they're doing
The implicit social contract is exchanging free wifi for corporate surveillance of public spaces
This infrastructure is planned for expansion to other cities using street lamps and bus shelters
money_quotes: When you use that wifi, they can track what you do.
Even if you don't use it, if you walk by with a wifi-enabled device (smart phone), the phone booth will sense your device and tag you.
Presumably this is not charity.
The overall negotiation then is that we are willing to exchange free wifi for having our experience in a public space tracked by a private company.
I'm not sure we are all thinking that's a good deal.
Prepare to enter into a realm of existence one step closer to Blade Runner.
posts.key_insight: Free public wifi from tech companies isn't charity—it's a surveillance infrastructure that transforms public spaces into privately monitored advertising platforms.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques a New York Times debate on happiness for lacking precision about what 'happiness' actually means. She introduces the distinction between hedonic pleasure (short-term sensory gratification) and eudaimonic pleasure (long-term fulfillment from accomplishment). Using this framework, she reinterprets each debater's argument, showing how the confusion stems from conflating these two fundamentally different concepts. Her core argument is that meaningful discussions about whether to pursue or measure happiness require first clarifying which type of happiness we mean.
key_points: Hedonic pleasure is short-term sensation (eating Doritos, seeing a naked body, a child's laugh) while eudaimonic pleasure comes from long-term accomplishment and goal fulfillment
Aristotle considered hedonic happiness 'vulgar' because not all desires worth pursuing produce genuine wellness
Corporate measurement of happiness focuses on hedonic pleasure because brain stimulation can be measured and manipulated, ignoring workers' long-term goals
When people are asked if they're happy, they apply random weights to these two different kinds of happiness depending on context
Better data and debates about happiness require explicitly distinguishing between short-term and long-term happiness
money_quotes: Once you focus on optimizing happiness, you will be asking for trouble.
Aristotle invented this concept, deeming happiness vulgar, and stressing that not all desires are worth pursuing as, even though some of them may yield pleasure, they would not produce wellness.
Davies imagines a world where our corporate masters have a perfect view into our brains and have figured out how to stimulate our pleasure centers so that we are maximally 'productive.'
This approach is deliberately unrelated to our eudaimonic pleasure, because it's not focused on our long-term goals, but rather the goals of our employer.
When we are asked whether we're happy, we often interpret it to be some combination of those two kinds of happiness, with possibly random weights assigned to each.

We could get better data and better debates around 'happiness as a thing to strive for or not' if we distinguished between short-term happiness and long-term happiness. It's not that hard to do, and it obviously matters.
posts.key_insight: Debates about measuring and optimizing happiness are fundamentally confused because they fail to distinguish between hedonic pleasure (short-term sensation) and eudaimonic pleasure (long-term fulfillment), a distinction that matters enormously for questions of manipulation and social control.

posts.summary: This advice column features Aunt Pythia responding to reader questions ranging from the meta (how to write interesting advice column questions) to the personal (unrequited love for a best friend) to the systemic (the appalling underrepresentation of Black men in mathematics PhDs). The column's emotional center is the response to a young person in love, where Aunt Pythia delivers a passionate defense of vulnerability and risk-taking. The most substantive question addresses the stark reality that only about 10 Black men per year received math PhDs over a 20-year period, with the writer pointing out that diversity initiatives often focus on women while leaving Black men 'out in the cold.' Aunt Pythia validates the outrage and acknowledges the inadequacy of current resources.
key_points: Vulnerability and emotional risk-taking are undervalued in modern life - 'gut spilling' is presented as courageous rather than foolish
The statistics on Black men in mathematics are staggering: only 203 Black men received math PhDs in the US from 1992-2012
Diversity initiatives in STEM often focus on women while neglecting the crisis of Black male underrepresentation
Framing a doubling from 1% to 2% as 'improvement' is a soulless manipulation of statistics
Love has its own logic and demands expression regardless of practical concerns about timing or outcomes
money_quotes: You know what there isn't enough of nowadays? I'll tell you what. Gut spilling. We are all so careful not to offend or appear vulnerable we forget that none of that really matters.
What really matters is living life fully and taking chances and going out on a limb and being the person you always wanted to be.
If she says no you will be crushed, to be sure, but in fact you will be crushed if you say nothing. It will just take longer and feel less courageous.
One black guy per year in the ENTIRE COUNTRY has been getting a math PhD for two decades…? JESUS CHRIST ON A STICK, THAT'S SOME FUCKERY, PLAIN AND SIMPLE.
If you have no soul, you can convince people of improvement by saying it doubled, but it's still really, really fucked.

I also notice there are various programs and events for women and minorities in mathematics... My gripe is that many of these programs are exclusively intended for women, leaving minority men, especially black men, out in the cold while universities are patting themselves on the back for publicly fulfilling their diversity initiatives.
posts.key_insight: Both personal courage in love and systemic courage in addressing racial disparities in mathematics require us to stop hiding behind comfortable half-measures and confront uncomfortable truths directly.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the start of Occupy Summer School, a three-week morning program in downtown Brooklyn. The program appears to be an educational initiative connected to the Occupy movement's alternative banking group. The first day focuses on introductions, setting expectations, and community building with the participating kids. She notes her blogging may be limited during this intensive period but hopes to document the experience.
key_points: Occupy Summer School is a three-week intensive morning program in Brooklyn
The program is connected to the Alternative Banking working group from Occupy Wall Street
The curriculum includes introducing young people to the Occupy movement and its ideas
There's an emphasis on participant-driven learning by asking what students hope to gain
money_quotes: We're very excited about the line-up, and we have high hopes for the final event.

Today we introduce the kids to ourselves and Occupy, we talk about the next three weeks, we ask them to introduce themselves to us, we ask what they are planning to get out of the experience, and of course we eat donuts.
posts.key_insight: Grassroots movements can extend their impact through educational programs that introduce young people to alternative perspectives on finance and economic justice.

posts.summary: This is a brief, humorous post where O'Neil shares anti-suffrage advertisements from the early 20th century while noting she's busy with Occupy Summer School. She highlights the absurdity of historical anti-women's suffrage arguments by focusing on one particularly ridiculous concern mentioned in vintage ads: the risk of women getting leather stains on their hosiery if given political responsibilities. The post uses humor to underscore how flimsy and bizarre the arguments against women's rights often were.
key_points: O'Neil is running Occupy Summer School and plans to document it through a blog
She shares historical anti-suffrage advertisements to highlight absurd arguments against women's voting rights
The post uses humor to critique the triviality of historical objections to women's equality
money_quotes: Quick question: how often did women of yore get leather stains on their light colored hosiery? Is that really a thing?
posts.key_insight: Historical arguments against expanding rights often relied on absurdly trivial concerns that look ridiculous in hindsight.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil examines evidence-based sentencing models that predict 'recidivism risk' and are used by judges in at least 20 states to determine prison sentence lengths. She argues these models use attributes that serve as proxies for race and class, effectively punishing people for being poor. The post questions whether recidivism prediction is even the right framework, comparing it to Minority Report-style pre-crime punishment. While acknowledging the four legitimate factors judges consider in sentencing, O'Neil identifies critical flaws: the models don't measure how prison itself affects recidivism, and they may overweight public safety concerns while treating drug offenders more harshly than violent criminals.
key_points: Recidivism risk models are used in 20+ states and often rely on attributes that are proxies for race and class
Predicting future crimes to justify longer sentences is philosophically troubling—essentially punishing people for crimes they haven't committed
The four traditional sentencing factors are punishment, deterrence, public protection, and rehabilitation
These models fail to measure whether prison itself changes recidivism risk, making them fundamentally incomplete
Drug offenders score higher risk than violent criminals, but only the latter truly threaten public safety
money_quotes: Hidden underneath the computer code, a given judge is essentially putting someone away for longer because they are poor.
When we consider recidivism we are judging someone more harshly if 'people like them,' however that is defined, have recommitted crimes after being in jail.
In other words, we are judging them more harshly because we suspect they might commit a crime in the future. This is Minority Report type stuff.
The models don't measure the effect of prison on recidivism risk.
Drug offenders have higher recidivism risks than people who commit violent crime, but we really only care about the latter in terms of the public good.
Aside from using problematic inputs in recidivism models, we need to carefully examine how we want to weight those four factors.
posts.key_insight: Recidivism risk models embed racial and class bias while punishing people for predicted future crimes rather than actual offenses, and they fail to measure the very thing they claim to optimize.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil introduces $heriff, a browser add-on that reveals how online prices vary based on location and browsing history. The tool was inspired when its creator and a friend discovered they were being shown different prices for the same hotel room while shopping simultaneously. $heriff works by comparing your price against 'clean browser' requests from academic servers worldwide, exposing location-based price discrimination. The project also crowdsources data from users to study how browsing history affects pricing. O'Neil frames this as both a consumer protection tool and a contribution to scientific understanding of algorithmic pricing.
key_points: Online retailers like Staples and Amazon have been documented showing different prices to different customers based on location and browsing data
$heriff compares your quoted price against 'clean browser' requests from servers at academic institutions worldwide to isolate location-based price variations
The tool crowdsources user data to study how browsing history and cookies affect pricing, which requires widespread adoption to be effective
Price discrimination online goes beyond legitimate factors like sales tax and shipping, with variations too large to explain by tariffs alone
The researchers behind $heriff also study 'search discrimination' where the products shown to users change based on tracking data, not just prices
money_quotes: It allows you to see how prices for goods you're interested in buying would change depending on where the request is coming from. And sometimes the answer is 'a lot,' even on Staples.com or Amazon.com.
Your personal request for price, coming from your browser, might depend on location as well as your cookies and referral url, among other things.
Among other things, this could be a way of figuring out how to intentionally set your input data so that you get the best price for a given product.
The more people who use $heriff and donate their data to the project, the faster we will learn about how prices vary.
It's possible that tariffs come into play, but they don't seem consistent. Also, the prices vary too much to be accounted for by tariffs.
posts.key_insight: Online price discrimination based on location and browsing history is real and measurable, but transparency tools that crowdsource data can help consumers both understand and potentially game these algorithmic pricing systems.

posts.summary: This advice column features Cathy O'Neil as 'Aunt Pythia' answering reader questions on topics ranging from book reviews to dating philosophy to career advice. She critiques the evolutionary psychology book 'Sperm Wars' for presenting theories as settled science without appropriate uncertainty, reviews the concept of dating as an 'arbitrary and misogynistic construct,' offers practical advice on sexual compatibility and compromise, and counsels a software developer on transitioning to analytics while urging them to consider the ethics of algorithm deployment.
key_points: Scientific claims about human behavior should include theories, evidence, and measurements of uncertainty rather than presenting conclusions as settled fact
Traditional dating rituals are criticized as artificial constructs that make women passive receivers while pressuring men to perform
Sexual compatibility requires direct communication, willingness to compromise, and ensuring both partners' needs are addressed
Career transitions into data science require machine learning skills, but practitioners should also consider the ethical implications of building and deploying algorithms
Women's fertility cycles being obscure 'by construction' serves evolutionary purposes by making sex about human interaction, not just reproduction
money_quotes: I'd like to see theories and then evidence, with measurements of uncertainty. I'd like to become part of the process of puzzling out whether a certain habit we humans have fallen into is due to our genetics or our socialization.
It's an arbitrary and misogynistic construct. It makes the woman a passive receiver and at the same time puts too much pressure on the man to perform. Fuck Dating.
Dating isn't a well-defined thing, or if it is, then it's weird and uncomfortable and synthetic.
Find joy in the moment, and in the moment be the person you wish you always were, including being curious and kind to the person you're sharing dinner with.
Please consider the ethics of building and deploying algorithms.
The scientific certainty it presents of 'why people do what they do' with respect to sex is a huge turn-off for me.
posts.key_insight: Whether evaluating scientific claims about human behavior, social rituals like dating, or career choices in technology, we should reject false certainty and artificial constructs in favor of genuine inquiry, authentic human connection, and ethical reflection.

posts.summary: Marc Joffe argues that Congressional Republicans are wrong to block Chapter 9 bankruptcy protection for Puerto Rico. He demonstrates that bond investors were already compensated for default risk through higher yields, so claims of unfair treatment ring hollow. The post analyzes Puerto Rico's complex debt structure across numerous entities with varying credit quality, showing why bankruptcy reorganization would help distressed municipalities while allowing healthy ones to signal their strength. Joffe also dismantles the austerity argument by showing that deficits actually grew under the Republican governor and have shrunk under the current administration, making Republican lectures about fiscal responsibility hypocritical.
key_points: Bond investors knowingly accepted default risk in exchange for yields 150-500 basis points above Treasuries, so they cannot claim to be unfairly punished by restructuring
Puerto Rico's $72 billion debt is spread across dozens of entities with vastly different fiscal health—from solvent Carolina to bankrupt Maunabo—making Chapter 9's entity-by-entity approach essential
Historical precedents from the Depression era show that sub-sovereign debt restructurings typically extended maturities without principal losses, unlike Detroit or Greece
Deficits actually peaked under Republican Governor Fortuño ($5.2 billion) and have fallen significantly under the current Democratic-aligned administration
Puerto Rico's debt-to-GDP ratio of 37% (using internationally comparable GDP) is far below the US federal government's 74%
money_quotes: While buyers of Puerto Rico bonds may have known that issuers did not have access to Chapter 9, they were aware that default was a distinct possibility – and that is all that really counts.
Thus anyone who purchased Puerto Rico bonds over the last several years was compensated for default risk.
The Chapter 9 process would provide an essentially bankrupt community like Maunabo with the ability to reorganize its finances in a more sustainable manner.
During Fortuño's last full fiscal year, 2011-2012, total governmental revenues were $15.8 billion and total expenditures were $21.0 billion. The $5.2 billion deficit was the worst in ten years.
Puerto Rico's fiscal policy has thus been more austere under the current left-of-center government than under the prior Republican administration.
Congressional Republicans seem poorly positioned to lecture Puerto Rico about fiscal responsibility.
posts.key_insight: The Republican argument against Puerto Rico bankruptcy protection fails on both counts: investors were already compensated for default risk through higher yields, and the island's fiscal discipline has actually improved under Democratic governance while deteriorating under Republican leadership.

posts.summary: Puerto Rico faces an unsustainable debt crisis compounded by high prices, few jobs, and a severe drought. The crisis stems partly from 'triple exempt' bonds that made borrowing too easy, similar to Greece's situation. While some advocate extending Chapter 9 bankruptcy to Puerto Rico, legal complexities around its commonwealth status make this ineffective. A federal financial control board, similar to what was used in 1990s Washington D.C., is proposed as an alternative, though this would strip Puerto Ricans of financial autonomy. The fundamental problem remains political neglect of Puerto Rico by U.S. politicians.
key_points: Puerto Rico's triple-exempt bonds created perverse incentives that made borrowing too easy, leading to unsustainable debt levels
Extending Chapter 9 bankruptcy wouldn't actually help because it would redefine Puerto Rico as a state (which can't declare bankruptcy) and special bond provisions prevent restructuring
A federal financial control board is proposed as precedent-based alternative, though it would remove Puerto Rico's control over its own finances
Debt restructuring has historical precedent and default risk is inherent to all debt, making complaints about restructuring somewhat disingenuous
Political neglect of Puerto Rico's interests is the core obstacle to any solution
money_quotes: And now we have to decide how much the people should suffer for the results of the bond markets.
This made it too easy for Puerto Rico to borrow money and has put it in a hole, very analogous to the Greek situation.
Personally, I don't even understand that complaint; surely everyone realizes that any debt might go into default, and it hardly matters exactly what that procedure looks like.
The biggest problem is that, even now, no politician seems to really care about Puerto Rico, except to fight against it becoming a state.
It would essentially mean handing over control over its finances to the board. Culpepper notes that this could even happen without consent.
posts.key_insight: Puerto Rico's debt crisis reveals how legal complexity and political neglect combine to trap a territory in financial suffering, with proposed solutions either legally unworkable or democratically problematic.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil provides a mid-program update on Occupy Summer School, a program teaching high school students about activism and organizing. The program made local news in the Metro, featuring the teenage participants. Over the first week, students learned from union organizers, activists, and sociologists about topics ranging from Black Lives Matter to union organizing to creative protest tactics. The curriculum combined theoretical discussions about power and inequality with practical planning of actual protests, including a bake sale highlighting the gender pay gap and a demonstration against subway fare policies.
key_points: The program uses 'stack' discussion methods and focuses on student-chosen issues including inequality, Black Lives Matter, taxation, and gender issues
Students learned practical organizing skills from experienced activists, union members, and sociologists
The curriculum emphasizes hands-on protest planning, including a bake sale where women pay 78 cents and men pay one dollar to illustrate the pay gap
One student's $100 subway ticket sparked a protest demanding unlimited metro rides for high school students on all days
The program structure gradually shifted power to students, with Thursday's Black Lives Matter discussion being the first where 'girls really took over'
money_quotes: The girls didn't know much about unions, and were interested to learn how power can be created through numbers.
This was the first time where the girls really took over the discussion and the grownups in the room were merely listening.
The day ended by planning a bake sale where women would be charged 78 cents and men one dollar for the same brownies, to illustrate the difference in pay.
Adam came and talked to them about sociology, what is power (power is the opposite of dependence), and his work helping organize workers at Walmart.
posts.key_insight: Teaching activism to young people works best when it combines theoretical understanding of power structures with immediate, practical opportunities to organize around issues that directly affect their lives.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her 1999 attempt to dress her children in gender-neutral clothing, only to discover the retail landscape offered nothing but rigidly gendered pink and blue sections. While she notes that companies like Svaha now make gender-neutral kids' clothes, she points out these cost four times more than standard options, making them accessible mainly to well-off parents. She also pushes back on the marketing claim that such clothes 'empower' children, arguing that young kids already assume they can do anything until adults introduce limitations. She concludes that Star Trek uniforms remain the ideal precisely because they're devoid of any message or style signaling.
key_points: Mainstream children's clothing in the late 1990s and early 2000s was rigidly divided into gendered pink and blue sections with no overlap
Gender-neutral clothing options now exist but cost roughly four times more than standard gendered clothes, making them a privilege of affluent parents
Marketing claims that clothes 'empower' children are misguided because external definitions typically limit rather than expand kids' sense of possibility
Young children naturally assume unlimited potential until adults and social conventions impose restrictions
The ideal clothing would be completely devoid of style or message, like Star Trek uniforms
money_quotes: There's a pink section and a royal blue and red section. Nothing in between, and no overlap.
Like organic food, open-ended gender categorical clothing is firmly within the realm of the well-off parent.

Most times, when a kid is defined externally, through a shirt or a social convention or an adult's comment, or even another kid's comment, it's an exercise in limiting that kid, not expanding him or her.
Kids assume they can do anything until we tell them otherwise.
When you say to a young girl, 'You can be a scientist too, you know!' she thinks, 'I never thought I couldn't. Wait, why should I think I couldn't?'
The great thing about them is how utterly vapid they are of style or message.
posts.key_insight: Well-intentioned messaging that tells children they 'can' do something paradoxically introduces the idea that they might not be able to, making neutral, message-free presentation the more empowering choice.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces a demonstration organized by Occupy Summer School students to protest gender and racial inequality. The protest features two creative activism tables: a 'bake sale' charging men $1 and women 77 cents for cupcakes to highlight the pay gap, and a brownie table with toothpick flags containing racial inequality statistics. The post connects these educational tactics to urgent real-world stakes, citing Sandra Bland's death following a minor traffic stop. O'Neil invites readers to join the Brooklyn event to support the young activists.
key_points: Students designed a two-table demonstration using food as a vehicle for consciousness-raising about inequality
The cupcake pricing differential (77 cents vs $1) directly illustrates the gender pay gap in a tangible way
Racial inequality statistics are delivered via toothpick flags on brownies, noting that people of color are 30% of the population but 60% of those imprisoned
The post connects lighthearted activism tactics to life-and-death stakes, referencing Sandra Bland's death
Young women from UAI are leading the organizing effort
money_quotes: From the men/women pay gap to how the police arrest black and brown people for minor nuisance crimes, the girls from the UAI have figured out thoughtful ways of raising consciousness while having fun.
At the first table, there will be a 'bake sale' where cupcakes will be 'sold' for $1 to men but 77 cents to women, to protest unequal pay.
While people of color make up about 30 percent of the United States' population, they account for 60 percent of those imprisoned.
Everyone loves free food, of course, but given that black women like Sandra Bland get killed in this country for minor traffic infractions, there's a deeply serious side to it as well.
posts.key_insight: Creative, accessible activism tactics can make abstract inequality statistics viscerally concrete while honoring the deadly serious stakes behind the numbers.

posts.summary: The post explores a fascinating mathematical curiosity discovered at the author's high school math camp: when triangular numbers are highlighted on the Ulam Spiral (a way of mapping natural numbers onto a 2D grid), they form a pattern with exactly 17 spiral arms. O'Neil provides a rough proof using asymptotic analysis, showing that the spacing of triangular numbers relative to the spiral's structure produces approximately 17 evenly-spaced arms per 6 rows of the spiral. The proof relies on Gauss's formula for triangular numbers and the geometric properties of the Ulam Spiral's construction.
key_points: The Ulam Spiral is a method for mapping natural numbers onto a 2D grid by spiraling outward, and primes notably cluster along diagonal lines in this representation
Triangular numbers (1, 3, 6, 10, 15...) form a distinct 17-armed spiral pattern when highlighted on the Ulam Spiral
The proof uses asymptotic approximation: for large values, each layer of the spiral contains approximately 2.83 triangular numbers
After 6 rows of the spiral, you encounter approximately 17 triangular numbers (since 6 × 2.83 ≈ 17), explaining the 17-arm pattern
The pattern holds asymptotically but shifts very slightly as the spiral grows larger
money_quotes: We loyal alums of HCSSiM have a tradition of going back every July 17th to celebrate 'Yellow Pig day,' which consists of a talk where founder and director Kelly talks extensively about fun facts regarding the number 17
When you highlight the triangular numbers in the Ulam Spiral, instead of the primes, then you get something that looks weird
OK so if you count those spiral arms, you'll see there are 17 of them. But does that last forever? And if so, why?
Well, the answer is going to be yes.
This is when things get asymptotic.
So far we've figured out that, when n is huge, then after meeting the kth triangular number on the nth row, we will see two more, and get most of the way to a third, by going one more row
posts.key_insight: The seemingly magical appearance of exactly 17 spiral arms when triangular numbers are plotted on the Ulam Spiral emerges naturally from the asymptotic relationship between the growth rates of triangular numbers and the spiral's geometry.

posts.summary: The post argues that the Greek debt crisis should be understood not as a moral failing of Greece, but as a consequence of reckless German banking decisions. German and French banks heavily invested in Greek bonds partly because European regulations allowed them to classify these bonds as 'zero risk' despite their higher yields. When the 2008 crisis hit, Germany bailed out its own banks, effectively transferring what was a banking problem onto taxpayers. The subsequent Greek bailouts were largely loans used to pay back the original creditors, further entangling European governments while devastating the Greek economy.
key_points: German banks invested heavily in Greek bonds because Basel regulations let them classify the debt as zero-risk while still collecting higher yields
The German bank bailout of 2008 transformed a banking problem into a taxpayer problem, preceding the Greek crisis framing
Greek bailouts were largely loans to pay back original creditors, not actual economic aid to Greece
The 'moral weight of debt' narrative obscures the role of creditors who should have known better in a free market
Yield premiums on Greek bonds existed precisely because the market recognized more risk than the zero-risk designation suggested
money_quotes: Even the IMF has declared the current Greek deal untenable, but that hasn't seemed to interfere with the actual negotiations.
Whenever there's a market with a spectrum of products, the ones with the biggest yield for a given risk profile will be snatched up the fastest, because people want to maximize profits.
Either way, when the Germans bailed out their banks, they took what was a bank problem and made it into a taxpayer problem.
The very reason Greek bonds had yield was because the market was differentiating it from German bonds.
The Greeks might not have needed bailing out if the original Greek bonds had been refused by risk-averse bankers in the first place.
If we're looking for who deserves blame in this story, we might want to circle back to the German bankers who couldn't resist subprime mortgages and Greek bonds.
posts.key_insight: The moralistic framing of Greek debt obscures that German banks made reckless investments enabled by flawed regulations, then shifted their losses onto taxpayers through bailouts.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil recommends a lecture by David Kung examining gender and racial achievement gaps in mathematics. She notes the lecture provides historical perspective on math's 'sad history' regarding these disparities and advocates for ending passive lecturing as a teaching method. The post is brief, serving primarily as a pointer to Kung's more detailed analysis.
key_points: David Kung has researched gender and racial achievement gaps in mathematics education
Mathematics has a troubled historical record regarding inclusion and equity
The lecture argues for abandoning passive lecturing in favor of more engaging teaching methods
money_quotes: math has a sad history
posts.key_insight: Mathematics education has historically failed women and minorities, and reforming teaching methods away from passive lecturing is part of addressing these gaps.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where Aunt Pythia (Cathy O'Neil) responds to reader questions on topics ranging from personal bias in blogging, frustrations with tech industry's superficial approach to social problems, Google Chrome's always-on listening features, and career advice for aspiring data journalists. The column blends personal reflection with sharp observations about the data science field, particularly criticizing the 'hack poverty' mentality of tech workers who lack understanding of social contexts. O'Neil offers practical career advice while acknowledging the frustrations of doing substantive policy work in an era that celebrates flashy corporate tech solutions.
key_points: Tech workers' desire to 'hack poverty' reflects a critical lack of familiarity with the context of public interest work and social good
Having strong opinions and biases is part of having a personality - the key is being willing to discuss specific examples when challenged
We already trust Google and Apple with enormous amounts of personal data (email), making voice listening just another increment of surveillance
The academic camp of big data consists of researchers genuinely trying to advance fields like medicine but frustrated by privacy laws
Building an impressive online portfolio of newsworthy projects matters more than credentials for breaking into data journalism
money_quotes: Do you ever wake up not knowing what you want to do when you grow up? And then you realize you're far too old to feel that way?

Not punching someone in the face when they tell me they want to 'hack poverty' or any number of other things that speak to a critical lack of familiarity with the context of public interest or work for social good.
I agree that there is far too much absolute bullshit out there, and I'd like to help by promoting substantial work.
As for how creepy this is, it really depends on how much we trust Google and Apple. And the answer is: a fuck ton.
We let Google read all our emails already, don't forget.
There are certain things I'm happily opinionated and even stubborn about, but that's what it means to have a personality, isn't it?
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's enthusiasm for 'hacking' social problems often masks a fundamental ignorance of the complex contexts in which public interest work actually operates.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines a troubling case of academic publishing gone wrong, where a highly-cited 2010 paper claiming Twitter could predict stock markets with 87% accuracy faced serious replication failures and criticism. When a trader wrote a formal complaint letter to the journal, two reviewers initially supported publishing it. However, the editor inexplicably allowed the original paper's author to serve as the deciding reviewer, who predictably rejected the complaint. O'Neil argues this represents a fundamental breakdown in academic peer review standards, though she acknowledges the complainant's profit motive doesn't make him entirely sympathetic either.
key_points: The Twitter mood/stock prediction paper was cited 1300 times despite claims that experienced traders and hedge funds found the strategy unprofitable when tested
Standard market logic suggests if such a strong signal existed, hedge funds would have already exploited and depleted it
Two of three reviewers initially supported action on the complaint letter, calling the original claims 'severely exaggerated'
The original paper's author was allowed to serve as the deciding reviewer on whether to retract his own work - a clear conflict of interest
Proper procedure would have been to publish the complaint letter with an opportunity for the author to respond publicly, not to give the author veto power
money_quotes: People in hedge funds have been trolling for signal in all sorts of news and social media text-based ways for a long while, and there's simply no way that they would have ignored such a strong signal all the way into 2008.
If it was real, they wouldn't have ignored it, and it would have faded.
That's what's cool about academic publishing, right? That we could just be super wrong and people can say what they think and then we get to have this open conversation?
The original paper's performance claims are convincingly shown to be severely exaggerated.
I'm pretty certain that asking the original author to be the deciding vote on whether a paper gets retracted isn't – or should not be – standard practice.

I'm not incredibly sympathetic with someone trying to make money off of a published algorithm and then getting pissed when they lose money instead. I'm willing to admit that more than one of these parties is biased.
posts.key_insight: Academic peer review breaks down when journals allow original authors to serve as deciding reviewers on challenges to their own work, undermining the self-correcting mechanism that gives science its credibility.

posts.summary: O'Neil responds to Rachel Thomas's Medium article arguing that women leaving tech is an environmental problem, not just a pipeline issue. She validates these concerns with her own experience of poor maternity policies, unreasonable hours, and frat-like culture. However, she pushes for more rigorous analysis, asking how women's retention rates compare to men's and to other fields. She argues that without proper context and breakdown of reasons, we can't effectively address the problem or know whether pipeline-focused efforts are worthwhile.
key_points: The tech industry's gender problem is environmental (retention) not just pipeline (recruitment)
Tech culture issues include poor maternity policies, unreasonable hours, and exclusionary social dynamics
Women leave engineering at 42% vs 17% for men, but more comparative context is needed
Pipeline efforts are potentially wasted if the jobs themselves prove unattractive to women
Having seniority and credentials (like a Ph.D.) may buffer women from the worst treatment
money_quotes: It's not just a pipeline problem, it's an environmental problem.
The maternity policies are crap, the long hours are unreasonable, and the frat-like atmosphere exhausts me.
No, I do not want to play ping pong during my lunch hour.
I was already a grownup, with a Ph.D., when I entered this stuff, and as such I'm allowed to have stronger opinions than the average engineer.
It would be ridiculous to spend an enormous amount of time on that if, once they get a job, that job proves unattractive.
posts.key_insight: Focusing on getting women into tech is pointless if we don't also fix why they leave at more than twice the rate of men.

posts.summary: The trucking industry claims a worker shortage of 30,000 drivers, but Cathy O'Neil argues this is manufactured by employers unwilling to pay adequate wages or address deteriorating working conditions. While trucking companies lobby Congress to lower the driving age to 18, they ignore basic supply and demand: surveillance technology has degraded driver autonomy, large companies have pushed out independent truckers, and turnover rates are staggering. The so-called shortage is simply employers refusing to compensate workers fairly for difficult, demanding work that keeps them away from home for days at a time.
key_points: The trucking 'shortage' could be solved by paying more or improving conditions, but companies prefer lobbying for dangerous policy changes like allowing 18-year-old drivers
Surveillance technology installed to ensure safety has been weaponized to micromanage and harass drivers, degrading their quality of life
Large companies used surveillance to lower costs and push out independent truckers, fundamentally changing an industry that prided itself on independence
High turnover rates are the predictable result of worsening conditions without compensating wage increases
money_quotes: Funny how demand and supply and efficient markets go out the window when there's a political point being served.
When you make your workers lives worse, and you don't compensate them with cash money to make up for it, you find your workers quitting.
There's no worker shortage, there's simply an unwillingness, on the employers' side, to face up to the facts.
For an industry that has historically prided itself for its independent nature, this change does not sit well with drivers.
posts.key_insight: Claims of worker shortages often mask employers' refusal to pay market wages or maintain decent working conditions, while surveillance technology marketed as safety measures gets repurposed as tools of worker control.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' responds to reader questions on topics ranging from racial profiling in traffic stops to managing emotionally draining friendships. The centerpiece discusses white privilege through the lens of two traffic stop anecdotes, where the columnist argues that discrimination is 'statistical rather than deterministic' - individual incidents can't prove privilege, but patterns reveal it. She also addresses questions about sexual boundaries in relationships and how to handle friends who constantly dump their problems. The column maintains a casual, irreverent tone while tackling substantive social issues.
key_points: Discrimination and privilege are statistical phenomena rather than deterministic - you can't prove any single incident was affected by race, but patterns across many interactions reveal systemic bias

The columnist describes witnessing a white man screaming profanities at a Black female cop giving him a ticket, noting it was 'absolutely inconceivable' the roles could have been reversed with the same behavior
On draining friendships: have compassion but don't make others' problems your own problems, as 'that's not helpful to either of you'
People who talk a lot about their problems typically don't want advice to fix them - advice-giving mainly helps the giver feel like a good friend
money_quotes: That's the thing with discrimination, it's statistical rather than deterministic.
Instead of thinking about specific events being white privilege events, think overall about how often your interactions with power are pleasant or unpleasant.

I am most definitely a white woman and it has worked for me. Of course, those police might just have been nice to everyone; again, it's statistics, and I can't prove my white privilege, but I don't doubt it one bit either.
It was absolutely inconceivable that if the roles had been reversed, and it had been a white male cop giving a ticket to a black women, I would have seen her acting in the same way.
Listen to her, have compassion, smile and give her love and support, but don't get yourself empathetic to the point of suffering yourself, because that's not helpful to either of you.
People who talk a lot about their problems don't want advice to fix them.
posts.key_insight: Privilege and discrimination operate statistically across many interactions rather than being provable in any single incident, which is why examining patterns matters more than analyzing individual events.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil considers whether to transition her advice column 'Aunt Pythia' from written to video format after being inspired by a Tig Notaro advice video. She weighs the pros and cons with characteristic humor, noting benefits like audience connection and showing real cooking, while joking about wardrobe requirements and occasional baking shortcuts. The post solicits reader feedback while playfully dismissing potential critics.
key_points: Tig Notaro's video advice format struck her as 'moving and intimate'
Video format could create stronger audience connection
The casual, humorous tone of the column would translate differently to video
She's genuinely soliciting reader input on the format change
money_quotes: I thought it was moving and intimate, just like an advice column should be.
doesn't have to wear anything below the waist
sometimes fakes it and bakes Pillsbury cinnamon rolls
If you hate Aunt Pythia then I don't really care what you think.
posts.key_insight: The best advice-giving format is one that creates genuine intimacy and connection with the audience, whether written or video.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that Occupy Summer School (OSS), an educational experiment connected to the Occupy movement, has been featured in The New Yorker's Talk of the Town column. Journalist Alex Carp attended more than half of the summer school sessions and wrote up the experience. The post also mentions coverage from German Public Radio and includes a personal note identifying O'Neil as the blue-haired person in an accompanying photo. This is essentially a brief celebratory announcement sharing media coverage of the grassroots educational initiative.
key_points: Occupy Summer School received mainstream media recognition in The New Yorker
A journalist embedded with the program for an extended period to write the piece
The initiative also attracted international attention from German Public Radio
O'Neil was personally involved as a participant/organizer in the summer school
money_quotes: I'm super proud to say that Alex Carp, a journalist who was present for more than half of Occupy Summer School last month, did a fantastic job of writing up the OSS experiment
I'm the blue-haired person on the left
posts.key_insight: Grassroots educational experiments emerging from social movements can gain legitimacy through mainstream media coverage.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil explores how Uber drivers could collectively game Uber's surge pricing algorithm to their benefit.
