She speculates about how surge pricing likely works—triggering when rider demand exceeds driver supply in a neighborhood—and notes that while Uber wants to avoid surges to keep riders happy, drivers benefit from them. She describes how drivers could coordinate to artificially create surge conditions, either by congregating outside surge zones or using apps like Blinkr to simultaneously turn off their Uber apps, reducing supply. The post ends with a pointed observation about the legal ambiguity: since Uber classifies drivers as independent contractors rather than employees, they can't be 'fired' for such collective action.
key_points: Uber's surge pricing algorithm likely triggers based on the ratio of riders to available drivers in defined neighborhoods
Uber and its drivers have misaligned incentives—Uber wants to minimize surges while drivers benefit from them
Drivers could game the system by coordinating to artificially reduce supply, triggering surge pricing
Apps like Blinkr already exist to help drivers coordinate such strategies
Uber's classification of drivers as contractors creates a legal gray area where collective action resembles a strike but may not be actionable
money_quotes: Uber wants to keep their riders happy, but to do that they actually tend to want to avoid creating surge situations, since surge situations usually imply riders wait longer and pay more.
That means Uber drivers have a great incentive to game the system and create artificial surges.
All they'd need to coordinate this is something like a walkie talkie system. Or an app.
It's something like an Uber strike, and it requires coordination, but I don't think it's illegal, right?
I mean, Uber can't fire them for doing this, since they aren't employees, right?
posts.key_insight: Uber's gig economy model—classifying drivers as contractors while controlling their pay through algorithms—creates a vulnerability where collective action to game the pricing system occupies a legal gray zone that Uber designed for its own benefit.

posts.summary: A veteran long-haul truck driver examines the growing challenges facing the OTR trucking profession, focusing on invasive surveillance through inward-facing cameras and electronic logging devices. The post details how ELDs, while preventing dangerous overdriving, create perverse incentives that can compromise driver safety and pay, and explains how drivers game these systems through loopholes. The author also exposes how 'per diem pay' schemes benefit companies at drivers' expense while obscuring true income figures. The piece concludes by acknowledging trucking's traditional advantages—low barriers to entry, autonomy, and practical benefits—while warning that automation may soon eliminate these jobs entirely.
key_points: Inward-facing cameras that constantly monitor drivers are becoming standard, with Swift Transportation leading the trend, threatening the autonomy that makes trucking appealing

Electronic logging devices create impossible choices between sacrificing pay, parking unsafely, or recording violations—leading drivers to exploit system loopholes like timing accelerations to avoid detection
Hours-of-service rules fail to prevent employers from ordering night drivers to suddenly drive all night, with no time-of-day protections after industry lobbying suspended 2013 reforms
Per diem pay schemes reduce reported trucker incomes by up to 20% while benefiting companies through lower payroll taxes and hidden administrative fees
Automation threatens to eliminate trucking jobs entirely, making the profession a poor long-term career choice for younger workers
money_quotes: That's a lot of time to be in front of a running camera, never knowing for sure who might be watching you.
Such orders went into my personal 'go ahead, fire me if you need to' (for refusing) category many years ago, but this shouldn't have been necessary.
This sweet tax deduction sours into something called per diem pay when employers decide to get in the middle and 'reimburse' drivers instead.

The reason I left my longtime, relatively well-paying OTR job five years ago wasn't so much because my employer switched over to using per diem pay, it was more because of a deceptive 'more pay in your pocket' ad campaign it foisted on drivers.

One of the standard pros people cite is you don't have a boss breathing down your neck. You do have one telling you where to go and when, but yes, the physical distance helps. Unfortunately inward-facing cameras threaten to obliterate the no-boss-breathing advantage.
Viewed strictly within the context of job-seeking my years of formal math education have served little to no useful purpose to date.
posts.key_insight: Trucking's traditional appeal—autonomy, decent pay for accessible work, freedom from direct oversight—is being systematically eroded by surveillance technology and corporate pay schemes that shift risks onto drivers while automation looms as the final threat.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where Cathy O'Neil (as 'Aunt Pythia') answers reader questions on topics ranging from crepe recipes to dating advice to the mathematical foundations needed for data science. The most substantive response addresses whether someone needs innate math talent to become a data scientist, where she argues that understanding concepts like matrix inversion isn't about effortless genius but about developing the detective skills to debug models when they produce garbage. She emphasizes that data science requires understanding computational fragility—like what happens when you try to invert a nearly-singular matrix—because models never work correctly on the first attempt. She also briefly dismisses a misogynistic article about women in tech and provides thoughtful dating advice focused on self-improvement and putting oneself out there.
key_points: Data scientists don't need to memorize matrix inversion formulas, but must understand computational fragility when dealing with nearly-singular matrices
A data scientist's job is mostly figuring out why their model is 'fucking up massively'—models never work the first, second, or 17th time
Real-world data is always an approximation of truth, which creates computational problems that manifest as garbage outputs rather than clear error messages
Mathematical aptitude for data science isn't about instant effortless understanding—it's about developing detective skills to backtrack from wrong answers to root causes
The word 'shrill' in an article is an immediate tell that the author is a woman hater
money_quotes: The data scientist's job is mostly to figure out why their model is fucking up massively.
Models never work the first, second, or 17th time they are run, so you'd better be good at understanding what's going wrong.
When you're dealing with real world data, everything is an approximation of truth.
None of this stuff is easy or effortless, so throw away that misconception immediately.
You're not supposed to 'invert' an uninvertible matrix, but you of course can invert a matrix that has incredibly small but non-zero determinant. What you end up with is garbage.
Good news is he gave himself away early with the word 'shrill'; after that I knew he was a woman hater.
About half of self-confidence is the belief that other people will want to spend time with you.
posts.key_insight: The real skill in data science isn't mathematical brilliance but the detective work of understanding why computations produce garbage—and the conceptual knowledge to trace problems back to their mathematical roots.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her intention to join the futurism movement, motivated by concerns about oligarchy, racist policing, and the disappearance of middle-class jobs. She critiques the field as dominated by white men aged 55-65 who obsess over technology while ignoring social issues. O'Neil argues that women are underrepresented in futurism because its core visions—immortality, brain uploading, techno-utopianism—appeal primarily to those disconnected from bodily realities and blind to technology's downsides. She plans to inject social concerns into futurist discourse, arguing that current models are fatally flawed because they focus on technological variables while ignoring human factors.
key_points: The futurism field is dominated by older white men who focus on technological optimism while ignoring social issues like racism, inequality, and job displacement
Women are less drawn to futurism because its core visions (living forever, brain uploading) seem disconnected from embodied human experience
Successful people in tech suffer from an 'I win' blindspot that makes them uncritical of systems that reward them while harming others
Current futurist models are flawed because they use too few variables, focusing on technological change while excluding human and social factors
The surveillance economy benefits those creating the technology while harming those being surveilled, like truckers being monitored
money_quotes: The only way to compete with robots is to become one
Being reminded of death once a month makes it impossible to be so silly
I call this the 'I win' blindspot, whereby people who are generally rewarded in a system seem to think the system must be great
Why should the white guys have all the fun of deciding what the future might look like?
Men grow pubes at the age of 14 and nothing ever seems to change again
They focus on technological change without taking into account human beings

If you're the one creating the surveillance software or analyzing the surveillance footage, you're getting paid really well to promote 'progress.' Not so much the story for the truckers being surveilled
posts.key_insight: Futurism's narrow focus on technological progress while ignoring social issues stems from the demographic homogeneity of its practitioners, who benefit from current systems and remain blind to their harms.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques the Effective Altruism movement's shift from fighting global poverty to funding AI apocalypse research, which she sees as tech elites using pseudo-scientific reasoning to justify ignoring present-day problems. She draws on Dylan Matthews's Vox piece about an EA conference at Google where attendees dismissed global poverty as a 'rounding error' compared to existential AI risks. O'Neil argues this reasoning—using astronomical numbers to calculate expected value of preventing robot takeovers—is a way of 'nerdifying' the messy world to avoid actually improving it. She compares this to the hedge fund 'Bill Gates Life Plan' of getting rich first and doing good later, suggesting the movement applies scientific-sounding altruism to fundamentally vain and greedy impulses.
key_points: Effective Altruism has shifted from fighting global poverty to funding AI research, with CS majors convincing each other that computer science is the best way to save the world
The movement uses astronomical expected-value calculations (10^54 lives, billionths of percentage points) to justify prioritizing speculative future threats over present suffering
This logic is unfalsifiable—as Matthews notes, you could use it to justify giving $10 to a wizard claiming to prevent human extinction
The movement assumes potential future people should count equally to people who definitely exist today
Silicon Valley billionaires funding these think tanks adds a self-congratulatory tone to what may be rationalized greed
money_quotes: The point is that it's a way of nerdifying the current messy world and thereby have an excuse for not improving things now.
Compared to that, multiple attendees said, global poverty is a 'rounding error.'
At the risk of overgeneralizing, the computer science majors have convinced each other that the best way to save the world is to do computer science research.
This whole movement might simply be a way of applying a sheen of scientific objectivity and altruism to a vain and greedy impulse.
I really do believe that effective altruism could be the last social movement we ever need.

Anyone who's worked at a hedge fund has heard far too many people with a similar 'Bill Gates Life Plan': first, amass asstons of money by hook or by crook, and then, and only then, deploy their personal plans for charity and world improvement.
posts.key_insight: Effective Altruism's pivot to AI existential risk is a sophisticated rationalization that lets tech elites ignore present-day suffering while feeling morally superior about speculative future problems.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her participation on the Program Committee for Bloomberg's Data For Good Exchange, a one-day event preceding the Strata NY conference. The event features speakers including Jake Porway and includes a competition for papers that contribute to the public good. As a committee member, she will review and rate submitted papers. She expresses enthusiasm about the quality of submissions she's seen so far.
key_points: Bloomberg is hosting a Data For Good Exchange event on September 28th
The event leads into O'Reilly's annual Strata NY conference
A paper competition focuses on research contributing to the public good
O'Neil's role involves reviewing and rating submitted papers
money_quotes: I'm happy to announce that I'm on the Program Committee for Bloomberg's Data For Good Exchange.
There's a competition for papers that contribute to the public good.
I've taken a quick look at the papers and they look pretty amazing.
posts.key_insight: Data science communities are organizing around applying their skills for public benefit through events like the Data For Good Exchange.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the reported teacher shortage through an economic lens, arguing that 'labor shortages' should be analyzed by examining wages, working conditions, benefits, and training costs. She contends that teaching has become toxic due to Value-Added Models, teaching to the test, and attacks on tenure, while salaries haven't compensated for increased risk. Drawing parallels to trucking and fruit picking, she argues these shortages are manufactured by degraded conditions rather than genuine lack of willing workers. Her conclusion is straightforward: improve conditions or pay more.
key_points: Labor shortages should be analyzed through four factors: wages, job conditions, benefits, and training costs
Teaching conditions have deteriorated due to Value-Added Models and Education Reform measures that remove autonomy
Tenure is under attack but salaries haven't risen to compensate for the added job insecurity
Similar dynamics affect truckers and fruit pickers - degraded environments without corresponding wage increases
Lowering barriers to entry is not a solution when job quality matters (illiterate teachers, dangerous truckers)
money_quotes: Whenever we hear about a 'labor shortage' in a given profession, we should think about four things: Wages, Conditions on the job, Benefits, including retirement, Cost/length of training.
When you take the above points together, you realize that it's not a salary thing so much as an environment that has become toxic.
A capable person, however earnest, would think twice before entering such an industry.
This is particularly true right now, when tenure is on the chopping block but the salary hasn't risen to compensate for the added risk.
We've got some skilled workers whose environments have been severely degraded, and whose salaries have not risen in response.
The solutions are obvious: either make their lives better or give them more money, or both.
posts.key_insight: Labor shortages are typically symptoms of degraded working conditions and inadequate compensation, not genuine scarcity of willing workers.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reports on Population Council research testing four strategies to delay child marriage in sub-Saharan Africa: community conversations, school supply assistance, livestock incentives, and combined approaches. The study tracked outcomes for girls aged 12-17 across Ethiopia, Tanzania, and Burkina Faso, measuring marriage rates, sexual experience, and pregnancy. Different strategies proved effective for different age groups, and economic conditions significantly impacted child marriage rates. The research highlights how girls' sexuality is commoditized in the marriage market, with even a few years' delay substantially improving their negotiating power and life outcomes.
key_points: Economic desperation drives child marriage - families trade daughters for livestock dowries or marry them off due to poverty
Different intervention strategies work for different age groups (12-14 vs 15-17)
External economic shocks, like coffee farmers being swindled, dramatically increased child marriage rates
Girls who marry young are often already out of school, so school-based interventions miss the most vulnerable
Delaying marriage even a few years significantly improves girls' autonomy and reduces intergenerational poverty
money_quotes: The reasoning is often desperate and economic: I need these cows, I will give up my daughter for them.
These girls' sexuality has been utterly commoditized for the marriage market, and their autonomy is basically nonexistent.
Even delaying marriage from 12 until they are 16 makes a real difference in their negotiating power.
The younger they are married and start having babies, the more likely they are to live in poverty for another generation.
Often the young girls who are going to be married young are also not going to school, so it makes little sense to focus efforts only on girls in school.
It's refreshing to see scientific experimental design and data collection being used for such a good cause!
posts.key_insight: Rigorous experimental research reveals that even modest economic interventions like livestock incentives can meaningfully delay child marriage, giving girls crucial years to develop autonomy in systems that have commoditized their futures.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where Cathy O'Neil (as 'Aunt Pythia') answers reader questions with her friend 'Sister of My Sister.' Topics include whether to pursue math graduate school, gender disparities in college admissions, reflecting on meaningful advice given, and navigating a sexless marriage. The college admissions discussion is the most substantive, examining statistics showing women face lower acceptance rates at elite schools and exploring possible explanations including application behavior differences, SAT score disparities, and institutional incentives around future endowment contributions.
key_points: When uncertain about pursuing a passion like math grad school, do what you'd do if you weren't insecure - wanting to read math in spare time is a very good sign
Elite private colleges show significant gender gaps in acceptance rates favoring men, possibly constituting de facto affirmative action for male applicants
Statistical disparities in admissions could reflect differences in application behavior, but more likely reflect institutional preferences embedded in algorithms and processes

Colleges may implicitly favor male applicants because men are more likely to become major endowment contributors in a sexist society - such biases get embedded in processes rather than stated explicitly
The most rewarding advice helps people stop worrying and start feeling good now, because 'there's really no time to lose when it comes to just getting on with your life'
money_quotes: Do what you'd do if you weren't insecure.
We never know whether we have what it takes for anything. At least we who are not crazy narcissistic don't.
A cynical person would point out that what colleges really care about is future endowment contributions, and in our sexist society men are more likely to be the contributors to that.
It's more likely to be embedded in an algorithm or at least a process, as many such assumptions are.
Both 'grades' and 'SAT scores' are somewhat arbitrary systems of judgement, neither of which are particularly convincing to me of inner intrinsic worth.
There's really no time to lose when it comes to just getting on with your life.
For an unromantic atheist such as myself, marriages are not simply friendships, they are sexual arrangements.
posts.key_insight: Institutional biases often get embedded in algorithms and processes rather than being explicitly stated, making them harder to identify and challenge even when statistical evidence suggests discrimination.

posts.summary: This guest post by a restaurant professional explains why there's a shortage of talented cooks in the American restaurant industry. The author argues that the tipping system creates a two-tiered labor model where servers earn significantly more than kitchen staff, suppressing cook wages to unsustainable levels. Some restaurants are experimenting with administrative fees to redistribute wages more fairly, but these efforts face resistance from servers unwilling to take pay cuts. The post concludes that until the industry reconciles its contrasting payment models and pays cooks more, the talent shortage will persist.
key_points: The tipping system allows restaurant owners to pass labor costs for servers onto consumers while keeping cook wages artificially low to maintain slim profit margins
Every dollar increase in menu prices automatically increases server tips by 20 cents, making it impossible to raise cook wages without also raising server pay
Some restaurants are implementing 20% administrative fees to pool revenue and redistribute wages, raising cook pay to $15-18/hour from $12-13
Cooking offers poor long-term career prospects: physically grueling work, wages that don't increase substantially with experience, and ownership as the only path to financial security
The chef shortage is fundamentally a redistribution problem requiring either lower server wages or significantly higher menu prices
money_quotes: We could ponder a litany of reasons why talented cooks are not flocking to the kitchens, but if you prefer to stop reading now, just reference Mathbabe's entirely accurate post on labor shortages. Or, we could just pay cooks more.
Restaurant owners are able to pass off the cost of labor for employing servers onto their consumers.
Thankfully, we are all alcoholics and willing to pay 400% markups on our beer and only a 30% markup on our burgers.
Every dollar increase in the price of line item on your receipt increases a consumers cost by $1.20, the server happily pocketing the difference.
That's wealth redistribution in the restaurant world!
Celebrity chefs may glamorize the industry, but their presence in the marketplace is more akin to celebrity than chef, and their salaries have little bearing on real wage growth of labor force.
Cooking is not an ideal position to age into: the physicality of the work and hours only become more grueling, and your wages will not increase substantially with time.
I'm currently looking for a serving job and I can tell you a few places I'm not applying to.
posts.key_insight: The restaurant industry's chef shortage isn't a mystery of labor supply—it's a predictable consequence of a tipping system that structurally suppresses kitchen wages while enriching front-of-house staff.

posts.summary: O'Neil adapts Gary Chapman's 'five love languages' framework to workplace motivation, proposing seven distinct reasons people work: money, security, status, social connection, positive contribution, relief from boredom, and passion. She argues that most work motivations are actually independent of money, which has implications for hypothetical post-scarcity 'Star Trek economies.' The post suggests that people driven by passion, contribution, or status would thrive without financial necessity, while those who value money for its own sake would resist such changes. O'Neil positions herself as motivated by positive contribution and uses the framework to normalize different motivations without judgment.
key_points: Work motivations can be categorized into seven distinct 'languages': money, security, status, social connection, positive contribution, relief from boredom, and passion
Most of these motivations are independent of money, suggesting people would continue working even if basic needs were met
The love languages framework helps reserve judgment about people with different values—understanding we're 'wired differently'
In a post-scarcity economy, only those who value money for its own sake would be deeply disappointed
Those who benefit most from the current money-based system are also most likely to obstruct alternatives
money_quotes: Personally I care about making a positive contribution to the world, but most employers only offer money.
Some people value money past the point of security, which is why I separated out those two.
I'm including musicians and artists, people who do stuff in spite of having no reason to think it will ever pay.
Would we all just play video games 12 hours a day and eat too much? Would we feel useless and dried up and depressed?
If you are the type of person who works out of passion, this new world order wouldn't slow you down a bit.
My guess is that the only people that would be deeply disappointed are the people who now really *really* like money for its own sake.
They are the very people who might create obstructions to the Star Trek economy's existence, because they are both powerful and rich in this setup, and potentially have the most to lose.
posts.key_insight: Most people's work motivations are actually independent of money, which means a post-scarcity economy would only truly threaten those who value wealth for its own sake—and those same people have the power to prevent such a future.

posts.summary: This advice column addresses three substantive questions: whether a parent should spend savings on travel versus hoarding for children's college, how to cope with dating exhaustion in the age of Tinder, and a clarification about a crepe recipe. The dating advice is the centerpiece, offering a sociological critique of how dating apps have commoditized romance and made the 'dating apocalypse' visible. O'Neil argues that many men have learned to repress their feelings to 'win' at dating, treating it like any other competitive system. She advises the lovelorn questioner to seek out idea-focused social spaces and approach new connections as potential friendships rather than romantic prospects.
key_points: Dating apps like Tinder haven't created the commodification of love—they've just made it more visible and universal
Many men excel at learning arbitrary rules of power systems and exploiting them ruthlessly, whether in academics, finance, or dating
The solution to dating exhaustion is to invest in friendships rather than romantic pursuits, as 'friend bucks' may eventually become convertible currency
Parents shouldn't make themselves miserable for the sake of college savings, especially when creative solutions like subletting exist
Seeking out idea-focused spaces like meetups and Nerd Nites is more likely to yield genuine connections than dating apps
money_quotes: We've entirely commoditized sex, love, and even affection, and especially in places like New York where there are so many beautiful and single women, the single man feels like an idiot for settling with one.
I think (many) men are better at learning the rules of a system and exploiting them viciously to their benefit.
I've always been amazed how quickly the men around me adapt to the petty and arbitrary rules of power and status, whether in academics, finance, or engineering startups.
It is by no means obvious that we should make ourselves miserable for the sake of college costs.
You might have only $10 to spend on love, but you might have thousands of friend bucks in the bank.
Tinder is making every place feel like New York.
posts.key_insight: The modern dating market operates like any other competitive system where some players learn to repress their humanity to 'win,' making genuine connection increasingly difficult to find through conventional channels.

posts.summary: The post examines comparative advantage theory—the economic argument that countries (or spouses) should specialize in what they're relatively better at—and systematically dismantles its real-world applications. O'Neil explains the mathematical validity of the concept while cataloging its failures: job displacement, diminishing returns, technological inequality, and most critically, its use as justification for exploitation. She then applies this critique to household labor division in marriage, arguing that 'scientific' allocation of chores based on comparative advantage inevitably disadvantages women who have been socialized to be more efficient at domestic work. The post concludes that marriages should optimize for minimizing resentment rather than maximizing economic efficiency.
key_points: Comparative advantage is mathematically valid but fails in practice due to job losses, diminishing returns, technological disparities, and monoculture risks
The theory is weaponized to justify exploitation—telling Bangladesh to accept poverty wages and women to accept doing 75% of housework as 'scientifically optimal'
Women's apparent 'advantage' at housework is a product of training and expectation, not innate ability, and grows larger with practice
Economic theories applied to marriage ignore that we don't actually measure time spent on tasks, and the measurement itself poisons relationships
Marriage should optimize for minimizing long-term resentment, not productivity—sometimes doing tasks you hate helps you understand your partner's experience
money_quotes: What is most troublesome about the modern worshipping of comparative advantage is that we end up using it as an excuse to exploit people.
Given existing conditions, the US should make computers, not work very hard, and be rich, Bangladesh should stitch T-shirts for Old Navy at 30 cents an hour, work really hard, and be poor.
In the sexist soup straight couples all swim in, 'don't keep score, everybody do what you're best at' seems to invariably end up at the equilibrium 'woman does 75% of the shitwork.'
What the comparative advantage crowd says is you are not even allowed to be mad about this, women, it is ratified by science.

One of the problems with applying an economic theory to a marriage is that we don't actually keep track of how much time it takes us to do various things, and even if we did do that we'd probably do it wrong.
My advice to married couples is to ignore scientific arguments, and instead think about a system that will minimize longterm resentment, which is poison in any marriage or relationship.

The essential ingredients in any marriage is a sense of teamwork, the dedication to alleviating the other person's suffering, and a promise to encouraging one another's fulfillment. And economics doesn't have much to say about those things.
posts.key_insight: Economic theories like comparative advantage, however mathematically elegant, become tools of oppression when applied to human relationships—whether between nations or spouses—because they rationalize existing power imbalances as optimal outcomes.

posts.summary: This post curates recent articles exposing the problematic aspects of big data and algorithmic systems. The linked pieces cover algorithmic fairness (noting the paradox that preventing racist algorithms requires explicitly testing for race), Google's potential to influence elections through search rankings, the need for social scientists to collaborate with technologists, and how data mining systematically disadvantages minority groups. The collection also includes a critique of MOOCs, reframing their high dropout rates as features serving employers rather than bugs harming students. Together, these links paint a picture of technological systems that embed and amplify social biases while often escaping accountability.
key_points: To ensure an algorithm isn't discriminatory, you must paradoxically take protected characteristics like race into account when testing it
Search ranking has outsized power over political trust, with higher-ranked candidates gaining credibility regardless of merit
Social scientists need to work alongside computer scientists on human behavior data, prioritizing research questions over data availability
Data mining techniques systematically disadvantage minority groups in ways that often go unexamined
MOOC dropout rates may serve employers as applicant filters rather than representing educational failures
money_quotes: Fairness is not automatic in algorithms, and the somewhat surprising fact that, in order to make sure an algorithm isn't racist, for example, you must actually take race into consideration when testing it.
When a given candidate was artificially lifted in terms of rank, people started to trust them more.
Google's meaningless response: 'Providing relevant answers has been the cornerstone of Google's approach to search from the very beginning.'
High failure rates and dropouts are features, not bugs, because they represent a way to thin pools of applicants for potential employers.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic systems don't automatically produce fair outcomes—they require deliberate scrutiny and often embed the very biases they claim to transcend.

posts.summary: The post argues that tipping systems create power dynamics where consumers hold power over service workers who depend on tips. O'Neil extends this analysis to the gig economy, where rating systems have replaced tipping but maintain the same power imbalance while making the transaction invisible. She worries that the 'seamless' nature of apps like Uber obscures these dynamics, making it harder to teach children gratitude and empathy. The piece questions whether 'innovation' and 'disruption' are euphemisms for making rich people's lives easier while weakening workers' job security.
key_points: Power is the inverse of dependency - you have power over those who depend on you
Tipping gives consumers temporary 'little lord' status and power over service workers
Rating systems in the gig economy replicate tipping's power dynamics while hiding the transaction
The invisibility of gig economy payments makes it harder to recognize and balance power relationships
'Innovation' and 'disruption' may be code for benefiting the wealthy while weakening worker protections
money_quotes: Power is the opposite of dependency.

Some people exploit this role, demanding too much, asking for special favors, and enjoying having someone do our bidding. Others are over-sensitive to slights; if they feel like their status is being questioned at any level, they switch from little lords to little tyrants.

The worst thing that could happen to a poorly rated rider is that he gets kicked off the app and has to use another rider app, but the worst thing that can happen to a driver with a bad rating is he could lose his job.
We have gone from a transactional relationship, in a restaurant, to a inequitable faux-friendship.
The first step in extending gratitude to people who are extending us a service is to know when that transaction is occurring.
I want them to know that service is not servitude, and that everyone is a person just trying to do their best.
Maybe we're watering down the appearance of power relationships because we have redefined the word 'innovation' as 'ways to make rich peoples lives easier.'
We call something a 'disruption' if a bunch of people's job security is weakened and they need to rely on rating systems in order to scrape by.
posts.key_insight: The gig economy's invisible transactions and rating systems maintain the same power imbalances as tipping while obscuring them behind a veneer of friendly 'sharing,' making exploitation harder to recognize and resist.

posts.summary: The National Labor Relations Board issued a ruling that redefines 'employee' to include franchise and contractor workers, allowing them to negotiate directly with parent corporations like McDonald's rather than just local franchise owners. Previously, McDonald's maintained legal separation from its workers despite controlling nearly every aspect of their jobs through surveillance, policies, and tight franchise margins. This framework prevented large-scale unionization since workers could only bargain with individual franchise owners operating on thin margins. The new 'joint employer' designation means parent corporations now share liability for worker treatment and cannot simply shut down franchises that unionize.
key_points: 90% of McDonald's low-level employees technically work for franchise owners, not the corporation, despite the corporation controlling their work conditions at a minute level
The previous legal framework prevented effective unionization by forcing workers to negotiate only with margin-constrained local franchise owners
The 'joint employer' ruling extends beyond franchises to contractors, making it harder for corporations to create legal distance from workers they effectively control
Parent corporations can no longer shut down a franchise simply for unionizing under the new ruling
money_quotes: 90% of the low-level employees at McDonalds don't technically work for McDonalds at all; instead, they work for a local franchise owner, who in turn rents stuff, including the McDonalds brand, from the parent corporation.

In spite of the technical and legal framework, the parent corporation controlled the burger flippers at a minute level, through surveillance, customer service policy, branding requirements, and most importantly through controlling the margins of the franchise owner.

The legal separation, which was solidly working until yesterday, meant that employees couldn't complain about bad treatment of their employer, McDonalds, but rather had to complain only about the way the local franchise owner treated them.
It will likely mean that it will in general be much harder for corporations to create legal distance between itself and the people hired to do work for that company.
posts.key_insight: Corporations that control workers through surveillance, policy, and economic pressure cannot escape liability simply by inserting a legal intermediary between themselves and those workers.

posts.summary: This Aunt Pythia advice column addresses four reader questions spanning identity and isolation in academia, asexual dating, finding meaning as a pure mathematician, and financially preparing to support aging immigrant parents. The column's through-line is navigating difficult life circumstances while staying true to oneself. Cathy O'Neil draws on her own experience as a self-described 'bulldozer' to counsel a WOC grad student feeling isolated, validates an asexual reader's desire for companionship while offering practical resources, gives nuanced permission to a mathematician questioning academia, and provides pragmatic guidance on eldercare planning. The advice consistently emphasizes authenticity over accommodation and practical problem-solving over rumination.
key_points: Being outspoken and calling out injustice may cause social isolation, but the people who stick around can be trusted—authenticity acts as a filter for genuine relationships
Rare relationship preferences (like asexuality) require targeted searching in specialized communities rather than hoping to find matches in general social circles
Leaving academia isn't a cure for existential dissatisfaction—some people are 'identity crisis prone' and will question their choices regardless of context
Financial planning for aging immigrant parents requires concrete research into eligibility for benefits and learning from others in similar situations
The advice rejects the premise that happiness should be the ultimate goal, linking to a previous post titled 'I don't actually want you to be happy'
money_quotes: In my darkest moments I succumb – temporarily – to the idea that I need to stop calling out intolerant and/or obnoxious behavior, that I should just sit there silently not mentioning injustice.
Truth is, people can't handle you because they're not secure enough. They don't 'know what to do with you' so they avoid you.
One great aspect of being completely over-the-top real is that other people self-select for us. The ones who stick around can be trusted.
I refuse to accept the notion that minority grad students have to be at the bottom of the food chain. I get paid too little and love my field too much to not take pride in my work.
Some people (like my husband) are made for academia and would suffer outside it, and other people (like myself) have left academia but it's not like that suddenly made them happy.
Teaching is the shit work of academia. Some of your students – most, actually – are disinterested, but some are not. They are awesome.
posts.key_insight: Authenticity and directness may cost you superficial social acceptance, but they're ultimately more valuable because they filter for genuine relationships and self-respect.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines why some people, including assault survivors themselves, frame sexual violence as inevitable rather than blaming perpetrators. She argues this isn't simply victim-blaming but reflects fundamentally different worldviews about male nature. She identifies three distinct frameworks: one where men can control urges and women should be free, another where some men are inherently predatory (requiring women to avoid them), and a third where no man can be trusted and women must be protected through measures like early marriage. O'Neil suggests these aren't right-versus-wrong positions but rather reflect different lived realities and cultural expectations.
key_points: The debate over sexual assault responsibility reflects deeper disagreements about whether male violence is controllable or inevitable
Survivors who blame themselves may be operating from a worldview where some men are simply uncontrollable predators
In contexts of war, poverty, and insecurity, assuming all men are threats becomes a survival strategy that leads to practices like child marriage
These different frameworks aren't about being right or wrong but about having different expectations of what men are like
The 'logical' argument against victim-blaming fails to persuade when people's lived experience tells them male violence is inevitable
money_quotes: You can't paint yourself into a corner and then say whose brush is this?
Just as it would be silly to ask a lion not to kill an antelope when it's hungry, and similarly it would be ridiculous to think that (some) men wouldn't rape a woman if they had the chance.
It doesn't make her wrong and us right, it just means we have different expectations of what men are like.
Marriage is seen as a cover of respect and protection for women.
If someone faces this kind of reality, they have to work within it, even if it means marrying off their 13-year-old daughter before she's ready or willing. It's a bad choice versus a worse choice.
We hear arguments like the above from reasonable, thoughtful people, who have plenty to lose by being wrong.
posts.key_insight: Debates about sexual assault responsibility are really proxy arguments about whether male violence is a controllable choice or an inevitable force of nature that women must navigate around.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques the emerging litigation finance industry, where hedge funds and financiers bet on lawsuit outcomes. She uses the example of predatory medical lending for transvaginal mesh injury cases, where financiers exploit desperate women by offering surgery financing in exchange for settlement proceeds, then inflate costs and take most of the payout. O'Neil argues this industry will make legal proceedings more expensive (like college tuition with easy loans) and further corrupt an already unfair justice system. The real solution is reducing money's influence on justice, not increasing it.
key_points: Litigation finance involves wealthy investors betting on court case outcomes, creating a growing industry that hedge funds are entering
A predatory scheme targets women needing mesh injury surgery: financiers offer loans, collude with surgeons to inflate costs, then take most of the legal settlement
More money flowing into lawsuits will create an arms race, making legal proceedings more expensive overall (similar to college tuition inflation from easy loans)
Hedge funds exploit system biases as arbitrage opportunities rather than improving justice
The justice system needs reforms that reduce money's influence, not financial products that amplify it
money_quotes: It's horrible, but it's really just one example of a large industry of what is known as litigation finance, which just means the world where people with lots of money decide to bet on outcomes of court cases.
We've already seen what happened with college tuition when something that's already too expensive gets access to loans: it gets even more expensive. It's an arms race.
The justice system is already super unfair, and it's not like hedge funds are running into this game to improve the system. Rather, they are there to exploit the system. That's what hedge funds do.
So if they have detected a bias in the system, they are going to treat it like an arbitrage situation and throw money at it for all they're worth.
The side effects of that will have nothing to do with justice.
The real reforms we need to see with the justice system is a way for money to be less of a factor, not more.
posts.key_insight: When hedge funds detect inefficiencies in the justice system, they treat them as arbitrage opportunities to exploit rather than problems to fix, making an already unfair system worse.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the achievement gap between rich and poor students is fundamentally an economic problem, not a teacher quality problem, yet politicians persistently blame and punish teachers rather than address inequality. She surveys failed reform efforts—Michelle Rhee's teacher firings in D.C., charter school conversions in New Orleans, and Florida's bizarre SAT-score bonuses for teachers—showing none have closed the gap. The post critiques these approaches as correlation-causation errors: we see poor kids near teachers before tests and blame teachers, when poverty is the actual cause. O'Neil concludes that only equalizing school funding and addressing societal inequality will make real progress.
key_points: The achievement gap is primarily an economic problem requiring economic solutions like higher taxes, better minimum wages, and stronger safety nets
Blaming teachers is a convenient political scapegoat because addressing inequality is politically difficult
D.C.'s teacher firing approach under Michelle Rhee failed—the achievement gap is now wider than before her tenure
Florida's new SAT-score bonus system is absurd because it provides no motivation for teachers to change behavior—they either qualify based on decades-old test scores or they don't
Equalizing school funding and addressing societal inequality are the only approaches likely to work, but neither is politically easy
money_quotes: Politicians everywhere have decided to simply assign blame to the school teachers who the last people to be seen with poor kids before they take the standardized tests.
It's a correlation versus causation problem, actually.

Originally we focused on more tests, thinking that shining a light on the problem would automatically solve that. That didn't work, so we turned our focus to teachers, again mostly as an easily available knob to turn.
It's the equivalent of giving tall men extra money to buy suits.
I'm waiting for the meta-analysis that shows the achievement gap doesn't respond to firing bad teachers, or charter schools, or even more testing, or any other easy target.
As long as a deep inequality of opportunities exists, and mobility is low, we can expect a lack of investment in poor people.
posts.key_insight: The achievement gap is a poverty problem being misdiagnosed as a teacher problem because addressing inequality is politically harder than firing teachers.

posts.summary: O'Neil announces that high school students from Occupy Summer School have self-organized into an after-school activism group called 'Project Occupy' at their school. The students formed an organizing committee and plan weekly topic-based meetings covering issues like cultural appropriation. O'Neil expresses pride in the students' initiative and reflects on what adults learned from them about effective, generous protest tactics. The students aspire to eventually host events and create media content around issues they're passionate about.
key_points: High school students independently organized a continuing activism program after a summer educational initiative
The group will hold weekly topic-based meetings with readings and videos as material
Students taught adult organizers about keeping protests 'fun and generous' with symbolic gifts
Future plans include events, podcasts, and YouTube content around social issues
money_quotes: They are, as always, very sharp and observant. Teenagers are the best.
I told them how much they've taught us about keeping protests fun and generous, and giving away gifts of food or balloons (actually, both) to make their point well-received.
Gotta keep things fun.
posts.key_insight: Youth activism succeeds when young people are given educational foundations and then trusted to self-organize around issues that matter to them.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines Facebook's patent to assess creditworthiness based on friends' credit scores, using it to illustrate how big data can perpetuate inequality. She explains the legal concept of disparate impact—when algorithms unfairly disadvantage certain groups—and notes its uncertain application to algorithmic decision-making. Her central argument is that neoliberal ideology, which frames all decisions as individual rational profit-seeking, makes it nearly impossible for people to understand fairness arguments that require considering group dynamics. She proposes distinguishing between individual profit goals and broader societal goals of a fair financial system.
key_points: Facebook's patent to judge creditworthiness by friends' credit scores could penalize people for circumstances beyond their control, like attending a poorer high school
Disparate impact doctrine—holding processes accountable for unfair outcomes regardless of intent—has legal precedent but remains unclear for algorithms
Neoliberal ideology frames decisions as individual profit-maximization, making group-level fairness concerns difficult to even conceptualize
The big data industry often dismisses fairness concerns because distinguishing rich from poor is precisely how they operate
Understanding algorithmic harm requires looking through two lenses: the company's profit goals and society's need for a fair financial system
money_quotes: Just because you're paranoid doesn't mean nobody's out to get you, right?
Most of us are friends with a bunch of people from high school, and if I happened to go to a high school filled with poor kids, then loans for me would be ruled out by this method.
Since this ideology has us acting as individuals, and ignoring group dynamics, the disparate impact argument is difficult if not impossible to understand.
Why would anyone not distinguish between a poor person and a rich person before making a loan? That's the absolute heart of how the big data movement operates.
Fairness isn't even definable unless you give context, and context is exactly what this mindset ignores.
Since every interaction boils down to game theory and strategies for winning, 'fairness' doesn't come into the equation.
posts.key_insight: Neoliberal ideology's focus on individual rational actors makes it structurally impossible to see how algorithms can systematically harm groups, even when the harm is unintentional.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reflects on the difficulty of finding meaningful data science work outside academia that aligns with her values. She expresses frustration with the job market being dominated by finance and advertising roles, which she refuses to apply to. After experiencing the discouraging process of applying to competitive positions she only partially wants, she proposes a new approach: proactively reaching out to organizations doing valuable work to offer consulting services. The post is a candid admission of career struggle and a call for advice from her readers.
key_points: The data science job market is heavily skewed toward finance and online advertising, leaving few options for those with ethical concerns about these industries
Even the more interesting job postings attract over 100 applicants, making the search demoralizing
She proposes flipping the job search: instead of responding to postings, identify mission-driven organizations and offer expertise directly
Working for large consultancies like IBM would likely route her back to finance clients anyway
money_quotes: What can a non-academic mathematician do that makes the world a better place?
So far my attempts at sorting through the LinkedIn 'data science' jobs are leading to a huge list of finance and online advertising jobs (which I don't apply to).
I've applied to a bunch of jobs I only kind of want and haven't heard back from almost any of them. It's kind of depressing. Actually it's super depressing.
Why don't I start with organizations that I think are doing cool things and offer them my data science expertise?
Obviously one way would be to try to get a job at IBM, but then I'd be back to working for clients in finance.
posts.key_insight: For data scientists with ethical commitments, the conventional job market offers mostly work that conflicts with their values, requiring a more proactive approach to find meaningful employment.

posts.summary: O'Neil follows up on a previous post about her struggle to find meaningful data science work, sharing the advice her readers offered. She notes that most data science jobs either make rich people richer (finance) or poor people poorer (ad tech). The post compiles suggestions ranging from creating your own job to working with nonprofits to joining Data Science for Social Good. She expresses gratitude while noting that many other data scientists share her frustration about the lack of obvious ways to use their skills for good.
key_points: Most advertised data science jobs serve finance (enriching the wealthy) or ad tech (exploiting the poor)
The job market fails to offer profitable opportunities for socially beneficial work
Strategies for finding meaningful work include creating positions that don't exist and approaching companies proactively
Many data scientists share the frustration of wanting to do good but lacking obvious paths to do so
Nonprofits need data help but come with their own challenges
money_quotes: Most advertised data science jobs either make rich people richer (finance) or make poor people poorer (ad tech)
I consider it my go-to example of how 'the market' fails, if you think the market is supposed to offer profitable opportunities to do stuff that is worthwhile and/or important for society
It's kind of the opposite
Quite a few other data scientists wrote saying they, too, want to make the world a better place and are frustrated by the lack of obvious chances to do this
Obviously I'm not alone
posts.key_insight: The data science job market is structured to reward work that increases inequality rather than addressing social problems, leaving ethically-minded practitioners with few obvious paths forward.

posts.summary: O'Neil highlights two blog posts by young women in STEM: one by MIT student Michelle G. about intelligence, gender, and stereotype threat, and another by Harvard student Meena Boppana about her experiences as a female math competition winner. O'Neil uses these posts to amplify women's voices in male-dominated fields while adding her own commentary, including a pointed jab at Larry Summers and an acknowledgment that despite her skepticism of math contests, she appreciates what Boppana gained from them. The post also promotes an upcoming panel on gender inclusivity in mathematics at Harvard.
key_points: Young women in STEM are producing valuable writing about their experiences with gender and intelligence
Stereotype threat remains a relevant issue that even experts can learn more about
Women's experiences in competitive math spaces deserve attention and amplification
O'Neil maintains nuanced views—appreciating individual experiences while critiquing systemic structures like math contests
money_quotes: And who doesn't love nerdy young women!?
I thought I knew all about that stuff but I learned quite a bit from her post.
p.s. Larry Summers, I hope you read this.
Even though I personally think math contests kind of suck, I appreciate how much Meena got out of them.
posts.key_insight: Amplifying young women's voices in STEM—and engaging critically with their experiences—is itself an act of pushing back against gender bias in these fields.

posts.summary: This is a personal nostalgia piece where O'Neil reflects on her high school years in the late 1980s, describing the social dynamics of cliques and the central role that music played in teenage identity. She identifies herself as a 'misfit' who listened to classic rock and describes the brief alliance between misfits and druggies at her school's smoking section. The post culminates in her discovery of Neutral Milk Hotel's album as the retrospective soundtrack she wishes she'd had during those formative years.
key_points: Mixed tapes were a profound form of social currency and identity expression among 1980s teenagers
High school social hierarchies created distinct musical identities for each clique
The 'misfits' were ironically their own cohesive group, united by not fitting in elsewhere
Music can retroactively capture emotional truths about periods of our lives, even if it didn't exist then
money_quotes: A sure sign of long-lasting and meaningful friendship would be if one teenager made a mixed tape and gave it to another teenager.
That would be a deep sign, both of kinship and, of course, of musical identity.
Personally, I was a misfit. That doesn't mean that I didn't belong in any clique, it means, ironically, that I fit in with 'the misfits.'
I wore an army jacket and was deeply misunderstood, and sometimes in the summer I'd tie dye shirts and refuse to participate in things.
Hey, it's a fantasy, and they don't always make sense.
posts.key_insight: The music that best captures a period of our lives sometimes didn't exist yet, because emotional truth transcends chronology.

posts.summary: This advice column covers several topics: a critique of three dating/romance books that oversimplify complex social dynamics, advice on high-frequency trading controversies (dismissing Flash Boys as relatively unimportant compared to larger financial crimes), encouragement to apply for multiple academic positions at the same institution, commentary on American hero worship and inequality, and guidance for mentoring a young man with unrealistic life plans. Throughout, Aunt Pythia offers pragmatic, sometimes cynical takes on modern social and economic problems.
key_points: Dating and romance books tend to oversimplify complex social dynamics like gender economics and sexual identity
High-frequency trading scandals, while real, are minor compared to systemic financial crimes like the 2008 crisis - 'business as usual' in finance
Harvard Business School alumni recognize inequality as a major problem but propose solutions (tax reform, deregulation) that would actually worsen it
Hero worship and 'winner take all' mentality has eliminated support structures and forced workers to do multiple jobs
Individual human stories drive social change more than statistics - we need stories that represent systemic issues rather than aspirational hero worship
money_quotes: That's what finance people do.

Lewis is a great writer, and he tells a great story, but this time I think he just kind of randomly chose the good guys and bad guys and convinced everyone something terrible was going on because it sells books, when in fact it's business as usual in the world of high frequency trading.
It's obviously terrible to only rely on your sex appeal, especially as you age and are suddenly unfuckable.
If you are the only person stopping to smell the roses, you will get trampled from behind. It's a collective action problem, and a cultural problem.
The way humans work is we care about individual stories.
What we need to do is make sure the individual stories we hear about are examples of larger issues important to a lot of people, rather than just aspirational hero-worshipping schlock.
posts.key_insight: Systemic problems in finance and society persist because we focus on dramatic individual stories rather than the mundane, larger-scale issues that actually harm people.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that minimum wage debates miss the fundamental question of what constitutes a living wage. She criticizes opponents who claim to protect workers by keeping wages low, comparing this logic to giving sick people insufficient medicine to avoid running out of pills. The current New York minimum wage of $8.75/hour translates to just $17.5K annually—impossible to live on. Rather than clinging to inadequate jobs to keep unemployment statistics low, she advocates determining what a living wage actually is and setting minimums accordingly, even if that number varies by location.
key_points: A simple formula: hourly wage × 2 = annual salary in thousands (so $8.75/hour = $17.5K/year)
Opponents of raising minimum wage claim to help the poor while ignoring whether current wages are survivable
Keeping wages low to preserve jobs is like rationing medicine—everyone stays sick
The $15 figure may be right for some places but wrong for others; living wages should vary by location
If our economic model assumes jobs should support workers, we must ensure they actually do
money_quotes: I once figured out that if you take someone's hourly wage in dollars, and you multiply by 2, then you get their yearly wages in thousands of dollars.
It's weird because it sounds like Person B is arguing for the sake of the poor, but they're ignoring the vital question of what is a living wage.
You have a sick population, and they all need 3 pills per day to stay well. The pills are expensive, though, and so the people in charge of pill distribution give most people 2 pills per day.
For the sake of those theoretical people, then, they give out only 2, and everyone remains sick.
For the sake of holding on to crappy jobs that pay below living wages, and where the employees need food stamps to survive, we don't raise the bar so they can actually sustain someone in a basic way.
It's almost like we're desperate to hold on to them because otherwise our unemployment rate would be higher.
For as long as we live in a country where the model is that a job is supposed to support you, we should make sure it actually does.
posts.key_insight: Arguments against raising minimum wage claim to protect workers while ignoring whether current wages are actually survivable—a logical contradiction that prioritizes job counts over human welfare.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares data science job listings from organizations she finds ethically appealing but isn't pursuing herself. The two positions—at Harmony Institute (studying media's social impact) and Paperless Post—represent the kind of data work she wants to amplify: mission-driven or ethically neutral companies with good cultures. She explicitly excludes finance and ad tech from future listings, signaling her priorities about where data scientists should direct their talents. The post functions as both job board and implicit critique of the broader data science job market.
key_points: O'Neil uses her platform to amplify data science jobs at organizations she considers ethical alternatives to mainstream tech
Harmony Institute focuses on understanding media's social impact through data—studying information diffusion, framing, and emotional responses
Paperless Post emphasizes they don't sell user data and have a product people actually pay for, distinguishing themselves from ad-driven models
The explicit exclusion of finance and ad tech positions reveals O'Neil's stance on which industries deserve data science talent
Both positions emphasize collaboration, autonomy, and mission over pure technical optimization
money_quotes: I've been getting lots of people writing to me recently with data science jobs that I think look like nice jobs, with nice people, but I am personally not going to apply for.
You know how to hack code and wrangle data, but you're just as concerned with big questions as big data.
We have a product that people enjoy and pay for, and we don't sell our user data.
If you have a data science position that is not in finance, or ad tech, and is with super nice people, I plan to do it at least one more time.
posts.key_insight: Where data scientists choose to work is itself an ethical decision, and there are meaningful alternatives to finance and ad tech for those who look.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts her two-day jury duty experience, focusing on a comedic incident involving an unattended phone's repeated Amber Alerts. A particularly loud and hostile woman spent an hour complaining about the annoying noise, demanding someone turn it off, while becoming increasingly agitated. The twist: after nearly an hour of complaints, someone finally discovered the offending phone belonged to the loud woman herself. The post is a character study in irony and human behavior under the tedium of civic duty.
key_points: Jury duty creates a peculiar social pressure cooker where boredom amplifies small annoyances into major incidents
The clerk Bill exemplifies patient professionalism in the face of difficult people, described as 'a saint'
The loud woman's hostility throughout both days foreshadowed her role as the unwitting source of her own complaint
Group dynamics in forced waiting situations create contagious reactions - both frustration and laughter
The story serves as a perfect illustration of projection and lack of self-awareness
money_quotes: It's like you're on a two day plane ride to nowhere.
Being prepared to do it was surely enough.
The guy next to me kept mumbling, 'I gotta get outta here' under his breath, while shaking his leg furiously.
That guy is a saint.
This time the loud woman added some sarcastic comments about how SOME people seemed to think her suffering was FUNNY, which made me simultaneously laugh harder and consider suicide.
Readers, it *was* her phone.
posts.key_insight: The person most loudly complaining about a problem is sometimes the one causing it, a lesson in self-awareness delivered through perfect comedic timing.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) responds to reader questions about personal dilemmas. The questions cover maintaining a difficult friendship with someone who has mental health issues, whether to confess romantic feelings to a long-time platonic friend, finding meaningful internships, and whether to feel guilty about automating jobs. O'Neil's advice emphasizes directness, action over stagnation, and systemic thinking about societal challenges like automation.
key_points: When dealing with difficult friends, express frustration while you still have goodwill left rather than waiting until you're completely burnt out
Reading about personality disorders can help you understand that you're not responsible for curing someone else's issues
On romantic feelings: stop waiting and take action, because prolonged stagnation serves no one
On automation and job loss: 'protect the people, not the jobs' - focus on retraining and education rather than resisting technological progress
For finding internships, seek out underfunded organizations doing good work rather than well-organized places with formal programs
money_quotes: Too often, people only express frustration at the point of no return, so the underlying message is, 'you cannot convince me to be your friend anymore, it's too late.'

When you encounter someone with a personality disorder, you feel bewildered and confused – and sometimes even partially responsible to help – but then you realize that you are not alone in your confusion, and that you are not capable of curing them.
A wise man (Suresh Naidu) once said to me, 'protect the people, not the jobs.' I think he's right.
Instead of pushing to avoid automation, a futile gesture to save unnecessary and outdated jobs, we should be thinking about pushing for free college and training for the jobs of the future.
I need you to move the fuck on. Holy crap, lady, you gotta get your love life moving here.
The ones that advertise for internships are way too overstaffed and organized.
posts.key_insight: Whether dealing with difficult relationships, career decisions, or societal shifts like automation, the best approach is honest action rather than passive stagnation or guilt.

posts.summary: This guest post by a pre-settlement funding company CEO defends his industry while acknowledging its problems. Pre-settlement funding provides cash advances to plaintiffs in personal injury cases who can't wait years for settlements, with repayment contingent on winning. While the author argues this service helps level the playing field against insurance companies, he admits lack of regulation has allowed bad actors to charge predatory rates (35-40% per six months) without proper risk assessment. He proposes rate caps as a solution that would both protect consumers and force better underwriting, thereby limiting funding to meritorious cases only.
key_points: Pre-settlement funding is non-recourse: plaintiffs repay nothing if they lose, making it risky for funders but protective for borrowers
Insurance companies deliberately delay settlements to induce desperation and extract lowball offers from financially vulnerable plaintiffs
Most funding companies charge 35-40% per six-month period regardless of case risk, with additional broker and application fees
High rates allow companies to skip proper underwriting, which attorneys prefer because it's faster despite being worse for their clients
Rate caps would force better underwriting and limit funding to genuinely meritorious cases
money_quotes: Funding does more than ensure fair and complete compensation for the injured—it ensures that our justice system is blind to an individual's economic standing.
No matter how obvious the negligence or big the damages, insurance companies always delay compensation. It's the oldest and most effective trick in the book: gain leverage by inducing desperation.
Simply because a lack of regulation led to a swarm of bad actors doesn't mean pre-settlement funding should go away.
Believe it or not, attorneys whose clients bug them enough for cash will take a quicker funding process over lower rates every time.
They only realize the mistake when it comes time to repay the funder.
Rate caps will do more than stop unreasonable rates. They will also make it impossible for frivolous lawsuits to get funding and hurt our economy.
posts.key_insight: Pre-settlement funding could promote justice system equity, but without rate caps, the industry's profit incentives create predatory lending disguised as plaintiff empowerment.

posts.summary: O'Neil recaps her participation in a Harvard event launching Gender Inclusivity in Mathematics (GIIS), noting media coverage in The Crimson and Le Monde. She mentions giving 'tough love advice' alongside Moon at the event, held in the same room where she took her qualifying exams. The post also highlights a student organizer's blog reflection on identity and mathematics, and discusses her Le Monde feature alongside mathematician Leila Schneps about destructive algorithms in education and politics.
key_points: Harvard launched Gender Inclusivity in Mathematics (GIIS) with O'Neil as a featured speaker
The event sparked student reflection on the relationship between identity and mathematics
O'Neil discussed harmful algorithms including Value-Added Models for teachers and political micro-targeting in Le Monde
Collaboration with Leila Schneps highlights ongoing work on bad math in court systems
money_quotes: This took place in the same room in which I wrote my quals, which might explain why I'm looking confused and horrified.
I am quoted in the article talking about destructive algorithms like the Value-Added Model for teachers and even political micro-targeting.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic systems in education and politics deserve the same critical scrutiny as those in courtrooms.

posts.summary: The VW emissions cheating scandal has sparked important conversations about algorithmic accountability and inspection. Tufekci argues for auditable logs that regulators can inspect, noting that casinos already do this with slot machines. Columbia Law professor Eben Moglen warned in 2010 that proprietary software is 'unsafe building material' because it can't be inspected. The irony is that automakers resisted code inspection claiming individuals would cheat on emissions tests—exactly what VW itself did. Algorithms are marketed as objective and fair, but this case proves we must inspect them anyway.
key_points: The VW scandal reveals that algorithms marketed as 'objective' can be deliberately designed to deceive
Auditable logging systems already exist in regulated industries like casinos, proving algorithmic inspection is feasible
Automakers' argument against code transparency—that individuals would cheat—proved ironic when manufacturers themselves cheated
Automation creates a fundamental trade-off: we gain efficiency and scale but lose control and understanding of what's actually happening
money_quotes: Proprietary software is an unsafe building material because you can't inspect it.
Algorithms are marketed as 'objective' and 'fair.'
There's a trade-off when we take advantage of automation and algorithms: we get efficiency and scale, on the one hand, and on the other we lose control.
We don't really know what's happening and when.
The very least we could do is ask them.
posts.key_insight: When we delegate decisions to algorithms without inspection mechanisms, we trade away control and accountability for efficiency—and we have no way of knowing when that trust is being exploited.

posts.summary: O'Neil explores the decision-making process behind Volkswagen's emissions cheating scandal, questioning how the company decided to install software that detected emissions tests and altered performance. She outlines how VW was caught when independent researchers found NOx emissions up to 35 times higher than allowed during real-world driving. The post speculates about whether this was institutional short-term thinking, overconfidence from past unpunished cheating, or a small group of engineers seeking bonuses—drawing parallels to similar deceptions in education and finance.
key_points: VW's diesel cars passed emissions tests through software that detected testing conditions and cheated accordingly
The fraud was discovered because results were 'too good to be true'—independent researchers wanted to copy VW's success
The decision to cheat reflects patterns seen in other industries: teachers changing test scores, finance professionals cooking books for bonuses
The scandal raises questions about whether this was isolated or industry-wide practice
money_quotes: Specifically, I want to know what happened back in 2009 when they started cheating. What was that conversation like?
Basically they were discovered because their results were 'too good to be true.'
It's kind of like when teachers and principals change the results on students' tests so they can get a bonus: short term thinking, and kind of obvious if you track erasure marks.
The evidence they'd need for that theory is that they cheated all the time like this and had never gotten caught, or they knew others did.
This kind of thing happens all the time in finance, where you cook the books in a short term way to get a pay-out.
posts.key_insight: Corporate fraud often follows predictable patterns across industries—from education to finance to automotive—driven by short-term incentives and the belief that cheating won't be detected.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil confesses to having an enormous yarn collection that had been scattered throughout her house in bags. With help from a professional organizer friend, she finally consolidated and organized her stash. Rather than feeling embarrassed by the sheer volume—which she estimates rivals an average yarn shop—she embraces the beauty of her collection and the joy her knitting hobby has brought her over the years.
key_points: Her yarn collection had grown so large it was hidden in bags throughout every corner of her home
She tends to buy excess yarn for projects and keeps the leftovers, plus purchases sale yarn she hasn't yet used
The organization process led to an appreciation of her collection's beauty rather than shame
She jokes the stash could function as a backup yarn shop if she ever needs money
money_quotes: I have a deeply alarming amount of yarn, which has hitherto been gathered in smallish bags tucked all over the house, in every nook, cranny, and corner.
I have officially tamed the yarn beast.
I always buy a bit too much yarn and then keep the extra.

I am beyond shame and embarrassment at this point, because at some point, when all my yarn was splayed across my entire living room and dining room, I realized how amazingly beautiful it all is and how much I've gotten out of my hobby over the years.
I think I might actually have more yarn than the average yarn shop.
Life is too short to be ashamed of your passion.
posts.key_insight: Embracing your passions fully—even when they seem excessive to others—can bring unexpected joy and appreciation.

posts.summary: John Santerre, a PhD student and former photojournalist, shares lessons from a decade of working with nonprofits and social good organizations as a data scientist. He argues that the most important skill is educating organizations about your capabilities and limitations, while accepting that your most sophisticated technical skills may go unused. The work requires flexibility, solo resilience, and redefining success metrics—measuring impact by organizational change rather than technical achievement. He concludes that meaningful work doesn't require nonprofit status, and that being effective means embracing a generalist advocacy role rather than pushing specialist expertise.
key_points: Working at nonprofits means accepting a 'Faustian bargain'—your finely tuned skills will often fall on deaf ears, and your most useful contribution may be far simpler than your capability
You will likely work alone without peers to brainstorm or advocate with you, making it harder to build credibility and share the burden of navigating new environments
Success metrics differ radically: impact should be measured by how you change how an organization approaches problems, not by technical sophistication or prediction accuracy
Social good work exists across organizational types—a for-profit preventing drug addiction relapse may be more impactful than a nonprofit tracking bird migrations
Being effective requires shifting from specialist to generalist mindset, serving as an advocate rather than pushing the boundaries of your craft
