money_quotes: The most rewarding and challenging aspect of working at a nonprofit is the responsibility you bear to educate the organization about the limitations and potential you present.
Just as the violinist Joshua Bell was ignored in the DC Metro but adored in the philharmonic, so too will many of your finely tuned skills fall on comparatively (computationally?) deaf ears.
In my experience, you have/get to 'check' your craft at the door.
The inculcation required to become a specialist in our craft can blind us to the impact our client requires.
I have to judge my contribution not by the number of trailing digits of prediction accuracy, but by the impact I have on how biologist's approach the problem.
Social good doesn't require a 501c3 status.
I find I am overwhelmed with options, but only if I am flexible in what role I expect to provide.
posts.key_insight: Effective data science for social good requires checking your specialist ego at the door and measuring success by organizational impact rather than technical sophistication.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil recounts giving a lightning talk on algorithmic ethics at Strata+Hadoop World, a corporate big data conference. She presented five ideas about how data scientists inadvertently cause harm by blaming victims through flawed correlations in HR, criminal justice, and education algorithms. Despite the importance of the topic, only 40 people attended her session. She contrasts the sparse attendance at ethics talks with the bustling expo floor full of companies selling data science hype, concluding that the conference is mostly a frivolous celebration of big data rather than a venue for serious ethical reflection.
key_points: Data scientists aren't trained in ethics, making it difficult to 'not do bad' before trying to 'do good' with data

Algorithms conflate correlation with causation, leading to victim-blaming: HR systems that penalize women, recidivism tools that further punish poor Black people, and teacher evaluations that blame educators for systemic failures
Data scientists have become de facto policy makers without the accountability or training that role requires
The corporate big data conference prioritized selling 'magical thinking' over ethical considerations, with ethics sessions sparsely attended
money_quotes: In order to do good with data, first you have to not do bad.
We haven't actually figured out the difference between correlation and causation.
Think about the HR algorithm that decides never to hire another woman engineer because it notices how badly women engineers fare in the workplace.
We use recidivism algorithms to figure out that poor black people are more likely to be arrested, and we decide to punish them even more as a result.
Data scientists are de facto policy makers. We shouldn't be.
Few people there were spending too much time fretting about ethics.
Dozens of data science platforms extending the hype of the power of big data were set to sell you magical thinking.
Almost completely frivolous but fun for the participants, as long as you don't get caffeine poisoning.
posts.key_insight: Data scientists wield policy-making power through their algorithms but lack both the ethical training and accountability that such influence demands.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the CFPB's struggle to enforce disparate impact laws against auto lenders who charge higher rates to minority borrowers. The core problem is that auto lenders don't collect race data, forcing regulators to use proxy methods like zip codes and names to estimate borrower demographics. This creates uncertainty about who was actually discriminated against and who deserves restitution. She argues this uncertainty benefits lenders who push for minimal settlements, especially when the same expert consultants work for both regulators and banks. O'Neil sees this as the beginning of a new field: building auditing models to detect discrimination and other algorithmic harms.
key_points: Auto lenders don't collect race data unlike mortgage lenders, forcing regulators to use imprecise proxies like names and zip codes to identify discrimination victims
Disparate impact models are inherently squishy—two analysts building models on the same data will reach different conclusions based on methodological choices
The same statistical expert consulting for both banks and regulators creates conflicts that help banks minimize settlements
When enforcement requires statistical probability rather than certainty, lenders gain leverage to avoid accountability
The solution requires developing standardized auditing methods for algorithmic and process discrimination
money_quotes: The thing about these modern cases of measuring disparate impact is that it's a model, and models are extremely squishy things.
Two people asked to build a disparate impact model on the same data will likely come up with different answers, because all sorts of decisions have to be made on the way.
You end up firmly in a land of statistics, where each borrower is assigned a probability of being minority and a probability of having been screwed.
The lenders, who are paying for this all, tend to lean on the side of not giving any money away at all unless we're sure.
The excuse for that questionable arrangement is that there aren't enough statisticians who can do this work (my hand is raised!).
Building models that call bullshit on other models.
posts.key_insight: When discrimination must be proven through statistical models rather than direct evidence, the inherent uncertainty in those models becomes a tool for perpetrators to minimize accountability.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where Cathy O'Neil, writing as 'Aunt Pythia,' answers reader questions on topics ranging from family obligations to stock market hours to gender dynamics in the workplace. The most substantive exchanges tackle imposter syndrome (which O'Neil reframes as external mistreatment rather than internal insecurity), reciprocity in intimate relationships, and the privacy risks of DNA testing. O'Neil brings her characteristic directness to each question, often challenging conventional framing and encouraging readers to see systemic issues behind personal problems. The column blends humor with genuine insight, particularly when addressing how successful women face backlash rather than mere self-doubt.
key_points: What's called 'imposter syndrome' in successful women is often better understood as external backlash and mistreatment from peers threatened by their success
The 'Lean In' advice is flawed because it ignores the hostile environment women face at higher levels of success
DNA testing companies pose privacy risks especially for financially insecure or uninsured people, and ancestry/health results are often scientifically unreliable
Stock markets aren't 24/7 because liquidity requires coordinated participation, and humans still oversee trading algorithms
Communication and reciprocity are basic requirements of healthy relationships
money_quotes: They say to 'Lean In,' but I say, to what? To these douchebags? I'd rather not.
When it's called 'Imposter Syndrome,' it's often a way for people to dismiss us as inwardly insecure and, therefore, incompetent.
It's used as an excuse to explain the mysterious forces which keep us from succeeding further, in fact.
My advice to you is, call it 'being an Uber Cunt that nobody can handle' and refer to it – breezily and often – as a superpower.
People don't trade 24 hours a day because people aren't already trading 24 hours a day.
The algorithms could run all day and night, but at the end of the day people watch over those algorithms (really!) and they want to sleep.
I don't think I'd be too worried about my DNA being used, but that's likely because I'm not financially insecure, I'm a US citizen, and I have health insurance.
This is not yet science.
posts.key_insight: What gets labeled as women's internal 'imposter syndrome' is often external punishment for daring to succeed beyond what male peers find comfortable.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces she's teaching a day-long data science tutorial in Stockholm, primarily for statisticians at Statistics Sweden. She frames the workshop around the key question of what distinguishes data science from traditional statistics, planning to demonstrate through hands-on iPython notebooks using 'found data' and algorithmic techniques. She notes the irony that Swedish government statisticians measuring inflation have cleaner, more reliable work than data scientists scraping Twitter for brand sentiment. She also emphasizes wanting to show how results can depend heavily on seemingly minor choices like hyperparameters.
key_points: The tutorial aims to answer the question 'what's the difference between statistics and data science?' through practical examples rather than abstract definitions
Data science works with messy 'found data' requiring scraping, cleaning, and algorithmizing, unlike the structured data of official statistics
Swedish government statisticians measuring inflation are arguably better off than data scientists trying to divine brand sentiment from Twitter
Seemingly benign choices of hyperparameters can significantly change analytical results, a point she wants to emphasize to statisticians
money_quotes: So that's my plan for the day, to create a few iPython notebooks with examples of the kind of data and algorithmic techniques that you'd typically find in nature.

I think once these statisticians see those examples they will be comfortable knowing how much better off they are in Sweden measuring the inflation rate (currently at -0.2%) than we are trying to understand whether people like specific brand names by scouring Twitter.
I definitely want to spend at least some time showing the audience how much the answer can depend on seemingly benign choices of hyperparameters and so on.
After all, data scientists are not all stats majors.
posts.key_insight: Data science differs from traditional statistics primarily in working with messy, found data rather than carefully collected measurements, and practitioners should be aware that analytical choices can dramatically affect results.

posts.summary: The post examines the statistical challenges the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau faces when measuring disparate impact in auto lending discrimination cases. O'Neil argues that even if race data were directly available, numerous methodological choices around pooling data by location, time, and dealer create uncertainty. Banks exploit this ambiguity by arguing for whichever statistical model minimizes their liability. She advocates for standardized measurement approaches not because they'd be perfect, but because they would prevent gaming between competing models and ensure consistent treatment across cases.
key_points: Proving individual racism is nearly impossible; disparate impact is inherently a statistical question comparing pools of minority vs. white applicants
Pooling data increases statistical power but masks variation across locations, time periods, and individual dealers
Separating data to account for these factors reduces sample sizes and increases error bars, creating a fundamental tradeoff
Banks game the system by always arguing for whichever model minimizes their liability
Standardization of measurement methods would reduce gaming even if the chosen standard isn't perfect
money_quotes: It has to be said that it's difficult if not impossible to prove an individual case of racism.
The good thing about pooling is that you have more data and thus smaller error bars; it's easier to make the case that disparate impact has taken place beyond a reasonable statistical doubt.
Because there are various models that could be used, there's always one model that ends up costing the bank the least. They will always argue for that one.
They even have an expert consultant who works both for the CFPB and the banks and helps them game the models in this way.
I'd suggest we have some standards for measuring disparate impact, so that the 'gaming between models' comes to an end.
Not because it's perfect, but because it will prevent banks from complaining that other banks get treated better.
posts.key_insight: The absence of standardized statistical methods for measuring discrimination allows banks to game enforcement by always advocating for whichever model minimizes their liability.

posts.summary: O'Neil reviews Bethany McLean's 'Shaky Ground,' which traces the bizarre history of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac from the Great Depression through the 2008 crisis to present day. She highlights how these mortgage giants were placed in conservatorship rather than nationalized primarily for accounting optics, and how their recent profits have been entirely siphoned to the Treasury despite still having shareholders. The post catalogs the massive government subsidies for homeownership while noting that homeownership rates are actually declining, especially for minorities. O'Neil concludes that the entire system is irrational, politically entangled, and that strengthening renter protections makes more sense than continuing to prop up homeownership mythology.
key_points: Fannie and Freddie were kept in conservatorship rather than nationalized primarily because nationalization would have added their debts to the national deficit
Hedge funds who bought shares cheaply are suing because all profits now go to Treasury, leaving shareholders with nothing
The 'American dream' justification for these entities is undermined by the fact that most mid-2000s loans were for refinancing and second homes, not first-time homebuyers
Government support through Fannie/Freddie plus mortgage tax deductions dramatically inflates housing prices while homeownership rates, especially for minorities, continue to decline
The current system leaves no financial cushion for another crisis, yet there's no political will to change anything
money_quotes: Speaking as a non-homeowner, I think this is crap.
People still 'hate' Fannie and Freddie but nobody seems to have a plan to transition to a system whereby the government isn't the final backstop for mortgages.

The combination of the cheap backstop represented by Fannie and Freddie, in addition to the mortgage tax deduction represents a FUCKTON of government support for homeownership, and also raises the prices of houses by an extreme amount.
It's fascinating stuff, and also completely baffling as well, partly because it's so deeply entwined with politics, so there's really not a lot of rational logic attached to it.
After reading this book, I can only conclude that it's time to strengthen renter laws.
Most of the loans in the mid-2000's were for refinancing and for second homes, so it wasn't like that was actually happening.
posts.key_insight: The U.S. mortgage system funnels enormous government resources into inflating housing prices under the guise of promoting homeownership, while actual ownership rates decline and renters get nothing.

posts.summary: The post argues that housing has been mythologized as a wealth-building investment when the actual returns barely keep pace with inflation. The perceived gains from homeownership are largely illusions created by leverage, forced savings through mortgage payments, and mental accounting errors. While families recall buying homes for small down payments that grew into large values, they overlook the total mortgage payments made and the decades-long time horizon. The strategy is actually risky because leverage amplifies losses as much as gains, as millions of foreclosure victims discovered. The author concludes that home prices have been artificially propped up by tax subsidies and cultural mythology.
key_points: Housing price gains typically only keep pace with inflation, not generate real wealth
The appearance of large returns comes from leverage - putting down a fraction of the price but capturing all gains
People mentally ignore the total mortgage payments when calculating their 'investment return'
Mortgages force regular saving, which is the real source of accumulated wealth, not the house itself
Leverage is dangerous: a 20% price drop can wipe out all family savings
money_quotes: Housing is not a good investment, and it never was.
While things turned out well, the house was not that great an investment.
Actually, the gain on houses typically just barely holds its own against inflation.
If you only put up a fraction of the money but get all of the gain, you can multiply the returns earned. Wall Street loves this game and calls it leverage.
If prices drop, you lose bigtime – as millions of families losing their houses to foreclosure these days are learning to their sorrow.
So the saving they had to do to pay the mortgage, more than the house, was what resulted in their having money for the kid's college and retirement.
Buying a house on leverage is a risky investment.
posts.key_insight: The wealth families built through homeownership came from forced savings and leverage, not from housing being a good investment - and that leverage makes it far riskier than the cultural mythology suggests.

posts.summary: This advice column tackles several reader questions, with the main focus on a female math PhD student upset after being asked on a date publicly at an academic conference. Aunt Pythia counsels her to develop thicker skin, give the man benefit of the doubt, and not conflate awkwardness with injustice. Other questions address mathematical hagiographies (which she rails against as dead white male worship), the Ashley Madison hack (she's unconcerned about moral culpability), and a study showing women may be favored in tenure-track hiring (she accepts the data and urges calm analysis). Throughout, she advocates for pragmatism over idealism and refuses to treat minor slights as major offenses.
key_points: Being asked on a date awkwardly at a conference is uncomfortable but not an 'injustice' - women in math need thick skin for real battles ahead
Mathematical hero-worship and genius narratives (always about men) are harmful and off-putting to aspiring mathematicians who don't fit the mold
We cannot fully separate our mathematical selves from our personal/romantic selves - collaboration is inherently intimate
One study showing women favored at one hiring stage doesn't erase systemic sexism elsewhere in the pipeline
On Ashley Madison: marriages 'ruined' by the hack were probably already troubled - nobody is forced to ruin their marriage over data
money_quotes: The thing about men (and women) is that nobody knows what they're doing, but mostly they're not trying to be bad people.
Having a crush on you is his problem, not yours.
You're going to need a thicker skin to be a woman warrior in mathematics. Sad but true. Save the word 'injustice' for when it's really needed.
When is NPR going to realize that mathematicians are just people who like puzzles?

Maybe I became like this from all the fucking mathematical hagiographies of dead white men that were so unlike me that I simply turned it off inside me in order to be able to imagine myself as a successful mathematician.
Fuck that idolatry. I would never give my kids that crap to read.
I figure many of the marriages that are going to be 'ruined' because of Ashley Madison were kind of sucky anyway.
posts.key_insight: Not every uncomfortable experience is an injustice, and conflating awkward social situations with systemic discrimination dilutes the fight against real barriers women face in mathematics.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews Hugh Sinclair's book exposing how microfinance has devolved from a poverty-reduction tool into predatory lending that exploits the poor. The original promise—that poor people are budding entrepreneurs needing only capital—proved false, with 90% of loans being consumption loans rather than business investments. Interest rates in weakly regulated countries reach 140-200%, making it impossible for borrowers to benefit. Even in controlled experiments with no loan sharking, economists found no measurable benefit from microfinance. The fundamental conflict between profitability and helping the poor means greed nearly always wins, while the feel-good narrative persists.
key_points: 90% of microfinance loans are consumption loans (TVs, services), not investment loans for entrepreneurship
Interest rates reach 140-200% in countries with weak usury laws, functioning as extortion rather than aid
Microfinance funds failed to do due diligence because exposing problems would spook investors and collapse the system
Even the rare success stories (like the Mongolian head processor) simply displaced competitors rather than growing the economy
Controlled economic experiments in ideal conditions found no benefit from microfinance
money_quotes: The promise of microfinance was this: that poor people are budding entrepreneurs, who simply don't have access to the capital required to make their dreams come true. Turns out that's pretty rare in practice.
There's an inherent conflict of interest between making profitable loans and helping the poor, and greed nearly always wins.
The feel-good message of helping the poor just seems too good to give up.
Let's face it, that kind of extortionist interest rate doesn't help anyone.
I've often said, cynically, that companies don't really want data consultants because those consultants get to see the most embarrassing stuff.

Most of the entrepreneurial efforts that the poor are likely to engage in are nothing more than microfinance arms races that do little to help the local economies but do one thing for sure, namely impose a tax on business that is taken out of the local community entirely and distributed back to the rich world.
My conclusion is that microfinance is a failure in almost all ways, and for almost all people.
posts.key_insight: Microfinance's fundamental flaw is the irreconcilable conflict between generating returns for investors and genuinely helping poor borrowers—a tension that consistently resolves in favor of profit at the expense of the vulnerable.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on advice she gave to a female math PhD student who was upset after being asked on a date at a conference by a male collaborator. Initially dismissive of the woman's distress due to her own 'pro-love hippy' perspective on romantic attention, O'Neil reconsidered after reader feedback. She introduces 'Assumption A'—that romantic interest expressed between colleagues makes collaboration impossible—and acknowledges this creates asymmetric harm in male-dominated fields where women lose potential collaborators disproportionately. While revising her stance to advise math men against asking out women in their field, she maintains her original practical advice for the woman to remain professional and warm.
key_points: The author initially didn't understand why being asked out at a conference would upset someone, due to her own comfort with romantic attention in professional settings
Reader feedback led her to adopt 'Assumption A': once romantic interest is expressed, mathematical collaboration becomes impossible for many people
In male-dominated fields, this dynamic disproportionately harms women by shrinking their pool of potential collaborators
She distinguishes between sexuality and sexism, warning against conflating the two when addressing structural problems in academia
Despite changing her view on whether men should ask out women in math, she stands by advising women to remain warm and professional rather than withdrawing
money_quotes: The thing about men (and women) is that nobody knows what they're doing, but mostly they're not trying to be bad people.
I don't actually think we can separate our mathematical selves from our self selves, and sexual/romantic parts of us emerge no matter how hard we try to restrict them.
It's like, imagine she has a list of 'possible collaborators' and he just went and crossed out his name from her list.

If the women are all hit on by men, then they all exclude themselves as collaborators. This isn't much of a problem for the men, who have plenty of other potential collaborators, but it is a huge problem for the women.
I do think we're in danger of conflating two issues, namely sexuality and sexism.
I personally love math guys and I think they're wonderful partners, and of course I'm married to one of them. But I really do get the logic.
It deserves to be examined and ignored if it is deemed not useful. And if there's anything that's not useful, it's a rule that limits options for women's math careers.
posts.key_insight: In male-dominated fields, romantic advances from colleagues create an asymmetric burden that disproportionately shrinks women's professional networks, even when no malice is intended.

posts.summary: This advice column covers several topics: celebrating aging rather than fearing it, navigating competitive workplace dynamics in finance, and most substantially, addressing a female physicist's struggles with credit attribution, professional respect, and whether to stay in academia. Aunt Pythia argues that the worship of youth is misguided and that aging should be embraced for the freedom it brings. On workplace dynamics, she notes that money and status create tribalistic competition that inhibits genuine connection. Her core advice to the struggling physicist is that power dynamics and credit-jockeying are universal in academia, affecting everyone regardless of gender, and the solution is strategic planning rather than self-blame or personality change.
key_points: Aging should be celebrated rather than feared - many current worries become irrelevant with age, and we should expand our definition of beauty to include older bodies
Workplace social dynamics are often driven by money and status hierarchies, making genuine friendship difficult; finance culture particularly rewards tribal in-group behavior
Academic credit and authorship disputes are universal power dynamics, not personal failings - everyone jockeys for position, and strategic planning before collaborations is essential
Women who are 'blunt and opinionated' are often told to tone it down, while men with the same traits face no such pressure - this is a form of gender-based taming that should be resisted
Before leaving a field or job, distinguish between disliking the politics (which exist everywhere) and disliking the actual work itself
money_quotes: Heck, more than half of my problems stem from concerns I simply won't have when I'm 75, and the other stuff will probably also seem dumb.
You might think this means letting go of vanity, but I'd argue it just means finding sagginess beautiful, which is much easier if you think about it.
As a rule of thumb, you can count on the people that make the most money to feel less like being friends, and more like ignoring the 'unimportant people.'
The currency is professional status, not cash money, and since professional status is slightly harder to measure, it makes people slightly less focused on it.

Whenever someone talks about how a woman is blunt and opinionated, I think about all the men who are even more completely blunt and opinionated and who never get flack for it, and I realize it works to their advantage, and that people are just trying to tame and sublimate us blunt opinionated women, and fuck that.
Think about things that involve power and make a plan. Not so that you take advantage of others, obviously, but so that you end up with what you think is fair.
I'm never going to tell a brilliant woman (or man) to stay in a miserable job on principle.
posts.key_insight: Professional struggles around credit and respect often feel personal but are usually systemic power dynamics - the solution is strategic planning rather than self-blame or personality change.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil examines the emerging Theranos scandal, where the $9 billion blood-testing company faces serious questions about whether its technology actually works. She proposes a crowdsourced verification approach where people compare Theranos results against standard lab tests, citing one person who found significant discrepancies between the two. The post highlights how Theranos's secretive behavior—refusing to let scientists validate their technology while assembling a board of military-industrial figures rather than scientists—suggests the company may be fraudulent. O'Neil concludes by sharing a PR response from Theranos that deflects rather than addresses the core concerns.
key_points: Theranos claims revolutionary blood testing technology but refuses to provide validation data for public scrutiny
A first-person comparison showed Theranos results differed from Stanford lab results by 7% for HCT and over 30% for platelets
The company's strategic choices—avoiding scientific validation, slow market entry through a single partner, using competitors' technology—suggest the core product doesn't work
The board composition (former Secretaries of State and Defense) rather than scientists raises questions about the company's actual priorities
Crowdsourced independent testing could expose whether Theranos technology is legitimate
money_quotes: Well, does the blood test technology work or not? It's frustratingly hard to know.
We just want to compare Theranos blood test results against them.

Armed with all of these advantages, you choose to avoid allowing scientists or regulators to validate your technology, enter the marketplace through a single partner (Walgreens) at a glacial pace, and conduct most of your business using existing technologies that are not your revolutionary product.
Are you choosing this strategy because your technology doesn't actually work, because you are incompetent, or because you hate capitalism?
Bonus question: if the technology doesn't work, why is your board a Who's Who of the military-industrial complex instead of a group of scientists who can help?
Or else we just ignore Theranos entirely, because it's gotta be a fraud given the way they're acting.
posts.key_insight: When a technology company refuses scientific validation while filling its board with political heavyweights instead of domain experts, its behavior reveals more about its legitimacy than any PR statement could.

posts.summary: A neuroscientist delivers a scathing critique of the EU's Human Brain Project, arguing that its goal of simulating a human brain is scientifically absurd. The author systematically dismantles the project's premises by pointing out that we cannot even simulate the 302-neuron brain of a roundworm despite having complete wiring diagrams and extensive data. The rant highlights fundamental gaps in neuroscience: we don't understand brain connectivity at fine scales, don't know what makes human brains special, can't agree on basic biophysical parameters, and don't even know what aspects of neural activity matter. The author compares the project's flashy simulations to cargo cult science—superficially similar to the real thing but lacking any understanding of underlying principles.
key_points: Despite complete connectome data for C. elegans (302 neurons), we still cannot simulate or understand how even this simple brain works, proving that data does not equal understanding
Brain connectivity is essentially unknown at fine scales—we have only millimeter-resolution maps, while the relevant circuitry operates at microscopic levels
Reported values for basic biophysical parameters vary wildly between studies, making accurate simulation impossible
Fundamental questions remain unanswered, like whether spike timing or spike rate carries information, even in the most-studied brain region (cat visual cortex)
The project's flashy visualizations are 'cargo cult science'—superficially similar to brains but lacking the principles that make them work
money_quotes: Simply put, data does not equal understanding.

We can't simulate the brain of C. Elegans, a very well studied roundworm in which every animal has exactly the same 302-neuron brain and we know the wiring diagram and we have tons of data on how the animal behaves.
One lengthy detailed study will find a parameter to be 35±4, and the next in-depth study will find the same parameter to be 12±3. So what should you use in your simulation?
The next time you see a pretty 3D picture of many neurons being simulated, think 'cargo cult brain'.
That simulation isn't gonna think any more than the cargo cult planes are gonna fly.
We can only create pretty things that are superficially similar in the ways that we currently understand, which an enlightened being would just laugh at.
In private the neuroscientists would say something more like 'hey, maybe it's crazy, but it'll bring a bunch of money.'
posts.key_insight: Massive data collection and impressive visualizations cannot substitute for genuine scientific understanding of underlying principles—a lesson the billion-euro Human Brain Project exemplifies.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that homeownership is no longer a sound investment despite its cultural status as the American Dream. She explains that government policies historically inflated housing prices while simultaneously excluding minorities from the market during its most profitable era. Now that homes are overpriced and risky, she warns against pushing minorities into homeownership as a belated wealth-creation strategy. The post distinguishes between signing a mortgage and actually owning a home, noting that with small down payments, 'homeowners' bear enormous risk for minimal equity. She concludes that we should explore alternative wealth-building strategies rather than perpetuating the homeownership myth.
key_points: Government policies (tax deductions, Fannie/Freddie, 30-year mortgages) artificially inflated housing prices while systematically excluding minorities during the best buying era
A 3% down payment doesn't constitute real ownership—it's a highly leveraged, risky financial instrument where the 'owner' bears all the downside
The only genuine benefit of a mortgage is forced savings, but this requires an improbable string of good fortune to actually build wealth
Wealthy minority neighborhoods still have less home value per dollar of income than wealthy white neighborhoods, showing discrimination persists
Rather than pushing minorities into a rigged and overpriced market, we should develop alternative wealth-building mechanisms
money_quotes: When someone says 'don't buy a house,' what black people might hear is that only white people will ever own homes in this country, and that is somehow the way things should be.
The easier it is to get financing, the higher prices get. Look at college tuition.
Homes were set up, by the government, to be a good investment about 50 or 60 years ago. That doesn't mean they are a good investment now.
People seem to think they own a home when they've signed a mortgage.

If you bought a pie and only had 3% of the money for it, you wouldn't really think it was your pie, because your slice is extremely small. Plus if a dog came and ate up the pie, you'd be responsible for rebuilding the pie.
A mortgage is basically a highly leveraged and risky financial instrument for the homeowner.
But that's a lot of ifs.
Of course this starts with them making enough money to have extra to save.
posts.key_insight: Pushing minorities into homeownership now, after government policies created and then closed the window for housing as wealth-creation, is not reparative justice—it's setting people up for financial risk in an overpriced market.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on the stark contrast between her own thoughtful comment section and the hostile, unmoderated discussions found elsewhere on the internet. Using an Atlantic article about gender imbalance on math conference panels as an example, she shows how quickly comments devolve into sexist attacks. She wrestles with possible solutions—turning off comments, algorithmic moderation, or manual moderation—while acknowledging each has tradeoffs. She concludes that her personal moderation approach works only because of her site's small scale, leaving the larger problem unsolved.
key_points: Unmoderated internet discussions typically devolve into hostile, low-level attacks that drive away thoughtful participants
The math panel gender disparity is worth understanding beyond simple blame, but comment sections make nuanced discussion impossible
Moderation solutions all have significant costs: expense, censorship concerns, or scalability limits
Freedom of speech objections to moderation often reflect weak understanding of what free speech actually means
Small-scale manual moderation can create good conversation, but doesn't scale to larger platforms
money_quotes: On a daily basis I think to myself, 'damn my commenters are smart, and thoughtful, and they make me think and rethink my positions.'
Only people willing to resort to very low level, hostile accusations are willing to wade into that muck.
To some that stinks of censorship, but on the other hand those people often have a weak understanding of freedom of speech.
What do we owe to the idea that everyone gets their say, and what do we owe to the idea that we want to have an actual meaningful conversation?
Once they're in, they're in. It doesn't always work – sometimes I have to delete further comments by someone, if they become disrespectful – but it mostly does.
posts.key_insight: The internet's promise of open discourse is undermined by the hostility of unmoderated spaces, forcing a tradeoff between inclusivity and meaningful conversation that no current solution adequately resolves.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) responds to reader questions on retirement planning, emotional crushes in academia, surviving early motherhood, and an inappropriate request. The column opens with a personal anecdote about her son's broken leg and gratitude for modern medicine. The substantive advice challenges unrealistic financial expectations, validates intense emotional experiences in grad school, and offers hard-won parenting wisdom about surviving the newborn phase.
key_points: The expectation that average Americans save $2-3 million for retirement is unrealistic given student loans, housing costs, and childcare expenses - retirement policy will have to change
Developing intellectual and emotional crushes on mentors is normal and even enviable - the intensity of feeling is a feature, not a bug, as long as you control your actions
The first 4-6 months of a newborn's life are genuinely brutal, and the key is tracking small daily improvements rather than expecting immediate competence
Ethical investing options exist (like fossil-fuel-free portfolios) but often carry more risk than conventional investments
money_quotes: Think about that as a social policy: hey everyone, I know you're still paying off your student loans, and that the cost of renting is sky high, and homes are already overpriced and poised not to rise, and daycare costs more than ever, but please save $2 million on top of everything else. WTF.
Not a viable expectation for the average household. Politically speaking, retirement in this country is going to have to change as the post-Boomer population gets old and continues to be broke.
I feel sorry for people who aren't like us, and for whom it takes weeks if not years to develop strong emotions for people and things. They don't get to experience the intensities that we do!
You can't control your feelings directly, but you can control what you do in response to them.
The first 4 months of a baby's life, and especially the first 6 weeks, is really really hard.
In the words of my wise mother, sometimes you just have to muddle through.
Good babysitting is worth it. Go into debt temporarily if necessary, it's still cheaper than therapy.
posts.key_insight: Society's expectations around money, emotions, and parenting are often unrealistic, and the healthiest response is to do your best, forgive yourself for being human, and recognize when the system itself is broken rather than blaming yourself.

posts.summary: After her son broke his leg in a soccer accident, O'Neil experienced the kindness of New York City cab drivers who showed extraordinary patience and care while helping transport her injured child. She contrasts the stereotype of an anonymous, pitiless city with the reality of these workers who went far beyond their job requirements. One driver in particular managed to move her son so smoothly he felt like he was levitating. The post is a heartfelt tribute to the everyday humanity of service workers.
key_points: Cab drivers showed remarkable patience despite the economic cost of waiting for a crying, injured child
One cabbie's strength and gentleness made the painful transfer feel almost effortless
These acts of kindness aren't exceptional but represent what O'Neil believes is normal behavior for cabbies
In a city that seems anonymous, service workers provide crucial human connection
money_quotes: He was 'infinitely strong and infinitely patient' and somehow managed to port my son backwards across the back seat in a perfect, smooth motion, that made him think he was levitating.
I don't even think I was particularly lucky; I think that's actually pretty normal for cabbies.
In a big, crowded city that might seem anonymous and pitiless, it's a super comforting feeling to know they are there.
They are some really great people, willing and able to help out strangers all the time.
It's not good money to be waiting around for a crying 7-year-old to calm down and move one more inch.
posts.key_insight: The everyday kindness of service workers like cab drivers provides an essential human safety net in cities that might otherwise feel cold and anonymous.

posts.summary: O'Neil explains why she and her husband have boycotted watching football after years of fandom. She argues that the accumulated evidence of CTE among players—with studies showing 96% of deceased players tested positive—makes it unconscionable to be entertained by a sport she wouldn't let her own children play. She draws a parallel to military service, noting that privileged families avoid both while others bear the costs. The piece criticizes the NFL's forced patriotism and militaristic rhetoric as a distraction from the real harm being done. She concludes that the violence, the money protecting the status quo, and the absurd intensity around wins and losses have finally made her walk away from the game.
key_points: CTE is nearly universal among football players, with a study finding 96% of deceased NFL players tested positive for the brain disease
There's a moral inconsistency in being entertained by a sport you wouldn't allow your own children to play
The NFL's forced patriotism and military rhetoric serves to distract from addressing the sport's violence problem
Unlike military service, which serves a societal need, nobody needs to play football—making it easier to simply boycott
The intense shame and gravity attached to college football losses is absurdly disproportionate and potentially harmful to young players
money_quotes: I have three sons, and I wouldn't let any of them play football. So what does it mean that I let myself be entertained by other people playing it?
A good approximation of a complete list would be 'all football players, ever.'
One of the most uneasy aspects of my enjoyment of football has always been the way the NFL and even college football coaches and media play up and play to the military aspects of the game.
It makes you think of the worst kinds of forced patriotism. Rudy Giuliani-ism, if you will. It's not earnest.
What the fuck is wrong with us? Why do we take these games so seriously?

The NFL is avoiding dealing with the problem, because there's so much frigging money on the table. Instead they shove yet more military might talk and fake patriotism down our throats, hoping we won't think too hard about it between rounds of beer.
We need to enjoy the victories and ignore the defeats.
posts.key_insight: Enjoying football while knowing you'd never let your own children play it reveals a moral contradiction that implicates viewers in the harm done to players' bodies and brains.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares the slide decks for a data science tutorial she's presenting in Stockholm, divided into three parts: Data (2.5 hours covering data acquisition, storage, and exploratory analysis), Models (2.5 hours on the modeling process including proxies, algorithms, and overfitting), and Product (1.5 hours on publishing and productionizing data science work). She notes that most of her material was gathered from the web, highlighting how the internet has become an essential learning and teaching tool.
key_points: The tutorial covers the full data science lifecycle from data acquisition through productionization
The modeling section addresses crucial concepts like defining success, choosing proxies, and avoiding overfitting
The product phase includes academic publishing, data journalism, and production code as valid outputs
The web has become an indispensable resource for both learning and explaining data science concepts
money_quotes: Almost everything in these slide decks is stolen from the web, and the more I worked on my presentation the more I realized how much of a tool the web itself has become for learning and explaining things.
posts.key_insight: A comprehensive data science education spans from raw data handling through modeling to productionization, and the open web has become the primary resource for assembling such curricula.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions about graduate school trauma, conference etiquette, career transitions from academia to industry, and dating challenges. She proposes starting a feminist women's magazine with practical advice. The responses blend humor with genuine insight, covering topics from mental health coping strategies to the limitations of online dating algorithms. O'Neil draws on her own experience transitioning from academia to quantitative finance to advise a PhD student about industry opportunities.
key_points: A meditation technique for processing past trauma: set aside dedicated time to think about painful events, then mentally visualize them shrinking in importance as your life expands around them
Math PhDs seeking industry careers face a paradox: companies once trained candidates from scratch, but now demand specialized skills even for entry-level data science positions
Online dating algorithms use 'very few, poorly chosen statistics' that disadvantage anyone who doesn't fit conventional desirability metrics
The proposal for a feminist magazine focuses on practical empowerment: birth control choices, finding reliable tradespeople, and rejecting patriarchal pressure around body modification
money_quotes: Give yourself some time each day to think about what happened. The important thing about setting aside time to think about it is that, the rest of the day, you don't have to.

That room is your existence, or your mind if you'd prefer it, and that box is pretty small compared to the size of the room. If that box consisted of stinky cheese, it would be stifling if the room were small, but since the room is enormous and growing larger all the time, it's barely noticeable.

Don't do what I did, which was just move to the only job I absolutely knew about existing, namely being a quant at D.E. Shaw, simply because I got recruitment emails about the job and knew people who had done it.
Personally I was turned down recently for a data science job because I didn't have experience with a specific algorithm, which I found bizarre.
That's the shitty thing about online dating. They ask for very few, poorly chosen statistics, and if you don't fit into what people think is desirable, you're totally fucked.
Bring condoms, don't be negligent.
posts.key_insight: Whether dealing with past trauma, career uncertainty, or dating frustration, the solution often involves acknowledging constraints honestly while actively expanding your perspective and options beyond the narrow metrics others use to judge you.

posts.summary: O'Neil defends the gender wage gap statistic against critics who dismiss it as 'misleading' because women 'choose' lower-paying jobs. She argues that career choices are heavily socialized, not independent, and that the simple median comparison actually captures these systemic factors elegantly. The ideal comparison would be between people who 'might have' pursued various careers absent barriers, but since that data is impossible to collect, comparing all women to all men is the best available proxy. She illustrates the statistic's validity by noting that arbitrary groupings (like birth hour) wouldn't show wage gaps, proving gender is a meaningful variable tied to systemic discrimination.
key_points: The 'women choose lower-paying jobs' argument ignores that career choices are heavily socialized, not freely made
Controlling for occupation actually removes the very discrimination we're trying to measure
The ideal comparison—people who might have pursued careers without barriers—is impossible to measure directly
Pooling all workers by gender is statistically correct because it captures systemic effects across all career paths
Arbitrary demographic splits (like birth hour) show no wage gap, proving gender discrimination is real and measurable
money_quotes: I think that, taken as a whole, including socialization and how our culture values work, and so on, the simplistic statistic represented by the gender wage gap is actually pretty sophisticated.

It's not enough to only consider 'women who became mathematicians versus men who became mathematicians' when comparing ultimate wages. That would ignore the implicit socialization element that keeps women away from higher-paying jobs.
I'm pretty sure not as many men had their 6th grade teacher explicitly tell them not to study math because they 'wouldn't need it later in life' like I did.
Some jobs are shunned by men for mostly social reasons, and they just happen to also be relatively underpaid as well.
It's not misleading at all, in other words, when you take into account weird social rules we have around who should do what job and how much that job should be valued.

Imagine that we instead had separated the population into two different groups: the humans that were born during an even hour of the day versus the humans that were born during an odd hour of the day. We'd not expect to see a huge wage gap then, would we?
posts.key_insight: The gender wage gap's simplicity is its strength: by not controlling for occupation, it correctly captures the systemic discrimination that shapes which occupations women enter in the first place.

posts.summary: This brief post shares two links the author finds important while traveling in Stockholm. The first is a Huffington Post essay from her Occupy Wall Street group arguing that free market ideology harms public health. The second promotes a Slate Money podcast interview with Mehrsa Baradaran about her book on banking inequality, which advocates for postal banking to serve underbanked populations.
key_points: Free market ideology is framed as a public health issue by the Occupy alternative banking group
The US should revive postal banking to serve those excluded from traditional banking
Financial inequality affects 'the other half' who lack access to fair banking services
money_quotes: Free Markets Ideology is Making Us Sick
Bottomline, we should start a postal bank. Or restart one, rather.
posts.key_insight: Both free market ideology and lack of accessible banking are systemic problems that disproportionately harm ordinary people.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares an amusing email from a Columbia University Public Affairs Officer seeking a math professor to help a Fox News reporter calculate a simple percentage. The reporter needed help explaining what percentage $325,000 is of $60 billion for a story about fraud in the NY State budget. The post frames this as an example of innumeracy in journalism, presented without extensive commentary but with clear implied criticism. O'Neil bookends the anecdote with a brief update about her data science tutorial in Stockholm.
key_points: A Fox News reporter contacted Columbia University seeking help with basic percentage calculation
The calculation in question ($325,000 as a percentage of $60 billion) is elementary arithmetic
The request highlights innumeracy among journalists covering financial and political stories
The fraud amount represents an infinitesimally small portion of the budget (about 0.0005%)
money_quotes: A reporter with Fox News just called looking for some help calculating percentages for a story he's preparing for tonight's broadcast.
Do we have any mathematics professors who could help with this calculation in the next hour or so?
posts.key_insight: When journalists lack basic mathematical literacy, they risk misrepresenting the significance of numbers to the public—and apparently need university professors to divide two numbers.

posts.summary: Social impact bonds are financial instruments designed to incentivize private investment in solving social problems like recidivism and homelessness, with payouts tied to achieving measurable outcomes. O'Neil argues that these instruments are fundamentally flawed because they invite gaming and manipulation of success metrics. She highlights a damning example where Goldman Sachs rigged results in a Utah preschool program by using an English vocabulary test on non-English speakers, then claiming success when kids learned English. The post warns that private financial interests are incompatible with genuine social problem-solving, comparing the approach to charter schools that juice numbers while failing to scale. The worst-case scenario involves financiers betting against positive outcomes while influencing results directly.
key_points: Social impact bonds transfer social problem-solving to private financiers who profit from achieving measured outcomes, but this creates perverse incentives to game metrics rather than solve problems
Goldman Sachs manipulated a Utah education bond by using an inappropriate English vocabulary test on non-English speakers, guaranteeing 'success' when kids simply learned the language
The approach mirrors charter school problems: unaccountable, number-juicing, and solutions that don't scale
Financial instruments are 'flexible little doodads' that can be shorted or manipulated, meaning investors might actually bet against social outcomes
Basic research and genuine social progress require separation from direct profit motives
money_quotes: That's the thing about financial instruments, they are flexible little doodads.

We're asking for help from private finance companies to solve big hard societal problems, and we're putting huge money on the line. There's a problem with this approach. We asking for gaming such as the above. We should expect to see more of it.
Financial incentives are not compatible with the scientific approach, and basic research depends on money not being directly involved.
When private financiers want to get involved in this stuff, it's because they can profit off of it, not because they want to help.
Worst case scenario: financiers are betting against the 'socially beneficial' outcomes. It's possible, we saw it happen in the housing crisis.
From 2006 to 2009, 30 to 40 percent of the children in the preschool program scored below 70 on the P.P.V.T., even though typically just 3 percent of 4-year-olds score this low.
posts.key_insight: Social impact bonds don't align private profit with public good—they align private profit with whatever metrics can be most easily gamed.

posts.summary: Federal investigators found former Duke cancer researcher Anil Potti guilty of research misconduct after years of investigation, but the real scandal is that Duke administrators ignored warnings two years earlier. A medical student named Bradford Perez sent detailed, technically convincing evidence of data fabrication to Duke deans in 2008, but they pressured him to stay quiet. The investigation only began in 2010 when Potti was caught lying about being a Rhodes Scholar. Meanwhile, cancer patients received wrong treatments based on fabricated research, with only two of eight who sued still surviving.
key_points: Anil Potti received minimal punishment—just five years of supervised research—despite fabricating cancer treatment data that harmed patients
A medical student provided Duke deans with detailed technical evidence of fraud in 2008, two years before any investigation began
Duke administrators pressured the whistleblower to stay silent rather than investigating his claims
The investigation was only triggered by Potti's resume fraud (fake Rhodes Scholar claim), not the scientific misconduct
There appears to be no accountability mechanism for university administrators who ignore credible misconduct reports
money_quotes: Of the eight patients who sued, Shoffner said, she is one of two survivors.
Instead of taking up the matter seriously, the deans pressured Perez to keep quiet. And nothing more happened for two more years.
The deans at Duke suck.
Why are they not under investigation? What would deans have to do – or not do – to get in trouble? Is there any kind of accountability here?
His punishment is that he won't do research without government-sponsored supervision for the next five years. Not exactly stiff.
posts.key_insight: Institutional failures to act on credible whistleblower reports can be just as harmful as the original misconduct, yet administrators face no accountability for enabling fraud that harms patients.

posts.summary: A proposed House bill (HR1737) would strip the CFPB of its ability to enforce anti-discrimination rules against auto lenders who charge minorities higher dealer markups. The CFPB has been holding banks accountable for discriminatory lending practices using disparate impact theory, but Republicans argue the methodology for detecting racism is flawed. O'Neil argues this is a dangerous position—that imperfect measurement shouldn't excuse ignoring discrimination entirely. She notes that the real goal should be eliminating racist practices, not just compensating victims, and points to BB&T's switch to flat fees as a model solution. The post frames this as an early test case for algorithmic auditing of institutional discrimination.
key_points: Dealer markups—discretionary fees added after credit risk assessment—are consistently higher for Black and Hispanic borrowers, constituting illegal discrimination under disparate impact theory
Ally Financial doesn't deny their loans were racist; they only dispute the degree of racism and the measurement methodology
The Republican argument that imperfect racism measurement means no enforcement is exactly what disparate impact law was designed to prevent
The statistical objection about false positives (whites miscategorized as minorities) would actually dilute measured discrimination, not inflate it
BB&T's switch to flat fees shows the real solution: eliminate discretionary practices that enable discrimination
money_quotes: Ally Financial doesn't claim their loans weren't racist. They simply claim that they were less racist than the CFPB thinks.
The Republican argument is: if you can't specify exactly how racist this practice is, then you can't stop people from doing it at all.

In statistical terms, we are worried about false positives – white people who might receive a compensation check for racist practices – and the proposed solution is to ignore all the actual victims of racist practices – so all the true positives. It's throwing the baby out with the bathwater.
The actual goal is to put an end to the racist policies themselves.
Pushback against those policies is a vote to keep shady, discriminatory negotiations with car dealers that, generally speaking, screw minorities.

This is just the beginning of a much larger debate around what kind of processes we can audit with algorithms, what constitutes evidence of discrimination, and how quickly – or slowly – the laws are going to catch up with technology.
It's a litmus test, and it's on the verge of failing badly.
posts.key_insight: Demanding perfect measurement of discrimination before allowing any enforcement is a strategy for permitting discrimination to continue unchecked.

posts.summary: O'Neil previews her attendance at a Columbia Tow Center conference examining the relationship between Silicon Valley and journalism. She highlights panels on ethics, censorship, and digital rights featuring prominent tech critics like Zeynep Tufekci and Kate Crawford. The post also mentions her own quoted appearance in a piece about predictive policing, connecting her ongoing work on algorithmic accountability to the conference themes.
key_points: The conference examines how Silicon Valley platforms like Facebook and Google affect journalism, particularly around censorship and digital rights
Notable tech critics and journalists (Tufekci, Crawford, Angwin, Bell) are featured speakers
O'Neil connects the conference to her own work being cited in critical journalism about predictive policing
money_quotes: I'm particularly excited for the 10:45am panel which features Zeynep Tufecki and Kate Crawford, among others, discussing ethics arising from the relationship between Silicon Valley platforms like Facebook and Google and journalism, especially when it comes to censorship and digital rights.
posts.key_insight: The growing entanglement between Silicon Valley platforms and journalism raises urgent questions about censorship, digital rights, and the ethics of algorithmic gatekeeping.

posts.summary: This advice column covers several topics: dating etiquette and the art of flirting, being a woman in mathematics, and career advice for finance-adjacent fields. The dating advice emphasizes that 'making a move' without prior flirting is inherently creepy—flirting serves as a consent-obtaining mechanism that establishes mutual interest before any romantic escalation. For the woman in math struggling to articulate why male-dominated environments feel hostile, Aunt Pythia validates her feelings while advising her to seek out supportive mentors and communities. The career advice acknowledges finance's insider culture problem but pragmatically suggests getting industry experience before moving to regulation.
key_points: Flirting is a required social technology for establishing consent—skipping it makes romantic advances inherently creepy regardless of context
The 'flirt ladder' metaphor: both parties should climb together, matching each other's signals, never racing ahead
Women in math face real cultural hostility that's hard to articulate; the solution is finding better people rather than accepting bad culture
Math culture's inability to welcome women is math's loss, not individual women's failure
Finance deliberately makes itself seem impenetrable to outsiders, which perpetuates insider corruption
money_quotes: Think of flirting as a means of obtaining consent for a move.
Creeps are people who aren't paying attention to what the woman actually wants and are just barreling ahead based on what they want.
Maybe we're both just feeling bad for math's culture itself, that it can't seem to get itself together to be a welcoming place for all these wonderful women.
Make your worst case scenario that you never humiliate anyone or call into question their basic dignity, and you'll be rising the level of discourse by a mile and a half.
One of the things that finance successfully does as a field is to make itself seem impenetrable for people not on the inside.
The other effect that's making things worse is that only money-grubbing jerks ever do go in.
posts.key_insight: Social norms that seem restrictive—like expectations around flirting before dating, or criticism of hostile academic cultures—exist because they require people to pay attention to what others actually want rather than barreling ahead based on their own desires.

posts.summary: O'Neil highlights a Nature article about cognitive biases in scientific research and techniques to counteract them. She notes the concept of 'blind data analysis' as intriguing but questions its applicability to exploratory data science where stories emerge from data exploration. She emphasizes open source research as crucial for enabling adversarial review approaches like 'devil's advocacy' in practice. The post calls for meta-analysis of debiasing techniques themselves to measure their effectiveness.
key_points: Scientists systematically fool themselves through cognitive biases, but structured techniques can help counteract this
Blind data analysis is difficult to apply in exploratory data science where narrative emerges from the data
Open sourcing research is essential for enabling adversarial review approaches in practice
Meta-analysis of debiasing techniques would help identify which methods work best in different contexts
money_quotes: It's not clear how it would exactly work in a typical data science situation, where you perform exploratory data analysis to see what the data looks like, and you form a 'story' based on that.

One thing they mentioned in the article but not in the graphic is the importance of having your research open sourced, which I think is the way to let the 'devil's advocacy' and 'team of rivals' approaches actually happen in practice.
I'd love for someone to somehow measure the ability of the above debiasing techniques to see which work well, and under what circumstances.
posts.key_insight: Open sourcing research may be the most practical way to implement adversarial debiasing techniques in science, though we still need meta-analysis to measure which debiasing methods actually work.

posts.summary: O'Neil announces her participation in a Berkeley-NYU conference on responsible use of open data in government and private sectors. She expresses interest in hearing from NYC's Chief Analytics Officer about the de Blasio administration's approach to data and predictive modeling. The post previews the conference panels, with O'Neil noting she'll discuss how private companies use data to the detriment of the public. She signals skepticism about the emerging narrative that companies will voluntarily donate data for public benefit.
key_points: The conference addresses both intended and unintended consequences of data sharing, including privacy and discrimination concerns
O'Neil plans to discuss ways private companies use data against public interests and what should change
She expresses skepticism about the concept of private companies 'donating' their data for the public good
The tension between private data collection/use and public benefit is a central conference theme
money_quotes: I will probably veer off to all kinds of ways that private companies use data to the detriment of the public, and how that should change.
To be honest I'm a bit skeptical of the concept I've heard floated about recently that private companies will 'donate' their data for the public good, but I'd love to be wrong.
posts.key_insight: The promise of private companies voluntarily sharing data for public benefit deserves scrutiny, given the fundamental tension between corporate data practices and public welfare.

posts.summary: O'Neil provides a behind-the-scenes update on the editing process for her upcoming book 'Weapons of Math Destruction.' She walks through the various stages of book production—from content edits to line editing to galleys to printing overseas. The post serves as both an explanation for reduced blog activity and an honest reflection on why books, despite being 'the opposite of sexy,' benefit from rigorous editing. She announces a tentative September 2016 launch date.
key_points: Book editing involves multiple distinct phases: content edits, line editing (commas, formatting), galleys, and cover design
The production timeline from editing to printed books takes months, including overseas printing and shipping
The careful, tedious editing process is precisely what makes books better than blogs
The book's full title reveals its thesis: 'how big data increases inequality and threatens democracy'
money_quotes: Right now I'm eyeball deep in line edits for my book, Weapons of Math Destruction: how big data increases inequality and threatens democracy.

my brain is completely occupied with this stuff, which is the opposite of sexy but on the other hand is exactly why the book will be better (hopefully!) than a blog – it will be actually carefully edited.
they print a bunch of copies in China and have it all shipped back here by boat. The process takes months and it's all new to me.
posts.key_insight: The unglamorous, painstaking work of careful editing is what elevates a book above a blog post.

posts.summary: O'Neil investigates whether charter schools systematically exclude difficult students by analyzing NYC Department of Education data. Using two proxies for challenging student populations—students with disabilities and English language learners—she finds charter schools have significantly fewer of both groups compared to traditional public schools. The statistical analysis, including t-tests with very small p-values, confirms this isn't random chance. While she notes this isn't an indictment of any specific charter school, she argues that performance comparisons between charter and non-charter schools must account for these demographic differences to be meaningful.
key_points: Success Academy maintained a 'Got To Go' list of students to push out, and doesn't accept new students after fourth grade—both forms of selection
Charter schools in NYC have statistically significantly fewer students with disabilities and English language learners than traditional public schools
T-tests confirm the demographic differences between charter and non-charter schools are not due to random chance (p-values much smaller than 0.05)
Some charter schools fall within normal ranges, so this is a statistical pattern rather than a universal indictment
Performance comparisons between charter and public schools are misleading without accounting for these demographic differences
money_quotes: There was a 'Got To Go' list of students at one charter school in the Success Academy network. These were troublesome kids that the school was pushing out.

I am not against charter schools as a concept. I love the idea of experimentation, and to the extent that charter schools perform experiments that can inform how public schools run, that's interesting and worthwhile.
From talking to friends of mine who run NYC schools, I learned of two proxies for difficult students.
The main conclusion of this analysis is to say that, generally speaking, charter schools don't have as many kids with disabilities or poor language skills.
When we compare their performance to non-charter schools, we need to somehow take this into account.
This is not an indictment on any specific charter school, but rather a statistical statement about wanting to compare apples to apples.
Those very small numbers are the p-values which are much smaller than 0.05.
posts.key_insight: Charter school performance metrics are misleading unless we account for the fact that they systematically serve fewer students with disabilities and English language learners than traditional public schools.

posts.summary: O'Neil struggles to find something in finance to be thankful for on a Thanksgiving podcast episode. She considers consumer loans, inflation, the financial system, and insurance, but finds significant drawbacks to each. Consumer loans raise prices and create crushing debt; inflation is unpredictable; the financial system exploits poor people. She settles tentatively on insurance as the most defensible option, acknowledging it mostly functions as intended to smooth out life's risks.
key_points: Consumer loans help in rough times but also inflate prices of everything, particularly education
Inflation benefits debtors but can spiral out of control
The financial system functions remarkably well day-to-day but systematically squeezes poor people for nickels and dimes
Insurance, despite problems like insane medical pricing for the uninsured, largely works as intended to even out life's risks
money_quotes: I'm grateful for consumer loans. After all, they help us out in rough times and allow us to invest in ourselves and our futures through mortgages and student loans. On the other hand, they also raise the price of everything through their availability.

I could just be grateful for the entire financial system working at all. If you think about how much we depend on its functioning, to take out loans, to use our credit and debit cards, and to get paid monthly, it's kind of amazing.
On the other hand, if you think about the way finance deals with poor people, squeezing them for nickels and dimes, then you kind of lose respect.
In fact it makes you want to be grateful for the CFPB instead, but that's not financial enough.

But if you just think about how much insurance actually does for us, whether it's medical or fire or car or life insurance, then you appreciate that it more or less functions as intended: to even out the bumpy risks of everyday life.
posts.key_insight: Even when actively trying to find gratitude for financial systems, the exploitation of vulnerable people keeps undermining the case.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where Cathy O'Neil, writing as 'Aunt Pythia,' responds to reader questions on topics ranging from unconscious sexism in academic publishing to workplace sexual harassment intervention. The most substantive exchange involves a mathematician who realized years later that he had written a condescending rejection letter because he assumed the author was female. O'Neil validates that everyone harbors sexist biases and proposes concrete solutions: blind review processes for journal submissions, similar to blind auditions in orchestras. She also addresses a bystander who intervened in a sexual assault but used diversion rather than direct confrontation, arguing that while well-intentioned, this approach fails to create consequences or protect future victims.
key_points: Women are just as susceptible to sexist bias as men - unconscious bias is universal and requires systematic solutions rather than individual vigilance
Blind review processes (obscuring author names) are the practical answer to publication bias, modeled on blind auditions that transformed orchestra hiring
Intervention in harassment situations should introduce 'serious friction' - calling out illegal behavior directly and involving authorities, not just diverting the perpetrator
True believers who convince themselves their harmful actions are righteous are 'the scariest people around'
Seeking acknowledgment for past interventions may not be welcome if the intervention was incomplete
money_quotes: Women are just as sexist as men. I often find myself wondering if I spoke too condescendingly to women.
Do what it takes to make it a blind audition.
Once this system is in place you will have accomplished way more than you are now by kicking yourself needlessly and fruitlessly.
The problem with diversion, as a technique, is that it doesn't address the underlying issue, and it doesn't call it out as fucked up.
Introduce serious friction into his predatory ways, in other words, and it will at least slow him down, and at best get him fired.
People have an amazing ability to believe what they want to believe.
Some people are true believers, and they are the scariest people around.
posts.key_insight: Systemic solutions like blind review processes are more effective than individual guilt or vigilance at combating unconscious bias, because bias is universal and self-awareness alone cannot overcome it.

posts.summary: This guest post by computer scientist Ernie Davis challenges Brian Greene's evolutionary explanation for why humans find math difficult. Davis argues that claiming math is hard because our brains evolved for survival doesn't actually explain anything, since many other cognitively demanding tasks that were equally useless for cavemen (reading novels, telling jokes, understanding stories) are easy and pleasant for us. He points out that our ancestors encountered parabolic motion and spherical geometry daily yet never saw magic wands, yet we'd rather read fantasy novels than study parabolas. The post concludes that without understanding how the mind performs cognitive tasks, we cannot explain why some are harder than others.
key_points: Evolutionary 'just-so stories' about cognitive limitations often explain nothing because they can't account for why some equally survival-irrelevant tasks are easy while others are hard
Computational complexity doesn't explain math difficulty either—AI research suggests basic math is computationally easier than understanding stories
Our cognitive apparatus has many characteristics that would have been unhelpful for primitive survival, undermining the 'caveman optimization' narrative
Primitive humans should not be underestimated—they created number systems, art, and language, not just fled from lions
We must understand how cognitive processes work before we can explain why some tasks are harder than others
money_quotes: Saying that math is hard because it was useless for avoiding lions and catching bison doesn't answer the question, because there are many other tasks that were equally useless but are easy and pleasant for people: reading novels, singing songs, looking at pictures, pretending, telling jokes, talking nonsense, dreaming.
If our experience with artificial intelligence is any indication, doing basic mathematics is much easier computationally than understanding stories.
You find a feature of the human mind that you dislike, or one that you think is an ineradicable part of human nature, and you make up a story about why it was good for the cavemen.

Our ancestors on the savannah saw parabolic motion whenever they threw a stone; they experienced spherical geometry whenever they looked up at the starry sky. They never encountered a magic wand or a magic ring.

Most people find it easier and much more enjoyable to read and remember and discuss four volumes of intricate tales about magic rings or seven about magic wands than to read a few dozen pages with basic information about parabolas.
Until we have a much better understanding of how the mind carries out these various cognitive tasks, no explanation of why one task is harder than another can possibly hold much water.
posts.key_insight: Evolutionary explanations for why math is difficult fail because they cannot account for why other survival-irrelevant cognitive tasks like reading fiction or understanding jokes are easy and enjoyable.

posts.summary: This guest post by Professor Nii Attoh-Okine argues that underrepresented minority students are conspicuously absent from the big data revolution, despite opportunities across multiple industries. Using his experience at major conferences like Strata and Hadoop, he observes that minority participation as both attendees and presenters is virtually nonexistent. He frames this as both a moral imperative and a practical necessity, noting that underrepresented minorities will soon constitute the majority of school-age children in the US.
