The post concludes with concrete suggestions for how the big data community can actively engage these students through mentorship, institutional partnerships, and conference inclusion.
key_points: Underrepresented minority students are virtually missing from big data conferences, seminars, and activities despite the field's rapid growth
Demographic shifts mean minorities will soon make up the majority of school-age children, representing an untapped pool of STEM talent
The responsibility falls on both sides—the big data community may not be reaching out, and minority students may not be reaching in
Concrete solutions include developing invested mentors, partnering with minority-serving institutions, and actively inviting underrepresented researchers to conferences
The talent exists within underrepresented groups; the community simply needs to make the effort to engage them
money_quotes: With all the opportunities, hype, and advancement, it is very clear that underrepresented minority students are virtually missing in the big data revolution.

At a recent Strata and Hadoop conference in New York, one of the premier big data events, it was very disappointing and even alarming that underrepresented minority students (participants and presenters) were virtually nonexistent.
Minority groups underrepresented in STEM fields will soon make up the majority of school-age children in the states.
This means that currently underrepresented minorities are a rich pool of STEM talent, if we figure out how to tap into it.
Please do make the effort to engage and include underrepresented minority students because there is so much talent within this group.
posts.key_insight: The big data community is missing out on a growing pool of talent by failing to actively engage underrepresented minority students, and bridging this gap requires intentional outreach rather than passive inclusion.

posts.summary: The post examines a whitepaper about the White House Police Data Initiative, which encourages police departments to share their data publicly. O'Neil argues that the fundamental problem isn't transparency but data collection itself—police departments simply don't gather information that might reflect poorly on them. She points out that even basic data like fatal police shootings lacks a reliable national registry, and information about public trust in police or jail conditions remains systematically uncollected. The piece concludes with skepticism about reform, noting that facilities like Rikers can't even be trusted to accurately count inmate fights.
key_points: Police departments only collect data that makes them look efficient or successful, creating systematic blind spots
The 'open data' initiative is fundamentally flawed because you cannot open data that was never collected
Voluntary data collection efforts have failed, leaving journalists like The Guardian to track police shootings better than the FBI
Critical information about public trust in police and conditions for arrested individuals remains uncollected
Even when data exists, institutions like Rikers have been caught manipulating basic statistics
money_quotes: You can't open up data that you don't collect.
We are starting too downstream.
When we ask police departments to 'open up' their data, we are assuming they collect the data we want. But they only collect the data that makes them look efficient or successful.
We don't have a high-quality national register of fatal police shootings, and the Guardian has a better one.
They cannot even be trusted to count inmate fights.
posts.key_insight: Transparency initiatives fail when they assume institutions collect inconvenient data—the real problem is that powerful actors systematically avoid measuring what might hold them accountable.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques H.R.4002, a bipartisan criminal justice reform bill backed by the Koch brothers that would require prosecutors to prove criminal intent in federal cases. While acknowledging the need for reform of drug sentencing laws, she argues this bill goes too far by extending protections to white-collar crime. She connects this to her research on algorithmic discrimination, warning that the bill would make it impossible to prosecute those who hide behind opaque algorithms. The post argues this would incentivize corporations to deliberately obscure criminal decisions within algorithms to avoid liability.
key_points: The bill requires proving defendants 'acted with intent' and knew their conduct was unlawful, which would effectively shield white-collar criminals
Corporations already use algorithms as shields against accountability, treating them as objective 'voices of science' that nobody interrogates
The law would create perverse incentives for companies to hide criminal decisions inside algorithms to claim ignorance
America already has a two-tiered justice system where white-collar criminals rarely face consequences
Criminal justice reform shouldn't come at the cost of making financial crimes even harder to prosecute
money_quotes: Combined with 'broken windows' policing, those laws have sent entire generations of minority men to jail.
One of the trickiest things about the current state of affairs is that nobody in particular understands the algorithms they are using.
They think of them, in a way, as 'the voice of God.' Actually it's more like 'the voice of science,' because they are assumed to be scientific and objective.

If this law were on the books, there'd be every incentive in the world for corporations to hide tricky or criminal decisions in algorithms just for the reason that afterwards they would be able to say they didn't know about it.
It would be an invitation for obfuscation.
Have you noticed a lot of people going to jail for their part in mortgage fraud? Neither have I.
We already live in a 2-tiered justice system; let's not make it even worse.
posts.key_insight: Criminal intent requirements in white-collar crime would incentivize corporations to deliberately hide illegal decisions inside opaque algorithms, making accountability impossible.

posts.summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil's humorous alter ego) answers reader questions on topics ranging from linguistics to career satisfaction to workplace gender dynamics. A PhD student struggles with enjoying learning math but hating research, prompting advice about patience, choosing faster-moving fields, and finding projects that genuinely excite. Two questions address gender in professional settings: a male professor worried about perceptions of his friendship with a female student, and a manager concerned about socializing more with male than female reports. The column blends irreverent humor with practical wisdom.
key_points: Research requires patience, humility, and 'a lack of caring about other people' - traits that can be developed but aren't innate for everyone
There is no perfect job; even dream gigs come with frustrations, so switching careers won't eliminate problems
Data science offers faster feedback loops than pure mathematics - 'projects go faster, people care about minor advances' - but the questions weren't asked by Diophantus
Managers should proactively include women in informal socializing rather than assuming they prefer to be 'left alone' - just give advance notice so people can arrange childcare
Having chemistry with someone in a professional context isn't inherently problematic if you're not acting on it inappropriately
money_quotes: There is no perfect job. You can quit one job, or one career, and then start a new one, and you'd still have problems.
Doing research requires patience, and a certain dose of humility, and a lack of caring about other people.

Data science is a faster, easier field than algebraic number theory. Projects go faster, people care about minor advances, and so on. On the other hand, the questions you answer weren't asked by Diophantus.
Alcohol has the magical ability to help people find common interests. You don't need to know what they are in advance.
You're doing nothing wrong, they're all jealous. Please enjoy each other.
Should I just grow up more and accept that the world wasn't designed to be enjoyable?
posts.key_insight: Career satisfaction comes not from finding a perfect path but from honestly assessing what you enjoy, choosing work with feedback loops that match your temperament, and accepting that every job has frustrations.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the tech industry is failing elderly people by focusing on surveillance devices for caregivers rather than tools that enhance communication and quality of life. She uses her father-in-law's hearing difficulties as an example of how existing technology like voice recognition could easily help but isn't being deployed. The 'Silver Tech' industry largely produces monitoring tools that treat elderly people as problems to be managed rather than individuals to be empowered. Meanwhile, the data industry that does focus on the elderly often does so predatorily, selling lists of vulnerable seniors to scammers. O'Neil calls for a shift toward thoughtful, non-predatory technology designed with elderly end-users in mind, especially as Boomers retire with higher expectations for their quality of life.
key_points: Existing technology like voice recognition could help elderly people communicate but isn't being adapted for their use
Most 'Silver Tech' focuses on surveillance and monitoring rather than enhancing independence and interaction
The data industry actively exploits elderly people through predatory marketing lists with names like 'Suffering Seniors'
Technology for the elderly is often designed without the end-user in mind, making it too difficult to use
As the population ages, there's growing need for tools that address specific limitations while preserving quality of life
money_quotes: I'm more interested in tools for older people to use that would make their lives more interactive, not merely so that they can be safely left alone for longer periods of time.
There's a corner of the big data industry that's already hard at work thinking about the elderly, but it's in the realm of predatory consumer offers.
Data warehousing companies like Acxiom selling lists of names, addresses, and email addresses with names like 'Suffering Seniors' and 'Aching and Ailing' for 15 cents per person.
They don't seem to be designed with the end-user in mind very often.
The Boomers are retiring, and they won't want their quality of life to be diminished to the daytime soap opera watching that my grandmother put up with.
We'll have more and more people who are perfectly healthy in all sorts of ways except one or two, and who could really benefit from some thoughtful and non-predatory technology solution.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry treats elderly people as subjects to be monitored or exploited rather than as users deserving thoughtfully designed tools that enhance their independence and connection.

posts.summary: O'Neil discovers that house keys aren't duplicated by physical copying but by reading embedded codes, leading her down a rabbit hole of lock mechanics. She learns her Medeco keys have 6 pins with both depth levels (6 options) and angles (3 options), creating 34 million possible combinations. Her curiosity then turns to master key systems, which work by having multiple slices in each pin. The investigation ends with a security concern: master key systems are potentially vulnerable, and she wonders if Medeco's angled cuts truly add protection or if master keys could still be derived.
key_points: Keys are duplicated using embedded codes (depth and angle for each cut), not by physical copying from the original
Medeco 6-pin keys have 18 choices per cut (6 depths × 3 angles), yielding 34 million possible combinations
Master key systems work by adding extra slices to pins, allowing multiple key codes to open the same lock
The existence of master key systems significantly reduces security because knowing one key narrows down the master key possibilities
Mathematical curiosity about everyday objects can reveal both elegant engineering and potential vulnerabilities
money_quotes: Keys have embedded codes? I had no idea. I thought they duplicated keys by shaving one key to look just like another.
And yes, I was worried that after many many copies, my house keys would stop working, since in each round the approximation to the truth would get slightly worse.
There are 18 choices for each cut, because 6 depth levels and 3 angles, and therefore there are 18**6 = 34 million different codes altogether, which makes me feel safe.
Well, it turns out that a master key system will have pins that have more than one slice. That means that two (actually, 2**6 = 64) different codes will work on the same lock.
So I'm ending up feel a bit less safe after all.
posts.key_insight: Everyday objects like house keys encode surprisingly elegant mathematical systems, but understanding the math behind security mechanisms often reveals their vulnerabilities rather than reinforcing confidence in them.

posts.summary: O'Neil promotes her Occupy Wall Street offshoot group, the Alternative Banking group, which continues to meet weekly at Columbia University. The group maintains several ongoing projects including essay series at Huffington Post and Occupy Summer School. She highlights their curated reading list as a valuable resource for quality financial and economic articles. The post serves primarily as an invitation to engage with the group's work and resources.
key_points: The Alternative Banking group emerged from Occupy Wall Street and still meets weekly at Columbia
The group produces essays for Huffington Post and runs Occupy Summer School
They maintain a curated reading list with archives going back two years
O'Neil considers the reading list higher quality than most articles she encounters
money_quotes: I know I waste my time every week reading lots of articles that are not nearly the quality of the ones listed on this reading list.
I think it's already pretty amazing what the group has accomplished, and I'm proud to be part of it.
posts.key_insight: Sustained grassroots financial reform efforts like the Alternative Banking group continue their work long after the initial protest movement fades from headlines.

posts.summary: Piper Harron reflects on her unconventional mathematics PhD thesis, which she describes as initially intended as a 'grenade' against academia but evolved into something more constructive. She candidly discusses spending 10 years on her dissertation while battling imposter syndrome, feeling behind her peers, and processing the systemic pressures of being a Black woman in mathematics. Her awakening to feminism and anti-racism, catalyzed by Trayvon Martin's death, led her to rewrite her thesis as something accessible to non-mathematicians. She hopes it will show that research math isn't alien and help graduate students feel less alone in their struggles.
key_points: Harron entered Princeton with only 7 math classes compared to peers' 20-30, creating persistent feelings of inadequacy
Her brain processes mathematics differently than most mathematicians—she needs complete logical understanding rather than hand-waving explanations
Stress from graduate school literally hospitalized her, leading her to consciously 'give up stress' and leave without graduating in 2009
The killing of Trayvon Martin and subsequent acquittal awakened her to conscious anti-racism and prompted her to restart her thesis entirely
She deliberately wrote her thesis to be accessible to non-mathematicians and struggling graduate students, not for established mathematicians
money_quotes: TEN YEARS?! WHAT KIND OF PERSON SPENDS TEN YEARS ON ONE DOCUMENT AND STILL HAS TO BUILD UP COURAGE TO DISCUSS IT OPENLY?
Please tell me the rules I must abide by in order to make no waves!
I can't just understand the forest, I also need the trees, and the leaves, and the space between, and I need to be able to go forwards and backwards logically between it all.
The way people talk to you when you don't understand what they're saying. It's its own terrible language and I had to listen to it for years.
I realized stress had sent me to the hospital and I was thoroughly against that on principle, so I gave up stress.
A black teenaged boy was shot dead for no reason and his assailant was found not guilty because black boys are scary.
I am thrilled at the idea that mathematicians might read and enjoy my thesis, but I didn't write it for them.
posts.key_insight: Academia's rigid structures and unspoken rules can hospitalize you, but reclaiming your work as something accessible and human—rather than performing for gatekeepers—transforms it from a weapon of escape into something that might actually help others.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the retirement of her advice column 'Aunt Pythia' after a long run. She provides a condensed summary of her most common advice topics: becoming a data scientist, understanding why math is difficult, dating advice, and encouraging people to act on their feelings. The column ends on a triumphant note with a reader writing back to report that following Aunt Pythia's advice to confess a long-term crush resulted in a successful relationship. The tone is warm, self-deprecating, and celebratory of the column's quirky mix of technical and personal guidance.
key_points: To become a data scientist, learn linear algebra and statistics fundamentals, build projects, and understand how to communicate technical concepts to non-technical audiences
Math feels hard because practitioners constantly push to the edge of their knowledge rather than lingering on familiar territory
Dating advice boils down to: be human, listen, make effort without desperation, and don't obsess over one person if it's not working
When you have a mutual crush and sense reciprocation, take the risk—you only live once
money_quotes: She has truly enjoyed handing out consistently bad and possibly dangerous suggestions to all you wonderful and earnest people.
Math is hard because we rarely spend time dawdling over thing we already know, however beautiful they are; instead we push ourselves to the very limits of known theory.
We are impatient and hungry for more knowledge. That's a good thing, and it's human nature, but it's also nice to dawdle sometimes.
Think about just how cool Galois Theory is every now and then.
Be a human, listen well, try to seem like you're making an effort, but don't act desperate.
There are lots of people in the world, so don't obsess with one of them if things aren't working out, but if things are working out then try to appreciate it daily.
Go for it. You only live once.
Thanks for making my advice seem good!
posts.key_insight: The best advice—whether about math careers or love—comes down to taking informed risks, appreciating what you have, and not overthinking what you can't control.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil appeared on NPR's 'To The Point' to discuss predictive policing and whether Big Data can prevent crime. She notes she was the most skeptical among four guests, which included a journalist, a techno-futurist, and a public safety director. The brief post serves primarily to share the podcast link with her readers.
key_points: O'Neil participated in an NPR discussion on predictive policing
She positioned herself as the most skeptical voice among the panel guests
The panel represented diverse perspectives: journalism, tech optimism, and law enforcement
money_quotes: I was definitely the most skeptical of the four guests
posts.key_insight: O'Neil signals her characteristic skepticism toward Big Data claims in policing, though this post is primarily a podcast announcement rather than substantive analysis.

posts.summary: Brian Conrad's notes summarize the 2015 Oxford workshop on Mochizuki's claimed proof of the ABC Conjecture via Inter-universal Teichmüller Theory (IUT). Despite gathering top arithmetic geometers including Faltings and Wiles, the workshop failed to produce the hoped-for 'aha moment' that would clarify how IUT actually proves anything. The first three days provided useful background on pre-IUT material, but the final two days became incomprehensible to the audience, with speakers unable to provide satisfying examples or explain why elaborate constructions were necessary. Conrad concludes that the burden now falls on those who claim to understand IUT to communicate its key insights more effectively, noting that three years and three workshops have not produced the kind of clear exposition that accompanies other mathematical breakthroughs.
key_points: The ABC Conjecture is equivalent to Szpiro's Conjecture for elliptic curves, and Mochizuki's approach works entirely in this more abstract setting where concrete consequences cannot be used to 'test' the method

IUT encodes arithmetic invariants of elliptic curves in terms of 'Frobenioids' - categorical structures that retain key information while admitting operations with no direct geometric interpretation, potentially including characteristic-0 analogues of Frobenius maps

The workshop revealed that much of Mochizuki's extensive prior work on general Frobenioid theory is actually unnecessary for proving Szpiro's Conjecture, but navigating to the relevant portions remains extremely difficult

Communication completely broke down in the final days: repeated audience requests for illuminating examples were met with claims that examples had been given, when none satisfying to experts had been provided
The 'inter-universal' aspects involving set-theoretic universes appear to be about generality for future developments rather than being logically necessary for ABC itself
money_quotes: Nobody is convinced of the correctness of a proof by knowing how many hours have been devoted to explaining it to others; they are convinced by the force of ideas, not by the passage of time.

It felt like taking a course in linear algebra in which one is repeatedly told 'Consider a pair of isomorphic vector spaces' but is never given an interesting example despite repeated requests and eventually one is told 'you have been given examples.'

For every subject I have ever understood in mathematics, there are instructive basic examples and concise arguments to illustrate what is the point to generally educated mathematicians. There is no reason that IUT should be any different.
The primary burden now is on those who understand IUT to do a better job of explaining the main substantial points to the wider community of arithmetic geometers.
Three years have passed. Waiting another half-year for yet another workshop is not the answer to the current predicament.

If someone says they understand a theory and does not provide instructive visibly relevant examples and concise arguments that clearly illustrate what is the point then they are not trying hard enough.
It is the efficient communication of great ideas in written and oral form that inspires people to invest the time to learn a difficult mathematical theory.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical correctness requires not just logical validity but communicable insight—if experts cannot explain why a proof works to other experts, the burden of proof remains unmet regardless of time invested.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' is nearing its final draft and will be produced as an audiobook by Random House. She reflects on the long, uncertain journey of writing the book and the relief of finally seeing tangible progress. The post captures the emotional arc of completing a major project—from the disorienting uncertainty of research to the satisfaction of a finished product. She expresses enthusiasm about potentially narrating the audiobook herself, having developed an appreciation for how the book sounds read aloud.
key_points: The book is finally approaching completion after a long period of uncertainty about whether it would come together
Random House has agreed to produce an audiobook version
O'Neil enlisted friends to read chapters aloud to her as part of the editing process
She reflects that she might not have started the project if she'd known how difficult and uncertain it would be
She's campaigning to be chosen as the audiobook narrator
money_quotes: I likely wouldn't have taken it on at all if I'd actually known how big and how long, and how uncertain I'd be, so often, that it would end up being a book.
So, I guess, here's to running into things with almost pure ignorance!

What's cool about this phase is, first of all it seems real, and countable, like you can feel the progress happening, whereas so many other moments seemed completely lost in time, swallowed up by a very loud hum of uncertainty.
Now, at least, I know there's a book here.
This is happening, people! Fuck yes!
posts.key_insight: Major creative projects often succeed precisely because we don't fully understand their scope when we begin—pure ignorance can be a gift that keeps us from being paralyzed by the enormity of the task.

posts.summary: O'Neil humorously reviews the infamous 1978 Star Wars Holiday Special, describing it as the perfect movie to watch while high due to its inexplicably long, subtitle-free scenes featuring Chewbacca's family. She admits to watching it despite not being in the ideal altered state, finding enjoyment mainly through her teenagers' amusement at how bad it is. The post notes George Lucas's reported desire to destroy every copy of the film.
key_points: The Star Wars Holiday Special features extremely long scenes of Chewbacca's family without subtitles
The film's pacing makes it ideal viewing for people in an altered state
Even devoted Star Wars fans will struggle to remember why they're watching it
George Lucas reportedly wanted to track down and destroy every copy
money_quotes: Sometimes I find an activity that's so perfect for the state of being high that I am deeply jealous of the people who can achieve it.
Each of those scenes is inexplicably long and devoid of subtitles.
It's really not a stretch to say that every scene in the entire movie is inexplicably long.
That's perfect for high folks, who are known to drive at 15 miles an hour on the highway and worry they're speeding.
I like hanging out with teenagers rolling on the rug in laughter because it's so bad it's good.
If he 'had enough time, he'd track down every copy of this film and destroy it.'
posts.key_insight: The Star Wars Holiday Special is so legendarily bad that it can only be properly appreciated either while intoxicated or through the lens of teenagers finding joy in its awfulness.

posts.summary: O'Neil catalogs five notable consumer scams from 2015, arguing that global supply chain opacity enables widespread fraud. From VW's emissions cheating to fake artisanal chocolate to mislabeled yarn, she demonstrates how companies exploit consumers' inability to verify product claims. The common thread is that authentication came from external testing or allergic reactions, not internal accountability. She questions whether we'll develop better authentication systems or simply see scams proliferate.
key_points: Global supply chains are so opaque that consumers cannot verify claims about how products are made or what they contain
The VW scandal was exposed by external road tests, not whistleblowers, revealing a failure of internal accountability
Marketing and branding (like 'artisanal' or 'bamboo') can successfully mask inferior or fraudulent products
Food mislabeling, including horsemeat substitution, is a recurring problem across years and retailers
Consumer protection often depends on individuals with allergies or expert knowledge detecting fraud accidentally
money_quotes: I blame it on our global supply chain that's entirely opaque and impenetrable to the outsider.
We have no idea how things are made, what they're made with, or how the end results get shipped around the world.
Seriously, anything goes. And that's probably not going to change.
What's amazing to me is that no whistleblower did anything; we learned about it from road tests by an external group.
It just goes to show you how much marketing plays a part in this stuff.
We eat horsemeat all the fucking time.
The fiber community was thrown into a tizzy when this came out; we yarn snobs turn up our noses at nylon.
posts.key_insight: The opacity of modern supply chains makes fraud nearly undetectable, leaving consumers dependent on accidental discoveries rather than systemic accountability.

posts.summary: The post explores what precipitation forecasts actually mean and how to evaluate their accuracy. O'Neil proposes a rigorous method using 101 bins (0-100% probability) to collect forecast-outcome pairs over time, then measuring accuracy by comparing predicted probabilities against actual rain frequencies. She argues that squared error is the ideal penalty metric because it's minimized when forecasts match true probabilities and rewards models that commit to confident predictions near 0% or 100% rather than hedging at 50%. The analysis reveals that a 'dumb' forecast using historical averages would score poorly despite being technically accurate within its single bin, while the ideal forecast would only use bins 0 and 100.
key_points: A 37% rain forecast should mean that 37 out of 100 times with identical conditions, measurable precipitation occurs
Squared error is the optimal penalty metric because it's minimized when bin labels perfectly match actual rain frequencies
The 50% bin represents maximum uncertainty and accumulates the highest penalty regardless of outcome
Models improve by either making bin predictions more accurate or by moving forecasts closer to 0% or 100% certainty
Geographic averaging across land masses distorts forecasts, but pinpointed location apps are making this obsolete
money_quotes: The ideal forecast would always just be 1 or 0: it's either going to rain or it's not.
Putting an hourly forecast into the '50' bin is like giving up – you're going to get a big penalty no matter what, because again, it's either going to rain or it isn't.
There's a forecast out there that we want to make sure is deeply shamed by any self-respecting scoring system.
This is a super dumb forecast, and we want to make sure it doesn't score as well as God or mother nature, and thankfully it doesn't.
If there's an 80% chance that precipitation will occur over half the land mass and a 0% chance it will occur over the other half, the forecast will read 40%.
posts.key_insight: Good probability forecasts should be judged not just on accuracy but on their willingness to make confident predictions, since hedging at 50% is mathematically the worst possible strategy.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that while black box algorithms are increasingly used as tools of social control against teachers and civilians (particularly young men of color), there's a glaring absence of similar accountability mechanisms for police. She notes that existing police metrics like CompStat focus only on arrests and tickets, ignoring data on police violence. The post was prompted by the Tamir Rice case, where a prosecutor refused to 'second-guess' officers who killed a 12-year-old. O'Neil contends that our priorities are backwards when we scrutinize teachers more than police who kill children.
key_points: Black box algorithms function as tools of social control, primarily targeting teachers (Value-Added Models) and civilians (predictive policing)
Police accountability metrics like CompStat measure only arrests and tickets, not violence or injuries caused by officers
Data on police shootings and injuries isn't formally analyzed, possibly not even collected systematically
Society invests heavily in identifying 'bad teachers' while doing little to identify dangerous police officers
The Tamir Rice case exemplifies the lack of accountability, with prosecutors explicitly refusing to 'second-guess' police
money_quotes: How black box algorithms have become tools of social control.
What's missing here? Why haven't we built, for example, models which assess police?
How about how many people the policeman has shot or injured? As far as I know, that data isn't analyzed, if it's even formally collected.
I do think our country has got its priorities backwards when we put so much focus and money towards getting rid of the worst teachers but we do very little towards getting rid of the worst cops.
The prosecutor was quoted as saying, 'We don't second-guess police officers.' I maintain that we should do exactly that.
We should collect and analyze data around police actions as long as children are getting killed.
posts.key_insight: If we're going to use algorithmic tools of social control against teachers and civilians, intellectual consistency demands we also build accountability systems that scrutinize police behavior—especially their use of violence.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares a personal story about her youngest son's recovery from a broken leg, where the physical healing was quick but the psychological barrier to walking again proved far more challenging. After weeks of frustration, conflicting medical advice, and a misdiagnosis of 'hysterical paralysis,' she finally gave up the intense pressure and took her son on a relaxing swimming trip. Once the stress was removed, he simply decided to walk again. The experience taught her that sometimes the best parenting means stepping back and trusting children to overcome obstacles on their own timeline.
key_points: Medical professionals offered conflicting and sometimes unhelpful advice, including an expensive psychiatric referral for 'hysterical paralysis'
Physical therapy provided some progress but the breakthrough came when pressure was removed entirely
The pool allowed her son to feel 'fully able-bodied' again, rebuilding his confidence without the stress of expectations
The hardest parenting lesson is learning when to stop trying to fix things and simply trust your child
money_quotes: It's been a blizzard of pancakes and lost teeth, and almost nothing has really fazed me.
The guy told me he had 'hysterical paralysis' and gave me the number of a psychiatrist who charges $1500 per hour and doesn't take insurance.
I could see how joyous he became in the water, where there was no sense of gravity and he was once again fully able-bodied.
I think he would have slept in it if I'd let him.
On the way out of the elevator and on to the check-out desk, my son said to me, 'I'm just going to walk now.' And he did.
The hardest part has been learning to trust my kids to get through difficult things even when I can't help them directly.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most effective parenting intervention is removing pressure and trusting children to find their own path through difficult challenges.

posts.summary: The post examines the backlash when the video game Rust changed from giving all players white avatars to randomly assigning race. White players complained vocally about potentially being assigned black avatars, despite minorities having played as white characters without complaint for years. O'Neil notes the irony that white players would accept being a green alien but not a black man, and suggests games could further challenge players by including experiences like menstruation or pregnancy.
key_points: When Rust randomly assigned race to avatars, complaints and racist language increased dramatically from white players
Black and Hispanic gamers play more video games than whites but rarely complained about always being cast as white characters
White players would accept being a randomly colored alien but objected to being assigned a black avatar
The gaming industry is dominated by white male designers, which shapes default representation
O'Neil proposes games could be used educationally to help people experience different physical realities like menstruation
money_quotes: White people are okay with being cast as a green alien avatar, but no way they're going to be cast as a black man. WTF, white people?
They were not complaining about being cast as a white man in the initial version, because it's so common.
I'd love to see men in video games dealing with getting their period.
Those who advance to the next level can experience being pregnant and suffering sciatica. Or maybe even hot flashes and menopause, why not?
posts.key_insight: The outrage over randomly assigned race in video games reveals that white identity is treated as the invisible default, while any other identity is seen as an imposition.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' is now available for pre-order and reflects on how book publishing differs from her previous work in mathematics and data science. She notes that while academic papers and data science projects end once published, a book's publication is just the beginning of spreading its message. She also announces hiring a booking agent to manage the growing volume of speaking requests, acknowledging this may surprise academics accustomed to free speaker availability.
key_points: Book publishing inverts the typical academic workflow - the hard work of writing is just a precursor to the main event of publication and promotion
In math and data science, projects end when published; with books, publication marks the beginning of spreading the message
She's hired a booking agent to handle speaking requests and will now charge real money for talks
The shift from giving away expertise for free to charging for it represents a break from academic norms
money_quotes: Nowadays I'm working on endnote formatting rather than having existential crises about the content.
By the time you've finished submitting your paper, you're kind of over your result and you want to move on.
This whole process, as long and as arduous and soul-sucking as it's been, is just a pre-cursor to the actual event.
The point of writing the book wasn't simply for me to know the stuff and then move on, it's for me to spread that message.
This might offend some people, especially academics who are used to having people available to donate their time for free.
posts.key_insight: Unlike academic work where publication is the finish line, book publishing transforms the author's role from researcher to messenger, making the real work begin only after the writing ends.

posts.summary: The $64 billion weight loss industry thrives despite no evidence that diets work beyond 12 months. O'Neil argues that people continue paying for ineffective programs due to three psychological mechanisms: the belief they'll be the statistical exception, the entertainment value of fantasizing about a 'new thin self,' and the moral approval society bestows on dieters. The industry exploits these vulnerabilities, with the final phase being guilt when people inevitably fail—leading them to keep paying for nothing. She draws parallels to lottery ticket purchases and gym memberships as examples of 'quasi-moralistic self-help fads' that extract money through hope rather than results.
key_points: Weight loss programs have no evidence of long-term effectiveness beyond 12 months, yet remain a multi-billion dollar industry
Three psychological mechanisms sustain the industry: exceptional thinking ('I'll beat the odds'), entertainment value of fantasizing about transformation, and social/moral approval for dieters
The asymmetry of public support—people announce diets but not diet failures—reinforces the cycle
Guilt from perceived personal failure keeps people paying even after they've stopped participating
The weight loss industry operates on the same principles as lotteries and gym memberships: selling hope rather than results
money_quotes: There's really no evidence that weight loss programs work long-term, but they are expensive. They keep doing studies but they never come out with any positive results beyond 12 months. That's because they don't have any evidence.
The plan will auto-renew and charge my credit card unless I cancel it, which is tantamount to admitting defeat.

If we were expected to pay a doctor to set the bones of our broken leg, but it only worked for a few months before our leg started breaking again, we'd call the doctor a quack and demand our money back. But somehow with diets it's different.
The 'I'm an exception' law of human nature, whereby everyone thinks they somehow will prove to be an exception to statistical rules.
It's all about imagining a 'new you,' as if you also get a personality transplant along with losing weight.

Since people are expected to have control over their weight, they are given moral standing when they announce their diet; it is a sign they are finally 'taking control.' Never mind that their chances of long-term success are minimal.

Because we've bought in to the idea that people have direct control over their weight, when people end up giving up, they feel personally guilty and end up paying extra money for basically nothing in return.
posts.key_insight: The weight loss industry profits not from delivering results but from exploiting the gap between statistical reality and individual hope, sustained by a culture that moralizes weight and rewards the appearance of trying.

posts.summary: O'Neil investigates Michigan's 'Emergency Manager' law, which allows state-appointed officials to replace elected local governments in financially distressed municipalities. Prompted by Rachel Maddow's coverage of the Flint water crisis, she maps which Michigan cities are under receivership. By overlaying these locations with census data showing Black population distribution, she reveals a striking visual correlation. The cities stripped of their elected officials are predominantly Black communities, suggesting a racial dimension to this 'suspension of democracy.'
key_points: Michigan's Emergency Manager law allows appointed officials to replace elected local governments, effectively suspending democratic representation
The Flint water crisis, where poisonous water was pumped to save money, occurred under an Emergency Manager
Visual comparison of receivership cities with census data reveals these municipalities have predominantly Black populations
Both individual cities and Wayne County (which includes Detroit) have been placed under receivership
money_quotes: Instead of having elected officials, some specially chosen towns have instead 'Emergency Managers,' who do things like save money by pumping in poisonous water.
What is the racial make-up of the towns who are in receivership?
posts.key_insight: Michigan's Emergency Manager law disproportionately strips democratic representation from Black communities, as demonstrated by mapping receivership cities against census racial demographics.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil comments on a Cuomo administration announcement about technological upgrades to NYC subways, including mobile tickets, wi-fi in stations, surveillance cameras on all trains and buses, and USB charging stations. She expresses skepticism about the priorities and longevity of these upgrades, suggesting that surveillance infrastructure will be maintained far better than amenities meant to serve the public.
key_points: NYC subways are getting mobile tickets, station wi-fi, surveillance cameras, and USB charging stations
The surveillance cameras will be on all subways and buses
Public amenities like USB chargers will likely fall into disrepair while surveillance infrastructure persists
There's an implicit critique of government priorities favoring monitoring over service
money_quotes: My guess: the surveillance cameras will continue to function long after the USB chargers get filled with gum.
posts.key_insight: When governments invest in both public amenities and surveillance infrastructure, expect the surveillance to be maintained while the amenities decay.

posts.summary: This is a light personal post where O'Neil shares a moment of stress relief during final book edits by enjoying music with her three sons. She highlights songs by Rob Cantor as family favorites and mentions wanting to see him perform live. The post culminates in sharing the 'O'Neil/de Jong family anthem' - a Songify This video. It's a rare purely personal glimpse into her family life, stepping away from her usual algorithmic critique.
key_points: O'Neil uses family music time as stress relief during intense book editing
Rob Cantor is a family favorite, with each son having their preferred song
The family has an unofficial anthem they regularly sing together
The post shows O'Neil's self-deprecating humor, calling herself 'an emo'
money_quotes: I'm working through final edits today, and it's terribly stressful, so I'm glad I spent last night with my three sons listening to their favorite music.
Here's my fave Cantor tune (obviously, because I'm an emo)
We *make up* reasons to sing this song, and it can fairly be called the O'Neil/de Jong family anthem.
posts.key_insight: Even data scientists immersed in algorithmic critique need moments of pure family joy and silly songs to balance the stress of serious work.

posts.summary: O'Neil debunks the myth that the NYC SHSAT school matching algorithm is computationally complex, arguing the real delay is political rather than technical. She and her husband devised a simple algorithm over lunch that could run in minutes once tests are scored. The actual complexity lies in the non-SHSAT public school matching system, not the exam-based placements. She suspects the delay exists to prevent the SHSAT results from creating unfair competitive pressure against the regular public school matching process.
key_points: The SHSAT matching is a straightforward greedy algorithm: rank students by score, assign each their top available choice in order
The algorithm could run in about 5 minutes of computer time once tests are scored
The only real complication is handling ties to avoid lawsuits from students with identical scores and preferences
The 4-month delay is likely political—preventing SHSAT results from undermining the separate public school matching process
People conflate the simple SHSAT algorithm with the genuinely complex non-SHSAT school matching system
money_quotes: I often hear people say that the algorithm to figure out what SHSAT school a given kid gets into is super complicated and that's why it takes 4 months to find out the results. But yesterday at lunch, my husband and I proved that theory incorrect by coming up with a really dumb way of doing it.
The above algorithm would take very little time to actually run. As in, 5 minutes of computer time once the tests are scored.
I'm pretty sure they need to make sure that two kids with the same exact score and the same preference would both either get in or get out (because think of the lawsuit if not).
It would foster too much unfair competition between the systems.

I'm guessing the algorithm for matching non-SHSAT schools is actually pretty complicated, which is I think why people keep talking about a 'super complex algorithm.' It's just not associated to the SHSAT.
posts.key_insight: Technical complexity is often blamed for bureaucratic delays that are actually caused by political or institutional considerations.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that New York City's housing market has split into two competing segments: one for people who need homes and one for wealthy investors treating apartments as assets. The luxury investment market is winning, driven by foreign money laundering, global inequality, and perverse tax incentives that make holding empty apartments nearly frictionless. She proposes dramatically increasing real estate taxes on luxury properties, especially empty ones, to shift incentives back toward housing people rather than storing wealth. This would force wealthy owners to sell to actual residents, bringing prices down and making the city livable again.
key_points: NYC has two competing housing markets: affordable housing for residents and luxury real estate for investors
Tax breaks and low property taxes make it profitable to hold empty apartments as assets
Developers rationally choose to build luxury units for oligarchs rather than studios for actual residents
Current tax policy creates exactly the wrong incentives by exempting non-residents from income tax and keeping property taxes artificially low
Higher taxes on luxury and empty apartments would force sales to actual residents and normalize prices
money_quotes: The people who want to live in New York are being shut out by the people who want to own apartment-shaped assets.
In the long run we might have a city that consists of mostly empty apartments.
We are actually setting up incentives for the situation we have: empty luxury apartments.
If an apartment – especially an empty apartment – actually costs the owner a lot of money, they'd sell it, and they'd sell it to a person that would actually live there.
Don't worry, the billionaire owners can afford it, and if they can't, then they can sell it to a mere millionaire who lives in Park Slope.
The rich people could simply shift their interest to the fine art market or some other place where holding assets doesn't cost as much.
posts.key_insight: Tax policy that makes holding empty luxury apartments cheap transforms housing from shelter into an asset class, and only higher taxes can restore housing's primary purpose of being lived in.

posts.summary: O'Neil responds to a New York Times article lamenting the low percentage of women at Davos by arguing that women's absence from the World Economic Forum is actually something to be proud of, not ashamed of. She characterizes Davos as a 'dick-measuring contest' where elites gather in luxury to superficially discuss poverty while primarily advancing their own interests. Rather than pushing for gender parity at the event, she suggests the more beneficial action would be to cancel Davos entirely, as the forum represents and perpetuates the very inequality it claims to address.
key_points: The push for more women at Davos assumes attendance is inherently desirable, which O'Neil rejects
Davos brings together finance, tech, and 'thought leader' types who prioritize self-dealing over solving problems
The forum's discussions of inequality are disingenuous given its role as a networking event for the powerful
Women having 'better things to do' than attend Davos is framed as a point of pride rather than exclusion
money_quotes: One thing the article didn't consider is the question of whether it's actually a good thing that women aren't at Davos.
I'm proud that women have better things to do than spend their time in high-security luxury to disingenuously discuss the world's poor.
Davos is a force of inequality.
And while they might discuss the world's big problems – like increasing inequality itself – I'm pretty sure they try much harder to help themselves than to solve those problems.
Let's just cancel Davos altogether, shall we? That will do the world more good than getting more women to attend.
posts.key_insight: Demanding inclusion in corrupt institutions validates those institutions—sometimes the more radical position is to reject the premise that attendance equals progress.

posts.summary: O'Neil recaps a meetup by data analyst Todd Schneider, who analyzed 1.1 billion NYC taxi and Uber trips using what he calls 'medium data' - datasets that require creative solutions but can still run on personal computers. She highlights his practical techniques for handling large datasets, including using external storage and reducing geographic granularity. The post also surfaces an intriguing finding: Uber appears to be taking market share from taxis rather than increasing overall rides, which contradicts claims that Uber increases traffic congestion.
key_points: Medium data refers to datasets too big for easy analysis but manageable on personal computers with clever techniques like geographic aggregation
Schneider reduced data complexity by converting geocoded events into weighted counts on 10-meter grid squares before visualization
The taxi/Uber analysis suggests ride-sharing is a zero-sum game - Uber gains come from taxi losses, not new trips
This finding challenges de Blasio's argument that Uber increases NYC traffic congestion
Key advice for viral data analysis: if you're not genuinely interested in the topic, no one else will be either
money_quotes: Todd calls this 'medium data' because, after some amount of work, you can do it on a personal computer. I dig it.
The most important of which was, if you're not interested in this topic, nobody else will be either.
It actually seemed to imply that the overall number of rides hasn't changed much; it's been a zero-sum game.
Uber doesn't increase traffic (if 'the number of rides' is a good proxy for traffic); rather, it's taking business away from medallion cabs.
This corroborates my fear of the dreaded post-3pm flight.
posts.key_insight: Practical data analysis techniques can transform unwieldy datasets into accessible insights that challenge political narratives, as Schneider's 'medium data' approach revealed Uber may be displacing taxis rather than adding traffic.

posts.summary: This extremely brief post directs readers to a Bloomberg article featuring two graphs about wealth and race. O'Neil lets the data visualizations speak for themselves rather than providing her own analysis. The post implies the graphs are self-evidently powerful in illustrating racial disparities in wealth accumulation. By describing the article as 'amazing' and the graphs as 'fantastic,' she signals strong endorsement of the piece's findings.
key_points: Bloomberg published compelling data visualizations about race and wealth
The graphs are presented as self-evident proof requiring little commentary
The title connects racial inequality to 'Race to the Top' education policy rhetoric
money_quotes: Bloomberg has a pretty amazing article today with two fantastic graphs.
The graphs pretty much say it all.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes data visualizations are so powerful they require no additional commentary to make their point about racial inequality.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques Kinvolved, a new app that tracks Harlem schoolchildren's attendance and sends alerts to parents. While the app may have good intentions and could help some families, she argues the data it generates about poor Black and brown children is inadequately protected. She highlights that the user agreement can be changed at any time and that bankruptcy could lead to data being sold to brokers. FERPA's loopholes allow such apps to access education records without meaningful constraints on future use.
key_points: Kinvolved tracks attendance of Harlem schoolchildren and texts parents about tardiness or absences
The user agreement allows the company to change terms at any time, creating future risk
Bankruptcy could lead to valuable tracking data being sold to data brokers
FERPA inadequately protects children because it designates such apps as 'school officials' with 'legitimate interest'
The most vulnerable populations face the greatest surveillance while having the least data protection
money_quotes: It's exactly my worst fear.
What about when they go out of business? I'm thinking the data might be valuable at that point, and their investors might want some money back.
Data brokers would love to get their grubby little hands on such data to add a layer to their profiles of poor black and brown kids.
This is a situation where FERPA, which is the federal child privacy law, is clearly not strong enough.
I'm not singling out Kinvolved for bad intentions; for all I know they mean well and they might even help some kids and families.
It is yet again data concerning the nation's most vulnerable population.
posts.key_insight: Well-intentioned educational technology still creates dangerous surveillance infrastructure over vulnerable communities when data protection laws are inadequate.

posts.summary: O'Neil reports from the CPDP conference in Brussels, where she's learning about privacy and data protection issues. She attended a panel on privacy and the world's poor, where she discovered an algorithm in Poland that sorts unemployed people—a system that is both opaque and high-impact, with information laws mysteriously not applying. She notes the stark contrast between European privacy protections and the weaker American framework, and expresses enthusiasm for learning how Europeans approach data protection enforcement.
key_points: An algorithm in Poland is being used to sort unemployed people, operating with total opacity despite its significant impact on people's lives
Open information laws don't apply to this Polish algorithm for 'inscrutable reasons,' highlighting how transparency rules often have convenient exceptions
European privacy and data protection laws are much stronger than American ones, with the UK being an exception
The conference focuses on software tools that investigate privacy and data protection in practice
Brussels embodies values O'Neil appreciates: pedestrian-dominated, unsanitized, with visible counterculture
money_quotes: As is typical of many of the algorithms I'm interested, it's both entirely opaque and high impact; the open information laws also don't apply for inscrutable reasons.
It's got the right values and it's not overly sanitized. Trump's got it wrong once again.
I can't help thinking that Brussels was created out of some indulgent American fantasy of Europe that confused Paris and Amsterdam and added a bunch of chocolate stores, beer, and waffles.
posts.key_insight: Algorithms that sort and classify vulnerable populations like the unemployed operate with opacity and immunity from transparency laws, making them particularly dangerous.

posts.summary: O'Neil endorses Adam Grant's NYT column arguing that forcing children into structured practice confuses correlation with causation—just because musicians practiced doesn't mean forced practice creates musicians. She advocates instead for teaching ethics and letting children find their own passions, noting that kids forced into music lessons rarely continue playing as adults. Drawing on her own experience growing up in competitive Lexington, Massachusetts, she observes that the most pressured children were often miserable and abandoned their instruments. She concludes by citing a Harvard study showing 80% of youth believe their parents prioritize achievement over caring, and argues that her approach of not forcing piano practice is precisely why her children understand what actually matters.
key_points: Forcing kids to practice instruments confuses correlation with causation—passion drives practice, not the reverse
Parents should focus on teaching ethics and then trust children to find their own interests
Children forced into displaying precocity are often miserable and abandon those activities as adults
80% of youth report their parents prioritize achievement or happiness over caring for others
Creativity isn't the primary goal—generosity and ethics matter more
money_quotes: That's confusing causation with correlation, the most common of all parental crimes.
Why would I pay for expensive lessons that they don't want? Why would I set myself up to remind them to practice when they could care less? It sounds like torture for everyone involved.
These kids were miserable, and they hated their violins and cellos.
I know, it's not a lot of evidence, but I still think I'm right, because it's parenting and people are totally irrational when it comes to this kind of thing.
If I had to choose, I'd say I want my kids to be generous and ethical.
The power and frequency of parents' daily messages about achievement and happiness are drowning out their messages about concern for others.
posts.key_insight: Teaching children ethics and trusting them to find their own passions produces better outcomes than forcing achievement-oriented activities that confuse correlation with causation.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the Flint water crisis was not bad luck but a direct result of suspending democracy through emergency management, which disproportionately affects poor communities of color. She criticizes media coverage that frames corporate water bottle donations as solutions while ignoring systemic causes. The real issue is decades of neglected water infrastructure, with federal funding cut by 80% since 1977 and an estimated $1 trillion needed for updates. O'Neil contends that infrastructure neglect functions as a punitive tax on the poor, and without addressing these root causes, similar crises will occur nationwide.
key_points: The water switch was made by an unelected emergency manager appointed by Governor Snyder, removing democratic accountability from those who endangered lives
Emergency management disproportionately targets municipalities with poor people of color, stripping their democratic powers
Federal funding for water infrastructure has been cut 80% since 1977, requiring $1 trillion to update crumbling systems
Corporate donations of water bottles and inadequate filters are band-aids that distract from systemic failures
Infrastructure neglect functions as a regressive tax on the poor, who live where pipes are oldest and most decrepit
money_quotes: Flint residents don't need water bottles, they need democracy.
The entire point of emergency management is to remove accountability from the actors who put people's lives in danger under the guise of saving money.
When we ignore infrastructure we are inviting yet another punitive tax on the poor, and as it happens, a life-long debilitating level of lead poisoning.
We just let it rot at the local level, in other words, and deal with it – or not – in the most expensive ways, when it's already an urgent situation.
Guess where the pipes are the oldest and most decrepit? You guessed it, where poor people live.
posts.key_insight: The Flint crisis wasn't a failure of infrastructure but a failure of democracy—when you remove elected accountability from governing decisions, the people who suffer are invariably the poor.

posts.summary: O'Neil announces her upcoming talk at Harvard's Data Privacy Lab, where she'll present a technical version of her 'Weapons of Math Destruction' thesis and propose technological tools for auditing opaque algorithms. She emphasizes measuring algorithm outputs rather than scrutinizing source code as an approach to understanding black-box systems. The post highlights her interest in meeting Latanya Sweeney, who as FTC chief technologist can advise on what tools regulators might actually adopt. O'Neil expresses a practical concern about developing useful tools rather than academic exercises.
key_points: Proposes examining black-box algorithms by measuring outputs rather than scrutinizing source code
Defines 'Weapons of Math Destruction' as opaque, widespread, and destructive algorithms
Seeks regulator input to ensure developed tools will actually be adopted
Notes a gap between what technologists can build and what regulators know is possible
money_quotes: I want to examine the question of how we understand a black-box algorithm in terms of measuring its outputs (as opposed to scrutinizing the source code).
I don't want to spend 4 years developing tools that nobody would use.
I have the impression that they generally speaking don't know what kind of tools are possible.
posts.key_insight: Effective algorithmic accountability requires bridging the gap between what technologists can build and what regulators know to ask for.

posts.summary: O'Neil challenges the common misconception that algorithms are inherently fair simply because they're mathematical, using a 'resting bitch face' detection algorithm as an example of hidden bias. However, she argues that algorithms have one significant advantage over human decision-making: they're auditable. Using the example of wildly inconsistent bankruptcy court rulings, she suggests that algorithmic processes, while not automatically fair, could at least be systematically tested and improved. She concludes that we urgently need to start auditing high-impact algorithms rather than blindly trusting them.
key_points: The claim that algorithms are objective simply because they're mathematical is false - modeler choices embed human biases
Bankruptcy courts show extreme inconsistency in rulings, with similar cases yielding vastly different outcomes based on which judge hears them
Unlike opaque human decision-making, algorithms are 'black boxes we can play with' - we can test inputs and observe outputs systematically
Current algorithms often serve those in power, contributing to phenomena like 'big data blacklisting'
The solution isn't to eliminate algorithms but to audit the widespread, high-impact ones
money_quotes: People who make their living writing and deploying algorithms like to boast that they are fair and objective simply because they are algorithmic and mathematical. That's bullshit, of course.
No algorithm is going to prove that without further inquiry.
Algorithms are not by their nature fair. But sometimes their specific brand of unfairness might still be an improvement, because it's at least measurable.
It would be a black box that we could play with.
Algorithms represent decision processes that are vulnerable to inspection more than most human-led processes are.
The current generation of algorithms often work for people in power.
We need to start auditing our algorithms, at least the ones that are widespread and high impact.
posts.key_insight: Algorithms aren't inherently fair, but unlike human decision-making, their unfairness is at least measurable and auditable—an advantage we're failing to exploit.

posts.summary: O'Neil announces her Slate essay 'The Ethical Data Scientist' which argues for bringing better ethics to data science practice. She highlights that ethics education should be integrated into data science training programs. She points to Berkeley's School of Information as a model, noting their undergraduate and graduate courses on data science ethics. The post serves as both promotion for her longer piece and a call for readers to share other ethics education initiatives in the field.
key_points: Data scientists need formal ethics education as part of their training
Berkeley's School of Information offers model courses on data science ethics at both undergraduate and graduate levels
The field needs more visibility into existing ethics education efforts
Ethics in data science is an emerging but underdeveloped area of focus
money_quotes: One thing I call for in the essay is the teaching of ethics to aspiring data scientists
posts.key_insight: Ethics education must become a standard component of data science training, not an afterthought.

posts.summary: This is a brief recommendation post where the author shares a new blog called 'Time for One More' that she finds inspiring. She connects it to her own ongoing project examining the relationship between elderly people and technology. The post serves primarily as a pointer to external content rather than presenting original analysis.
key_points: Recommends a blog called 'Time for One More' for its quality writing
Links the recommended blog to her research interest in elderly people and technology
References her previously announced project on big data, technology, and the elderly
money_quotes: Gorgeous writing, and an inspiration for my new project on thinking about the elderly and technology.
posts.key_insight: Quality writing about aging can inspire deeper thinking about how technology intersects with elderly populations.

posts.summary: O'Neil highlights the explosion of math enrichment programs for young people while pointing out their fundamental accessibility problem. She cites Atlantic data showing the U.S. has an 8-to-1 ratio of rich to poor math whizzes, far worse than other developed nations. The gains in advanced math achievement are almost entirely captured by children of the highly educated. She poses the challenge of how to democratize these programs when qualified teachers have lucrative alternatives. O'Neil suggests this may be a transitional problem that solves itself once enough 'fun math' speakers exist to train future generations.
key_points: Math enrichment programs have proliferated but remain expensive and inaccessible to low-income families
The U.S. has an 8-to-1 ratio of wealthy to poor advanced math students, compared to 3-to-1 in South Korea
Gains in advanced math achievement are almost entirely limited to children of highly educated parents
The percentage of low-income students reaching advanced math levels by high school 'rounds to zero'
The core tension: qualified math teachers have lucrative alternatives, making it hard to keep enrichment affordable
money_quotes: The ratio of rich math whizzes to poor ones is 3 to 1 in South Korea and 3.7 to 1 in Canada, to take two representative developed countries. In the U.S., it is 8 to 1.
By the end of high school, the percentage of low-income advanced-math learners rounds to zero.
The extreme elitism involved has, frankly, kept me from devoting myself to creating or running one of these programs.
The beauty of math is that you really only need a stick in the sand (and time, and a devoted teacher and ready students) to make it happen.
Once we have enough people who speak 'fun math,' there will be enough people to train the next generation.
posts.key_insight: America's math enrichment boom is widening inequality rather than closing it, with the benefits flowing almost exclusively to already-privileged children.

posts.summary: Mount St. Mary's president Simon Newman, a former private equity executive, fired two professors for disloyalty after they exposed his plan to manipulate college rankings. Newman wanted to 'cull' 20-25 freshmen before the enrollment deadline by using a student survey disguised as a personal motivation assessment. The goal was to boost the retention rate by 4-5% to improve US News & World Reports rankings. The scandal reveals how algorithmic ranking systems create perverse incentives that harm students, and raises questions about how widespread such gaming practices might be at other institutions.
key_points: A university president planned to use a deceptive student survey to identify freshmen to push out before enrollment deadlines, purely to game retention metrics
The survey was presented to students as measuring 'motivation, success, and happiness' with 'no wrong answers' while actually being used to identify students to expel
Professors who exposed the scheme were fired for 'disloyalty,' demonstrating how institutional power can silence whistleblowers
The US News & World Reports college ranking system creates incentives for schools to harm students rather than help them
The same tool (a survey algorithm) can be used for good or evil depending on institutional intent
money_quotes: Newman, a private equity asshole, got confused as to where he was working and decided to fire anyone who disagreed with him, referring to disloyalty as the cause.
This is hard for you because you think of the students as cuddly bunnies, but you can't. You just have to drown the bunnies.
A wonderful example of how an algorithm can be used for good or evil.
This gaming of the US News & World Reports model has got to stop, this shit is nuts.
It makes me wonder how many other places are doing stuff like this and not getting caught.
At least at this university the president was stupid enough to tell the professors the plan, right?
posts.key_insight: College ranking algorithms create perverse incentives that lead institutions to harm students in order to game metrics, and the same survey tools can be weaponized against the very people they claim to help.

posts.summary: P.J. Karafiol describes Math Circles of Chicago, a free math enrichment program he founded in 2010 to address elitism in math education. The program now serves 500 students across five locations in Chicago, including some of the city's poorest neighborhoods, focusing on collaborative problem-solving rather than lectures or competitions. The organization operates on a donor-supported 'NPR model' with no financial barriers to entry—just a handwritten essay about why students want to participate. Karafiol argues that for many students, math circle provides their only exposure to 'real mathematics' as opposed to arithmetic and rote procedures taught in schools.
key_points: Math Circles of Chicago is 100% free and donor-supported, serving 500 students in grades 5-12 across five locations including Chicago's poorest neighborhoods
The program focuses on collaborative problem-solving (graph theory, number theory, geometry) rather than lectures, competitions, or worksheets
The only admission requirement is a handwritten essay about why students want to do math—no academic prerequisites or fees
The organization sponsors Chicago's only youth math research symposium (QED) where 150+ students present original research
Karafiol distinguishes between 'arithmetic' taught in schools and 'real mathematics,' arguing most students never encounter the latter
money_quotes: Mathematics was what was in those books; the subject I hated in school was arithmetic.
For many of the kids we serve, math circle is the only exposure they get to what I (or my father) would call real mathematics.
Students have to write, by hand (no typing allowed!), a one-page essay about why they want to do math on Saturdays.
We've shown that math circles can exist (and thrive) outside of traditional university environments.
Our programs are currently growing faster than our fundraising–which is a great problem to have.
posts.key_insight: Making math enrichment accessible requires removing financial barriers entirely and meeting students in their own communities, not expecting them to come to elite institutions.

posts.summary: Dr. Mark Tomforde describes CHAMP, a math outreach program at the University of Houston that serves predominantly Black and Hispanic students from Third Ward and Sunnyside—among America's most dangerous and impoverished neighborhoods. The program brings high school students to campus twice weekly for Math Circle-style lessons and SAT/ACT tutoring, while connecting them with college student mentors who share similar backgrounds. Tomforde argues that mathematics is uniquely positioned as an equalizer because math skills are critical gatekeepers for high school graduation, college admission, and STEM success. He emphasizes that mathematical ability is equally distributed across populations, but opportunity is not—meaning countless potential Einsteins are lost to poverty and under-resourced schools. The piece calls for universities to break out of their bubbles and engage surrounding communities, framing this not as charity but as central to their educational mission.
key_points: CHAMP serves students from neighborhoods where 1 in 11 people are victims of violent crime annually, bringing them to a university campus for math enrichment and mentorship
Mathematics functions as a critical gatekeeper: it's often the biggest obstacle to high school graduation and a primary factor in college admissions and STEM success
Exposing students to college mentors who look like them and share their backgrounds addresses stereotype threat and provides crucial role models
Mathematical ability is equally distributed across populations, but opportunity is not—creating what Tomforde calls 'one of the greatest tragedies of the modern age'
Homogeneity in STEM is self-defeating: diverse perspectives and unconventional thinking are essential for actual problem-solving
money_quotes: Math ability is spread pretty much equally through the population, and we see there are almost no low-income, high-performing math students.

What if the next Einstein or the next Steve Jobs or the person capable of curing cancer is born in poverty and attends an under-resourced school in inner city America? Their potential contributions will most likely be lost.

We have created a system in which only a homogenous group of individuals ever have the chance to work on these problems. If we are actually interested in solving our problems, this just doesn't make sense.
This involvement in the local community is not ancillary to a university's educational mission, but rather central to it.
Too often universities exist within a bubble, disconnected from their surrounding community.

Quality mathematics education can improve high school graduation rates, help students get into a better college with more support, and improve the chances of success for students majoring in STEM fields.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical ability is equally distributed across all populations, but access to mathematical opportunity is not—making math education both a symptom of inequality and a uniquely powerful lever for addressing it.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares her family's experience rewatching Star Trek: Deep Space Nine and their fascination with Morn, a background character who appears consistently but never speaks. She appreciates how DS9 differs from other Trek series by featuring non-Federation characters without military training, providing relief from the sometimes excessive Academy worship. The post celebrates the show's civilian characters and culminates in her discovery of the Morn Watchers fan club, which she playfully offers to restart.
key_points: Deep Space Nine's fixed setting creates a different narrative dynamic focused on recurring tensions rather than exploration
The show's inclusion of non-Federation characters offers a refreshing break from Star Trek's sometimes over-the-top military academy culture
Morn is a compelling paradox: a consistently present character who never speaks on screen, yet other characters reference his talkativeness
The character's name is an anagram of Norm from Cheers, who inspired the role
money_quotes: That's the Star Trek series that you watch when you've recently watched all of Next Generation and Voyager and you're feeling desperate.
Now, it makes sense for soldiers to be trained, but this Star Trek Academy is really a cross between Harvard and West Point, and the worshipping of the culture there can be really over the top.
So it's nice to see Star Trek characters that have nothing to do with the military. They're just hanging out.
And although by the second season or so Morn shows up very consistently, he never, ever speaks. Not once.
Anyway, if there are any other Morn Watchers out there, I'm up for restarting the fan club. This can be taken as the initial newsletter.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most compelling characters are the ones who simply exist in a space without demanding attention or advancing plots.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares two pieces of personal news: the release of her book cover for 'Weapons of Math Destruction' and an upcoming live performance with her bluegrass band. She playfully obsesses over her book's Amazon ranking despite promising herself she wouldn't, finding humor in the absurdity of ranking metrics. The post serves as both a book pre-order announcement and an invitation to see her band play at a Brooklyn venue.
key_points: The book cover for 'Weapons of Math Destruction' has been publicly released
O'Neil admits to tracking Amazon rankings despite her self-promise not to, showing self-aware humor about metrics obsession
Her bluegrass band, the Tomtown Ramblers, is playing their first public venue gig
The post reveals O'Neil's life outside data science criticism - she plays music and values community
