money_quotes: I'm proud to say that, more than six months before release date, it is the 714th best selling book in the category of statistical books inside of Educational & Reference books inside Business & Money books!
It's also the 860,121th best selling book overall. Considering there are more than 129 million books overall, that's pretty fucking amazing.
Maybe my book will be the 130 millionth book of all time.
Think of it as a church alternative, but with booze.
Our harmonies are amazing and we err on the side of murder ballads.
posts.key_insight: Even someone who critiques algorithmic metrics for a living can't resist obsessing over their own Amazon ranking, revealing the universal human vulnerability to quantified validation.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts a dream about Archie Bunker being elected president, drawing an implicit parallel to the 2016 election cycle. She explores the paradox that Norman Lear created Archie Bunker to be disliked, yet audiences embraced him as lovable and relatable. The post examines how views considered 'laughably old-fashioned' in the 1970s have resurfaced in mainstream politics. O'Neil questions whether the 'Archie Bunker for president' concept was ever truly a parody, suggesting it reflected genuine political sentiment all along.
key_points: Norman Lear intended Archie Bunker to be strongly disliked, but audiences loved him instead—he was voted TV's #1 character in 2005
Bunker's bigotry was excused because he was portrayed as hardworking, loving, and 'basically decent' with views that were 'merely a product' of his environment
There was an actual 'Archie Bunker voting bloc' in 1972 and a parody presidential campaign, suggesting the archetype has real political power
The post implicitly connects this 1970s character to the rise of Trump in 2016, questioning how much has really changed
money_quotes: He had brought along all of his racist, misogynistic, antisemitic, and homophobic thoughts and mannerisms.
The opinions expressed by the title character were laughably old-fashioned *at the time*.
Show creator Norman Lear originally intended that Bunker be strongly disliked by audiences. It didn't work.

Rather than being motivated by malice, he is portrayed as hardworking, a loving father and husband, as well as a basically decent man whose views are merely a product of the era and working-class environment in which he has been raised.
I guess my question is, how much of a parody is this really?
As a child, I didn't get why people liked Archie Bunker.
posts.key_insight: The 'lovable bigot' archetype that was supposed to be satirical has always reflected a real and powerful political constituency that mainstream culture repeatedly underestimates.

posts.summary: The post argues that neoliberalism, the dominant ideology of the past 40 years, is finally being challenged on multiple fronts. O'Neil points to the failures in Flint and Ferguson as natural consequences of neoliberal policies that blame individuals for systemic failures. She finds hope in the failure of social impact bonds, voter rejection of corporatist politics, and the repeal of No Child Left Behind as signs that people are recognizing the limits of market-based solutions to social problems. The post concludes by contrasting two possible futures: continued decline or governments reclaiming their responsibilities from market forces.
key_points: Neoliberalism equates financial success with moral worth, blaming individuals rather than systems when 'boats don't rise'
Social impact bonds and privatized solutions to inequality are fundamentally flawed because private companies will always choose profitability over comprehensive solutions
Some services—education, economic opportunity—must be provided by government because markets will cherry-pick profitable segments
The repeal of No Child Left Behind represents acknowledgment that running institutions like businesses with naive metrics trivializes difficult problems
Political chaos, while ugly, shows voters rejecting standard SuperPAC-funded corporatist politics
money_quotes: When nothing trickles down, when boats don't rise, here is the explanation that follows: it is not the system that it is at fault, but the character of those people who failed to prosper in it.
We shouldn't expect a neoliberalized private sector economy to address problems that stem from inequality.
If you find yourself trying to financialize things like incarcerated teens and early childhood education for poor kids, you're likely not going to see the point of long-term investments.

There are some things that government should actually provide to everyone, like education and economic opportunity, where private companies will always want to pick and choose who will be more profitable.
Educational institutions do not benefit from being run like businesses, and naive metrics of success when it comes to truly difficult problems only serve to distract and trivialize.

They pick themselves up off their collective asses, take their responsibilities seriously – and in particular take the economic reins from central bankers – and start providing the services, infrastructure, progressive tax systems, and opportunities that their constituents need.
posts.key_insight: Market-based solutions cannot solve problems rooted in inequality because private interests will always prioritize profitability over comprehensive social good.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil enthusiastically recommends her friend Ernie Davis's book 'Mathematics, Substance and Surmise,' a collection of essays exploring the ontology of mathematics from multiple perspectives. She admits she hasn't finished it yet but couldn't contain her excitement. The book covers topics ranging from mathematical collaboration to computer-assisted proofs to the fundamental question of whether mathematical objects exist. O'Neil connects it to her own philosophical musings about truth and precision, using her provocative claim that 'the sun goes around the earth' as an example of how statements can be imprecise yet true. She closes with a genuine invitation for the book's contributors to come discuss mathematics at her home.
key_points: The book is a collection of essays on mathematical ontology written by multiple authors, edited by Ernie Davis and his father
It explores how mathematical collaboration has changed, how computers have become partners in mathematical discovery, and whether mathematical objects truly exist
O'Neil connects the book to her theory that precision and truth are not the same thing - a less precise statement isn't necessarily wrong
The essays provide substantive mathematical context while prompting readers to think differently about foundational questions
O'Neil's enthusiasm is so genuine she extends an open dinner invitation to all the book's contributors
money_quotes: Bloggers are notoriously bad at impulse control
What does it mean to 'know' a formula for the 4th power of pi is true without having a proof for it?
Is the number 2 a noun or an adjective? Is mathematics about the world or only about itself?

It's imprecise yet true, and that when someone corrects me and tells me the earth goes around the sun, that becomes another statement which is of course more precise and accurate statement but still not entirely precise and accurate!
So why am I wrong and they're right? What do humans mean when they say something's right, anyway?
The book consistently makes you think differently, while also giving you substantive and grounded mathematical context in which to do your thinking
posts.key_insight: Mathematical truth and precision are not synonymous - a statement can be imprecise yet true, raising fundamental questions about what it means to be 'right' about anything.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the Apple vs. FBI encryption debate is fundamentally misdirected. Rather than celebrating Apple as a privacy hero standing up to government overreach, we should be asking why our government isn't protecting our privacy in the first place. Apple is a profit-seeking corporation accountable to shareholders, not citizens, and will comply with laws when forced. The real conversation should be about demanding democratic accountability from government institutions that are making unilateral decisions about privacy trade-offs without public input.
key_points: Framing Apple as 'good' and government as 'bad' concedes too much and oversimplifies a complex democratic problem
Apple is accountable to laws and shareholders, not citizens—they cannot be privacy heroes even if they wanted to be
The FBI is making internal calculations about privacy trade-offs without measuring or communicating the risks to the public
Citizens should demand a government that acknowledges its duty to protect privacy while investigating risks
The focus on Apple's operating systems and technical details is a sideshow distracting from the real democratic governance issues
money_quotes: We are reduced to relying on an enormous, profit seeking corporation to make a stand for our rights.
Apple is not accountable to us. They are a private company.
They are not heroes, because they cannot be, even if they wanted to be.
Right now the FBI is falling far short, trivializing the risk to the rest of us when backdoors are created and used at scale.
They have made an internal calculation that the trade-offs are well worth the risks, without really having a conversation with the public in which they even measure the risks.
And those risks are our risks.
Let's stop talking about Apple and start talking about democracy in the era of big data.
posts.key_insight: When we rely on corporations to protect our rights instead of demanding democratic accountability from government, we've already lost the more important battle.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her role as lead plaintiff in a class-action lawsuit challenging New York State's sales tax on tampons and other menstrual products. She frames the tax as discriminatory since it treats essential hygiene products as non-necessities. The suit was developed by her friend Laura Strausfeld, a Columbia Law School graduate who crafted the legal theory. This brief announcement signals O'Neil's willingness to move from critique to direct legal action on issues of gender equity.
key_points: O'Neil is taking direct legal action as lead plaintiff rather than just writing about the issue
The lawsuit challenges the classification of tampons as taxable luxury items rather than tax-exempt necessities
The legal theory was developed by Laura Strausfeld, demonstrating collaboration between activists and legal experts
This represents the intersection of gender equity, tax policy, and systemic discrimination
money_quotes: Exciting news, today I can officially announce myself as a lead plaintiff on the New York State class-action tampon tax case.
I've been talking about this unfair tax for years with my friend and Columbia Law School JD Laura Strausfeld, who developed the legal theory for this class-action suit.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes challenging systemic unfairness requires moving beyond analysis and commentary to direct legal action.

posts.summary: O'Neil announces her role as lead plaintiff in a lawsuit challenging New York State's tampon tax, noting Governor Cuomo's quick response supporting repeal. She argues that male squeamishness about menstruation is precisely why such unfair policies persist. While celebrating the tax lawsuit as progress, she pushes further: tampons should be freely accessible like toilet paper, especially for poor and homeless women who can't even buy them with food stamps. The post uses humor and directness to highlight how absurd it is that a basic hygiene product is taxed as a luxury and unavailable in most public restrooms.
key_points: Male squeamishness about menstruation enables discriminatory policies to persist unchallenged
The tampon tax repeal is progress but insufficient - the real goal should be free accessibility
If men menstruated, tampons would already be free and ubiquitous like toilet paper
Homeless and poor women are most harmed by current policies, including inability to buy menstrual products with food stamps
The classification of tampons as non-medical or unnecessary despite involving blood is absurd
money_quotes: One thing I've figured out through all this is that the squeamishness alone – exhibited mostly by men – is a large part of why the unfair tax exists in the first place.
It's like, if we don't think about it, it won't exist.
If men had periods, I've always said, then tampons would be free; or at least as free as toilet paper.

I've spent countless hours and dollars desperately locating a tampon in the middle of a conference or workday, because there are very few bathrooms that bother to supply these cheap little wads of cotton balls.
Right now you can't even purchase them (or pads) with food stamps. They are somehow considered unnecessary and/or non-medical, even though they directly concern blood.
Let's not stop until they are freely accessible, especially to poor women, and *especially* especially to homeless women.
posts.key_insight: Male discomfort with menstruation creates policy blind spots that harm women, and eliminating the tampon tax is just the first step toward treating menstrual products as the basic necessity they are.

posts.summary: O'Neil announces her upcoming talk at QCon London on algorithm auditing, using recidivism modeling as a timely case study. She notes the irony that the talk preceding hers will discuss the very type of algorithmic system that desperately needs auditing. The post surveys current tools for algorithmic auditing, including the FairTest toolkit, while highlighting a critical gap: these tools struggle when protected attributes like race and income aren't explicitly included in datasets. She suggests proxy methods using zip codes and names to infer race, but acknowledges the significant limitations.
key_points: Recidivism algorithms that influence sentencing decisions urgently need auditing given systemic racism and class bias in the justice system
Current auditing tools like FairTest can detect bias but require explicit protected attributes (race, income) to function properly
When protected attributes are missing from datasets, auditors must resort to proxy inference methods using zip codes and surnames
The technical challenge of auditing algorithms is compounded by data availability problems that many modelers face
money_quotes: Given how deeply racist and anti-poor our justice system is, it's a big question whether or how data-driven studies or algorithms can improve it.
The need for auditing algorithms could not be more front and center.

If you have a dataset showing the history of a bunch of prisoners, their recidivism scores, and their subsequent sentencing lengths, you'd like to know whether the algorithms was biased against blacks or against poor people.
But if you don't have the column 'race' or 'income,' it's a lot harder to do that analysis.
Not all modelers even have that, so it gets tricky pretty fast.
posts.key_insight: Auditing algorithms for bias is technically possible but practically constrained by the frequent absence of protected attributes in the very datasets we need to examine.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts refusing to switch airplane seats so an orthodox Jewish man wouldn't have to sit near women, connecting this incident to a broader personal transformation. She describes how her recent loss of desire to have more children fundamentally changed her relationship with men—she no longer unconsciously accommodates them or forgives offensive behavior from attractive people. The piece is a candid reflection on how biological drives she wasn't even aware of had been shaping her social interactions for years. She concludes that this hormonal shift has made her more clear-eyed about people, judging them by what they say rather than their charm or attractiveness.
key_points: Refusing to accommodate sexist religious practices on an airplane prompted deeper self-reflection about accommodation patterns
The desire for children created an unconscious bias toward forgiving and accommodating attractive men
Losing that biological drive felt like losing 'a sense of smell' that had been distorting her perception of people
She now evaluates people based on their words and ideas rather than their charm or physical appeal
The change is described as subtle but profound—she's 'immune to the charm of beautiful people' while still appreciating wit, intelligence, and empathy
money_quotes: I think it has to do with the fact that I no longer want to have children.
There was something in my head that I didn't even recognize until it was gone, something that made me size up every man I met as a potential baby daddy.
They honestly could get away with saying dumb or even offensive things around me and I'd instantly forgive them. I didn't even notice doing it. It's like I was drunk on their youth.
The moment I stopped wanting kids, I stopped accommodating men.
It's like I've lost a sense of smell, but one that distracted me from observing people in other ways.
I have become immune to the charm of beautiful people.
To me it's unreasonable to interrupt a bunch of women's lives to accommodate a man, especially when we're trying to get some goddamn work done.
posts.key_insight: Unconscious biological drives can shape our social behavior and judgments in ways we don't recognize until those drives disappear.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that free trade agreements have been sold to Americans by emphasizing cheaper consumer goods while ignoring the devastating impact on American workers, jobs, and wages. She endorses Jared Bernstein's critique that globalization treats Americans as consumers rather than workers. While acknowledging that free trade benefits workers in developing countries like China and Bangladesh, she frames it as a win-lose rather than win-win situation for American workers. She calls on economists to honestly acknowledge the damage done to American workers and warns that failure to address these grievances fuels support for Trump and similar politicians.
key_points: Free trade defenders focus on low prices for consumers while ignoring job losses, weakened unions, and declining wages for workers
Trade agreements facilitate offshoring jobs to countries with lower wages, creating a win-lose dynamic where developing country workers gain and American workers lose
The economic framing of Americans as 'consumers' rather than 'workers' obscures the real costs of globalization
Both Trump and Bernie Sanders supporters are motivated by legitimate grievances about free trade's impact
Economists and policymakers need to honestly acknowledge these harms and develop mitigation strategies
money_quotes: The defense of globalization rests on viewing Americans primarily as consumers, not workers, based on the assumption that we care more about low prices than about low wages.
When you hear someone talk about free trade, you can count on them to talk about cheaper products but to sidestep all the American jobs that have been lost through free trade agreements.
It's not a win-win situation; it's more of a win-lose, where Chinese workers win huge and American workers lose medium.
Free trade means we enrich other countries with opportunities that they're hungry for and are willing to do for less.
The people who have been damaged or threatened by free trade in this country are many; possibly a majority.
They have a real gripe, and they don't see, and possibly don't care about the benefits to Chinese workers that their suffering represents, and they're super pissed off.
posts.key_insight: Free trade has been framed as benefiting everyone through lower prices, but this framing deliberately obscures that American workers—not just consumers—bear real costs that fuel dangerous political backlash.

posts.summary: Tom Adams presented to the Alt Banking group his analysis of the imminent collapse of NYC's luxury real estate market. Drawing parallels to the oil industry's oversupply crisis, he explains how easy credit fueled overdevelopment of luxury condos without regard for actual demand. The market is particularly vulnerable because ultra-luxury real estate isn't a necessity commodity but more like art, dependent on a finite pool of ultra-wealthy buyers who have largely already made their purchases. With thousands of units hitting the market and sales declining, developer defaults are beginning and the bubble appears ready to burst. The crash could ultimately benefit ordinary New Yorkers by forcing a conversation about housing priorities.
key_points: Easy post-crisis credit created oversupply in luxury housing, mirroring the oil industry's fracking boom and subsequent collapse
The luxury real estate market is opaque and slow-moving compared to commodities, with brokers managing information leaks to protect their interests
There are 520 units listed over $10 million but only 177 such apartments sold in 2015, indicating severe oversupply
International buyers who profited from oil and commodities are a shrinking pool due to falling oil prices and China's economic problems
Developer defaults and rental conversions are already beginning, potentially creating opportunities for affordable housing advocacy
money_quotes: Instead of seeing the economic law of supply and demand kicking in, however, we saw the opposite, at least temporarily: companies that were in need of cash to pay their creditors regularly pumped as much oil as possible to make their payments, resulting in even more supply and the collapse of the market.

While ultra luxury condos are a 'hard asset' they aren't really a commodity: no one 'needs' a $20 million condo like they need oil or wheat. It's really more like art, where the market is whimsical and changes when a particular collector or two die or lose interest.
If you count the number of very rich people who are on the market for Manhattan apartments that cost more than $100 million, or even $10 million, you'll soon realize they mostly already bought them.

Everyone and their uncle wanted a piece of the luxury real estate market, which has been a gold mine for developers for years, to the point where they've been building like crazy without looking around them for indications to pause.

The dynamic of success has made land prices so high that it's become unattractive to build affordable housing, and moreover the labor with which the luxury apartment building is built is non-union to save on costs.

The impending luxury housing crash should be an opportunity for community groups to demand a new conversation about what land and housing are supposed to be for, hopefully convincing city officials that they represent all New Yorkers, not just the super rich.
posts.key_insight: The NYC luxury real estate bubble, inflated by easy credit and speculation rather than actual demand, is poised to burst in ways that could finally force a reckoning about housing priorities for ordinary New Yorkers.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil pushes back against a parenting advice column that frames teenager communication styles as the problem in family conflicts. She argues that teenagers who 'refuse to engage' are often responding rationally to parents who have previously shut them down for expressing opinions. O'Neil contends that difficult teenagers are frequently the product of difficult parents who can't handle being challenged. She celebrates teenagers as brutally honest critics and advises parents to listen rather than dismiss.
key_points: Parenting advice typically focuses on teenage behavior while ignoring parental contributions to conflict
Teenagers who refuse to engage are often responding rationally to being previously shut down
Parents frequently struggle with being challenged more than teenagers struggle with authority
Teenagers serve as honest critics who point out hypocrisies that adults have learned to politely ignore
The solution is for parents to listen to uncomfortable truths rather than discipline dissent
money_quotes: Parents regularly attack and/or discipline their children for thinking differently, having dissenting voices, and generally speaking trying to establish their independence.
Parents think that they have difficult teenagers, but the reverse is just as often the case: teenagers have difficult parents.
Teenagers are easier to deal with if for parents who like their views challenged and who can react positively to having their hypocrisies pointed out to them.
That's what teenagers do, and god bless them, they are the most honest critics in the world.
By the time they're grown up they've learned to lie to make people feel better, but I'd rather spend time with a teenager any day of the week.
Listen to your teenager, because he or she is probably telling you something honest and true, in fact something so honest and so true that you can barely recognize it and it sometimes hurts.
Don't kill the messenger.
posts.key_insight: When teenagers shut down or refuse to engage, they're often responding rationally to parents who can't tolerate being challenged—the problem isn't difficult teenagers but difficult parents.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that data, not money, is the true currency of the digital age, explaining how Google, Facebook, and Amazon dominate their markets by controlling vast datasets about users. She examines Amazon's new patent for selfie-based payment authentication as a strategic move to acquire facial recognition data that Facebook already possesses. The post connects this corporate data grab to broader surveillance implications, warning that widespread facial recognition could make location tracking ubiquitous. O'Neil concludes with a rare call to action, urging readers to resist this particular form of data collection before it becomes normalized.
key_points: Data is the true currency of the digital age - tech giants dominate not because of customer volume but because of data extraction capabilities
Google's data monopoly is genuinely anti-competitive because competitors cannot access equivalent user data, regardless of service quality
Amazon's selfie payment patent is a strategic play to acquire facial recognition data that currently only Facebook possesses
Facial recognition technology could eliminate the need for GPS tracking - faces alone would be sufficient to track people through video surveillance
Users actively help build these surveillance systems by labeling photos and providing training data
money_quotes: When you think of currency in the digital age, stop thinking money, not even Bitcoin. Start thinking about data.
We're not, we're just its product, or rather our data is.
It's not simply a question of how often we use their services, it's a question of how much data they can extract from us while we do so.
This is a pure play for a new dataset, that so far only Facebook has had access to.
You want to empty your shopping cart? You'll have to give us more data, thanks.
So far the facial recognition technology isn't very good, but it could get better fast if we take a selfie video every time we buy a can of coffee online.
If that happens, we won't need the GPS in our phones to give away our locations, because just by having faces we will be doing enough.
I've resigned myself to lots of data collection, but this selfie stuff is going too far.
posts.key_insight: Tech companies frame new authentication features as conveniences, but they're actually mechanisms for extracting valuable biometric data that expands corporate and government surveillance capabilities.

posts.summary: This is a brief introductory note by Cathy O'Neil announcing a guest post written by her 7-year-old son Wolfie about his broken leg. The post itself contains minimal content beyond the introduction, serving primarily as a framing device for her son's contribution. O'Neil mentions Wolfie's recent accomplishment of learning to bike.
key_points: O'Neil occasionally features guest posts from family members, including her young children
The post documents a personal family milestone (Wolfie learning to bike)
This represents a departure from O'Neil's typical algorithmic critique content
money_quotes: Today's guest post is by Wolfie O'Neil, my 7-year-old son
posts.key_insight: This brief post serves as a personal interlude in O'Neil's typically analytical blog, showcasing the human side behind the data scientist persona.

posts.summary: O'Neil reviews the Flint Water Advisory Task Force Report, which she finds compelling in its assignment of blame to Michigan's state government and emergency management system. She highlights how the report frames the crisis as environmental injustice—not requiring malicious intent, but resulting from systems that failed to protect a predominantly Black, impoverished community. The report draws parallels to historical medical abuses of African Americans, including the Tuskegee syphilis study. O'Neil endorses the report's conclusion that emergency management prioritized fiscal concerns over public health, stripping residents of democratic participation in decisions affecting their lives.
key_points: The Flint crisis demonstrates environmental injustice occurs through systemic failure, not necessarily malicious intent
Michigan's emergency management system prioritized budget concerns over public health in a majority-Black community
The report explicitly connects Flint to historical medical abuses of African Americans like Tuskegee
Environmental justice requires both fair treatment AND meaningful public participation in government decisions
Residents under emergency management were denied democratic input into decisions that poisoned them
money_quotes: Environmental injustice is not about malevolent intent or deliberate attacks on specific populations, nor does it come in measures that overtly violate civil rights.

Flint residents, who are majority Black or African American and among the most impoverished of any metropolitan area in the United States, did not enjoy the same degree of protection from environmental and health hazards as that provided to other communities.
By virtue of their being subject to emergency management, Flint residents were not provided equal access to, and meaningful involvement in, the government decision-making process.
Measuring blood lead levels without removing the sources of lead from the environment appears the equivalent of using Flint's children (and adults) as human bioassays.
Among African American seniors, the protracted Flint water crisis echoes the tragic Tuskegee syphilis study.
posts.key_insight: Environmental injustice doesn't require racist intent—it results from systems that strip vulnerable communities of democratic participation while failing to protect their health.

posts.summary: O'Neil traces the predictable lifecycle of metrics in education reform: from well-intentioned measurement to punitive accountability system that fails to address the original problem. She uses standardized testing as her primary example, showing how tests meant to identify struggling schools instead became tools to punish them. Now she sees the same pattern emerging with 'grit' metrics, where Angela Duckworth's research on resilience is being transformed into scorecards for children. O'Neil warns that despite Duckworth's own cautions about misuse, the metric will likely follow the same destructive trajectory. The post is a preemptive strike against yet another measurement system that will blame individuals for systemic failures.
key_points: Metrics follow a predictable lifecycle: invented to understand a problem, they become tools of punishment rather than support, ultimately failing to solve the original issue
Standardized testing was meant to identify schools needing help but instead became a punitive system that blamed teachers for poverty-correlated outcomes
Rich students' scores have improved faster than poor students' despite decades of 'accountability' measures
Grit metrics, despite their creator's warnings against misuse, are poised to repeat the same destructive pattern
The fundamental problem is treating low scores as moral failings rather than indicators of where resources are needed
money_quotes: Instead of seeing a 'bad score' as something that indicates a need for more resources and support, it becomes a moral failing: take responsibility for your terrible score and do something about it.
The fact that the scores were extremely correlated to poverty was explained away by saying our teachers had 'given up on poor students.'

Thus began the era of high stakes testing for students and teachers, where teachers were 'held accountable' for their students' progress on standardized tests, if not their scores. It's purely punitive, and a far cry from the original purpose of helping out those who need it.
The rich students, to put it bluntly, are improving faster than the poor ones.
The measurement isn't perfect, and everyone admits that, but nevertheless nobody argues against using it, since knowing something is better than knowing nothing.
Let's not go there.
posts.key_insight: Well-intentioned metrics inevitably become punitive accountability tools that blame individuals for systemic problems, and grit scores for children will be no exception.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that while the FBI eventually accessed the San Bernardino shooter's phone through an expensive, unscalable technical process, the real issue remains unresolved. No legal precedent was set, and Apple continues to strengthen encryption by default. She contends that framing this as an 'Apple victory' misses the point: a private corporation is now making de facto privacy policy for Americans without democratic input. O'Neil calls for a genuine public conversation about what types of data the government should access, rather than leaving these crucial decisions to corporate discretion.
key_points: The FBI's method of accessing the phone was expensive and technically complex, making it difficult to scale to widespread surveillance
No legal precedent was established, leaving the fundamental encryption debate unresolved
Apple is unilaterally setting privacy policy through technical decisions, which is anti-democratic regardless of whether we agree with their stance
Obama's 'Swiss Bank account' analogy falsely equates all personal data and relies on the flawed 'nothing to hide' argument
The public deserves a nuanced discussion distinguishing between types of data the government may legitimately access
money_quotes: Apple is not accountable to the public in any real way; Tim Cook could change his mind tomorrow about whether to care about consumer security and we wouldn't be able to do anything about it.
It's Apple vs. everyone, and Apple is feeling pretty good about its chances.
I happen to agree with Apple this time. But it's a screwed up and tenuous situation, and it's deeply anti-democratic.

We haven't actually had the urgently needed conversation about whether Americans have the right to encrypted communication. Instead, we're relying on a private company to make de facto policy for our benefit.
The government may claim access to our financial information, for tax purposes, but should never have access to our love letters.
Obama's comparing the individual's desire for privacy with a Swiss Bank account is a smear tactic.
posts.key_insight: Letting a private corporation make de facto encryption policy—even when we agree with the outcome—is anti-democratic and sidesteps the urgent public conversation Americans need to have about digital privacy rights.

posts.summary: This post offers four quick observations spanning consumer marketing, surveillance, workplace gamification, and autonomous vehicles. O'Neil highlights an upcoming conference on racial bias in government surveillance while dismissing a conference that uses gamification rhetoric to extract data from workers. She critiques how 'millennial preferences' are really just adaptations to economic precarity. The post concludes with concerns about self-driving cars potentially enabling wasteful vehicle usage when cars can run errands without passengers.
key_points: Government surveillance disproportionately impacts poor minority neighborhoods, making it a present-day crisis rather than a future dystopia
Corporate rhetoric about millennials 'liking' gamification ignores that they've simply adapted to economic conditions of debt and job insecurity
Workplace games are surveillance tools that generate millions of data points for algorithmic HR decisions
Self-driving cars could dramatically increase wasteful car usage by enabling empty vehicles to run errands
money_quotes: Anyone worried about the dystopian future of government surveillance should learn about what's happening right now to poor minority neighborhoods.
Typically, conflates what millennials 'like' with what they've had to put up with because they were born into a world of enormous student debt and terrible job security.
Serious games can generate up to millions of data points that can then be fed into machine-learning algorithms to help employers make smart HR decisions to win the war for talent.
If I can go to sleep while I'm in my car and it's driving to Lexington, Virginia, what will stop me from visiting my buddy Aaron every weekend?
Why even be in the car while it's going somewhere? I can send my car to do errands I don't want to do.
posts.key_insight: Technologies marketed as conveniences or preferences often mask surveillance mechanisms and ignore the economic coercion that drives adoption.

posts.summary: This guest post by Maxwell Feiner, a high school student, introduces three free online math tools he uses regularly. Brilliant.org provides challenging problems that require deeper thinking beyond standard formulas. Desmos offers an interactive graphing calculator with powerful features like adjustable variables and animations. WolframAlpha serves as a computational engine for graphing and solving various mathematical queries. The post includes practical examples and tutorials for each tool.
key_points: Brilliant.org functions as a social platform for challenging math problems that require insight beyond standard classroom methods
Desmos allows users to create interactive graphs with adjustable variables, sliders, animations, and movable points
WolframAlpha can handle 1-D, 2-D, and 3-D graphing plus a wide range of computational queries
All three tools are free to use and accessible to students
money_quotes: Think of it as a social site for math.
I like the problems a lot because they require insight beyond what is taught in school classes.
They normally cannot be solved using one formula or pre-learned method, but instead require deeper thought and a combination of different concepts in order to be solved.
It is simple to use, but at the same time very powerful.
This is just barely scratching the surface of what can be done with the powerful tool.
posts.key_insight: Free online tools like Brilliant, Desmos, and WolframAlpha can significantly enhance math learning by providing challenging problems and powerful computational aids that go beyond traditional classroom resources.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that breaking up big banks is not a technical problem but a political one. She outlines numerous policy mechanisms that could reduce bank size, from progressive capital requirements and trading restrictions to outright nationalization. After nearly five years attending Alt Banking meetings, she concludes that the real obstacle isn't a lack of viable plans but a lack of political will to implement them. She defends Bernie Sanders against criticism that his bank breakup plans lack specifics, arguing that political will is the crucial missing ingredient.
key_points: Multiple policy mechanisms exist to shrink big banks: progressive capital requirements, size limits, trading restrictions, reserve requirements, Glass-Steagall revival, or nationalization
The problem is not a shortage of plans but a shortage of political will to implement any of them
Banks could be given incentives to shrink themselves or simply be legally prohibited from exceeding certain sizes
Criticisms of Sanders for lacking specific bank breakup plans miss the point—political will matters more than technical details
Once political will exists, experts who study these issues can work out the implementation details
money_quotes: There are lots of ways to break up the big banks.
Progressive taxes that max out at 100% profit when the bank is as big as Bank of America.
We could give banks 4 years to get compliant.
We could go the other way entirely, and nationalize one of the big banks, or all of them, and let them remain big but treat them like utilities, and make them super duper boring.
What we do have a shortage of, though, is political will.
When I hear people complaining about Sanders not have a specific enough plan for breaking up the big banks, I think it's hogwash.
What Sanders has, which is sorely needed, in fact is the crucial missing ingredient to any discussion around big banks, is the political will to do something.
Once the political will is there, the details can be sorted out by people who think about this stuff all the time.
posts.key_insight: The barrier to breaking up big banks isn't a lack of policy options—it's the absence of political will to pursue any of them.

posts.summary: O'Neil addresses the debate about whether algorithms can be 'racist,' acknowledging the technically valid point that algorithms merely reflect their training data. However, she argues that since the tech industry aggressively markets AI as human-like and intelligent, it's fair game to describe algorithmic outputs in human terms like 'racist.' She concludes that regardless of terminology, companies like Google bear responsibility for monitoring and correcting discriminatory outputs, even when there's no racist intent in the engineering.
key_points: Algorithms are mirrors that reflect the biases in their training data, not intentionally racist agents
The tech industry consistently anthropomorphizes AI through marketing imagery of human brains and bodies
If we market AI as human-like, we shouldn't be surprised when people describe its behavior in human terms
Companies have a responsibility to monitor for discriminatory outputs and correct them, regardless of intent
The Google image search results for 'unprofessional hairstyles' revealing predominantly Black women's hair is a clear example of algorithmic bias in action
money_quotes: I'll stop calling algorithms racist when you stop anthropomorphizing AI
Algorithms are simply mirroring back to us what we've fed them as training data, and in that sense they are no more racist than any other mirror.
The algorithm isn't (intentionally) racist, because it's not intentionally anything.
We are marketing AI as if it's human. And since we do that, we are treating it and reacting to it as quasi-humans would.
When it seems racist, we're going to say the AI is racist. And I think that, all things considered, it's fair to do this, even though there's no intention there.

Even though I do not suspect any Google employee of making their algorithms prone to this kind of problem, I still think they should have an internal team that's on the look-out for this kind of thing and address it.
I know I'm anthropomorphizing the google algorithms when I talk about them like children, but what can I say, I am a sucker for marketing.
posts.key_insight: If the tech industry insists on marketing AI as intelligent and human-like, it forfeits the right to complain when people hold those systems to human standards of accountability for discriminatory outcomes.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that advance review copies (galleys) of her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' are now available, ahead of the September 6th publication date. She shares a photo of the proof copies she received and jokes about immediately celebrating with drinks. The post serves as both a book promotion and a lighthearted personal update, with links to pre-order from Amazon and Barnes & Noble.
key_points: Galleys are preliminary versions sent to reviewers and people who might provide blurbs or publicity
The actual book won't be available until September 6th
O'Neil has proof copies available to give away to visitors
The post captures a personal milestone moment in the book publication process
money_quotes: This pic is blurry because I immediately started drinking upon receiving the packages.
I know, I know, it's mean of me to show you this when the actual book isn't available until September 6th, but what can I say, I'm kind of mean.
Plus, if you come over I'll give you one, so I'm not that mean.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted announcement marking a personal milestone in the publication of what would become an influential critique of algorithmic bias.

posts.summary: O'Neil analyzes the Chicago Police Department Task Force Report, highlighting data that reveals systematic racial bias in policing. The report documents how young black males are stopped at rates nearly equal to their total population, while whites are only stopped when contraband probability is much higher. Despite massive numbers of complaints against officers, 40% go uninvestigated and only 7% are sustained. The post excerpts key findings showing that community distrust of CPD is justified by substantial evidence, the police accountability system is fundamentally broken, and collective bargaining agreements have codified the 'code of silence' into official policy.
key_points: Black and young Chicago residents are disproportionately stopped by police, with young black males stopped at rates nearly matching their total population
Whites are only stopped when the probability of finding contraband is much higher, revealing a clear double standard
The police accountability system fails at every level: 40% of complaints uninvestigated, only 7% sustained, and discipline rarely imposed
Police union contracts actively obstruct accountability by allowing officers to wait 24 hours before statements, confer with others, and amend statements after viewing evidence
The oversight agency IPRA has been run by former law enforcement who allowed findings to be reversed without record
money_quotes: The community's lack of trust in CPD is justified.
The total number of stops for young black males is nearly as large as the population of young black males.
Whites are only stopped when the probability of 'finding contraband' is much higher.
The collective bargaining agreements between the police unions and the City have essentially turned the code of silence into official policy.
Chicago's police accountability system is broken.
Children in some areas of the City are not only being raised in high-crime environments, but they are also being mistreated by those who have sworn to protect and serve them.
In 2014, only 3 out of every 1,000 arrestees had an attorney at any point while in police custody.
posts.key_insight: Data reveals that Chicago's policing crisis isn't just about individual bad actors but about a systematically broken accountability structure where union contracts, oversight failures, and institutional bias work together to shield misconduct.

posts.summary: The post examines a controversy at Columbia where a machine learning assignment about Stop and Frisk data was poorly framed, sparking student protests. O'Neil argues this incident highlights the urgent need for CS departments to teach ethics alongside technical skills. She then explores a Stanford paper proposing algorithms to detect and reduce racist policing practices, while expressing skepticism about real-time algorithmic interventions. She suggests precinct-level analysis of hit rates might be more effective than equipping individual officers with algorithms, concluding that while data can help combat racist practices, the implementation details remain unclear.
key_points: CS departments have an obligation to teach ethics of machine learning alongside mathematical techniques
Machine learning algorithms are not objective because they're trained on historical data reflecting racist practices
Stop and Frisk data shows lower 'hit rates' for finding weapons on Black people than white people, even in high-crime areas, suggesting racial bias
Real-time algorithms for individual officers risk gaming and evasion; precinct-level analysis may be more effective
The concept of 'reasonable suspicion' is fundamentally a statistical construction that judges have applied inconsistently
money_quotes: Machine learning algorithms are not objective, because the data going into them are historical artifacts of racist police practices.

I've been saying for years that the CS department at Columbia, like many CS departments everywhere, has an obligation to teach and think about the ethics of machine learning as well as the mathematical techniques.
One of the best things about this article is the historical context it gives about the extent to which 'reasonable suspicion' is a statistical construction.
There are obvious issues around gaming such a model, or otherwise learning to evade undesired outcomes.
Although I am excited about the idea of using data to track and help prevent racist practices, I don't think we know exactly what that would look like in practice.
posts.key_insight: Data analysis could help identify and reduce racist policing patterns, but implementation at the systemic level is more promising than giving individual officers real-time algorithmic tools that could be gamed.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews Tom Slee's book 'What's Yours Is Mine,' which critiques the sharing economy's gap between feel-good marketing and exploitative reality. Slee uses data analysis to expose how companies like Uber and AirBnB systematically mislead the public about their business models while skirting regulations and labor protections. The book examines how reputation systems function as surveillance and control mechanisms rather than trust-builders, and how these platforms erode various forms of commons. O'Neil particularly highlights Slee's argument that the sharing economy represents a 'pattern of betrayal' where ideals of community and sustainability have been captured by venture capital and Wall Street.
key_points: AirBnB's statistics are consistently misleading rather than outright fraudulent, sharing only data that makes them appear more 'sharing-y' while making bulk profits from commercial rentals
Uber's value largely derives from avoiding responsibility for safety, insurance, accessibility, and proper employment classification
Reputation systems function as surveillance tools enforcing 'emotional labor' on workers in precarious positions, not genuine trust mechanisms
Sharing economy companies impose new forms of commerce on various commons (cultural, regulatory, public space) under the guise of 'disruption'
The movement represents a betrayal where community-oriented ideals have become 'the playground of billionaires, Wall Street, and venture capitalists'
money_quotes: He exposes the hyper-capitalism behind the feel-good marketing of Uber, AirBnB, Instagram, TaskRabbit, and other companies.
Cooking the books looks bad, but it's not the norm... most aren't fraudulent, but they're consistently misleading.
A large part of Uber's value is represented by skirting responsibility for safety, for avoiding insurance issues, for avoiding having capacity for wheelchairs, and the like.
What does it mean when all reviews are 4 or 5 stars? How could a 1-star review ever compensate for something really going bad?

We trust strangers on Sharing Economy platforms for the same reason we trust hotel employees and restaurant waiters: because they are in precarious jobs where customer complaints can lead to disciplinary action.
The reputation system is a way to enforce 'emotional labor'; service providers are compelled to manage their feelings and present the face that the platform demands.
This is known in Silicon Valley as 'disruption,' and it's not always an improvement.

What started as an appeal to community, person-to-person connections, sustainability, and sharing has become the playground of billionaires, Wall Street, and the venture capitalists extending their free-market values even further into our personal lives.
posts.key_insight: The sharing economy's reputation systems don't build genuine trust—they manufacture compliance through precarity, transforming workers into surveilled servants who must perform friendliness to avoid algorithmic punishment.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil exposes how academic researchers across multiple disciplines accepted money from the Consumer Credit Research Foundation, a payday lending lobbying group, in exchange for allowing industry lawyers to edit their research papers. The investigation, conducted by Freakonomics through FOIA requests, reveals that one economist allowed CCRF lawyers to write entire paragraphs of pro-payday lending propaganda that appeared verbatim in his published work, despite claiming the organization had no editorial control. This compromised research was then used to lobby state legislatures, where most payday lending regulation occurs. O'Neil argues this undermines public trust in academic objectivity and creates additional obstacles for regulators like the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau.
key_points: Academic researchers from economics, law, data science, and management took money from payday lending lobbyists for favorable research
FOIA requests revealed that industry lawyers wrote entire paragraphs that appeared verbatim in supposedly independent academic papers
The compromised research was used to lobby state legislatures, which control most payday lending regulation
Only 40% of the implicated academics are economists, suggesting the corruption extends beyond the discipline already tainted by Inside Job
Some institutions are legally blocking FOIA requests, suggesting more compromised research may exist
money_quotes: We like to think of academic researchers as fair, objective, and politically neutral.
Even though we've all seen Inside Job and know not to trust economists, the rest of academia seems relatively safe. Right?
Email chains that show Fusaro let a lawyer from CCRF write whole paragraphs of pro-payday lending propaganda that made it verbatim into his paper, decrying the phrase 'cycle of debt' as meaningless.
This is in spite of Fusaro's claim in this same paper that CCRF had held no editorial control.
The statehouse is the end-goal for such lobbyists.
The CFPB has enough work on its hands without so-called impartial research being compromised left and right.
posts.key_insight: Academic research credibility is being systematically corrupted by industry money, with lobbyists literally writing passages in supposedly independent papers to influence state-level regulation.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that economist Yanis Varoufakis will be a guest on the Slate Money podcast, where she'll have the opportunity to interview him about his new book on the European economic crisis and its implications for America. She mentions they'll likely discuss timely issues including Puerto Rico, Brexit, and the Greek economy. The post is notably informal and playful, with O'Neil openly admitting to having a 'celebrity crush' on Varoufakis and soliciting questions from readers who might be 'more level-headed' than she is.
key_points: Varoufakis is promoting his book 'And the Weak Suffer What They Must?' about Europe's crisis and America's economic future
The interview will cover current economic issues including Puerto Rico, Brexit, and the Greek economy
O'Neil solicits questions from readers, acknowledging her admiration for Varoufakis might affect her objectivity
The post demonstrates O'Neil's informal, self-deprecating blogging style
money_quotes: I have a celebrity crush on Yanis.
I'm wondering if anyone a bit more level-headed would like to come up with smart questions I should ask him.
Here's Yanis killing it with a crazy shirt in the Greek parliament.
posts.key_insight: Even serious economic commentators can be fans of the public intellectuals they cover, and acknowledging that bias openly is more honest than pretending to pure objectivity.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' is entering the pre-publication phase, with blurbs being solicited from carefully selected readers. She reflects on the psychology of this moment—enjoying positive early feedback while bracing for inevitable criticism when the book reaches a wider audience. She also announces a book tour starting in September 2016, primarily on the East Coast with possible stops in San Francisco and the Midwest, and solicits suggestions from readers for venues and communities to visit.
key_points: The book is in the 'blurb phase' where publishers send copies to influential people likely to respond positively
O'Neil acknowledges the book was written to be provocative and anticipates critical backlash
She's savoring the brief window of mostly positive feedback before wider public scrutiny
The book tour will begin September 6th, 2016, ideally kicking off with a party featuring her band
money_quotes: We've carefully chosen people who probably won't hate the book.
I'd like to hold on to this moment for as long as possible, because when the book is reviewed more widely, the critics – at least some of them – will hate the book.
That will be tough, but of course I wrote it to be provocative.
So I hope my skin is thick enough for that.
posts.key_insight: The pre-publication period offers authors a brief, curated window of positive feedback before facing the full spectrum of public criticism that provocative work inevitably attracts.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews Yanis Varoufakis's book on Europe's economic crisis, highlighting his argument that the Eurozone's fundamental design flaws make Greek debt essentially unpayable without restructuring or political union. She traces how the monetary union was built on romantic ideals rather than sound economics, creating an undemocratic system where technocrats override popular will during crises. The review emphasizes Varoufakis's critique of German dominance in European monetary policy and his colorful characterizations of bailout politics. O'Neil concludes with her own prediction that Greek default and Eurozone fragmentation are likely within three years.
key_points: Unpayable debt cannot be collected without restructuring, and Eurozone countries lack the currency tools to pay without either debt forgiveness or fiscal union
The European monetary union was treated as an axiom rather than proven as a theorem, built on romantic ideals of unification rather than economic soundness
The Eurozone's design is fundamentally undemocratic, with technocrats overriding popular will during crises
German banking interests have dominated European monetary policy, imposing austerity on weaker economies
Greek bailouts primarily served to transfer money to German and French banks while postponing the Eurozone's structural problems
money_quotes: A debt may be a debt but an unpayable debt does not get paid unless it is sensibly restructured.
Debt was a symptom of Europe's awful institutional design, not its problem.
That monetary union is good for Europe's economy and consistent with European democracy ought to be a theorem. Europe, however, decided to treat it as an axiom.
The Eurozone was an attempt by Europeans – mostly Germans and French – to make money 'apolitical' in the name of the unification of Europe. This was never going to work.
The Troika is the oligarchs' and the tax evaders' best friend.
The first 6 times he mentions this it's convincing, but after 10 references I started wanting to hear what they'd say.
posts.key_insight: The Eurozone's crisis stems not from individual countries' fiscal irresponsibility but from a fundamentally flawed institutional design that treated monetary union as self-evidently good rather than something requiring democratic structures and fiscal mechanisms to actually work.

posts.summary: O'Neil reviews 'Prime Numbers and the Riemann Hypothesis' by Barry Mazur and William Stein, praising it as an accessible yet rigorous introduction to deep mathematical concepts. The book combines elegant theoretical explanations with computational illustrations, covering topics from basic prime number theory to the Riemann zeta function. She particularly admires that the authors break the 'third rail' taboo of asking general audiences to engage with formulas and technical work. However, she critiques the book's heavy focus on white male mathematicians, noting the stereotype threat this poses for diverse readers.
key_points: The book successfully makes advanced mathematics accessible to readers who may have forgotten basic definitions, while still teaching new things to professional mathematicians
The author pairing works well: Barry Mazur provides elegant 'pure thought' explanations while William Stein contributes illuminating computational graphs
Unlike most popular math books, this one dares to include formulas and asks readers to work hard for understanding
The representation of mathematicians in the book skews entirely toward white males, creating potential stereotype threat for other readers
money_quotes: I learned a lot reading it, even though I'm supposed to know these things already. But I really didn't, and now I'm glad I do.

The book does exactly what most general audience books dare not do – including mine – which is to say, they use formulas, and graphs, and generally speaking ask the reader to work hard, in the name of enlightenment, beauty, and wonder.
Technical work for the reader such as this is, normally speaking, something of a third rail.
In this book the authors run straight for that rail, set up a game of catch, and invite you to join their picnic in the rain, electric shock be damned.
They're having so much fun that you can't help yourself.
As an educator I want to be aware of the stereotype threat that this sets up for other readers.
posts.key_insight: The best popular mathematics writing dares to ask readers to engage with real formulas and technical concepts rather than dumbing things down, but must also consider whose stories get told.

posts.summary: This post promotes the Alternative Banking Group's essay arguing for a reimagined approach to globalization. The central thesis is that while the Trans Pacific Partnership represents harmful pro-corporate globalization, the concept of international cooperation itself shouldn't be rejected. O'Neil and her group advocate for trade agreements designed to benefit the 99% rather than corporate interests. The post announces both the Huffington Post essay and an upcoming Left Forum panel discussion on the topic.
key_points: The TPP should be rejected, but international cooperation as a concept should not be
Generic anti-globalization sentiment conflates bad trade deals with beneficial international cooperation
There's a distinction between pro-corporate globalization and globalization that could work for ordinary people
The Alternative Banking Group has developed a blueprint for trade agreements serving the 99%
money_quotes: We should reject the TPP but we should embrace the idea of international cooperation.
When we generically decry 'globalization', we are throwing the baby out with the bathwater.
We actually want globalization, just not the kind of pro-corporate globalization that we've grown to despise.
It is important not to confuse the foulness of the TPP's proposed rules with the otherwise difficult, but not altogether undesirable, prospect of super-national rules of law.
posts.key_insight: Opposing specific exploitative trade deals like the TPP shouldn't mean abandoning the goal of international cooperation—instead, we need to reimagine globalization in service of the many rather than the few.

posts.summary: Forced arbitration clauses in corporate contracts prevent consumers and employees from suing companies or joining class-action lawsuits, creating a system heavily stacked against individuals. The cost-benefit calculation of pursuing arbitration rarely makes sense for individuals, and the arbitration process itself favors corporations since businesses often choose the arbitrators. The CFPB has issued new rules to eliminate these clauses for consumer financial lending products, which is significant given how prevalent they are (up to 99% in payday loans). However, the rules don't cover nursing homes, for-profit colleges, or employment contracts, leaving significant gaps in consumer protection.
key_points: Forced arbitration clauses eliminate both individual lawsuits and class-action options, making it nearly impossible to address systemic corporate misconduct
The arbitration process is structurally biased because companies choose arbitrators who may want repeat business
The cost-benefit math of individual arbitration is 'particularly cruel' since winning doesn't help future victims
CFPB's new rules will eliminate forced arbitration for financial products like credit cards and payday loans
Significant gaps remain: nursing homes, for-profit colleges, and 22% of employee contracts still use these clauses
money_quotes: That's part of the fine print when you sign a corporate contract as a consumer or as an employee which states that you will not sue the company for bullshit they might pull, like unfair fees, no overtime pay, or even discriminatory practices.
It's a massively stacked deck; the calculation of how much it will cost, in terms of lawyers, versus how much you'd get if you won you case means that very few people choose the arbitration option.
The arithmetic is particularly cruel since, once class-action suits are out of the way, you're not even benefitting other people who will come after you and find the same shit being pulled.
The process entirely depends on a arbitrator being fair and impartial but, believe it or not, the business in question chooses this person.
payday loan 99%
posts.key_insight: Forced arbitration clauses are a legal mechanism that corporations use to immunize themselves from accountability by making it economically irrational for individuals to seek justice.

posts.summary: O'Neil pushes back against the viral framing of a story where an economist was questioned on a plane for writing equations. She argues the real story isn't about math being under attack, but about an irrational passenger who reported a man for doing math while appearing 'swarthy.' The airline's response of removing and questioning the economist was standard procedure once terrorism was invoked, but the instigating complaint was absurd. O'Neil criticizes the media's framing while noting her New York-honed instinct for recognizing irrational behavior in public.
key_points: The media framed this as 'math under attack' when it was really about one paranoid passenger's irrational behavior
The airline followed standard protocol once terrorism allegations were made, which O'Neil finds understandable
The passenger's behavior (passing notes, reporting math as suspicious) marked her as the actual problem
Racial profiling played a role - the man was described as 'swarthy Italian'
Urban experience makes you better at recognizing that seemingly normal people can be 'utterly insane'
money_quotes: The article's headline should have read: Crazy Woman Removed From Plane
Some paranoid freak decided that an integral sign on the page of notes of a swarthy Italian man next to her posed a terrorist threat.
I'm just so used to sitting next to perfectly normal looking people on the subway who turn out to be utterly insane, that I'm ready to assume anyone at all is fucking nuts.
Note-passers older than 13 are already suspect; get up and walk over to someone if you're worried.
Some woman was nuts, she indicated as much to the flight crew, and they removed her from the flight. Math isn't under attack.
posts.key_insight: When irrational people invoke terrorism to harass others, the story isn't about what they feared—it's about their irrationality.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil makes a personal appeal to her readers for help finding logic puzzles suitable for her 7-year-old son, who has developed a nightly riddle habit after discovering the classic wolf-sheep-cabbage river crossing problem. She notes that searching for 'riddles' online yields wordplay-based puzzles rather than the logic-based challenges she's seeking. The post ends with her discovering that 'logic puzzles' is the better search term, sharing a useful resource she found.
key_points: The wolf-sheep-cabbage river crossing puzzle is an ideal example of what she's looking for: logic-based, not wordplay-based
Standard 'riddle' searches return sophisticated wordplay rather than accessible logic problems
The distinction between 'riddles' and 'logic puzzles' matters for finding age-appropriate content
Crowdsourcing from blog readers can solve everyday parenting challenges
money_quotes: Every night he demands a new riddle as good as that one, and to tell you the truth I don't know many very good riddles.
If you google 'list of riddles' you come up with something that's far too sophisticated and involves word play rather than logic.
posts.key_insight: The terminology we use to search for things matters—'riddles' and 'logic puzzles' return very different results despite seeming synonymous.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that the Facebook trending news controversy reveals a fundamental misunderstanding about algorithms: people wrongly assume algorithms are neutral while human curators are biased. She points out that Facebook's human curators were explicitly training the algorithm, meaning any bias they introduced would be perpetuated by the machine learning system. The post challenges the naive belief that replacing humans with algorithms removes bias, asserting that algorithms are just as biased as their human creators.
key_points: People trust algorithms more than human curators, wrongly assuming algorithms are neutral
Facebook's curators were explicitly serving as training data for the algorithm that would replace them
Any bias introduced during the human curation phase gets perpetuated by the machine learning algorithm
Human involvement in curation doesn't make a process more or less biased than algorithmic curation
Understanding the depth of bias requires explicit scrutiny, regardless of whether humans or algorithms are involved
money_quotes: People trust algorithms too much, more than they trust people.
They were there not to work, but to serve as training modules for Facebook's algorithm.
Any anti-conservative bias that they were introducing at this preliminary training phase would soon be taken over by the machine learning algorithm, to be perpetuated for eternity.
Algorithms are just as biased as human curators.
We should not be offended when humans are involved in a curation process, because it doesn't make that process inherently more or less biased.
We won't understand the depth of bias of a process unless we scrutinize it explicitly with that intention in mind.
posts.key_insight: The bias in algorithms isn't eliminated by removing humans—it's inherited from them, since machine learning systems are trained on human decisions and perpetuate those biases indefinitely.

posts.summary: Google announced it will ban payday loan advertisements through AdWords, requiring lenders to show APR of 36% or less and loan terms of at least 60 days. O'Neil sees this as a positive step but raises questions about enforcement, given payday lenders' history of finding loopholes around regulations. She notes that Google is effectively becoming a quasi-regulator, leapfrogging agencies like the CFPB. The move represents a significant judgment call beyond banning obviously illegal products, suggesting Google is beginning to acknowledge responsibility for vulnerable users in its advertising ecosystem.
key_points: Google's new policy bans payday loan ads with APR above 36% or terms under 60 days, but enforcement questions remain about lead aggregators and lender workarounds
Google is effectively acting as a quasi-regulator, moving faster than official bodies like the CFPB to protect consumers
The payday lending industry's 'free speech' objection is baseless since Google is a private platform, but the framing reveals how we might think about ad platforms as public spaces
Targeted advertising creates predatory 'back alleys' where vulnerable individuals are selected by zip code and search queries for exploitation
This represents a turning point where Google made a moral judgment call rather than just banning obviously illegal content
money_quotes: Google is leaping over regulators like the CFPB, which kind of makes Google into a quasi-regulator itself.
Imagine Google ads as a kind of corporate town square, where companies get to broadcast their wares to potential customers.

We should really imagine it as a series of back alleys in which the most vulnerable and desperate individuals, chosen just as much for their zip codes as for their search query, are taken aside and promised their problems will all go away if they just sign on the dotted line.
Instead of just getting rid of truly obvious bad actors (illegal drugs, counterfeit goods, explosives), Google has actually gone further and made a judgement call, and a good one.
They are starting to acknowledge the responsibility they have to the worst-off of the people who inhabit the environment they create.
Their argument is along the lines of calling it 'discriminatory and a form of censorship,' as if there's some freedom of speech issue here. There isn't, since Google is a private platform.
posts.key_insight: When tech platforms make moral judgments about what ads to allow, they become de facto regulators with more power and speed than government agencies—a responsibility they're only beginning to acknowledge.

posts.summary: The post reviews a White House report on big data and civil rights, authored by top Obama administration officials including CTO Megan Smith and Chief Data Scientist DJ Patil. O'Neil praises the report for debunking the myth of algorithmic objectivity and addressing problems of biased training data, opacity, and disparate impact. The report examines case studies in credit, employment, education, and criminal justice. O'Neil is particularly excited by the conclusion's call for algorithmic auditing and external testing of big data systems, which aligns with her own professional aspirations after her book tour.
key_points: The White House report explicitly debunks the 'widely held assumption that algorithms are objective'
Case studies cover credit lending, employment, higher education, and criminal justice - showing both opportunities and risks
The report calls for 'algorithmic systems accountability' including external auditing and testing for fairness
Big data can both detect bias AND perpetuate, exacerbate, or mask discrimination
The report advocates for machine learning systems designed with fairness constraints built in
money_quotes: It also demonstrates the risks involved, particularly how technologies can deliberately or inadvertently perpetuate, exacerbate, or mask discrimination.
The report itself debunks the widely held assumption that algorithms are objective.
Promote academic research and industry development of algorithmic auditing and external testing of big data systems to ensure that people are being treated fairly.
Readers, this is what I want to do with my life, after my book tour. So it's nice to see people calling for it like this.
posts.key_insight: Government recognition that algorithms are not objective and require external accountability mechanisms represents a significant step toward addressing algorithmic discrimination.

posts.summary: O'Neil questions the true purpose of higher education after reading about failing for-profit colleges that enrich founders while saddling students with debt. She notes the proliferation of 'cash cow' MA programs at elite institutions like Columbia, observing that diversity concerns disappear when international students pay full tuition. Rather than accepting education's stated purpose of knowledge transmission, she offers eight cynical alternative explanations ranging from social networking to corporate training cost-shifting. The post suggests the education system has drifted far from its ostensible mission.
key_points: For-profit colleges exemplify education's corruption: high debt, poor outcomes, rich founders
Elite universities run 'cash cow' MA programs that abandon diversity principles for revenue
The knowledge transmission theory of education is 'probably the least likely option'
Education may function primarily as a ranking system for employers
Companies have shifted training costs onto workers via degree requirements as unions declined
money_quotes: It's enough to make you wonder what education is for.
And, whereas we 'care about diversity' in many of our college programs, in many of these they are utterly dominated by Chinese kids who get specific STEM versions of student visas.
It's a way to get these kids edumacated, for them to accumulate important knowledge. This is probably the least likely option.
It's a way to socialize our young people and help develop their networks and social skills, so that someday they can call on their friends to deploy insider trading.
It's a way to give day jobs to people who dedicate their time to gaming the US News & World Report college ranking model.

It's a way for companies to avoid training their workers and force them to pay for training before they get offered a job, now that unions have been decimated and nobody stays with a company for more than a few years.
posts.key_insight: Higher education has evolved from knowledge transmission into a complex system serving institutional revenue, employer convenience, and social sorting—with actual learning as perhaps its least important function.

posts.summary: This is a link roundup post where O'Neil curates articles on topics she finds noteworthy: universities functioning as financial institutions, flawed DNA testing algorithms in criminal justice, pharmaceutical company charity schemes, Facebook's algorithmic opacity, and debunking breakfast mythology. The post serves as a window into her intellectual concerns, highlighting systemic issues with algorithms, institutional corruption, and scientific misinformation. She briefly editorializes on each link, expressing particular concern about opaque algorithms in criminal justice and skepticism about Facebook's accountability.
key_points: DNA testing algorithms represent a 'Weapon of Math Destruction' - opaque, widespread systems that wrongly imprison people
Facebook's algorithmic manipulation continues unchecked due to lack of transparency requirements
Pharmaceutical charity programs are designed to be profitable rather than altruistic
Universities increasingly function like hedge funds and private equity firms
Scientific claims about breakfast being essential have been debunked
money_quotes: Opaque, widespread algorithms that put the wrong people in jail.
How much more public manipulation will Facebook get away with before they give some transparency to their algorithm? The answer is: a lot.
No, breakfast isn't magical – eat it if you're hungry, skip it if not.
