posts.key_insight: Opaque algorithmic systems, from DNA testing to social media, operate without accountability while institutions that should serve the public instead serve private interests.

posts.summary: O'Neil celebrates ProPublica's groundbreaking investigation into the COMPAS recidivism risk model, which revealed systematic racial bias in criminal sentencing algorithms. The analysis found that black defendants were nearly twice as likely to be falsely labeled high-risk, while white defendants were twice as likely to be incorrectly classified as low-risk. The study controlled for prior crimes, future recidivism, age, and gender, yet black defendants remained significantly more likely to receive higher risk scores. O'Neil argues this report validates concerns she and others have raised about algorithmic bias and sets a new standard for auditing such systems.
key_points: Black defendants who didn't reoffend were nearly twice as likely as white defendants to be misclassified as high-risk (45% vs 23%)
Even controlling for prior crimes, future recidivism, age, and gender, black defendants were 45-77% more likely to receive higher risk scores
ProPublica set a new transparency standard by publishing their methodology, data, and analysis code on GitHub
Recidivism risk models have been deployed 'naively' across judicial systems with scores presented as inherently fair
The report provides hard proof for concerns that algorithmic bias critics had been raising without concrete evidence
money_quotes: The piece explains in human terms what it looks like when algorithms are biased.
Black defendants who did not recidivate over a two-year period were nearly twice as likely to be misclassified as higher risk compared to their white counterparts.
This is a triumph for the community of people (like me!) who have been worrying about exactly this kind of thing but who haven't had hard proof until now.
The transparency involved is hugely important, because nobody will be able to say they don't know how these statistics were computed.

Until now, recidivism risk models have been deployed naively, in judicial systems all across the country, and judges in those systems have been presented with such scores as if they are inherently 'fair.'
People deploying these models will have pressure to make sure the models are audited for racism before using them.
posts.key_insight: ProPublica's COMPAS investigation provides the first hard proof that recidivism risk algorithms systematically discriminate against black defendants, setting a new standard for algorithmic accountability.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil highlights three alarming examples of big data misuse that emerged in a single day. Cable One uses FICO scores to determine service quality for customers. Chicago Police use social media data mining to identify gang members, then circularly validate their algorithm by arresting people on the list. Most egregiously, a startup called Faception claims to identify terrorists through facial recognition, essentially reviving phrenology with a tech veneer. O'Neil argues these examples vindicate her earlier warning that big data is becoming the new pseudoscience.
key_points: Cable One discriminates service quality based on FICO scores, giving worse treatment to lower-scoring customers
Chicago Police's gang prediction algorithm suffers from circular validation - they arrest people on their list, then cite arrest rates as proof the list works
Faception represents a disturbing revival of phrenology, claiming to identify terrorists and personality types from facial features
These systems lack scientific rigor and independent auditing despite making consequential decisions about people's lives
money_quotes: Yesterday was, for some reason, a big day for terrible ideas in the big data space.
We don't turn people away... but the cable company's technicians aren't going to 'spend 15 minutes setting up an iPhone app' for a customer who has a low FICO score.
I'd like to see a slightly more scientific audit of this system.
Personality is influenced by genes, facial features are influenced by genes, therefore facial features can be used to predict personality.
I think my previous claim that Big Data is the New Phrenology was about a year too early.
posts.key_insight: Big data systems increasingly make consequential decisions using pseudoscientific methods and circular logic, reviving discriminatory practices like phrenology under a veneer of technological sophistication.

posts.summary: A New York judge ruled that teacher Sheri Lederman's growth score evaluation was 'arbitrary and capricious,' validating long-standing criticisms of value-added models in education. Lederman's score dropped from 14 to 1 between years despite her students performing consistently well on state tests. The judge found multiple flaws in the statistical methodology, including unfair penalties for teachers with very high or low-performing students, unreliability with small class sizes, and the inability to explain year-to-year score swings. O'Neil sees this ruling as corroboration of her critiques of algorithmic evaluation systems.
key_points: Growth scores and value-added models can produce wildly inconsistent results for the same teacher with similar student outcomes
The statistical method unfairly penalizes teachers at both extremes—those with very high-performing or very low-performing students
Small class sizes make growth models less reliable, yet they're still used to evaluate teachers
The system defines a fixed percentage of teachers as 'ineffective' each year regardless of actual student performance trends
The state could not explain why Lederman's score swung so dramatically despite consistent student performance
money_quotes: I'm feeling extremely corroborated this week, what with the ProPublica report on Monday and also the recent judge's ruling on a teacher's growth score.
McDonough decided to vacate her score, describing it as 'arbitrary and capricious'.
He found that Lederman's small class size made the growth model less reliable.
He was troubled by the state's inability to explain the wide swing in Lederman's score from year to year, even though her students performed at similar levels.
He was perplexed that the growth model rules define a fixed percentage of teachers as ineffective each year, regardless of whether student performance across the state rose or fell.
posts.key_insight: When algorithmic evaluation systems produce wildly inconsistent scores that the state cannot explain, and mandate fixed failure rates regardless of actual performance, they fail the basic test of fairness that any consequential scoring system should meet.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil highlights a data analysis by lawyer-turned-data-scientist David Colarusso examining racial and economic bias in Virginia's criminal sentencing. Using recently opened criminal justice data, Colarusso found that race affects sentence length far more than income, though both matter. His analysis showed that a Black defendant would need to earn over half a million dollars more than a white peer to receive equivalent treatment. O'Neil praises this transparent, accessible approach to exposing systemic bias as a counterweight to harmful uses of big data.
key_points: Race affects sentencing length significantly more than income/class in Virginia's criminal justice system
A Black man would need to earn $500,000+ more than a white peer to receive equivalent sentencing treatment
The analysis uses open criminal justice data with transparent methodology and publicly available code
This type of analysis represents a positive use of big data compared to systems that 'double down on poor and black folks'
Making technical analysis readable and accessible to non-technical audiences is valuable for exposing systemic bias
money_quotes: For a black man in Virginia to get the same treatment as his Caucasian peer, he must earn more than half a million dollars $90,000 a year.

Compared to shitty ways of using data, which end up doubling down on poor and black folks, this kind of analysis shines a light on how the system works against them, and gives me hope that one day we'll fix it.
His explanation of each step is readable by non-technical people, it's a real treasure.
The answer he came up with is race, by a long margin, although he also found that class matters too.
I am so glad people are doing this.
posts.key_insight: Transparent data analysis of criminal justice systems reveals that racial bias in sentencing dwarfs economic bias, and this kind of accountability-focused work offers hope for systemic reform.

posts.summary: The post argues that the combination of mathematical and genetic complexity creates an intimidation effect that shields flawed algorithms from public scrutiny. O'Neil examines two cases: DNA forensics testing, which suffers from contamination issues and inconsistent results across labs yet heavily influences jury verdicts, and genetic tests claiming to predict opioid addiction risk, which lack transparent accuracy claims and scientific backing yet are being used to deny pain medication. She contends that this intimidation factor allows potentially inaccurate tools to be deployed in high-stakes decisions affecting people's freedom and health.
key_points: Math and genetics each intimidate the public, but combined they create a 'double intimidation' that removes most people from even the possibility of critique
DNA forensics tests have become so sensitive that contamination is a serious problem, yet standards, training, and quality vary significantly across labs
Juries are extremely swayed by DNA evidence—sexual assault cases with DNA are 33 times more likely to result in guilty verdicts
Genetic opioid addiction risk tests lack scientific backing and transparent accuracy claims, yet are being marketed to deny pain medication to workers
We need new standards of evidence before allowing scientific-sounding tools to be used against people in consequential decisions
money_quotes: If you combine those two things, you've basically floored almost the entire population. They remove themselves from even the possibility of critique.

The tests have gotten so sensitive that it's increasingly difficult to know if the DNA being tested comes from the victim of a crime, the perpetrator of the crime, the forensics person who collected the sample, or some random dude who accidentally breathed in the same room three weeks ago.

Ironically, you have a technology that was meant to help eliminate subjectivity in forensics. But when you start to drill down deeper into the way crime laboratories operate today, you see that the subjectivity is still there.
So in other words, people in pain who are rated 'high risk' might be denied pain meds through such a test that has no scientific backing but sounds convincing.
Enough with this intimidation. We need new standards of evidence before we let people wield scientific tools against people.
posts.key_insight: The intimidation factor of combining math and genetics shields flawed algorithms from scrutiny, allowing unvalidated tools to make high-stakes decisions about people's freedom and health.

posts.summary: Guest author Kareem Carr, drawing from his experience validating cancer research algorithms, proposes three principles for preventing harmful algorithmic systems. He argues that open datasets enable scrutiny of proprietary algorithms, expert manual curation provides essential error detection, and causal models should be demanded over merely predictive ones. The post emphasizes that bad data science obscures real-world algorithm performance while good data science creates deeply understood, transparent systems. His central thesis is that rigorous validation and transparency are the antidotes to algorithmic harm.
key_points: Open datasets allow analysis of algorithms even when source code is hidden, revealing error patterns and failure modes
Expert human judgment, especially panels of domain specialists, provides crucial validation that algorithms alone cannot
Predictive models fail under policy interventions while causal models remain accurate despite external changes
Bad data science relies on canned validation approaches without critically examining how and why failures occur
Validation and error characterization are essential parts of data science, not optional add-ons
money_quotes: When your algorithms can potentially affect the way physicians treat their dying patients, it really brings home how critical it is to do data science right.
Open datasets allow us to analyze algorithms even when they are too complex to understand or when the source code is hidden.
While humans are by no means perfect, when careful consideration of an algorithmic result by experts implies that the algorithm has failed, it's important to take that message seriously.
We should demand truly causal models rather than the merely predictive.
Predictive models perform well when there are no external modifications of the system. Causal models continue to be accurate despite exogenous shocks and policy interventions.
Frequently, we create the former, yet try to deploy them as if they are the latter with disastrous consequences.
Bad data science obscures and ignores the real world performance of its algorithms.
Good data science creates algorithms which are deeply and widely understood.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic harm can be prevented through rigorous validation practices: open datasets for scrutiny, expert curation for error detection, and causal models that withstand real-world interventions.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on the irony of speaking about algorithmic injustice against the poor while staying at a luxury hotel overlooking the Louvre. She draws a parallel between historical injustices (reparations paid to British slave owners rather than slaves) and modern algorithmic discrimination, specifically Wisconsin's use of proprietary recidivism scores in sentencing. She questions whether including a flawed algorithmic score as 'one of many factors' in decision-making actually makes the overall process fair. The post closes with a celebration of middle-aged rebellion against social expectations.
key_points: British slave owners received £17 billion (in today's money) in compensation after abolition - a fact that should reframe modern reparations debates
Wisconsin's Supreme Court is weighing whether proprietary recidivism algorithms deny due process and are discriminatory
The 'one of many factors' defense for algorithmic scores doesn't hold up - a flawed input still corrupts the overall process
O'Neil acknowledges the hypocrisy of discussing poverty and surveillance from a position of luxury, but proceeds anyway
money_quotes: And yes, it's bizarre to be in such a nice place so I can go complain about how poor people are being surveilled and mistreated. I accept all cries of 'hypocrite!' that you might want to throw at me, but know that at least I'm a self-aware hypocrite.
If there's a shit score, but it's only one of many factors that are used in a process that's supposed to be fair, doesn't that mean the overall process isn't fair?
Very interested to see how this turns out.
Seems to me this should change our perspective on reparations.
posts.key_insight: Including a discriminatory algorithmic score as 'one of many factors' in decision-making doesn't cleanse the process of unfairness - it just obscures it.

posts.summary: O'Neil offers sardonic summaries of talks she attended, using humor to critique tech utopianism while highlighting genuinely valuable insights. She skewers Don Tapscott's blockchain evangelism and Andrew McAffee's techno-optimism about inequality and climate change. In contrast, she signals genuine appreciation for Carl Pomerance's math talk and Monica Lewinsky's nuanced take on cyber bullying. Lewinsky's point about how clicking on shame-based content trains algorithms receives the most substantive treatment, suggesting O'Neil found real value there.
key_points: Blockchain evangelists make grandiose claims about solving inequality without concrete mechanisms
Tech optimists use selective metrics and catchy names to declare paradigm shifts that conveniently absolve the wealthy of responsibility
Individual clicking behavior directly trains and reinforces clickbait algorithms, making users complicit in online shaming
The contrast between dismissive and respectful synopses reveals which ideas O'Neil finds substantive versus hollow
money_quotes: Next stop, inequality solved by blockchain.
Also, great news, we rich people no longer need to worry about global warming because technology will solve that like it solves everything else.
When you click on shame-based news articles, you are training the online clickbait algorithm, and you become part of it.
Resist clicking, be nice.
I am giving in to the urge to be extremely snarky today.
posts.key_insight: Tech utopianism often dresses up vague promises in paradigm-shift language, while the real insight about algorithmic complicity comes from understanding how our clicks actively shape harmful systems.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates the arrest of Norman Seabrook, head of the Correction Officers' Benevolent Association, who was charged with investing union members' pensions in a failing hedge fund for kickbacks. She details her long-standing disgust with Seabrook's fear-mongering radio ads that dehumanized Rikers prisoners while he systematically protected abusive guards. O'Neil documents his pattern of obstruction, including shutting down prison bus systems to prevent testimony against officers and training guards to abuse prisoners off-camera. She expresses hope that his removal will finally enable reform of Rikers Island.
key_points: Seabrook ran fear-mongering radio ads dehumanizing prisoners while protecting guards who beat handcuffed teenagers
He systematically obstructed reform by shutting down bus systems to block testimony and removing investigators he couldn't control
He trained officers to conduct abuse 'behind a curtain or off camera' and coached them on creating cover stories
The arrest charges involve investing union pension funds in a failing hedge fund for personal kickbacks
His removal may finally enable meaningful reform of Rikers Island's brutal conditions
money_quotes: We protect you from all the people who'd kill your wife and rape your daughter.
He's a bully who leads and protects an army of bullies.
This is a guy who, in order to prevent a prisoner from testifying against one of those officers, had the entire bus system from Riker's shut down.
This is a guy who told people to 'follow the rules' so that they don't have 'to sit down at a table and come up with a story that doesn't make any sense.'
I've honestly had nightmares about my sons somehow fucking up and going to Riker's, and I'd like those to end.
posts.key_insight: Institutional corruption thrives when union leadership prioritizes protecting abusers over accountability, turning what should be oversight into systematic obstruction of justice.

posts.summary: O'Neil explains why she deleted her LinkedIn account before Microsoft's $26 billion acquisition, citing spam, bogus requests, and 'hyped up crap about magic bullets using big data' as reasons. She argues the Microsoft acquisition represents a significant drop in alignment between the platform and its users' interests. The post warns that years of professional social data people have carefully built will now belong to a corporation that can change privacy policies at will. She suspects Microsoft will use the network for marketing experiments and urges readers to delete their accounts.
key_points: LinkedIn had already become a spam-filled platform pushing big data hype before the acquisition
The $26 billion Microsoft acquisition transfers valuable personal professional data to a corporation with different incentives
Privacy policies can be changed unilaterally, leaving users with no real protection
Data that individuals protect as personally valuable becomes extremely valuable when aggregated by corporations
Corporate acquisitions represent a 'severe drop in alignment' between platform and user interests
money_quotes: The content I saw when I bothered to log in were consistently hyped up crap about magic bullets using big data.

All that social data, the professional contacts and so on that people have built up over years and years, is being handed over to a huge and powerful corporation that can change the privacy policy whenever they'd like.
This is data that people protect as personally valuable individually; it is clearly quite valuable more generally.
I also suspect they'll submit the LinkedIn network to experiments to see how professionals respond to various marketing campaigns. Yuck.
I'm not saying that LinkedIn had motives that were perfectly aligned with its users, but I'm seeing this as a severe drop in alignment.
posts.key_insight: When platforms are acquired, years of carefully cultivated personal data get transferred to new corporate owners with different agendas and the power to change privacy rules unilaterally.

posts.summary: This guest post by Kevin H. Wilson argues that the high school mathematics curriculum should be radically restructured around statistics, programming, and data analysis rather than the traditional calculus-focused track. Wilson identifies four biases preventing change: the historical difficulty of computation, the preference for easily measurable skills, the isolation of learning within classrooms, and the challenges of retraining teachers. He proposes merging algebra with programming, replacing geometry with discrete math and data structures, substituting Algebra II with statistics courses using open datasets, and making machine learning the capstone course instead of calculus. The ultimate goal would be community-based capstone projects where students use data to advocate for local change.
key_points: Programming should be taught as a tool for data manipulation across subjects, not isolated in computer science classes
Statistics, not calculus, should be the pinnacle of high school math because computers have made statistical analysis vastly easier than when curricula were designed
The current math curriculum's extreme linearity (each year depends entirely on the previous) disadvantages students who fall behind, unlike humanities where late bloomers can catch up
Community involvement in education—through grading, mentoring, and providing real-world projects—could transform learning outcomes
Teacher training and professional development remain massive obstacles to curricular change, as demonstrated by Common Core's botched rollout
money_quotes: Programming is simply a tool which allows data to be manipulated.
Statistics, not calculus, should be the pinnacle achievement of high school, not relegated to box-and-whisker plots and an AP course which is accepted by relatively few universities.

Our generally balkanized curricula often lead to a general perception among students that knowledge is a bunch of tiny islands whose inhabitants are called the 'Good at Math' or the 'Good at English.'

Any professional data scientist or statistician will tell you this is the easy part. Once you've got a nice CSV filled with data, it's usually pretty clear what the battery of models are that you would probably run on that data.
The hard parts about statistics and data science is what comes before you even start to model the data. How was it generated? What assumptions does that imply about your data?
Teachers, for all their professionalism and hard work, are given very few opportunities to learn and teach new subjects.
Imagine a world where students build hardware and place it around their community to measure the effects of pollutants or the weather or traffic.
posts.key_insight: The computer revolution has made statistical analysis trivially easy compared to when math curricula were designed, yet schools still treat calculus as the pinnacle while relegating statistics to an afterthought—a mismatch that fails to prepare students for a data-driven world.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that student debt from predatory for-profit colleges should be forgiven as a 'sunk cost' that the government incurred through regulatory failure. She explains how for-profit colleges like Corinthian, ITT Tech, and University of Phoenix gamed the federal aid system through questionable accreditation and deceptive marketing to poor students. While the government hesitates due to expense and precedent concerns, O'Neil contends the debt will never be repaid anyway, so acknowledging it as a sunk cost allows for learning from the mistake and fixing the broken accreditation system.
key_points: For-profit colleges exploited federal student aid by obtaining dubious accreditation and lying to vulnerable students about educational outcomes
The DOE's failure to maintain accreditation standards created this debt crisis, making the government responsible for the losses
Framing unpayable debt as a 'sunk cost' rather than hoping for repayment enables proper problem-solving and systemic reform
The DOE has begun addressing the root cause by tightening accreditation standards and shutting down problematic accreditors
Debt forgiveness is the practical conclusion since the money will never be recovered from students who were defrauded
money_quotes: I claim these are sunk costs and should be treated as such.
Once you let your standards go that far, you are on the hook.
Although it looks 'expensive' to forgive this debt, there's really no other option, because it's never getting paid back.
That's what happens when you let a predatory industry prey on the most vulnerable.
What's good about acknowledging sunk costs is that you can learn your lesson and fix the problem that got you into this mess.
When you don't acknowledge sunk costs, you're in the wrong mindset, hoping against hope that somehow the money will be paid back. It won't.
We need to turn off the federal aid spigot for bad colleges.
posts.key_insight: Treating for-profit college debt as a sunk cost rather than a recoverable asset allows the government to stop wishful thinking and actually fix the broken accreditation system that enabled predatory lending.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques the 'smart city' movement as a cynical exercise in surveillance capitalism disguised as innovation. She argues that most smart products exist not because they solve real problems, but because Silicon Valley has too much money chasing unnecessary ideas. The essay highlights the absurdity of having smart WiFi kiosks but no public bathrooms, smart tampons but no free tampons for homeless women. O'Neil exposes how 'smart' infrastructure like NYC's LinkNYC kiosks are really about harvesting data and selling targeted ads, not serving public needs. The core problem isn't technological enthusiasm—it's that profit motives determine what gets built while actual human needs go unaddressed.
key_points: Most 'smart' products are old products with sensors added so companies can pretend they have new businesses worth funding
Smart city infrastructure trades public space and anonymity for surveillance and advertising revenue
We have smart WiFi kiosks but no public bathrooms because bathrooms don't generate ad revenue
The question isn't whether technology is 'smart' but whether there's profit in it—helping poor women with tampons is 'merely the right thing to do'
LinkNYC's funding explanation deliberately obscures that users are the product being monetized
money_quotes: Can we add sensors to tampons to figure out when the tampon is drenched in blood? WOULD PEOPLE PAY FOR THAT?
The answer is, generally, no fucking way, but there's too much money in Silicon Valley for that answer to be heard.
We have 'smart' tampons but still don't make normal tampons available to, for example, homeless women or high school girls.
It's not because we are in love with the concept of 'smart' technology. It's because that's where the money is.
It's not profitable to give poor women tampons, it's merely the right thing to do.
The smart city fad is a boondoggle, a way of giving public space access to private advertising companies in exchange for a few nickels and a surveillance state.
It's in a sense selling a commodity—public space, and the concept of anonymity within a large city—that we didn't even know we could measure, never mind commoditize.
We cannot expect actual ongoing problems to be addressed. Because we're too busy being smart.
posts.key_insight: The 'smart' city movement isn't about solving problems—it's about monetizing public space and personal data while actual human needs remain unfunded because they lack profit potential.

posts.summary: O'Neil explores the mechanics of gerrymandering, explaining how parties manipulate district boundaries to maximize wins (by thin margins) while concentrating opponents into 'loss' districts. She discusses algorithmic solutions for creating compact, fair districts, then examines detection methods like compactness scores and mean-median statistical tests. However, she reveals a crucial complication: even fairly-drawn compact districts show statistical bias because Democrats naturally cluster in cities and college towns, effectively 'gerrymandering themselves.' This geographic reality means Republicans benefit from any districting system, giving them no incentive to reform it.
key_points: The optimal gerrymandering strategy is to win many districts by small margins while losing few districts by huge margins
Algorithmic solutions exist to create compact, fair districts with equal voter populations
Two main detection methods are compactness scores (perimeter-to-area ratio) and statistical bias tests (mean vs median vote share)
Democrats' natural geographic clustering in cities creates inherent 'self-gerrymandering' that benefits Republicans even in fairly-drawn districts
The structural advantage Republicans gain from any districting system removes their incentive to support reform
money_quotes: The system was invented well before the internet, and it is a ridiculous and unnecessary artifact from the days when information didn't travel well.
If you think about it for a while, you'll come up with the following observation: you should win by a thin margin but lose huge.
Democrats are quite concentrated in cities and college towns, and then they are more sparse elsewhere. They, in essence, gerrymander themselves.
There's no reason to believe we'll ever get out of this stupid districting system, because it gives an inherent advantage to Republicans. So why would they agree to tossing it?
In this crazy pseudo-democratic world of ours, we're still voting locally and asking delegates to ride their horses to a centralized location to cast a vote for the group.
posts.key_insight: Even perfectly fair, algorithmically-drawn districts would still favor Republicans because Democrats' natural geographic clustering in cities creates an inherent structural disadvantage that no technical solution can fix.

posts.summary: The Office of the Inspector General for the NYPD released a report using statistical analysis to demonstrate that 'Broken Windows' policing doesn't reduce felony crime. The six-year study found no correlation between quality-of-life summonses or misdemeanor arrests and drops in felony crime. In fact, as quality-of-life enforcement dramatically declined between 2010-2015, felony crime did not increase. The report recommends the NYPD take a more data-driven approach and analyze whether their enforcement disproportionately impacts minorities and young males. The NYPD has called the report flawed and promised a response within 90 days.
key_points: OIG-NYPD found no statistical evidence linking quality-of-life enforcement (summonses, misdemeanor arrests) to the drop in felony crime over six years
Quality-of-life summons rates dramatically declined between 2010-2015 with no corresponding increase in felony crime
The report recommends analyzing whether enforcement disproportionately impacts black and Hispanic residents, males aged 15-20, and NYCHA residents
The NYPD disputes the findings and will respond within 90 days
money_quotes: OIG-NYPD found no evidence that the drop in felony crime observed over the past six years was related to quality-of-life summonses or quality-of-life misdemeanor arrests.
Whatever has contributed to the observed drop in felony crime remains an open question worthy of further analysis.
The report goes on to say that the NYPD should take a more data driven approach to deciding what's actually working and what isn't.
Very happy about this report, it's been a super long time coming.
posts.key_insight: Statistical analysis reveals that the foundational assumption of Broken Windows policing—that aggressive enforcement of minor violations reduces serious crime—has no empirical support, making the case for data-driven reform of police practices.

posts.summary: In the wake of Brexit's surprising outcome, the post examines why polls, betting markets, and financial markets all predicted a Remain victory when Leave actually won. The author notes that polls had consistently shown Remain ahead by 52-48%, but the actual result was the exact opposite. Rather than offering definitive answers, O'Neil presents five hypotheses for the polling failure, ranging from demographic voting patterns to social desirability bias to methodological limitations in exit polling. The piece serves as a cautionary tale about the limits of prediction when human behavior and social pressures complicate data collection.
key_points: Polls, betting markets, financial markets, and exit polls all pointed to Remain winning, yet all were wrong by a significant margin
Older voters, who skewed toward Leave, may have been underweighted despite being more likely to actually vote
Social desirability bias may have caused Leave voters to hide their intentions due to accusations of racism against the Leave campaign
Betting and financial market positions may reflect hedging strategies rather than genuine predictions of outcomes
Exit polls have a methodological bias toward urban areas, which skewed Remain
money_quotes: The actual results were the opposite, Remain lost by 48% to 52%.
Stock markets can also embed beliefs, and in this case they definitely seemed to think Britain would vote to remain in the EU.
People lie in polls, and given that the Leave campaign was being accused of racism, it's maybe easier to lie towards Remain than the other way around.

People might have actually put money in the betting markets, including the financial markets, that have nothing to do with their belief of the outcome but rather represents a hedge for another position.
As for the exit polls, they are easier to take in cities, where there are a lot of people, but where there also tend to be more 'Remain' voters.
posts.key_insight: When social pressure makes certain positions uncomfortable to admit publicly, traditional polling methods may systematically misread voter intent, revealing the gap between what people say and how they actually behave.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reflects on a This American Life episode about fatness that challenged her own assumptions about weight and identity. She relates to the first story about accepting being fat and rejecting euphemisms like 'overweight,' but the third story forces her to confront her own internal fat-shaming toward people larger than her 'Lane Bryant Fat' category. She admits to harboring judgmental thoughts toward very fat people despite knowing how hard she's tried to change her own weight. The piece ends with her personal dilemma about gastric bypass surgery for diabetes prevention, which feels like betraying her hard-won fat identity.
key_points: The descriptor 'fat' is preferable to 'overweight,' which implies a temporary problem needing fixing
There are gradations of fatness - 'Lane Bryant Fat' women can find clothes and feel normal, while very fat people face true isolation and accommodation barriers
Even people who have struggled with their weight can harbor internal fat-shaming toward those larger than themselves
Childhood weight-based punishment (like withholding allowance for not losing weight) creates lasting shame
Gastric bypass surgery as diabetes treatment creates identity conflict for those who have built a fat-positive self-image
money_quotes: She'd prefer the descriptor 'fat' than the alternative, 'overweight,' which is both a useless euphemism and a judgment, that it was somehow a temporary problem that would soon be fixed. Fuck that.
She is now married to a man who admitted on tape that he wouldn't love her if she were fat.
On a normal day, some little voice inside me, some mean part of me, says, at least I haven't let myself go that much.

Considering how hard I know I've tried in the past to change, you'd think I would be more enlightened about this issue, but until I heard this radio segment, I had never examined my own, internal version of fat shaming.
For a time as a tweenager, I wasn't given my allowance unless I'd lost enough weight each week. It was cruel, humiliating, and it imbued me with a shame that lasted longer than I'd care to admit.
For someone who has spent 20 years establishing a (Lane Bryant) fat identity, it's actually really confusing to imagine opting for the knife. I'd feel like a turncoat.
posts.key_insight: Even those who have fought to accept their own fatness can harbor internal fat-shaming toward people larger than themselves, revealing how deeply society's weight biases are internalized.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her experience recording the audiobook version of 'Weapons of Math Destruction' at Random House studios. She describes the challenges of voice recording, including retakes for flat tone and difficult phrases. Despite initial worries about finding typos or outdated content, she expresses satisfaction with the book while bracing for future negative reviews. She also announces the book's new website featuring early blurbs and a positive Publisher's Weekly review.
key_points: Recording an audiobook is surprisingly difficult, requiring many retakes for tone and emphasis
Certain phrases (like 'assist statistics') are nearly impossible to say aloud
The author is pleased with the book's content but anxiously anticipates negative reviews
The book has received positive early coverage including a Publisher's Weekly review
money_quotes: I'm constantly having to retake sentences, either because I thought my tone was too flat (I hate flat audiobook readers!), or wasn't emphasizing the right words, or because the words are just hard to say.
I promise to never, ever write the phrase 'assist statistics' in anything that might someday be read out loud, ever, anywhere.
I'm happy with the book! Is that ok to say (not out loud!!)? I'm holding on to this delicious feeling until the nasty reviews come out.
After that I'll just cry inside at all times.
posts.key_insight: Even authors who write confidently about weighty topics like algorithmic bias experience vulnerability and anxiety when their work faces public judgment.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the NSF should fund arXiv rather than leaving it to beg for small personal donations. She points out that arXiv plays a pivotal role in basic research across multiple scientific fields, yet operates on less than $1 million per year while the NSF has a $7.5 billion budget. Given the NSF's stated mission to 'promote the progress of science,' she sees the lack of support as inexplicable and outrageous.
key_points: arXiv operates on less than $1 million annually yet is left begging for $5 donations
The platform serves as critical infrastructure for mathematics, physics, computer science, and other fields
The NSF has a $7.5 billion budget and a mission to promote scientific progress
Journal publishing is expensive and time-consuming, making arXiv even more essential
money_quotes: What the fuck is wrong with the NSF? Why isn't it supporting the arXiv?
I have been offended and enraged recently to receive pleading emails from members of the hard-working Cornell University Library arXiv Team for money. As in, please give us $5.
This is a ridiculous state of affairs.
Right now arXiv... plays an absolutely pivotal role in basic research in this country, especially given the expense and time-consuming journal publishing process.
I cannot think of a better way for it to fulfill its mission than to support the maintenance and expansion of the arXiv.
posts.key_insight: Essential scientific infrastructure like arXiv should be publicly funded rather than left to survive on individual donations when federal science agencies have billion-dollar budgets.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the question of when AI is appropriate depends less on the technology itself than on how results are interpreted and applied. Using recidivism risk models as a case study, she demonstrates how algorithms like COMPAS perpetuate racial bias by producing different error rates for Black and white defendants. Rather than calling for elimination of such models, she advocates for understanding what they actually measure, questioning why they're used punitively, and retooling them to help rather than harm disadvantaged populations. She concludes that AI development isn't inherently inappropriate, but the homogeneous teams building it and their narrow optimization-focused training make most current AI applications problematic.
key_points: The definition of 'AI' at a White House event was never discussed, effectively encompassing any computerized decision-making system
The COMPAS recidivism model demonstrates algorithmic racism through different false positive/negative rates for Black versus white defendants
Recidivism scores could be retooled to identify who needs the most resources rather than who deserves longer punishment
Most data scientists have no training beyond optimization techniques, leaving them ill-equipped to consider social implications
AI teams' homogeneity in gender, class, and race creates a monoculture that produces narrow thinking and massive misunderstandings
money_quotes: People still automatically assume algorithms are more fair and objective than humans, and sometimes they are, but they are by no means perfect.
If our goal were to permanently brand and isolate a criminal class, we couldn't look for a better tool.
Building AI is almost never inappropriate in itself, but interpreting results of AI decision-making is incredibly complicated and can be destructive or constructive.
Part of AI – a big part – is the assumption that AI can solve every problem in essentially the same way.
AI teams are, generally speaking, homogenous in gender, class, and often race, and that monoculture gives rise to massive misunderstandings and narrow ways of thinking.
What is it about our punitive and vengeful justice system that makes us punish people in advance for crimes they have not yet committed?
posts.key_insight: The appropriateness of AI depends not on the technology itself but on whether diverse, thoughtful teams interpret and apply its results in ways that help rather than harm vulnerable populations.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil endorses Campaign Zero's call to end Broken Windows policing, arguing that this approach has criminalized communities of color and created feedback loops that perpetuate bias. She critiques a New York Times study on police shootings for ignoring how uneven policing increases the likelihood of police interactions for Black Americans. Using NYC's Stop and Frisk as a case study, she shows how the policy disproportionately targeted minorities while being ineffective at addressing violent crime. O'Neil connects this to predictive policing algorithms, explaining how biased data from these encounters gets fed into systems that compound the original discrimination in sentencing and parole decisions.
key_points: Broken Windows policing leads to over-policing of minority communities and excessive force in minor situations
Studies on police shooting bias are incomplete if they don't account for the vastly higher chance of police interactions for Black Americans
NYC's Stop and Frisk stopped young Black men more times than there were young Black men in the city, yet only 1 in 1000 stops linked to violent crime
Biased policing data feeds into predictive policing and sentencing algorithms, creating self-reinforcing cycles of discrimination
The Broken Windows theory was never proven effective and optimizes for the wrong definition of success
money_quotes: It's much more likely for blacks, especially young black men, to end up in an interaction with cops. And that's due in large part to the broken theory of Broken Windows policing.
The number of stops of young black men exceeded the entire city population of young black men (168,126 as compared to 158,406).
About 1 in 1000 such stops are directly linked to a violent crime.
When City Hall decides to use predictive policing based on this data, they end up over policing the same neighborhoods, creating even more uneven and biased data.
That continuing stream of data even ends up in sentencing and paroling algorithms, making it more likely for those same over-policed populations to stay in jail longer.
It's high time we get rid of the root cause, the theory of Broken Windows, which was never proven in the first place.
posts.key_insight: Broken Windows policing creates a vicious cycle where biased enforcement generates biased data that then feeds algorithmic systems, compounding discrimination at every stage from patrol routes to prison sentences.

posts.summary: O'Neil reviews 'The Wellness Syndrome,' a book arguing that modern wellness culture has become a tool for shifting blame from systemic failures onto individuals. The authors trace how politicians from Thatcher to Clinton promoted individual responsibility as a shield against governmental accountability. The result is a depoliticized populace that internalizes shame for failing to be sufficiently optimized, rather than questioning inadequate job markets or discriminatory systems. O'Neil connects this to her own work on how employers increasingly control workers' bodies and lives through wellness programs and data collection.
key_points: Wellness culture transforms from self-care into self-blame: if anything is wrong with your life, it's because you failed to optimize yourself
The 'positive mindset' movement pairs perfectly with neoliberal politics, depoliticizing economic precarity by making it a personal failing
The gig economy demands workers appear perpetually happy and employable while taking full responsibility for increasingly unstable conditions
Politicians across the spectrum (Thatcher, Blair, Reagan, Clinton) weaponized individual responsibility to deflect from governmental accountability
O'Neil wonders how online dating and futurist subcultures like Kurzweil's immortality pursuit represent extreme manifestations of this syndrome
money_quotes: It's not just that you are expected to care for yourself, it's that you are blamed if you don't, and conversely, if there's anything at all wrong with your life, then it's because you've failed to sufficiently take care of yourself.

We are asked to enjoy ourselves while we take full personal responsibility for our own wellness, which in the age of the gig economy means we always appear happy to stay lively and infinitely employable under increasingly precarious economic conditions.
God forbid we ever accept any actual limit to our powers of reinvention, because that is equivalent to giving up.
We don't think of ourselves as political victims when we fall prey to a narcissistic worldview in which we are never fit enough, never eating enough organic kale, and never productive enough.
We internalize the shame and the definition of ourselves as 'improperly optimized.'
In the end, we all walk around with tiny little versions of Reagan's welfare queens in our heads.
posts.key_insight: Wellness culture serves as a depoliticizing force that transforms systemic failures into personal inadequacies, making people blame themselves rather than question the systems that constrain them.

posts.summary: This guest post by Brian D'Alessandro critically examines Roland Fryer's Harvard study claiming zero racial bias in police shootings. The analysis identifies two fundamental flaws: the data comes from a single precinct (Houston) using self-reported data, creating potential selection bias, and the sampling methodology conflates different populations in ways that can mathematically mask real bias. D'Alessandro demonstrates through Bayes' rule how a biased sampling scheme can neutralize actual population-level bias, making the study's conclusions unreliable. He argues the real question isn't whether cops shoot equally at armed suspects of different races, but why black men are more likely to face police force in the first place.
key_points: Data from a single police precinct creates selection bias—departments with better track records may be more willing to share data
The sampling methodology drew positive cases (shootings) from a different distribution than negative cases (justified force arrests), invalidating the comparison
Biased sampling can mathematically undo real population bias, as shown through Bayes' rule application
The study bisects a causal chain: if black people face more non-lethal force (which the study confirms), they're more likely to end up in escalated situations leading to shootings
The paper was released without peer review and pushed through the NY Times, creating sticky headlines that are hard to undo
money_quotes: A police department with a culture that successfully avoids systematic racial discrimination may be more willing than others to share their data than one that doesn't.
A biased sampling mechanism can in fact undo the bias that exists in the original data population.

The issue at hand is not whether cops are more likely to shoot at black suspects who are pointing guns straight back at the cop. The more important questions, which is not addressed, is why are black men more likely to pushed up against the wall by a cop in the first place.
Could it be possible that an officer is more likely to find his/her life in danger when a black, as opposed to a white, suspect reaches for his wallet?
I see the causal chain being unreasonably bisected here, and this is a pretty significant conceptual error.
Headlines are sticky, and first impressions are hard to undo.
Any bias in the population can therefore effectively be undone by a sampling scheme that is also racially biased.
posts.key_insight: A study's sampling methodology can mathematically mask the very bias it claims to measure, making headlines about 'no racial bias in police shootings' dangerously misleading when the underlying data and methods don't support such conclusions.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the launch of ORCAA (O'Neil Risk Consulting and Algorithmic Auditing), a company dedicated to auditing algorithms and assessing their risks. She discusses her research into algorithmic auditing methods, highlighting Oaxaca's decomposition as a framework for separating discriminatory outcomes into 'worse attributes' and 'worse coefficients.' Using credit scoring as an example, she explains why both the model's coefficients and the choice of input variables matter for detecting discrimination. She acknowledges a key limitation: current methods can detect problems but don't necessarily tell us how to fix them.
key_points: ORCAA aims to audit algorithms and conduct risk assessments, starting as consulting and potentially evolving into a formal auditing firm with open methodologies
Oaxaca's decomposition separates outcome differences into two components: the effect of different attributes between groups and the effect of different coefficients applied to those attributes
In algorithmic fairness, both the coefficients (the recipe) and the input selection matter—discriminatory coefficients are problematic, but so is choosing discriminatory inputs
A fundamental limitation of current auditing methods: they can detect problems but don't prescribe solutions
money_quotes: ORCAA will audit algorithms and conduct risk assessments for algorithms, first as a consulting entity and eventually, if all goes well, as a more formal auditing firm, with open methodologies and toolkits.

The coefficients are the ingredients in the recipe that takes the input and gives the output, so if they fundamentally discriminate against blacks, for example, that would be bad (but it has to be carefully defined!).
We also care about which inputs we choose in the first place, which is why there are laws about not being able to use race or gender in credit scoring.
Importantly, this analysis won't necessarily tell us what to do about the differences we pick up.
We can detect problems but we don't learn how to address them.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic auditing must examine both the model's coefficients and its choice of inputs to detect discrimination, though detecting problems is easier than knowing how to fix them.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil warns about a dangerous new partnership between ZestFinance and Baidu that will use Chinese citizens' browsing history to determine credit scores. She argues that the phrase 'giving consumers more access to credit' is propaganda that masks discriminatory practices. The partnership combines ZestFinance's philosophy that 'all data is credit data' with Baidu's massive trove of personal search data, including sensitive health and employment queries. Unlike the U.S., China lacks anti-discrimination laws for credit scoring, meaning anything goes. O'Neil sees this as a nightmare scenario where people's most vulnerable moments become weapons against them, with no transparency or appeals process.
key_points: The phrase 'we give consumers more access to credit' is code for ignoring discriminatory costs while highlighting marginal benefits
ZestFinance's claim that they can't explain why spelling correlates with creditworthiness is likely a cover for discriminating against poorly-educated people
Baidu's search data captures people's 'most vulnerable hopes and fears' - health concerns, job searches - which will now affect credit decisions
China lacks the anti-discrimination laws that provide at least some protection in U.S. credit scoring
The system offers no transparency about why credit is denied and no way to appeal decisions
money_quotes: That's code for a longer phrase: 'we're doing anything at all we want, with personal information, possibly discriminatory and destructive, but there are a *few* people who will benefit from this new system versus the old, so we're ignoring costs and only counting the benefits for those people, in an attempt to distract any critics.'
Between you and me, I think he's lying. I think he just doesn't like to say he happily discriminates against poor people who have gone to bad schools.
The company collects information on a person's most vulnerable hopes and fears.

Now put these two together, which they already did thankyouverymuch, and you've got a toxic cocktail of personal information, on the one hand, and absolutely no hesitation in using information against people, on the other.
Someone who recently googled for how to treat an illness might not get that loan, even if they were simply trying to help their friend or family member.
For a small amount of very carefully handled loss of privacy, to get more easily available credit, I think that's going to be an easy choice.
posts.key_insight: When companies claim alternative credit scoring 'expands access,' they're often masking systems that weaponize people's most vulnerable moments against them while evading accountability.

posts.summary: The Computer Fraud and Abuse Act criminalizes violations of website terms of service, even when researchers are investigating algorithmic discrimination for the public good. O'Neil highlights how this law impedes important work like ProPublica's investigation into racist recidivism models. She cites research arguing that discrimination audits, while technically violating ethical guidelines, serve a clear public good and should be permitted. The authors argue that moving discrimination into algorithms has actually protected the wicked by making it harder to detect while simultaneously restricting the audit methods that could expose it. The post advocates for CFAA reform to include auditing exceptions in the public interest.
key_points: The CFAA currently criminalizes terms of service violations even when done for legitimate research purposes
ProPublica's influential recidivism algorithm investigation was likely a technical CFAA violation
Traditional discrimination audits (like sending matched resumes to employers) have always bent ethical guidelines but are accepted as serving the public good
Algorithmic discrimination is harder to detect than face-to-face discrimination, yet the CFAA makes it even harder to audit
The law treats corporate processor time as more precious than the resources wasted in decades of in-person audit studies
money_quotes: The CFAA currently criminalizes violations of terms of services for websites, even when those terms of service are written in a narrow way and the violation is being done for the public good.
The CFAA keeps researchers from understanding how algorithms work.
The movement of unjust face-to-face discrimination into computer algorithms appears to have the net effect of protecting the wicked.

Algorithmic discrimination may be much more opaque and hard to detect than earlier forms of discrimination, while at the same time one important mode of monitoring—the audit study—has been circumscribed.

The CFAA treats computer processor time as and a provider's 'authorization' as far more precious than the minutes researchers have stolen from honest landlords and employers over the last few decades. This appears to be fundamentally misguided.
posts.key_insight: By criminalizing the methods researchers need to audit algorithms for discrimination, the CFAA inadvertently protects algorithmic bias from scrutiny.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares a roundup of recent developments she finds encouraging, from the elimination of New York's tampon tax to new efforts at algorithmic accountability. She highlights a new bail algorithm from the Arnold Foundation, praising its avoidance of race and class proxies while criticizing its marketing claims of 'objectivity' without transparent auditing. The post also points to Julia Angwin's call for algorithmic accountability, new research on debiasing sexist word corpora, and concerns about underpaying Mechanical Turk workers in academic research.
key_points: New York eliminated the tampon tax, part of a broader push for menstrual equality
The Arnold Foundation's new bail algorithm avoids education and other class proxies, but its claims of 'objectivity' remain unsubstantiated without transparent auditing
Julia Angwin is calling for algorithmic accountability in a New York Times op-ed
Researchers are using vector algebra to debias sexist language in word corpora
Mechanical Turk workers powering academic research are being underpaid and deserve minimum wage protections
money_quotes: As my friend Josh says, we have to remember to celebrate our victories!!
The good news is, they aren't using educational background and other race and class proxies in the algorithm.
The bad news is, they're marketing it just like all the other problematic WMD algorithms out there.
I believe the consistent part, but I'd like to see some data about the claim of objectivity.
At the very least, Arnold Foundation, can you promise a transparent auditing process of your bail algorithms?
Let's require a reasonable hourly minimum wage for academic work. NSF?
posts.key_insight: Even when algorithms avoid obvious proxies for race and class, claims of 'objectivity' ring hollow without transparent auditing processes.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the American 401(k) system is fundamentally flawed both in practice and theory. Most Americans aren't saving adequately for retirement, and even if they were, individual savings accounts are mathematically inefficient compared to pooled systems. She contends that expecting everyone to save for worst-case scenarios (living to 105 with expensive end-of-life care) represents massive societal inefficiency. The solution is to expand Social Security, which pools risk effectively and remains financially viable despite popular misconceptions.
key_points: Americans have consistently failed to save adequately since the shift from pensions to 401(k)s
Individual retirement savings are mathematically inefficient because everyone must prepare for worst-case longevity scenarios
Pooled retirement systems spread risk efficiently—some die young, some live long, but costs even out
Social Security isn't going broke and could be strengthened to provide adequate retirement income
The current system fails both practically (people don't save) and theoretically (individual saving is inefficient)
money_quotes: Ever since we chucked widespread employer based pension systems for 401K's and personal responsibility, people just haven't done very well saving.
It's actually, mathematically speaking, extremely dumb to have 401K's instead of a larger pool of retirement money like pensions or Social Security.
For everyone to prepare for the worst is total overkill. Extremely inefficient to the point of hoarding, in fact.
Lots of people die young and are relatively 'cheap' for the pool, and some people live really long but since it's all pooled, things even out.
Social Security isn't going broke, could easily be made whole and then some, and is the right thing to do – both morally and mathematically – for our nation.
posts.key_insight: Individual retirement savings are not just failing in practice—they're mathematically inferior to pooled systems like Social Security because risk-pooling eliminates the need for everyone to save for worst-case scenarios.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil criticizes Microsoft for deflecting responsibility for a facial recognition tool designed to read emotions at political rallies by suggesting ethical questions should be directed to 'futurists.' She argues this response both absolves technologists of accountability for their creations and absurdly positions futurists—a group she characterizes as homogeneous Silicon Valley insiders obsessed with the singularity—as moral authorities. O'Neil contends that futurists lack the historical and political knowledge needed to address civil liberties concerns, comparing them to the Empire in Star Wars while protesters represent the Rebel Alliance.
key_points: Microsoft's spokesperson deflected ethical responsibility for surveillance technology by claiming such questions belong to 'futurists, not technologists'
Technologists are using rhetorical strategies to avoid accountability for tools they intentionally designed for social control
Futurism as a field is dominated by white male Silicon Valley insiders focused on singularity speculation rather than civil rights or political history
There is a fundamental disconnect between who creates surveillance tools and who is positioned as responsible for their ethical implications
The people most affected by these technologies (protesters, dissidents) have no voice in the 'futurist' circles being invoked as authorities
money_quotes: Yesterday one of my long-standing fears was confirmed: futurists are considered moral authorities.
I think that would be a question for a futurist, not a technologist.
So yeah, I created this efficient plug-and-play tool of social control, but that doesn't mean I expect people to use it!
There's no reason to imagine that they are well-versed in the history or in the rights of protesters or of political struggle.
In Star Wars terms, the futurists are the Empire, and Black Lives Matter are the scrappy Rebel Alliance.
It's pretty clear, to me at least, that we wouldn't go to Emperor Palpatine for advice on ethics.
posts.key_insight: Tech companies deflect moral responsibility for surveillance tools by invoking 'futurists' as ethical authorities, despite futurists being ill-equipped to address civil liberties and representing the same power structures that threaten them.

posts.summary: This guest post by mathematician Matilde Marcolli challenges the Silicon Valley monopoly on futurist thinking by tracing its themes back to early 20th century Russian Futurism and Cosmism. She argues that the crucial difference between capitalist and anarchist futurism isn't the topics they explore—immortality, space, AI—but whose future they envision. The post introduces Anarcho-Transhumanism as an anti-capitalist alternative that embraces technology for liberation rather than elite preservation. Marcolli highlights 'morphological freedom' as a key concept, emphasizing transgender rights and disability rights over botox treatments for billionaires. She concludes that science and technology have enormous potential when used inclusively rather than for profit and exploitation.
key_points: Silicon Valley futurism's core themes (digital immortality, space colonization, AI superintelligence) all originated in Russian Futurism and Cosmism a century ago
The essential distinction between futurisms is not their subject matter but their vision of who benefits—wealthy elites or all of humanity
Anarcho-Transhumanism offers an anti-capitalist, pro-technology alternative rooted in movements like Afrofuturism, Cyberfeminism, and Xenofeminism
Morphological freedom in anarchist thinking prioritizes transgender rights and challenging ableism, not luxury enhancements for the wealthy
Grassroots initiatives like biohacker spaces and DIY synthetic biology represent technology serving liberation rather than capital
money_quotes: It is easy to laugh off all these ideas as the typical preoccupations of a group of aging narcissist wealthy white males, whose greatest fear is that an artificial intelligence may one day treat them the way they have been treating everybody else all along.

The crucial difference in these forms of futurism does not lie in the themes of speculation, but rather in the role of humanity in this envisioned future. Is this the future of a wealthy elite? Is this the future of a classless society?
The Vitruvian Man, which was the very symbol of Humanism, was also a symbol of the intrinsically exclusionary nature of Humanism.
When children are given the possibility to design and 3D print their own prosthetics, they make colorful arms that launch darts and flying saucers and that make them look like superheroes.
Science and technology have enormous potential, if used inclusively and for the benefit of all and not with goals of profit and exploitation.
posts.key_insight: The question that defines any futurism isn't what technologies it imagines, but whose liberation those technologies are meant to serve.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that the final physical copy of her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' has arrived, sharing photos of her editor and son holding it. She notes the book has received a Reuters review and was listed in Scientific American's new releases. She apologizes in advance for the upcoming promotional blitz, acknowledging that self-promotion isn't her usual style but is necessary for book launches.
key_points: The physical copy of 'Weapons of Math Destruction' has been printed and is available for pre-order
The book has already received media attention including a Reuters BreakingViews review and Scientific American listing
O'Neil is self-aware and somewhat apologetic about the necessary self-promotion authors must do
Her son has volunteered to become a meme to help with book sales
money_quotes: I've been told it's my job for the next few months to be a PR person for my book, so I guess that's what I'm up to.
If you come here for ideas and are turned off by cheerleading, feel free to temporarily hate me, and even unsubscribe to whatever feed I'm in for you!
But please buy my book first.
posts.key_insight: Even authors who write serious critiques of algorithmic power must navigate the awkward transition from intellectual commentator to book promoter.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that Donald Trump operates like a biased machine learning algorithm, lacking moral principles but following a consistent method: optimizing for crowd engagement at rallies. He iteratively tests rhetoric, keeping what gets reactions and discarding what bores. This process, trained on biased data (rally attendees), produces outputs that work for his audience but alienate everyone else. O'Neil uses this analogy to illustrate broader dangers of algorithmic decision-making and critiques utopian visions of robot politicians.
key_points: Trump's behavior can be modeled as an optimization algorithm maximizing 'not being boring' rather than following principles or ideology
His 'training data' is biased because rally attendees are unrepresentative of the broader population, leading to outputs that appeal to them but repel others
The lack of underlying beliefs makes his policy positions inherently vague and malleable based on crowd response
Algorithms controlling society face the same fundamental problems as human politics: someone must define success metrics and training data
There is no 'pure rationality' in politics—it always involves choosing whose desires to prioritize
money_quotes: He doesn't have any principles to speak of, that might guide him. No moral compass.
That doesn't mean he doesn't have a method. He does, but it's local rather than global.
If they respond by getting agitated, that's a lot better than being bored. That's how he learns.
He's got biased training data, because the people at his rallies are a particular type of weirdo.
He's perfectly objective, as in morally neutral. He just follows the numbers.
He could be replaced by a robot that acts on a machine learning algorithm with a bad definition of success.
It's a great way of understanding how machine learning algorithms can give us stuff we absolutely don't want, even though they fundamentally lack prior agendas.
There's no such thing as pure rationality in politics, it's much more about picking sides and appealing to some people's desires while ignoring others.
posts.key_insight: Algorithms, like Trump, can produce harmful outcomes not through malicious intent but through bad success metrics and biased training data—there is no escaping the need for human judgment about values.

posts.summary: O'Neil uses her personal experience with a backyard pool to challenge our perception of 'clean' swimming pool water. After her pool turned green from two weeks without maintenance, she observed how massive amounts of liquid chlorine transformed it back to blue. She argues that what we call 'clean' pool water is actually so chemically toxic that algae cannot survive in it. The post is a lighthearted observation about semantics rather than a health warning, clarifying in an update that she's not anti-chemical, just questioning the word 'clean.'
key_points: The green Olympic pools in Rio sparked public health concerns, but 'clean' pools aren't actually clean in any natural sense
Pool water that appears clean is actually so chemically treated that no algae can live in it
The transformation from green to clear requires multiple rounds of chemical treatment
The post is about accurate language, not about chemicals being harmful
money_quotes: Pools that look clean are not clean. They would be better described as, 'so toxic that algae cannot live in it.'
Long story short: don't be deceived by 'clean' pool water. There's nothing clean about it.
I'm just saying 'clean water' isn't an appropriate description. It's not as if it's pure water, and we pour tons of stuff in to get it to look like that.
posts.key_insight: What we call 'clean' pool water is actually chemically-treated water so toxic that nothing can live in it—a reminder that common descriptive language often obscures reality.

posts.summary: O'Neil challenges the common definition of alpha as risk-adjusted returns above market performance, arguing that alpha actually represents returns uncorrelated to the market. She explains that the standard regression formula only captures market-correlated risk, leaving other risks invisible in the error terms. Using CAPM theory embeds assumptions that alpha should be zero, but her quant definition makes no such assumptions. The post warns that this distinction matters because hedge funds and portfolio managers exploit the ambiguity to hide risk while claiming impressive performance metrics.
key_points: Alpha is commonly defined as excess returns above market given risk, but quants define it as returns uncorrelated to market performance
The standard regression formula only displays market-correlated risk; remaining risks are unmeasured and hidden in error terms
Alpha is not truly risk-adjusted, only market-risk-adjusted, which means portfolios can have large alpha while carrying substantial hidden risk
Risk itself cannot be directly measured and is typically inferred from volatility of past returns, which failed spectacularly in 2008
Portfolio managers exploit this ambiguity to underestimate risk and claim high Sharpe ratios
money_quotes: Alpha is not truly risk-adjusted. It's only market-risk-adjusted.
The above regression formula only displays the market-correlated risk, and the remaining risk is unmeasured.
Past performance is not always indicative of future performance.
Lots of credit instruments like credit default swaps move very little on a daily basis but then suddenly lose tremendous value overnight.
Portfolio managers are constantly underestimating the risk they take on, partly because – or entirely because – they can then claim to have a high Sharpe ratio.
The problem lies in the possibility that people are bragging when they shouldn't, especially when they're hiding risk, and especially especially if your money is on the line.
posts.key_insight: The financial industry's flexible definition of alpha allows portfolio managers to hide substantial risk while claiming superior performance, putting investors' money at risk.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines a peer-reviewed study evaluating Chicago's predictive policing 'Heat List' system, which claims to identify individuals likely to be involved in shootings. The study found that being on the list did not protect people from becoming victims of violence, nor did it reduce crime rates beyond pre-existing downward trends. However, individuals on the list experienced dramatically increased police surveillance and were more likely to be arrested. The key finding is that the list predicts who police will arrest, not who will commit crimes—essentially forecasting police behavior rather than criminal behavior. O'Neil argues this exposes the circular logic of predictive policing systems that claim success based on arrest rates.
key_points: The Heat List did not reduce homicides or shootings beyond pre-existing downward crime trends
Being on the list did not protect individuals from becoming victims of violence
Individuals on the list were 50% more likely to have police contact and 90% had some interaction with police
The list predicts arrests but doesn't cause criminal behavior—people on it were already more likely to be arrested regardless of increased contact
We cannot attribute success to policing policies without controlling for meta-effects like overall crime trend declines
money_quotes: Individuals on the SSL are not more or less likely to become a victim of a homicide or shooting than the comparison group
The treated group is more likely to be arrested for a shooting
The statistically significant reduction in monthly homicides predated the introduction of the SSL
We cannot automatically attribute success to any policing policy in the context of meta-effects
The additional contact with police did not result in an increased likelihood for arrests for shooting, that is, the list was not a catalyst for arresting people for shootings
From now on, I'll refer to Chicago's 'Heat List' as a way for the police to predict their own future harassment and arrest practices
posts.key_insight: Predictive policing systems like Chicago's Heat List don't actually prevent crime or protect people—they simply predict and facilitate increased police surveillance and arrests of targeted individuals.

posts.summary: This is a promotional announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares the upcoming book tour events for her new book 'Weapons of Math Destruction: How Big Data Increases Inequality and Threatens Democracy.' The post lists approximately ten public events across major US cities including New York, Seattle, San Francisco, Washington D.C., Cambridge, and Madison between September and October 2016. Several events feature conversations with notable figures like Felix Salmon and Jen Golbeck. The post serves purely as an informational calendar for readers who want to attend.
key_points: Book tour spans from September 8 to October 22, 2016 across major US cities
Events include readings, signings, and in-conversation formats with notable journalists and academics
Venues range from bookstores to libraries to conferences like Privacy.Security.Risk. 2016
The book's subtitle 'How Big Data Increases Inequality and Threatens Democracy' signals its critical thesis
money_quotes: Weapons of Math Destruction: How Big Data Increases Inequality and Threatens Democracy
posts.key_insight: This is a logistical book tour announcement rather than a substantive blog post, though the book title itself signals O'Neil's thesis that algorithmic systems can amplify societal harms.

posts.summary: O'Neil explains the BISG (Bayesian Improved Surname Geocoding) methodology, which uses last names and zip codes to estimate racial probabilities via Bayes' rule, relying on America's persistent residential segregation. She addresses Republican criticism that called it 'junk science' when the CFPB used it to detect racial discrimination in auto lending. O'Neil argues the methodology could be improved by updating priors with auto lending population distributions, which would address the technical complaints. She suggests the real opposition isn't statistical but political—similar to voter fraud concerns where rare errors are used to justify inaction on systemic problems. The post includes Python code implementing BISG as a practical resource for researchers.
key_points: BISG methodology exploits American residential segregation and distinctive surnames to estimate race probabilities using Bayesian updating
Republican 'junk science' criticism centers on the methodology using general population data rather than auto-lending-specific distributions—a fixable technical issue
The real political objection parallels voter fraud rhetoric: highlighting rare false positives (white people receiving discrimination restitution) to justify ignoring widespread true positives
Bayes' rule allows for updating priors with domain-specific data, which would technically satisfy critics but likely wouldn't change their opposition
O'Neil provides open-source Python implementation because existing tools were only in expensive proprietary software
