I say sense because it really doesn't guarantee free speech at all, and people are kicked off social media all the time, for random reasons as well as for well-thought out policies.
To ignore their contributions would be foolish from the perspective of the public.
It's also difficult to precisely quantify the externalities of the kinds of problems stemming from a complete indifference and immunization from consequences that the platforms currently enjoy.
posts.key_insight: Platforms are not neutral conduits but profit-driven machines whose designs actively shape content spread, making their legal immunity from that content increasingly untenable.

posts.summary: O'Neil warns about two emerging technologies that will dramatically worsen the fake news problem: software that can edit recorded speech and technology that can manipulate facial expressions in video. She argues that combining these technologies will enable the creation of entirely fictitious but believable video conversations between public figures like Trump and Putin, making our current fake news challenges seem minor by comparison.
key_points: Speech editing technology now allows manipulation of what someone appears to say in recordings
Facial expression editing (which she attributes to Pixar-style animation advances) can insert any expression into video
The combination of these technologies enables creation of completely fabricated but convincing video content
Current fake news problems will seem 'quaint' compared to what's coming
money_quotes: Put those two technologies together and you've got Trump and Putin having an entirely fictitious but believable conversation on video.

My buddy Josh Vekhter is visiting from his Ph.D. program in computer science and told me about a couple of incredibly creepy technological advances that will soon make our previous experience of fake news seem quaint.
posts.key_insight: The convergence of speech and facial manipulation technologies will soon enable synthetic video content so convincing that it will fundamentally undermine our ability to trust video evidence.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil rejects traditional New Year's resolutions as individualistic, self-defeating exercises that distract from more important work. She argues that conventional resolutions make us focus on personal imperfections when we should be thinking bigger about societal issues. Instead, she offers her own unconventional list that prioritizes meaningful work, family, political engagement, and resistance over self-improvement. The post is a call to stop obsessing over personal flaws and start doing 'heavy lifting' on what matters. Her resolutions range from serious (categorizing algorithmic fairness research for activists, forming a plan to revive the Left) to deliberately provocative (gain 10 pounds, let the kitchen stay messy).
key_points: Traditional resolutions are 'flabby goals' designed for failure and self-blame
The individualistic focus of resolutions distracts from collective action and bigger thinking
Practical coping mechanisms matter more than perfectionist self-improvement
Political resistance and tracking who enables or fights injustice should be priorities
She's using a Ford Foundation grant to make algorithmic fairness research accessible to activists
money_quotes: They're flabby goals that we're meant not only to fail to achieve but to feel bad about personally.
What's worst to me is how individual and self-centered they are. They make us focus on how imperfect we are at a time when we should really think big.
We don't have time to obsess over details, people! Just get your coping mechanisms in place and do some heavy lifting, will you?
Let my kitchen get and stay messy so I can get some goddamned work done.
Keep up with the news and take note of how bad things are getting, who is letting it happen, who is resisting, and what kind of resistance is functional.
Gain 10 pounds and start smoking.
posts.key_insight: Stop wasting energy on individualistic self-improvement guilt and redirect it toward collective action and political resistance.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that recidivism risk algorithms are inherently discriminatory due to a fundamental mathematical trade-off between accuracy and fairness. Citing a 2011 academic paper by Kamiran and Calders, she explains that when training data reflects historical bias against certain groups, optimizing for accuracy necessarily perpetuates that discrimination. The paper demonstrates this trade-off is linear or sub-linear—reducing discrimination costs only a fraction of accuracy. O'Neil traces the data bias to broken windows policing and systemic racism in the justice system, referencing Michelle Alexander's work on mass incarceration.
key_points: There is a mathematical trade-off between algorithmic accuracy and discrimination when training data contains historical bias
Discrimination is measured as the difference in positive outcome rates between favored and discriminated groups
Reducing discrimination by 1% costs only a fraction of 1% in accuracy—a favorable trade-off
Recidivism algorithms optimized purely for 'catching bad guys' will inherently be discriminatory
The underlying data bias stems from broken windows policing and systemic racism in the justice system
money_quotes: When you have a dataset that has a 'favored' group of people and a 'discriminated' group of people, and you're deciding on an outcome that has historically been awarded to the favored group more often, then you cannot expect to maximize accuracy and keep the discrimination down to zero at the same time.
For every 1% loss of discrimination you can expect to lose a fraction of 1% of accuracy.

Any algorithm that is optimized for 'catching the bad guys,' i.e. accuracy, which these algorithms are, and completely ignores the discrepancy between high risk scores for blacks and for whites, can be expected to be discriminatory.
The bias is due to the history of heightened scrutiny of black neighborhoods by police which we know as broken windows policing, which makes blacks more likely to be arrested for a given crime.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing recidivism algorithms for accuracy while ignoring fairness guarantees discrimination because the historical data itself encodes systemic bias.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that despite their discriminatory nature, recidivism risk models should be fixed rather than abandoned, since the current justice system is already fundamentally unfair. She outlines three technical approaches from academic research—massaging, reweighing, and sampling training data—that can reduce algorithmic bias while complying with anti-discrimination laws. She notes the irony that these laws, which forbid considering race in decisions, actually constrain our ability to correct for discrimination. The post emphasizes that fixing these models requires broader societal conversations about justice and sentencing goals.
key_points: The current justice system is already discriminatory, so abandoning biased algorithms doesn't solve the underlying problem
Three preprocessing techniques can reduce bias: massaging training data (switching classifications), reweighing data, and sampling data
Anti-discrimination laws paradoxically prevent us from using information that could help avoid discrimination
Racial and class discrepancies in risk rates could be used to experiment with interventions that address root causes
Fixing recidivism models requires difficult conversations about justice, sentencing goals, and balancing stakeholder interests
money_quotes: The truth is, the current justice system is fundamentally unfair, so throwing out algorithms because they are also unfair is not a solution.
We're constrained by anti-discrimination law to not use all the information that might help us avoid discrimination.
This constraint, generally speaking, prevents us from doing as good a job as possible.
We might also use the racial and class discrepancies in recidivism risk rates as an opportunity to experiment with interventions that might lower those discrepancies.
I do not claim that this is a trivial process. It will in fact require lots of conversations about the nature of justice and the goals of sentencing. Those are conversations we should have.
posts.key_insight: Rather than abandoning flawed recidivism algorithms, we should fix them using bias-reduction techniques while recognizing that anti-discrimination laws ironically limit our ability to correct for the very discrimination they aim to prevent.

posts.summary: The post examines how algorithms can facilitate anti-competitive price-fixing, both intentionally and unintentionally. It highlights a case of deliberate algorithmic collusion among poster sellers, then introduces a more troubling scenario: a German petrol price-tracking app that inadvertently discourages price cuts by enabling instant competitor matching. The post warns that current legal frameworks are inadequate to address algorithmic collusion, especially when AI systems 'self-learn' to coordinate prices without explicit human instruction.
key_points: Algorithms can be deliberately programmed to collude on pricing, as in the cinema poster seller case
Unintentional algorithmic collusion may be even more concerning - price transparency tools can eliminate incentives for price competition
When competitors can instantly match any price cut before consumers benefit, no one has reason to cut prices
Current antitrust law has no framework for holding anyone liable when AI systems independently learn to coordinate prices
money_quotes: Not all such algorithmic collusion is necessarily so intentional.

As the algorithm instantly detects a petrol station price cut, allowing competitors to match the new price before consumers can shift to the discounter, there is no incentive for any vendor to cut in the first place.

Particularly in the case of artificial intelligence, there is no legal basis to attribute liability to a computer engineer for having programmed a machine that eventually 'self-learned' to co-ordinate prices with other machines.
posts.key_insight: Algorithms don't need to conspire to collude - market transparency itself can destroy price competition when machines react faster than consumers can benefit from discounts.

posts.summary: O'Neil revisits two previous posts with important clarifications. On algorithmic pricing, she concedes that automated price changes aren't necessarily harmful to consumers beyond occasional 'flash crash' disruptions. On recidivism algorithms, she acknowledges a more nuanced research paper that distinguishes between crude discrimination definitions and 'well-calibrated' models that account for different base rates. However, she maintains that even well-calibrated models are problematic if the underlying base rate differences stem from biased policing practices rather than actual behavioral differences.
key_points: Automated pricing algorithms may cause weird disruptions like flash crashes but aren't necessarily bad for consumers
A 'well-calibrated' recidivism model can have different prediction rates for different groups if those rates match actual recidivism rates
It's mathematically impossible to simultaneously achieve well-calibration, equal false positive rates, and equal false negative rates across groups
Different 'base rates' between racial groups may reflect discriminatory policing rather than actual behavioral differences
New York's introduction of recidivism scoring for bail hearings makes these theoretical questions urgently practical
money_quotes: The most important one is the question of why the base rates for blacks and whites are so different.
If it's because of police practice, at least in part, or overall increased surveillance of black communities, then I'd argue 'well-calibrated' is insufficient.
We need to be putting the science into data science and examining questions like this.
We cannot assume the data is somehow fixed in stone. All of this is a social construct.

This could be great if fewer people waste time in jail pending their hearings or trials, but if the people chosen to stay in prison are chosen on the basis that they're poor or minority or both, that's a problem.
posts.key_insight: Even mathematically 'well-calibrated' algorithms can perpetuate discrimination if the data they're trained on reflects biased systems like over-policing of minority communities.

posts.summary: O'Neil continues her exploration of fairness in algorithmic scoring systems, demonstrating that while you cannot simultaneously achieve calibration, equal false positive rates, and equal false negative rates across groups with different base rates, you can achieve any two of these three criteria. Using a visual example with simplified groups, she shows how adjusting for equal false positive rates necessarily creates unequal false negative rates when the model remains well-calibrated. She argues that in recidivism scoring, prioritizing equal false positive rates may be the better tradeoff since the harm of incorrectly imprisoning someone longer is more severe than letting someone off earlier.
key_points: A well-calibrated model cannot have both equal false positive and false negative rates across groups with different base rates
You can choose to optimize for two out of three fairness criteria, forcing a deliberate tradeoff
Different base rates between populations mathematically guarantee that mislabeling rates will differ
In criminal justice contexts, equalizing false positive rates may be preferable because wrongful imprisonment is more harmful than early release
O'Neil questions whether recidivism algorithms should be used at all, or are even constitutional
money_quotes: You cannot simultaneously ask for a model to be well-calibrated, to have equal false positive rates for blacks and whites, and to have equal false negative rates unless you are in the presence of equal 'base rates' or a perfect predictor.
The good news is that you can ask for two out of three of these.
Because we are assuming the model is well-calibrated, every time we have one kind of mistake we have to make up for that mistake with exactly one of the other type.
What we've done is sacrificed some false negative rate balance for a perfect match on the false positive rate, while keeping the model well-calibrated.

The harm that comes from unequal false positive rate – sending someone to jail for longer – is arguably more toxic than an unequal false negative rate, which means certain groups are let off the hook more often than the others.
I'm not even sure why their use is constitutional, to tell the truth.
Given that they are in use, I think it makes sense to try to make them as good as possible, and to investigate what 'good' means in this context.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic fairness requires choosing which type of unfairness to accept, and in criminal justice, we should prioritize not wrongly punishing people over not wrongly releasing them.

posts.summary: This appears to be an empty or placeholder post with only a title 'This might be interesting' and no actual content. Without any text to analyze, it's impossible to determine the author's argument, thesis, or conclusions. The post may have been a draft that was accidentally published, or the content may have been removed or failed to load during archival.
key_points: No substantive content is present in this post
The title suggests the author intended to share something noteworthy but the body is missing
Word count of 0 confirms no text content exists
posts.key_insight: This post contains no content to analyze - it appears to be an empty placeholder or failed archive.

posts.summary: O'Neil documents her experience knitting a pussyhat during Trump's inauguration and discovering that the knitting community had organically mobilized for political activism. She describes seeing pink yarn and pussyhats everywhere—at Vogue Knitting Live, in yarn stores, on planes heading to D.C., and proliferating on Ravelry. The post celebrates how craft communities can become unexpected sites of political resistance, channeling creative energy into collective action.
key_points: The pussyhat project transformed a solitary craft into a visible form of collective political protest
Knitting communities proved naturally suited for activism due to their collaborative, generous culture
The phenomenon spread rapidly across physical spaces (yarn stores, planes) and online platforms (Ravelry)
Crafting and activism aren't separate activities—the same people doing one are often doing the other
money_quotes: I thought I was going to enjoy the juxtaposition of activist-to-insane hobbiest, but I was wrong – knitters were activists too!
Now that it's happened, it's obvious that knitters are a perfect community for activism.
We're friendly, community-oriented, and desperate for an opportunity to make something and give it away.
Because it gives us an excuse to buy more yarn.
It took less than two hours because I was using super bulky yarn and because I had lots of anxious energy to tap into.
posts.key_insight: Craft communities like knitters are natural activists because their culture already values generosity, community, and creating things with purpose.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares that she has become a Bloomberg View columnist. She links to her first column about how fake news that deceives humans will also deceive AI systems. The post serves primarily as a notification to her blog readers about this new platform for her writing.
key_points: O'Neil has joined Bloomberg View as a columnist
Her first column explores the vulnerability of AI systems to fake news
The implicit argument is that AI trained on human-generated data inherits human susceptibility to misinformation
money_quotes: If Fake News Fools You, It Can Fool Robots, Too
posts.key_insight: AI systems are not immune to fake news—they inherit the same vulnerabilities as the humans whose data trains them.

posts.summary: This brief post announces two new publications by Cathy O'Neil: a Bloomberg column titled 'When Algorithms Come for Our Children' and a book review for Science Magazine of Andreas Weigend's 'Data for the People.' The post serves primarily as a pointer to these external pieces rather than containing substantive analysis itself. The book review title suggests O'Neil found Weigend's perspective too narrowly focused on tech-insider viewpoints while overlooking equity concerns.
key_points: O'Neil has a new Bloomberg column focused on algorithms affecting children
She reviewed 'Data for the People' by Andreas Weigend for Science Magazine
Her review criticizes the book for neglecting issues of access and equality
The review title suggests tech insiders often have blind spots about who benefits from data systems
money_quotes: When Algorithms Come for Our Children
A tech insider's data dreams will resonate with the like-minded but neglect issues of access and equality
posts.key_insight: Tech insider perspectives on data often fail to address fundamental questions of access and equality.

posts.summary: This brief post documents the author's participation in the JFK Airport protest against Trump's immigrant ban in January 2017. O'Neil shares footage and photos from the protest while highlighting how information about additional demonstrations spread through crowd chants. She also raises concerns that despite a judicial stay on the Executive Order, there are reports of border patrol agents not complying with court orders.
key_points: The author participated in the JFK Terminal 4 protest against the immigrant ban
Protests served as information networks, with details about future demonstrations spreading through chants
A judicial stay on the Executive Order may not be effectively enforced
Reports emerged of border patrol agents defying court orders at Dulles Airport
money_quotes: One of the cool things about the protest is how messages were sent and spread through the chants.
In case you've heard that a judge put a stay on the Executive Order about immigrants, there are plenty of reasons to question that.
It's also possible that border patrol agents are not obeying those orders.
posts.key_insight: Legal victories against unjust policies mean little if enforcement agencies refuse to comply with court orders.

posts.summary: This guest post urges New York City residents to apply for the ID NYC municipal identification card as a form of resistance against potential federal immigration enforcement. The program, established in 2014, allows residents to obtain photo ID without standard government documents, making it particularly useful for undocumented immigrants. A recent court ruling required the city to retain application records indefinitely, creating a potential database that could be subpoenaed by federal authorities. The author argues that if millions of additional New Yorkers apply, it would 'gum up the works' and make any fishing expeditions through the records far more difficult. The post provides practical instructions for applying and frames this bureaucratic act as accessible civic resistance.
key_points: ID NYC allows residents to obtain government photo ID using non-standard documents like foreign passports and utility bills, benefiting undocumented immigrants
A December 2016 court ruling forced the city to retain application records indefinitely, creating potential vulnerability to federal subpoena
The strategy is to overwhelm the database with applications from citizens and documented residents, making it harder to identify undocumented applicants
About 1 million people have applied, leaving 6.8 million eligible New Yorkers who could dilute the pool
The application process is straightforward and comparable to mailing a package at the post office
money_quotes: These applications could be used for fishing expeditions looking for our undocumented neighbors.
The solution? Gum up the works.
Blast the program with lots and lots of applications from NYC residents so that any authority that does manage to subpoena applications has an immense archive to wade through.
Yes, the Mayor has pledged to fight to keep this paperwork private. But we can't be sure how the courts will act when push comes to shove.
Maybe this isn't the most effective form of resistance, but it is an easy one that may do some good.
posts.key_insight: When government databases designed to help vulnerable populations become potential surveillance tools, one form of resistance is for non-vulnerable people to flood those databases with their own information, creating protective noise.

posts.summary: This is a brief link post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg column about Cambridge Analytica. The post references a Vice Motherboard article about Cambridge Analytica's role in Brexit and Trump's election, and links to her Bloomberg response titled 'Trump's Secret Sauce Is Just More Ketchup.' The dismissive title suggests O'Neil is skeptical of claims about Cambridge Analytica's revolutionary data capabilities.
key_points: O'Neil wrote a Bloomberg response to reporting about Cambridge Analytica's influence on Brexit and Trump
The headline 'Trump's Secret Sauce Is Just More Ketchup' signals skepticism about Cambridge Analytica's supposedly groundbreaking techniques
This post serves primarily as a pointer to her longer-form analysis elsewhere
money_quotes: Trump's 'Secret Sauce' Is Just More Ketchup
posts.key_insight: O'Neil appears to view Cambridge Analytica's much-hyped data operation as more ordinary than revolutionary, suggesting the 'secret sauce' is overhyped marketing rather than breakthrough technology.

posts.summary: This guest post by Becky Jaffe provides a curated list of progressive organizations to support in the wake of political changes. The list covers immigrant and refugee rights, civil rights and legal defense, LGBTQ rights, disability rights, democracy-building, and environmental causes. It's presented as a starting point for people wanting to connect with community organizations that advocate for marginalized groups. The author promises a Bay Area-specific follow-up and encourages readers to create similar regional lists.
key_points: The post is a practical resource guide rather than an analytical piece, providing direct links to dozens of organizations
Organizations are categorized by focus area: immigrant rights, civil rights, LGBTQ, disability, democracy, and environment
The framing emphasizes supporting 'the most marginalized sectors of our society' during a time of political threat
The author encourages readers to create similar localized lists for their own regions
money_quotes: Let's get busy supporting each other, people!
We have our work cut out for us and much joyful organizing ahead.

This list of national organizations is by no means comprehensive, just a good place to start if you want to get plugged in to community organizations that build power for the most marginalized sectors of our society.
posts.key_insight: In times of political crisis, concrete action through supporting established advocacy organizations is more valuable than abstract discussion.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg column where she argues that Donald Trump represents a kind of political singularity. The post itself contains no substantive argument, serving only as a pointer to the external piece. The actual analysis and arguments are contained in the linked Bloomberg article rather than this blog entry.
key_points: Post serves as announcement for Bloomberg column
The provocative title 'Donald Trump is the Singularity' suggests a comparison between Trump and the AI concept of technological singularity
No substantive content is present in the blog post itself
money_quotes: Donald Trump is the Singularity
posts.key_insight: This is a redirect post with no analyzable content beyond its provocative title linking Trump to the concept of singularity.

posts.summary: O'Neil promotes a viral Twitter campaign encouraging people to send postcards addressed to 'President Bannon' as a strategy to undermine Steve Bannon's influence over Trump. The tactic exploits Trump's ego by making him jealous of attention given to his advisor. The campaign gained significant traction on Twitter and was covered by USA Today. O'Neil frames it as a small but emotionally satisfying act of resistance.
key_points: The strategy relies on psychological manipulation—exploiting Trump's sensitivity to being overshadowed
Viral social media campaigns can translate into mainstream media coverage
Small symbolic acts of protest can provide emotional catharsis even if their direct impact is uncertain
money_quotes: How do you get rid of the influence of Steve Bannon's whispering in Trump's ear? The best strategy I've heard is to make Trump jealous of the attention.
It's a small act but it might make you feel great to do it.
posts.key_insight: Exploiting Trump's ego and need for attention may be more effective than direct confrontation in undermining his advisors' influence.

posts.summary: This is a brief event announcement for a panel discussion at NYU Journalism School about algorithms, data, and democracy. Cathy O'Neil will be speaking alongside ProPublica journalist Julia Angwin, moderated by Robert Lee Hotz. The event offers both in-person attendance and livestream options.
key_points: Panel discussion on algorithms, data, democracy, and news at NYU Journalism School
Features Julia Angwin, known for algorithmic accountability reporting at ProPublica
Event available via livestream for remote viewers
money_quotes: I'll be talking data, democracy, and the news with the amazing Julia Angwin
posts.key_insight: An invitation to a public conversation exploring how algorithms intersect with democracy and journalism.

posts.summary: This post links to a Bloomberg View column by Cathy O'Neil. The title suggests the article examines how Facebook is taking on a regulatory role in financial services, likely critiquing the platform's expanding influence over financial decisions without traditional oversight. The post itself contains no substantive content beyond the link.
key_points: Facebook is positioned as having regulatory-like power over financial matters
The post directs readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column for the full argument
posts.key_insight: The title implies a critique of how tech platforms like Facebook are acquiring quasi-regulatory power over financial systems without democratic accountability.

posts.summary: This brief link roundup post shares three pieces of O'Neil's recent work and collaborations on big data's societal impacts. She highlights her Bloomberg column examining how big data and search algorithms are distorting historical understanding, particularly around Holocaust information. She also points to a Vice collaboration on making technology serve human interests, and promotes a practical guide for requesting personal data from Cambridge Analytica.
key_points: Big data and search algorithms are actively undermining public trust in historical facts and statistics
Technology can and should be restructured to work for people rather than against them
Individual action against data surveillance is possible through formal data requests to companies like Cambridge Analytica
The fight against algorithmic manipulation requires both critique and practical resistance tools
money_quotes: big data is undermining our understanding and faith in historical facts and in statistics
how technology can be made to work for us
disrupting the big data surveillance state
posts.key_insight: Combating big data's harmful effects requires both intellectual critique and practical tools that empower individuals to reclaim their data from surveillance companies.

posts.summary: This is a brief blog post linking to a Bloomberg View column about the risks of health tracking devices like FitBits when combined with insurance company data practices. The post's title argues that insurance and big data are fundamentally incompatible, suggesting that the data collected by wearable devices could be used against consumers rather than to help them. O'Neil frames the 'free' health tracker as potentially costly, implying that the trade-off between convenience and privacy/fair treatment may not favor consumers.
key_points: Health tracking devices that seem beneficial could have hidden costs when data is shared with insurers
The insurance industry's use of big data creates fundamental conflicts with consumer interests
Free technology often comes with privacy trade-offs that aren't immediately apparent
money_quotes: That Free Health Tracker Could Cost You
Insurance and Big Data Are Incompatible
posts.key_insight: When insurance companies gain access to granular health data from wearable devices, the power asymmetry allows them to use that information against consumers rather than for their benefit.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post linking to the author's Bloomberg View column titled 'Bigger Data Isn't Always Better Data'. The post contains no substantive content beyond the link itself.
posts.key_insight: This post is a link announcement only; the actual content resides on Bloomberg's website.

posts.summary: This is a brief linking post directing readers to the author's Bloomberg View column about how data systems can criminalize immigrants. The post itself contains no substantive content beyond the headline and link. The actual analysis and arguments are contained in the external Bloomberg article, which is not included here.
key_points: The post serves as a pointer to external content on Bloomberg View
The title suggests the column examines how data and algorithms can frame immigrants as criminals
No substantive argument or analysis is present in the blog post itself
posts.key_insight: This linking post suggests O'Neil's Bloomberg column explores how data systems can be weaponized against immigrant populations, but the blog post itself contains no extractable analysis.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces two significant personal developments: she will give a TED talk despite previously criticizing the format, and she plans to undergo bariatric surgery. She acknowledges her change of heart on TED, joking that things can become 'so douchey they come out the other side and are super cool.' More substantially, she commits to documenting her surgery journey on her blog, using it as a lens to explore broader themes about science, evidence, bias, and personal identity.
key_points: O'Neil is giving a TED talk in the 'Our Robotic Overlords' section despite her past criticism of TED
She plans to chronicle her bariatric surgery journey publicly on her blog
The surgery documentation will serve as a vehicle for examining how scientific evidence is corrupted by money and shame
She intends to explore themes of bad data, biased publishing, willpower, and feminist identity through this personal experience
money_quotes: I haven't always been the biggest fan of such things. But I've changed my mind/ sold out/ decided that it might just be great.
Sometimes things get so douchey they come out the other side and are super cool.
I'm giving a talk in the section called Our Robotic Overlords, so that's a very good sign.
If that kind of thing doesn't interest you, feel free to never come back, but if that kind of thing does interest you, then buckle up!
How money and shame corrupt our understanding of scientific evidence.
How bad data and bad technologies and biased academic publishing prevent us from learning optimally.
posts.key_insight: Personal experiences like medical decisions can become powerful lenses for examining systemic problems in how science, data, and evidence function in society.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that while diets statistically fail for the vast majority of people, we persist in treating diet failure as a personal choice rather than a near-universal outcome. She distinguishes between the technical freedom to eat (having access to food) and meaningful choice, noting that people who invest significant effort and money into dieting still fail at rates around 99%. The post challenges the common response of successful dieters offering advice, pointing out this ignores the statistical reality. She compares the 'choice' to go off a diet to the 'choice' to breathe—technically possible to resist, but only briefly.
key_points: Statistical evidence shows diets don't produce lasting weight loss beyond about six months for most people
Individual success stories don't invalidate population-level statistical facts about diet failure
The moment of 'going off' a diet often happens in a kind of amnesia, not as a deliberate decision
If 990 out of 1000 people fail at something despite trying hard, calling it a 'choice' to fail seems wrong
There's a difference between technical freedom (access to food) and meaningful choice in behavior
money_quotes: When I say 'diets don't work,' here's what I mean. I mean that, statistically speaking, people who go on diets don't successfully lose and keep off weight for more than about six months.

They will always imagine that, because they are themselves examples of someone who has lost weight and kept it off for more than two years through dieting, dieting does in fact work, and we should all try what they've tried.
It's a kind of momentary amnesia, and before they know what's happened they're eating something they shouldn't have.
If 1000 people went to a lot of trouble to do something, and they all tried but 990 of them failed to do it, would we decide they had made the choice not to do it?
I'm ready to say there's something else at work here, something more basic than free will.
It's like our choice to breathe. We can't decide not to do it. Or we can, but only for a bit.
posts.key_insight: When nearly everyone fails at something despite genuine effort, we should question whether 'choice' is the right framework for understanding that failure.

posts.summary: O'Neil uses dieting as a case study to demonstrate proper statistical thinking, showing a hierarchy from useless anecdotal evidence to meaningful population-level studies. She argues that personal success stories about dieting are statistically meaningless when 97% of people in rigorous studies fail to lose weight. The post also dismantles the 'willpower' theory of weight loss by using herself as a counterexample—someone with extraordinary discipline who remains heavy. She suggests that her early dieting attempts, enabled by willpower, may have actually caused metabolic damage that made her heavier long-term.
key_points: There's a vast difference between 'this worked for me' and 'this would work as public health policy'—most people conflate the two
The gold standard for diet advice should be: can you transform a population of morbidly obese people to normal weight? (Current answer: no)
Studies tracking all participants, not just completers, show 97% of people lose no weight and many gain weight
The willpower theory of weight loss is contradicted by people with extreme discipline who remain heavy
Early dieting interventions may cause lasting metabolic damage, making the 'solution' part of the problem
money_quotes: What I'd love is for people to understand how much difference there is between a personal experience (1) and advice we'd have on public health (5).
If you can come up with something to tell Medicare about how to have a population of morbidly obese people become a population of regular weight people, then you win.
If you're tempted to tell me about a lifestyle change that worked for you, please don't, because that's not statistical.
Readers, I'm the fucking center of the disciple in willpower universe over here.
I'm guessing I'd be thinner if I'd had less willpower, in other words.
I went on a fat-free diet, on the advice of my doctor, that fucked me up; I lost 30 pounds but then gained something like 75.
posts.key_insight: Individual success stories about weight loss are statistically meaningless noise that obscure the population-level reality: we have no intervention that reliably helps most people lose weight.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that the obesity epidemic has no single known cause, despite the many explanations commonly offered. She systematically debunks the 'blame the individual' approach by noting that exercise has increased yet obesity has risen, and that genetics can't explain rapid population-wide changes. While listing eleven potential contributing factors—from sugar consumption to gut bacteria to glyphosates—she emphasizes that none alone explains the trend. Her core argument is that intellectual honesty requires admitting uncertainty rather than scapegoating individuals, especially children.
key_points: Exercise rates have actually increased, yet obesity has risen—and research shows exercise doesn't drive significant weight loss
Genetics can't explain the obesity epidemic since the gene pool hasn't changed significantly in recent decades
Multiple plausible factors exist (diet, environment, gut biome, chemicals) but none is definitively the single cause
Many hypotheses are difficult to test because they involve hard-to-avoid environmental factors of modern life
Individual blame is intellectually dishonest when we don't actually understand the systemic causes
money_quotes: One of the most ridiculous aspects of the 'blame the individual' approach to obesity is the overall trend of fatness throughout the country and the world over the past few decades.
The rates of obesity have skyrocketed and we simply don't know why.
In fact we're getting more exercise and it doesn't seem to help.
Our gene pool hasn't changed that much recently.
All of them sound plausible, and none of them are likely the single answer.
People hate not knowing stuff, so they pretend they know. That's not helpful.
We need to be scientists and try testing out hypotheses.
Until we've figured it out, I'd like us to admit I don't know why we're all so fat. And I'd like us to stop blaming individuals, especially children.
posts.key_insight: Blaming individuals for obesity is intellectually dishonest when the population-wide trend clearly points to systemic causes we haven't yet identified.

posts.summary: This is a brief link post directing readers to two of O'Neil's Bloomberg columns. The first examines the dystopian implications of algorithmic price discrimination, while the second analyzes Snap's business model and its need to deeply understand user psychology. The post itself contains no substantive analysis, serving purely as a pointer to external content.
key_points: Links to Bloomberg column on algorithmic price discrimination
Links to Bloomberg column on Snap's data collection imperatives
Both pieces appear to continue O'Neil's theme of critiquing how companies exploit personal data
posts.key_insight: This link post highlights O'Neil's ongoing Bloomberg coverage of algorithmic exploitation and corporate data practices.

posts.summary: Arthur Doskow offers a systematic critique of algorithmic decision-making systems, arguing that organizations often adopt these tools without adequately questioning whether quantitative methods can reliably make the human judgments they're meant to replace. He emphasizes that algorithmic systems merely substitute individual human bias for consistent algorithmic bias, and whether this represents improvement must be assessed case by case. The post advocates for rigorous testing, meaningful accountability (including CEO sign-off on system claims), reformed software contracts that hold developers liable, and recognition that 'being humane may cost money.' Doskow concludes by identifying the core tension: optimization techniques designed for inanimate problems are now being applied to life-altering human decisions with little consideration for human consequences.
key_points: If someone cannot describe in detail how they would make a decision (hiring, parole, teaching evaluation), they cannot devise an algorithm to do it reliably
Reliance on algorithmic decision-making replaces arbitrary individual bias with consistent algorithmic bias—whether that's an improvement must be assessed situationally
Organizations purchasing 'black box' solutions should use RFP processes to demand transparency about inputs, validation, and test cases
Software contracts that absolve developers of all consequences need to change, especially for black box evaluative systems
The inhumanity of algorithms is an externality—optimization techniques designed for inanimate objects are now being applied to life-altering human decisions
money_quotes: If someone cannot describe in detail how they would evaluate a teacher, or make a credit decision or a hiring decision or a parole decision, then it's hard to imagine how they would devise an algorithm that would reliably perform the function in their stead.
Reliance on a quantitative decision methodology essentially replaces a (perhaps arbitrary) individual bias with what may be a reliable and consistent algorithmic bias.
Organizations that do not take advantage of an RFP process to ask these detailed questions and demand thorough and responsive answers have only themselves to blame.
Software contracts are notoriously developer-friendly, basically absolving the developer of all possible consequences arising out of the use of their product. This needs to change.
Being fair, being humane may cost money. And this is the real issue with many algorithms.
In economists' terms, the inhumanity associated with an algorithm could be referred to as an externality.

We are now to the point where we have human beings being compared to poisonous Skittles, and where life altering decisions of great import are being subjected to optimization processes which objectify people, view them as resources or threats, and give little or no consideration to the very human consequences of their deployment.
posts.key_insight: Algorithms cannot reliably automate decisions that humans themselves cannot clearly articulate, and treating human welfare as an optimization externality has profound ethical costs that organizations must explicitly account for.

posts.summary: Ernie Davis critiques the media coverage of Boston schools' switch from Mercator to Gall-Peters maps, arguing that framing either projection as 'more accurate' is misleading. He explains that all flat maps involve tradeoffs: Gall-Peters preserves area but distorts shapes, while Mercator preserves shapes but distorts area. The mathematically correct solution is simple: use globes, which are now inexpensive and provide perfect scale models with no distortion. Davis worries that presenting map projections as competing theories based on political preference obscures the actual geographical truth that globes perfectly represent.
key_points: No flat map can be both shape-preserving and area-preserving—it's mathematically impossible to perfectly flatten a sphere
The Mercator vs Gall-Peters debate is a false dichotomy when globes provide a distortion-free alternative
Globes are now affordable ($10-30) yet were inexplicably absent from media coverage of the map debate
Teaching map projections as politically-driven choices risks implying there's no objective geographical truth
Globes enable teaching far beyond geography: great circles, seasons, astronomy, and non-Euclidean geometry
money_quotes: I hope that they are not being taught that these two maps are two competing theories about the geography of the world and that choosing between them is all a matter of your point of view and your political preferences, and that there is no actual truth of the matter.
However, there is absolutely no planar map of the world that can succeed in being both shape preserving and area preserving. There is just no way to perfectly flatten out the surface of a sphere.
The obvious, perfect solution is — drumroll — a globe. A globe is an (essentially) perfect scale model of the geography of the earth, with no distortion of any kind.

You can get an inexpensive 6-inch globe for $10. You can get a good 11-inch globe for $30. I do find it surprising that none of the articles I've seen about the choice of maps even mentions this, best, option.
The original meaning of 'geometry', after all, is 'measuring the earth.'
At the minimum, hopefully, early and extensive exposure to globes will deter students from growing up to believe that the earth is flat.
posts.key_insight: When a mathematically perfect solution exists (globes), framing an educational debate as a choice between two imperfect alternatives (map projections) obscures objective truth and risks teaching relativism where none is warranted.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about managing algorithmic systems. The post itself contains no substantive content beyond the link and title. The actual argument and analysis exist in the external Bloomberg piece, not in this blog entry.
key_points: Post serves as a pointer to external Bloomberg View article
Title suggests focus on governance/oversight of algorithmic systems
No substantive analysis present in the blog post itself
posts.key_insight: This is a link-only post with no analyzable content beyond signaling the topic of algorithmic governance.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post about an upcoming performance by the TomTown Ramblers band on Sunday. The post contains no substantive content beyond the invitation to attend. It appears to be a personal announcement rather than analytical content typical of the blog.
key_points: Announcement of TomTown Ramblers performance on Sunday
Personal invitation to readers to attend
money_quotes: Please join us if you can!
posts.key_insight: This is a non-substantive personal announcement post with no analytical content to extract.

posts.summary: Following Congress's vote to allow ISPs to sell browsing data without consent, Angela Grammatas proposes a counterintuitive strategy: instead of hiding from data collection, flood it with meaningless noise. She illustrates how targeted advertising can manipulate voters through a hypothetical 'Jane Internet' who gets selectively shown political ads based on her cat website visits. The solution is Noiszy, a browser plugin that generates fake browsing data to confuse targeting algorithms. If adopted widely, this approach could weaken the correlations that make targeted advertising effective, potentially forcing companies to earn user engagement rather than exploit it.
key_points: Traditional approaches of erasing or blocking data collection are becoming ineffective against sophisticated tracking
Targeted advertising enables political manipulation by showing personalized messages that opponents never see and can't counter
Flooding your browsing history with random noise could break the 'filter bubble' by making your true interests harder to identify
If enough people generate fake data, targeting algorithms would lose their predictive power, making retargeting less profitable
The Noiszy browser plugin automates the creation of misleading digital footprints to obscure real browsing behavior
money_quotes: Hiding from data collection isn't working.
Instead of restricting our data, it's time to create more – a lot more.
A flood of meaningless data could create a noisy cover that makes our true behavior hard to understand.
Jane's attempt to be a well-informed voter has been usurped by retargeting. And, perhaps most importantly, Jane doesn't even know this has happened.
The 'No on A' campaign never has any opportunity to discuss or debate the point. They may not even know that the cats-related topic has been raised.
When organizations lose the ability to 'figure us out' from our browsing data, they'll have to work harder to build products and content that people willingly engage and share data with.
Targeting algorithms are happily churning away on our data, pushing whatever messages the highest bidder wants us to see, and we have no obvious way to feed back into the cycle.
posts.key_insight: When you can't hide from surveillance, you can still resist it by drowning your signal in noise.

posts.summary: This brief post celebrates the intersection of knitting, science activism, and political resistance ahead of the March for Science. O'Neil highlights 'resistor hats' - knitted hats featuring the electronic resistor symbol - as a creative form of protest. She showcases variations including chemistry-themed, DNA helix, water molecule, and combination pussy hat/resistor designs, all available as free patterns on Ravelry. The post is essentially a celebration of nerdy craftivism.
key_points: Resistor hats combine knitting culture with science advocacy and political resistance
The knitting community has created multiple science-themed variations for the March for Science
Free patterns are available on Ravelry and craftivism sites, making participation accessible
The post highlights the creative energy of 'knitting nerd activists'
money_quotes: So, anyone surprised? I'm not. But I am excited.
Knitting nerd activists are endlessly creative
posts.key_insight: Science advocates are finding creative ways to merge crafting culture with political resistance, creating accessible forms of protest.

posts.summary: O'Neil celebrates the news that Steve Bannon was demoted from the National Security Council, attributing it partly to the viral #PostcardsToBannon campaign she had previously promoted. She views this as a small but meaningful victory for resistance efforts. The post also briefly introduces her Bloomberg column arguing against using big data for jury selection.
key_points: The #PostcardsToBannon campaign reportedly irritated Trump enough to contribute to Bannon's demotion
Grassroots resistance tactics can have real political impact
O'Neil argues in her Bloomberg column that big data jury selection is a bad idea
money_quotes: I'm super happy to say that, according to the New York Times, Bannon was demoted yesterday in part because the President Bannon/ #PostcardToBannon campaign really got under Trump's skin.
Obviously Bannon hasn't been kicked out of the White House, but I think we can all agree this is a step in the right direction.
posts.key_insight: Small acts of resistance, like postcard campaigns, can accumulate into meaningful political pressure.

posts.summary: This is a brief meta-post announcing Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View debate with economist Noah Smith about whether capitalism reduces inequality. O'Neil directly challenges Smith's earlier column claiming capitalism shrinks inequality, stating she believes the evidence contradicts his position. The post serves primarily as a pointer to the full debate published on Bloomberg View rather than presenting the full argument itself.
key_points: O'Neil is publicly debating Noah Smith's claim that capitalism naturally reduces inequality
She explicitly states 'the facts are not on his side,' signaling her data-driven skepticism
The post demonstrates O'Neil's commitment to challenging mainstream economic narratives with evidence
money_quotes: I don't think the facts are on his side.
posts.key_insight: Claims about capitalism's benefits deserve rigorous empirical scrutiny rather than ideological acceptance.

posts.summary: This is a brief link post directing readers to two Bloomberg View articles by Cathy O'Neil. The first article discusses the United Airlines passenger removal incident and what it reveals about societal conceptions of dignity. The second is a philosophical essay exploring hypothetical brain-computer interfaces for instant knowledge transfer. The post itself contains no substantive analysis, serving purely as a pointer to external content.
key_points: Links to Bloomberg View article analyzing the United Airlines dragging incident through a dignity lens
References a philosophical essay on hypothetical brain-uploading technology
Demonstrates O'Neil's range from current events criticism to speculative philosophy
money_quotes: United Airlines Exposes Our Twisted Idea of Dignity
posts.key_insight: This link post offers no original analysis but signals O'Neil's interest in examining how corporate behavior and technology intersect with human dignity.

posts.summary: This is a brief placeholder post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about the robot apocalypse. She also mentions an upcoming recap of her TED experience. The actual substantive content lives in the linked Bloomberg article rather than this blog post.
key_points: O'Neil recently spoke at TED and plans to share that experience
She published a column at Bloomberg View about AI and automation concerns
The post serves primarily as a cross-promotion to her mainstream media writing
posts.key_insight: This post is a brief redirect to O'Neil's Bloomberg column on AI risks rather than containing original analysis.

posts.summary: Phil Goff argues that despite the federal government's retreat from police accountability under the Trump administration, data science offers a path forward for civil rights protection. The Center for Policing Equity has built the National Justice Database, the first standardized database of police behavior, with voluntary participation from departments covering one-third of the US population. Google's $5 million commitment to automate data processing will dramatically accelerate analysis that once took months. Goff calls on data scientists to become 'JusticeNerds' and apply their skills to questions of policing equity, arguing that metrics-based accountability can proceed regardless of federal support.
key_points: When the police accountability crisis began, there was no national data on police behavior—no stops, use of force, policies, or officer profiles—making accountability nearly impossible
Police chiefs themselves organized to create a national database, requesting standardized metrics to understand and address racial disparities in their departments
Google's commitment will transform months-long data analysis into hours or days, eliminating excuses for departments that collect data but claim ignorance about disparities
The work requires distinguishing racial disparities rooted in broader inequality (employment, housing, education) from those stemming from police policies, psychologies, or behaviors
Organizations across the justice reform field are urgently hiring data analysts willing to apply technical skills to civil rights questions
money_quotes: Without metrics, the job of holding police accountable is nigh impossible.

When the crisis of public trust in police began, we had no national data on police behavior. Nothing on stops. Nothing on use of force. Nothing on policies. Nothing on officer psychological profiles. Nothing.
The metrics represent the ability to hold police accountable to the values of equality we should all share.
There will no longer be any reasonable excuse for a department who collects data on any of these factors to say they don't know what they mean.
Our country needs professional JusticeNerds, and we are in short supply.
Doing this work is exciting. It's energizing. And, most importantly, it staves off the temptation to despair when it seems that progress is in retreat.
posts.key_insight: Data infrastructure and analytics can create police accountability independent of federal support, but only if technically skilled people choose to apply their abilities to justice work.

posts.summary: This is a brief pointer post directing readers to the author's Bloomberg View column about female engineers at Facebook and Simpson's Paradox. The post itself contains no substantive argument, serving only as a cross-posting notice to drive traffic to the full article on Bloomberg. The actual analysis of gender bias at Facebook and the statistical phenomenon of Simpson's Paradox is contained in the linked external piece.
key_points: Post serves as a redirect to Bloomberg View column
Topic concerns female engineers at Facebook
Simpson's Paradox is the analytical framework used
No substantive content in the blog post itself
posts.key_insight: This post contains no analyzable content beyond a link to an external column about applying Simpson's Paradox to Facebook's gender data.

posts.summary: This anonymous guest post argues that tech's informal culture, which blurs professional and social boundaries, creates unique barriers for women seeking mentorship. The author recounts her experience with a VIP who ignored her prepared questions, talked at her for an hour, then pursued her with unwanted emails and texts. She notes that even well-intentioned senior men may avoid mentoring women to prevent appearances of impropriety, while senior women are too few to fill the gap. The post concludes that tech's emphasis on networking and connections, combined with these mentorship barriers, will continue to stall diversity in the industry.
key_points: Tech's informal culture of hoodies and happy hours makes professional interactions indistinguishable from social or romantic ones, disadvantaging women
The author's VIP meeting exemplifies the mentorship problem: she came prepared with questions but was talked at, then received unwanted contact
Men in power may avoid mentoring women to prevent appearances of impropriety, creating a chilling effect on legitimate mentorship
Senior women mentors are too scarce due to historical attrition and hostility to fill the mentorship gap
Tech's network-dependent success model perpetuates inequality because women and minorities lack equal access to these networks
money_quotes: For a culture that emphasizes meritocracy so heavily, tech is much more of a who-is-who than I ever realized before moving out to the Bay Area.
I wanted mentorship, and instead ended up getting weird emails and texts.
Getting coffee with a young woman can look like a date even if it is not, and men in positions of power are especially wary of sexual harassment allegations.

The informal culture of tech, in which men frequently grab a drink with a male mentor but often do not feel comfortable doing the same with a woman, means that it is difficult for women to get access.
At least in politics, it is more clear whether a mentor is inappropriately hitting on you in a professional setting, because that setting is clearly professional.
As long as succeeding in tech involves being well-connected in a way that women and minorities in tech are not, diversity in the industry will stall.
The past year has felt more like The Social Network than I ever could have imagined – creepy but well-connected mentors, hiring decisions made over drinks, and all.
posts.key_insight: Tech's meritocracy myth obscures a reality where success depends on networks that women cannot equally access due to the industry's informal culture that blurs professional and personal boundaries.

posts.summary: This is a brief post linking to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column where she critiques Yuval Harari's ideas about human-robot merger. The post signals skepticism toward transhumanist narratives, suggesting Harari's predictions are not to be taken seriously. O'Neil frames herself as unconvinced by the techno-utopian vision of humans merging with machines.
key_points: O'Neil is skeptical of Yuval Harari's predictions about human-technology merger
She characterizes Harari's ideas as potentially performative rather than sincere ('putting us all on')
The title signals personal resistance to transhumanist ideology
money_quotes: I'd Rather Not Merge With Robots, Thank You
Yuval Harari is putting us all on
posts.key_insight: O'Neil dismisses transhumanist predictions about human-robot merger as hype that shouldn't be taken at face value.

posts.summary: This brief post announces O'Neil's Bloomberg View column arguing against using Value-Added Models (VAM) to evaluate teachers. She signals this is among her most important work on the topic, suggesting the algorithmic approach to teacher grading may finally be losing credibility. The post serves primarily as a pointer to her longer argument about why these statistical models are fundamentally flawed for high-stakes personnel decisions.
key_points: Value-Added Models for teacher evaluation are deeply problematic algorithms
O'Neil considers this critique among her most important Bloomberg columns
The title suggests optimism that VAM may be losing institutional support
money_quotes: The VAM Might Finally be Dead
Don't Grade Teachers With a Bad Algorithm
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic teacher evaluation models are bad algorithms that shouldn't be used for high-stakes grading decisions.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her massive knitting project completed as a coping mechanism for post-election anxiety. She bought yarn the day after the 2016 election and finished the 144-square blanket exactly 100 days after the inauguration. The project served as a way to channel nervous energy and manage overwhelming emotions during a politically turbulent period. She also knitted pussy hats and other projects during the same timeframe.
key_points: Knitting served as a deliberate anxiety management strategy during political upheaval
The project's timeline was symbolically tied to political milestones (election day to 100 days post-inauguration)
The scale of the project (144 squares plus additional items) reflects the intensity of the anxiety being processed
Creative, tactile activities can serve as effective coping mechanisms for political distress
money_quotes: I bought the yarn on the day after the election in an effort to counterbalance my wildly unbalanced thoughts and emotions.
It was a very successful coping mechanism for anxiety.
Because, dude, that's a lot of nervous energy.
Serious question, what do non-knitters do to deal with their anxiety?
posts.key_insight: Creative projects with tangible, repetitive work can serve as powerful coping mechanisms during periods of political anxiety and emotional turmoil.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg column about how politically motivated manipulation of data poses a threat to democratic processes. The post itself contains no substantive argument, serving only as a pointer to the external article. O'Neil uses her blog to cross-promote her mainstream media writing on data integrity issues.
key_points: Politically driven data manipulation ('finagling') is a threat to democracy
O'Neil maintains a regular Bloomberg column on data and society issues
The post functions as a redirect rather than containing original analysis
money_quotes: Unreliable Data Can Threaten Democracy
posts.key_insight: When data becomes a tool for political manipulation rather than objective measurement, democratic decision-making is undermined.

posts.summary: This is a brief blog post that links to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column comparing Trump's cognitive style to Google's ad testing methodology. The post itself contains no substantive analysis, serving only as a pointer to external content. The intriguing title suggests the column explores how Trump's thinking resembles path-independent algorithmic processes, where outcomes don't depend on the sequence of inputs.
key_points: Post is a link announcement to Bloomberg View column
Draws parallel between Trump's cognition and Google ad testing
Suggests Trump exhibits 'path-independent' thinking patterns
money_quotes: Donald Trump's Path-Independent Theory of Mind: How the U.S. president is like a Google ad test
posts.key_insight: The post merely promotes external content comparing Trump's mental processes to algorithmic A/B testing, with no substantive analysis in the post itself.

posts.summary: This guest post by Eugene Stern eviscerates a New York Times profile that praised William Sanders' value-added models for teacher evaluation. The article's central criticism is that the Times treated the bell curve distribution of teacher ratings as evidence of the model's validity, when in fact the Central Limit Theorem guarantees that almost any measurement—including random noise—will produce a bell curve. Stern argues this is mathematical illiteracy dressed up as scientific credibility, pointing out that a completely meaningless metric would produce the same impressive-looking distribution.
key_points: The New York Times profile used the bell curve distribution of value-added scores as evidence of the model's scientific validity
The Central Limit Theorem explains why bell curves appear everywhere—including in random noise that means nothing
Any variable depending on many independent factors will produce a bell curve, regardless of whether it measures anything meaningful
Value-added models may simply be measuring statistical noise rather than actual teacher quality
Mathematical-looking results can intimidate people into accepting conclusions that don't follow from the evidence
money_quotes: The implication here is that value added models have scientific credibility because they look like math — they give you a bell curve, you know.
That sounds sort of impressive until you remember that the bell curve is also the world's most common model of random noise.

The central limit theorem is wonderful because it helps explain the world: it tells you why you see bell curves everywhere. It also tells you that random fluctuations that don't mean anything tend to look like bell curves too.
If I decided to rate teachers by the size of the turds that come out of their ass, I could wave around a lovely bell-shaped distribution of teacher ratings.
Back in the bad old days, we didn't know how to distinguish between good and bad teachers, but the Turd Size Model produces a shiny, mathy-looking distribution — so it must be correct!
Or maybe we should just rate news articles based on turd size, so this one could get a Pulitzer.
posts.key_insight: A bell curve distribution doesn't validate a measurement's meaning—the Central Limit Theorem guarantees that even complete nonsense will produce one.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares a knitting project she created to support period equity activism - a tampon-themed hat designed for her friend Laura Strausfeld, who was speaking at a rally for safe feminine care products. The post briefly mentions O'Neil's personal involvement in the issue as a plaintiff in the successful New York State tampon tax case. She learned a new knitting technique called 'intarsia in the round' to create the hat and plans to share the pattern on Ravelry.
key_points: O'Neil designed a 'Period Equity (tampon) Hat' for activist Laura Strausfeld to wear at a D.C. rally
O'Neil was a plaintiff in the successful NY State tampon tax lawsuit, with Strausfeld as the legal strategist
Laura Strausfeld founded Period Equity to continue advocating for menstrual product access and fairness
The hat design was inspired by the Period Equity organization's website
money_quotes: I've gone and done it, folks: I've designed a 'Period Equity (tampon) Hat'
I had to learn a new technique called 'intarsia in the round' in order to knit this hat
I was a plaintiff on the New York State tampon tax case, which we won
posts.key_insight: Personal activism can take creative forms, from legal action to crafting protest gear for causes you believe in.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about algorithmic hiring at Fox News. The post itself contains no substantive analysis, serving purely as a link to external content. The actual argument and insights would be found in the linked Bloomberg article, not in this blog post.
key_points: Post serves as a redirect to Bloomberg View column
No substantive content or argument presented in the blog post itself
Links to full archive of author's Bloomberg columns
posts.key_insight: This post is merely an announcement linking to external content and contains no analyzable argument or insight.

posts.summary: This brief post serves as a pointer to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column criticizing Silicon Valley entrepreneurs. The core argument, captured in the subtitle, is that tech leaders prioritize exciting, ambitious projects like space travel over addressing accountability and societal problems their technologies create. O'Neil frames this as a fundamental misalignment between tech industry incentives and public interest.
key_points: Tech entrepreneurs prefer glamorous moonshot projects over fixing problems they've created
Accountability is treated as boring compared to futuristic ventures
Silicon Valley's priorities don't align with addressing societal harms
money_quotes: Don't Expect Tech to Care About Your Problems
Interplanetary travel is way more fun than accountability
posts.key_insight: Tech leaders would rather escape to Mars than be held accountable for the problems their platforms create on Earth.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional announcement for Stacks Project hoodies being sold to support a mathematical research initiative. The post directs readers to sign up by June 16th to receive hoodies in time for the Stacks Project Workshop in Michigan. It's a community-building effort for mathematicians interested in algebraic geometry.
key_points: Stacks Project hoodies are available for purchase with a June 16th deadline
The hoodies tie into the Stacks Project Workshop happening in Michigan in late July
The initiative is a collaborative effort with Wei Ho and Pieter Belmans
money_quotes: Nerds, you're in luck!
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted call to action for the mathematical community to show support for the Stacks Project through merchandise.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about the myth of genius in mathematics. The post itself contains minimal content beyond the link and title. The referenced column appears to argue that mathematicians are not all geniuses, challenging common perceptions about mathematical talent. The post serves primarily as a pointer to her external writing rather than containing substantive analysis itself.
key_points: The post announces a Bloomberg View column about genius myths in mathematics
O'Neil challenges the perception that all mathematicians are geniuses
The post is primarily a link aggregator for her Bloomberg writing
money_quotes: A Mathematician's Secret: We're Not All Geniuses
posts.key_insight: The myth that mathematicians must be geniuses is harmful and inaccurate, suggesting mathematical ability is more accessible than commonly believed.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about the problems with technology controlling education. The post itself contains no substantive argument, serving only as a pointer to the full article published on Bloomberg. O'Neil uses her blog to cross-promote her mainstream media writing.
key_points: Post announces a new Bloomberg View column on tech in education
No substantive content is provided in the blog post itself
Links to both the specific column and her full Bloomberg archive
posts.key_insight: This announcement post contains no analyzable argument; the substantive content exists only in the linked Bloomberg column.

posts.summary: A data scientist father discovered that his children's elementary school had been incorrectly rated by GreatSchools.org for years due to a publishing error. Despite the low rating, his family's personal experience was positive, yet he watched neighbors avoid or leave the school based solely on the single-digit score. After persistent emails, GreatSchools acknowledged their error and corrected the rating from 5 to 8. The post illustrates how algorithmic ratings can create self-fulfilling prophecies that damage communities, even when the underlying data is simply wrong.
key_points: Parents routinely trust algorithmic school ratings over their own positive personal experiences
The low rating was caused by a publishing system error, not actual school performance
Families avoided or fled the school based on a single erroneous digit, causing real community harm
The author had to persistently push back against corporate cut-and-paste responses to get the error acknowledged
The corrected rating now raises questions about whether 'bandwagon parents' will suddenly view the same school differently
money_quotes: It was sad to see people trust a single digit score more than a personal experience.
When they would move, we'd ask what in the school do you dislike and they would acknowledge their personal experience was positive but they wanted to move to a 'better' school.
The perception that Quatama is a low performing school was completely erroneous and based off a math system gone wrong.
My thought that the same way there are bandwagon fans, there are bandwagon parents.
This will open up a large question about the morality of publishing overly simplified data.
posts.key_insight: A simple publishing error in a school rating algorithm drove families away from a quality school for years, demonstrating how algorithmic systems can inflict real damage on communities even when they're just plain wrong.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares the charming story of how a spontaneous St. Patrick's Day celebration with her son Wolfie led to an actual trip to Ireland. What began as dressing up for a parade evolved into a shared obsession through flag projects and YouTube castle videos. After her son repeatedly asked when they could visit, she finally bought tickets on impulse. The post is a lighthearted departure from her usual analytical content, focusing purely on family adventure.
key_points: A St. Patrick's Day celebration sparked a shared mother-son interest in Irish culture
Wolfie's creative projects (flags on door, drawing countries with their flags) deepened the obsession
Persistent childlike enthusiasm ('Mom, when can we go?') eventually overcame adult procrastination
The trip represents spontaneous decision-making triumphing over endless planning
money_quotes: Even though we're only technically 25% and an eighth Irish, we're actually, down deep, 100% Irish.
We discussed blarney, the need for embellishment for a really good story.
He agreed that drunk people are funny and the musical tradition is friendly and fun.
Ireland became a small obsession for me and Wolfie.
OK shit, I'll go home and buy tickets.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best adventures come from finally saying yes to a child's persistent question instead of endlessly deferring.

posts.summary: This is a personal travel diary documenting a mother-son trip to Dublin, Ireland. The post chronicles their experiences over four days, including dealing with travel frustrations, staying at a luxurious castle hotel, horseback riding lessons, visiting the Cliffs of Moher, and stumbling upon Dublin's Pride Parade. The narrative emphasizes leisure, bonding activities like swimming and chess, and embracing Irish culture through food, music, and spontaneous encounters with street musicians.
key_points: Travel logistics proved challenging with unexpected car rental fees and navigating Dublin traffic while driving on the left side
The Dromoland Castle Hotel represented an aspirational luxury experience that clearly impressed the author
A running joke developed about experiences being 'more Irish' if done on horseback, stemming from Wolfie's riding lesson
The trip serendipitously coincided with Dublin's Pride Parade, adding unexpected festivity to their visit
The author brought her fiddle and arranged a lesson with a street musician encountered outside a restaurant
money_quotes: Wolfie described this experience as 'both awesome and mortifying.'
Really no trip would be complete without a full day of doing nothing at all.
When I say we did nothing, it goes without saying that we went swimming in the hotel pool, because we believe that is a solemn duty of vacationers.
This lad couldn't be more Irish unless he was on a horse.
There were quite a few drunken horsemen rushing their horses through the streets this way and that and generally causing confusion and mayhem.
posts.key_insight: This post is a lighthearted family travel diary without connection to the author's usual themes of algorithms, data science, or social critique.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares lighthearted observations about hotel stays, touching on the excessive mirrors (particularly near toilets), the inadequate coffee pod allotments, and paradoxically, her love of hotels' anonymous blandness. The post is an informal, humorous departure from her usual analytical content, written while traveling with her child. She offers self-deprecating solutions to each complaint, like walking around without glasses to avoid mirror reflections and stealing coffee pods from maid carts. The piece celebrates hotels' durability and lack of preciousness as features rather than bugs.
key_points: Hotels have an inexplicable abundance of mirrors in unflattering locations, particularly near toilets
Coffee pod allotments in hotels are woefully inadequate for serious coffee drinkers
Despite complaints, hotel rooms offer comforting anonymity and are designed to be nearly indestructible
The post acknowledges its own whimsy, framed as adult conversation deprivation after a week with an 8-year-old
money_quotes: What's the deal with sitting down at the toilet and seeing yourself in a mirror, sitting down at the toilet? It's not a good look for anyone, I'd wager.
People who do look good at the toilet would look good in any position whatsoever.
