The way I deal with the mirror problem is I walk around the room without my glasses on so I can't see anything. It solves the problem of the mirrors but also produces its own problems.
Is there any serious coffee drinker who could make do on such a small amount of caffeine?

The way I deal with the lack of coffee problem is I steal coffee pods off of the maid carts in the hallways whenever I get the chance. This solves the problem of too little coffee but leads to the problem of feeling somewhat guilty all the time.
Let me say how much I love hotel rooms, and especially how utterly anonymous they are. They're so comfortingly bland!
Everything is designed with disgusting behavior in mind, so you don't have to worry too hard about messing something up.
In a hotel room there's basically nothing to break because it's all bolted onto the wall and/or stain resistant. It's heaven.
posts.key_insight: Hotels' anonymous, indestructible blandness is actually a feature that frees guests from the anxiety of being a good houseguest.

posts.summary: O'Neil provides a personal update on her progress toward bariatric surgery, having completed all paperwork and awaiting insurance approval. She explains the pre-surgery requirements including a two-week protein-shake-only diet designed to shrink her fatty liver for the laparoscopic procedure. The post details the four stages of post-surgery dietary recovery, from liquid-only intake to eventually eating normal but much smaller portions. She acknowledges the intimidating behavioral changes required but expresses hope based on others' experiences that reduced hunger will make the transition manageable.
key_points: A strict two-week pre-surgery starvation diet is required to reduce liver size, as 'non-alcoholic fatty liver' is common in heavier patients and can interfere with surgical tools
Post-surgery recovery involves four distinct dietary phases, starting with 1-ounce liquid intake every 20 minutes and gradually progressing to normal food in small portions
Long-term lifestyle rules include never eating and drinking simultaneously, avoiding carbonated beverages, limiting fat to 25% of diet, and taking vitamins for life
The key selling point from others who've had the surgery: you're not hungry all the time despite eating far less
money_quotes: Of course I do get impatient, mom, but I just hold it inside and I think about positive things like that we'll eventually be home and that we'll be able to see our family
It turns out that many people of my weight have 'non-alcoholic fatty liver,' which just means a liver that's bigger and contains more fat than a normal liver
The good news is that livers respond quickly to dieting, so the two week extreme diet goes pretty far in decreasing the size of the liver to a manageable obstacle
This sounds like a miracle to me, as a person whose hunger rages at me like someone screaming in my ears on a daily basis
I'm taking a leap of faith, knowing that I'm pretty good at following plans I've set for myself, and also knowing that once you've developed a habit, it's not that hard to follow it
posts.key_insight: The promise that makes drastic surgical intervention worthwhile is freedom from constant, overwhelming hunger—a transformation that those who haven't experienced it might not fully appreciate.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil explains her decision to undergo bariatric surgery, framing it as a quality-of-life choice rather than an aesthetic one. She openly states she loves her body and dreads the physical changes surgery will bring, but argues that long-term health concerns—particularly diabetes risk and its effects on the brain—outweigh her reservations. She lists practical considerations including her desire to bike in summer heat, family history of arthritis and joint problems, and the reality that doctors treat overweight patients poorly. The post sets up a future series examining the shaming tactics, financial incentives, and bad medical information she's encountered around the decision.
key_points: She values her body as it is and explicitly rejects the notion that weight loss equals self-improvement
Diabetes prevention is her primary motivation, especially its long-term effects on cognitive function
Practical mobility concerns—being unable to bike in summer heat—significantly impact her quality of life
She acknowledges systemic medical bias against overweight patients as a factor in her timing
The decision is framed pragmatically: why wait for inevitable joint surgeries when intervention now is safer
money_quotes: I love my round body.
I have spent months trying to come to terms with that but I haven't yet.
I like my brain even more than I like my round body.
I felt like a prisoner all summer, cooped up inside the house and getting less and less fit.
Post-bariatric surgery patients complain about being cold, not hot. I'd rather be cold, because then I can wear one of my hand-knit sweaters outside while biking.
Doctors don't treat overweight people well.
I want to be one of those active grandmas that takes their grandchildren to the zoo, and then bikes home.
I don't want to be defeated by global warming, nor do I want to be forced to move to Maine.
posts.key_insight: Medical decisions about one's body can be simultaneously an act of self-love and a pragmatic calculation about future quality of life, rejecting the false binary between body acceptance and health intervention.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post about Cathy O'Neil's upcoming trip to London for promotional events tied to the softcover release of her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' with Penguin UK. She shares a promotional card and invites London-based readers to attend her events.
key_points: O'Neil is traveling to London for the WMD softcover launch
Penguin UK is organizing multiple promotional events
She invites readers to attend her London appearances
posts.key_insight: This is a brief event announcement with no substantive analytical content.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that on her 45th birthday, she received approval from her insurance company for bariatric gastric sleeve surgery after six months of working toward this goal. She expects to have the surgery within two weeks. In preparation, she must begin a strict pre-operative diet of protein shakes and celery only, which means giving up coffee temporarily and carbonated beverages permanently. She expresses excitement about proceeding with this major health decision.
key_points: Insurance approval for bariatric surgery came after a 6-month process that began in January
The surgery is scheduled to happen within approximately two weeks
Pre-op requirements include a strict protein shake diet with no coffee or carbonated beverages
This post follows up on her earlier explanation of why she chose to pursue bariatric surgery
money_quotes: Happy birthday to me, folks! I'm 45 today and my present was that I got the call I've been working towards for 6 months.
In preparation I need to start a strict 'pre-op' diet consisting of protein shakes and nothing else except possibly celery.
Wish me luck, friends! I'm super psyched.
posts.key_insight: After six months of navigating insurance requirements, O'Neil shares a personal health milestone, revealing a more vulnerable side outside her usual algorithmic critique.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to an external article O'Neil wrote for The Guardian/Observer. The piece was published to coincide with the UK softcover launch of her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction.' The linked article addresses how algorithms can deceive and what can be done about it. The post itself contains no substantive argument, serving purely as a pointer to her journalism work elsewhere.
key_points: Announcement of Guardian/Observer article publication
Tied to UK softcover book launch
External article focuses on algorithmic deception and accountability
posts.key_insight: This post serves as a brief redirect to O'Neil's Guardian article on algorithmic accountability rather than containing original analysis.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her experience preparing for bariatric surgery, describing her strict pre-operative liquid diet and an unexpected health scare from sodium deficiency. She discovered that the 'meal replacement' drinks prescribed didn't contain adequate salt, causing weakness and disorientation despite her careful compliance. After researching online forums, she found that pre-op diet protocols vary widely and many patients don't follow them strictly. The post blends personal medical narrative with her characteristic skepticism about supposedly authoritative guidelines.
key_points: The prescribed meal replacement drinks claiming to provide complete nutrition were actually deficient in sodium, causing dangerous symptoms
Self-directed research and problem-solving (adding chicken broth) resolved what strict protocol compliance had caused
Online forums revealed wide variation in pre-op diet protocols and widespread non-compliance among patients
Following medical directions 'carefully' can sometimes make you sick when those directions are flawed
There's a spectrum of non-compliance from reasonable modifications to dangerous behavior like eating sandwiches before surgery
money_quotes: It's upsetting that I was making myself sick by following directions carefully.
I modified my diet to include chicken broth and now I feel perfectly fine, but it made me wonder how the directions could be so badly off.
They're very disgusting, being chalky and sickeningly sweet, but I've been extremely diligent, learning to drink them quickly and try not to gag.
nobody, or at least very few people, seem to actually follow these diets
some people are hilariously bad at following their diet
except for the crazy people who are eating sandwiches right before surgery and planning not to tell their surgeon
that could actually kill you
posts.key_insight: Even when you follow medical protocols diligently, the protocols themselves may be flawed, requiring patients to become their own advocates and problem-solvers.

posts.summary: This is a brief personal update from Cathy O'Neil announcing that she successfully came through surgery. She reports being mobile already and expresses appreciation for her pain medication. She expects to be discharged the following day.
key_points: Successfully completed surgery
Already ambulatory post-operation
Positive experience with pain management
Anticipating quick discharge
money_quotes: I love the pain control button.
posts.key_insight: A quick personal health update sharing good news about a successful surgery and speedy recovery.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her recovery experience one week after bariatric sleeve surgery, having just stopped pain medication. She critiques online resources that minimize the surgery's severity by focusing on incision pain rather than the intense internal pain from having most of her stomach removed. Despite moments of regret and panic during recovery, she reports rapid improvement and no lasting regret. The post concludes with her decision to reinvent her wardrobe entirely in goth style as she loses weight.
key_points: Online medical resources fundamentally misrepresent bariatric surgery recovery by focusing on incision pain when 99.5% of pain comes from internal trauma
The surgery is marketed as minimally invasive (laparoscopic, one-day hospital stay) but is actually 'a crude and unsparing act'
Recovery improves dramatically day by day - each night was 'tons better' than the previous one
She frames the weight loss as a health decision (avoiding diabetes, enabling exercise) rather than aesthetic goals
She's using the body transformation as an opportunity to completely reinvent her personal style
money_quotes: I had most of my stomach yanked out and the remainder tied together with twine.
All of my nerves inside my body are well aware of this fact and stayed busy for at least five days continuously notifying me of this act of brutality.
So, long story short, the first real observation about my bariatric sleeve surgery is this: it's a crude and unsparing act.
They're the same assholes who talk about how they orgasmed during childbirth.
I think the actual situation is that those websites are written by people who haven't actually experienced the surgery themselves.
It's amazing, actually, how quickly we can heal.
I don't care. The point of this was to avoid diabetes and be able to stay healthy and exercise, even in the summer.
posts.key_insight: Medical information sources often sanitize or minimize the reality of procedures because they're written by people who haven't actually experienced them.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil directs readers to her Bloomberg View column about the threat big data poses to health insurance. The post itself contains no substantive argument, serving only as a pointer to external content. She signals her return to regular blogging after a hiatus.
key_points: O'Neil published a column at Bloomberg View about big data and health insurance
The provocative title suggests big data will be used to deny or price people out of health coverage
This is a return-to-blogging announcement post
money_quotes: Big Data Is Coming to Take Your Health Insurance
posts.key_insight: The post title alone signals O'Neil's concern that big data will be weaponized against consumers in the health insurance market.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil provides a positive health update following bariatric surgery, explaining her decision-making process and recovery progress. She describes her changing relationship with food and her goal of returning to cycling. The post reveals that the turning point came when she could no longer bike challenging hills, triggering fears of a destructive feedback loop of declining fitness. She addresses her identity as a 'fit fat person' and her long-term thinking about staying active into old age. The surgery was motivated both by pre-diabetic concerns and a desire to maintain an active lifestyle for decades to come.
key_points: A specific failure—being unable to face steep bike hills near Poughkeepsie—triggered the realization that declining fitness creates a self-reinforcing downward spiral
The decision to get bariatric surgery was driven by long-term thinking about health and mobility at age 74, not just immediate diabetes concerns
Everyone she interviewed about the surgery had the same regret: not doing it sooner
Her self-identity as a 'fit fat person' who could always point to her biking ability was disrupted when that became no longer true
Recovery is going well with careful eating, longer walks, and plans to return to cycling
money_quotes: When you don't bike regularly, you get out of shape, which makes it even harder to bike when the weather gets better.
Once I realized this destructive feedback loop was happening, it really bothered me, and moreover fucked with my long-held notion of myself as a fit fat person.
I started thinking long term about my health, and how I wanted to be one of those active old ladies, shouting and carrying on at protests.
Although I was carrying my 300 pound body pretty well at 44, it would be much harder to do that at 74.
Their biggest regret seemed to be that they hadn't done it earlier.
It's fun, as long as you don't make people listen to your work-out schedule or anything. If I start doing that, please slap me.
posts.key_insight: Recognizing a destructive feedback loop in your health—where declining ability makes improvement harder—is the moment to intervene, not when you've hit bottom.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column titled 'Trump and Statistics Don't Mix.' The post itself contains no substantive argument, serving only as a pointer to external content. It follows a common blogging practice of cross-posting links to published work elsewhere.
key_points: The post announces a new Bloomberg View column about Trump and statistics
No substantive content is included in the blog post itself
Readers are directed to Bloomberg for the full column
posts.key_insight: This is a link post with no analyzable content; the actual argument exists in the referenced Bloomberg column.

posts.summary: Michael J. Barany revisits his controversial argument that 'math is everywhere' is a misleading truism that obscures mathematics' historical role as exclusive knowledge wielded by elites. He examines responses to his original Scientific American essay, finding that historians often dismissed the political stakes while mathematicians engaged primarily with implications. Barany argues against the popular view that teaching more people math solves exclusion, instead advocating for political and ethical accountability frameworks that don't require mathematical expertise. He contends that separating math from its practitioners is precisely the error that prevents meaningful reform, since mathematics has always been guarded through intertwined technical and social barriers. The essay concludes that recognizing math's embedded inequality creates ethical imperatives rather than utopian goals of universal access.
key_points: The 'math is everywhere' framing is a political and pedagogical claim, not a philosophical one, and emphasizes what math can be 'in principle' rather than what it has been 'in practice'
Separating mathematics from its users and creators is the fundamental error that enables continued exclusion while claiming universal accessibility
The 'good guy with math' argument parallels flawed gun control logic—simply arming more people with mathematical knowledge doesn't address structural power imbalances
Historical exclusion in mathematics isn't a bug but a feature: it's fundamental to math's place in the world that access is restricted
Reform requires political, ethical, and structural approaches rather than just expanding mathematical education, since the mathematical elite who wield power must be held accountable by non-experts
money_quotes: The philosophy of mathematics is political, and the politics of mathematics are philosophical.
It is precisely because math can't be separated from its practice and its place in society that, in a meaningful sense, the theorems of geometry do care and have always cared who you are.
It is fundamental to math's place in the world that it is not open to everyone.
The most misleading aspect of the claim that math is everywhere is its timeless formulation, set apart from movement and change, of opportunities for structural reform.
Decades of policy debates have taught us the dangerous fallacy of these claims when applied to guns instead of mathematics.
Those who have felt alienated or excluded from mathematics need not blame themselves for failing to grasp the mathematics that is supposedly all around them.
The only way to stop a bad guy with math is a good guy with math.
History tells us this isn't a bug; it's a feature.
posts.key_insight: Mathematics cannot be separated from its social context and power structures, so meaningful reform requires holding mathematical elites accountable through political and ethical frameworks rather than simply expanding mathematical education.

posts.summary: This brief post promotes O'Neil's Bloomberg View article about the Google diversity memo controversy. She argues that the public debate conflates two separate issues that should be addressed independently: biological differences between genders and workplace bias against women. O'Neil suggests that by decoupling these discussions, we can have a more productive conversation about women in tech and other prestigious, well-paid fields.
key_points: The public conversation about women in tech is fundamentally misframed
Biological gender differences and workplace bias are distinct issues being incorrectly merged
The Google diversity memo controversy exemplifies this confused discourse
Separating these issues is necessary for meaningful progress
money_quotes: We're having the wrong conversation about women in tech.

We need to decouple two very different issues that have arisen amid the commotion about diversity at Google: biological differences between genders, and bias against females working in tech and more generally in well-paid, prestigious jobs.
posts.key_insight: The debate about women in tech becomes unproductive when biological arguments and bias discussions are conflated rather than examined separately.

posts.summary: O'Neil recounts her experience recovering from bariatric surgery and her disastrous decision to start weighing herself weekly despite 23 years of evidence that scales were harmful to her mental health. After creating a predictive weight loss curve and then feeling devastated when she came in one pound over her made-up projection, she spiraled into toxic weight loss chat boards before a news event (Bannon's firing) snapped her out of it. She threw away her scale, recognizing that she had surgery to be healthy, not to lose weight, and that health cannot be measured on a scale. She criticizes the medical establishment's obsession with goal weights and concludes with a philosophy about discarding controllable things that cause harm.
key_points: Scales have a uniquely destructive psychological effect that even 'scientific framing' cannot neutralize
Weight loss chat boards are indistinguishable from pro-anorexia forums when you remove the medical terminology
The medical establishment's obsession with goal weights undermines the actual health goals of bariatric surgery
There's a gendered dimension to scale anxiety that men often don't experience
Having control over something harmful means you have the power to eliminate it entirely
money_quotes: I didn't have this surgery to lose weight, I had this surgery to be healthy. And that's not something you can measure on a scale, or even once a week.
If you removed the words 'bariatric' from these chats, they'd be indistinguishable from those famous websites that exchange tricks on anorexia.
1 pound is the difference between a poop and not a poop.
I came in 1 pound more than 'expected' based on this totally made up, unscientific graph I had built from nothing.

There are lots of things that we have no control over and that suck. Then there are things we do have control over and that suck. My new motto is, if it's something in the latter category, throw it the fuck away.
I stood up from my seat, walked over to my scale, and threw it the fuck away.
posts.key_insight: Quantifying health through metrics like weight can undermine actual wellbeing, and recognizing when a measurement tool is harmful gives you the power to reject it entirely.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares that her TED talk has been published. The talk, titled 'The era of blind faith in big data must end,' addresses the dangers of uncritical acceptance of algorithmic decision-making. The post itself contains minimal content beyond the link and a personal note hoping readers will enjoy it.
key_points: Announcement of TED talk publication
The talk's thesis is captured in its title: blind faith in big data is problematic and must end
Represents O'Neil's broader campaign to bring algorithmic accountability to mainstream audiences
money_quotes: The era of blind faith in big data must end
posts.key_insight: Society's uncritical trust in big data and algorithms needs to be challenged and replaced with skepticism and accountability.

posts.summary: This guest post by Becky Jaffe presents a curated reading list of Black history and literature as a response to white supremacy, particularly in the context of the 2017 political climate. The author arranges book and documentary titles into a poem format, creating an artistic framework for education. The list spans African and African American literature, slave narratives, civil rights history, and contemporary works. Jaffe explicitly frames this educational approach as resistance, urging readers not to 'give an ideological inch to the white nationalists in the white house.' The post serves as both a practical resource and a call to action through learning.
key_points: Education and learning Black history is presented as a form of resistance against white supremacy
The reading list encompasses diverse formats including novels, memoirs, slave narratives, poetry, and documentaries
Works span from historical slave narratives (Frederick Douglass, Harriet Jacobs) to contemporary voices (Ta-Nehisi Coates, Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie)
The creative presentation as a poem demonstrates how book titles themselves carry powerful meaning
The list includes both African and African American perspectives, broadening the scope beyond U.S. history
money_quotes: One way to disarm the dangerous ideology of white supremacy is to teach and learn Black history inside and outside of the classroom.
Let us not give an ideological inch to the white nationalists in the white house.
We Are the Ones We Have Been Waiting For
Anything We Love Can Be Saved
posts.key_insight: Countering dangerous ideologies requires proactive education, and the titles of freedom literature themselves form a powerful collective statement when read together.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about tools and efforts to combat problematic algorithms. The post itself contains no substantive analysis, serving purely as a pointer to external content. O'Neil uses her characteristic framing of algorithms as 'overlords' to signal the adversarial relationship between automated systems and the public interest.
key_points: Announces a new Bloomberg View column on fighting bad algorithms
Frames algorithms as 'overlords' suggesting power imbalance and need for resistance
Directs readers to her archive of Bloomberg columns for more content
money_quotes: Look Who's Fighting Our Algorithmic Overlords
posts.key_insight: The post serves as a gateway to O'Neil's journalism on algorithmic accountability rather than offering standalone analysis.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her experience relearning how to eat after weight loss surgery, discovering her body now rejects meat but accepts fish and tofu. She describes the difficulty of finding healthy food while traveling in areas dominated by fast food chains, highlighting systemic issues with American food availability. She critiques the overly strict nutritional guidelines from her surgeon's office, which she finds impractical and based on flawed causal reasoning. Ultimately, she chooses to listen to her body's new signals rather than forcing compliance with rules that require eating unappetizing combinations like meat with diet mayonnaise.
key_points: Post-surgery, her body has fundamentally changed what foods it can tolerate, rejecting meat while accepting fish, tofu, and Thai food
Traveling in suburban America revealed how unhealthy, addictive, and cheap food dominates available options - a systemic health problem
The nutritionist's guidelines are designed for non-compliance, making them absurdly strict for someone actually trying to follow them
Nutritional advice based on correlation (lean eaters lose weight) doesn't mean the advice will work when prescribed to others
She's choosing to trust her body's new signals over rigid rules, prioritizing satisfying food over forcing down unpleasant 'compliant' meals
money_quotes: Out of desperation, I google searched 'good healthy food near me' and it came up with two results: Dunkin Donuts and a martini bar.

The food available in places like this is unhealthy, addictive, and omnipresent. Not to mention very, very cheap. Which is to say, there is a systemic problem we will have to face sooner or later when it comes to health.
I mean, yuck. Who wants to force down chunks of chicken or beef with diet mayonnaise? I'd rather never eat meat in the first place.
I cannot make it a success with sheer force of will.
I'd rather be listening to my body and eating good food than ignoring my body and eating plastic.

It's easy enough to do as a nutritionist: if you notice that people who eat high fat foods don't lose as much weight as people who eat lean foods, it's natural to tell everyone to eat lean foods. But that doesn't mean such advice will be heeded or will work.
posts.key_insight: Medical and nutritional guidelines often confuse correlation with causation and design for non-compliance, making them counterproductive for people actually trying to follow them.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about the opioid crisis. The post contains no substantive content beyond linking to the external article. It serves purely as a cross-posting notice for her blog audience.
key_points: Post is a link announcement to Bloomberg View column
No substantive analysis or argument contained in the blog post itself
References ongoing opioid crisis as the column topic
posts.key_insight: This post contains no original content to analyze—it is merely a pointer to external writing published elsewhere.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her dramatic weight loss following bariatric surgery, using vivid metaphors (gallons of milk, sponges) to describe the physical transformations her body is undergoing. She shares her theory that fat cells lose weight uniformly, creating increasingly spongy tissue and excess skin. Drawing on a failed diet attempt seven years earlier, she speculates about gut biome changes and their role in sustainable weight loss. Most pointedly, she pushes back against congratulations that equate thinness with beauty, arguing she preferred her fuller body aesthetically and only pursued weight loss for health reasons.
key_points: Weight loss of 43 pounds equals about 5 gallons of milk—a visceral way to conceptualize the physical reality of losing mass
Fat loss appears to happen uniformly across all fat cells, leading to 'spongy' tissue and significant excess skin
Gut microbiome changes may be a key mechanism behind why bariatric surgery succeeds where willpower-based diets fail
Society's conflation of thinness with beauty is harmful—O'Neil explicitly states she found her larger body more attractive
Sustainable weight loss requires psychological adjustment to bodily changes, not just dietary discipline
money_quotes: You should look at yourself as a container – most likely a bag – of liquid, he said, much like a plastic container of milk.
My skin is excessive for my needs, if you catch my drift. And that's not going away.
It was delicious, my friends, and it broke me. Somehow I could never look back.
Some people do fight every minute of every day to stay thin, but I wouldn't want to be one of them. Too many other things to do.
Please don't equate thin with beautiful. I liked my body so much more when I was fat, when I looked like an enormous buddha, full and round.
I didn't do this to look better, I did it to be healthier, to bike in the summer and to avoid diabetes.
I'm hoping to eventually find a way to love my body in spite of how it looks, but it's a monumental challenge.
posts.key_insight: Weight loss is not a triumph of beauty but a complicated negotiation between health, identity, and a society that wrongly equates thinness with worth.

posts.summary: This is a brief post linking to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about transparency in digital advertising. She frames the piece as imagining an internet designed to serve citizens rather than consumers, suggesting a fundamental tension between commercial interests and public good in how platforms like Facebook and Google operate. The post itself serves primarily as a pointer to the full argument published elsewhere.
key_points: The internet as currently designed prioritizes consumer relationships over citizen relationships
Facebook and Google's ad data should be made transparent to the public
There's a distinction worth drawing between being treated as a consumer versus being treated as a citizen
money_quotes: I try to imagine an internet optimized for citizens rather than consumers
posts.key_insight: The framing of internet users as 'consumers' rather than 'citizens' fundamentally shapes how platforms treat and potentially exploit them.

posts.summary: This is a brief post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about the ethical implications of 'gaydar' algorithms. The piece features an interview with Michal Kosinski, the researcher behind a controversial algorithm that claimed to detect sexual orientation from facial images. O'Neil frames this as an example of how algorithms can become 'creepy' and raises questions about data scientists' ethical responsibilities.
key_points: O'Neil interviewed the author of the controversial gaydar algorithm about ethics
The post highlights concerns about algorithmic systems that can infer sensitive personal attributes
Data scientists bear ethical responsibility for the tools they create
money_quotes: 'Gaydar' Shows How Creepy Algorithms Can Get
posts.key_insight: Algorithms that can infer sensitive personal information like sexual orientation raise urgent questions about data scientists' ethical obligations.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares two new essays she published. The first, in Boston Review, examines futurism and how to critically evaluate futurist claims. The second, in Bloomberg View, argues that the Equifax data breach prompted the wrong public conversation about personal data. The post serves primarily as a pointer to her work published elsewhere rather than containing substantive analysis itself.
key_points: O'Neil published an essay on futurism in Boston Review titled 'Know Thy Futurist'
Her Bloomberg View column argues the Equifax hack discussion missed the real issues around personal data
The post demonstrates O'Neil's regular commentary across multiple mainstream publications
money_quotes: we're having the wrong conversation about personal data
posts.key_insight: The Equifax data breach sparked public debate, but the conversation focused on the wrong aspects of how personal data is collected and used.

posts.summary: This brief post announces Cathy O'Neil's appearance on WBUR's Open Source radio program with Christopher Lydon. The discussion focused on Max Tegmark's book 'Life 3.0' about artificial intelligence. O'Neil notes her personal connection to the show from when she lived in Somerville. The episode featured fellow guests Erik Brynjolfsson and Yarden Katz.
key_points: O'Neil appeared on the radio program Open Source with Christopher Lydon
The discussion centered on Max Tegmark's book 'Life 3.0' about AI and the future
Other guests included economist Erik Brynjolfsson and researcher Yarden Katz
posts.key_insight: This is a brief media appearance announcement rather than a substantive post, pointing readers to a radio discussion about AI futures.

posts.summary: Three months after gastric sleeve surgery, Cathy O'Neil reflects on the physical, dietary, and psychological changes she's experienced. She's dropped several clothing sizes and adopted an all-black wardrobe for simplicity. Her relationship with food has fundamentally changed—hunger no longer feels like a panic alarm, and her taste preferences have shifted toward simpler foods. She's started running again for the first time in twelve years, partly to manage anxiety about parenting, politics, and the state of the world. She candidly discusses the temptation of alcohol and the loss of food as a comfort mechanism, while noting that thin people use food for comfort without stigma.
key_points: The surgery changed the nature of hunger itself—from a 'loud panic button' requiring immediate action to a manageable signal allowing time to cook
Taste preferences shifted dramatically, with previously bland foods now tasting rich and sweet/buttery cravings disappearing
She acknowledges the psychological challenge of losing food as a coping mechanism while noting thin people comfort-eat without judgment
Exercise has become necessary for managing anxiety about parenting teenagers, misogyny, and political cruelty
She's wary of alcohol becoming a replacement comfort, noting it 'works really really well' post-surgery
money_quotes: Before, if I got hungry, it was like a loud panic button in my brain had been set off.
Nowadays, I realize I'm getting hungry, but it simply means that I have to start thinking about food pretty soon.
It's hard being a woman in this age of misogyny. It's hard being a human in this age of political cruelty.
I have lost food as a source of comfort.
Before you blame my obesity on that, let me suggest that thin people comfort themselves with food all the time but aren't accused of bad behavior.
Long story short, I'm doing great, and I feel healthy, and the world still is what it is.
posts.key_insight: Weight loss surgery doesn't just change your body—it fundamentally alters your relationship with hunger, comfort, and coping mechanisms, forcing you to find new ways to deal with life's anxieties.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares that she published her first op-ed in the New York Times. The op-ed, titled 'The Ivory Tower Can't Keep Ignoring Tech,' argues that academia needs to engage more seriously with technology and algorithms. The post itself contains minimal content beyond the announcement and link to the full piece.
key_points: O'Neil published her first op-ed in the New York Times
The op-ed calls on academia to stop ignoring the tech industry and its algorithmic impacts
This represents a milestone in bringing her algorithmic accountability message to mainstream audiences
money_quotes: The Ivory Tower Can't Keep Ignoring Tech
posts.key_insight: Academia has a responsibility to critically engage with the tech industry and its algorithms rather than remaining detached in the ivory tower.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post linking to two of O'Neil's Bloomberg View columns. The first examines Ray Dalio's algorithmic management system at Bridgewater, questioning whether it simply reinforces its creator's biases. The second addresses Facebook's fundamental design problems and the challenge of preventing users from sharing biased and misleading content. Both pieces continue O'Neil's ongoing critique of how algorithms and platforms can amplify human biases rather than correct them.
key_points: Ray Dalio's management algorithm at Bridgewater may reinforce rather than eliminate human biases
Facebook's design may be fundamentally broken when it comes to preventing misinformation spread
Algorithms created by humans tend to embed and amplify their creators' biases
Social media platforms face inherent challenges in moderating biased content
money_quotes: Ray Dalio Has an Unbelievable Algorithm
Does it merely reinforce its maker's biases?
Maybe Facebook Is Broken
How can you stop people from sharing biased and misleading stuff?
posts.key_insight: Algorithms and platforms, whether in hedge fund management or social media, risk amplifying human biases rather than providing objective solutions.

posts.summary: This brief post announces two items: a new Bloomberg View column about false negatives in algorithmic decision-making for life-and-death situations like autonomous weapons, and an upcoming Occupy Alt Banking book club meeting. The Bloomberg piece argues that algorithms will perpetuate human mistakes unless we understand what data we're missing. The book club will discuss contrasting perspectives on capitalism through Milton Friedman's 'Capitalism and Freedom' and Eric Williams' 'Capitalism and Slavery.'
key_points: False negatives in algorithms can have lethal consequences, particularly in contexts like autonomous weapons
Algorithms inherit and repeat human blind spots unless we actively identify what's missing from our data
The juxtaposition of Friedman and Williams suggests an interest in examining capitalism from radically different ideological and historical perspectives
money_quotes: False Negatives Can Be a Matter of Life and Death
Algorithms will repeat our mistakes unless we know what we're missing
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic systems amplify our existing blind spots, making the identification of false negatives—what we fail to see or measure—a matter of life and death.

posts.summary: O'Neil announces the upcoming 2018 Women's March, noting she discovered it through the telltale sign of pink yarn shortages at craft stores. She urges readers to participate and marks the date while connecting the march's importance to the ongoing #MeToo movement. The post links to revelations about radio host John Hockenberry's systematic harassment and discrimination against women of color colleagues as evidence of why such activism remains necessary.
key_points: The 2018 Women's March will take place on January 20th in cities across the country
The pink pussy hat phenomenon has become culturally significant enough to cause yarn shortages
The #MeToo movement makes the march more timely than ever
Hockenberry case illustrates intersection of sexual harassment and racial discrimination
money_quotes: I found out there's going to be another Women's March by – you guessed it – going to a yarn store and finding there'd been a run on pink yarn.
There's never been a better time for this, I'm sorry to say.
posts.key_insight: Grassroots movements like the Women's March gain cultural traction that becomes visible even in unexpected places like yarn store inventory.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil promotes two upcoming speaking engagements. She will be giving a talk at Tufts University on Thursday and participating in a data science education roundtable in Washington D.C. on Friday morning. The D.C. event will be livestreamed and is open to in-person attendance.
key_points: Speaking at Tufts University on Thursday
Participating in a D.C. roundtable on data science postsecondary education on Friday
The D.C. event will be livestreamed for remote viewers
money_quotes: I'll be in D.C. on Friday morning to talk data science education with mucky-mucks.
posts.key_insight: A quick logistical post announcing speaking engagements on data science education.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares her new Bloomberg View column about the lack of reliable data on sexual harassment prevalence. She also announces she's quitting Twitter because it was negatively affecting her wellbeing. The post itself contains minimal analysis, serving primarily as a pointer to her external column.
key_points: O'Neil published a Bloomberg View piece arguing we lack sufficient data to understand sexual harassment prevalence
She announced quitting Twitter due to its negative emotional impact
The post highlights the gap between #MeToo visibility and actual statistical understanding
money_quotes: We lack the data needed to know how prevalent it is.
I quit Twitter, at least for now. It just kept bringing me down.
posts.key_insight: Even amid heightened awareness of sexual harassment through movements like #MeToo, we still lack the rigorous data needed to truly understand the scope of the problem.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about sex robots. The linked piece apparently argues that sex robots might create competition for men rather than replace women, flipping the typical narrative about this technology. The post is too short to contain substantive analysis itself, serving primarily as a teaser for her external writing.
key_points: Promotes a Bloomberg View column on sex robots
Subverts the common framing that sex robots threaten women
Suggests sex robots could create 'healthy competition' for men
money_quotes: Maybe Sex Robots Will Make Men, Not Women, Obsolete
They might at least create some healthy competition
posts.key_insight: The post teases a contrarian take on sex robot discourse, suggesting the technology's social impact may disadvantage men rather than women.

posts.summary: O'Neil responds to Trump's reported vulgar comments about Haiti by refusing to repeat them and offering a pointed contrast. She draws on her personal experience traveling in Haiti to make a moral comparison between Haitian people and the president. The brief post uses restraint and dignity to make its critique more powerful than outrage would. Her refusal to perpetuate the comments while still addressing them is itself a rhetorical choice.
key_points: Deliberately refuses to amplify harmful rhetoric by not repeating the president's words
Uses personal travel experience to provide counter-testimony about Haitian people
Redefines 'wealth' as spiritual richness rather than material or political power
Implies the president is impoverished in ways that matter most
money_quotes: I will not repeat what the president said yesterday about Haiti, because it is too mean spirited to perpetuate.
In my travels in Haiti I met people wealthier in spirit than our poor president.
posts.key_insight: True poverty is spiritual, not material, and refusing to amplify cruelty is itself a form of resistance.

posts.summary: O'Neil shares a photograph from Haiti of a young boy pointing to the sky, using it as a pointed response to Trump's reported 'shithole countries' comments about Haiti and African nations. The brief post serves as both a tribute to Haiti's dignity and a rebuke of presidential disrespect. She frames the child's gesture as a reminder that moral authority transcends political power.
key_points: A chance encounter in Haiti three years prior becomes politically resonant in light of Trump's comments
The child's spontaneous gesture of pointing to God carries symbolic weight against presidential disrespect
Moral and spiritual authority is positioned as superior to political power
money_quotes: There is a power higher than that of any president.
posts.key_insight: The simple faith of a Haitian child serves as a quiet but powerful rebuke to political leaders who demean entire nations.

posts.summary: The author shares her recent diagnosis of Graves' Disease, an autoimmune disorder causing thyroid hyperactivity, anxiety, and compulsive exercise. She explains the condition is treatable with medication (Methimazole) or potentially radioactive iodine therapy. The diagnosis actually brought relief by explaining her deep anxiety, sleep troubles, and rapidly changing eyesight. She notes that quitting Twitter has helped her anxiety, and she's channeling her hyperactive energy into training for races. The post ends with a plug for her band's upcoming gig.
key_points: Graves' Disease causes thyroid hyperactivity leading to anxiety, sleep problems, and compulsive exercise - but it's treatable
Autoimmune diseases cluster together; she already had autoimmune gastritis before this diagnosis
The diagnosis provided relief by explaining mysterious symptoms like deep anxiety and rapidly changing eyesight
Quitting social media has been beneficial for managing anxiety
She's channeling the hyperactive energy into training for a 5K and a sprint triathlon
money_quotes: It's an autoimmune disease, and it's treatable, so please don't worry.
Once you've gotten one autoimmune disease you're more likely to get another. They cluster.
Finding out I have Graves' Disease is actually kind of good. It explains the deep anxiety I was feeling and the trouble I was having sleeping.
I'm not sure if that's because the meds are working or because I'm just acclimating to Trump.
Or it might just be because I quit Twitter, which was bringing me down consistently. Having no social media habits is good for anxiety problems!
I've always said that people who get really into exercise are the most boring people in the world because all they ever want to talk about is exercise.
I've really become insufferable. Please tell me to shut up when you next hear me drone on.
posts.key_insight: A medical diagnosis can be strangely liberating when it finally explains inexplicable feelings of anxiety and unrest.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View article about personality tests in hiring. The full argument about how personality tests fail American workers by filtering out candidates for wrong reasons is contained in the linked Bloomberg piece, not in this blog post itself. The post serves as a pointer to her external journalism work.
key_points: Personality tests used in hiring often filter out candidates for illegitimate reasons
These tests are failing American workers rather than serving them
O'Neil's critique is part of her broader Bloomberg View commentary on algorithmic and data-driven systems
money_quotes: All too often, they filter people out for the wrong reasons.
posts.key_insight: Personality tests in hiring processes serve as gatekeepers that exclude qualified workers based on flawed criteria.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about pizza insurance. The column argues against the proliferation of trivial insurance schemes that distort the concept of insurance. O'Neil suggests that while insurance serves useful purposes for significant risks, extending it to minor purchases like pizza represents a problematic trend. The post itself serves primarily as a link to the full argument rather than containing substantive analysis.
key_points: Insurance is being extended to increasingly trivial purchases like pizza delivery
Many current insurance schemes distort the original useful concept of insurance
The proliferation of micro-insurance products may not serve consumer interests
money_quotes: No, I Don't Want to Insure My Pizza
Insurance can be useful, but too many schemes are distorting the concept
posts.key_insight: The expansion of insurance into trivial domains like pizza delivery represents a distortion of insurance's legitimate purpose.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares that she has written her first book review for the New York Times. The review covers two books about Virtual Reality by Jeremy Bailenson and Jaron Lanier. The post itself contains no substantive analysis, serving only as a pointer to the external review.
key_points: O'Neil wrote her first NYT book review
The review covers two VR books by Bailenson and Lanier
The post is essentially a link announcement with no substantive content
money_quotes: I just wrote my first book review for the New York Times.
posts.key_insight: This is a simple announcement post directing readers to an external book review, with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: This guest post by Eugene Stern argues that virtual reality poses an amplified threat because it scales up the most troublesome aspects of existing technology: personalization, addictiveness, and marketing manipulation. VR will collect unprecedented amounts of biometric and behavioral data through eye tracking, heart rate monitoring, and brain activity sensors, enabling hyper-targeted manipulation. The immersive nature of VR will make virtual experiences far more addictive than smartphones or social media. Most concerning, VR advertising will achieve unprecedented precision in measuring and predicting consumer behavior. The post concludes that VR's greatest danger is that it represents 'more of the same, but on steroids' - amplifying problems we've already failed to address.
key_points: VR massively scales up both data collection capabilities (eye tracking, biometrics, brain activity) and the complexity of personalized manipulation possible with that data
The addictiveness of current technology will seem quaint compared to personalized VR sex and immersive social simulations
VR advertising will achieve unprecedented effectiveness by precisely measuring physiological responses and predicting which ads will work for each individual
VR could be a 'great leveler' offering virtual access to elite experiences, making constant marketing seem like a small price to pay
Society has shown no ability to confront technology addiction, surveillance, or manipulation - VR will make all of these ten times more powerful
money_quotes: In a contest between your family, friends, and VR set over who knows you better, it's hard to see the humans having a chance.
VR is going to make the virtual world way more addictive.
The internet is funded by advertising. Why wouldn't VR be?
VR might enable advertisers to fully exploit you economically, to optimize and control all of your purchasing power.
We haven't been troubled yet whenever our technology asks us to give up control to gain comfort.
The scariest thing about VR might be that it could be more of the same, but on steroids.

If we've shown no societal ability so far to confront technology addiction, data collection and surveillance, or media manipulation, what happens when VR technology renders all of these ten times more powerful?
posts.key_insight: VR's greatest danger isn't that it introduces new problems, but that it dramatically amplifies existing technology harms - surveillance, addiction, and manipulation - that society has already proven unable to confront.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that most Americans have little reason to care about stock market performance because they lack meaningful market exposure through savings or retirement accounts. She contends that the stock market has decoupled from worker prosperity, with recent evidence showing an inverse relationship where wage increases actually trigger market declines. The wealthy are insulated through diversification while ordinary workers have almost nothing at stake. Recent policy changes like the tax bill have further entrenched this anti-correlation between market gains and worker welfare.
key_points: A majority of Americans have less than $1000 in retirement savings, giving them virtually no direct stake in market performance
Wealthy investors are insulated from market swings through diversified portfolios and hedge funds
Productivity gains decoupled from median wages long ago, breaking the link between corporate profits and worker prosperity
Recent wage increases caused market drops because shareholders have 'gotten used to keeping all the corporate profits'
The tax bill exemplified how market-positive news has become worker-negative news
money_quotes: Who cares if the stock market tanks? No, really.
It's been a long time since the market has been a good proxy for the economy as a whole.
In fact, it's become just the opposite: good news for workers means bad news for the market.
The bottom line is that shareholders have gotten used to keeping all the corporate profits.
Good news for the market has meant bad news for workers for the past year and a half.
He gave out goodies to rich people with the abstract promise that this will end up in the pockets of workers.
For the foreseeable future, I'm thinking that workers should be cheering a tanking market.
posts.key_insight: The stock market no longer represents broad economic health but rather tracks the extraction of value from workers to shareholders, making market declines potentially favorable for ordinary workers.

posts.summary: This post appears to be a brief, humorous observation about Amazon's customer Q&A feature, likely showcasing an absurd or ironic exchange between customers. The post relies on visual content or embedded examples that aren't captured in the text excerpt. O'Neil seems to be highlighting the comedic dysfunction of crowdsourced product information, where questions and answers often miss each other entirely or produce nonsensical results.
key_points: Amazon's Q&A feature often produces unintentionally comedic exchanges
Crowdsourced information systems can fail in amusing ways
The gap between customer questions and community answers reveals systemic issues
posts.key_insight: Even simple crowdsourced systems like Amazon Q&A demonstrate how algorithmic platforms can produce absurd outcomes when human behavior doesn't match the system's assumptions.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about virtual reality and inequality. The linked piece appears to argue that VR technology could be used to pacify poor people by giving them virtual experiences while real-world inequality grows unchecked. The dark framing ('virtual hamster cage') suggests technology as a tool of social control rather than liberation. O'Neil explicitly notes her pessimistic mood when writing the piece.
key_points: Virtual reality could become a mechanism for managing inequality rather than addressing it
Technology may serve to distract the poor from material deprivation
The 'hamster cage' metaphor frames VR as a tool of containment and control
money_quotes: Are You Poor? Here's Your Virtual Hamster Cage
Technology could erase the limits of inequality
I was feeling dark when I wrote this
posts.key_insight: Virtual reality technology could become a dystopian tool for making extreme inequality tolerable rather than a force for human flourishing.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques a Jane Brody New York Times article about exercise and diets, pointing out its logical contradictions. She argues that Brody acknowledges diets don't work, yet still recommends them, and titles her piece to imply exercise causes weight loss despite evidence to the contrary. O'Neil sees this as a frustrating example of incoherent health advice that shames people for accepting their bodies.
key_points: The article being critiqued admits diets don't work but recommends them anyway
People accepting their bodies as they are is framed negatively by Brody
The title misleadingly implies exercise leads to weight loss when evidence suggests otherwise
Health journalism often contains internal contradictions that go unexamined
money_quotes: I'm off of Twitter and Facebook, so my blog is starting to become my go-to rant spot once again.
Diets don't work.
People are beginning to understand this.
They're starting to accept their bodies as they are and go on with their lives.
This makes Jane Brody feel bad for them, because they really should feel bad for themselves.
posts.key_insight: Health advice that acknowledges its own ineffectiveness while still prescribing the same solutions reveals more about cultural attitudes toward bodies than about actual health.

posts.summary: This brief post announces a Bloomberg View article about Facebook's potential to measure the impact of Russian ads on the 2016 election. O'Neil argues that Facebook has the data and capability to determine whether election meddling made a difference, but questions whether the company has the willingness to conduct such analysis. The post serves primarily as a pointer to the fuller argument in the Bloomberg piece.
key_points: Facebook possesses the data to measure Russian ad impact on the 2016 election
The company could conduct a natural experiment but chooses not to
Corporate willingness, not technical capability, is the barrier to transparency
money_quotes: The company could probably measure the effect of election-meddling if it wanted to.
posts.key_insight: Facebook has the power to answer crucial questions about election interference but lacks the motivation to pursue uncomfortable truths.

posts.summary: This post shares a satirical poem by Ernie Davis responding to the revelation that Palantir secretly deployed predictive policing technology in New Orleans for six years without public knowledge. The poem draws parallels between Tolkien's palantíri (seeing stones that corrupted their users) and Peter Thiel's Palantir company, suggesting that wielding such powerful surveillance tools without wisdom leads to downfall. It traces Thiel's career from PayPal billionaire to Trump supporter, criticizing his arrogance and anti-intellectual stance. The poem recounts how political consultant James Carville brokered a secret deal to bring Palantir's predictive policing to New Orleans, bypassing city council oversight entirely. Davis argues this secrecy represents a betrayal of public trust and warns of the civil rights implications of algorithmic policing.
key_points: Palantir secretly tested predictive policing technology in New Orleans for six years, with only the mayor and police department aware
The poem draws a deliberate parallel to Tolkien's palantíri, whose power exceeded the wisdom of those who used them, leading to their corruption
Peter Thiel is portrayed as brilliant but dangerously arrogant, supporting authoritarianism while scorning education and expertise
Predictive policing circumvents the evidentiary standards of criminal justice by classifying people as vectors rather than proving specific acts
The secrecy of the arrangement, hidden even from city council, represents a fundamental betrayal of democratic accountability
money_quotes: But using a device whose might / Exceeds your wisdom risks a fearful fall.
No AI program has succeeded / In mastering the basic common sense.
But building an AI detector / For criminal propensity's no sweat. / You map a person to a vector / And classify it with a neural net.
Policing with predictive code / The guardians of security delights, / But leads us on a risky road / Toward bias and ignoring civil rights.
As long as, in the course of life, / No cousin or acquaintance goes astray.
Conclude that those who hold the reins / Of power have betrayed the public trust.
posts.key_insight: Secret deployment of predictive policing technology, hidden from democratic oversight, represents a dangerous union of corporate arrogance and government power that threatens civil liberties.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about algorithmic bail reform. The piece argues that Philadelphia should be cautious about implementing risk-assessment algorithms in its bail system. O'Neil suggests that big data solutions alone cannot fix the fundamental problems with bail, continuing her broader critique of algorithmic decision-making in criminal justice.
key_points: Risk-assessment algorithms are being proposed as solutions to broken bail systems
Big data alone is insufficient to fix systemic problems in criminal justice
Philadelphia is specifically considering implementing such an algorithm
The post serves as a pointer to a longer Bloomberg View analysis
money_quotes: Big Data Alone Can't Fix a Broken Bail System
Philadelphia should think twice about its risk-assessment algorithm
posts.key_insight: Technological solutions like risk-assessment algorithms cannot substitute for addressing the deeper structural problems in the bail system.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares a personal update about her running progress, having completed her second 5K race. She improved her time by 5 seconds (from 39:00 to 38:55) and found the race easier than her first attempt in February. She's now training for a sprint triathlon in May, combining running, biking, and swimming. The post is deliberately lighthearted and self-deprecating, celebrating small victories and the joy of simply participating rather than competing.
key_points: Completed second 5K with modest improvement (5 seconds faster, 0.21% improvement)
Training philosophy focuses on finishing without stopping rather than speed or placement
Preparing for a sprint triathlon by building stamina with multi-activity days
Finds joy in being a slow runner because it makes faster runners feel good about themselves
Running serves as an outlet for 'urban aggression' and she's grateful for her health
money_quotes: I told you I'm boring now, so here's proof.
By the end of the race I'm kind of the last always-runner and ahead of most of the sometimes-walkers.
For those interested, that's 0.21% faster. Did I mention that I'm a slow runner? I'm also a very consistently slow runner.
I honestly enjoy being that slow runner whom everyone passes, because it makes them feel really fast, and I'm happy for them, and I don't mind at all being slow.
I'm just grateful to be healthy.
It's nice sometimes to do something easy instead of forcing yourself to do something hard.
posts.key_insight: There's value in showing up and finishing at your own pace rather than competing, and gratitude for health matters more than performance metrics.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about algorithmic fairness. The post itself contains no substantive argument, serving purely as a promotional link to her external writing. The referenced piece addresses the challenge of defining and encoding fairness into algorithmic systems. O'Neil suggests that increased reliance on algorithms necessitates grappling with fundamental questions about what fairness means.
key_points: Algorithms require explicit definitions of fairness to function equitably
Growing algorithmic dependence makes defining fairness an urgent priority
The post serves as a gateway to O'Neil's mainstream media writing on algorithmic accountability
money_quotes: If we're going to rely on algorithms, we'll have to figure it out.
posts.key_insight: As algorithms increasingly govern our lives, we must first solve the philosophical problem of defining fairness before we can encode it into machines.

posts.summary: This brief post links to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column arguing that the ethics of self-driving cars shouldn't be a primary concern. She suggests that road deaths create strong incentives for accountability that are missing in other algorithmic domains. The post serves as an announcement directing readers to her fuller argument in Bloomberg.
key_points: Self-driving car ethics receive disproportionate attention compared to other algorithmic harms
Road deaths create natural accountability incentives that other AI applications lack
The visibility of car accidents forces companies to care about outcomes
money_quotes: Road deaths create an incentive to care that is woefully lacking elsewhere.
posts.key_insight: The real problem isn't self-driving car ethics—it's that other algorithmic systems cause harm without the visible accountability that fatal car accidents demand.

posts.summary: This is a brief pointer post directing readers to an interview Cathy O'Neil gave to Wired UK about establishing a Hippocratic Oath for data science. The interview explores the ethics of AI and the need for accountability mechanisms in the field. O'Neil suggests that data scientists need ethical guidelines with real enforcement power, not just aspirational principles.
key_points: Data science needs formal ethical guidelines similar to medicine's Hippocratic Oath
Any such oath must have 'teeth' - real enforcement mechanisms and consequences
The interview covers broader AI ethics beyond just data science practices
money_quotes: To work for society, data scientists need a hippocratic oath with teeth
posts.key_insight: Ethical guidelines for data scientists are meaningless without enforcement mechanisms that hold practitioners accountable for the societal harm their work can cause.

posts.summary: This is a brief post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg View article about Congressional hearings on Facebook. The post argues that Congress is failing to address the fundamental issues with Facebook and tech platforms. O'Neil advocates for a 'data bill of rights' as the necessary policy response. The post serves primarily as a pointer to her more detailed argument in Bloomberg.
key_points: Congress is not addressing the core problems with Facebook during hearings
Americans need fundamental data rights protections
The solution requires systemic policy change, not just grilling executives
money_quotes: Congress Is Missing the Point on Facebook
Americans need a data bill of rights
posts.key_insight: Congressional oversight of tech companies fails without establishing fundamental data rights for citizens.

posts.summary: Guest author Allison Pacelli, a mathematics professor at Williams College, reflects on her connection with Cathy O'Neil and promotes her PZ Math Calculus Prep Camp. She argues that math is genuinely difficult for everyone eventually, yet society wrongly accepts mathematical illiteracy. Pacelli contends that math education too often focuses on memorization rather than understanding, and that her camp addresses this by teaching the conceptual foundations of calculus. The post emphasizes that mathematical thinking builds transferable skills like logical reasoning, precision, and perseverance that benefit students across all fields.
key_points: Math is inherently difficult for everyone at some point, regardless of intelligence or past success, yet society uniquely tolerates people claiming they're 'not a math person'
Mathematical thinking improves general problem-solving, logical reasoning, and even writing skills across all disciplines
Traditional math education fails students by emphasizing memorization of formulas rather than genuine conceptual understanding
A week-long calculus prep camp can give students the conceptual foundation and confidence they need to succeed in formal calculus courses
With the right teacher or materials, everyone can enjoy and succeed at mathematics
money_quotes: Math is hard. Very hard! No matter how intelligent someone is or how amazing their past accomplishments, math eventually becomes difficult for anyone.
It's perfectly acceptable for someone in society to say, 'oh I'm not a math person.'
Math makes you smarter. It makes you a better problem solver. It makes you better at persevering and trying new ideas until you find a solution.
Unfortunately, far too often, math is taught in schools as a bunch of formulas to memorize and rules to use.
When I first took Calculus in high school, I did very well and got a 5 on the AP exam. But I didn't truly understand it.
Mathematical thinking also requires that we be very precise and clear. This precision and clarity translates into better arguments in any paper or debate.
posts.key_insight: True mathematical understanding, rather than formula memorization, builds transferable thinking skills that benefit students across all areas of life.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares her NPR Morning Edition appearance discussing alternatives to Facebook. The post links to the NPR segment exploring what a better social network might look like in light of Facebook's flaws. No substantive argument is made in the post itself, as the content exists in the linked audio segment.
key_points: O'Neil appeared on NPR's Morning Edition to discuss social media alternatives
The discussion centered on whether relying on a single dominant social network is problematic
The segment explored what a better social network might look like
money_quotes: Would We Be Better Off If We Didn't Rely On 1 Social Network?
posts.key_insight: The dominance of a single social network like Facebook raises questions about whether alternative models might better serve society.

posts.summary: This guest post announces a new speaker series on Mathematics and Democracy, covering topics like election theory, redistricting, algorithmic governance, and quantitative fairness. The inaugural talk features CMU professor Wesley Pegden, who served as an expert witness in the Pennsylvania gerrymandering case. Pegden's team developed a probability theorem that provides statistical rigor to methods for detecting gerrymandering. The Pennsylvania Supreme Court used this mathematical evidence to rule the state's congressional districting an unconstitutional partisan gerrymander.
key_points: A new speaker series launches exploring intersections of mathematics and democratic processes
Topics span election theory, redistricting, algorithmic social infrastructure, and quantitative fairness
Wesley Pegden's mathematical theorem was used as evidence in Pennsylvania's landmark gerrymandering case
The theorem uses randomness to detect gerrymandering with statistical rigor
money_quotes: The series will host a scholarly conversation on a broad range of issues where mathematics touches on matters of democracy.
His team has proven a nice result in probability theory that adds further statistical rigor to an important new method for measuring gerrymanders.
I will discuss a theorem which allows us to use randomness to detect gerrymandering of Congressional districtings in a statistically rigorous way.
posts.key_insight: Mathematical rigor and probability theory can provide objective, statistically sound methods for detecting partisan gerrymandering in court cases.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that her algorithmic auditing company ORCAA was featured in Wired magazine. The article by Jessi Hempel covers ORCAA's first customer engagement auditing Rentlogic. This brief post serves as an announcement directing readers to the full Wired piece about algorithmic accountability in business.
key_points: ORCAA (O'Neil Risk Consulting & Algorithmic Auditing) received mainstream media coverage in Wired
Rentlogic was ORCAA's first customer for algorithmic auditing services
The Wired article frames algorithmic auditing as a way for businesses to prove fairness
money_quotes: WANT TO PROVE YOUR BUSINESS IS FAIR? AUDIT YOUR ALGORITHM
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic auditing is emerging as a viable business service that companies can use to demonstrate the fairness of their automated systems.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that her algorithmic auditing company ORCAA was featured in MIT Technology Review's 'Download' section on emerging technology. The brief post celebrates recent media coverage, noting that both the MIT Technology Review piece and a Wired article from the same week have generated business inquiries for ORCAA. O'Neil expresses optimism about the growing interest in algorithmic accountability services.
key_points: ORCAA was featured in MIT Technology Review's emerging technology section
The company audits algorithms for bias
Recent media coverage (MIT Tech Review and Wired) has generated business inquiries
There is growing mainstream interest in algorithmic accountability
money_quotes: This and the Wired piece last week have led to a bunch of inquiries for ORCAA, which is super exciting!
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic auditing is gaining recognition as an important emerging field, with mainstream tech publications now covering companies that hold algorithms accountable for bias.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg Opinion column about the problematic nature of crime data. The column argues that marijuana arrests serve as one of the few reliable measures of racial bias in policing because usage rates are similar across races but arrest rates differ dramatically. O'Neil warns that marijuana legalization, while positive, will eliminate this important barometer for measuring systemic bias in the criminal justice system.
key_points: Crime data is fundamentally flawed and unreliable for measuring actual criminal behavior
Marijuana arrests uniquely reveal policing bias because usage is similar across races while arrests are not
Legalization will remove one of the few objective measures we have for detecting discriminatory enforcement
The data we use to build predictive policing algorithms is itself a product of biased systems
money_quotes: Let's Not Forget How Wrong Our Crime Data Are
When marijuana is legalized, we'll lose our only reliable barometer of bias in arrests.
posts.key_insight: Marijuana arrest data serves as a rare window into policing bias precisely because usage rates are equal across races, and losing this metric through legalization means losing one of our few tools for measuring systemic discrimination.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces that her algorithmic auditing company ORCAA was featured in Fast Company. The piece highlights ORCAA's 'seal of approval' concept for algorithms, comparing it to organic certification for food. The post is brief, serving mainly to share the coverage and credit the designers who created the certification seal.
key_points: ORCAA received press coverage in Fast Company for its algorithmic certification work
The company offers a 'seal of approval' for algorithms analogous to organic food certification
The seal was designed by GoodGoodWork
money_quotes: The food we eat has quality certifications. Why shouldn't the algorithms that shape our world?
posts.key_insight: Algorithms that affect people's lives should be subject to independent quality certification, similar to how we certify food safety.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains her unconventional approach to teaching programming concepts through self-published zines—short, illustrated booklets traditionally associated with punk and art movements. She argues that zines are an efficient teaching tool because they make intimidating technical content accessible and fun, allowing busy professionals to learn on their commute. The format's low barrier to entry—both for readers who find illustrated content less intimidating and for creators who don't need drawing skills—makes it an effective way to spread programming knowledge.
key_points: Zines transform intimidating technical material into approachable content that people actually read and learn from
The format is highly efficient: complex tools like tcpdump, strace, and eBPF can be meaningfully explained in a 24-page illustrated booklet
Even readers new to programming engage with advanced content because the format feels accessible rather than overwhelming
Zines are cheap to produce at scale ($300 for 500 copies), making them practical conference giveaways
Drawing skill is not a prerequisite—information organization matters more than artistic ability
money_quotes: So suddenly comics aren't just for fun– they're a tool that you can use to teach new ideas!
It turns out that most people don't have time to read long programming books. And a lot of useful ideas can be explained really quickly!
Fun, accessible content *works*. People understand it.
Because it's presented in an adorable illustrated 24-page zine people are like 'oh how interesting and cute!' and don't hesitate to pick it up, read it on their commute to work, and learn something.
I didn't understand 100% of it but a lot of it made sense!
When trying to teach, information content and organization is a lot more important than drawing skills.
posts.key_insight: Making technical content visually fun and compact removes the intimidation barrier that keeps people from learning, proving that accessibility and depth aren't mutually exclusive.

posts.summary: This is a brief post linking to a Bloomberg View essay arguing that Facebook's influence on elections should be studied by scientists. O'Neil proposes treating Facebook like a jurisdiction that needs election observers, suggesting external scrutiny of how the platform affects democratic processes in the 2018 midterms.
key_points: Facebook's role in elections warrants independent scientific study
The platform should be subject to election observer-style oversight
External accountability mechanisms are needed for social media's democratic impact
money_quotes: Let's Send Facebook Some Election Observers
Scientists should study its role in the 2018 vote
posts.key_insight: Social media platforms like Facebook have become so influential in elections that they should be subject to the same kind of external observation and scrutiny we apply to traditional electoral systems.
