posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about the Harvard admissions controversy. The post itself contains no substantive argument, only linking to the external essay with the provocative claim that college admissions will never be fair, but suggests we can build a better system if we acknowledge this reality.
key_points: O'Neil wrote a Bloomberg View essay about the Harvard admissions controversy
Her thesis is that elite college admissions will never be truly fair
She suggests recognizing this unfairness could help us build better systems
money_quotes: College Admissions Will Never Be Fair
If we recognize this, we can build a better system
posts.key_insight: Acknowledging the inherent unfairness of elite college admissions is a prerequisite for improving the system.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg View essay criticizing Google Glass. The post expresses clear disdain for both Google Glass and airplane flights, teasing her argument that the device's utility in specific contexts doesn't justify its broader existence. The actual substantive arguments are contained in the linked Bloomberg article rather than this blog post itself.
key_points: Google Glass is something worth hating despite its usefulness in certain applications
Utility in specific contexts doesn't automatically make a technology good for society
The post serves primarily as a pointer to her more detailed Bloomberg analysis
money_quotes: The device's usefulness in certain applications doesn't make it good.
posts.key_insight: A technology's usefulness in narrow applications doesn't redeem its broader negative implications for society.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg View column criticizing the Gates Foundation's education reform efforts. The post frames the foundation's big-data approach to improving public schools as not merely unsuccessful but actively harmful. O'Neil signals her core argument through the column's subtitle: that well-intentioned data-driven reforms can cause real damage when implemented poorly.
key_points: The Gates Foundation's education reform initiative is characterized as a failure
The critique goes beyond ineffectiveness to claim the reforms caused actual harm
Big-data approaches to education reform are implicitly questioned
money_quotes: The Gates Foundation's big-data experiment wasn't just a failure. It did real harm.
posts.key_insight: Data-driven education reform, even when backed by enormous resources and good intentions, can cause real harm when it treats students and teachers as optimization problems.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces an event celebrating the revival of Science For The People, a publication returning after nearly 30 years. The event features a mini-documentary about the organization's revitalization and readings from their new essay collection on geoengineering. This is a brief promotional post directing readers to the NYC event.
key_points: Science For The People is publishing again after a hiatus since 1989
Their first new publication focuses on the science and politics of geoengineering
A mini-documentary about the organization's revival will premiere at the event
money_quotes: Celebrate the return of Science for the People!
posts.key_insight: Science For The People, a publication examining the intersection of science and politics, has been revived after nearly three decades.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to a Bloomberg View column about the author's path to becoming a mathematician. The post mentions that dominoes and mathematical proofs played a formative role in her decision. The actual content and arguments are contained in the linked Bloomberg article rather than the blog post itself.
key_points: The author wrote a Bloomberg View column about her journey to mathematics
Dominoes played a role in inspiring her mathematical career
Mathematical proofs can be empowering experiences
money_quotes: Proofs can be empowering
posts.key_insight: Mathematical proofs can serve as empowering experiences that inspire people to pursue careers in mathematics.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares that she co-authored an op-ed in the Financial Times with Bart Schermer. The op-ed, titled 'Audit the algorithms that are ruling our lives,' argues for algorithmic accountability and transparency. The post itself contains minimal content beyond the link and acknowledgment that the article is behind a paywall.
key_points: O'Neil collaborated with Bart Schermer on a Financial Times op-ed
The op-ed calls for auditing algorithms that govern daily life
The full argument is inaccessible without a subscription
money_quotes: Audit the algorithms that are ruling our lives
posts.key_insight: Algorithms increasingly control important aspects of our lives and need systematic auditing for accountability.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg View column about regulating technology and algorithms in the United States. The post indicates that America cannot simply follow Europe's regulatory approach (likely referring to GDPR) but suggests Senator Mark Warner has proposed viable alternatives. The substantive analysis appears in the linked Bloomberg article rather than this blog post itself.
key_points: The US needs its own approach to data and algorithm regulation rather than copying Europe
Senator Mark Warner has proposed ideas worth considering for tech regulation
The 'Wild West' framing suggests current US data practices are lawless and need governance
money_quotes: How America Can Stop Being the Wild West of Data
It can't follow Europe's example. But Senator Mark Warner has some good ideas.
posts.key_insight: America needs homegrown solutions for regulating technology and data rather than importing European frameworks wholesale.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg Opinion column arguing that Mark Zuckerberg and other major tech leaders are unprepared for the societal responsibilities their platforms carry. The post serves primarily as a teaser linking to the full argument published elsewhere. O'Neil characterizes her take as 'snarky,' signaling her critical stance toward Silicon Valley leadership.
key_points: Tech industry leaders like Zuckerberg lack the depth to handle the societal implications of their platforms
This criticism extends beyond Zuckerberg to 'all the big boys of tech'
O'Neil positions herself as a regular Bloomberg Opinion contributor offering critical tech commentary
money_quotes: Mark Zuckerberg Is Totally Out of His Depth
And so are all the big boys of tech
posts.key_insight: The titans of tech have built platforms with enormous societal impact but lack the wisdom and preparation to responsibly manage them.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional post where Cathy O'Neil shares her new Bloomberg Opinion column arguing that Facebook's reputation scores, while creepy, are probably beneficial. She also promotes a piece by her friend Laura Strausfeld about making tampons free and safer through better testing requirements when government entities become bulk purchasers.
key_points: O'Neil takes a nuanced position on Facebook reputation scores - acknowledging they're creepy but arguing they're useful
The post highlights how government bulk purchasing power could drive better safety standards for consumer products like tampons
O'Neil uses her platform to amplify other writers' work on policy issues
money_quotes: They're creepy, but probably a good idea.
As government entities like prisons and schools become big buyers, they should demand better testing and disclosure for consumers.
posts.key_insight: Reputation scoring systems and government purchasing decisions both represent institutional power that can be wielded for either surveillance or consumer protection.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post linking to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg Opinion column about Google and algorithmic bias. The post suggests that Trump's complaints about Google bias contain a kernel of truth, though not in the way he frames it. O'Neil argues that tech companies have a real problem with bias that they need to acknowledge, even if the specific political complaints are misguided.
key_points: Tech companies have a genuine bias problem they need to confront
Trump's complaints about Google contain partial truth, but the real issue differs from his framing
The bias in tech platforms is a systemic problem, not a partisan conspiracy
money_quotes: Tech Companies Need To Admit They Have A Problem
In a Way, Trump Is Right About Google's Bias
It's not harming him, but it's a real problem
posts.key_insight: Tech companies' algorithmic bias is a real problem worth addressing, even when the complaints come from bad-faith actors focused on the wrong issues.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg Opinion column arguing that the financial system may be fundamentally unfixable. The column's subtitle suggests that incremental improvements to risk management are insufficient and that a paradigm shift is needed. The post serves primarily as a pointer to her external writing rather than containing substantive analysis itself.
key_points: The financial system's problems may be structural rather than fixable through reform
Better risk management alone is insufficient to address systemic issues
A fundamental paradigm shift is needed rather than incremental improvements
money_quotes: Maybe This Financial System Can't Be Fixed
Better risk management isn't enough. We need a different paradigm.
posts.key_insight: Incremental reforms to financial risk management may be futile if the system itself is fundamentally flawed and requires a complete paradigm shift.

posts.summary: This guest post by Jianyin Roachell examines China's emerging National Social Credit System, which will quantify citizens' social worth based on purchasing behavior and conduct by 2020. The author distinguishes between two components: a commercial credit system (similar to FICO) and a social management system that blacklists offenders. Rather than simply condemning the system as Orwellian, Roachell argues that cultural context matters—Confucian values have long emphasized trusting authority. The post raises concerns about how integrating credit scores into dating apps could solidify class divisions and limit social mobility, while also suggesting big data could potentially be leveraged to reduce inequality.
key_points: China's AI will likely surpass the West due to training on 1.3 billion people's data with weak privacy protections
The social credit system has two distinct parts: commercial credit (financial trustworthiness) and social management (government blacklisting)
Cultural framing: Chinese firms play 'Mother' offering rewards while government plays 'Father' monitoring bad behavior
Integration of credit scores into dating sites may entrench class divisions across generations
The author proposes 'Digital Social Mobility'—using big data to increase opportunity rather than surveillance
money_quotes: By 2020, China will roll out a national social credit system to quantify every citizen's social worth in society through what they buy and how they behave.
In China, consumers are sucking on the tit of business discounts and freemium products, while the government is monitoring bad behavior through a very data-driven censorship system.
Companies are building machine learning models that train on 1.3 Billion people's personal data points.
It has always been in China's DNA to trust and obey the authorities even in the ancient chinese imperial times.
Over time this may further solidify the classes and may even amplify the effects, where the lower-class will stay poor, and upper class will stay wealthy.

There are so many opportunities for firms and government to work together to increase the value of the people, bring them out of poverty, and give policyholders the intelligence to redistribute wealth.
posts.key_insight: China's social credit system isn't simply dystopian surveillance—it's a complex fusion of commercial credit scoring and social control that could either entrench inequality or, if designed thoughtfully, potentially increase social mobility.

posts.summary: This is a brief promotional post announcing an upcoming performance by the TomTown Ramblers band at The Rockwood Music Hall. Cathy O'Neil shares her excitement about playing with her bandmates and promotes ticket sales. She highlights the original songwriting talent of band members Blair Bodine and Jamie Kingston, expressing humble appreciation for being part of such a talented group. The post also mentions merchandise will be available at the show.
key_points: TomTown Ramblers performing at Rockwood Music Hall on October 13th, 2018
Tickets available for $10
Band performs mostly original songs by members Blair Bodine, Jamie Kingston, and Jake Appel
Merchandise including t-shirts and shakers will be sold at the event
money_quotes: I'm kind of just amazed I'm allowed to be in this band, they're so freaking talented.
posts.key_insight: This is a personal announcement about the author's musical side project rather than her typical analytical content about algorithms and society.

posts.summary: O'Neil announces her Bloomberg Opinion piece arguing that Amazon's sexist recruiting algorithm should surprise no one in the tech industry. She contends that tech companies deliberately maintain 'plausible deniability' about algorithmic bias, feigning surprise when discriminatory systems are exposed. The post frames this as an ongoing pattern where biased algorithms are everywhere but companies actively avoid acknowledging them.
key_points: Amazon's sexist recruiting algorithm is part of a widespread pattern, not an isolated incident
Tech industry leaders maintain 'plausible deniability' by pretending not to know about algorithmic bias
Gender-biased algorithms are pervasive but companies deliberately avoid examining them
money_quotes: Amazon's sexist recruiting algorithm is not a surprise to anyone
The tech bros are trying to maintain plausible deniability
They're everywhere, but nobody wants to know about it
posts.key_insight: Tech companies' professed surprise at discovering biased algorithms is a deliberate performance designed to avoid accountability for discrimination they could have anticipated and prevented.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post consisting of only two words: 'I'm animated!!' The post appears to be a test or announcement about some animation feature, possibly related to the blog's technical functionality. There is no substantive argument or analysis present. The title 'The Truth About Algorithms' is entirely disconnected from the actual content, which contains no discussion of algorithms whatsoever.
key_points: The post contains no actual content about algorithms despite its title
Appears to be a test post or technical announcement about animation
The disconnect between title and content suggests this may have been a draft or placeholder
posts.key_insight: This post contains no substantive content to analyze—it appears to be a test or placeholder post unrelated to its title.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil offers a humorous yet philosophical guide to surviving excessive work travel. She argues that business class and airport lounges, while relatively better than coach, are still far worse than being home—a perspective that should temper class resentment between travelers. Beyond practical advice (pack peanut butter crackers, bring less stuff), she explores the psychological toll of constant travel: the shallow interactions, existential angst, and righteous anger that accumulate. Her core prescription is to remain 'open and connected' with strangers, treating each interaction as potentially meaningful, while also working on one's own sense of worthiness.
key_points: Business class should be compared to being home, not to coach—it's still worse than not traveling at all
Airport lounges deliberately prevent you from taking portable snacks, making peanut butter crackers essential packing
Constant travel creates 'righteous anger' and existential angst through endless shallow interactions
The key to surviving overtravel is remaining 'open and connected'—assuming each person you meet might expose something unexpectedly meaningful
Travel's hidden gift is forcing you to confront the hardest existential work: maintaining your own sense of worthiness
money_quotes: Long story short, I travel way. too. fucking. much.
If I wanted to hide an alcoholism problem, then business traveling would be The. Way. To. Do. It.
You'll never regret having less to carry. Unless you forget peanut butter crackers.
Traveling means having multiple shallow interactions with multiple people on a daily basis. It's enough to make you think there's no such thing as love, or meaningful connection.

Assume that the person you're about to meet is interesting, deep, thoughtful, and is about to expose something unexpectedly important and meaningful to you. But also, don't hold it against them at all if they don't.
Being nice is an invitation to conversation as well as a signal that you won't judge, which is even more important.
Interactions between humans, even when they're meaningful and kind, cannot heal one's wounds, and that it's only a person's own sense of worthiness that can ever do that.
Which is really all you can ask about something like travel, that it lets you work on the hardest thing of all, because honestly who has time in their normal lives to work on the hard stuff?
posts.key_insight: The real challenge of constant travel isn't logistics but maintaining genuine human connection and personal worthiness amid endless shallow interactions and displacement from home.

posts.summary: This guest post by Cathy O'Neil's daughter Aise details her voting choices in the 2018 New York elections with extensive reasoning. She voted for NYC ballot proposal #1 (campaign finance reform) but against #2 and #3, which she saw as creating unnecessary bureaucracy and mandating demographic discrimination. For state offices, she supported Green Party candidates for Governor, Attorney General, and Comptroller, prioritizing anti-corruption stances and progressive policies over Democratic establishment candidates she views as compromised by their ties to Governor Cuomo.
key_points: Campaign finance reform that amplifies small donations and reduces contribution limits is a positive step toward reducing the influence of moneyed interests in politics
Creating new government commissions with vague mandates and mayoral appointment power risks becoming vehicles for political patronage rather than democratic participation
Diversity requirements for political appointments constitute objectionable demographic discrimination that prioritizes identity over competence
Third-party voting (Green Party) is justified when major party candidates are compromised by corruption ties or insufficient on key issues like criminal justice reform
The Attorney General should actively challenge Wall Street and government corruption, not worry about being perceived as too aggressive
money_quotes: It is unseemly for candidates to be calling people and asking for thousand dollar sums of money.
When campaigns have the resources to maximize exposure, things become less about getting noticed and getting the money to be noticed and more about having the right ideas.
There's a very good chance that the committee does nothing of note: In which case it is unnecessary.
I want the borough president to appoint people who can get the president's policies implemented as competently as possible.
I want the Attorney General to be the sheriff of Wall Street.
Insofar as doing one's job leads to immoral outcomes, one should not do one's job.
Quite frankly I don't trust a major party candidate to be good on anti-corruption issues.
posts.key_insight: Voting decisions should prioritize anti-corruption credentials and policy substance over party loyalty, even when that means supporting third-party candidates unlikely to win.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief personal post where the author shares her discovery that karaoke is more enjoyable when performed in French, based on her experience during a trip to Paris. The post is playful and conversational rather than analytical, ending with a humorous challenge to readers to disagree with her assessment.
key_points: The author recently visited Paris
She participated in karaoke on her last night there
She found the French karaoke experience superior to other versions
money_quotes: I dare you to tell me I'm wrong.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes a blog post is just a delightful personal observation shared with readers, no deeper analysis required.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg Opinion column about international AI competition and surveillance. The column argues that China represents a cautionary tale for AI-enabled dystopia and that the U.S. should oppose surveillance state practices. The post itself contains minimal content beyond the link and headline.
key_points: China's AI development represents a warning about surveillance state futures
International competition for AI dominance is framed as a new Cold War
The U.S. has a responsibility to stand against surveillance state practices
money_quotes: Want To See Your Dystopian Future? Look at China
The U.S. needs to stand against the surveillance state.
posts.key_insight: The race for AI dominance between nations carries dystopian risks, with China's surveillance state offering a preview of what unchecked AI deployment looks like.

posts.summary: O'Neil reports from the 2019 Joint Mathematics Meeting where she received the MAA Euler Book Prize and reconnected with the math community. She reflects on the state of academic mathematics, noting she's less worried about MOOCs disrupting teaching than she once was, but remains concerned about the adjunctification of faculty positions. She also critiques the slow pace of change in mathematical publishing and credentialing systems, wondering when collaborative projects like the Stacks Project will become the norm.
key_points: The MOOC threat to traditional math teaching has not materialized as feared
The replacement of tenured faculty with adjunct staff continues to erode quality of life for math PhDs
Mathematical publishing and credentialing systems remain stuck in metric-gaming patterns
Collaborative open projects like the Stacks Project represent an obvious but unrealized future for mathematical contributions
The math community remains warm and welcoming despite institutional challenges
money_quotes: I registered for this year's Joint Math Meeting by claiming to be Press so I think it's only fair that I blog from the conference.
Math nerds are and always will be unbelievably adorable.

I was quite worried, for many years, that MOOCs and other 'flipped classroom' type scenarios would take over calculus teaching. I'm no longer so worried about that, because I simply haven't heard of it working on a broad scale.
The profession of academic mathematics is not a growing or improving field in terms of quality of life for the median Ph.D. grad.
It seems like there's just as much gaming, counting, and other kind of dumb metric stuff going on as ever.

I'm wondering when people will start seriously contributing to things like the Stack Project – and figure out a way of giving credit to people for those contributions – because it seems like the obvious future of mathematical contributions.
posts.key_insight: While the MOOC threat to math education has faded, academic mathematics still faces structural problems in how it treats faculty and credentials contributions.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg Opinion piece about facial recognition technology. The linked article argues that Amazon cannot adequately fix the problems with facial recognition because companies lack proper incentives to address the technology's 'creepiness' and potential for misuse. O'Neil suggests that market forces alone are insufficient to curb the harms of facial recognition systems.
key_points: Companies like Amazon lack incentives to fix facial recognition problems
Market forces are insufficient to address facial recognition's potential for harm
The 'creepiness' of facial recognition technology is a feature companies won't self-correct
money_quotes: Amazon Can't Fix Facial Recognition
Companies lack incentives to stop the creepiness
posts.key_insight: Corporate self-regulation of facial recognition technology will fail because companies have no financial incentive to limit a profitable but invasive technology.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares her essay 'A History of Cyborg Sex, 2018-73' published in Boston Review. The post itself contains no substantive argument, serving only as a pointer to the external publication. O'Neil expresses pride in the piece, which appears to explore the future history of human-robot sexual relationships.
key_points: O'Neil published a speculative essay about sex robots in Boston Review
The essay takes a future-historical perspective spanning 2018-2073
The post demonstrates her engagement with technology ethics beyond pure algorithms
money_quotes: Guys I'm super proud of this Sex Robots essay I wrote for Boston Review
posts.key_insight: This announcement post points to O'Neil's speculative fiction work on human-robot intimacy, showing her range beyond technical algorithm critique.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her second book 'Shame Machine: an owner's manual,' a follow-up to Weapons of Math Destruction with the same editor and publisher. The book will explore shame as a social mechanism—examining who profits from it, who gains power through it, and how it operates in the age of big data. She traces her interest to diverse experiences including insurance battles over bariatric surgery, Twitter interactions, and the value-added model used to score teachers. The book represents a more personal direction than her previous work, which is why she hasn't been testing ideas publicly on her blog. She's now transitioning from planning to active writing, with a tentative January 2021 release date.
key_points: The book examines shame as social infrastructure: who profits from it, who maintains power through it, and when it's valid versus cruel
O'Neil sees shame proliferating in both political strategies and interpersonal interactions, amplified by big data
Her interest stems from personal experiences (insurance/bariatric surgery) and observed injustices (teacher scoring with value-added models, Twitter pile-ons)
This project is more personal than Weapons of Math Destruction, leading her to develop ideas privately rather than on her blog
She frames shame as 'the informal glue that holds our system together'
money_quotes: When, why, and how do we shame each other? Who profits from shame? Who maintains power or gains power through shame?
When is shame valid, and when is it simply mean and cruel?
It seems like shame is the informal glue that holds our system together.
So naturally I started nerding out bigtime.
This time it's quite a bit more personal, so I've been hesitant to write about it openly while I was still thinking it through.
posts.key_insight: Shame functions as invisible social infrastructure—systematically deployed by those in power, amplified by big data, and worthy of the same rigorous analysis we apply to algorithms.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil explores why we need a concept of 'group shame' rather than treating shame as purely an individual experience. She argues that shame is inherently social—it only exists in relation to group norms—yet our language and frameworks focus almost exclusively on individual narratives. Using the #MeToo movement as an example of shifting norms creating new forms of group shame, she begins developing a taxonomy of shame types ('punching down, punching up, punching nowhere') and argues that understanding group shame dynamics is essential for predicting social outcomes and changing exploitative systems.
key_points: Shame cannot exist outside a group context because it's always experienced relative to a shared norm
Individual experiences of shame are variations on a larger theme—understanding the norm requires understanding the collective experience
The #MeToo movement illustrates how shifting norms create new forms of group shame, with reactions ranging from deep shame to defensive outrage to activism
'Punching down' shame (bullying shame) statistically works well at maintaining power structures and profits, which is why exploitative industries persist
Changing shameful dynamics requires changing the underlying norm itself, but this must be understood as individuals in a group experiencing changes collectively
money_quotes: Shame is social. There's no such thing as shame outside the group experience.
It's fair to say that the men who should be feeling the most amount of shame probably aren't the ones who are.
Maybe the most important thing about group shame is that, statistically at least, it works really well.
Fat people keep feeling shamed about their bodies and they keep diligently signing up to pay for a solution that won't work. Assault victims know they will not be heard.
The individual experience of shame, for each person in a group that shares such a norm, is just a variation on a larger theme.
What would need to change to make the weight loss industry unprofitable? For the military to actually address the problem of rape and sexual assault?
posts.key_insight: Shame operates as a group phenomenon with statistically predictable outcomes, and understanding its collective dynamics—rather than treating it as purely individual—is essential for dismantling exploitative systems that profit from or maintain power through shame.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that shame functions as an overlooked but powerful form of social control that explains many societal patterns we take for granted. She uses Japanese hikikomori as a case study, noting how shame keeps them isolated and their families silent, while also leading to their criminalization. She contrasts this with how we'd respond to a physical health crisis affecting the same number of people. The post also examines how shame silenced sexual assault victims in the military, with the system itself perpetuating trauma. O'Neil concludes by noting that shame is difficult to measure because it's informal, operates through silence rather than action, and comes with a built-in amnesia that makes us avoid examining it.
key_points: Shame is a form of soft, informal power that is omnipresent yet overlooked because we're so familiar with it
Shamed groups like hikikomori face a double burden: they're blamed for being a burden on society while also being criminalized despite not being violent as a group
If a physical condition caused the same widespread dysfunction as hikikomori experience, we'd treat it as a national health crisis rather than blaming victims
Shame's power is uniquely coercive because it forces silence and inaction rather than compelling visible behavior
Shame is nearly impossible to measure because it's informal, triggers avoidance, and manifests as absence rather than presence
money_quotes: Shame is power. It's a specific kind of informal, soft power, that I believe is overlooked but is incredibly influential.
It's the nature of a stigmatized and shamed group to be also criminalized.
That is power. The system itself perpetrated shame on the victims of sexual assault, keeping them in line and silent about their suffering and trauma.
Shame has an amazing power of coercive silence, and it works systematically in certain specific ways on certain specific people.
Instead, shame often forces us *not* to do something we otherwise *would* do.

We would complain, cry out that we are suffering or traumatized, but instead we are shamed into thinking it's our fault that we are suffering, and we should constrain ourselves by any means necessary, and stay silent.
How do you measure what's not happening because of shame?
posts.key_insight: Shame operates as invisible power precisely because it enforces silence and inaction, making its effects nearly impossible to measure while systematically controlling marginalized groups.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil examines shame as a social mechanism that operates in tension with free market ideology. She argues that shame traditionally functioned to get individuals to act against narrow self-interest for collective benefit, but our market-obsessed culture has created new scoring systems (dating apps, college rankings, social media metrics) that generate novel forms of shame. The piece uses the PUA/incel phenomenon as an example of how market logic applied to human relationships produces toxic outcomes. O'Neil suggests we've externalized and privatized traditional shaming mechanisms through technology and scoring systems, creating an overwhelming proliferation of ways to feel inadequate.
key_points: Shame traditionally serves a prosocial function—getting individuals to sacrifice self-interest for group benefit—but this only works when norms are reasonable and achievable
Market ideology and scoring systems have created new domains of shame (dating apps, college rankings, social metrics) that quantify human worth in reductive ways
The incel/PUA phenomenon exemplifies how market logic applied to dating produces dehumanizing frameworks and desperate attempts to conform
Understanding the flaws in scoring systems helps distance oneself from the shame they produce, but most people maintain 'blind faith' in these metrics
We've effectively externalized and privatized shame mechanisms through technology, replacing old notions of worth as family members and citizens with numerical scores
money_quotes: Shame often works really well to get individuals to act against their self interest in relatively small ways so that the group as a whole works more smoothly and is better off, at least ideally.

My theory is that, in part because of our weirdly religious belief in free markets, coupled with the explicitly market-flavored nature of dating apps and other technological intermediations on the sex and dating fronts, we end up with bizarre pseudo-scientific theories of attractions and attractiveness that purport to 'explain' everything about sex and dating but are fundamentally efforts to blunt the power of shame.
People really do care about rankings and scores, far beyond what I'd consider reasonable.

When you understand the flaws in scoring systems, you lose admiration for the system itself, which on the one hand helps you distance yourself from your score and any associated shame, and on the other hand allows you to see just how much blind faith and undue grief we as a society project onto such systems.
There's been a massive and largely undescribed conflict between the two systems of powers represented by the informal social mechanism of shame and more formal market mechanisms.

You could almost say that we've come to replace some of our old-fashioned notion of self-worth as family members, as citizens, and definitely as consumers by the scores that we've achieved. In that sense we've externalized and even privatized the dominant shaming mechanisms.
posts.key_insight: The proliferation of scoring systems and market logic into every domain of life has transformed shame from an informal social mechanism for collective benefit into a privatized, externalized system of quantified inadequacy.

posts.summary: This brief post announces a new Bloomberg Opinion column about facial recognition technology being used by Chinese insurance companies. O'Neil was motivated to write after becoming alarmed by developments in China's use of AI for health insurance decisions. The post serves primarily as a pointer to her longer Bloomberg piece, which examines how Chinese insurers are using facial recognition as a cautionary example for American readers. She frames the Chinese case as a preview of what could happen to health insurance in the U.S.
key_points: Chinese insurers are deploying facial recognition technology in ways that affect health insurance access
O'Neil views these developments as alarming enough to break from a writing hiatus
The Chinese case serves as a warning for potential similar developments in U.S. health insurance
AI and facial recognition are being weaponized against consumers in insurance contexts
money_quotes: I got so riled up about some incredibly terrible facial recognition stuff going on in China that I wrote a Bloomberg piece
China Knows How to Take Away Your Health Insurance
A big Chinese insurer's facial-recognition software offers a cautionary tale for the U.S.
posts.key_insight: China's deployment of facial recognition in health insurance decisions previews a dystopian future that Americans should actively work to prevent.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil recounts her week as a 'Theme Speaker' at Star Island, a Unitarian-Universalist retreat off the coast of New Hampshire. She spent six days presenting her forthcoming book on shame to an enthusiastic audience, using Occupy-style progressive stack discussion. The experience, which she humorously describes as 'cult-like' with its communal singing and rituals, proved invaluable for testing her book's narrative. By the end, both she and her son were completely won over by the community's warmth and traditions.
key_points: Star Island is a century-old UU/UCC retreat that operates week-long programs with strong community rituals
O'Neil presented six days of talks covering shame across domains: fat shaming, poverty shaming, social media, and AI-automated shame
The progressive stack discussion format allowed for meaningful audience participation and feedback
The experience validated her book's narrative structure through engaged audience response
Despite joking about 'cult' dynamics, she found the community's traditions genuinely meaningful
money_quotes: I want you to imagine burning man except for New England WASP-y families of four with lots of knitting and choral singing experience.
I was often to be found sitting on the porch knitting in a rocking chair.
The audience never diminished, and I think it might have actually gotten bigger by the end of the week!
We all danced around the island holding hands and singing about promises to return next year.
By the next day I was no longer worried, because he absolutely had and we *will* be returning next year.
posts.key_insight: Testing ideas with an engaged, patient community can validate a book's narrative and build author confidence in ways solitary writing cannot.

posts.summary: In this eulogy delivered at her father's memorial, Cathy O'Neil reflects on the complicated legacy of her difficult father through ten 'silver linings.' Rather than offering sanitized praise, she honestly catalogues his flaws—intellectual arrogance, sexism, eugenic beliefs, cruelty—while crediting him with inadvertently teaching her valuable lessons. His intimidating nature prepared her to face powerful men like Larry Summers; his wrongheadedness sharpened her critical thinking skills; his cruelty taught her to be a kinder parent. The piece is a masterclass in honoring the dead without dishonoring the truth.
key_points: Growing up with a difficult father prepared her to never be intimidated by powerful, arrogant men in professional settings
His refusal to follow social norms taught her to question rules rather than blindly follow them—useful for a female intellectual
His wrongheaded beliefs (sexism, eugenics, climate denial) forced her to develop strong critical thinking and debate skills
His sporadic cruelty taught her the value of consistent kindness as a parent—learning what not to do
Despite his flaws, his genuine generosity with hospitality and his love of ideas left positive marks on her character
money_quotes: Larry Summers was known as a pushy, physically imposing and intellectually arrogant bully. He was all those things. He was also a pussycat compared to my dad.
Most norms or even laws can be interpreted as rules of thumb to be considered and held up to inspection rather than thoughtlessly followed.
He turned me into an intellectual critic, which has made me a ton of money over the years. So thanks dad.
I want to thank my dad for helping me understand our current president at a deep level.
My father taught me the value of consistent kindness by displaying the wreckage of sporadic cruelty.
Just as his brutality was never subtle, his generosity was never fake.
He was such an asshole he wouldn't even mind being called an asshole.
posts.key_insight: The most difficult people in our lives can become our greatest teachers—not through their wisdom, but through the critical thinking and resilience we develop in response to their failures.

posts.summary: The post highlights an overlooked hygiene gap during the COVID-19 pandemic: while people focus on handwashing, they ignore that their constantly-touched phones accumulate and redistribute germs. O'Neil walks through a typical daily scenario showing how washing hands becomes futile when we immediately pick up a dirty phone. She notes the irony of discussing pandemic hygiene while touching her face and phone, and acknowledges that solving this problem will require new cleaning procedures that most people haven't considered.
key_points: Handwashing is undermined when we immediately touch contaminated phones afterward
Phones are constantly touched throughout the day, accumulating germs from every surface we contact
The irony of discussing hygiene practices while engaging in the very behaviors that spread germs
COVID-19 can survive on phone surfaces for extended periods, making this a real transmission risk
Developing effective phone-cleaning habits will require careful attention to avoid recontamination
money_quotes: I'm on the phone talking about this, while copiously touching my face and my dirty phone.
It occurred to me that washing our hands won't be all that helpful if our phones are dirty, which they of course will be because we cannot stop using our phones even for a minute.
Go on subway, touch things, read phone, get to destination, touch things, put down phone, go to the bathroom, wash hands carefully, pick up phone again.
The only germs we're not getting on our phone is actual bathroom germs, and that's only if you don't bring your phone with you to the bathroom, which let's face it people do in general.
I'm dizzy with the procedure we will have to follow assuming we have wipes that actually clean our phones and our hands.
posts.key_insight: Our phones are the weak link in pandemic hygiene because they constantly touch our hands and faces, making handwashing alone insufficient.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that due to inadequate COVID-19 testing in the U.S., confirmed case counts are unreliable indicators of the pandemic's severity. She proposes using hospital bed utilization as a better proxy for tracking the outbreak's true scale. While acknowledging this metric has limitations—including lag time and confounding factors like postponed elective surgeries—she argues it's more reliable because hospitals track beds accurately and the data is less susceptible to political manipulation. She calls for journalists to collect and compare hospital bed data across national chains.
key_points: Confirmed COVID-19 case counts are unreliable due to severely inadequate testing rates in the U.S.
Hospital bed utilization provides a more politically-independent metric since hospitals reliably track this data
The death count is likely undercounted because many coronavirus deaths went untested
This proxy still underestimates the problem due to postponed elective surgeries and reduced transmission of other illnesses from social distancing
Data transparency websites should add 'tests administered' columns to contextualize their numbers
money_quotes: For terrible political and cultural and of course capitalistic reasons, we are not getting tested at reasonable rates.
We are not actually seeing enough to know how bad things really are.
Considering that we don't know who is infected, we should use as a proxy of our problem not the 'confirmed case' count but rather the 'hospital beds used above normal' count.
Counting beds is relatively free of political manipulation.
Our death count is most assuredly way too low because there have likely been plenty of people dying of coronavirus who just never got tested.
posts.key_insight: When official metrics are corrupted by political and systemic failures, we should seek alternative proxies that are harder to manipulate and closer to ground truth.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that official COVID-19 statistics are systematically undercounting both cases and deaths due to inadequate testing and inconsistent reporting. She estimates actual US cases are roughly 10x the confirmed count, based on the logic that only the sickest 10% get tested. She notes that death counts are also unreliable, with nursing home deaths excluded in some countries and China's numbers particularly suspect. The post reflects the frustration of a data scientist who fundamentally distrusts the available data while trying to reason through what the true numbers might be.
key_points: New York's testing requirement of near-hospitalization means confirmed cases represent only about 10% of actual infections
Multiplying confirmed US cases by 10 suggests 1.43 million actual cases versus the official 143K
Death counts are underestimated because they exclude nursing home deaths in countries like Spain and Italy
China's case and death numbers are particularly untrustworthy, which matters because we need to understand their post-lockdown trajectory
Being a skeptical data scientist means not trusting official data and using real-world knowledge to estimate reality
money_quotes: Long story short, being a skeptical data scientist means not trusting the data whatsoever.
The best we can do is use the data and our real world knowledge to ballpark what might actually be happening. We will never know the true numbers.
All the above are ballpark reckoning, but honestly I trust my numbers more than any official ones.
We've been hearing stories told in Spain and Italy that their death counts are not including horrible fucking things that have been happening in nursing homes.
And if we don't trust their death counts, we should also not count their confirmed case count.
I could be wrong about that too.
posts.key_insight: Official pandemic statistics are so compromised by testing limitations and political incentives that back-of-envelope estimates based on hospitalization rates may be more accurate than reported numbers.

posts.summary: This post is a practical DIY tutorial for making cloth face masks during the early COVID-19 pandemic. O'Neil provides a step-by-step sewing guide using dishcloths, wire for nose fitting, and elastic cord. She references an existing pattern but aims to simplify the instructions for accessibility. The post includes material recommendations with links, measurements, and photos of each step. It's a departure from her usual algorithmic criticism, reflecting the urgent community response to PPE shortages in spring 2020.
key_points: Thicker cotton materials like dishcloths provide better filtration for DIY masks
A cardboard template allows for efficient batch production of multiple masks
The design includes a wire channel for nose fitting to improve seal
The construction uses an inside-out turning technique common in sewn items
Elastic cord threaded through flaps creates adjustable ear loops
money_quotes: But to be honest I found it hard to follow which is why I'm going to tell you quite plainly how to do this relatively quickly.
This has been used a bunch and is kind of a mess. That's ok.
I got 12 cardboard outlines on! That means 3 masks.
posts.key_insight: During a crisis, even data scientists turn to direct action—sometimes the most useful thing you can do is make masks and share clear instructions.

posts.summary: Gary Cornell, a mathematician with Type 2 diabetes, responds to Trump's promotion of hydroxychloroquine as a COVID-19 treatment by highlighting a Johns Hopkins study showing dangerous interactions with metformin—a drug taken by over 150 million people worldwide. He argues that Trump's cavalier 'what do you have to lose' rhetoric ignores serious risks for diabetics and others on common medications. The post emphasizes that hydroxychloroquine has 332 known drug interactions, 59 of them major, making blanket recommendations potentially dangerous. Cornell concludes that promoting this drug without proper safety data, especially for vulnerable populations, borders on malpractice or worse.
key_points: A Johns Hopkins study found concerning interactions between hydroxychloroquine and metformin, a drug used by 150+ million diabetics globally
Trump's 'what do you have to lose' framing dangerously ignores drug interactions and side effects that could harm or kill patients
Hydroxychloroquine has 332 documented drug interactions, with 59 classified as major
Mouse study results aren't conclusive but suggest serious enough risks to warrant caution
Promoting untested drug combinations without safety data is potentially criminal when done by politicians
money_quotes: I'm not a doctor nor do I play one at daily press briefings.
Who the &^%$ knows if taking this will kill me?

Absent proof it is safe for people taking metformin, pushing it if you are taking metformin except in life or death situations would seem to me malpractice for a doctor and practically criminal for an economist or politician.
So, answer is, you have a lot to lose.
People there are not general considered practitioners of psycho ceramics, in other words.
posts.key_insight: Politicians promoting drugs with 'what do you have to lose' rhetoric ignore that millions of people on common medications like metformin could lose their lives due to dangerous drug interactions.

posts.summary: This brief post announces a new Bloomberg column examining the reliability of Covid-19 data. O'Neil argues there are at least ten reasons to question the accuracy of the pandemic statistics being reported daily. She notes that delayed data from Easter weekend will likely compound existing data quality issues. The post suggests that the true impact of the pandemic may never be accurately measured due to systemic data collection problems.
key_points: Covid-19 data reporting has significant reliability issues that warrant skepticism
Holiday weekends like Easter create additional delays and distortions in the data
The pandemic's actual toll may remain permanently unknowable due to data limitations
money_quotes: The pandemic's true toll might never be known.
posts.key_insight: The daily Covid-19 statistics we rely on for understanding the pandemic are fundamentally unreliable, and we may never know the true scope of its impact.

posts.summary: This brief post links to Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg column arguing that COVID-19 contact tracing apps will be ineffective. The core critique is that such technological solutions fail to protect the most vulnerable populations. O'Neil positions this as part of her broader skeptical analysis of tech solutionism during the pandemic.
key_points: COVID-19 tracking apps are presented as ineffective public health tools
The technology fails to address the needs of those most at risk from the virus
This reflects O'Neil's consistent skepticism of algorithmic solutions to social problems
money_quotes: The Covid-19 Tracking App Won't Work
It does nothing for the people who are most at risk
posts.key_insight: Contact tracing technology, like many algorithmic solutions, fails precisely where it matters most—protecting vulnerable populations who often lack access to the technology itself.

posts.summary: This brief post links to O'Neil's Bloomberg column critiquing how the actual COVID-19 curve differed from the idealized 'flattened curve' models promised to the public. She frames it as an observation about the gap between models and reality, with a stark warning that deaths would continue longer than anticipated. The post serves primarily as a pointer to her longer analysis published at Bloomberg.
key_points: Models promised a flattened curve that reality did not deliver
The duration of COVID-19 deaths would extend far beyond initial projections
Mathematical models can create false expectations when communicated to the public
money_quotes: This is Not The Flattened Curve We Were Promised
An empirical observation about models versus reality
The dying won't be over anytime soon
posts.key_insight: The idealized 'flatten the curve' models gave the public false hope about the trajectory and duration of COVID-19 deaths.

posts.summary: This brief blog post announces a new Bloomberg column written in collaboration with ageism activist Ashton Applewhite about the dangers facing older people during the COVID-19 pandemic. The column argues that using biased pandemic data to allocate resources could perpetuate high mortality rates among the elderly. O'Neil connects her expertise in algorithmic bias with Applewhite's advocacy work to highlight how data-driven decisions during crises can discriminate against vulnerable populations.
key_points: Pandemic data used for resource allocation may contain biases that harm older people
Collaboration between data science and activism perspectives can illuminate overlooked harms
Crisis-time algorithmic decisions require scrutiny for age-based discrimination
money_quotes: Using biased data to allocate resources could perpetuate a high mortality rate.
posts.key_insight: Data-driven pandemic responses risk encoding ageism into life-or-death resource allocation decisions.

posts.summary: This is a brief link-sharing post where Cathy O'Neil promotes two of her recent Bloomberg opinion pieces. The first draws an analogy between Trump's behavior and a malfunctioning machine learning algorithm that's 'running low on data.' The second addresses COVID-19 testing requirements for ending social distancing, arguing that test quality matters more than quantity. The post serves primarily as a pointer to her longer-form journalism rather than containing substantive analysis itself.
key_points: O'Neil uses machine learning concepts as a lens for analyzing political behavior
She connects algorithmic thinking to pandemic policy decisions around testing
The post demonstrates her practice of cross-posting between her blog and mainstream media outlets
money_quotes: Trump Is a Machine-Learning Algorithm Gone Wrong
His bizarre behavior suggests he's running low on data
More coronavirus tests won't be enough to stop social distancing. Better tests might be.
posts.key_insight: O'Neil consistently applies algorithmic and data-science frameworks to explain phenomena outside traditional tech contexts, from political behavior to public health policy.

posts.summary: This brief blog post serves as a link roundup sharing two of Cathy O'Neil's Bloomberg opinion columns. The first column argues that pandemic-driven automation could present an opportunity for workers to negotiate better terms with their 'robot overlords.' The second is a personal reflection mourning the impact of COVID-19 on New York City's subway system. The post itself contains no substantive analysis, functioning purely as a pointer to her longer-form work elsewhere.
key_points: The pandemic is accelerating workplace automation trends
O'Neil sees potential for workers to negotiate amid automation disruption
COVID-19 is fundamentally changing urban infrastructure like public transit
The NYC subway represents something worth mourning as a civic institution
money_quotes: The pandemic will accelerate automation. This might be an opportunity for workers.
The pandemic is dealing one of the city's greatest features a brutal blow.
posts.key_insight: Major disruptions like pandemics accelerate automation but may also create windows for workers to reshape the terms of that transition.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil identifies the new FEMA model used during the COVID-19 pandemic as a 'Weapon of Math Destruction' (WMD). She argues that this algorithm, which was reportedly used to pressure Arizona's governor into reopening early, meets her three criteria for a WMD: it's important (affects life-and-death decisions), secret (not open to public scrutiny), and destructive (potentially causing harm). The post links to her Bloomberg column calling for public transparency in reopening decisions.
key_points: The FEMA model used to guide state reopening decisions qualifies as a 'Weapon of Math Destruction'
The algorithm was allegedly used to pressure Arizona's governor into reopening early
WMDs are defined by three characteristics: important, secret, and destructive
Decisions affecting public health should involve public data and open debate rather than opaque algorithms
money_quotes: The new FEMA model – which was used to strong-arm the Arizona governor into opening early – is a WMD, i.e. important, secret, and destructive
A Secret Algorithm Is Deciding Who Will Die in America
Decisions on reopening should involve public data and debate
posts.key_insight: Government algorithms that make life-and-death decisions without public transparency are dangerous weapons that undermine democratic accountability.

posts.summary: This is a brief pointer post directing readers to a Bloomberg column O'Neil wrote about COVID-19 epidemiological models. The column, informed by mathematician Jarod Alper's YouTube talk, examines how various COVID models work and their limitations. O'Neil signals her critical stance through the subtitle indicating that while no models are perfect, some fail more fundamentally than others.
key_points: O'Neil wrote a Bloomberg column analyzing COVID-19 prediction models
The analysis draws on mathematician Jarod Alper's explanatory YouTube talk
The framing suggests a spectrum of model quality, from imperfect to fundamentally flawed
money_quotes: None are perfect; some are downright dumb.
posts.key_insight: COVID-19 models vary dramatically in quality, and understanding their limitations is essential for interpreting pandemic predictions.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the reproduction rate R(t) is a more reliable metric for tracking Covid-19 than raw case counts or model predictions. She explains that R(t) can be estimated from multiple data sources (hospitalizations, cases, deaths) and is robust to testing biases since it only measures trends. However, she warns that deliberate political manipulation of data by states eager to reopen undermines even this approach. The piece advocates for following this single hyperparameter rather than getting lost in complex model outputs.
key_points: R(t) can be triangulated from multiple data sources, creating a more trustworthy composite picture than any single metric
The reproduction rate is insulated from testing bias because it tracks trends rather than absolute numbers
Several states (Texas, Florida, Georgia) have manipulated Covid data to justify reopening prematurely
Following a hyperparameter like R(t) is more practical than trusting complex model predictions
money_quotes: R(t), which is a hyperparameter in most Covid-19 models, is a much better and more trustworthy figure to follow than any other particular data set.
We can piece together a trustworthy patchwork quilt of R(t)'s that might be more trustworthy than any particular version.
R(t) is insulated from the bias we know exists in these figures (due mostly to not enough tests) and only cares about trends, so as long as the bias is consistent we don't care about it.
We've seen many states performing Covid-19 data manipulation (Texas, Florida, and Georgia for example) in order to open up sooner than they honestly should.
Basically, they're juicing the numbers. That's a kind of political bias we cannot overcome easily.
posts.key_insight: When data quality is suspect, tracking the trend-based reproduction rate R(t) across multiple sources is more reliable than trusting any single dataset or complex model.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post linking to O'Neil's Bloomberg column about the standoff between colleges and students/parents over tuition during COVID-19. The core argument is that colleges charging full tuition for online education creates a game of chicken scenario. O'Neil suggests that online education should be priced accordingly, and that the pandemic will force colleges to justify their value proposition.
key_points: COVID-19 has created a confrontation between colleges and families over the value of education
Online education should come at a reduced 'online price' rather than full tuition
The pandemic will force colleges to prove their worth to students and parents
There's an implicit standoff where neither side wants to blink first on pricing
money_quotes: Covid-19 Will Make Colleges Prove Their Worth
Online education should come at an online price
posts.key_insight: The pandemic has turned college tuition into a high-stakes negotiation where institutions must finally justify whether their premium pricing delivers premium value.

posts.summary: This brief post promotes O'Neil's Bloomberg column arguing that mass incarceration, inequality, and racism are root causes of pandemic vulnerability. Drawing on research from Measures for Justice, she contends that these interconnected social ills weakened community immune systems and made both the U.S. and the world more susceptible to COVID-19. The post serves as a gateway to her fuller argument that structural racism isn't just a social justice issue but a public health catastrophe.
key_points: Mass incarceration weakens community immune systems, increasing pandemic vulnerability
Racism, inequality, and incarceration are interconnected causes of the COVID-19 crisis
The research from Measures for Justice provides empirical backing for these claims
These factors affect pandemic outcomes both domestically and globally
money_quotes: Mass incarceration, inequality, and racism cause epidemics both here and worldwide
Mass incarceration and other social ills made the world more vulnerable
posts.key_insight: Structural racism and mass incarceration aren't just social justice issues—they're public health infrastructure failures that amplify pandemics.

posts.summary: This brief post links to O'Neil's Bloomberg column criticizing Sheryl Sandberg's leadership at Facebook during a crisis period. The post's title suggests that Sandberg's famous 'Lean In' philosophy of ambitious career advancement may be inappropriate when the company faces serious ethical problems. O'Neil implies that Facebook needs moral leadership rather than corporate ladder-climbing mentality. The timing (June 2020) places this during controversies around Facebook's handling of political content and misinformation.
key_points: Sandberg's 'Lean In' career philosophy may be misguided during Facebook's ethical crises
Facebook lacks adequate moral leadership
Sometimes stepping back or leaving is more appropriate than pushing forward
Corporate ambition can conflict with ethical responsibility
money_quotes: Sheryl, honey, if this is you leaning in, please lean out
Facebook needs better moral leadership
posts.key_insight: The aggressive career ambition celebrated in 'Lean In' becomes a liability when it keeps leaders clinging to positions at ethically compromised companies rather than taking a moral stand.

posts.summary: This brief post introduces O'Neil's Bloomberg column about missing data in police misconduct records. She frames the absence of police accountability data as a critical problem, describing missing data as 'the darkest, scariest kind of data.' The post advocates for filling these informational gaps as essential to police reform efforts.
key_points: Missing data represents a dangerous blind spot in understanding police misconduct
Recent efforts are beginning to expose previously hidden police behavior data
Systemic reform requires comprehensive, transparent data collection
money_quotes: the darkest, scariest kind of data, namely missing data
We are getting some of those holes filled in when it comes to police misconduct, and we need more
posts.key_insight: The deliberate absence of police misconduct data is itself a form of institutional protection that enables continued abuse.

posts.summary: This is a brief post linking to a Bloomberg column about facial recognition technology. O'Neil argues that corporate moratoriums on facial recognition, like Amazon's, are insufficient and that meaningful regulation requires Congressional action. The post serves as a pointer to her fuller argument published elsewhere.
key_points: Corporate self-regulation on facial recognition is inadequate
Congressional legislation is necessary to address facial recognition concerns
Amazon's voluntary pause on selling facial recognition to police doesn't solve the underlying problem
money_quotes: That would take an act of Congress.
posts.key_insight: Voluntary corporate moratoriums on facial recognition technology are meaningless without binding Congressional legislation.

posts.summary: This brief post promotes O'Neil's Bloomberg column arguing that existing crime risk score data can be repurposed to guide police defunding efforts. She suggests that the same algorithmic tools used for predictive policing inadvertently reveal which social problems have been inappropriately delegated to law enforcement. The post positions this as making defunding practically straightforward since the analytical groundwork already exists.
key_points: Crime risk scores, originally designed for predictive policing, actually predict police presence rather than crime itself
This existing data can be repurposed to identify where police are handling problems better suited to other social services
The analytical infrastructure for defunding already exists - it just needs to be reframed
money_quotes: Crime-risk scores predict police, and not in a good way
Crime-risk scores reveal the problems that society has shunted onto law enforcement
posts.key_insight: The same predictive policing algorithms that critics have long attacked for bias can now serve as a roadmap for identifying which police functions should be transferred to social services.

posts.summary: O'Neil briefly updates readers on her move to Somerville, MA after 15 years, confessing to hoarding tendencies revealed by the process. The main substance is a pointer to her Bloomberg column introducing 'quantified dread' - a concept describing situations where things are getting worse and the rate of deterioration is accelerating. She applies this framework to America's COVID-19 response, arguing the country is dangerously complacent.
key_points: Moving after 15 years revealed hidden hoarding tendencies in clothing, shoes, and yarn
Introduces 'quantified dread' as a mathematical concept: when things worsen and the rate of worsening accelerates
Argues America's calm response to COVID-19 is dangerously misplaced
Frames unwarranted optimism as potentially an 'existential threat'
money_quotes: I discovered I am a major hoarder in the categories of clothing, shoes, and yarn. This is something that is easy to deny when you don't have to empty out large closets but is impossible to deny when you do.
Quantified dread, which loosely speaking when things are getting worse and the rate at which they're getting worse is getting worse
This is a case where optimism may be an existential threat.
posts.key_insight: When both a trend and its acceleration are negative - quantified dread - optimism becomes dangerous rather than helpful.

posts.summary: O'Neil expresses deep concern about Florida's COVID-19 trajectory in July 2020, predicting daily death counts would soon reach 600. Drawing on both personal connection to Florida and data analysis, she argues the state is particularly vulnerable to the pandemic—in some ways more so than Manhattan. The post serves as a teaser for her Bloomberg column where she explains her methodology for this grim prediction.
key_points: Florida's vulnerability to COVID-19 may exceed Manhattan's despite different demographics
Data analysis suggests daily death counts could reach 600 in the coming weeks
Personal connection to Florida through family history motivates her concern
The prediction is based on 'data and some reckoning'—quantitative analysis combined with interpretation
money_quotes: It's a really vulnerable place, in some ways more so than Manhattan.
According to the data, and some reckoning, I figure the daily death counts will soon hit 600.
posts.key_insight: Data analysis in July 2020 suggested Florida's COVID death toll trajectory could rival or exceed New York's catastrophic early pandemic numbers.

posts.summary: The post introduces a Bloomberg column about the International Baccalaureate Organization's grading algorithm that has negatively impacted students like Hadrien, who aspires to study engineering. O'Neil argues the algorithm operates in a secret, powerful, and destructive manner, making it a classic example of a 'weapon of math destruction.' The piece serves as a cautionary tale about algorithmic grading systems deployed during the pandemic.
key_points: The IB's grading algorithm assigns grades in a secretive manner without transparency
The algorithm has real consequences for students' futures, including college admissions
Secret, powerful, and destructive algorithms qualify as 'weapons of math destruction'
Algorithmic grading systems deployed hastily during COVID-19 have caused significant harm
money_quotes: The International Baccalaureate Organization's new grading algorithm assigns grade in a secret, powerful, and destructive manner
This qualifies it as a 'weapons of math destruction'
posts.key_insight: When algorithms secretly determine students' grades and futures without transparency or accountability, they become weapons of math destruction that can derail lives.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reflects on her March 2020 prediction that the coronavirus pandemic would trigger a massive political uprising. While other 'thought leaders' predicted optimistic outcomes like reduced polarization and renewed faith in experts, O'Neil foresaw that the unequal response to the crisis would expose how wealthy communities would be protected while poor communities would be devastated. She argues that the pandemic revealed how massive political action is possible when causes are deemed urgent, making the chronic dismissal of marginalized populations' needs impossible to ignore. Writing in July 2020, she expresses sadness at being proven correct.
key_points: Other experts predicted optimistic post-pandemic outcomes like less polarization and renewed trust in science
The pandemic response would reveal a stark divide between how wealthy vs. poor communities were treated
Multitrillion-dollar bailouts proved that rapid, massive political action is possible when deemed urgent
The combination of long-ignored needs and visible proof of political capacity would radicalize marginalized populations
Being proven right about social upheaval brings no satisfaction
money_quotes: The aftermath of the coronavirus is likely to include a new political uprising—an Occupy Wall Street 2.0, but this time much more massive and angrier.

We will see the extent to which rich, well-connected and well-resourced communities will have been taken care of, while contingent, poor and stigmatized communities will have been thoroughly destroyed.
We will have seen how political action is possible—multitrillion dollar bailouts and projects can be mobilized quickly—but only if the cause is considered urgent.

This mismatch of long-disregarded populations finally getting the message that their needs are not only chronically unattended, but also chronically dismissed as politically required, will likely have drastic, pitchfork consequences.
It makes me sad to feel so right about this.
posts.key_insight: The pandemic exposed that governments can mobilize massive resources instantly when motivated, revealing the chronic dismissal of marginalized communities' needs as a political choice rather than an impossibility.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the inadequate federal response to COVID-19 means citizens must take matters into their own hands. She proposes crowdsourcing CO2 level data in indoor spaces like schools, airports, and workplaces to assess ventilation quality. The post serves as a brief introduction to her longer Bloomberg piece on the topic. She frames this as a practical, citizen-driven solution to a public health information gap.
key_points: The federal COVID response has been counterproductive, leaving citizens to fend for themselves
CO2 levels serve as a proxy for ventilation quality in indoor spaces
Crowdsourcing this data could help people make informed decisions about which spaces are safe
Schools, airports, and workplaces are priority locations for monitoring
money_quotes: Let's face it, the federal response to COVID has been counterproductive.
We're on our own.
We should crowdsource CO2 levels in places like schools, airports, and buildings where people work, so we know the ventilation is good.
posts.key_insight: When institutional responses fail, crowdsourced data collection can empower citizens to protect themselves.

posts.summary: O'Neil acknowledges her prediction of 600 daily COVID deaths in Florida was wrong—the actual figure is 184—but argues the discrepancy may be due to data manipulation rather than genuine success. She points to narrow definitions of COVID deaths, lagging reporting, and changing methodologies as reasons to doubt Florida's official numbers. The post links to her Bloomberg column exploring why Florida's death toll appears lower than New York's despite high case counts.
key_points: Her prediction of 600 daily deaths was off by a factor of three, with actual numbers at 184
Florida's data practices—narrow death definitions, reporting lags, methodology changes—raise credibility concerns
The apparent success in keeping deaths low may be an artifact of how deaths are counted rather than actual outcomes
She maintains epistemic humility while still questioning official narratives
money_quotes: I was wrong to predict 600 daily deaths by COVID in Florida by now; right now it's at 184.
But the fishy data coming out of Florida makes me think I'm not as wrong as I wish I were.
Narrow definitions of what counts as a COVID death, lagging data, and changing methodologies make me doubt the official numbers.
posts.key_insight: Being wrong about a prediction doesn't mean the underlying analysis was flawed when the data itself is unreliable.

posts.summary: O'Neil analyzes Trump's repeated claim that COVID-19 testing is the problem because it reveals too many cases. Rather than dismissing this as nonsense, she attempts to understand the mental model behind it. She concludes that Trump operates like an algorithm, treating metrics and data points as more real and important than the underlying reality they represent. This insight—that Trump prioritizes measurable outputs over human outcomes—explains his consistent focus on TV ratings and case counts over actual deaths.
key_points: Trump's testing complaint reveals a worldview where metrics matter more than the reality they measure
Taking Trump 'seriously' means modeling what beliefs would make his statements internally consistent
Trump treats case counts and TV ratings as more real than people dying—a pattern that's 'weird but consistently true'
The post frames Trump as operating like an algorithm: optimizing for data outputs rather than substantive outcomes
money_quotes: For him, metrics like case counts or TV ratings are somehow more real than people dying of coronavirus.
It's weird but consistently true, and I think we should understand it.
The president is focused on data, independent of substance.
posts.key_insight: Trump's fixation on reducing testing to lower case counts reveals he operates like an algorithm—optimizing metrics while being indifferent to the underlying human reality those metrics represent.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that the chaotic school reopening plans during COVID-19 are not accidental failures but deliberate political choices. Based on conversations with educators, she concludes that students and teachers are being treated as pawns in a political game. The post links to her full Bloomberg column exploring how jurisdictions from New York City to Florida are mishandling reopenings. She frames the disaster as intentional rather than incompetent.
key_points: School reopening plans are described as 'a total freaking disaster'
The failures are characterized as deliberate, not accidental
Students and teachers are being used as pawns in political games
The critique spans multiple jurisdictions from NYC to Florida
money_quotes: not only is the school reopening plan a total freaking disaster, but it's absolutely deliberately so
students and teachers are pawns in a political game
posts.key_insight: The dysfunction in school reopening plans reflects deliberate political choices rather than mere incompetence.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil shares updates on her recent work covering algorithmic harms across multiple platforms. She announces a Bloomberg column warning that UK algorithmic grading scandals are harbingers of worse to come, a New York Times book review on gender in mathematics, and her appearance in the Netflix documentary 'The Social Dilemma.' She also highlights related work by Meredith Broussard on algorithmic grading and Yael Eisenstein on Facebook's polarization business model.
key_points: UK algorithmic grading scandals (IB and A-levels) represent the beginning of a larger wave of harmful algorithms in education
O'Neil characterizes problematic algorithms as a 'mutant army' that will increasingly affect students
The post connects her ongoing work across multiple media platforms (Bloomberg, NYT, Netflix) to a consistent theme of algorithmic accountability
She amplifies allied voices working on similar issues of algorithmic harm and tech platform manipulation
money_quotes: the recent algorithmic grading scandals in the UK are just the beginning of an oncoming mutant army of crap algorithms
posts.key_insight: The UK algorithmic grading failures aren't isolated incidents but early warnings of a coming wave of poorly designed algorithms that will increasingly harm students and educational systems.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that TikTok's recommendation algorithm, like all recommendation engines, can be manipulated by whoever controls it to amplify or suppress any type of content. She points out that this capability to shape what users see is inherent to how recommendation systems work. Notably, she clarifies that the ability to manipulate these algorithms isn't inherently negative—it can be used for beneficial purposes as well as harmful ones.
key_points: Recommendation engine controllers have the power to emphasize or suppress any content they choose
TikTok's algorithm operates like other recommendation engines in this regard
