The ability to manipulate recommendation algorithms is actually a feature, not a bug—it can serve good purposes
The real question is who controls the algorithm and what their intentions are
money_quotes: whoever controls TikTok's algorithm can of course tamp down or emphasize whatever kind of content they want, misinformation or otherwise
being able to manipulate recommendation algorithms is in general a good thing!
it can be used for good or for evil
posts.key_insight: The power to manipulate recommendation algorithms is neither good nor bad—what matters is who wields that power and toward what ends.

posts.summary: O'Neil warns that the announcement of a safe and effective COVID-19 vaccine could paradoxically make the pandemic worse. Her argument centers on humanity's poor ability to anticipate feedback loops—people tend to overreact to good news by prematurely abandoning precautionary behaviors. She fears that vaccine announcements will trigger dramatic behavioral changes like discarding masks before the vaccine has actually been widely distributed and taken effect, undermining its potential benefits.
key_points: Humans are systematically bad at anticipating feedback loops, especially during the pandemic
Good news consistently triggers overreactions that worsen outcomes
A vaccine announcement could cause premature abandonment of protective behaviors
The gap between vaccine announcement and actual widespread protection creates a dangerous window
Masks will remain necessary even after a vaccine becomes available
money_quotes: We're terrible at anticipating feedback loops, especially in the world of coronavirus.
We keep overreacting to good news by making things worse.
Once a safe and effective vaccine is announced, people will change their behavior dramatically, undermining the good news and making it effectively bad.
posts.key_insight: Good news about a vaccine could become bad news if people prematurely abandon protective behaviors before the vaccine has actually made them safe.

posts.summary: This brief post promotes O'Neil's Bloomberg column arguing that the concept of 'pre-existing condition' protections should be expanded beyond healthcare. She suggests the principle that people shouldn't be penalized for circumstances beyond their control has applications in many other domains of life, not just medical insurance.
key_points: Pre-existing condition protections in Obamacare should be defended
The concept of protecting people from discrimination based on pre-existing conditions should expand to other areas beyond health
This represents a broader principle about not penalizing people for circumstances outside their control
money_quotes: The problem of pre-existing conditions extends far beyond health.
posts.key_insight: The principle behind healthcare's pre-existing condition protections—that people shouldn't be penalized for factors beyond their control—should be applied more broadly across society.

posts.summary: This brief post previews O'Neil's Bloomberg column arguing that a Biden victory would not mark an end to problems but rather the beginning of enormous catch-up work. She emphasizes that the challenges facing America extend far beyond reversing Trump-era damage, particularly regarding the unchecked power of big tech companies. The post frames the post-Trump period as a 'hangover' requiring sustained policy effort rather than celebration.
key_points: A Trump departure would be a 'best case scenario' but not a solution in itself
Big tech's power and malevolence requires urgent attention regardless of who is president
The policy backlog extends beyond simply undoing Trump administration actions
Recovery will require 'massive' work, suggesting the scope of neglected issues
money_quotes: In the best case scenario that Trump is gone in January, we have a massive amount of work to catch up on
There's so much to fix beyond what Trump has broken
posts.key_insight: Political change alone won't solve systemic problems like big tech's unchecked power—the real work of reform begins after the election.

posts.summary: O'Neil reflects on her disappointment with political polling after the 2020 election, admitting she fell for polls that told her what she wanted to hear. She argues that consuming polling data is more of an emotional addiction than genuine scientific inquiry. The post links to her Bloomberg Opinion column calling for Americans to break their dependence on political polls, suggesting that doubling down on polling methodology won't help the country understand itself better.
key_points: Political polling functions as an emotional addiction rather than a source of genuine understanding
Polls tend to tell people what they want to hear, creating a feedback loop of confirmation bias
The solution isn't better polling methodology but rather breaking the addiction entirely
money_quotes: I cannot believe I fell, once again, for the polling that gave me the information I wanted to hear.
It is indeed an emotional addiction, rather than a scientific curiosity.
I think we'd all be better off shedding our addiction to political polling.
posts.key_insight: Political polling has become an emotional crutch that confirms our biases rather than a tool for genuine understanding, and we should treat it like an addiction to be broken.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares her Bloomberg opinion piece about Black voter power in swing states following the 2020 election. She frames the piece as an uncharacteristically non-technical political commentary and a celebration of Stacey Abrams's voter mobilization work. The post serves primarily as a pointer to her external Bloomberg column rather than containing substantive analysis itself.
key_points: O'Neil wrote a political opinion piece for Bloomberg celebrating Black voter power in swing states
The piece specifically honors Stacey Abrams's voter mobilization efforts
She argues that discouragement campaigns targeting Black voters should no longer be effective after the 2020 election results
O'Neil acknowledges this is a departure from her usual data-focused, technical writing style
money_quotes: I wrote an uncharacteristically non-nerdy political opinion piece for Bloomberg, my way of finding yet another way of celebrating Stacey Abrams
After the 2020 election, discouragement campaigns shouldn't work anymore
posts.key_insight: The 2020 election demonstrated the decisive power of Black voters in swing states, making voter suppression through discouragement tactics less viable.

posts.summary: This guest post by Aise O'Neil argues that despite alarming headlines about inflation from both right-wing commentators and financial markets, the current high inflation readings are largely illusory. The author presents three reasons: standard inflation measures are distorted by comparing current prices to the anomalous crash during COVID lockdowns, more stable measures like median CPI show no concerning trends, and economic theory suggests inflation has significant inertia that prevents sudden jumps to hyperinflation. The post concludes that unemployment levels post-pandemic will be the real determinant of future inflation, and that Wall Street's inflation bets represent a temporary opportunity before markets recognize the readings as transitory.
key_points: Year-over-year inflation measures are misleading because they compare current prices to the anomalous crash during early pandemic lockdowns, creating an illusion of accelerating inflation
Median CPI and median PCE inflation measures are more stable and reliable indicators that show no concerning upward trend
Economic theory suggests inflation has strong inertia from embedded expectations, making a sudden jump from persistently low inflation to hyperinflation unlikely
The real question for future inflation is post-pandemic unemployment levels - high unemployment would push inflation down, not up
Wall Street's inflation bets represent a temporary arbitrage opportunity until markets recognize the readings as transitory distortions
money_quotes: When that number is higher than it was last month, that could tell us about what happened recently in terms of the CPI/PCE index, or it could tell us about what happened to the CPI/PCE index last year.

The next annual inflation figure will cover the aftershock of the shutdown price decline but not the price decline itself, while at the same time it will include price growth we are experiencing during the re-opening.
It is also probably a good idea for economic policymakers to ignore transitory shocks to the best extent possible.
What theory tells us is that it is unlikely we will go from inflation persistently undershooting 2% PCE for years to hyperinflation.
If we cannot ensure a full, rapid recovery to this economic crisis, and likely we can't, then inflation will probably be heading down, not up.
One should not pay too much attention to the ideas of Tucker Carlson and Glenn Beck (that's a more general rule).
This high inflation is illusory and eventually wall street will catch on.
posts.key_insight: High inflation readings in 2021 are a statistical artifact of comparing current prices to the anomalous COVID crash, not evidence of genuine inflationary pressure, and better measures like median CPI show no cause for alarm.

posts.summary: This guest post argues that upcoming labor reports will overstate job growth due to how seasonal adjustments work. Seasonal adjustments are statistical techniques that account for predictable yearly patterns in economic data, but they rely on historical trends that COVID-19 has disrupted. Virtual schooling has weakened the usual summer employment drops in education and among parents (especially women) who normally leave the workforce during school breaks. Since seasonal adjustments will still correct for the historical pattern of summer job losses that won't actually occur this year, the adjusted figures will artificially inflate apparent job growth in May and June 2021.
key_points: Seasonal adjustments correct raw economic data based on historical patterns, but these corrections assume incoming data follows the same seasonal trends as past data
Summer typically reduces employment both directly (education workers on break) and indirectly (parents, especially women, leaving workforce for childcare)
COVID-era virtual learning has already weakened these seasonal patterns, as many education support jobs disappeared and parents stayed home year-round
The August-September 2020 data confirms this effect: seasonally adjusted figures showed declines because the expected school-year job surge didn't materialize
The same logic means May-June 2021 reports will show inflated job growth because the expected summer job losses won't fully occur
money_quotes: The idea of seasonally adjusting data is that the meaning of a data point can depend on the time of year.
Seasonal adjustments to incoming data will be based on rigorous analysis of historical data and will implicitly presume incoming data will display the same seasonal trends as past data.
I'd argue that the disruption Covid has made to schooling is going to disrupt seasonal trends in employment.
With weaker seasonal effects now, seasonal adjustments which are based on historical data will over-account for them.
This misleading seasonally adjusted data will be what the media reports in terms of the unemployment rate and monthly job creation.
posts.key_insight: Statistical methods that work well under normal conditions can produce misleading results when unprecedented disruptions like COVID-19 break the historical patterns those methods depend on.

posts.summary: This guest post argues that rising home prices are driven by a speculative bubble rather than a genuine housing shortage, contrary to mainstream media narratives. The author points to historically low interest rates, white flight to suburbs, and self-sustaining speculation as the real causes. Key evidence includes rising home construction metrics (new home starts, residential investment, and new home sales) during the pandemic—data inconsistent with a shortage theory, which would predict declining production. The author suggests the 'shortage' framing is deliberately pushed by the home construction industry lobby to advocate for deregulation while keeping prices high.
key_points: A housing shortage would cause decreased home production, but data shows home starts, residential investment, and new home sales have all increased during the pandemic
Record-low interest rates combined with white flight from cities created speculative demand that became self-sustaining as buyers rushed to capture price gains
The National Association of Home Builders pushes the shortage narrative to advocate for deregulating zoning, building safety, and labor protections
An increase in housing supply doesn't directly translate to more housed people—it may just mean more second homes or allow household formation changes
News interpretation of economic data often serves corporate lobby interests rather than public understanding
money_quotes: Homelessness is the greatest moral evil of our time.
An increase in housing supply may not actually house new people. It may just mean more second homes or it may allow more students to move out of their parents' households.
The consequence of white flight and low borrowing cost has set off a speculative bidding war as home prices grow higher and higher.
Most of the economic experts in the housing market work for construction companies or related enterprises and have an agenda.
A lot of high level information you get from the news you read is not the truth so much as a lobby's version of the truth.

The job of the news is not just to provide facts to us but to interpret the facts for us. Unless you're a powerful corporation or association of small corporations, that interpretation is probably not being done in your own interests.
Bubbles are one of many pieces of evidence that markets aren't efficient.
Houses are good to live in, not gamble with.
posts.key_insight: The difference between a bubble and a shortage lies in whether home production rises or falls—and the data showing increased construction during the pandemic supports the bubble theory while contradicting the industry-promoted shortage narrative.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil reflects on why blogging remains valuable in 2021, prompted by her son Aise starting his own blog. She defends blogging as an 'old fashioned' form against the dominance of Twitter and Facebook, arguing it remains uniquely suited for developing fully-formed ideas. She credits her own blog with enabling her to eventually write a book, and proudly highlights her son's early posts on economics topics like hyperinflation, housing markets, and labor reports.
key_points: Blogging provides space for fully-formed ideas that social media feeds cannot match
The practice of blogging builds skills in argumentation and precise claims with evidence
Blogging enables iterative thinking—moving from initial ideas to deeper 'so what?' questions
O'Neil credits her blog and readers with making her book possible
Her son Aise is carrying on the tradition with data-driven economics posts
money_quotes: Who starts blogging in 2021? After all, it's an old fashioned writing form, and not so many people spend their days reading blogs when there are Twitter TLs and Facebook feeds to scroll through.
Blogging is a great way to get an idea out there, fully formed, on a daily or nearly daily basis.
It's good practice with making arguments, and forming precise claims with evidence.
Most of all it gets you past the initial idea formation stage to the next one, where you get to ask, so what? or what next? kinds of questions.
I never would have written a book without this blog, and of course my readers, who are the best blog readers ever.
posts.key_insight: Blogging remains a uniquely valuable medium for developing ideas iteratively and building the intellectual foundation for larger works, even in an age dominated by social media.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Cathy O'Neil shares her excitement about receiving physical copies of her new book 'The Shame Machine.' The post consists of a single enthusiastic sentence and a photo of the books. It serves as a personal milestone marker rather than substantive analysis. The post celebrates the tangible arrival of what would become her exploration of how shame is weaponized in society.
key_points: O'Neil's book 'The Shame Machine' has been published and physical copies have arrived
The post captures the author's genuine excitement at this milestone moment
This signals a new direction in her work examining shame as a social and economic tool
money_quotes: Look what came in the mail today, I'm so psyched!
posts.key_insight: Sometimes a blog post is simply a moment of celebration marking the arrival of years of work in physical form.

posts.summary: This brief post announces that Cathy O'Neil has stopped actively blogging on mathbabe.org. The site will remain online as an archive of her previous work. She expresses hope that readers will continue to find value in the archived content.
key_points: The blog is no longer being actively updated
All previous content will remain accessible as an archive
The author maintains goodwill toward her readership
money_quotes: I've stopped blogging, but I'm keeping mathbabe.org around as an archive, which I hope you enjoy!
posts.key_insight: Even when ending a project, preserving the archive allows past work to continue providing value to readers.

posts.summary: O'Neil examines the ongoing rebellion of law schools against US News & World Report rankings, sparked partly by revelations that Columbia falsified self-reported data. She traces how the rankings became the first 'Weapon of Math Destruction'—an opaque scoring system that warped higher education by incentivizing gaming rather than genuine improvement. The current negotiation between US News and law deans over formula tweaks perfectly illustrates her core thesis: algorithms are opinions embedded in math. The disagreement over what factors to weight reveals that rankings have always been subjective value judgments masquerading as objective measurement.
key_points: Columbia was caught lying about self-reported data, triggering widespread scrutiny of the rankings system
The rankings caused a destructive feedback loop: colleges gamed metrics, education worsened, costs rose, and administrative bloat increased
US News and law deans want completely different formula adjustments, proving the rankings are subjective value choices
The rankings' power comes from public trust in numbers that 'seem scientific' rather than actual validity
US News's proposed tweaks ignore student debt and school spending while deans want credit for diversity and financial aid
money_quotes: It was essentially the first Weapon of Math Destruction, if you will, because it was the first opaque scoring system that had widespread and devastating impact.

They ended up gaming it so badly that everything got warped, including most importantly the lives of teenagers, and education got worse, and most expensive, and nobody won, except maybe the huge number of people who now work in administration.
Algorithms are opinions embedded in math.
These are all stabs in the dark as to how a given person might actually decide to value a given school.
It's all opinion, it's always been opinion.
Their opinions are archaic, undependable, and totally reliant on a public that just trusts numbers because they 'seem scientific.'
posts.key_insight: The negotiation over ranking formula changes exposes that algorithmic scores are not objective truths but subjective value judgments that derive false authority from mathematical presentation.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that ChatGPT deserves neither the fear nor celebration it's receiving. The technology lacks any model for truth—it simply mimics patterns from training data without genuine understanding. While it may help students cheat, this merely exposes how weak our existing proxies for measuring learning already are. She points to Meta's Galactica as evidence that these systems are essentially 'bullshit generators' that can replicate the easy, surface-level aspects of writing while being fundamentally incapable of producing new ideas or genuine insight.
key_points: ChatGPT has no model for truth; perceived thoughtfulness comes from human wisdom in training data plus our tendency to give credit to anything that sounds like formulated thought
Technology is exposing how weak our proxies for measuring student learning have always been—cheating via the internet is not new
The future likely holds more one-on-one oral exams and fewer tests of memorized knowledge that the internet can simply provide
These AI systems can replicate what is 'easy' about expert writing (jargon, style, structure) but none of what is hard (new ideas)
Galactica's failure demonstrates these tools are 'deep fakes for science'—producing plausible-sounding nonsense
money_quotes: It doesn't have a model for truth; it's simply writing words and phrases that are akin to the patterns it has observed in the historical speech it was fed.

Any kind of thoughtfulness that might be observed in its output is a combination of the human wisdom that is embedded in its training data and the projected credit that we tend to give others when we hear them making an attempt to formulate thoughts.
The thing about technology is that it is interfering with the very weak proxies we have of measuring student learning, namely homework and tests.
It was a bullshit generator.
It sometimes worked but often just made shit up. Deep fakes but for science.
Here's one thing it will never do: create new science. And that's why there's nothing here to be all that excited about.
Galactica can do all the easy stuff but none of the hard stuff, and so why should we be impressed?
posts.key_insight: Large language models can only replicate the superficial patterns of human expertise while remaining fundamentally incapable of the genuine understanding and novel thinking that makes that expertise valuable.

posts.summary: O'Neil introduces a collaborative philosophy project exploring whether dignity can be embedded into social media design, using Donna Hicks' ten essential elements of dignity as a framework. She starts with 'Acceptance of Identity'—the ability to express authentic identity without fear of judgment—and immediately confronts the difficulty: automated hate speech detection fails, and human moderation is expensive and harmful to workers. She proposes a 'hotel room' model where spaces are private by default, require invitation, and have active moderation, contrasting it with current broadcast-friendly platforms. She acknowledges this may be impossible but points to Ravelry's knitting community as evidence that focused, purpose-driven spaces can minimize identity-based attacks.
key_points: Current social media fails to confer dignity and creates mostly toxic human experiences, though its benefits mean we should fix it rather than abandon it
Donna Hicks' framework from peace treaty negotiations provides concrete, testable conditions for dignity that can be applied to platform design
Automated hate speech detection doesn't work, and human moderation is both expensive and psychologically harmful to workers
A 'hotel room' model—private spaces, invitation-only, active moderation—could reduce hate speech but might sacrifice the frictionless connection that makes platforms engaging
Purpose-driven communities like Ravelry demonstrate that when people have something substantive to discuss, identity-based attacks naturally decrease
money_quotes: I do not think that means it will always be a great place to be. We should know by now that it's a tool, and depending on how that tool is used it could be wielded as a weapon.
Fully half of the evil shit going on now on social media is related to ridiculous, bigoted attacks on the basis of identity.
Automated bots looking for hate speech does not and will not work, and having an army of underpaid workers scanning for such speech is expensive and deeply awful to them.
That might be too much of a burden to be instant fun, but it also might lead to better conversations and way less disbursement of hate speech.
The critical use case is that someone should be able to identify themselves in all sorts of ways without fear of being yelled out or judged.
I really don't see a lot of this on Ravelry. And I think it's because it's already got something to talk about, so we don't have to name call, because we're busy.
posts.key_insight: Dignity-preserving social media may require sacrificing frictionless broadcasting for invitation-based spaces with shared purpose—because when people have something substantive to discuss, they're too busy to attack each other's identities.

posts.summary: O'Neil critiques a Washington Post food columnist who writes about how diets statistically don't work, only to undermine her own argument by describing her personal weight loss success. The columnist acknowledges that diets are shams that exploit people's desires, yet concludes by saying she personally diets because 'being fat made me unhappy.' O'Neil argues this creates a contradictory message that implies weight is a personal choice and that dieting does work if you just try hard enough. She calls out the cognitive dissonance of simultaneously debunking diets while engaging in fat shaming.
key_points: The columnist correctly identifies that diets statistically don't work and exploit people's insecurities
Despite this evidence-based critique, the columnist undermines her argument by sharing her own dieting 'success'
The piece implies fatness is a choice and a source of unhappiness that individuals should fix
O'Neil identifies the contradiction: you can't admit diets don't work while simultaneously fat shaming
money_quotes: a food columnist writes about how diets are shams, how they statistically don't work, and they play on people's desires to live a different life – all good things to point out – and yet – yet!!
So, we are left to assume that it is after all a choice, and that dieting *does* work, but just not commercial dieting? Give me a break lady.
When you admit that diets don't work, stop with the fat shaming.
posts.key_insight: It's hypocritical to cite evidence that diets don't work while simultaneously reinforcing the narrative that weight is a personal failing to be corrected.

posts.summary: O'Neil defends her use of Wegovy after losing 60 pounds, arguing that criticism from both left and right stems from misguided notions about shame and weight. The right believes weight loss should be painful repentance, while the left argues people shouldn't want to lose weight at all. She distinguishes between cosmetic weight loss (which she dismisses) and medically necessary treatment for those at serious health risk. Drawing on Susan Sontag's work on illness stigma, she predicts anti-obesity medication will become normalized just as cancer treatment shed its moral baggage.
key_points: Both conservative shame-based arguments and progressive body-acceptance arguments miss that severe obesity causes real health problems beyond aesthetics
There's a meaningful distinction between people losing weight for vanity (40 pounds or less) versus those with genuine medical need
The focus on Wegovy's side effects and failures reflects publication bias—successful treatments don't make news
Obesity stigma parallels historical stigma around cancer, and will likely fade as treatment becomes standard
The drugs' benefits clearly outweigh risks for people with or approaching diabetes
money_quotes: My premise is that it's not even actually a choice, and therefore it's unreasonable to expect people to conform to a norm that has them 'making a better choice.'
It's actually a pain in the ass to carry around 100 extra pounds: it's hard on your knees, your hips, your joints in general.
So, to be clear, fuck those people, and there are a lot of them. They are just vain and I don't care about them.
Who is going to publish an article about drugs that work great?
People used to be blamed for getting cancer. Now we (mostly) know better.
I am looking forward to the day, which I predict will come soon, when anti-fatness medicine will be standard, affordable, and accessible.
posts.key_insight: The debate over weight-loss drugs conflates cosmetic vanity with genuine medical need, and both left and right critiques ultimately perpetuate the same shame they claim to oppose or reject.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil responds to an academic paper claiming black-box auditing is insufficient for AI systems, systematically dismantling each of their five criticisms. She argues the academics misunderstand how real-world auditing works: auditors use actual historical data rather than synthetic inputs, focus on outcomes rather than understanding internal mechanisms, and deliberately avoid the conflict of interest that comes from also being the engineers who fix problems. Her core contention is that auditing should measure harm to stakeholders, not explain how algorithms 'think.'
key_points: Academic criticisms of black-box auditing reflect a disconnect from how auditing actually works in practice outside academia
Real black-box audits use historical data on how actual people were treated, not heuristics or synthetic inputs
Audits are meant to determine whether something is going wrong, not to identify exactly where or why—that's the engineers' job
Seeking to 'understand' algorithms is misleading and unattainable; even the coders don't really understand them
Separating auditing from fixing creates necessary independence and avoids conflicts of interest
money_quotes: Audits are not supposed to identify where things go wrong, they are supposed to decide whether something is going wrong.
We do not rely on heuristics or synthetic inputs, we instead see how actual people were actually treated by these systems.
Even the coders don't really 'understand' algorithms (just ask them).
Explanations of algorithms are almost always nonsense.
I'd suggest you stop trying to understand 'how an algorithm thinks' and start testing whether an algorithm is causing meaningful harm to stakeholders.
If you want to be an engineer paid to fix problems, don't call yourself an auditor.
There would be a conflict of interest if that were the same job, because you'd be incentivized to find problems to fix, or to only find fixable problems.
posts.key_insight: Algorithmic auditing should focus on measuring real-world harm to stakeholders using actual historical data, not on achieving theoretical 'understanding' of how systems work internally.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that abortion restrictions may unexpectedly become a men's rights issue due to paternity testing technology. Historically, men could deny paternity to avoid responsibility, but DNA tests now make this impossible. With abortion access restricted in some states, men can no longer pressure partners into terminating pregnancies to avoid financial obligations. This creates a new dynamic where wealthy, powerful men who previously would have quietly supported abortions may now face court-ordered child support, potentially shifting the politics of reproductive rights.
key_points: Paternity tests have fundamentally changed men's ability to deny responsibility for pregnancies
The combination of abortion restrictions and DNA testing creates new financial exposure for men
Men who previously could pressure partners toward abortion now face consequences in restrictive states
Wealthy and powerful men may be particularly affected by this shift, as courts can enforce child support
money_quotes: Abortion is seen as a woman's issue, which of course it historically has been, but with the advent of paternity tests, it could easily and quickly become a man's issue.
Back before abortion was legal, and especially if the women were unwed or otherwise shameable, the fathers could just deny paternity.
For the past few decades if a man were worried about being forced to pay he could try to talk his girlfriend or mistress into an abortion. That's obviously gotten much harder in some places.
This scenario makes abortion a man's issue in a way that I am interested to see play out in red states.
posts.key_insight: The intersection of abortion restrictions and paternity testing technology may force men—especially wealthy ones—to confront reproductive politics as a personal financial issue rather than just a women's issue.

posts.summary: O'Neil celebrates the progress of the tampon tax abolition movement, highlighting her friend Laura Strausfeld's successful advocacy work featured in a Washington Post op-ed. The post reflects on O'Neil's own involvement as a plaintiff in a New York lawsuit against the tampon tax years earlier. She explains how Strausfeld strategically coined the clinical term 'menstrual discharge collection device' (MDCD) to help male legislators understand these products' necessity. The movement is gaining momentum, with Texas recently eliminating its tampon tax.
key_points: The tampon tax abolition movement has achieved significant success, with momentum building nationwide
Strategic reframing through clinical terminology ('MDCD') proved effective in getting male legislators to understand menstrual products' necessity
Texas eliminating its tampon tax signals broader political acceptance of the issue
Sustained advocacy over many years (since at least 2016) is finally paying off
money_quotes: Holy shit I look amazing holding tampons

It's a hilarious description ('womansplaining') of why a ridiculous term, namely 'menstrual discharge collection device,' or MDCD, was coined for one purpose: to get men in power to understand that tampons and other menstrual products are things that prevent women from bleeding all over subway cars.
They somehow didn't get that until the name was changed! No kidding!
Texas just got rid of theirs, and that bodes very well.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes achieving policy change requires reframing basic concepts in jarring clinical terms to force those in power to actually confront what they're legislating.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques a CBS 'Eye on America' segment that gave uncritical airtime to Big Tech leaders like Yann LeCun to discuss AI fairness. She highlights the irony of LeCun now discussing AI fairness when he previously dismissed it as 'above his paygrade.' The piece examines how mainstream media amplifies industry talking points without skeptical voices, noting LeCun's contradictory positions on regulation and autonomous weapons. O'Neil argues this represents a collapse in AI journalism where powerful tech figures control the narrative about their own products' ethics.
key_points: CBS devoted an hour to letting Big Tech leaders self-report on AI fairness without critical counterpoints
LeCun previously dismissed AI fairness as 'above his paygrade' but now gets paid to discuss it on TV
Academia has been coopted into becoming propagandists for Big Tech rather than independent critics
LeCun opposes AI regulation while claiming to trust institutions to limit AI harms - a contradiction
Media should interview skeptics who can imagine alternative futures, not just industry insiders
money_quotes: Thinking about fairness in AI was 'above his paygrade,' a laughable comment even at the time, when he was the Head of Deep Learning at Facebook.
I'll bet there are few folks working there who actually get paid to think about fairness, and zero that get paid close to what LeCun gets paid.
LeCun maintains that fear of long term existential risk is pure sci-fi (even a broken clock!)
LeCun also 'sees an upside' in autonomous weapons, which he argues are 'necessary for the protection of democracy' instead of being good or bad.
If we have folks like LeCun weighing in on the ethics of AI and autonomous weapons, we must have reached a kind of event horizon in field of AI journalism, where nothing means anything.
How about interviewing someone else less optimistic and/or who can imagine another future?
posts.key_insight: When media lets powerful tech executives be the primary voices on AI ethics and fairness, journalism has collapsed into corporate propaganda.

posts.summary: Google's Gemini image generator produced historically absurd images like black Nazis and female popes because the company overcorrected for a known problem: datasets dominated by white male faces. Rather than thoughtfully addressing diversity in context, Google applied a blanket 'diversity at scale' approach that ignored historical and cultural specificity. O'Neil argues this reflects a fundamental misunderstanding that equity and fairness can be automated universally, when in reality these concepts require narrow, contextual application.
key_points: Google was trying to avoid the 'pale male' dataset problem common at tech companies, especially privacy-conscious ones that only use consenting employees' images
The fix—injecting diversity into everything at scale—backfired because it ignored historical context
Equity and fairness are inherently contextual concepts that cannot be universalized or automated
The same discriminator (like a FICO score) can be fair in one context and unfair in another
The failure stems from both laziness (not thinking carefully) and capitalism (doing it right would be expensive)
money_quotes: The real mistake they made is to think that equity or fairness is something that can be done at scale.
In reality equity and fairness are narrowly defined, contextual notions.
You cannot decide that 'FICO scores are legitimate discriminators' as a universal rule, just as 'diverse skin tones and genders' is not a universal good.
'Diverse' is not even a well-defined notion unless you specify a geographic area or culture.
It's a result of a combination of laziness (as in, they just didn't think very hard about this) and capitalism (as in, it would be expensive to do this right).
posts.key_insight: Fairness and diversity cannot be implemented as universal rules at scale—they require contextual judgment that tech companies are too lazy or cheap to provide.

posts.summary: This interview features a former Effective Altruism participant who joined through philosophy classes and the One for the World pledge organization, eventually working there after college. She describes her growing disillusionment with EA as it shifted from practical global health work toward abstract longtermism and AI safety concerns. The interviewee criticizes EA's culture of hyper-rationality, its treatment of time as an economic resource (having assistants do laundry), and its cult-like recruitment tactics targeting young students. She sees the movement as dominated by men who use philosophical questions as self-indulgent intellectual exercises while claiming moral legitimacy, and notes the deliberate pivot away from tangible short-term impact toward speculative future scenarios.
key_points: EA has shifted dramatically from practical global health and development work toward abstract longtermism and AI safety, abandoning tangible present-day impact
The movement functions like a cult: it dictates career paths, eating habits, how to think, and demands lifelong commitment through intense recruitment of young students
The emphasis on 'marginal hour' optimization creates toxic behavior where leaders have young assistants do their laundry because their time is supposedly more valuable
80,000 Hours funnels people into a narrow set of approved careers, primarily AI research and politics, leaving no room for community, art, or emotional fulfillment
The hyper-rational, spreadsheet-thinking approach loses essential human elements like empathy, passion, and emotional connection to charitable work
money_quotes: There are a lot of people in EA who just wanted a legitimate reason or excuse to sit around and talk about these big questions. But that made it feel like it's a real job and they're doing something good in the world instead of just sitting in a room and talking about philosophy.

There were people, there were higher-ups in the EA movement who would hire assistants, like young students or recent graduates who really just wanted to work in EA and were willing to get any job to be part of the movement, and they would have them picking up their laundry.
I think longtermism is just an excuse for a bunch of guys to legitimize the fun philosophical questions that they think are really interesting.
In this philosophical framework of impactfulness and effectiveness, there was no room for love or community or empathy or creativity.
It is kind of terrifying how intensely strategic they were when we're talking about 19-year-old students trying to figure out the best way to trick other 19-year-old students into coming.

I think it is possible to pick out parts of EA that are really good and helpful and smart without buying into other parts of it. And I think that's easier for adults who have come into it later in life, and harder for people who enter the movement as students and are kind of brainwashed from the get-go.
posts.key_insight: Effective Altruism has evolved from a practical charitable giving movement into a cult-like ideology that uses philosophical abstraction about the far future to avoid doing tangible good in the present while demanding total lifestyle commitment from young recruits.

posts.summary: Victor Zhenyi Wang, a data scientist with a disability, recounts attending a rationalist conference where philosopher Jonathan Anomaly presented on 'liberal eugenics' - voluntary genetic selection for traits like intelligence - to an uncritical audience. Wang challenges the utilitarian premise that disabled lives are 'worse off,' citing disability rights advocates who argue their lives are valuable and that disability-related challenges stem from societal structures, not inherent deficits. He critiques how metrics like QALYs inherit society's biases against disabled people and how cost-benefit analysis has historically been weaponized to deny disabled people basic rights and accommodations. Wang warns that techno-utopian visions built on these frameworks would systematically marginalize disabled people, returning to an era of institutionalization.
key_points: The author experienced the surreal discomfort of being in a room of 'do-gooders' discussing the elimination of people like himself, with no audience pushback
Disabled people argue their lives are not 'worse off' - disability shapes identity, and constraints become the foundation for rich, satisfying lives
Challenges faced by disabled people are properties of society, not inherent to disabilities - like how glasses eliminated shortsightedness as a meaningful limitation
Metrics like QALYs are biased because they rely on non-disabled people's imagined ratings of disabled life quality
Cost-benefit analysis has historically been used to deny disabled people accommodations and basic dignities, from Reagan's executive orders to absurd analyses on preventing prison rape
money_quotes: It is a surreal experience to be in a room full of people who believe that they would like to do good in the world and at the same time be a person who they would prefer not to exist at all.
We take constraints that no one would choose and build rich and satisfying lives within them.
What Johnson is claiming is that it is really a property of society that disabilities constrains the ability for one to attain the capabilities they wish for.
This metric is going to inherit all the biases society already has against disabled people.
The language of cost benefit analysis has become an ubiquitous excuse to deny all sorts of basic rights in American politics.
It is tempting and beyond that terribly easy to use the language of cost effectiveness as an instrument of dispossession.
In that world, which seems not so far off, we will be marked as lesser, our dignities and rights denied to us.
posts.key_insight: The utilitarian logic underlying effective altruism and techno-optimism, when combined with cost-benefit analysis, creates a framework that systematically devalues disabled lives and has historically been used to deny them basic rights and dignity.

posts.summary: This is a guest post by Sander O'Neil (likely Cathy's son) presenting a JavaScript-based clock/map/star chart that tracks the sun's position relative to Earth. The project combines orbital mechanics with geographic visualization, showing the sun's current and future positions on both a polar star chart and an equirectangular world map. The implementation uses Wikipedia-sourced orbital dynamics data and iteratively solves for Earth's position using mean anomaly calculations. The post explains each visual element and the mathematical principles behind the simulation, including handling Earth's elliptical orbit and axial tilt.
key_points: The clock displays the sun's position using two visualizations: a polar star chart showing angular distance from zenith, and a world map showing the subsolar point
The implementation models Earth's actual elliptical orbit rather than using simplified circular approximations
Mean anomaly calculations require iterative estimation since there's no closed-form solution for position from mean anomaly
Various orbital corrections account for misalignments between Earth's orbit, rotation, and calendar (e.g., apoapsis being 182 days from New Year)
money_quotes: This is a star chart, but just for the sun.
The way I have actually gotten these positions is basically the most complicated possible method.
This is technically not an exact solution for the earths location in orbit but it gets 5 times more accurate with each correction so its indiscernible after 10 corrections
You will see odd corrections here, some constants thrown in. This is because the orbit and rotation of the earth are not all aligned.
posts.key_insight: Accurately tracking the sun's position requires modeling Earth's elliptical orbit with iterative numerical methods rather than simple circular approximations.

posts.summary: O'Neil observes that AI advertising has saturated public spaces like Bay Area billboards and airports, suggesting a classic bubble pattern. She argues that VC money has flooded into AI startups with the mandate to build first and find customers later, resulting in mediocre products nobody wants. She supports her skepticism with striking financial data: Big Tech spent $246 billion on AI infrastructure in 2024, OpenAI loses $2 for every $1 it makes, and trillion-dollar investments are planned despite unclear demand. She predicts this bubble will burst badly, possibly with Trump's tariffs taking the blame.
key_points: AI advertising has completely saturated public spaces like airport billboards, indicating massive marketing spend
The pattern suggests VCs funded AI startups with a 'build first, find customers later' approach
Big Tech spent $246 billion on AI infrastructure in 2024 with over $1 trillion planned for the next five years
OpenAI, the market leader, loses $2 for every $1 in revenue and expects $11 billion annual losses by 2026
The bubble's collapse may be wrongly attributed to Trump's tariffs rather than fundamental business problems
money_quotes: Every single one of them was advertising some narrowly defined, or ludicrously broadly defined, or sometimes downright undefined AI.
It's almost like VC money has been poured into all of these startups with the single directive, to go build some AI and then figure out if they can sell it.
Now there's a shitton of useless (or, as Tressie McMillam Cottom describes it, deeply 'mid') AI products that were incredibly expensive to build and nobody fucking cares about it at all.
OpenAI loses $2 for every $1 it makes.
So, um, I think this is going to be bad. And it might be blamed on Trump's tariffs! Ironic.
posts.key_insight: The AI industry shows classic bubble dynamics: massive capital expenditure and ubiquitous marketing for products that lose money and lack clear demand.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates Bernie Sanders and AOC's 'Fighting Oligarchy Tour' through red states as a necessary revival of Occupy-era populism. She recounts an MSNBC exchange where pundits couldn't fathom why Democrats would campaign in Utah except for media attention. O'Neil argues this reveals the Democratic Party's fundamental blindness: they've replaced genuine voter engagement with poll-watching and surgical electoral strikes. The party has forgotten that changing minds requires actually talking to people, not optimizing turnout models. This strategic myopia, she contends, is why red states keep getting redder.
key_points: The Democratic Party has replaced genuine voter engagement with data-driven electoral optimization, focusing only on swing states
Media and party operatives literally cannot conceive of political activity that isn't about winning immediate elections
Oligarchic power has grown significantly worse since the original Occupy movement
Red states deepen their Republican lean partly because Democrats have abandoned persuasion in favor of turnout math
Sanders and AOC represent a return to the basic democratic act of talking to actual people about actual problems
money_quotes: Enough is enough. It's time for the next version of Occupy.
About fucking TIME some folks on the left talk to the people of these states about the way things are actually working against the working person.

At some point in the distant past, they stopped thinking about voters. Instead they started looking at numbers, and polls, and focusing very narrowly on incremental elections and surgical strikes into purple states.
Bernie and AOC are actually there to talk to actual people with actual problems, and trying to persuade those folks by paying actual attention to them and their problems.
This blindness to how people actually are makes the poll-watchers super blind to what really matters in terms of changing people's minds.
They literally don't see the point of talking to people who live in red states. And that's why the red states get deeper red.
posts.key_insight: Democrats have become so fixated on electoral math and polling data that they've forgotten politics is fundamentally about persuading actual human beings, not optimizing turnout in purple districts.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that AI researchers are exhibiting cultish behavior by treating language models as if they possess consciousness and honesty. She critiques the AI Futures Project for making speculative predictions reminiscent of flawed COVID models and for using language that presupposes LLMs have minds that could 'lie' or be 'honest.' She emphasizes that LLMs merely predict the next word based on training data and have no notion of truth or consciousness. The post concludes that projecting consciousness onto algorithms built with Reddit data is itself a form of self-deception.
key_points: AI hype has reached bubble territory, with researchers exhibiting cultish levels of buy-in to AI capabilities
Speculative AI timelines (like 'superhuman coder by 2027') resemble the flawed COVID death models - built for a false sense of control
LLMs have no concept of truth or honesty - they simply predict next words based on patterns in training data
Framing interpretability as 'not advanced enough to read an AI's mind' betrays an unwarranted belief in machine consciousness
The irony is that humans don't even know when they're lying to themselves, yet project this capacity onto algorithms
money_quotes: What's also weird, bordering on cultish, is how bought in the researchers are in the world of AI.
There is no notion of truth in an LLM, it's just predicting the next word based on patterns in the training data (think: Reddit).
The words they use to talk about how it's hard to know if it's lying or telling the truth betray the belief that there is a consciousness in there somehow.
Like we should try harder to summon up fake evidence of consciousness?
Can you even imagine having taken an actual philosophy class? Or were you too busy studying STEM?
Can it be lying to itself? No, because it has no consciousness.
We could be lying to ourselves or to each other at any moment! Like, right now, when we project consciousness onto the algorithm we just built with Reddit training data!
posts.key_insight: AI researchers are projecting consciousness and moral capacity onto statistical pattern-matching systems, revealing more about human self-deception than about machine intelligence.

posts.summary: O'Neil praises a South Park special episode called 'The End of Obesity' for its satirical take on weight loss culture, fat shaming, and the pharmaceutical industry. She notes the episode skillfully critiques multiple targets simultaneously: wealthy women using drugs for vanity, the inaccessibility of medications for those who need them most, body positivity performativity, healthcare bureaucracy, and pharmaceutical profiteering. She contrasts the show's incisive commentary favorably against mainstream journalism on the topic.
key_points: South Park manages to critique multiple aspects of weight loss culture simultaneously without losing nuance
The episode highlights class disparities in access to weight loss medications
Healthcare industry bureaucracy and pharmaceutical profiteering are portrayed as the true villains
The 'will power' narrative is called out as an inadequate substitute for real solutions
Most mainstream journalism on weight loss drugs fails to address underlying systemic issues
money_quotes: I've read tons of coverage about weight-loss drugs in the past few years by journalists, and it's mostly garbage and rarely gets at the underlying issues.
They truly villainize the health care industry, and in particular the way it tortures people that try to navigate the system.
They address the tired notion of 'will power' and how it should be sufficient as a replacement for real solutions to the problem of obesity.
posts.key_insight: A cartoon comedy special does a better job unpacking the systemic issues around obesity and weight loss drugs than years of serious journalism on the topic.

posts.summary: O'Neil responds to three AI articles—about AI shopping agents, AI eliminating entry-level jobs, and AI causing human extinction—by rejecting all three narratives as propaganda designed to make AI feel inevitable. She argues AI isn't actually good at these tasks, shouldn't be given control over finances, and certainly isn't conscious enough to kill humanity. Her solution is simple: don't cede control to AI in the first place, and maintain human oversight of important tasks like legal briefs and accounting.
key_points: The tech industry pushes three narratives simultaneously: AI as helpful autonomous agent, AI as job eliminator, and AI as existential threat—all designed to make AI seem inevitable
AI's actual capabilities don't match the hype—it 'just isn't' that good at stuff
Giving AI control over finances and critical decisions creates unnecessary vulnerability to hackers and errors
The 'inevitable AI future' framing is a scare tactic to force adoption rather than critical evaluation
Human mindfulness and deliberation in tasks like shopping and document review is simply better than AI automation
money_quotes: So, putting this all together, how about we don't?
AI isn't that good at stuff (because it just isn't)
AI is definitely not conscious, will not be conscious, and will not work to kill humanity any more than our smart toasters that sense when our toast is done.
This is all propaganda, pointing in one direction, which is to make us feel like AI is inevitable, we will not have a future without it, and we might as well work with it rather than against it.
I have another plan: let's not cede control to problematic, error-ridden AI in the first place.
It's also just better to be mindful and deliberate when we shop anyway.
And let's not assume AI can take over things like accounting, because again, that's too much damn power.
Wake up, people! This is not a good idea.
posts.key_insight: The framing of AI as inevitable—whether as helpful agent, job destroyer, or existential threat—is propaganda designed to bypass critical evaluation and force adoption of technology we should simply refuse to cede control to.

posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the deployment of LLMs in the VA represents a dangerous turning point for AI adoption. While she has been cautiously observing chatbot adoption, noting most implementations have been overhyped and error-prone, the planned 83,000 DOGE job cuts to the VA signal something more sinister. She contends this is a deliberate strategy to replace critical healthcare staff with AI, creating a captive federal customer base for Silicon Valley while simultaneously pushing for a 10-year moratorium on AI regulation that would eliminate oversight.
key_points: LLMs have been mostly overhyped with limited real-world adoption due to frequent errors
Healthcare insurer AI deployments are concerning but lawsuits may provide some check on harm
The VA job cuts reveal a plan to force federal dependence on Silicon Valley AI products
The simultaneous push for a 10-year AI regulation moratorium would remove any oversight
Doctor administration work is critically important and not replaceable by AI
money_quotes: so far it's mostly been a weird and wasteful (and hugely overhyped) product from Silicon Valley that hasn't been picked up too much in reality, because it makes tons of mistakes
I worry about kids and other vulnerable people spending too much time with bots
Doctor administration work is important and not replaceable by AI
we now see the plan for what it is: remove critically important VA staff and replace them with VA, presumably as a way of forcing a long term federal client for Silicon Valley's latest product
And presumably with no oversight, judging from the 10-year AI regulation moratorium that these same guys are pushing
posts.key_insight: The VA job cuts combined with proposed AI regulation bans reveal a coordinated plan to create captive government markets for unregulated AI products at the expense of veterans' care.

posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques an Apple paper that tests Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) against Large Language Models (LLMs) on problems of increasing complexity. While the paper shows both fail at complex tasks, O'Neil argues it misses a deeper point: the Tower of Hanoi puzzle isn't actually harder for more discs once you understand the recursive solution. The fact that LRMs fail at 10 discs when the solution is trivially iterative proves they never truly understood the concept. She calls for abandoning the word 'thinking' to describe these models, as they're merely predicting tokens, not achieving genuine mathematical comprehension.
key_points: The Tower of Hanoi puzzle doesn't get harder with more discs if you understand the recursive solution - LRMs' failure at n=10 proves they never grasped the underlying concept
LRMs finding correct answers then overworking to wrong ones is further evidence of zero actual understanding
Her 16-year-old son understood the iterative solution in three minutes - demonstrating what real 'aha moment' comprehension looks like versus token prediction
Sam Altman's claim that superintelligent AI will solve climate change is exposed as absurd when AI can't handle a simple recursive puzzle
Even papers critiquing AI capabilities still fall prey to Silicon Valley's pseudo-scientific marketing language about 'thinking'
money_quotes: The LRMs really do not understand all of those blogposts they've been trained on explicitly, and thus have not successfully been shown to 'think' at all.

There's no real mathematical thinking going on here, because there's no 'aha' moment when the model actually understands the thousands of explanations of proofs of how to solve the Tower of Hanoi that it's been trained on.
They are, as always, predicting the next word in someone's blog post who is writing about the Towers of Hanoi.

Even though it's a critique of the status quo thinking around LRMs and LLMs, still falls prey to the most embarrassing mistake, namely of assuming the pseudo-scientific marketing language of Silicon Valley, wherein the models are considered to be 'thinking'.
If AI cannot understand the simplest and most mathematical and sanitary issue such as the Tower of Hanoi for n discs, it definitely cannot help us out of an enormously messy human quagmire.
I hope this kind of rhetoric is exposed once and for all, as a money and power grab and nothing else.
posts.key_insight: LRMs' failure on Tower of Hanoi at 10 discs isn't about complexity scaling - it's proof they never understood the recursive solution at all, despite being trained on thousands of explanations of it.

posts.summary: O'Neil responds to a Matteo Wong article in The Atlantic arguing that both AI hype men and critics are missing the point. She acknowledges that dismissive critiques like calling AI 'a racist pile of linear algebra' may understate its real-world impact. The core argument is that debates about whether AI 'thinks' are beside the point when it's actually replacing workers. O'Neil advocates for taking AI seriously as a transformative technology while remaining clear-eyed that it primarily benefits those making billions from it.
key_points: The AI debate has become polarized between over-hyping true believers and dismissive critics, with both sides arguing about the wrong things
Whether AI 'thinks' like a person is irrelevant if it can replace you at your job
AI is a technological tool that will make lives different but not necessarily better, depending on context
The real beneficiaries of AI are those making billions from it, not the general public
Critical analysis should focus on the many specific contexts where AI is being deployed rather than abstract debates about its nature
money_quotes: Who cares if AI 'thinks' like a person if it's better than you at your job?
Let's take it seriously, but not pretend it's the answer to anyone's dreams, except the people for whom it's making billions of dollars.
Like any technological tool, it's going to make our lives different but not necessarily better, depending on the context.
AI not going away, it's real, it's replacing people at their job, and we have to grapple with it seriously.
Given how many contexts AI is creeping into, there are a ton of ways to think about it. Let's focus our critical minds on those contexts.
posts.key_insight: The AI debate should move past abstract arguments about machine cognition and focus on the concrete ways the technology is reshaping work and society—primarily to the benefit of those profiting from it.

posts.summary: O'Neil draws a parallel between the 2008 financial crisis and the current cryptocurrency bubble, arguing that both involve government protection of markets for political reasons. She recounts her early skepticism of Bitcoin evangelists at Occupy Wall Street who couldn't explain how crypto would democratize banking. The post's central concern is that Trump and his family are so heavily invested in crypto that they have personal financial incentives to prevent a market crash, creating a more direct and troubling conflict of interest than even the Obama-era bailouts.
key_points: Bitcoin evangelists in 2012 couldn't explain how blockchain would make banking more democratic, only pushed adoption
The replacement of pensions with stock portfolios created political pressure to never let markets fail, distorting natural corrections
The Tea Party blamed borrowers (especially people of color) for the 2008 crisis rather than the system that enabled it
Trump's family has billions personally invested in crypto, creating unprecedented conflicts of interest
Government intervention to protect bubbles rewards the wrong people while victims bear the costs
money_quotes: Come back when you can get rewarded for saving energy instead of wasting it.
It seemed outrageous that nobody was losing their shirt except for the victims of the whole scam, because it was political suicide to allow the markets to collapse.
As pensions were replaced by stock portfolios, it became a requirement to never allow stocks to dip too low for too long. And the result was truly non-natural and perverted.
This time it's Trump and his actual family that is so heavily invested in crypto that they personally stand to lose billions of dollars if and when the bubble bursts.
Which leads me to wonder, what will they end up doing to prevent this particular bubble from bursting? I don't know, but I'm guessing it will be really gross.
posts.key_insight: When political leaders have personal billions at stake in a financial bubble, the inevitable government intervention to prevent its collapse will be even more corrupt than past bailouts.
